sessions.title: Arboreal-human intra-actions: A walkshop to explore living with trees in West London
sessions.description:
This walkshop* engages with questions of urban socio-environmental justice through an embodied exploration of living with London’s trees.

 Whilst walking through the streets around the RGS-IBG conference venue in West London, participants will be invited to tune into their embodied and affective experiences of what it is like to live with trees.

 By UN standards, London is a forest, with a canopy cover of 21% (Lösing, 2025). Recognising trees' role in mitigating the impacts of the climate emergency alongside their social and health benefits, the city has ambitions to increase that cover by 10% in the next decades (London Urban Forest Partnership, 2020). Yet trees are not evenly distributed, with global-majority communities and lower-income groups across the UK disproportionately more likely to live in neighbourhoods with a paucity of canopy cover (Friends of the Earth, 2023; The Woodland Trust, 2023).

 In these contexts, this walkshop posits trees as lively entities, intra-acting with humans and other more-than-humans (Barad, 2007; Bennett, 2010), shaping their environment whilst also being shaped by it and by anthropogenic/anthropocenic actions. The walkshop asks participants to think about what tree equity feels like, using The Woodland Trust’s Tree Equity Score Map to compare this West London site to other places. It also prompts participants to consider the ways in which austerity measures in the UK have impacted on the planting and maintenance of trees (Iozzi, 2025; Rotherham and Flinders, 2019; Woudstra and Allen, 2022).

 In addition, participants will have the opportunity to 'listen like a tree' using the more-than-human community toolkit, to further sensitise ourselves to the lives of trees.

 Methodologically, the walkshop will invite participants to reflect on what mobile, embodied, affective and collective methods can offer our engagements with questions of socio-environmental (in)justice in urban spaces (Bates and Rhys-Taylor, 2017; Rondel and Henneke, 2024).

 As an exploratory event, we invite participants to bring their own knowledges and expertises, to share and reflect.

 *walking is understood as walking or wheeling or any kind of moving through space that can be done by any body at any speed (Rose, 2025).

 Practicalities 

 The walkshop will take 2 ½ hours, including a 1-hour walk and a 30-minute outdoor activity. There will be a short briefing session ahead of the walk and time for reflections afterwards. We will invite you to document your experiences and reflections, and these can be uploaded (photos, sound-recordings, videos, or text) to a shared Padlet.

 The route will be approximately 3km long with pauses to rest. We will be moving on pavements and paved paths, with a possible short section on paths in Hyde Park. We will be crossing roads at designated crossing points with dropped kerbs and/or pedestrian crossings. Some of the roads will be busy with motorised traffic and cyclists. The footways will be busy with pedestrians. Depending on the weather, pollution levels may be high.

 Places are limited, please sign up in advance
 When signing up, please let us know of any accessibility requirements and we can explore supporting your participation.

sessions.title: Politicising Political Geography: Bridges, Dialogues, Solidarity
sessions.description: This event is a space for academics from across the field of political geography to take part in important conversations about the world and the discipline. PolGRG members are eager to build on ongoing conversations within the geographical community and explore how we translate these into real-world outcomes. Themes will cover academic responsibility, the place of research and higher education in a changing geopolitical conjuncture, the newly emboldened Islamophobias, racisms, and far-right politics in and beyond the UK, and other urgent issues. Rather than an extension of the conference’s predominant panel layout, this workshop will incorporate various formats, such as participatory exercises, roundtables, and article discussions. There would also be space for conversations around professional development, navigating the discipline, and teaching political geography.

sessions.title: Making geography matter in turbulent times
sessions.description:
In a period marked by significant uncertainty and rapid change—whether arising from shifting geopolitical relations, the escalating climate emergency, the cost‑of‑living crisis, or the increasingly precarious position of higher education—we find ourselves working and teaching within profoundly turbulent times. Geography, as a discipline centrally concerned with spatial relationships, power, environment, and society, is uniquely positioned to engage with these challenges. While there is broad consensus that geography matters, an enduring question remains: how do we effectively demonstrate its relevance and value to young people, policymakers, communities, and our own institutions?
 This seminar, hosted by GeogEd, provides a timely opportunity to reflect on and critically discuss the ongoing and future relevance of geography in addressing contemporary global and local challenges. The event will also mark the 50th anniversary of the Journal of Geography in Higher Education (JGHE), celebrating its longstanding contribution to advancing scholarship, pedagogy, and debate in geography education, and its role in shaping this agenda over the past five decades.
 Bringing together colleagues from across the diverse spectrum of geographical research, teaching, and practice, the seminar will create space to explore how we are collectively working to make geography matter—intellectually, socially, and institutionally—both now and in the future. The programme will combine a series of short presentations with a participatory workshop, encouraging dialogue, exchange of ideas, and collaborative reflection.
 Participants are invited to submit an abstract for a 10‑minute Lightning Talk to contribute to the afternoon seminar on the theme Making geography matter in turbulent times . These Lightning Talks will form the basis for subsequent discussion and will be followed by a workshop facilitated by Professor David Higgitt, Editor‑in‑Chief of JGHE.
 Colleagues who wish to attend and/or contribute to the seminar (online or in person)are warmly encouraged to complete the Making Geography Matter in Turbulent Times: GeogEd Pre-RGS Seminar Registration – Fill in form by 27 June 2026

sessions.title: Modelling Bodies/Modelling Cities
sessions.description: An interactive workshop with parallel sessions including the opportunity to explore the Royal Geographical Society and Kensington area by using a smartphone-enabled 3D modelling app, view an exhibition of a physical city model layered with real-time data, as well as contribute to a lively discussion on urban digital visualities (TBC).

The Women in Economic Geography Social Hour will be held on Tuesday, 1 Sept, 6-8pm at The Queen's Arms (30 Queen's Gate Mews, South Kensington, London SW7 5QL).
 All women and non-binary folks who work in or around the sub-discipline are encouraged to attend, meet, and network over drinks and snacks in a warm and welcoming space.

sessions.title: Chair's opening keynote: The shape of inequality and the weight of the world
sessions.description:
At a time of political upheaval, in an age of mistrust, on the cusp of unprecedented technological change, it is easy to lose sight of the shape of inequality, and the cost of going with the flow.

 This presentation reflects on what has gone wrong, whether there is hope, and how to turn it into a plan.

papers.title: The Shape of Inequality and the Weight of the World

sessions.title: Opening welcome reception
sessions.description: All delegates are invited to join us for the opening welcome reception from 7.30pm on Tuesday 1 September in the Royal Geographical Society (with IBG) Marquee.

sessions.title: Geo Plenary Panel: AI and Climate
sessions.description:
The next few decades could be the most disruptive and consequential in human history, as environmental uncertainty and technological change intensify and interact. The existential threat posed by global heating is well documented; as UN Secretary General Antonio Gueterres put it in April 2022, we are 'firmly on track toward an unliveable world', making it 'now or never' for the planet. At the same time, governments and their populations are starting to wake up to the potential existential risks threatened by rapid and largely unregulated developments in AI technology, as well as the myriad challenges presented by the socio-economic transformations already occurring at significant environmental cost.

 The interaction of these two threats is an issue of major importance in both scholarship and policy, yet the outcome remains an open question. Whilst more pessimistic analysts have warned that water and energy-intensive AI will further entrench existing inequalities and erode the social and environmental foundations of resilience, others have posited a brighter scenario. Could AI enable greater social mobility? Could it become more crisis-responsive, channeled towards innovations to address large-scale hazards and climate change-induced risk? In the eyes of some, each crisis may be the solution to the other, with AI enhancing human capacity to understand and handle the many challenges associated with a changing climate.

 This keynote will explore the threats, challenges, and opportunities of AI in the context of environmental sustainability and climate breakdown. Is its environmental cost insurmountable, or is there hope of a sustainable future with AI as an active partner? Our keynote speakers will consider these questions and more.

sessions.title: From Displacement to Dispossession: Carceral Urbanisms and the Politics of Banishment in Contemporary Europe
sessions.description:
This panel invites critical geographers to interrogate contemporary regimes of banishment proliferating across European cities. From the intensification and normalisation of border controls to the routinised violation of human rights; from the emergence of new forms of right-wing violence to the recrudescence of authoritarian populisms, European cities have become central infrastructures through which racialised exclusion and abjection are produced and governed. Urban space functions not merely as a backdrop but as a key technology in the manufacture and management of intersecting emergencies, carceral logics, and necropolitics. 

 The concept of racial banishment, as foregrounded in the seminal work of Ananya Roy (2019) and others, provides a critical entry point into these dynamics. Roy’s theorisation highlights the central role of the state and the constitutive place of race in the shift from displacement to dispossession. Banishment names not only the racial-colonial histories and dispossessive political economies of dwelling, but also Eurocentric and white supremacist regimes of the human articulated through the urban itself—a dispossession of personhood that sustains racial capitalism, mediated by anticipatory urban logics of punishment, peripheralisation, and premature death. At the same time, cities remain contested terrains in which practices of refusal, survival, and insurgent inhabitation continue to emerge against and within these violent conditions.

 As this panel aims to explore, the analytic of banishment foregrounds the historical and material relations that exceed its governing logics, raising as Roy argues, urgent epistemological and ontological questions for radical geography. How might we trace the genealogies, spatialities, and materialities through which banishment is actually (dis)assembled in everyday life? Setting in conversation European and North American cities at this moment, the panel poses an urgent political question concerning the position of cities vis-à-vis national politics: what might an affirmative urban political project look like amid national (and supranational) regimes of banishment? 

 These are questions that not only speak directly to the Conference’s focus on spatial inequalities and injustices but also foreground the development of spatial grammars that point to the making of more just and sustainable worlds.

papers.title: Resisting Racial Banishment at the Northern French Border
Affirmative Geographies and Communities of Method: Narrating a Counter-Athens Beyond Banishment
After Decampment/Démantèlement: Racial banishment, dispossession and habitation at city’s end
Carceral continuums in the urban space: The racialized exclusion of undocumented people in Turin, Italy.

sessions.title: Everyday Practices and Public Life in Urban China (1)
sessions.description:
Cities in China are in the midst of a profound, ongoing transition. Intertwined with macroeconomic and demographic shifts, this transition is grounded in changing urban landscapes and deeply felt in the rhythms of urban life. It is reshaping people's aspirations—regarding work, family, gender and ageing—also their everyday practices and embodied experiences, from evolving cultures of consumption and leisure to new ways of spending time, seeking connection, and building relationships in the city. In this sense, public space provides a compelling site for examining everyday urban life under these conditions, where multi-layered changes, challenges, encounters, and coexistence are juxtaposed, negotiated, reconfigured and lived.

 This session seeks to open a conversation about how to study, think, and write about Urban China through the lens of everyday practices and public life. It brings together interdisciplinary and empirically grounded research that foregrounds ordinary, mundane, and often overlooked dimensions of everyday life through which individuals navigate and negotiate urban change. By centring these lived experiences, the session demonstrates how close attention to everyday life generates conceptual insights and opens up methodological possibilities for urban geography.

sessions.title: Marginality and question of (in)equality and justice.
sessions.description:
The mission of the IGU Commission is to research marginality and the processes of marginalisation from different perspectives and with a geographical basis. The main focus is to better understand multi-scalar relations between the globalisation process and how geographical marginality evolves at the local and regional levels. Moreover, we seek to improve our understanding of local and regional responses to different forms of marginality and marginalisation processes.

 This aligns very closely with the Conference’s 2026 theme, namely: Geographies of inequalities: toward just places , in that our Commission’s work focusses on spatial, social and economic differences and inequalities across geographical space and potential interventions to address such disparities in a just manner.

 To this end, we have compiled a set of papers which we propose to present at the conference in 2026, centred on the shared topic of inequalities and marginalisation/de-marginalisation. The papers demonstrate the existing variety of marginality and marginalisation facets, whether social and/or spatial, encompassing rural, urban, and in-between places, as well as environmental (in)justice leading to marginalisation of groups. The selection also highlights different, and perhaps the most dominant, encounters with marginality in various places and regions across the world. We have organised the proposed papers and topics according to their main focus: social inequalities and marginalisation and environmental (in)justice, which mostly concern mixed social and cultural groups and the resulting marginalisation, and the underlying processes of marginalisation and environmental (in)justice and (attempts at) de-marginalisation in urban and rural places.

sessions.title: Critical Muslim Geographies: Trajectories and Research Pathways (1)
sessions.description:
Disquiet with the dominant framing of integration and difference in the existing body of work on the geographies of Muslims has, in recent years, led to emerging scholarly debates on Critical Muslim Geographies and related studies (Allouache, 2023; Fernandez, 2025, Sidaway, 2022). This call for a panel session invites pithy contributions and reflections that consider the prospects for advancing Critical Muslim Geographies and engage with the insights, challenges, thresholds, and intellectual solidarities to shape manifestos for this developing field (Sidaway, 2023). While there is a breadth of literature on Muslims in geography, we call to expand the depth and range of theoretical work (not least from within Islamic scholarship), as well empirical sites and experiences that inform Muslim geographies, thereby transcending thematic areas of focus that too often simply render “Muslim” as an adjective of diversity across space (Allouache, 2023). We share the ambition that being able to think of the world from “perspectives of the mosque from the inside looking out” (Ahmed, 2018, p. 206), rather than from the outside looking in, has potential to change the treatment of Islam and Muslims in geography.

 Panelists will recharge and move beyond established decolonial geographies by forging connections with adjacent literatures and other intellectual traditions. The panel discussion may be theoretical, historically oriented, methodological, or empirically grounded, and will contribute to advancing Critical Muslim geographies by engaging the following vectors:

 - Critical geography and Critical Muslim geography and geographers (capital, class, coloniality, difference, discourse, power, and racialisation, and the uneven and oft-contested production of landscapes, places, and spaces)
- Securityscapes, surveillance, control, and biopolitics
- Geographies of AI, STS, technopolitics, and Islam
- Geographies of Islamophobia; wider geographical analysis of “everyday” (Najib, 2021), historical and political Islamophobias 
- Labour; Islam and the city, the politics of homes, work, workplaces, sites of worship, umrah and haj
- Muslim environmentalisms and political ecologies 
- Histories and development of geographical thought minding and thinking with traditions of Islamic knowledge and learning 
- Decolonial pathways in the interface between geography, Islam and area studies
- Research methodologies, ethics and ways of fully realising Critical Muslim geographies’ potential (Intellectual and research community-building)

 The session is co-sponsored by the History and Philosophy of Geography Research Group (HPGRG), the Race, Culture and Equality Working Group (RACE), and the Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa Research Group (MENA-GEOG).

 ***

 REFERENCES 

 Ahmed, A.-A. (2018). The Other Ethical Approval: The Importance of Being “Islamic”. Fieldwork in Religion , 12 (2), 204-222. 

 Allouache, Y. A. (2023). Muslim geographies, positionality, and ways of knowing migration. Area , 55 (3), 381-389. 

 Allouache, Y. A. (2024). Makkah's hinterworlds: Jeddah, the Indian Ocean Hajj, and Southeast Asia. Singapore Journal of Tropical Geography , 45 (2). 403-412. 

 Fernandez, S. (2025) Networks of securitisation in the academy: The role of friendship, solidarity and radical geographies. Area , 00, e70051. 

 Najib, K. (2021). Spatialized Islamophobia . Routledge. 

 Sidaway, J. D. (2022). Beyond the decolonial: Critical Muslim geographies. Dialogues in Human Geography , 13 (3), 337-358. 

 Sidaway, J. D. (2023). A manifesto for critical Muslim geographies. Dialogues in Human Geography , 13 (3), 387-391.

sessions.title: Urban Creative Clusters and Upscaling of Socio-Spatial Exclusion in the Global South (1)
sessions.description:
The Global South is undergoing a cultural resurgence in a contradictory frame of both decolonisation and recolonisation. Start-up hubs, heritage precincts, film cities, and design parks based on regional and national cultural themes are promoted as creative clusters for economic growth, innovation and global competitiveness, and as symbolic representations of cultural pride. Modernist projects like Dashanzi Art District of Beijing (Yin, 2014), Porto Maravilha (Rio de Janeiro) (Broudehoux,, 2017), Seongsu-dong and Seoul's creative neighbourhoods of South Korea (Chang, 2023), Medellín’s social urbanism and cultural infrastructure of Colombia (Guerra, 2014), and Marina Bay of Singapore (Micheli, 2023) are also known to have led to adverse and exclusionary development. Such initiatives are linked to national urban policy frameworks (Roy, 2009). In many countries, in addition to focused programmes for heritage conservation, redevelopment and place-based regeneration, a ‘retrofitting’ framework under the Smart City infrastructure modernisation mission is used to this end. However, critical scholarship indicates that such an attempt to reconfigure culture and sites through the imagination of the past for reproducing historical social memories can also yield uneven socio-economic outcomes (Molho, 2020). The demolition and displacement of informal settlements and shops, the sanitisation of places from the poor and ‘cultural others’, and ‘selective’ heritage conservation and promotion are frequently observed across Africa, Eastern and South Asia, and Latin America. Smith (2006) calls this ‘authorised heritage discourse’, implying the promotion of official narratives on what should count as heritage (Appadurai, 1996). Creative clusters, as such, may reinforce inequalities based on class, caste, gender, and ethnicity rather than address them (El-Husseiny, 2020; Peck, 2005; Pratt, 2011). 

 This session invites papers based on empirical case studies, comparative studies (South and North, or South-South) and conceptual contributions on how creative cluster projects operate within countries of the Global South. In other words, the papers should foreground the urban realities of the Global South and should address, though not limited to, any of the questions of (a) How do urban planning and policy frameworks (like smart city) shape the heritage and creative clusters and urban spaces? (b) How does the development of creative clusters lead to demolitions, displacements, gentrification, and socio-spatial exclusions? (C) How do social and cultural belongings, and informality, negotiate their access to the creative clusters and their economies? (d) What forms of alternative policies and planning can help in socio-economically just and inclusive creative urban development? 

 Papers focusing on metropolitan cities, heritage towns/cities, and small and secondary cities are especially encouraged. 

 Keywords : Creative cluster, heritage, urban policies, exclusion, Global South

 Instructions for Contributors/Authors 

 If you would like to contribute a paper, please submit an abstract (400 words maximum) with 5-6 keywords to shaban@tiss.ac.in or submit online using the links given above for the type of sessions you want to participate in.

 At the end of the abstract, please include a short biography (including the full name of the author(s), position, institutional affiliations, and email address). In case you have any questions, please do write to shaban@tiss.ac.in

sessions.title: Contested Inclusions: Platform Power, (In)Visibility and Digital Inequalities
sessions.description:
Digital inclusion is frequently presented as a matter of connectivity, skills, or technological access (van Dijk, 2020) enabled by the democratisation promised by digital technologies. Such framings obscure the extent to which inclusion is increasingly organised through platform infrastructures and algorithmic (in)visibility regimes (Petre et al., 2019; Glatt, 2022) that actively structure who is able to participate, whose labour is rendered valuable, and which forms of engagement remain marginal or extractable. Across sectors ranging from governance (Boulianne, 2018) and environmental action (Feroz & Chiravuri, 2021) to gambling and digital play (Macey & Hamari, 2024), migration (Zou & Deng, 2022), health (Ahmed et al., 2025), creative economies (Pererva & Mazorenko, 2025) and cultural production (Poell et. al., 2022), digital platforms have become central mechanisms through which longstanding global and local inequalities are re-worked. 

 This session invites contributions that examine digital inclusion as a contested process shaped by political economy, design, and institutional power. We are particularly interested in research tracing how platform business models, interface standards, behavioural incentives, and governance arrangements embed presumptions about inclusion while distributing risks, responsibilities, and rewards unevenly across regions and populations. These dynamics structure access to citizenship, care, cultural participation, mobility, and economic opportunity along intersecting lines of gender, class, race, dis/ability, citizenship, and geographies, often reinforcing asymmetries between sites of data extraction, sites of platform ownership, and sites of digital labour. 

 By bringing interdisciplinary perspectives into dialogue, the session aims to move debates beyond celebratory narratives of digital democratisation and toward a more grounded understanding of how digital inclusion and exclusion are actively negotiated in practice. Contributions may engage empirical, theoretical, or methodological approaches that highlight both the structural conditions producing digital inequalities and the everyday strategies (Burgess, 2006) through which individuals and communities navigate, resist, or reconfigure them in diverse contexts.

papers.title: Just urban logistics: Tracing the spaces for resisting platform capitalism
Digital emancipation?: Examining the politics of datafied inclusion and the constitutive authority of networked governance
Asymmetries and inequalities in a labor market with increased digital remote working
Unequal Exchange: Platform Power, Serious Games, and Digital Inequalities in Ghana

sessions.title: Social Infrastructure and the Making of Just Places (1)
sessions.description:
This proposed session marks the third consecutive year in which we have advanced a conference theme focused on social infrastructure, reflecting a sustained and growing commitment within geographical scholarship to understanding its central role in addressing social and spatial inequalities. Responding directly to the RGS 2026 conference theme, Geographies of inequalities: towards just places , the session examines how social infrastructure shapes, sustains, and challenges spatial inequalities, and how it contributes to the making of more just places. Social infrastructure, traditionally understood as the spaces, services, institutions, and networks that support social life, plays a crucial role in structuring access to care, education, health, participation, safety, and belonging (Klinenberg, 2018; Latham & Layton, 2019). Its distribution, governance, and everyday use are increasingly uneven, reflecting and reinforcing broader inequalities related to class, race, gender, age, disability, migration status, and place (Marmot, 2015; Landman, 2019; Sezer & Van Melik, 2023). These inequalities are often intensified in contexts of crisis, including pandemics, climate change, austerity, and displacement, where social infrastructure can act both as a buffer against harm and as a site where exclusions are reproduced (Renner et al., 2024).

 At the same time, social infrastructure can function as a critical arena through which claims for social justice are articulated, negotiated, and contested, and through which alternative, more inclusive futures are imagined (Aelbrecht & Stevens, 2019; Klinenberg, 2018). This session invites papers that critically explore how social infrastructure operates across different spatial contexts, including urban, rural, and digital settings and at multiple scales, from the everyday to the institutional and policy level.

 Aligned with the Participatory Geographies Research Group, the session welcomes theoretical, empirical, and methodological contributions that engage reflexively with questions of power, ethics, representation, and responsibility in researching social infrastructure and justice (Pain, 2004; Kindon, Pain & Kesby, 2007). We encourage interdisciplinary perspectives and global case studies that illuminate both the potentials and limits of social infrastructure in advancing social change within and beyond the academy.

 We invite contributions that explore (but are not limited to) the following themes:

 - Social infrastructure as a foundation of spatial justice
- Inequalities in provision, access, and use of social infrastructure
- Social infrastructure and lived experience
- Power relations, governance, and institutional arrangements
- Social infrastructure in times of crisis and transition
- urban, suburban, rural, coastal, and peripheral settings, and at neighbourhood, city-regional, national, and transnational scales.
- Digital and hybrid social infrastructures
- Measuring, mapping and representing inequality
- Ethics, reflexivity, and responsibility in research and practice 

 This session encourages global perspectives and welcomes interdisciplinary contributions combining theoretical insights, empirical research, and case studies. Papers engaging at the intersection of social and cultural geographies and built environment disciplines, including architecture, landscape architecture, planning, and urban design, are particularly encouraged.

 References 

 Not included here.

sessions.title: Energy Transition and Social Justice: Geographies in the Climate Emergency
sessions.description: Amid the global drive toward low-carbon energy transition under the climate emergency, the spatial unevenness of transition processes and their intrinsic entanglement with energy transition justice have emerged as critical concerns. As the inescapable impacts of climate change and energy challenges continue to intensify, it is imperative to infuse the disciplinary positioning of energy geography with new connotations centered on justice and spatiality. Existing studies often treat energy transition and climate justice as disjointed domains, neglecting their inherent geographical interconnections, including but not limited to how spatial disparities in energy infrastructure deployment shape the distribution of transition costs and benefits, and how local geographical contexts mediate the implementation and outcomes of just transition policies under the climate emergency contexts. This session attempts to address this gap by adopting a geographical lens to unpack the spatial dynamics underpinning energy transition justice, offering a nuanced understanding of uneven transition pathways and their justice implications. Such an inquiry is vital for formulating context-specific low-carbon policies and advancing equitable climate governance in an era of climate emergency.

sessions.title: Mapping human-microbial relations (1): Complicating probiotic cultures: Imaginaries, governance, and exclusions
sessions.description:
Human-microbial relations have undergone a profound transformation over the past century. In the Global North, the negative consequences of Pasteurian paradigms of eradication (such as drug resistance, the rise of allergies, autoimmune diseases, or the loss of soil fertility) have prompted a ‘probiotic turn’ in cultural imaginaries that have so far been dominated by ideas of hygiene and surveillance. This trend reframes microorganisms as ‘Old Friends’ essential to human health and ecosystem function, and proposes approaching more positively and harnessing a diverse range of microbial functions and their multispecies entanglements. Processes from industrial agriculture to urban wastewater treatment highlight how our worlds intimately depend on the invisible and free labour of regulated microbial ecologies. Yet, convivial or flourishing socio-microbiomes centred on individual health tend to unfold in affluent contexts supported by antibiotic legacies and risk reproducing exclusionary logics.

 Building upon the understanding of microorganisms as active world building agents that co-create, enable, and constrain different forms of social life, this panel aims to differentiate, pluralise, and expand the changing attitudes, practices, and socio-bio-ecologies that constitute the ‘microbial turn’ in their geographically and historically specific contexts. We ask what happens when (unjust, inequitable) social formations encounter recalcitrant and indeterminate microbial agencies. How are microbes enrolled into human worlds (and vice versa), what kinds of transformations unfold and what possibilities come to light? In particular, we aim to investigate the (unintended, unpredictable) political, social, and ecological implications of dis/encouraging microbial growth, and to map how situated practices with microbes create patchy geographies that variously reflect, reinforce, and resist power imbalances.

 As the first of two linked sessions, 'Complicating Probiotic Cultures' interrogates 'post-Pasteurian' imaginaries across diverse techno-infrastructural and agro-ecological environments. The papers in this session demonstrate how probiotic practices facilitate novel forms of governance in which microbial lives are enrolled to produce value. By centering geographies of exclusion and accumulation, contributors challenge the convivial promise of the 'post-antibiotic' era, revealing the uneven power dynamics that underpin contemporary microbial management.

papers.title: The ‘probiotic turn’ as microbial frontiers of accumulation

sessions.title: More-than-Human Friendships (1)
sessions.description:
The more-than-human has swept geography, the social sciences, and humanities in recent years, having come to encompass life-affirming ways of living with and theorising relationships with and networks of others. In this more-than-human turn, there has been a seemingly endless fascination with all kinds of others and the spaces and places that they, and we, co-produce. From the city to the sea, the familiar to the otherworldly, the more-than-human world has perhaps never been more considered than in contemporary “human” geography. Beyond the boundaries of geography, our moral duties to the non-human world and their rights on and to the Earth have been the concern of politics and philosophy scholars, while creative practitioners have contemplated how best to represent this more-than-human world in art, poetry, and writing.

 Yet, despite this wealth of literature, we believe that there remains considerable scope for exploration, experimentation, and indeed playfulness within this ever-expanding field. Specifically, we are interested in how friendship might be useful, or at least interesting, in understanding more-than-human worlds, lives, deaths, futures, and endings. Friendship itself, although shaping everyday lives, has been relatively under-considered in geographical scholarship and even less so in relation to the more-than-human world. It is our intention with this session to explore how, if at all, friendship might shape or be shaped by the more-than-human relations and worlds.

papers.title: Bouzouki, my research companion
More-than-Human Friendships
Befriending the Otherworlds: exploring portals of consciousness for co-creating intuitive wisdom for harmonious Earth Futures
Imaging friendship: more-than-human intra-actions in new materialist video experiments

sessions.title: Place-based solidarities under strain? Contested local civic spheres in unequal times (1)
sessions.description: There has been a recent return to ‘place-based policy’ from the UK government, exemplified in policies such as ‘Levelling Up’ and ‘Pride in Place’. Such a discourse springs from a recognition of political and resourcing failures in centralised programmes. Alongside economic development, health policy is proposing moves towards ‘neighbourhood’ and ‘community’ health whilst ambitions for net zero are increasingly premised on localised programmes. Such initiatives seek to galvanise a sphere of everyday care and civic action in the service of welfare and sustainable development, whilst also responding to the ‘geographies of discontent’ (McCann 2020) shaped by profound inequalities between regions. This shift to a local focus may offer opportunities for neglected or ‘left behind’ communities. However, inequalities and ongoing conditions of austerity have hollowed out the local and shaped unstable and contested local civic spheres. Whilst there is everyday grassroots ‘experimentation’ happening in communities, this is a highly fragmented arena often involving community, faith-based, voluntary and private sector actors rather than public authorities (Thompson and Lorne 2023). Furthermore, the grievances of localised inequality and neglect are now playing a key role in the rise of far right movements in communities (Santamarina and Ince 2025) as well as populist backlash against other progressive policy such as local climate initiatives (Yazar 2024).  This panel will present research on topics including civic action and imaginaries in local contexts; critical accounts of health initiatives premised on a ‘place-based approach’; models of solidarity and social economic experiments combining social, and climate justice; analysis of far-right organising around the local as well as localised counter-movements

papers.title: (In)visibility, poverty and “helping out”

sessions.title: New Demographic Trends and Regional Development: Dynamics, Consequences, and Policy Responses
sessions.description:
Declining fertility, rising life expectancy, and the resulting population decline and ageing are defining mega-trends of our time. However, scholarship on the regional impacts of these trends remains relatively limited. The paper session aims to initiate a ‘regional turn’, shifting the focus toward regional transformations – across multiple dimensions and across various contexts – that are driven or shaped by the ongoing demographic changes. By providing a collection of regional analyses situated within this context, it highlights the importance of the regional scale for understanding the impacts of the demographic mega-trends shaping our world.

 The central premise is that the global slowdown in population growth has contributed to increasing regional demographic divergence, that is, regions are becoming increasingly different from each other over time in their demographic structures and trajectories. This divergence poses substantial challenges for balanced regional development, given the mutually constraining relationship between demographic dynamics and regional development trajectories. Regional economic performance, for example, is shaped by population size and structure, as reflected in concepts like the demographic dividend, human capital, and consumer city. Likewise, public service and infrastructure provision, often managed at the regional level, has been reported to face growing demand-supply imbalance in ageing regions, potentially contributing to rising social discontent. The profound economic and social impacts of demographic change, in turn, raise the importance of populations and their characteristics in the regional governance agenda, where ‘regional biopolitics’ is widely practised through discourses and policies such as ‘healthy ageing’ and the ‘learning regions’ . Ultimately, the mechanisms of uneven demographic transitions, selective out-migration, and population ageing, among others, intensify regional disparities, compounding the challenges faced by left-behind regions.

 To address the complexities of demographic divergence and regional disparities, we invite theoretically guided empirical research articles that address the following (non-exhaustive) questions and topics:

 - How do demographic trajectories manifest differently at the regional scale? and through what specific mechanisms, such as selective out-migration, varying population ageing, or uneven demographic transitions? does this divergence occur?
- How, and through what channels, does increasing demographic divergence contribute to escalating regional economic disparities?
- How do new demographic trends reshape the regional provision of public services and infrastructure, and to what extent do these shifts influence regional inequalities in welfare and well-being?
- How do, or should, regional governance agendas and policy instruments adapt to the socio-economic consequences of new demographic trends?

sessions.title: The mobility of children and youth in times of socio-ecological transformation: From school streets to cities for children (1)
sessions.description:
The active, independent mobility of children and adolescents has been declining for decades. Journeys primarily made on foot or by bike are increasingly being made by car, characterizing nowadays young people as the “backseat generation”. This has a negative impact on their physical health and cognitive development and limits their use of public space. Overall, environmental burdens and safety issues are increasing with higher car-use among parents and children. To tackle this, recent projects have emphasized young people’s needs in urban and transport planning by initiating, e.g., school or play streets, low-traffic neighbourhoods or other means of street transformation for youth. Taking children and adolescents into account as a planning principle for equitable urban areas and just transport systems is central to a socio-ecological transformation.

 Therefore, the session aims at combining research on children and youth in urban and transport geography, especially emphasizing the influence of urban street transformation on young people’s mobility and use of urban space as well as participation of children and youth towards creating a just and sustainable city.

 The session comprises empirical contributions that draw on sound theoretical reflections from inter- and transdisciplinary perspectives on the mobility of youth, children and their families, addressing e.g.:

 - children and youth in urban planning and street transformation projects (e.g. school streets)
- participation formats and methods for involving children and youth in urban and mobility planning
- promoting active mobility and designing streets or public spaces for children and youth

sessions.title: Environment and development futures - living productively with climate, extraction and biodiversity in Latin America
sessions.description: This panel brings together human geographers who work with scientists (including physical geographers) and community partners in Latin America on interdisciplinary questions around environment and development futures. It picks up on recent interests in ‘hopeful geographies’ to foreground some of the creative ways in which communities have made livelihoods productively in the past in the face of diverse environmental and development challenges and continue to do so in the contemporary period despite the shifting nature of long standing geopolitical, climatic and extractive threats. The panellists will spotlight scenarios and places where this often-invisible heritage of living productively with climate, extraction and biodiversity is being mobilised to imagine productive environmental and development futures at different sites and scales. They will discuss how to envisage a research agenda that can capture these processes and reflect on the challenges of embedding co-production into innovative methodological processes in socially and environmentally just ways.

sessions.title: Towards Just Futures: Negotiating Emotional Geographies of Safety and Place (1)
sessions.description:
For Children and Young People (CYP), justice is not only a legal or abstract concept, but an everyday, emotional and spatial experience; shaping how they move through space, relate to others, and imagine their futures. CYP are often overlooked as active participants in the making of justice (Blaisdell et al., 2021; Kraftl, 2013). Instead, justice is often encountered through adult-designed systems, rules and institutions that regulate their bodies, emotions and possibilities.

 At a time when uncertainty, violence, displacement, conflict, and insecurity are intensifying, CYP’s contributions to geographical debates are overlooked by adult-centric discourses and narratives. Yet, CYP are increasingly expected to live with the consequences of injustice while having limited power to shape responses to it (Khan, 2021; Pain et al., 2010). Within these conditions, justice is deeply entangled with violence and trauma (Pain and Cahill, 2022). Some CYP live with the impacts of violence inflicted upon them, while others are positioned as causing trauma through acts of violence within constrained, unequal and emotionally charged environments (Fraser, 2015). These experiences are not separate, but circulate through the same places, relationships and institutions, shaping how safety, responsibility and accountability are understood and enacted. In schools, streets, community spaces, the home and digital environments, CYP negotiate justice through emotions, as they navigate harm, its consequences and possibilities for repair (Andal, 2023; Marrun and Rodriguez-Campo, 2023).

 CYP occupy a contested role in the future imaginaries of a just world. As a beacon of hope and promise, CYP play a vital role in acting towards social, environmental, political, and economic justice (Paterson-Young et al., 2024). On the other end of the dualism, CYP are often underscored as vulnerable, inexperienced and naïve to the ‘real world’ or even seen as troublemakers and agitators (Schliehe, 2021).
 Session I & II seek to explore how justice is felt through practices of navigating harm, seeking safety, supporting peers, and challenging exclusion. Attending justice as a youth centred emotional geography foregrounds how CYP actively interpret, perform and produce justice in ways that are deeply relational, situated and tied to the places they move through.

papers.title: Relational security and insecurity in public space: Experiences and practices of young asylum seekers and refugees in Leipzig

sessions.title: Rural Spatial Justice and Place-Making from a Planetary Perspective (1)
sessions.description:
Processes of rural place-making are closely connected to questions of inequalities and injustice. Disparities in the distribution of wealth, resources and services between rural and urban areas, as well as between rural places, shape trajectories of place development and the identities attached to places. Processes of place-making in turn shape perceptions of spatial injustice, or the unfair treatment, informing a ‘rural consciousness’ through which rural residents interpret politics (Cramer, 2016). Questions of rural spatial justice are also evident in the variegated capacity of individuals to participate in making rural places – a ‘right to the rural’ paralleling the ‘right to the city’. Moreover, approached as a normative concept, rural spatial justice can inspire proactive and progressive place-making (Woods, 2023).

 This session specifically calls for papers that address these dynamics between rural place-making and spatial justice from a planetary perspective. Following principles outlined in Wang et at’s (2025) call for planetary rural geographies, a planetary perspective highlights the making and remaking of rural places within unequal global relations and associated questions of economic and social justice; the more-than-human composition of place and the questions of multi-species justice embedded in rural places; the justice or injustice of knowledge claims for describing, knowing and representing rural places, from colonial scientific legacies to indigenous thinking; and rural place-making with respect to planetary crises, including issues of environmental justice in the remaking of rural places by climate change and in conflicts over the repurposing of rural land for renewable energy, rewilding or carbon forestry, for example.

 As such, we understand place-making as a relational, contested, and uneven process shaped by multi-scalar forces, including climate change, agro-ecological transitions, infrastructure expansion, extractivism, conservation, migration, and food sovereignty struggles. We are particularly interested in how justice claims emerge across scales, and how rural places are made, unmade, and remade through interactions among human and more-than-human actors. 

 The session aims to foster dialogue between rural studies, critical agrarian studies, political ecology, and more-than-human geographies, advancing a relational and planetary understanding of rural spatial justice.

papers.title: When Fruit Trees Uproot the Grain: Agrarian Transition, Power, and More-Than-Human Place-Making in Rural China
Conservation–extractivism nexus and the more-than-human co-production of planetary rural landscapes in the Llanos, Colombia
Broiler farm planetary geographies: maps, food-feed regimes, metabolic justice
Rewilding rural justice: negotiating planetary and local claims in the Dartmoor landscape

sessions.title: Landing offshore wind: uneven coastal geographies of marine extractivism (1)
sessions.description:
The sea has become a resource frontier for the expansion of new energy technologies, especially offshore wind farms. Offshore wind is considered an important part of the solution to the climate crisis, energy security and as a catalyst for economic growth. Moreover, the oftentimes deceptive notion of offshore wind being “out of sight, out of mind” suggests that placing turbines at the sea would be less controversial and that their deployment does not create as severe conflicts on land as their counterparts onshore (Haggett 2008; 2011). However, even when these extractive activities take place under water or beyond the horizon, they still have multi-faceted impacts on land. Offshore wind farms, as a new form of marine extractivism, require the construction of e.g. onshore substations, pylons with high voltage cables, underground cables, harbour facilities for turbine construction, operation and maintenance activities, and auxiliary services which can all affect and reshape environmental and socio-economic conditions, cultural values ​​and the lived experiences of coastal communities (e.g. Glasson et al. 2022, Schneider & O’Neill 2025, Sornn-Friese et al. 2022). The expansion of offshore wind infrastructures and the emergence of the offshore wind industry have manifested in local and regional, yet geographically uneven, industrial development (e.g. Dawley et al. 2010, 2015; Gibbs & Jensen 2022; MacKinnon et al. 2022) ), thus engendering environmental, distributional and democratic disparities and inequalities. Since impacted communities and beneficiaries rarely overlap, the expansion of offshore wind raises critical questions of spatial and distributional justice and of democratic governance. Increasing protests from coastal communities along with the rapid expansion offshore wind farms vis-a-vis efforts to capture economic opportunities actualizes the need for knowledge about how land-based impacts manifest, where and why economic opportunities emerge, and to what extent industrial activities can be anchored locally. 

 This session seeks to shed critical light on the various manifestations of uneven geographies of expanding offshore wind developments, especially opportunities, challenges and conflicts emerging from land-based activities. We call for papers on this topic, exploring for example:

 - Stakeholder and/or public responses to offshore extractive projects

 - Spatial implications of the offshore wind value chain

 - Environmental and cultural implications and needs for coordinated spatial governance

 - Regional economic competition (e.g. between different harbours and industries) and collaboration on attracting new initiatives

 - Imaginaries and narratives of offshore wind developments

 - The role of institutions in steering industrial development of offshore wind energy

 - The role and fate of local and community identity (e.g. resisting, subverting, co-evolving)

 - The role and fate of existing infrastructures (assets, legacies..)

 - Interplay between industry, government, third sector, residents in shaping more locally appropriate and just offshore wind developments

 - Spatial implications of labour activities of the offshore wind industry

 - Benefit capture, skills development, distribution of job impacts (positive or negative)

 - Marine and/vs terrestrial spatial planning; multi-use and multi-functional landscapes

papers.title: Living the process: Local experiences of Norwegian offshore wind development
affiliations: Norwegian University of Science and Technology

Scalar Regimes and Governance Mismatches in Norwegian Offshore Wind Development

sessions.title: Building food justice through participatory and innovative methods for community food research (1)
sessions.description:
Persistent injustices such as food insecurity, unequal food access, land-use pressures, and diet-related disease continue to shape our food system. Community food can be a space for addressing inequalities and imagining alternative food systems. Whether in the form of urban agriculture, community supported agriculture, food banks, allotments, community meals or other forms, spaces of community food are often sites where inequalities are contested, making them sites of sustained interest from a research perspective.

 Community food organisations are frequently asked to participate in research in various roles. Without clear goals agreed from the start, expectations can be misaligned, leading to uneven outcomes, duplication of research (often driven by funding availability) and research fatigue. Short-term and often niche funding opportunities (and other factors including academic workload, university bureaucracy, third sector reliance on volunteers and their own funding cycles) can also make long-term relationships between academics and external partners difficult to maintain, despite increasing emphasis on the importance of ‘impact’ from the university side.

 Participatory research approaches produce knowledge, but they also redistribute power, centre lived experience, and challenge extractive research relations. This session aims to explore how participatory methods, and other methodological innovations, can make research with community food organisations more just and more democratically grounded. It explores tensions and opportunities in co-producing knowledge with community actors; how scholar-activists navigate power, positionality and ethics; and innovations, particularly in participatory methodologies, for addressing food insecurity, urban food governance; or other related topics.

sessions.title: Pracademic positionality in feminist research.
sessions.description: Positionality has been acknowledged as a fundamental element of feminist approaches within research (Devault, 1990; Parker, 2016; Peak et al, 2024; Bjorkdahl and Mannergren, 2021) and practice (Koleth and Temenos, 2022; Bassam, 2023). As pracademics (practitioners and academics) we have a unique positionality within our research, consistently navigating between the researched and the researcher, to drive the “difference” towards the “action” (Peake and Sharpe, 2025, Pg 122). The power imbalances we have identified within our research are present because of our involvement in other gender equity networks across the planning and architecture professions. This panel explores these imbalances and how we mediate them as feminist pracademics so that participants within our research are not influenced by a power imbalance in the same way that they may have been within traditional patriarchal practice (Didaskalou, 1996; Dutton, 2024; Dupius et al, 2022). Being situated within the communities that we are researching relies on meaningful connection through vulnerability with our participants (Kamarauskaite, 2025) and a consistent challenging of our positionality within the research we are doing.. The panel will explore how, within our pracademia, we can mitigate or acknowledge our positionality and the power imbalance present to ensure a feminist approach to research lin which “lived experiences then are not just things we write about but are what we write from and for.” (Choi, 2021)

sessions.title: Geographies of Language and the Languages of Geography Revisited
sessions.description:
It has been 25 years since the themed section ‘Geographies of Language/ Languages of Geography’ was published in Social and Cultural Geography (Desforges and Jones, 2001). Featuring papers from the RGS-IBG conference in 2000, it brought attention to the relationships between language, geography and identity and “the potential that a more sustained focus on languages may bring to geography” (Desforges and Jones, 2001). Since then, a wealth of scholarship on the Franco, Germanic, and, predominantly, Anglo, dominance of geographical knowledge has emerged (e.g. Desbiens and Ruddick, 2006; Kitchin, 2005; Mattissek and Glasze, 2016; Müller, 2021), considering the effects of the languages in which geographical knowledge is written, and how / where geographical knowledge is taught and circulated. However, geographical scholarship on the spatialities and social practices of language use and their relationships with identity, inclusion and power, have been more limited. 

 Yet, the spatial implications of languages include “the possibility [to] change the way that spaces are ordered” (Valentine, Sporton and Bang Nielsen, 2008), and thinking about language geographically can open new ways to “analyse the processes affecting language ability, use and transmission, as well as the association between such languages and various identities, representations, materialities and practices” (Jones and Lewis, 2019). This research area is only growing in importance due to globalisation and migration increasingly creating multilingual societies. 

 This session aims to bring together scholars working on the geographies of language and/or the languages of geography to take stock and revitalize discussion on the area, consider more recent and emerging scholarship on language within geography, and consider future avenues for geographical research on language. 

 References:

 Desbiens, C., and Ruddick, S. (2006) Speaking of Geography: Language, Power, and the Spaces of Anglo-Saxon ‘Hegemony’, Environment & Planning D: Society & Space, 24(1): 1-8.

 Desforges, L., and Jones, R. (2001) Geographies of Languages/Languages of Geography, Social & Cultural Geography, 2(3): 261-4.

 Jones, R. and Lewis, H. (2019) New Geographies of Language: Language, Culture and Politics in Wales, London: Palgrave Macmillan. 

 Kitchin, R. (2005) Commentary: Disrupting and Destabilizing Anglo-American and English-Language Hegemony in Geography, Social & Cultural Geography, 6(1): 1-15

 Mattissek, A., and Glasze, G. (2016) Discourse Analysis in German-language Human Geography: Integrating Theory and Method, Social & Cultural Geography, 17(1): 39-51.

 Müller, M. (2021) Worlding Geography: From Linguistic Privilege to Decolonial Anywheres, Progress in Human Geography, 45(6): 1440-66.

 Valentine, G., Sporton, D., and Bang Nielsen, K. (2008) Language Use on the Move: Sites of Encounter, Identities and Belonging, Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 33(3): 376-87.

sessions.title: Hormonal Geographies: Tracing the Endocrine Through the Anthropocene (1)
sessions.description:
Session Aim: This session seeks to bring together research on hormones and endocrine-disrupting chemicals to explore how understanding the endocrine system as co-modulated by both internal and environmental factors can inform responses to contemporary forms of hormonal governance and (dis)regulation. 

 In recent years, hormones have emerged as an important theme in the social sciences and humanities for their role in shaping health, reproduction, and gender, with particular attention to the ways hormones are sensed and negotiated in everyday life. Work on contraceptives, fertility, menopause, pregnancy, the uterus, polycystic ovary syndrome, and low testosterone in cismen, alongside critiques of hormonal gatekeeping in trans* communities and non-consensual hormonal interventions on people with intersex conditions, has shown how hormones function as tools of biopolitical governance, enforcing heterosexual reproductive futurity while pathologizing deviations from binary sex and gender norms (Appleton, 2022; Berg and Linander, 2024; Erikainen et al., 2025; Joanes, 2023; Wang, 2024). 

 Meanwhile, attention across multiple research strands has been growing to the consequences of exposure to 'endocrine-disrupting chemicals' - substances largely originating from industrial sources that interfere with the body's hormonal system. As many of these compounds are xenoestrogens (synthetic estrogens), queer and trans* scholars have explored how such exposures might 'queer' sexed and gendered embodiments (Ah-King and Hayward, 2014; Di Chiro, 2010; Zabalegui, 2024), while environmental justice perspectives have highlighted the unequal health burdens of toxicity among those communities living near industrial sites (Shadaan and Murphy, 2020). 

 This session seeks to bring research on hormones and endocrine disruption into conversation to highlight how they co-modulate the endocrine system, both through biochemical processes and as biopolitical regimes that manage exposure, regulate populations, and produce uneven conditions of harm and resistance. This convergence underscores urgent political stakes: in the Anthropocene, industrial, ecological, and socio-political transformations have created conditions where bodies are increasingly disciplined through biomedical intervention, access to gender-affirming care is restricted, while atmospheric exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals remains largely mitigated. At this critical moment, this session brings together diverse perspectives on hormones and endocrine disrupting chemicals to explore pathways towards more just and sustainable endocrine futures. 

 We invite theoretical, empirical, methodological, and ethical contributions focusing on hormones, endocrine disruption, and the endocrine system from feminist, queer, and trans perspectives, as well as from health geography and environmental justice approaches. 

 Potential topics may include, but are not limited to: 

 - The medicalisation and regulation of hormones 
- Gendered and life-course experiences of hormones 
- Hormonal health inequalities and endocrine health 
- Queer and Trans* hormonal experiences and practices 
- Fertility, reproduction, and contraception 
- Embodied and sensory experiences of hormones
- Sexuality and sexual practices 
- Endocrine-disrupting chemicals
- Environmental justice approaches to hormonally active pollution

papers.title: Toxic Geographies of Reproduction: Endocrine Disrupting Pesticides and Reproductive Futures in Chile
The market for (the crisis in) masculinity: testosterone production and markets in the United States and Canada
Infrastructures of access: tracing hormonal pharmaceutical geographies
Endocrine Disruption in Teesside, UK: Towards an Endocrine Ecologies Framework

sessions.title: Navigating the ordinary Anthropocene in the Global  North: local encounters and embodied knowledges
sessions.description:
We live in a world increasingly shaped by environmental and climate change. Events once understood as exceptional – floods, droughts, heatwaves, storms, or extreme cold – are becoming part of a new normal, subtly yet persistently reworking social relations, material environments, and everyday routines. These changes are not only registered through spectacular moments of crisis, but are sensed, felt, and embodied in the mundane practices of daily life.

 This session explores the navigation of the Anthropocene ordinary , with a particular focus on contexts in the Global North. While climatic disruptions have long been central to disaster research – especially in the Global South – the Global North has often been characterized by infrastructures, welfare systems, and political-economic arrangements that buffer risk and sustain assumptions of stability, predictability, and protection. Yet these assumptions are increasingly unsettled. Environmental change is now encountered locally, negotiated in everyday encounters between humans and more-than-human worlds, and made meaningful through situated forms of knowledge, practice, and affect.

 This session foregrounds the intersections of disruption and everydayness, attending to embodied experiences, emotions, and affects, as well as to the collective and individual practices through which people navigate environmental uncertainty. We are particularly interested in how (local) knowledges are continuously negotiated in mundane routines and moments of rupture, giving rise to new ways of living with, explaining, or contesting environmental change. Across contributions, we encourage critical attention to questions of power, intersectional vulnerability, agency, and social justice, including the colonial and racialized dimensions of climate change, and to more-than-human relations, interspecies care, and affective ethics.

papers.title: Farmers’ Local Encounters and Embodied Knowledges of Climate Adaptation in Wales
The trees are to blame – environmental meanings in the aftermath of flooding on the  Ahr River
Storying weather-heritage: textures of the ordinary Anthropocene in the East Anglian  Fens
Living on unstable foundations? – Navigating everyday climate change-induced housing insecurity  in Bordeaux and Rotterdam

sessions.title: Uneven waters: Exploring meanings and approaches to just climate adaptation in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North (1)
sessions.description:
Water risks and their uneven geographies have become a central concern for the planning and development of cities in the context of a global climate emergency and other intersecting crises. As cities confront the challenges associated with managing water extremes (both floods and droughts), climate adaptation efforts increasingly focus on controlling and redefining urban water's changing dynamics and flows. Such efforts are especially visible in ‘urban blue spaces’ —i.e., areas adjacent to salt, fresh, or brackish surface waters– where water-related vulnerabilities, value, and governance intersect to reshape infrastructure and everyday life. Yet the production of urban blue spaces is far from neutral. They result instead from layered histories of investment and neglect, formal and informal practices, and uneven distributions of risks, benefits, and responsibilities. Climate impacts and adaptation interventions can entrench or unsettle these longstanding inequalities, through individual and collective responses enacted by state agencies, private actors, community practices, and ecological processes. Critical urban and adaptation scholarship has begun to expose such uneven outcomes, but more work is needed to further unpack how water is managed in urban blue spaces and its unjust implications, and to reveal alternative, justice-oriented approaches.
 This session will present conceptual, methodological, and empirical contributions that interrogate how justice materializes or fails to materialize in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North. The presentations include comparative, interdisciplinary, and reflexive work that is attentive to power, knowledge asymmetries, and diverse ways of understanding water through the lens or justice.

papers.title: Feasibility, Pilots, and Demonstrations: Promises for ‘New’ Water Infrastructures in South Africa
Blue Promises, Uneven Outcomes: Fixing Rivers, fixing inequalities? Distributive and Procedural Justice in Urban River Restoration in Parisian urban area
Tracing (Post-)Colonial Planning and Urban Blue Justice in Paramaribo, Suriname
“A Bounded Existence”: Scalar and Intersectional Experiences of Uneven Water Governance in Cape Town’s Townships

sessions.title: United Cities, Divided Workers
sessions.description:
Across the globe, cities are being remade through infrastructures of speed, circulation, and digital coordination. Transport megaprojects, platform-mediated services, sensor-based management systems, digital payment infrastructures, and algorithmic governance promise integration, efficiency and urban ‘smartness’. These interventions are frequently framed as progressive - enhancing sustainability, connectivity, and economic opportunity. Yet the apparent unification of cities through infrastructure often produces new forms of labour fragmentation, control, and uneven exposure to risk. As cities become more interconnected, the workers who sustain them – construction workers, delivery drivers, domestic workers, waste workers, and others - face regimes of surveillance, precarity, subcontracting, and algorithmic management – impacting workers’ livelihoods and bargaining power. This is deepening the existing gendered, classed and racialised inequalities, and creating new socio-economic uncertainties.

 In this panel, we ask: how do projects that promise urban integration to generate new divisions in labour? (How) do infrastructural and digital transformations reshape who bears risk, who captures value and whose mobility is enabled or constrained?

 We invite critical contributions that question the political economy of contemporary urban transformation and foreground the labour that makes urban circulation possible. We are particularly interested in work that engages with critical infrastructure studies and labour geography on themes including, but not limited to:

 - Gender, racialisation, and the platformisation of domestic and care work
- Mobile labour, subcontracting chains, and fragmented accountability
- Urban logistics, just-in-time delivery, and the intensification of logistics labour
- Algorithmic management, data extraction, and surveillance in urban work
- Climate transition policies and the uneven redistribution of labour risks
- Critical infrastructure studies and labour governance
- Informal, migrant, and marginalised workers in “smart” cities

sessions.title: Tackling Energy Inequalities through Home Energy Advice and Retrofit (1): Conceptual and analytical tools for understanding place-based approaches (1)
sessions.description:
Decarbonising heat and buildings through energy retrofit remains essential for mitigating climate change and addressing spatially entrenched health, energy and economic inequalities. Emergent 'place-based' or 'neighbourhood' approaches, which integrate fabric, heating and smart technology interventions within spatially-defined programmes, are receiving increased attention in policy and practice in many countries. Often this is based on contentions that place-based approaches can accelerate retrofit delivery by connecting national strategy with local needs, developing local supply chains, fostering community and citizen engagement, and engaging with the financial, social and cultural realities of retrofit in places (Bolton et al, 2023; Macrorie et al, 2024; van Casteren et al, 2024).

 Place-based retrofit represents a departure from piecemeal interventions, embedding energy improvements within broader processes of neighbourhood regeneration and transformation. As such delivery may combine energy measures with wider improvements to green space, transport and public realm and/or incorporate innovative finance mechanisms or community co-design. However, as new ‘imaginaries’ of energy efficient neighbourhoods and places emerge, important questions of power, governance, justice and efficacy also arise (Robinson et al, 2024), including: Who envisions, governs, funds and implements these 'future neighbourhoods'? How do residents from diverse socio-economic, cultural and demographic backgrounds experience, respond to, and engage with such transformations? How democratic and inclusive are such processes? How do place-based approaches mobilise socio-spatial relations to accelerate delivery?

 This session invites papers that critically examine the theoretical foundations, practical implementation and social implications of place-based retrofit approaches, drawing on scholarship in place-making, energy transitions, governance, environmental justice and beyond. The sessions will employ a World Café approach over two sessions, each comprised of four papers and rotating discussion. We are open to a diverse range of abstracts that engage with the spatial and justice aspects of building decarbonisation and welcome submissions from all career-stages and non-academic organisations.

papers.title: Developing a Capability Approach for place based interventions aiming to catalyse adoption of heat pumps

sessions.title: Social Movements, Protests and Anti-tourism Activism
sessions.description:
Abstract 
 This proposed session addresses the conference theme, Geographies of Inequality , by foregrounding the political sociology/geography of tourism and examining how social movements, resident protests and anti-tourism activism are reshaping debates on sustainability, liveability and justice in tourist destinations. We invite contributions that connect political economy drivers with contentious politics, situating anti-tourism mobilisation within broader struggles over redistribution, recognition and decarbonisation. Conceptually, we encourage work that engages with the right to the city, tourism degrowth, mobility justice, platform-mediated short-term rentals, and environmental/climate justice, among other related exigencies. We particularly welcome papers that theorise mechanisms of diffusion and spillover (how claims, tactics and repertoires travel between islands, heritage cities and rural/Indigenous territories), coalition formation (tenants’ unions, neighbourhood associations, precarious workers, environmental NGOs, decolonial and feminist groups), and multi-scalar governance (street–court–ballot–platform arenas; municipal, regional, national and transnational policy venues). We also welcome studies that evaluate policy trajectories (from tourist taxes and cruise caps to short-let moratoria) and assess their distributional and ecological consequences toward anti-tourism sentiment. We hope that the session will move the needle beyond managerial “stakeholder” framings towards politically informed analyses of conflict, agency and institutional change in visitor economies. It will clarify when and how anti-tourism contention transforms governance and markets, and when it is co-opted or contained. 
 Keywords 

 Social movements, sustainable tourism, anti-tourism, mobility justice, tourism degrowth

 References 

 - Gómez-Bruna, D., & Martín-Duque, C. (2025). Understanding tourism as a problem on the public agenda in tourism destinations: consequences, intensity and urgency. International Journal of Tourism Cities, 1–16. https://doi.org/10.1080/20565607.2025.2545240

 - McGehee, N. G., Kline, C., & Knollenberg, W. (2014). Social movements and tourism-related local action. Annals of Tourism Research, 48, 140-155. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.annals.2014.06.004 

 - Milano, C., Novelli, M., & Cheer, J. M. (2019). Overtourism and degrowth: A social movements perspective. Journal of sustainable tourism, 27(12), 1857-1875. https://doi.org/10.1080/09669582.2019.1650054 

 Link to Special Issue 

 Papers included in this session will be invited to submit papers for consideration in a Special Issue of the International Journal of Tourism Cities - https://www.tandfonline.com/journals/rjtc20.

sessions.title: Urban Heat: Thermopolitics in More-Than-Human Cities (1)
sessions.description:
Extreme heat is rapidly emerging as one of the twenty-first century’s most pressing urban challenges, with profound implications for health, livelihoods, infrastructures, and everyday life. Far from affecting all urban inhabitants equally, urban heat intersects with entrenched inequalities in housing quality, energy access, infrastructural maintenance, and environmental conditions. As temperatures rise, the capacities of different urban populations to cope with, survive, and recover from heat differ starkly. Many policy and scholarly responses frame urban heat as a technical, environmental, or public health problem, while tending to overlook the highly political relations through which thermal vulnerability is produced, or indeed how power and governance operate through thermal relations.

 This panel seeks to develop a relational, more-than-human approach to urban thermopolitics. Where an emerging body of geographical work on urban heat has begun to acknowledge the importance of infrastructures and urban form in generating and distributing thermal discomfort, there has been only limited engagement with the more-than-human politics of heat. Connecting insights from more-than-human geographies, urban political ecology, and STS, the panel seeks to extend human-centred accounts of thermal inequalities by including not only infrastructures, technologies, and other forms of inanimate matter, but also sentient, living other-than-human beings. From energy grids and building materials to plants, animals, and microbes, we seek to understand the more-than-human relations that shape and are shaped by urban heat and its attendant inequalities.

 The panel invites contributions that examine how uneven thermal experiences and vulnerabilities emerge through interactions among people, infrastructures, technologies, animals, plants, and other-than-human actants, and how these relations shape who and what is rendered vulnerable, protected, or expendable in overheated cities. Empirically, we welcome papers that engage with a range of thematic concerns across diverse urban contexts; these could include addressing, for instance, one or more of the following questions: 

 - How are thermal inequalities produced through more-than-human relations in urban environments? How do interactions between non-human elements—such as buildings, materials, energy systems, vegetation, animals, and atmospheric conditions—and human bodies shape urban thermal environments, and how do these interactions unevenly distribute heat exposure, vulnerability, and comfort?
- What everyday more-than-human caring practices emerge in overheated cities? How do residents, community organizations, and informal networks negotiate heat through situated practices of care, repair, and adaptation, and how do these practices mobilize non-human actors such as materials, plants, and technologies?
- How is extreme heat framed in urban planning and policy? How do dominant approaches conceptualise heat as a risk, resource, or technical problem, and what human or other-than-human forms of agency, knowledge, and responsibility are foregrounded (or conversely, obscured) in these framings?
- Which alternative methodologies enable the study and advancement of thermal justice? Which innovative methods can be used to study extreme heat as a relational, more-than-human urban process, and how do these approaches contest dominant ways of knowing heat while enabling alternative imaginaries and practices of thermal justice?
