sessions.title: Contested depths: Subsurface geographies of the energy transition (1)
sessions.description:
The subsurface is an increasingly contested terrain. From geothermal energy extraction to carbon storage, heat networks to critical mineral mining, competing claims over what lies beneath our feet and impacts on the landscapes, heritage sites and natural environments above raise fundamental questions of access, ownership, and environmental justice. 

 Subsurface governance structures can reflect and reproduce existing power asymmetries. Property rights frameworks may privilege surface landowners or corporate extractors whilst marginalising communities who bear environmental risks. Regulatory regimes designed for fossil capitalism struggle to accommodate renewable subsurface uses like geothermal energy or seasonal thermal storage. Routine planning and regulatory decisions can foreclose future subsurface options before they are ever considered, locking in incompatible surface development and uneven transition pathways. Indigenous communities worldwide contest colonial appropriation of subsurface resources and assert sovereignty over traditional territories extending below ground.

 Whilst energy transitions extend across horizontal and vertical space, subsurface resources are often rendered invisible within planning systems, regulatory frameworks and public debate, even as they become central to decarbonisation strategies. This mismatch between an emerging net-zero subterranean energy regime and largely surface-oriented governance arrangements creates new sites of conflict, exclusion and lock-in.

 This session examines how subsurface allocation, regulation and contestation intersect with spatial inequalities across multiple dimensions: distributional (who benefits and who bears costs), procedural (who participates in governance), epistemic (whose knowledge counts) and scalar (coordination across regulatory jurisdictions). We're particularly interested in cases where governance fragmentation between surface and subsurface systems creates barriers to just energy transitions.

 We welcome theoretical, empirical and practitioner perspectives which explore how subsurface geographies connect to questions of spatial inequality, environmental justice and pathways toward more just and equitable energy and resource transitions, with particular attention to how planning, policy and governance arrangements shape what subsurface energy transition futures become possible in practice.

papers.title: More-than-human geographies of (sub-)terranean transformation: Contested hydropolitical spaces of contamination in and around Bitterfeld-Wolfen, Eastern Germany
affiliations: Just Transition Centre, Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg

sessions.title: Geographies of Concealment: Temporary Urban Barriers of Masking, Covering, and Hiding
sessions.description:
To conceal: not allow to be seen; hide. 

 This panel focuses on barriers as affective infrastructural tools (Bosworth, 2023; Henderson, 2008) that physically, visually, and behaviorally establish a temporary relation of masking, covering, or hiding space in the city. Through varying materialities, opacities, and scales, barriers impose a vertical limit that redirects movement, interrupts sightlines, and reshapes everyday spatial politics. 

 Temporary barriers have their own social functions (Brighenti 2019). Some are heavy and overtly securitised, such as Iraq’s concrete post-blast barriers (Murrani, 2016), Beirut’s Walls of Shame (Ghazal, 2024), or Turkey’s protest barricades (Ertür, 2016). Others are lighter, yet operate through visual forms of concealment and display, such as construction hoardings (Székely, 2019), disaster fencing, or scaffold nets. Across these forms, such structures generate what this panel terms a “geography of concealment”. They obscure political tensions, suspend access, and conceal future uncertainties while simultaneously regulating perception: determining what may be seen, remembered, and contested. These same barriers are also appropriated to create counter-visualities (Mirzoeff 2011), thereby subverting power dynamics in everyday urban spaces. 

 Across contexts shaped by crisis, securitisation, and speculative development (Jones, Robinson & Turner, 2012), temporary barriers increasingly operate as management devices of physical and visual concealment, or architectures of hiding (Abughannam et al., 2024). Concealment as such is not a neutral act but a political one that mediates power dynamics and contributes to the production of urban affects of fear, anticipation, and secrecy, as well as branding and aspirational images of urban development. This panel asks: 

 - In what ways do barriers function as tools for concealment, protection, or spatial control?
- How are barriers appropriated, contested, or reconfigured by residents, activists, or artists?
- How do hoardings and similar devices contribute to shaping urban imaginaries and projected futures?
- What is the meaning of “temporary” in these contexts — as a short-term condition, an ongoing state, or a strategic designation?

 The panel invites contributions from researchers, artists, architects, and policymakers to examine the role of concealment in shaping urban visual cultures, atmospheres, and politics. We welcome contributions from disciplines such as urban and legal geography; cultural policy; architecture; sociology; urban planning and design; and communication, media, and semiotics studies.

 Possible themes for 15-20min presentations include:

 - Concealment as a form of spatial and visual governance
- Barricading, fencing, and boarding up as strategies of securitisation
- Materials of concealment: plywood, railing, fencing, netting, vinyl, and concrete
- Construction screens, building wraps, and scaffolding nets as media surfaces
- Protest, occupation, and the tactical reworking of barriers 
- Hoardings, branding, and speculative urban futures 
- Concealment and heritage destruction

sessions.title: Uneven and Contested Geographies of Data Centers (1)
sessions.description:
In recent years, data centres have become an increasingly common subject of public and political debate (Edwards, 2024). The focus and criticism in these debates are usually directed at the massive consumption of natural resources (Brodie, 2024; Hogan, 2015), their effect on local communities (Bridges, 2024), and the concentration of geopolitical power – especially with regards to large data centers that are currently being planned or built in the context of the hype surrounding artificial intelligence. As essential infrastructure for living in our increasingly datafied worlds and for organising digital capitalism, data centres also raise a variety of geographical questions and problems. These range from the conflict-ridden embedding of data centres in local environments (Rone, 2024), to their role in larger infrastructure constellations (Atkins, 2021; Velkova, 2016), urban settings, and geopolitical issues such as digital sovereignty, digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019) and data localisation (Rosa, 2022). This session brings together conceptual and empirical contributions to critical data centre studies and research on the geographies of digital infrastructures. Against this backdrop, our session brings together contributions from researchers in digital geography, political geography, urban studies, political ecology and science and technology studies, examining the socio-technical imaginations, material effects and geopolitical and infrastructural embeddings of data centres. The papers engage with topics such as data centers in the Global South / Global Majority regions, contested infrastructuralization of data centers in rural and urban areas, the embedding of data centers in geopolitical strategies, programs, and conflicts, sociotechnical imaginations of AI data centers and social movements mobilizing against the data centers.

 Literature Atkins, E. (2021). Tracing the ‘cloud’: Emergent political geographies of global data centres. Political Geography, 86.

 Brodie, P. (2024). Smarter, greener extractivism: Digital infrastructures and the harnessing of new resources. Information, Communication & Society, 1–20.

 Bridges, L. E. (2024). Competing digital capacities: Between state-led digital governance and local data center tradeoffs. Information, Communication & Society, 27(10).

sessions.title: Towards just temporalities
sessions.description:
This asynchronous panel emerges from our shared reflection that spatial justice cannot be achieved without our sustained attention to time. Unequal temporal regimes that prioritise productivity and efficiency in both land and bodies function as central mechanisms through which spatial inequities are produced, normalised, and maintained (Sharma, 2015). These temporal structures actively produce and organise space, shaping whose bodies can dwell, work, and participate. Building on this reflection, this panel asks how geographers might think with embodied queer, crip, and feminist methodologies to organise spaces for participation and knowledge exchange that move beyond extractive and resource-based temporalities.

 Drawing on recent geographical engagements with crip temporalities (Evans et al., 2025; Boyd, 2025; Budworth, 2025; Senanayake, 2022), we examine how productivity and efficiency (Gregg, 2018; Chapman, 2023) structure institutional spaces (e.g. conferences) while also underpinning broader landscapes of public space, mobility, and access. Conceptually, we engage with notions such as “un-time” (Han, 2018); “crip time” (Kafer, 2013); “slow scholarship” (Mountz et al, 2015); “rest as resistance” (Hersey, 2022), ‘unwellness” (Khuc, 2024), “stop-time as crip time” (Dokumaci, 2018), and “strange-ing time” (Bradley, 2025) to understand how alternative temporalities can inform debates and practices of academic participation and inclusion. Methodologically, we follow feminist and queer scholarship that treats method as performative to explore how to enact just temporalities (Bjork, C., & Buhre, 2021) within the conference itself, experimenting with layered and differently paced forms of knowledge exchange. As such, we position the asynchronous format of the panel not only as an accessibility accommodation but as a methodological intervention to trouble dominant academic temporal expectations of immediacy, speed, and presence-as-value.

 Bringing together perspectives from queer and crip ecologies, critical feminist geographies, disability studies, and creative and design-based approaches, we open up a series of questions: 

 - How do extractive temporalities shape who can participate in spaces of knowledge production, academic life, and community organising? 
- Whose labour sustains dominant spatio-temporal arrangements of spaces of knowledge production and community organising?
- When and why did productivity and efficiency become dominant organising logics of modern life, and how have they been used to shape notions of disability and illness?
- What are non-normative temporalities alternatives, and who has access to them? Who has not? Who can “slow down” (Fung, 2025)?
- How might crip time and just temporalities extend across research, teaching, and other forms of academic labour? 
- How might creative practices make these temporal norms visible and open them to revision?

sessions.title: Everyday Practices and Public Life in Urban China (2)
sessions.description:
Cities in China are in the midst of a profound, ongoing transition. Intertwined with macroeconomic and demographic shifts, this transition is grounded in changing urban landscapes and deeply felt in the rhythms of urban life. It is reshaping people's aspirations—regarding work, family, gender and ageing—also their everyday practices and embodied experiences, from evolving cultures of consumption and leisure to new ways of spending time, seeking connection, and building relationships in the city. In this sense, public space provides a compelling site for examining everyday urban life under these conditions, where multi-layered changes, challenges, encounters, and coexistence are juxtaposed, negotiated, reconfigured and lived.

 This session seeks to open a conversation about how to study, think, and write about Urban China through the lens of everyday practices and public life. It brings together interdisciplinary and empirically grounded research that foregrounds ordinary, mundane, and often overlooked dimensions of everyday life through which individuals navigate and negotiate urban change. By centring these lived experiences, the session demonstrates how close attention to everyday life generates conceptual insights and opens up methodological possibilities for urban geography.

sessions.title: Geographies of inequalities and public policy for urban vulnerable populations in Latin America (1)
sessions.description:
The lives of vulnerable populations in Latin American cities are shaped by deeply rooted social, economic and political inequalities. This session seeks to advance a collaborative and policy-oriented conversation about how public policy can enable more just urban environments for those experiencing vulnerability and living with disadvantages. Building on scholarship about urban informality, everyday resilience and (creative) responses to marginalisation (Fundación IDEA, 2017; Fernández et al., 2023), we welcome empirical and theoretical contributions that examine how public policies actively shape, intensify or mitigate urban vulnerability, and how vulnerable populations respond to, negotiate, comply with or contest these policy interventions in their everyday lives. We also welcome work that critically examines the limits of existing policy frameworks (e.g., social protection, housing, climate resilience, informal economy) and explores the institutional, financial, and bureaucratic barriers and enablers to effective and actionable policy change. 

 This session builds on the earlier “Creative Vulnerabilities in Latin America” panel and workshop (RGS-IBG 2025) and seeks to move the debate further into public-policy grounds, bringing together theory and practice to reflect on policy design, implementation and lived outcomes.

sessions.title: Critical Muslim Geographies: Trajectories and Research Pathways (2)
sessions.description:
Disquiet with the dominant framing of integration and difference in the existing body of work on the geographies of Muslims has, in recent years, led to emerging scholarly debates on Critical Muslim Geographies and related studies (Allouache, 2023; Fernandez, 2025, Sidaway, 2022). This call for a panel session invites pithy contributions and reflections that consider the prospects for advancing Critical Muslim Geographies and engage with the insights, challenges, thresholds, and intellectual solidarities to shape manifestos for this developing field (Sidaway, 2023). While there is a breadth of literature on Muslims in geography, we call to expand the depth and range of theoretical work (not least from within Islamic scholarship), as well empirical sites and experiences that inform Muslim geographies, thereby transcending thematic areas of focus that too often simply render “Muslim” as an adjective of diversity across space (Allouache, 2023). We share the ambition that being able to think of the world from “perspectives of the mosque from the inside looking out” (Ahmed, 2018, p. 206), rather than from the outside looking in, has potential to change the treatment of Islam and Muslims in geography.

 Panelists will recharge and move beyond established decolonial geographies by forging connections with adjacent literatures and other intellectual traditions. The panel discussion may be theoretical, historically oriented, methodological, or empirically grounded, and will contribute to advancing Critical Muslim geographies by engaging the following vectors:

 - Critical geography and Critical Muslim geography and geographers (capital, class, coloniality, difference, discourse, power, and racialisation, and the uneven and oft-contested production of landscapes, places, and spaces)
- Securityscapes, surveillance, control, and biopolitics
- Geographies of AI, STS, technopolitics, and Islam
- Geographies of Islamophobia; wider geographical analysis of “everyday” (Najib, 2021), historical and political Islamophobias
- Labour; Islam and the city, the politics of homes, work, workplaces, sites of worship, umrah and haj
- Muslim environmentalisms and political ecologies
- Histories and development of geographical thought minding and thinking with traditions of Islamic knowledge and learning
- Decolonial pathways in the interface between geography, Islam and area studies
- Research methodologies, ethics and ways of fully realising Critical Muslim geographies’ potential (Intellectual and research community-building)

 The session is co-sponsored by the History and Philosophy of Geography Research Group (HPGRG), the Race, Culture and Equality Working Group (RACE), and the Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa Research Group (MENA-GEOG).

 ***

 REFERENCES 

 Ahmed, A.-A. (2018). The Other Ethical Approval: The Importance of Being “Islamic”. Fieldwork in Religion , 12 (2), 204-222.

 Allouache, Y. A. (2023). Muslim geographies, positionality, and ways of knowing migration. Area , 55 (3), 381-389.

 Allouache, Y. A. (2024). Makkah's hinterworlds: Jeddah, the Indian Ocean Hajj, and Southeast Asia. Singapore Journal of Tropical Geography , 45 (2). 403-412.

 Fernandez, S. (2025) Networks of securitisation in the academy: The role of friendship, solidarity and radical geographies. Area , 00, e70051.

 Najib, K. (2021). Spatialized Islamophobia . Routledge.

 Sidaway, J. D. (2022). Beyond the decolonial: Critical Muslim geographies. Dialogues in Human Geography , 13 (3), 337-358.

 Sidaway, J. D. (2023). A manifesto for critical Muslim geographies. Dialogues in Human Geography , 13 (3), 387-391.

sessions.title: Urban Creative Clusters and Upscaling of Socio-Spatial Exclusion in the Global South (2)
sessions.description:
The Global South is undergoing a cultural resurgence in a contradictory frame of both decolonisation and recolonisation. Start-up hubs, heritage precincts, film cities, and design parks based on regional and national cultural themes are promoted as creative clusters for economic growth, innovation and global competitiveness, and as symbolic representations of cultural pride. Modernist projects like Dashanzi Art District of Beijing (Yin, 2014), Porto Maravilha (Rio de Janeiro) (Broudehoux,, 2017), Seongsu-dong and Seoul's creative neighbourhoods of South Korea (Chang, 2023), Medellín’s social urbanism and cultural infrastructure of Colombia (Guerra, 2014), and Marina Bay of Singapore (Micheli, 2023) are also known to have led to adverse and exclusionary development. Such initiatives are linked to national urban policy frameworks (Roy, 2009). In many countries, in addition to focused programmes for heritage conservation, redevelopment and place-based regeneration, a ‘retrofitting’ framework under the Smart City infrastructure modernisation mission is used to this end. However, critical scholarship indicates that such an attempt to reconfigure culture and sites through the imagination of the past for reproducing historical social memories can also yield uneven socio-economic outcomes (Molho, 2020). The demolition and displacement of informal settlements and shops, the sanitisation of places from the poor and ‘cultural others’, and ‘selective’ heritage conservation and promotion are frequently observed across Africa, Eastern and South Asia, and Latin America. Smith (2006) calls this ‘authorised heritage discourse’, implying the promotion of official narratives on what should count as heritage (Appadurai, 1996). Creative clusters, as such, may reinforce inequalities based on class, caste, gender, and ethnicity rather than address them (El-Husseiny, 2020; Peck, 2005; Pratt, 2011). 

 This session invites papers based on empirical case studies, comparative studies (South and North, or South-South) and conceptual contributions on how creative cluster projects operate within countries of the Global South. In other words, the papers should foreground the urban realities of the Global South and should address, though not limited to, any of the questions of (a) How do urban planning and policy frameworks (like smart city) shape the heritage and creative clusters and urban spaces? (b) How does the development of creative clusters lead to demolitions, displacements, gentrification, and socio-spatial exclusions? (C) How do social and cultural belongings, and informality, negotiate their access to the creative clusters and their economies? (d) What forms of alternative policies and planning can help in socio-economically just and inclusive creative urban development? 

 Papers focusing on metropolitan cities, heritage towns/cities, and small and secondary cities are especially encouraged. 

 Keywords : Creative cluster, heritage, urban policies, exclusion, Global South

papers.title: Urban Creative Clusters and Gendered Representation: Advertising, Empowerment and Socio-Spatial Exclusion in Kolkata

sessions.title: What does Gender Equality, Disability and Social Inclusion mean for a just energy transition? An exploration from feminist and decolonial perspectives
sessions.description:
Current policy thinking acknowledges that any efforts to advance a just energy transition must incorporate Gender Equality, Disability, and Social Inclusion (GEDSI) concerns. Empirical evidence, however, shows a persistent gender and inclusion gap in the energy transition. This gap manifests in the limited participation of women and gender non-conforming people in the sustainable energy labour force, as well as in the limited knowledge of how gender relations and intersecting forms of social discrimination (such as racism or ableism) reproduce energy injustices in the energy transition. 

 Feminist research provides practical insights to deliver a just, sustainable, and inclusive energy transition, yet it also calls to challenge the very principles that inform policy efforts in the energy sector. Many energy projects that incorporate GEDSI concerns, for example through gender-disaggregated data collection or holding single-sex learning sessions, rarely tackle the root causes of discrimination and social inequality. Many projects focus on differences between men and women without questioning the homogeneous, universal categories used to characterise diverse groups and complex experiences of power relations and exclusion. Over-simplifying the relationships between gender relations, discrimination, and access to energy resources leads to decontextualised, inappropriate actions (such as when cookstove improvement programmes make inaccurate assumptions about cooking practices and fuel choices). Such generalisations portray women as passive victims or virtuous stewards in ways that increase their responsibility for delivering collective action without the corresponding rewards (such as when biogas-cooking programmes seek to ‘empower’ women but inadvertently result in additional domestic labour). 

 In this session we interrogate the components of a GEDSI-transformative approach for a just energy transition, with reference to experiences in Ethiopia, Malawi, Mozambique and Tanzania. The project identifies three areas of action: implementation actions that examine GEDSI barriers in practice, processes of institutional change, and capacity building programmes that tackle directly the gender gap through affirmative action.

papers.title: Whose Experiences Matter? Gender, Equality, Disability and Social Inclusion in Energy Curricula
Just energy transitions and the need for inclusive energy access innovation ecosystems
Understanding GEDSI barriers in accessing energy and engineering education for delivering a Just Energy Transition: Insights from Ethiopia, Malawi, Mozambique, and Tanzania
Whose energy transition? Mapping electric cooking through a GEDSI lens in Malawi, Mozambique, Tanzania and Ethiopia
Mapping Power: An Institutional Ethnography Framework for Advancing Gender Equality, Disability, and Social Inclusion in Energy Transitions

sessions.title: Hormonal Geographies: Tracing the Endocrine Through the Anthropocene (2)
sessions.description:
Session Aim: This session seeks to bring together research on hormones and endocrine-disrupting chemicals to explore how understanding the endocrine system as co-modulated by both internal and environmental factors can inform responses to contemporary forms of hormonal governance and (dis)regulation. 

 In recent years, hormones have emerged as an important theme in the social sciences and humanities for their role in shaping health, reproduction, and gender, with particular attention to the ways hormones are sensed and negotiated in everyday life. Work on contraceptives, fertility, menopause, pregnancy, the uterus, polycystic ovary syndrome, and low testosterone in cismen, alongside critiques of hormonal gatekeeping in trans* communities and non-consensual hormonal interventions on people with intersex conditions, has shown how hormones function as tools of biopolitical governance, enforcing heterosexual reproductive futurity while pathologizing deviations from binary sex and gender norms (Appleton, 2022; Berg and Linander, 2024; Erikainen et al., 2025; Joanes, 2023; Wang, 2024). 

 Meanwhile, attention across multiple research strands has been growing to the consequences of exposure to 'endocrine-disrupting chemicals' - substances largely originating from industrial sources that interfere with the body's hormonal system. As many of these compounds are xenoestrogens (synthetic estrogens), queer and trans* scholars have explored how such exposures might 'queer' sexed and gendered embodiments (Ah-King and Hayward, 2014; Di Chiro, 2010; Zabalegui, 2024), while environmental justice perspectives have highlighted the unequal health burdens of toxicity among those communities living near industrial sites (Shadaan and Murphy, 2020). 

 This session seeks to bring research on hormones and endocrine disruption into conversation to highlight how they co-modulate the endocrine system, both through biochemical processes and as biopolitical regimes that manage exposure, regulate populations, and produce uneven conditions of harm and resistance. This convergence underscores urgent political stakes: in the Anthropocene, industrial, ecological, and socio-political transformations have created conditions where bodies are increasingly disciplined through biomedical intervention, access to gender-affirming care is restricted, while atmospheric exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals remains largely mitigated. At this critical moment, this session brings together diverse perspectives on hormones and endocrine disrupting chemicals to explore pathways towards more just and sustainable endocrine futures. 

 We invite theoretical, empirical, methodological, and ethical contributions focusing on hormones, endocrine disruption, and the endocrine system from feminist, queer, and trans perspectives, as well as from health geography and environmental justice approaches. 

 Potential topics may include, but are not limited to: 

 - The medicalisation and regulation of hormones 
- Gendered and life-course experiences of hormones 
- Hormonal health inequalities and endocrine health 
- Queer and Trans* hormonal experiences and practices 
- Fertility, reproduction, and contraception 
- Embodied and sensory experiences of hormones
- Sexuality and sexual practices 
- Endocrine-disrupting chemicals
- Environmental justice approaches to hormonally active pollution

papers.title: Depletion, deficit and hormonal possibilities: A feminist geography of menopause
Endocrine landscapes: Chemical mattering of body imaginaries in trans and queer artistic work
Hormones as a Biopolitical Governance Tool: A lived mid-2020s trans experience

sessions.title: Social Infrastructure and the Making of Just Places (2)
sessions.description:
This proposed session marks the third consecutive year in which we have advanced a conference theme focused on social infrastructure, reflecting a sustained and growing commitment within geographical scholarship to understanding its central role in addressing social and spatial inequalities. Responding directly to the RGS 2026 conference theme, Geographies of inequalities: towards just places , the session examines how social infrastructure shapes, sustains, and challenges spatial inequalities, and how it contributes to the making of more just places. Social infrastructure, traditionally understood as the spaces, services, institutions, and networks that support social life, plays a crucial role in structuring access to care, education, health, participation, safety, and belonging (Klinenberg, 2018; Latham & Layton, 2019). Its distribution, governance, and everyday use are increasingly uneven, reflecting and reinforcing broader inequalities related to class, race, gender, age, disability, migration status, and place (Marmot, 2015; Landman, 2019; Sezer & Van Melik, 2023). These inequalities are often intensified in contexts of crisis, including pandemics, climate change, austerity, and displacement, where social infrastructure can act both as a buffer against harm and as a site where exclusions are reproduced (Renner et al., 2024).

 At the same time, social infrastructure can function as a critical arena through which claims for social justice are articulated, negotiated, and contested, and through which alternative, more inclusive futures are imagined (Aelbrecht & Stevens, 2019; Klinenberg, 2018). This session invites papers that critically explore how social infrastructure operates across different spatial contexts, including urban, rural, and digital settings and at multiple scales, from the everyday to the institutional and policy level.

 Aligned with the Participatory Geographies Research Group, the session welcomes theoretical, empirical, and methodological contributions that engage reflexively with questions of power, ethics, representation, and responsibility in researching social infrastructure and justice (Pain, 2004; Kindon, Pain & Kesby, 2007). We encourage interdisciplinary perspectives and global case studies that illuminate both the potentials and limits of social infrastructure in advancing social change within and beyond the academy.

 We invite contributions that explore (but are not limited to) the following themes:

 - Social infrastructure as a foundation of spatial justice
- Inequalities in provision, access, and use of social infrastructure
- Social infrastructure and lived experience
- Power relations, governance, and institutional arrangements
- Social infrastructure in times of crisis and transition
- urban, suburban, rural, coastal, and peripheral settings, and at neighbourhood, city-regional, national, and transnational scales.
- Digital and hybrid social infrastructures
- Measuring, mapping and representing inequality
- Ethics, reflexivity, and responsibility in research and practice 

 This session encourages global perspectives and welcomes interdisciplinary contributions combining theoretical insights, empirical research, and case studies. Papers engaging at the intersection of social and cultural geographies and built environment disciplines, including architecture, landscape architecture, planning, and urban design, are particularly encouraged.

 References 
 References not included here.

papers.title: Institutionalizing Social Infrastructure: labour, trust and care in a youth support organization

sessions.title: Geography in the World: Toward Latin American Dialogues
sessions.description:
This roundtable engages with Geography in the World Part 4 , a themed intervention from Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers (with IBG) that seeks to deepen dialogue with geographical knowledge produced beyond the Anglophone core—specifically in Latin America—while being frank about the practical and institutional obstacles that shape such encounters. The session is designed as a collective conversation about what it takes to move beyond “citational inclusion” toward sustained practices of reading, translation (linguistic and epistemic), and mutual recognition.

 We begin from a shared diagnosis: Latin American geographical scholarship remains structurally under-visible in many “international” publishing circuits, even as evaluation regimes within and beyond the region increasingly reward publication in English-language, commercial-indexed venues. These pressures intensify inequalities, distort research agendas, and reproduce the coloniality of knowledge production. At the same time, Latin America offers distinctive lessons—through its cooperative open-access infrastructures, its debates on scientific sovereignty, and its long traditions of politically engaged geography—about how disciplinary worlds might be reshaped.

 The roundtable will discuss practical strategies and collective responsibilities: editorial policies, peer review practices, translation infrastructures, mentoring and writing workshops, cross-journal collaborations, and the limits imposed by publisher logics and national assessment systems. The aim is to generate actionable ideas for building more plural, dialogical, and ethically grounded circuits of geographical knowledge—within and beyond Latin America—under the pressures of the current conjuncture.

 Core questions to frame the discussion 

 - What would it mean to reshape (not just “internationalise”) the spaces where geography is validated and circulated?
- Which interventions are institutional (journals, societies, universities) vs individual (authors, reviewers), and how do we rebalance the burden?
- How can we build translation practices that are ethical, funded, and durable, rather than ad hoc and extractive?
- What can Anglophone geographers learn from Latin American infrastructures (SciELO/Redalyc/Latindex; open access traditions) without romanticising them?

sessions.title: Counting and Tracing Inequality
sessions.description:
Inequalities are not only social and economic realities but distinctly spatial phenomena embedded in territories, infrastructures, and lived environments. Quantitative methods including spatial statistics, GIS-based modeling, inequality indices, accessibility analysis, regression techniques, and geospatial big data analytics enable researchers to move beyond descriptive narratives toward measurable, comparable, and policy-relevant insights. By quantifying disparities in access to services, environmental exposure, mobility, health outcomes, and socio-economic opportunity, these approaches help reveal hidden patterns of exclusion and concentration of advantage across scales. This session critically engage with the core question how innovative quantitative spatial analysis can support justice-oriented planning and decision-making process. It emphasizes methodological rigor alongside ethical and inclusive interpretation, ensuring that numerical models are socially grounded and context-sensitive. Contributors are invited to present empirical case studies, methodological innovations, and applied research that use quantitative tools to diagnose Spatial Inequality, evaluate interventions, and guide equitable resource allocation across urban, rural, and regional contexts. Work integrating GIS with statistical application, network analysis, composite index construction, and spatial decision-support systems is particularly welcome. 

 The session welcomes contributions from the post graduate students particularly and also researchers addressing Urban–Rural Divide, Uneven Development Patterns, Participatory Mapping and Community Spatial Knowledge towards equitable planning, Hazard and risk mapping. The expected outcome is to build a stronger bridge for integrating Qualitative Knowledge also includes GIS approach with Spatial Modeling and measurement and justice where quantitative evidence does not merely map disparities but actively informs strategies for creating more inclusive, balanced, and just places.

sessions.title: Map Room Conversations (1): Vietnam and the Visualisations of War
sessions.description: Maps establish conversations between spaces, bringing into dialogue locations, symbols, meaning, and people. The relation between mapping as practice and maps as objects has long been the subject of theoretical and methodological debate within and beyond the discipline of geography. In the sessions below we use the RGS-IBG map collection to continue this debate about maps and mapping through two types of conversation (which build on the first sessions of this type at the 2024 conference, see https://rgs-ibg.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/toc/10.1111/(ISSN)1475-4762.map-room-conv). The first is between two researchers who use maps from the collection to explore a shared interest from different perspectives. The second conversation will be between the researchers, who will present some of the maps they work with, and the participants in these interactive sessions. We invite you to take this opportunity for close encounters and conversations with the Society’s map collection.

sessions.title: International Journal of Urban and Regional Research Lecture: Endurance, Repair, Abolition: Re-thinking the Liveable City
sessions.description:
International Journal of Urban and Regional Research Lecture 

 Endurance, Repair, Abolition: Re-thinking the Liveable City Alex Vasudevan, University of Oxford 

 Discussants: Michele Lancione, Politecnico di Torino, and T atiana Thieme, UCL 

 Lecture abstract At the heart of this paper are a series of programmatic reflections on the possibilities of collective city life in our current intensifying emergency. How, the paper asks, can we assemble and sustain a more just urbanism in a time of ecological crisis, planetary-scale dispossession, rising revanchist populisms and hyper-militarised violence? Building on the author’s longstanding research on the spatial politics of urban squatting, the paper seeks to develop a more expansive framework for re-thinking the urban political across different sites and scales. More specifically, it sets out three speculative frames of reference that mark out the coordinates of a renewed approach to inhabiting the city otherwise. First, the paper revisits the modest methods of sheltering, endurance and survival adopted by squatters and the precarious political horizons that they summon into being. Second, it examines the practices of squatters as reparative acts of world-making that re-assemble the city as a space of care and experimentation. Finally, the paper explores the relationship between squatting and an abolitionist politics of resistance that seeks to challenge a global carceral order and the violent modes of banishment, dispossession, and expulsion it ultimately depends on. At stake here, the paper concludes, are important normative questions about the possibility of a liveable life and the re-making of an inhabitable world.

papers.title: Endurance, Repair, Abolition: Re-thinking the Liveable City

sessions.title: Critical Climate Geographies: Unsettling the Discipline (1): Empirics
sessions.description:
Parsons et al. (2025) recently reminded us that “climate change is everywhere within geographical scholarship. Yet, it is not equally everywhere.” This tension sits at the heart of what it means to engage in critical climate geographies today. Climate change permeates the discipline, yet the power relations, epistemic architectures, and political imaginaries that shape it often remain unexamined or unevenly articulated. In line with the inequalities theme of this years’ RGS-IBG Annual Conference, this session takes seriously the provocation that to understand climate change and its related inequalities, we must also unsettle the discipline itself - its assumptions, its boundaries, and its inherited ways of knowing.

 Building on catalytic interventions across human geography (e.g. Parsons et al. 2025; Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Liverman, 2024; Sultana 2022; Mahony & Hulme 2018; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010), we foreground the diverse trajectories that have pushed climate scholarship into new conceptual and political terrain. The aforementioned articles collectively map a field in which climate epistemologies, decolonial critique, multi‑scalar governance, and speculative climate futures have become central to understanding how climate change is made and remade through power, knowledge, and lived experience.

 Within this expanding geographical landscape, scholarship on climate epistemologies has interrogated the politics of knowledge production, revealing how climate expertise is constructed, circulated, and contested (Mahony and Hulme 2018; Liverman, 2024; Parsons et al. 2025). Decolonial and anti‑racist climate geographies have exposed how colonial legacies, racial capitalism, and structural inequalities underpin climate vulnerability and shape global policy imaginaries (Liverman, 2024; Parsons, 2024; Sultana 2022; Yusoff 2018; Baldwin 2016). Meanwhile, work on climate governance, security, and multi‑scalar politics has traced the entanglements of climate change with human security, adaptation, and state power (Rothe et al., 2025; Kythreotis et al., 2024; Westman, 2024; O’Brien 2012), while analyses of anticipatory and speculative climate futures highlight how imaginaries of socio‑ecological transformation shape political action and the horizons of possibility (Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Cox, 2024; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010).

 We invite papers that support, extend, challenge, or disrupt these trajectories. Contributions may address climate epistemologies and co‑production; decolonial, feminist, and anti‑racist approaches; digital and algorithmic climate governance; climate justice, migration, and security; embodied and affective climate geographies; urban and multi‑scalar governance; or speculative and transformative climate futures. We particularly welcome interdisciplinary, reflexive, and methodologically experimental work – ethnographic, creative, digital, speculative, or collaborative – that pushes the boundaries of what climate geography can be.

 If critical climate geographies are to meet the political and epistemic demands of the present moment, they must continue to unsettle the discipline: its categories, its hierarchies, and its claims to authority. This session aims to convene that unsettling.

sessions.title: Mapping human-microbial relations (2): Human-microbial encounters at the ‘margins’: Bodies, exposures, and infrastructures
sessions.description:
Human-microbial relations have undergone a profound transformation over the past century. In the Global North, the negative consequences of Pasteurian paradigms of eradication (such as drug resistance, the rise of allergies, autoimmune diseases, or the loss of soil fertility) have prompted a ‘probiotic turn’ in cultural imaginaries that have so far been dominated by ideas of hygiene and surveillance. This trend reframes microorganisms as ‘Old Friends’ essential to human health and ecosystem function, and proposes approaching more positively and harnessing a diverse range of microbial functions and their multispecies entanglements. Processes from industrial agriculture to urban wastewater treatment highlight how our worlds intimately depend on the invisible and free labour of regulated microbial ecologies. Yet, convivial or flourishing socio-microbiomes centred on individual health tend to unfold in affluent contexts supported by antibiotic legacies and risk reproducing exclusionary logics.

 Building upon the understanding of microorganisms as active world building agents that co-create, enable, and constrain different forms of social life, this panel aims to differentiate, pluralise, and expand the changing attitudes, practices, and socio-bio-ecologies that constitute the ‘microbial turn’ in their geographically and historically specific contexts. We ask what happens when (unjust, inequitable) social formations encounter recalcitrant and indeterminate microbial agencies. How are microbes enrolled into human worlds (and vice versa), what kinds of transformations unfold and what possibilities come to light? In particular, we aim to investigate the (unintended, unpredictable) political, social, and ecological implications of dis/encouraging microbial growth, and to map how situated practices with microbes create patchy geographies that variously reflect, reinforce, and resist power imbalances.

 As the second of two linked sessions, ‘Bodies, exposures, and infrastructures’ investigates human-microbial encounters at the ‘margins’. Presentations in this session focus on how uneven relations of gender, class, and race produce geographically and historically specific forms of human-microbial bio-sociality. This session frontstages intersections between microbial lives, technological infrastructures, and medical epistemologies to chart situated disturbances of the socio-microbial nexus. Here, metabolic disequilibrium and dysbiotic shifts emerge as precarious modulations of bodies and environments that defy binary categorisation.

papers.title: From the Shopfloor to the Riverbed: Unpackaging Human-Microbe Relations Through the Gut as Regulatory Alliances
On Viral States and Colonising Ecologies
Avoidance or tolerance regimes of exposure: Contested environmental prescriptions to urban pollen allergies
affiliations: Copenhagen Centre for Health Research in the Humanities, University of Copenhagen

sessions.title: Mapping Inequality Critically (2)
More-than-Human Friendships (2)
sessions.description:
The more-than-human has swept geography, the social sciences, and humanities in recent years, having come to encompass life-affirming ways of living with and theorising relationships with and networks of others. In this more-than-human turn, there has been a seemingly endless fascination with all kinds of others and the spaces and places that they, and we, co-produce. From the city to the sea, the familiar to the otherworldly, the more-than-human world has perhaps never been more considered than in contemporary “human” geography. Beyond the boundaries of geography, our moral duties to the non-human world and their rights on and to the Earth have been the concern of politics and philosophy scholars, while creative practitioners have contemplated how best to represent this more-than-human world in art, poetry, and writing.

 Yet, despite this wealth of literature, we believe that there remains considerable scope for exploration, experimentation, and indeed playfulness within this ever-expanding field. Specifically, we are interested in how friendship might be useful, or at least interesting, in understanding more-than-human worlds, lives, deaths, futures, and endings. Friendship itself, although shaping everyday lives, has been relatively under-considered in geographical scholarship and even less so in relation to the more-than-human world. It is our intention with this session to explore how, if at all, friendship might shape or be shaped by the more-than-human relations and worlds.

papers.title: ‘Call the flowers by our names’ – the role of friendship in the vegetal colonisation of Aotearoa New Zealand
Experiencing interspecies friendships: Childhood memories of multispecies forests
Alter-geopolitics and more-than-human friendship: Debordering Europe to non-human animals after Russia’s 2022 invasion of Ukraine
Co-composing Acousmatic Stories about More-Than-Human Friendships

sessions.title: Place-based solidarities under strain? Contested local civic spheres in unequal times (2)
sessions.description:
There has been a recent return to ‘place-based policy’ from the UK government, exemplified in policies such as ‘Levelling Up’ and ‘Pride in Place’. Such a discourse springs from a recognition of political and resourcing failures in centralised programmes. Alongside economic development, health policy is proposing moves towards ‘neighbourhood’ and ‘community’ health whilst ambitions for net zero are increasingly premised on localised programmes. Such initiatives seek to galvanise a sphere of everyday care and civic action in the service of welfare and sustainable development, whilst also responding to the ‘geographies of discontent’ (McCann 2020) shaped by profound inequalities between regions. This shift to a local focus may offer opportunities for neglected or ‘left behind’ communities. However, inequalities and ongoing conditions of austerity have hollowed out the local and shaped unstable and contested local civic spheres. Whilst there is everyday grassroots ‘experimentation’ happening in communities, this is a highly fragmented arena often involving community, faith-based, voluntary and private sector actors rather than public authorities (Thompson and Lorne 2023). Furthermore, the grievances of localised inequality and neglect are now playing a key role in the rise of far right movements in communities (Santamarina and Ince 2025) as well as populist backlash against other progressive policy such as local climate initiatives (Yazar 2024).  In this session we wish to consider some of the tensions of the local and the community as a sphere of social, economic and political change, and the prospects for solidaristic action given conflicting agendas and the place of the local in regressive politics. We also wish to hear about hopeful and optimistic experiments in local support, care and provisioning which may be able to navigate some of these tensions in place. 

 The panel will present research on topics including civic action and imaginaries in local contexts; critical accounts of health, climate or other local initiatives premised on a ‘place-based approach’; models of solidarity and social economic experiments combining social, and climate justice; analysis of far-right organising around the local as well as localised counter-movements

sessions.title: Participatory Ecological Urban Futures by Design
sessions.description:
This session explores participatory design approaches towards urban spatial justice and access to and with the natural world by bringing together a geographical and environmental psychological lens.

 Urban geographies reflect inequalities that shape unequal access to nature. Our collective futures for healthy, just and democratic spaces rely on sensory engagement with the ‘more than human’ worlds and sense(s) of belonging.

 We imagine how our bodies in connection with ‘nature’ (eco-bodyfulness) might inform understanding of citizenship now, and in the future, at a place-based (micro) and global (macro) level. Through guided movement, sensory reflection, case examples, and discussion, we will collectively rethink spatial justice through embodied relationships as humans who are a part of nature.

 We will explore:

 - What is a felt experience of spatial justice in cities?

 - To what extent is ‘sense of belonging’ a planetary health issue?

 - How does movement and connection with ‘nature’ influence our experience of place (micro v macro)?

 - How might we imagine human-nature led experiences to inform urban design, and what is the role of researchers to facilitate this?

 From this workshop, participants will take away a felt experience of urban landscapes to inform co-defined understandings and solutions for integrating human-nature insights into urban design.

sessions.title: The mobility of children and youth in times of socio-ecological transformation: From school streets to cities for children (2)
sessions.description:
The active, independent mobility of children and adolescents has been declining for decades. Journeys primarily made on foot or by bike are increasingly being made by car, characterizing nowadays young people as the “backseat generation”. This has a negative impact on their physical health and cognitive development and limits their use of public space. Overall, environmental burdens and safety issues are increasing with higher car-use among parents and children. To tackle this, recent projects have emphasized young people’s needs in urban and transport planning by initiating, e.g., school or play streets, low-traffic neighbourhoods or other means of street transformation for youth. Taking children and adolescents into account as a planning principle for equitable urban areas and just transport systems is central to a socio-ecological transformation.

 Therefore, the session aims at combining research on children and youth in urban and transport geography, especially emphasizing the influence of urban street transformation on young people’s mobility and use of urban space as well as participation of children and youth towards creating a just and sustainable city.

 The session comprises empirical contributions that draw on sound theoretical reflections from inter- and transdisciplinary perspectives on the mobility of youth, children and their families, addressing e.g.:

 - children and youth in urban planning and street transformation projects (e.g. school streets)

 - participation formats and methods for involving children and youth in urban and mobility planning

 - promoting active mobility and designing streets or public spaces for children and youth

sessions.title: Reversing the Gaze on Technology as a Dominant Tool for Informing Global Targets for Nature Conservation and Recovery: Building an international collaborative network and research agenda
sessions.description:
Digital technologies – from remote sensing to satellite telecommunications to AI – have been increasingly employed as a ‘global gaze’ to measure progress towards quantitative global targets[1] for nature conservation and recovery[2]
This global gaze, defined by the remote decision-making spaces where global targets are set, risks increasing inequalities if it overlooks the knowledge, priorities and realities of the people and places where nature conservation and recovery is planned or practiced. In response, there is rising demand - even as articulated in the targets themselves - to reverse the global gaze of nature conservation and foreground equitable, place-based human-nature relations. Yet there is an inherent tension between the global target-centrism of international decision-making processes, the digital measurement technologies they employ and the recognition of diverse place-based knowledges and priorities, which can be harder to measure and scale. This tension raises questions - from the technical to the ethical and ontological – about whether place-based knowledge and priorities can or should be ‘counted’ towards global targets and priorities, and the role of digital conservation technologies* in making this possible. This panel will be organized as a series of paper presentations that address this tension between the use of digital technologies to serve global priorities and its repurposing for place-based empowerment. This will be followed by a discussion of opportunities for further collaboration and research on this theme.

 *We define ‘digital conservation technologies’ very broadly, ranging from remote sensing to produce visual maps or soundscapes, to eDNA, to the use of social media, AI, etc. 

 [1] E.g. one prominent example among many of such targets, is the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework (KMGBF) “30x30” target to protect 30% of global land and waters by 2030. See also McDermott et al. 2022.

 [2] We use ‘nature recovery’ in a general sense to refer to efforts to restore and recover natural processes.

sessions.title: Beyond Human Bias: Re-imagining Equitable AI for Tourism’s Multiple Stakeholders
sessions.description:
Session Co-Organisers: Duygu Salman, Fiona Bakas, Aysun Bozanta 

 AI systems are increasingly embedded in tourism platforms, shaping how travellers navigate destinations, how places and local communities are represented, and how tourism workers experience labour and well-being. The Digital Revolution is a wave of innovation, leading to the growth of some tourism and leisure jobs and the disappearance of others, but it is reproducing longstanding gendered, racialised, and accessibility-related inequalities, while also generating new forms of algorithmic exclusion. The European Institute for Gender Equality warns that whilst sharing platforms can con­tribute to more gender equality, they can also reinforce sex­ism and inequality. Despite the infiltration of digitisation within many areas of tourism, very limited research exists on the impact of the rapidly technologically advancing phenomenon.

 Complementing these perspectives, this session foregrounds a technical perspective on how artificial intelligence—particularly large language models (LLMs), retrieval-augmented systems, and recommender algorithms—structures tourism’s information and decision-making landscapes. LLMs may also propagate stereotypical cultural representations. In addition, algorithmic management and automated moderation can misclassify or suppress content when linguistic or cultural contexts are not adequately captured . This session aims to integrate methods from AI auditing, fairness-aware machine learning, and dataset documentation with critical tourism geography. Thus, it highlights the need for research on transparent evaluation practices and equity-oriented governance frameworks to ensure that AI reshapes tourism in ways that reduce rather than reproduce structural inequalities related to tourism workers, customers and all related stakeholders.

 We invite contributions that explore how bias is encoded, amplified, or mitigated across tourism’s diverse stakeholder groups—including travellers, hosts and guides, platform employees, customer-service workers, and small tourism businesses whose livelihoods increasingly depend on AI transparency. By foregrounding feminist, intersectional, anti-racist, and accessibility-centred approaches, the session seeks to connect critical tourism studies with emerging debates in AI ethics, digital labour, platformisation and information systems.

 We particularly welcome papers that:

 - analyse how AI shapes experiences of mobility, safety, labour, and representation; 
- audit or critique LLM outputs, datasets, or platform algorithms; 
- examine how inequalities in algorithmic management and digital customer-service infrastructures influence tourism workers and tourists
- propose design frameworks, imaginaries, or governance practices for more equitable and inclusive AI use in tourism. 
- provide feminist analyses of how gender roles impact and are impacted by the digitisation and platformisation of tourism and leisure
- expand on how intersectional inequalities of tourism workers are amplified by the digitisation and platformisation of tourism
- explore how Ai and platformisation impact tourism workers’ and tourists´ well-being
- develop an understanding of who AI-mediated platforms are excluding and who they are including

 Empirical, theoretical, and methodological contributions are encouraged.

 Bringing together tourism geographers, digital geographers, AI scholars, and tourism technology and leisure practitioners, this session aims to open a critical space to rethink how AI could be designed and governed to reduce rather than reinforce geographies of inequality. Sponsored by Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group (https://www.gltrg.org)

sessions.title: Towards Just Futures: Negotiating Emotional Geographies of Safety and Place (2)
sessions.description:
For Children and Young People (CYP), justice is not only a legal or abstract concept, but an everyday, emotional and spatial experience; shaping how they move through space, relate to others, and imagine their futures. CYP are often overlooked as active participants in the making of justice (Blaisdell et al., 2021; Kraftl, 2013). Instead, justice is often encountered through adult-designed systems, rules and institutions that regulate their bodies, emotions and possibilities.

 At a time when uncertainty, violence, displacement, conflict, and insecurity are intensifying, CYP’s contributions to geographical debates are overlooked by adult-centric discourses and narratives. Yet, CYP are increasingly expected to live with the consequences of injustice while having limited power to shape responses to it (Khan, 2021; Pain et al., 2010). Within these conditions, justice is deeply entangled with violence and trauma (Pain and Cahill, 2022). Some CYP live with the impacts of violence inflicted upon them, while others are positioned as causing trauma through acts of violence within constrained, unequal and emotionally charged environments (Fraser, 2015). These experiences are not separate, but circulate through the same places, relationships and institutions, shaping how safety, responsibility and accountability are understood and enacted. In schools, streets, community spaces, the home and digital environments, CYP negotiate justice through emotions, as they navigate harm, its consequences and possibilities for repair (Andal, 2023; Marrun and Rodriguez-Campo, 2023).

 CYP occupy a contested role in the future imaginaries of a just world. As a beacon of hope and promise, CYP play a vital role in acting towards social, environmental, political, and economic justice (Paterson-Young et al., 2024). On the other end of the dualism, CYP are often underscored as vulnerable, inexperienced and naïve to the ‘real world’ or even seen as troublemakers and agitators (Schliehe, 2021).
 Session I & II seek to explore how justice is felt through practices of navigating harm, seeking safety, supporting peers, and challenging exclusion. Attending justice as a youth centred emotional geography foregrounds how CYP actively interpret, perform and produce justice in ways that are deeply relational, situated and tied to the places they move through.

sessions.title: Rural Spatial Justice and Place-Making from a Planetary Perspective (2)
sessions.description:
Processes of rural place-making are closely connected to questions of inequalities and injustice. Disparities in the distribution of wealth, resources and services between rural and urban areas, as well as between rural places, shape trajectories of place development and the identities attached to places. Processes of place-making in turn shape perceptions of spatial injustice, or the unfair treatment, informing a ‘rural consciousness’ through which rural residents interpret politics (Cramer, 2016). Questions of rural spatial justice are also evident in the variegated capacity of individuals to participate in making rural places – a ‘right to the rural’ paralleling the ‘right to the city’. Moreover, approached as a normative concept, rural spatial justice can inspire proactive and progressive place-making (Woods, 2023).

 This session specifically calls for papers that address these dynamics between rural place-making and spatial justice from a planetary perspective. Following principles outlined in Wang et at’s (2025) call for planetary rural geographies, a planetary perspective highlights the making and remaking of rural places within unequal global relations and associated questions of economic and social justice; the more-than-human composition of place and the questions of multi-species justice embedded in rural places; the justice or injustice of knowledge claims for describing, knowing and representing rural places, from colonial scientific legacies to indigenous thinking; and rural place-making with respect to planetary crises, including issues of environmental justice in the remaking of rural places by climate change and in conflicts over the repurposing of rural land for renewable energy, rewilding or carbon forestry, for example.

 As such, we understand place-making as a relational, contested, and uneven process shaped by multi-scalar forces, including climate change, agro-ecological transitions, infrastructure expansion, extractivism, conservation, migration, and food sovereignty struggles. We are particularly interested in how justice claims emerge across scales, and how rural places are made, unmade, and remade through interactions among human and more-than-human actors.
 The session aims to foster dialogue between rural studies, critical agrarian studies, political ecology, and more-than-human geographies, advancing a relational and planetary understanding of rural spatial justice.

papers.title: The lands that won’t yield: Rural discontent in times of harvest losses and accelerating climate change

sessions.title: Landing offshore wind: uneven coastal geographies of marine extractivism (2)
sessions.description:
The sea has become a resource frontier for the expansion of new energy technologies, especially offshore wind farms. Offshore wind is considered an important part of the solution to the climate crisis, energy security and as a catalyst for economic growth. Moreover, the oftentimes deceptive notion of offshore wind being “out of sight, out of mind” suggests that placing turbines at the sea would be less controversial and that their deployment does not create as severe conflicts on land as their counterparts onshore (Haggett 2008; 2011). However, even when these extractive activities take place under water or beyond the horizon, they still have multi-faceted impacts on land. Offshore wind farms, as a new form of marine extractivism, require the construction of e.g. onshore substations, pylons with high voltage cables, underground cables, harbour facilities for turbine construction, operation and maintenance activities, and auxiliary services which can all affect and reshape environmental and socio-economic conditions, cultural values ​​and the lived experiences of coastal communities (e.g. Glasson et al. 2022, Schneider & O’Neill 2025, Sornn-Friese et al. 2022). The expansion of offshore wind infrastructures and the emergence of the offshore wind industry have manifested in local and regional, yet geographically uneven, industrial development (e.g. Dawley et al. 2010, 2015; Gibbs & Jensen 2022; MacKinnon et al. 2022) ), thus engendering environmental, distributional and democratic disparities and inequalities. Since impacted communities and beneficiaries rarely overlap, the expansion of offshore wind raises critical questions of spatial and distributional justice and of democratic governance. Increasing protests from coastal communities along with the rapid expansion offshore wind farms vis-a-vis efforts to capture economic opportunities actualizes the need for knowledge about how land-based impacts manifest, where and why economic opportunities emerge, and to what extent industrial activities can be anchored locally. 

 This session seeks to shed critical light on the various manifestations of uneven geographies of expanding offshore wind developments, especially opportunities, challenges and conflicts emerging from land-based activities. We call for papers on this topic, exploring for example:

 - Stakeholder and/or public responses to offshore extractive projects

 - Spatial implications of the offshore wind value chain

 - Environmental and cultural implications and needs for coordinated spatial governance

 - Regional economic competition (e.g. between different harbours and industries) and collaboration on attracting new initiatives

 - Imaginaries and narratives of offshore wind developments

 - The role of institutions in steering industrial development of offshore wind energy

 - The role and fate of local and community identity (e.g. resisting, subverting, co-evolving)

 - The role and fate of existing infrastructures (assets, legacies..)

 - Interplay between industry, government, third sector, residents in shaping more locally appropriate and just offshore wind developments

 - Spatial implications of labour activities of the offshore wind industry

 - Benefit capture, skills development, distribution of job impacts (positive or negative)

 - Marine and/vs terrestrial spatial planning; multi-use and multi-functional landscapes

papers.title: Just and Resilient Offshore Wind Transitions? The Evolving Narratives of Energy Futures in Germany and Poland
Understanding emotional investment in the sociotechnical imaginaries of supporters and opponents of offshore wind
Matchsticks or Not? The Power of Metaphors in Shaping Imaginaries of the Offshore Wind Landscape
Conflicting imaginaries in the renewable energy transition around the Danish island of Bornholm
Mapping the Impacts: The Role of Counter-Mapping in Offshore Wind Development

sessions.title: Building food justice through participatory and innovative methods for community food research (2)
sessions.description:
Persistent injustices such as food insecurity, unequal food access, land-use pressures, and diet-related disease continue to shape our food system. Community food can be a space for addressing inequalities and imagining alternative food systems. Whether in the form of urban agriculture, community supported agriculture, food banks, allotments, community meals or other forms, spaces of community food are often sites where inequalities are contested, making them sites of sustained interest from a research perspective.

 Community food organisations are frequently asked to participate in research in various roles. Without clear goals agreed from the start, expectations can be misaligned, leading to uneven outcomes, duplication of research (often driven by funding availability) and research fatigue. Short-term and often niche funding opportunities (and other factors including academic workload, university bureaucracy, third sector reliance on volunteers and their own funding cycles) can also make long-term relationships between academics and external partners difficult to maintain, despite increasing emphasis on the importance of ‘impact’ from the university side.

 Participatory research approaches produce knowledge, but they also redistribute power, centre lived experience, and challenge extractive research relations. This session aims to explore how participatory methods, and other methodological innovations, can make research with community food organisations more just and more democratically grounded. It explores tensions and opportunities in co-producing knowledge with community actors; how scholar-activists navigate power, positionality and ethics; and innovations, particularly in participatory methodologies, for addressing food insecurity, urban food governance; or other related topics.

papers.title: The meaning of grassroots: reflections on a collaborative local food systems project
Whose value? Using participatory methods to understand public procurement of sustainable food
Care at the Threshold: Hidden Food Insecurity and Student Activism in Istanbul’s Campus Foodscapes
ECR confessions. The joys and challenges of participatory food future mapping in uncertain times

sessions.title: Big-Tech and the Governance of Labour and Bodies
sessions.description:
Recent scholarship highlights the growing influence of technology corporations in shaping political, social, and state practices. Big Tech platforms and AI technologies are increasingly entangled with state power, authoritarian logics, and new forms of governance, raising fundamental questions about political authority, public accountability, and democratic life (Jarret et al, 2025). The concept of “Big Tech authoritarianism” (Akbari 2025) foregrounds how tech giants not only mediate information but also align with or reproduce authoritarian logics of power, surveillance, and control. Likewise, emerging frameworks of AI authoritarianism (Dekeyser et al. 2025) argue that key features of AI-such as scalability, algorithmic incorporation of difference, and techno-determinist ideologies - actively reshape political governance and authoritarian dynamics across geopolitical contexts. 

 This session responds to a growing need to critically interrogate the political thrust of technological power and the growing political influence of tech capital at a time when digital platforms and algorithmic systems are increasingly central to shaping state practices, public discourse, citizen engagement, and the possibilities for resistance. Rather than treating “the digital” as a neutral domain, we invite geopolitically informed analyses that situate technology in the relational dynamics of power, space, and governance. This aims to build on existing scholarship theorizing data power (Thatcher & Dalton, 2021), technofascism (McElroy 2025; Coeckelbergh, 2026), or digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019), and aligns with recent calls to rethink governance frameworks in light of AI’s active modulation of political processes and the political synergies between Big Tech and state actors. 

 This session explores how Big Tech and AI are implicated in reconfiguring the practices, techniques, and imaginaries of governance, with a specific focus on the governance of labour and bodies.

sessions.title: Repoliticising Inequality: Organised Abandonment, Carceral Violence and Actively Working Towards Abolition (1): Mind: knowing domination
sessions.description:
This experimental 1-day session at this year's RGS conference, invites creative and conventional contributions that centre around abolitionist understandings of “organised abandonment” (Gilmore 2022, Arefin and Khan 2024, Mei-Singh 2024). Mei-Singh (2024) (drawing on Gilmore 2008 and Bhandar 2018) offers a brief overview of the state of the concept: 

 Existing scholarship on organized abandonment conveys how neoliberal state reorganization sacrifices particular places. This results in the flight of economic activity and jobs, environmental degradation, underemployment, the diminishment and privatization of public programs, and the disappearance of entire ways of life, all part and parcel of the disregard of those subjugated by a racial capitalist social order. 

 The session(s) invite activists, scholars, artists, community workers and others (at all career stages and from diverse locations) to come together - in ways that are life-giving, creative and transcend academic convention - to continue ‘sharpening our focus of how such abandonment results from plans, as well as abdications of responsibility’ (Gilmore 2023). Arefin and Khan (2024) maintain that organised abandonment is ‘not a passive withdrawal, but an active plot’. They (Ibid:) go on to add that this ‘invites the same precision in our counterplot’. 

 Therefore, the session(s) also invite us to continue sharpening our focus on how we counter and unmake organised abandonment. We see this countering and unmaking as ‘actively working towards abolition’ (Kaba nd) and finding, recovering and restoring lifeways that have been forcibly abdicated (Mei-Singh 2024). Sessions include papers and micro-workshop activities that help deepen how we understand organised abandonment and how we resist and restore in the wake of the ‘rupturing of life-giving relations’ (Mei-Singh 2024). This matters because grassroots activists continue to theorise abolition as an ethics, practice, struggle and way of being, that requires the development of “dual power” (Kaba and Ritchie 2021) where emergent and oppositional forms of resistance coexist and interdepend (Brown 2017, Ritchie 2023, Chhibber 2025, see also Hughes 2021). That is:

 understanding, opposing, fighting and unmaking carceral systems, organised abandonment and related ways of being 

 coexist and interdepend with 

 restoring, building and practicing life-affirming systems and ways of being that refuse to use organised abandonment and carcerality as solutions to entrenched social problems (Davis 2003, Kaba and Ritchie 2022, Ritchie 2023, Kass 2025). 

 ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀ ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

 We see this session not as a way of coming to a definitive disciplinary conclusion or resolution on what organised abandonment is but rather as a way of coming together with an openness for whatever emerges, be this contradictions, contingencies or interrelations. We encourage ‘acting and speaking in ways that are not about mastery, but rather about audacity or incompleteness, because [we] don’t think any of us can be complete in our understanding or in our pursuit of freedom on our own’ (Bhattachrya np 2025).

 ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀
