sessions.title: Grounding nuclear imaginaries: Placing geographies of nuclear ‘progress’, protest, and care (1)
sessions.description:
The nuclear continues to preoccupy our minds. As energy, weapon, mineral, medicine, and waste, nuclear issues and the social imaginaries that underpin them continue to exert a shaping force on global and local affairs. This session invites scholars and practitioners to attend to these contemporary imaginaries: their modes of production, internal logics and inconsistencies, the possibilities they enable or foreclose, and the forms of power that they underpin. 
 Two questions are of particular interest. The first is the extent to which contemporary nuclear imaginaries are tied to or inherit from ideas, knowledges, and aesthetics of the past. Are these novel formations, or do they bear the traces and traumas (Schwab, 2020) of bygone periods like the Cold War (Faux, 2024)? To what extent are efforts to renew the nuclear (for example the efforts to brand civil nuclear power as ‘green’) an effective strategy in re-ontologising it? At the heart of this is a consideration of the nature of nuclear imaginaries as a potent mode of reproducing historical patterns (and violences) as well as a new set of tools to grasp the specificity of the present. The second centres the relationship between nuclear imaginaries and place. In the last two decades, important ethnographic and historical research have sought to articulate various ‘local’ nuclear imaginaries tied to particular lands, nation-states, governments, and scientific organisations: for example, an American post-Cold War nuclear imaginary (Masco, 2008), British nuclear cultures (Hogg, 2016; Hogg and Brown, 2019; Hughes, 2012), and civic epistemologies of the Indian elite classes (Anderson, 2010; Haines, 2019). Yet, outside of this corpus, scholars note a tendency in popular imaginaries of the nuclear to untether from place. The historian Lisa Yoneyama (1999, 2023) calls this tendency nuclear universalism, where the abstraction of nuclear imaginaries – especially into global imaginaries by way of an aesthetics of apocalypse – obscures the structural violence inflicted upon specific communities and places erased by these imaginaries (Hecht, 2012; Nixon, 2011; Voyles 2015). The disembedding of imaginaries from place has an obscuring function; it is only by concealing the long-term material and ecological burdens of nuclear mining, processing, and waste storage that its inherent promises (e.g. of cheap and endless energy) can be upheld (Pottin, 2024). These are nuclear imaginaries that ironically inhibit imagination itself, making it hard to think otherwise (Deckard, 2022) and posing a challenge to forms of anti-nuclear resistance (Faison and Fields, 2024; Urwin 2025). 
 Taken together, these two questions guide what promises to be a lively exchange on the forms and functions of nuclear imaginaries in yet another supposed ‘nuclear renaissance’. The session turns on the central provocation that it ought to matter where thought takes place and how thought makes place. Only by attending to the worlding capacities of imaginaries might we be able to consider ways to shift them (Carpenter, 2016). We invite papers that consider the recursive relationships between nuclear imaginaries and nuclear places.

sessions.title: The Geographies of Generative AI in Higher Education
sessions.description:
Generative artificial intelligence is rapidly reshaping practices of learning, teaching, and knowledge production in higher education (Pratschke, 2024, HEPI, 2025). The widespread use of large language models by both students and faculty presents fundamental challenges to established pedagogical norms, as well as academic integrity, and institutional governance (Bertram Gallant and Rettinger, 2025). As universities struggle to respond to these disruptions, significant shifts are emerging in assessment practices, curriculum (re)design, and integrity and assurance mechanisms, as well as everyday pedagogical cultures of teaching and learning.

 Taking seriously the geographies of the university, this session invites critical reflection on how generative AI is reconfiguring the geographies of higher education; spatially, institutionally, ethically and relationally. Rather than focusing narrowly on technical or instrumental approaches such as prompt engineering, the session seeks to centre broader conceptual and empirical insights on how universities are being reshaped by cultures of artificial generativity, including changes in pedagogical spaces and cultures, shifts in students’ digital practices, as well as how forms of academic labour are being reimagined in response. 

 We welcome contributions in the broadest sense, including conceptual interventions, pedagogical reflections, and empirical case studies. Topics may include, but are not limited to:

 - experiences embedding critical AI literacy within geography degree programmes

 - analysis of institutional AI strategies and responses

 - critical AI politics and resistance amongst students and faculty

 - approaches to governance, assurance, and academic integrity

 - assessment redesign and curriculum innovation

 - AI and student experience enhancement and student-voice projects

 - the implications of generative AI for student wellbeing, pastoral care, and academic support.

papers.title: Marketisation, Managerialism and the AI “Crisis” in Higher Education
Ethical use of GenAI in Teaching and Learning: Insights from a Community of Practice Approach
AI in Action: Knowledge, ideology and practice of AI amongst university students
Cultural and Political Biases in Generative AI: A Critical Classroom-Based Study in Geography of Climate Change Education
Reflections from Across the AI Assessment Faultline: Diverging Practices of Re Design or Resistance in Geography Assessment
How can I help you? GenAI as a Partner in Academic Integrity

sessions.title: Uneven waters: Exploring meanings and approaches to just climate adaptation in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North (2)
sessions.description:
Water risks and their uneven geographies have become a central concern for the planning and development of cities in the context of a global climate emergency and other intersecting crises. As cities confront the challenges associated with managing water extremes (both floods and droughts), climate adaptation efforts increasingly focus on controlling and redefining urban water's changing dynamics and flows. Such efforts are especially visible in ‘urban blue spaces’ —i.e., areas adjacent to salt, fresh, or brackish surface waters– where water-related vulnerabilities, value, and governance intersect to reshape infrastructure and everyday life. Yet the production of urban blue spaces is far from neutral. They result instead from layered histories of investment and neglect, formal and informal practices, and uneven distributions of risks, benefits, and responsibilities. Climate impacts and adaptation interventions can entrench or unsettle these longstanding inequalities, through individual and collective responses enacted by state agencies, private actors, community practices, and ecological processes. Critical urban and adaptation scholarship has begun to expose such uneven outcomes, but more work is needed to further unpack how water is managed in urban blue spaces and its unjust implications, and to reveal alternative, justice-oriented approaches.
 This session will present conceptual, methodological, and empirical contributions that interrogate how justice materializes or fails to materialize in urban blue spaces across the Global South and North. The presentations include comparative, interdisciplinary, and reflexive work that is attentive to power, knowledge asymmetries, and diverse ways of understanding water through the lens or justice.

papers.title: Hydro-hazardscape of Seoul's Banjiha Housing: Assembling Techno-Politics and Compressed Modernity of Seoul
Ecology of drainage: Land use planning, stormwater and the production of environmental risk in Mumbai
Decentring flood adaptation approaches towards climate justice

sessions.title: Uneven geographies of decent work
sessions.description:
Renewed global attention to decent work as a policy goal (ILO, SDG 8) has positioned it as a pathway towards inclusive growth, reduced inequality, and social justice. Despite this, research consistently demonstrates that access to decent work is deeply uneven, contingent on place, scale, and intersecting social differences. Across diverse sectors and regions, work that is secure, dignified, and fairly remunerated remains inaccessible for many, while precarious, informal, and undervalued forms of labour are increasingly normalized. Access to decent work, under what conditions, and in which places, is still shaped by gendered, racialized, and classed hierarchies; migration regimes and bordering practises; platformisation and technological change; and environmental and political crises. These dynamics not only structure labour outcomes, but also actively produce unjust places through uneven distribution of risk, care, opportunity and recognition.

 This session invites contributions that critically engage with decent work both as a normative ideal and a lived, spatially situated experience. We are particularly interested in research that examines how (decent) work is defined, negotiated, denied and contested across different geographical contexts, and how workers, communities, and institutions attempt to make work and places more just. We also welcome papers that move beyond policy framings to foreground everyday practices, collective action, and alternative imaginaries of work, livelihood, and value.

 We welcome research grounded in diverse occupational and geographical contexts, including but not limited to service industries, care work, manufacturing, logistics, agriculture, platform labour, and informal economies. Areas to consider could include (this is an indicative rather than exhaustive list):

 - Uneven geographies of decent work across regions, sectors, and scales.
- Precarious, informal, and insecure labour and their implications for spatial justice
- Gendered, racialized, and migrant labour inequalities
- Care, social production, and the undervaluation of essential work
- Platform, gig, and algorithmic labour and new forms of control and exclusion
- Workers’ organising, solidarity economies, and place-based struggles for labour justice
- Crisis, uncertainty, and the reconfiguration of decent work
- Alternative visions of work, livelihood, and value beyond dominant economic models.
- Critiques of policy definitions of decent work and/or framing around inclusive and sustained economic growth

sessions.title: Tackling Energy Inequalities through Home Energy Advice and Retrofit (2): Examining how place-based retrofit unfolds in practice
sessions.description:
Decarbonising heat and buildings through energy retrofit remains essential for mitigating climate change and addressing spatially entrenched health, energy and economic inequalities. Emergent 'place-based' or 'neighbourhood' approaches, which integrate fabric, heating and smart technology interventions within spatially-defined programmes, are receiving increased attention in policy and practice in many countries. Often this is based on contentions that place-based approaches can accelerate retrofit delivery by connecting national strategy with local needs, develop local supply chains, foster community and citizen engagement, and engage with the financial, social and cultural realities of retrofit in places (Bolton et al, 2023; Macrorie et al, 2024; van Casteren et al, 2024).

 Place-based retrofit represents a departure from piecemeal interventions, embedding energy improvements within broader processes of neighbourhood regeneration and transformation. As such delivery may combine energy measures with wider improvements to green space, transport and public realm and/or incorporate innovative finance mechanisms or community co-design. However, as new ‘imaginaries’ of energy efficient neighbourhoods and places arise, important questions of power, governance, justice and efficacy arise (Robinson et al, 2024), including: Who envisions, governs, funds and implements these 'future neighbourhoods'? How do residents from diverse socio-economic, cultural and demographic backgrounds experience, respond to, and engage with such transformations? How democratic and inclusive are such processes? How do place-based approaches mobilise socio-spatial relations to accelerate delivery?

 This session invites papers that critically examine the theoretical foundations, practical implementation and social implications of place-based retrofit approaches, drawing on scholarship in place-making, energy transitions, governance, environmental justice and beyond. The sessions will employ a World Café approach over two sessions, each comprised of four papers and rotating discussion. We are open to a diverse range of abstracts that engage with the spatial and justice aspects of building decarbonisation and welcome submissions from all career-stages and non-academic organisations.

papers.title: Behind the dashboard: Data, prioritisation and justice in targeting place-based retrofit
Devolved governance for scaled quality housing energy retrofit: A comparative regional evaluation.
The contribution of community intermediary led, bottom-up, approaches to place based retrofit in a Just Transition: a case study from Levenshulme, Manchester.

sessions.title: Emerging voices in economic geography (1)
sessions.description:
This session invites postgraduate researchers (PGRs) and early career researchers (ECRs) to share emerging research, ideas, and provocations in economic geography. We aim to create a supportive and inclusive space for scholars at the early stages of their academic journeys to showcase work in progress, explore new directions, and connect with others working across diverse traditions and contexts.

 We welcome contributions that engage with any aspect of economic geography, broadly defined. This may include (but is not limited to) research on:

 - Economic inequalities and uneven development
- Digital, platform, and creative economies
- Gender, race, identity and the economy
- Geographies of markets, production, exchange and consumption
- Geographies of money, finance and financialisation
- Labour, work, livelihoods, and care
- Housing, real estate, and the built environment
- Regional, urban, and rural economies
- Sustainability, climate, and green transitions
- Industrial change, innovation, and entrepreneurship
- Informal, alternative, and community economies
- Intellectual histories and futures of economic geography
- Theories and theorising in economic geography
- Methods and methodological innovation in research on the economy

 The session is intentionally open and exploratory. We particularly encourage:

 - Papers based on PhD research or postdoctoral projects
- Works-in-progress or presentation of preliminary findings
- Contributions that experiment with methods, theory, or interdisciplinary approaches
- Submissions from researchers who are new to the RGS–IBG conference or academic conferencing more broadly

 Our goal is to foster constructive discussion, peer support, and confidence-building in a friendly environment. Presenters will be encouraged to share not only findings, but also questions, challenges, and future directions.

papers.title: Contextualizing AI workplace disruptions in the call center sector

sessions.title: Geographies of Intensity (1)
sessions.description:
Intensity appears to be a problem today. The crisis prone present seems to be characterised by too much of the wrong sort of intensity. Claims are made that subjects are too outraged or too disaffected. Whilst some are baited to outrage amid today’s digital economy, others lie flat as they feel a pernicious combination of expectation and economic downturn. Extended periods of stretched out waiting for fragile promises to materialise (Pettit 2023), coexist with hustle cultures of constant activity (Thieme 2025). Calls to foster passionate attachment to work coexist with quiet quitting (Hong 2022). Politics amid the extended end of neoliberalism and as mediated by digital capitalism is characterised as being full of uneasy mixtures of outrage and boredom. Maladies of intensity – from burnout (Han 2015) to overstimulation – appear to serve as the signature affects for a present characterised as at once too much and too little, both too intense and not intense enough. 
 But in addition to being key to diagnosing the present conjuncture in all its overdetermined complexity, intensity can be found across multiple geographies. Value is created through generating intensity, whether in new digital worlds, through branding and hype, or via entertainment forms (e.g. the Blockbuster film). Capitalism has frequently been understood to involve a logic of intensification as value is extracted from life, labour and land. Political forms offer and promise intensity, whether the quietness and disaffection of liberalism or the rage infused enjoyment of right-wing populism (Anderson & Secor 2025). Forms of power work through intensity. Foucault (1977), for example, writes of how sovereignty works through ‘visible scenes of intensity’ as he details the spectacle of the scaffold. Events that are apprehended as crises are mediated through intensities that can change how they become present in everyday life. Mediated in terms of the catastrophic, climate change might be met with boredom and other disaffected feelings, for some subjects. The ‘immigration crisis’ gathers intensities around it, as authoritarian intensities are performed through mediated spectacle of violent raids targeting racialised subjects. At the same time, intensity is central to the lived experience of place. Whether the buzz of a city, the density of a crowd (McFarlane 2016) or stillness of a landscape, particular kinds of good intensity are governed, desired, valued, and experienced. This set of sessions explores the cultures, politics and economies of intensity.

papers.title: Climate change and the politics of disaffection

sessions.title: Urban Heat: Thermopolitics in More-Than-Human Cities (2)
sessions.description:
Extreme heat is rapidly emerging as one of the twenty-first century’s most pressing urban challenges, with profound implications for health, livelihoods, infrastructures, and everyday life. Far from affecting all urban inhabitants equally, urban heat intersects with entrenched inequalities in housing quality, energy access, infrastructural maintenance, and environmental conditions. As temperatures rise, the capacities of different urban populations to cope with, survive, and recover from heat differ starkly. Many policy and scholarly responses frame urban heat as a technical, environmental, or public health problem, while tending to overlook the highly political relations through which thermal vulnerability is produced, or indeed how power and governance operate through thermal relations.

 This panel seeks to develop a relational, more-than-human approach to urban thermopolitics. Where an emerging body of geographical work on urban heat has begun to acknowledge the importance of infrastructures and urban form in generating and distributing thermal discomfort, there has been only limited engagement with the more-than-human politics of heat. Connecting insights from more-than-human geographies, urban political ecology, and STS, the panel seeks to extend human-centred accounts of thermal inequalities by including not only infrastructures, technologies, and other forms of inanimate matter, but also sentient, living other-than-human beings. From energy grids and building materials to plants, animals, and microbes, we seek to understand the more-than-human relations that shape and are shaped by urban heat and its attendant inequalities. 

 The panel invites contributions that examine how uneven thermal experiences and vulnerabilities emerge through interactions among people, infrastructures, technologies, animals, plants, and other-than-human actants, and how these relations shape who and what is rendered vulnerable, protected, or expendable in overheated cities. Empirically, we welcome papers that engage with a range of thematic concerns across diverse urban contexts; these could include addressing, for instance, one or more of the following questions: 

 - How are thermal inequalities produced through more-than-human relations in urban environments? How do interactions between non-human elements—such as buildings, materials, energy systems, vegetation, animals, and atmospheric conditions—and human bodies shape urban thermal environments, and how do these interactions unevenly distribute heat exposure, vulnerability, and comfort?
- What everyday more-than-human caring practices emerge in overheated cities? How do residents, community organizations, and informal networks negotiate heat through situated practices of care, repair, and adaptation, and how do these practices mobilize non-human actors such as materials, plants, and technologies?
- How is extreme heat framed in urban planning and policy? How do dominant approaches conceptualise heat as a risk, resource, or technical problem, and what human or other-than-human forms of agency, knowledge, and responsibility are foregrounded (or conversely, obscured) in these framings?
- Which alternative methodologies enable the study and advancement of thermal justice? Which innovative methods can be used to study extreme heat as a relational, more-than-human urban process, and how do these approaches contest dominant ways of knowing heat while enabling alternative imaginaries and practices of thermal justice?

papers.title: The Political Ecology of Heat: Insights from Everyday Heat Experience and Adaptation Practices in Overheated Favelas of Rio de Janeiro
Everyday experiences of heat: Towards an anti-caste urban political ecology of street dwelling in India
Planning for Extreme Heat Scenarios in the City: From the Planetary to the Local and Back Again
Care as Climate Infrastructure: Immigrant Women and the Thermopolitics of Urban Heat
Climate Shelters and the Thermopolitics of Urban Heat in Venice

sessions.title: Contested depths: Subsurface geographies of the energy transition (2)
sessions.description:
The subsurface is an increasingly contested terrain. From geothermal energy extraction to carbon storage, heat networks to critical mineral mining, competing claims over what lies beneath our feet and impacts on the landscapes, heritage sites and natural environments above raise fundamental questions of access, ownership, and environmental justice.

 Subsurface governance structures can reflect and reproduce existing power asymmetries. Property rights frameworks may privilege surface landowners or corporate extractors whilst marginalising communities who bear environmental risks. Regulatory regimes designed for fossil capitalism struggle to accommodate renewable subsurface uses like geothermal energy or seasonal thermal storage. Routine planning and regulatory decisions can foreclose future subsurface options before they are ever considered, locking in incompatible surface development and uneven transition pathways. Indigenous communities worldwide contest colonial appropriation of subsurface resources and assert sovereignty over traditional territories extending below ground.

 Whilst energy transitions extend across horizontal and vertical space, subsurface resources are often rendered invisible within planning systems, regulatory frameworks and public debate, even as they become central to decarbonisation strategies. This mismatch between an emerging net-zero subterranean energy regime and largely surface-oriented governance arrangements creates new sites of conflict, exclusion and lock-in.

 This session examines how subsurface allocation, regulation and contestation intersect with spatial inequalities across multiple dimensions: distributional (who benefits and who bears costs), procedural (who participates in governance), epistemic (whose knowledge counts) and scalar (coordination across regulatory jurisdictions). We're particularly interested in cases where governance fragmentation between surface and subsurface systems creates barriers to just energy transitions.

 We welcome theoretical, empirical and practitioner perspectives which explore how subsurface geographies connect to questions of spatial inequality, environmental justice and pathways toward more just and equitable energy and resource transitions, with particular attention to how planning, policy and governance arrangements shape what subsurface energy transition futures become possible in practice.

papers.title: Multi-use of the subsurface, a multi-value cost-benefit analysis.
How Are Critical Minerals Changing Geographies of Extraction?: Evidence From Peru, Serbia, and Zambia
Securing the Subsurface: Critical Minerals Exploration and the Governance of the Energy Transition in the UK
Contested Identities and Misrecognised Places: Recognition Justice in Subsurface Energy Governance

sessions.title: Wasteful geographies of (un-)building: tracing hidden earthly flows
sessions.description:
The built environment produces enormous volumes of waste amid an intensifying climate crisis, extractive urbanisation, and contested sustainability transitions. Building on work that foregrounds waste as a materially and politically charged condition of contemporary capitalism (Armiero, 2020; Lepawsky, 2018; Liboiron and Lepawsky, 2022; Moore, 2023; Pyyhtinen et al., 2023; Shapiro & McNeish, 2021;), this session turns to the earthly and mineral materialities that flow through construction and demolition terrains: excavated soils, buried rubble, concrete, sand, gravel, and other matter often considered waste or surplus. While there are extensive reflections on the role of waste for society in general, the mineral materialities of construction are mostly absent in these debates. Moreover, governing entities and (de-)construction companies treat these materialities as stable and inert, enabling economic exploitation through discourses of efficiency and utility.

 In this session, we break with this misconception: to the contrary, these materialities move, leak, and persist (Hird, 2013). Surplus and waste soils and rocks travel across sites, borders, classifications, and value regimes, at times framed as resources, at others as hazardous contamination (Meulemans, 2022; Wildeboer & Savini, 2023). In doing so, they trouble tidy narratives of circularity, efficiency, and environmental protection . In this context, what is considered waste, where it is sent, and who must live with its consequences are all deeply political questions. Construction waste management does more than simply manage debris; it redistributes harm, reconfigures land use, and entrenches unequal geographies of exposure and exclusion, often in ways that are difficult to see, map, or measure. And still, much of this process happens out of view, underground, or buried in bureaucratic and scientific classifications (Balayannis, 2020; Greeson et al., 2020).

 Our interest in the residual materials of current building practices sits alongside a growing body of work that calls for a radical rethinking of construction itself, from proposals for moratoria on new building and value shifts toward existing stocks (Malterre-Barthes, 2025) to architectural practices rooted in sufficiency, care and repair, and experiment with less extractive urban futures (Hertweck et al., 2023). These interventions challenge dominant types of governance, which are often still associated with the idea of endless building and economic growth, and acknowledge the inherited and ongoing damage of these governance practices.

 We invite papers that critically examine how flows of earthly wastes of the built environment contribute to the making of unjust places, for example, through the displacement of environmental risks, the marginalisation of already vulnerable communities, or the erasure of more-than-human worlds. We are especially interested in research that attends to what is made invisible and unmeasurable : forms of harm that resist quantification, or that are actively obscured in dominant planning, policy, and governance frameworks. 

 Format: Oral presentations (15 minutes each + discussion)

 Convenors: 

 Simona Popova, University of Luxembourg

 Delphine Rumo, Aalto University, Finland

 Michael Wittmann, University of Freiburg, Germany

papers.title: Hidden Debris: EndofLife Ships as Demolition Waste
Wasteful (de-)construction geographies and hidden earthly flows
Garbic Soils, Gases and EPA Audits: The Human-Made, Mobile Geologies of the Sunshine Landfill
Inherited Special Deals. Extended Networks of Material Deposition as Architectures of Cross- System Infiltration in Divided Germany

sessions.title: Uneven and Contested Geographies of Data Centers (2)
sessions.description:
In recent years, data centres have become an increasingly common 
subject of public and political debate (Edwards, 2024). The focus and 
criticism in these debates are usually directed at the massive 
consumption of natural resources (Brodie, 2024; Hogan, 2015), their 
effect on local communities (Bridges, 2024), and the concentration of 
geopolitical power – especially with regards to large data centers that 
are currently being planned or built in the context of the hype 
surrounding artificial intelligence. As essential infrastructure 
for living in our increasingly datafied worlds and for organising 
digital capitalism, data centres also raise a variety of geographical 
questions and problems. These range from the conflict-ridden embedding 
of data centres in local environments (Rone, 2024), to their role in 
larger infrastructure constellations (Atkins, 2021; Velkova, 2016), 
urban settings, and geopolitical issues such as digital sovereignty, 
digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019) and data localisation (Rosa, 2022). 
This session brings together conceptual and empirical contributions to 
critical data centre studies and research on the geographies of digital 
infrastructures. Against this backdrop, our session brings together 
contributions from researchers in digital geography, political 
geography, urban studies, political ecology and science and technology 
studies, examining the socio-technical imaginations, material effects 
and geopolitical and infrastructural embeddings of data centres. The 
papers engage with topics such as data centers in the Global South / 
Global Majority regions, contested infrastructuralization of data 
centers in rural and urban areas, the embedding of data centers in 
geopolitical strategies, programs, and conflicts, sociotechnical 
imaginations of AI data centers and social movements mobilizing against 
the data centers.

 Literature Atkins, E. (2021). Tracing the ‘cloud’: Emergent political geographies of global data centres. Political Geography, 86.

 Brodie, P. (2024). Smarter, greener extractivism: Digital 
infrastructures and the harnessing of new resources. Information, 
Communication & Society, 1–20.

 Bridges, L. E. (2024). Competing digital capacities: Between 
state-led digital governance and local data center tradeoffs. 
Information, Communication & Society, 27(10).

 Edwards, D., Cooper, Z. G. T., & Hogan, M. (2024). The making of critical data center studies. Convergence. 

 Hogan, M. (2015). Data flows and water woes: The Utah Data Center. Big Data & Society, 2(2).

 Kwet, M. (2022). Digital colonialism and Infrastructure as dept. 
Frontiers in African Digital Research, 65–77.Rone, J. (2024). The shape 
of the cloud: Contesting date centre construction in North Holland. New 
Media & Society, 26(10) 

 Rosa, F. R. (2022). Code Ethnography and the Materiality of Power in Internet Governance. Qualitative Sociology, 45(3), 433–455.

 Velkova, J. (2016). Data that warms: Waste heat, infrastructural 
convergence and the computation trafficcommodity. Big Data & 
Society, 3(2).

sessions.title: Chair's event: A conversation with Professor Jason Arday
sessions.description:
Join this 'fireside chat' between the Chair of Conference Professor Peter Hopkins and Professor Jason Arday, the 2002 Professorial Chair in the Sociology of Education at the University of Cambridge.

 The youngest Black person ever appointed to a professorship at Cambridge, join this session to hear from, and ask questions about, Jason’s experiences of - and research about - race, inequality and education.

 Focusing on how such inequalities are evident in our classrooms and lecture theatres, within our discipline, and within public spaces, this session draws attention to the challenges of addressing patriarchy and confronting the marginalisation and exclusion of queer, trans and non-binary people and knowledges in everyday institutional and public spaces.

 Featuring Dr Rae Rosenberg (University of Edinburgh), Tilen Kolar (Leeds) Dr Rachael Squires (Royal Holloway, University of London), Dr Kate Boyer (Cardiff University) and chaired by Dr Alessandro Boussalem (University of St Andrews).

sessions.title: Micro-Geographies of Spatial Injustice: (Im)mobility and Tourist Practices between Hyper-Full and Hyper-Empty Urban Landscapes
sessions.description:
Urban public space is increasingly shaped by sharp micro-geographies of inequality. In many cities both of the Global North and the Global South, public squares, streets, sidewalks, and crossroads are poised between hyper-full and hyper-empty spatial regimes.

 On the one hand, with the growing role of private actors in shaping forms and functions of public spaces, some central urban areas become increasingly saturated by tourism, consumption-related activities, and neoliberal strategies that foster aestheticisation and homogenization, as well as generating dense atmospheres in which visibility, access, and mobility are shaped by commercial and tourism-driven logics. Although not always conceived for tourism-related activities, big-scale urban regeneration programs or small-scale projects such as street pedestrianization often end up with fostering new flows of people, causing touristification or overtourism.

 On the other hand, peripheral districts are increasingly marked by infrastructural scarcity and spatial degrade, with wide “empty” spaces provided with insufficient street furnishings, weak lighting, lacking services, and minimal social infrastructures. Paradoxically, these voids act as barriers in themselves, generating forms of exclusion that are as material as those produced by overcrowding. In these districts, tourism practices play an ambivalent role, alternatively seen both as a tool of local development, especially within off-the-beaten-track itineraries, and a exclusionary instrument of further segregation that selectively make visible marginal areas trhough exactrivist approaches of slum tourism.

 Thus, this dialectics between void and empty, over and under used, fonctionally hyper-connoted and deprived spaces, hyperturistified and undertouristified can exacerbate micro-geographies of inequality at the urban scale, producing an increasingly fragmented urban patchwork where disparities are intensified and spatially entrenched.

 This session investigates how these contrasting spatial conditions shape everyday experiences of spatial injustice, uneven urban citizenship, and tourist practices as well as what are the emerging practices of adaptation or resistance. The focus is especially on bodies navigating margins (women, children/the elderly, gender-nonconforming people, people with disabilities, racialised subjects etc.) whose mobility patterns and visibility in public spaces are differently constrained and shaped by overcrowded touristified cores and infrastructural voids. 

 We invite contributions that analyse how such micro-geographies emerge, how they are negotiated, and how they reproduce (or challenge) contemporary urban power structures.

 Possible themes include (but are not limited to):

 •⁠ ⁠Inclusive/exclusionary Walk-ability and socio-spatial inequalities: spaces, patterns and methods of analysis

 •⁠ ⁠Overtourism/undertourism

 •⁠ ⁠Dialectics of hyper-full and hyper-empty spaces in the contemporary city 

 •⁠ ⁠Dialectics Global North/Global South; Centre/Periphery and their differences in terms of access and people mobility

 •⁠ ⁠Dialectics between formal and informal urbanism in shaping new forms of mobility and emerging everyday practices

 •⁠ ⁠Spatial injustice and everyday practices of resistance

 •⁠ ⁠Gendered and embodied experiences of fear and safety

 •⁠ ⁠Barriers and limits: from infrastructural scarcity to sensory atmospheres

 •⁠ ⁠Neoliberal place-making and the aesthetic governance of central districts

 •⁠ ⁠Peripheral emptiness as a form of exclusion or “slow violence”

 •⁠ ⁠Methodological approaches to studying micro-geographies of mobilities (walking methods, sensory ethnographies, visual analysis, participatory mapping, etc.)

 •⁠ ⁠Comparative perspectives across different national/urban contexts

 •⁠ ⁠Creative, performative, or activist practices of resistance

sessions.title: Everyday Practices and Public Life in Urban China (3)
sessions.description:
Cities in China are in the midst of a profound, ongoing transition. Intertwined with macroeconomic and demographic shifts, this transition is grounded in changing urban landscapes and deeply felt in the rhythms of urban life. It is reshaping people's aspirations—regarding work, family, gender and ageing—also their everyday practices and embodied experiences, from evolving cultures of consumption and leisure to new ways of spending time, seeking connection, and building relationships in the city. In this sense, public space provides a compelling site for examining everyday urban life under these conditions, where multi-layered changes, challenges, encounters, and coexistence are juxtaposed, negotiated, reconfigured and lived.

 This session seeks to open a conversation about how to study, think, and write about Urban China through the lens of everyday practices and public life. It brings together interdisciplinary and empirically grounded research that foregrounds ordinary, mundane, and often overlooked dimensions of everyday life through which individuals navigate and negotiate urban change. By centring these lived experiences, the session demonstrates how close attention to everyday life generates conceptual insights and opens up methodological possibilities for urban geography.

sessions.title: Disability and Cycling: critical reflections, lived experiences, policy and practice
sessions.description:
This session will launch, share ideas from, and respond to, a forthcoming 2026 Special Issue of the Open Access journal Active Travel Studies on Cycling and Disability. This contains over twenty contributions ranging from more traditional long form academic articles to lived experience pieces and policy-focused case studies, broadly sharing a critical approach to active travel rooted in disability studies. Perspectives in the Special Issue come from authors inside and outside academic and across the world (e.g. Indonesia, Japan, Ireland, Australia), with topics including, for example, reflections on racialised exclusions of Disabled cyclists, quantifying and mapping barriers to Disabled cyclists’ use of the cycle network, and analysis of experiences of cycling as a Disabled family group.

 To make this session more accessible, it will be hybrid will incorporate short pre-recorded contributions, for instance from lived experience authors unable to attend the conference, who have expressed interest in their voices being heard through such a contribution. The panel will include in-person contributors to the special issue and session co-organisers, who will speak to the themes of the special issue, and bring different perspectives (academics, Disabled cyclists, practitioners and policy-makers, etc.) into conversation. Those attending the session will gain insight into the latest work in this under-researched but rapidly developing field, including crucially the perspectives of Disabled people who cycle or who would like to cycle.

sessions.title: Violent Transitions: Environmental Crime, Energy Justice, and Resistance in Latin America
sessions.description:
Latin America's pivotal role in the global energy transition—hosting the "Lithium Triangle", vast wind corridors, and extensive hydropower networks—has generated a disturbing parallel phenomenon: the proliferation of environmental crimes committed in the name of sustainability. As multinational corporations and state actors accelerate renewable energy development across the continent, they are perpetrating systematic violations against Indigenous and rural communities that expose the violent underbelly of the "green economy". 

 This panel examines how environmental crimes are embedded within energy transition projects in Latin America, engaging with green criminology, political ecology, and decolonial perspectives. We focus on the criminal dimensions of renewable energy development: from the de facto theft of water and land through lithium brine extraction in Chile, Argentina, and Bolivia, to the violent repression of anti-dam movements in Colombia and Mexico, to the criminalisation of Indigenous leaders resisting wind farms in Oaxaca and La Guajira. 

 Drawing on emerging scholarship in environmental crime and violence (Goyes et al., 2017; Scheidel et al., 2020), this session interrogates how state-corporate alliances use legal and extra-legal mechanisms to secure access to territories and resources for "green" extraction. We examine how paramilitary groups have emerged around hydroelectric dam construction in Colombia (Martínez & Castillo, 2016); how lithium mining generates "green grabbing" that replicates colonial extractivism (Mejía-Muñoz & Babidge, 2023); and how wind energy projects trigger police repression and death threats against community defenders (Business & Human Rights Resource Centre, 2022). 

 The panel asks: How do energy transition projects function as sites of environmental criminality? What mechanisms enable state violence, corporate impunity, and the criminalisation of dissent? What actors—such as police forces—should be incorporated in the analysis of energy transitions? How do communities resist these green crimes, and what alternative frameworks of energy justice are emerging? 

 We invite papers engaging with: (1) Lithium and copper extraction, including water theft in the Atacama, Salta/Jujuy, and Uyuni; corporate regulatory capture; and criminalisation of Indigenous water defenders. (2) Hydropower violence, including paramilitary activities, extrajudicial killings of activists, and forced displacement. (3) Wind and solar projects, examining police repression, death threats against leaders, and "green grabbing" violating FPIC. (4) Theoretical frameworks conceptualising "energy crimes" and "green violence", exploring intersections of environmental crime, coloniality, and extractivism, or comparing renewable energy crimes with palm oil, mining, and forestry. (5) Resistance and justice, including community-led documentation, the Escazú Agreement, Indigenous energy sovereignty, and gendered environmental violence.

papers.title: Violent Transitions: Naming Structural Violence in Latin America's Renewable Energy Projects
Feminised ecologies and Hyper-masculine Infrastructure - Extractivism and Nation building in Kashmir.
Critical Materials and Green Crimes: Slow Violence and the Making of Sacrifice Zones in  Chile’s Copper–Lithium Frontier
A critical criminology of green extractivism
Environmental protection and sustainable development: An Analysis of the Lithium Exploration Project in the Vale do Jequitinhonha and Mucuri

sessions.title: Food Geographies Research Group Annual General Meeting (AGM)
Social Infrastructure and the Making of Just Places (3)
sessions.description:
This proposed session marks the third consecutive year in which we have advanced a conference theme focused on social infrastructure, reflecting a sustained and growing commitment within geographical scholarship to understanding its central role in addressing social and spatial inequalities. Responding directly to the RGS 2026 conference theme, Geographies of inequalities: towards just places , the session examines how social infrastructure shapes, sustains, and challenges spatial inequalities, and how it contributes to the making of more just places. Social infrastructure, traditionally understood as the spaces, services, institutions, and networks that support social life, plays a crucial role in structuring access to care, education, health, participation, safety, and belonging (Klinenberg, 2018; Latham & Layton, 2019). Its distribution, governance, and everyday use are increasingly uneven, reflecting and reinforcing broader inequalities related to class, race, gender, age, disability, migration status, and place (Marmot, 2015; Landman, 2019; Sezer & Van Melik, 2023). These inequalities are often intensified in contexts of crisis, including pandemics, climate change, austerity, and displacement, where social infrastructure can act both as a buffer against harm and as a site where exclusions are reproduced (Renner et al., 2024).

 At the same time, social infrastructure can function as a critical arena through which claims for social justice are articulated, negotiated, and contested, and through which alternative, more inclusive futures are imagined (Aelbrecht & Stevens, 2019; Klinenberg, 2018). This session invites papers that critically explore how social infrastructure operates across different spatial contexts, including urban, rural, and digital settings and at multiple scales, from the everyday to the institutional and policy level.

 Aligned with the Participatory Geographies Research Group, the session welcomes theoretical, empirical, and methodological contributions that engage reflexively with questions of power, ethics, representation, and responsibility in researching social infrastructure and justice (Pain, 2004; Kindon, Pain & Kesby, 2007). We encourage interdisciplinary perspectives and global case studies that illuminate both the potentials and limits of social infrastructure in advancing social change within and beyond the academy.

 We invite contributions that explore (but are not limited to) the following themes:

 - Social infrastructure as a foundation of spatial justice
- Inequalities in provision, access, and use of social infrastructure
- Social infrastructure and lived experience
- Power relations, governance, and institutional arrangements
- Social infrastructure in times of crisis and transition
- urban, suburban, rural, coastal, and peripheral settings, and at neighbourhood, city-regional, national, and transnational scales.
- Digital and hybrid social infrastructures
- Measuring, mapping and representing inequality
- Ethics, reflexivity, and responsibility in research and practice 

 This session encourages global perspectives and welcomes interdisciplinary contributions combining theoretical insights, empirical research, and case studies. Papers engaging at the intersection of social and cultural geographies and built environment disciplines, including architecture, landscape architecture, planning, and urban design, are particularly encouraged.

 References 

 Not included here.

papers.title: From Elite Practice to Public Service? Ballet and public Dance Conservatories as Social Infrastructure in the Parisian Suburbs

sessions.title: Independent Geographers:  Between the Centre and the Periphery of Geographical Knowledge
sessions.description:
This session is an effort to establish a network for independent geographers, aiming to provide support and highlight issues such as recognition by the broader academic community and access to financial and social support mechanisms. It aligns with ongoing initiatives to foster inclusivity within academia and at the RGS, particularly through efforts focused on equality, diversity, and inclusion, as well as the advancement of decolonial, multi-, and interdisciplinary knowledge production.Despite these efforts, independent scholarship remains frequently overlooked, in part due to ambiguities around its definition and the absence of both formal and informal support structures for independent scholars. “Independent scholar” has become an umbrella term encompassing a wide range of meanings, referring to those working under flexible or precarious conditions. The term “independent scholar” usually refers to a scholar without a permanent job, which also implies a lack of a regular wage and institutional affiliation, and as a result, a lack of recognition for their work.Currently, academia experiences an unprecedented crisis due to various challenges, such as a declining number of permanent jobs, increasing redundancies among staff, departmental closures or mergers, increasing socio-political pressure, and the rise of new technologies, which particularly affect younger academics who work under precarious conditions.Independent scholarship is also expected to receive more attention due to changes in how academic knowledge is produced and disseminated through more flexible ways, both in academic and non-academic, and institutional and non-institutional settings. This involves using more inclusive research designs and methods to actively incorporate communities and people in research projects. There has also been a recent interest in academia to open it up to broader society through initiatives such as open-access or alternative forms of publication.In this context, the number of independent scholars and geographers who meet this broad definition is expected to increase. This session is open to all geographers who believe they fit within this framework and aims to bring them together to discuss issues, including but not limited to:

 - A lack of recognition and the inability to get credentials from the wider academic community
- A lack of access to databases, journal archives, or information only accessible through institutional email and membership
- Difficulty of having access to funding sources for travel or research
- The independence of academic knowledge and whether independent academic knowledge can be produced outside academia.

 If you are interested in presenting your papers/ideas in the session, please send your abstract of no more than 250 words and your information (name, surname, email address, and institutional affiliation -if any-) by 20 February 2026 to Basak Tanulku at tanulkub@gmail.com. The session is planned to be online, and if there is sufficient interest, the organiser will apply for a hybrid format.

sessions.title: Area Panel: The afterlives of geographical research
sessions.description:
What does it mean for research to ‘end’? Beyond the closing of a funding account, the archiving of a website and storage of data, and the move to some ‘new’ project, topic, or paper, what endures from research after it has ‘ended’? Whilst research training has long recognised, and sought to account for, the messy and often unpredictable nature of research, this has tended to foreground questions of research design, ethical conduct, and careful planning. The closing stages of the lifecycle of research have received less critical reflection, often being framed in terms of dissemination pathways, public engagement, and the longue durée of impact work and relationships. Yet the lingering after-effects and influences of geographical research in the world cannot be easily reduced to these pathways alone. This panel seeks to explore how research may end and how it may endure, what remains in the messy afterlives of projects that take unexpected turns after they have officially ‘ended’, and what ethical questions are raised when we take seriously the afterlives of research.

 Drawing on Area ’s work to advance discussions of ethics, methodological innovation, and the life cycle of research, this panel addresses these topics through discussion of the ‘afterlives’ of research. By ‘afterlives’ we point to not only how research is met, represented, and responded to in the world beyond academia, often in unanticipated and sometimes uncomfortable ways, but also to the question of whether, and how, research ‘ends’. Afterlives might encompass the unforeseen weaponisation of research for a range of interests contrary to expectations, the unanticipated return of past work, concepts, or findings to influence current issues, or of research taking on new directions and connections with civil society, business, or social groups unforeseen at the point of ‘doing’ the research. How do researchers account for and manage these relations, and how do the afterlives of research come to influence and effect research practice? Equally, the requirement of funding agencies and institutions to store research data, materials, and outputs in a range of repositories opens up questions over the future use of research materials. In a context of rapidly expanding AI engagement across the University sector, the question of how such repositories and materials can, and will, be used in the future is a pressing one. 

 The panel will explore the following questions: 

 - How do geographers attend to the ‘afterlives’ of their research?
- What are the ethical challenges that the ‘afterlives’ of research pose for geographers?
- How are the ‘afterlives’ of research envisioned, planned for, and managed in the process of developing research? Is it possible, or even desirable, to plan for the ‘afterlife’ of research?
- In a context of expanding AI integration, how can geographers navigate demands for data to be available for diverse and often unknown future uses?
- What are the effects of the lives that our research takes on beyond academia on our experiences as geographers? What emotional and other investments are made in shaping the afterlives of research and how can we account for these?

sessions.title: Critical Climate Geographies: Unsettling the Discipline (2): Epistemologies
sessions.description:
Parsons et al. (2025) recently reminded us that “climate change is everywhere within geographical scholarship. Yet, it is not equally everywhere.” This tension sits at the heart of what it means to engage in critical climate geographies today. Climate change permeates the discipline, yet the power relations, epistemic architectures, and political imaginaries that shape it often remain unexamined or unevenly articulated. In line with the inequalities theme of this years’ RGS-IBG Annual Conference, this session takes seriously the provocation that to understand climate change and its related inequalities, we must also unsettle the discipline itself - its assumptions, its boundaries, and its inherited ways of knowing.

 Building on catalytic interventions across human geography (e.g. Parsons et al. 2025; Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Liverman, 2024; Sultana 2022; Mahony & Hulme 2018; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010), we foreground the diverse trajectories that have pushed climate scholarship into new conceptual and political terrain. The aforementioned articles collectively map a field in which climate epistemologies, decolonial critique, multi‑scalar governance, and speculative climate futures have become central to understanding how climate change is made and remade through power, knowledge, and lived experience.

 Within this expanding geographical landscape, scholarship on climate epistemologies has interrogated the politics of knowledge production, revealing how climate expertise is constructed, circulated, and contested (Mahony and Hulme 2018; Liverman, 2024; Parsons et al. 2025). Decolonial and anti‑racist climate geographies have exposed how colonial legacies, racial capitalism, and structural inequalities underpin climate vulnerability and shape global policy imaginaries (Liverman, 2024; Parsons, 2024; Sultana 2022; Yusoff 2018; Baldwin 2016). Meanwhile, work on climate governance, security, and multi‑scalar politics has traced the entanglements of climate change with human security, adaptation, and state power (Rothe et al., 2025; Kythreotis et al., 2024; Westman, 2024; O’Brien 2012), while analyses of anticipatory and speculative climate futures highlight how imaginaries of socio‑ecological transformation shape political action and the horizons of possibility (Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Cox, 2024; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010).

 We invite papers that support, extend, challenge, or disrupt these trajectories. Contributions may address climate epistemologies and co‑production; decolonial, feminist, and anti‑racist approaches; digital and algorithmic climate governance; climate justice, migration, and security; embodied and affective climate geographies; urban and multi‑scalar governance; or speculative and transformative climate futures. We particularly welcome interdisciplinary, reflexive, and methodologically experimental work – ethnographic, creative, digital, speculative, or collaborative – that pushes the boundaries of what climate geography can be.

 If critical climate geographies are to meet the political and epistemic demands of the present moment, they must continue to unsettle the discipline: its categories, its hierarchies, and its claims to authority. This session aims to convene that unsettling.

sessions.title: Energy inequalities and home retrofit (1): Equity in Practice - Advice, Access, and Retrofit Pathways
sessions.description:
Rapid increases in energy prices, compounded by the ongoing cost-of-living crisis, have created unprecedented challenges for households, with energy-related inequalities reaching record levels. These issues are attracting increasing academic and policy attention, given their profound consequences for health and well-being, as well as their wider implications for environmental sustainability and social justice.

 Existing research has documented how energy inequalities manifest across multiple scales - within homes, between regions, and across continents, highlighting disparities between different social groups (e.g. Bouzarovski & Simcock, 2017; Petrova & Simcock, 2017). Additional work emphasises the need for place-based and relational approaches to meeting energy reduction targets (Marsden et al., 2025, Middlemiss et al. 2024). 

 The provision of energy advice and home improvements through retrofit are widely recognised as critical interventions for reducing energy demand, improving affordability, and supporting decarbonisation (Bouzarovski et al. 2025; Sugar et al. 2025). Yet implementation and accessibility remain uneven, raising pressing social justice questions about who benefits from energy advice and retrofit schemes and who is left behind (e.g. Charles et al., 2025). 

 This session invites international contributions that critically examine the role of energy advice provision and home retrofit practices, specifically for tackling energy inequalities. We aim to foster discussion and generate new insights into how these interventions can shape fairer and more equitable energy futures. 

 Suggested areas may include (but are not limited to):

 - Innovative and emerging approaches to delivering household energy advice and retrofit interventions aimed at reducing energy inequalities

 - Challenges and opportunities for equitable access to advice and retrofit, including best practice examples and case studies 

 - Community-led initiatives and grassroots responses in the design and delivery of energy advice and retrofit 

 - Policy and governance frameworks for progressing energy equity 

 - The role of governing actors and cross-sectoral/collaborative partnership networks in energy advice and retrofit delivery

 Due to exceptional demand, this is Session 1/3.

papers.title: Home energy retrofit advice in the UK: Towards equitable provision
From advice to advocacy: collective assemblies and the governance of energy inequalities
Energy precarity no more? The importance of grounded and personalised support to accompany households through home energy retrofit programs

Energy equality through community-led renewables and retrofit

sessions.title: Infrastructures of Social Reproduction and Political Subjectivation: Housing, Class, and Urban Inequalities (1)
sessions.description:
In the context of intersecting housing, climate, and cost-of-living crises, access to basic socio-technical infrastructures—water, energy, mobility, and telecommunication as well as housing itself—has become increasingly precarious for many. Yet these infrastructures are central for maintaining social reproduction understood as the everyday work of sustaining life and labour power (see, e.g., Gillespie and Hardy 2021; Madden 2025; Meehan 2025). In recent years, scholars have increasingly focused on the crises of social reproduction arising from precarious access to such structures – entailing exhaustion, health issues, and limited participation in wage labor as well as intensifying urban inequalities (see, e.g., Madden 2025; Protschky and Formella forthcoming; Rao 2021). Research shows that alienation and depletion of social reproduction often inhibit solidarity and political action by those affected - due to limited time, power-imbalances between tenants, landlords, unhoused people and service providers, shame for one’s own situation or fragmentation of groups affected by infrastructural precarity (Latocha 2024; Protschky 2023; Reichle and Bescherer 2021; Rinn et al. 2022). While precarious access to infrastructures of social reproduction thus effectively impedes engagement against this precarity, scholars also point to venues for enabling political action, including practical support in reproductive activities, raising class consciousness, and bridging divides between class fractions (Gehriger 2025; Uhlmann 2023).

 This curated session invites contributions that take seriously the material and political consequences of infrastructural precarity for reproductive life and political subjectivation. Bringing Social Reproduction Theory into dialogue with critical housing and infrastructure studies, we ask how housing and infrastructure regimes shape the embodied, affective, and relational capacities through which people endure, comply with and resist precarious conditions while organizing everyday life.

 We are particularly interested in contributions that address questions such as:

 - How do precarious housing and infrastructural arrangements reorganise the conditions under which people can sustain everyday life and reproduce labour power?
- In what ways do exhaustion, alienation, shame, time poverty, and fragmentation inhibit solidarity and political organising—and how might they also become grounds for renewed political agency?
- How do finance-dominated housing regimes and state regulation govern social reproduction, and with what classed, gendered, and racialised effects?
- How can we theorise housing as an infrastructure and terrain of social reproduction—linking uneven materiality, lived experience, political governance, and struggle?
- What practices (mutual aid, reproductive support, tenant organising, coalition-building) enable political action and bridge divides between class fractions, such as tenants and unhoused groups?

 We welcome creative, conceptual, and empirical contributions, including but not limited to:

 - Housing and socio-technical infrastructures (e.g., water, energy, mobility, telecommunications) as material conditions of social reproduction 
- Financialisation, commodification, and the governance of social reproduction
- Tenancy, eviction, and the infrastructural politics of (un)housing
- ‘Lived’ housing crises: depletion, alienation, and everyday survival strategies
- Political subjectivation under infrastructural precarity: demobilisation and politicisation
- Class (re)formation through housing relations; reproductive relations and urban inequality
- Feminist political economy perspectives on dwelling, labour power, and urban class experience
- Organising amid crisis: care infrastructures, mutual aid, and bridging fragmented publics
- Comparative and relational approaches across cities/regions; methodological reflections

sessions.title: Mapping Intersecting Inequalities through Relief Maps: Scales, Processes and Lived Experience (1)
sessions.description: This session brings together a set of conceptual, empirical and methodological contributions that centre on mapping intersecting inequalities through the Relief Maps model. Drawing on findings from the ERC-funded INTERMAPS project, the session examines how inequalities are produced, lived, and negotiated across different places, scales, and everyday contexts. The central focus of the session is on mapping intersectional inequalities as lived experience. Relief Maps are mobilised across all papers as a shared feminist and intersectional framework that makes it possible to translate embodied experiences of inequality into spatial forms of analysis. Rather than treating mapping as a neutral act of representation, the session understands mapping as a situated and relational process through which inequalities can become visible. In this sense, the session draws on a multidimensional and relational understanding of place that goes beyond location alone. By engaging with place as the intersection of spatial settings, everyday practices, emotional attachments, and social positions, the papers show how intersecting power relations are materialised differently across homes, urban spaces, sites of activism, environmental contexts, and more-than-human relations. This approach allows for the analysis of inequalities across multiple scales, while remaining grounded in lived experience. By foregrounding intersectional mapping grounded in lived experience, the session contributes to geographical debates on spatial justice and shows how rethinking mapping practices can support the pursuit of more just places.

papers.title: New Developments in Mapping Intersectional Inequalities with Relief Maps
Feminist Approaches to GIS Data Analysis and Visualisation from. More-Than-Human Perspectives
Intersectional class-based housing discomforts: Domestic experiences of inequality
Quantitative approaches to mapping intersecting lived experiences of inequality across space

sessions.title: Mobilising care (1): exploring the imaginaries, expectations, politics and qualities of care in everyday travel
sessions.description:
Whilst time remains the dominant currency when thinking about what matters in everyday travel, it is increasingly clear that for many journeys outside labour relations, articulations of care sit at the heart of much travel decision-making (Ivins and Spinney 2024). As a result, time savings in particular, but also many other aspects of travel such as levels of comfort and safety are prioritized in the pursuit of care, rather than cost savings. Mobility and the ways we choose to travel are therefore central to the cultures and politics of care (Kent 2025). Pushing off from the premise that mobility contributes to the accomplishment of three broad forms caring relation (1) self-care: physical/ emotional work directed towards maintaining/ enhancing our personal wellbeing; (2) familial care: activities directed toward maintaining/ enhancing the wellbeing of others; and (3) environmental care: activities directed toward maintaining/ enhancing local and natural environments (Bowlby 2012:2101; Hobson 2006), this session seeks to expand and explore debates at the intersection of mobility and care. Against this backdrop the session seeks contributions engaging with (but not limited to) a range of themes and questions including: 

 - How is care accomplished through different mobilities; which mobilities (digital and physical) are seen to possess qualities that contribute to care, and which are not? 

 - To what extent does care underpin judgements and decisions about how we travel, what forms of care are evident, and where (self, others, environments) and when (towards immediate or future goals) is care directed? 

 - Caring expectations – what standards of care do we come to expect in relation to different mobilities, and how are these expectations shaped and ratcheted up? What role do different forms of mobility play in shaping standards of care and the qualities that are deemed important? 

 - How do different scales of mobility - encompassing daily travel and global mobility shape imaginaries and accomplishments of care? 

 - How do standards of care desired in mobilities differ within and across different groups in society, how do standards change over time and across regions and countries? 

 - How do particular embodiments arising from gender, age, labour, ethnicity, income, class, etc shape how we perceive and accomplish care, and the forms of mobility we choose to use? 

 - How do attempts to change travel behaviour towards more sustainable alternatives affect people’s ability to enact and accomplish care? What happens when care not be accomplished in ways and to standards of our choosing? 

 - What politics of care arise when considering the roles, contradictions, and potential ‘carelessness’ and harms of different mobilities in caring relations? 

 - How do physical and virtual mobilities intersect in the accomplishments of care?
