sessions.title: Nature, cities, and environmental inequalities: exploring the intersections of environment, human health, and social justice (1)
sessions.description:
The current debate on inequalities has well acknowledged the systemic disparities that cut across health, social, environmental, and economic domains in both the Global South and the Global North (Dorling, 2023). Over the recent decades, there has been growing recognition of the interconnected nature of these systems (Ellis et al., 2025), particularly through the lenses of sustainability and ecological health (Barrett, 2022). Social and economic inequalities frequently translate into unequal access to natural resources, environmental amenities, and essential services, factors that play a critical role in shaping both physical and mental health outcomes (Bratman et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2026).

 Environmental inequalities in access to nature are closely related to broader social and economic inequalities, with clear implications for health and well-being (Fratesi et al., 2024; Roberts et al., 2021). Communities facing persistent socioeconomic disadvantage are more likely to experience limited availability and lower quality of green and blue spaces, alongside higher exposure to environmental degradation and risk. These patterns are shaped by structural factors, including siloed organisation of spatial planning, land-use, and infrastructure investment decisions, which systematically privilege and marginalise different communities (Mayen Huerta et al., 2025). An intersectional and place-based analytical approach is therefore essential to investigate the mechanisms through which environmental inequalities are produced and maintained, and to inform integrated policy responses that address environmental justice and health inequalities holistically (Cohen et al., 2024).

 Meanwhile, with less than four years remaining to deliver the targets of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development under the Global Biodiversity Framework, the gap between commitments and lived practice is becoming increasingly concerning (Obura et al., 2026). The narrowing policy window heightens the need for evidence-based research to inform place-sensitive interventions, particularly those addressing inequalities rooted in socioeconomic and geographic conditions. In the UK, such disparities are recognised as having implications for long-term national security (Defra, 2026).

 This session seeks to further continue an interdisciplinary dialogue on the intersections of nature, human health, and equality, exploring both the challenges and opportunities. We welcome theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions that address, but are not limited to, the following themes:

 - The role of nature-based solutions, including green and blue spaces, in reducing health inequalities
- Innovative and community-based approaches to urban development and biodiversity integration
- Urban planning, environmental justice, and policies promoting equitable access to nature
- The impacts of climate change and environmental degradation on health inequalities
- Intersectional perspectives on environment, health, and social justice
- Digital technologies, AI, and smart-city tools in mapping and addressing health inequalities
- Nature, care, and social infrastructure in marginalised communities

papers.title: Uneven Ecologies of Transition: More-than-Human Environmental Justice on a Post-Industrial Waterfront
Planning for inclusivity in decision-making about urban green and blue spaces: a realist review of the involvement of communities
A Dose of Nature: Building a Natural Health Service in North Yorkshire's protected landscapes
Mapping inequalities in the urban tree canopy cover of the UK

sessions.title: Planning for Just Ocean Spaces
sessions.description:
Ocean space is a frontier for conservation and development in the context of expanding economic activities and increasing geopolitical dynamics. Over the last two decades or so, marine spatial planning (MSP) has gained traction as a key approach to managing these pressures. However, MSP is generally working with conventional, Euclidean notions of space that may not be well-suited to the task in hand (Jay, 2012). Contemporary spatial theorising provides new ways of conceiving of ocean space (Anderson & Peters, 2014) that may benefit MSP efforts by enabling it to move beyond simplified rational constructions of planning and deal with the complexities of ocean governance. 

 In this session we will discuss the ways in which marine planning has engaged with geographical and spatial theory, and debate the ways that MSP can be a response to the dynamic characteristics of the sea (Boucquey et al, 2016; Frazão Santos et al. 2020; Jay, 2018, 2019; Schlüter et al, 2020). In particular we will focus on how alternative conceptualisations can bring out new insights for MSP practice, such as on issues of justice, equity and fairness in the oceans, ocean development, use of public space, challenges such as displacement, and opportunities such as co-existence.

 Panel Discussants Stephen Jay Honorary Reader in Marine Planning, University of Liverpool; Ben McAteer, Queens University Belfast, and; Tim Stojanovic, University of St Andrews will reflect on multiple research projects, invite audience debate, and discuss the engagement between geography and planning theory and practice. 

 Bibliography: 

 Anderson, J. & Peters, K. (eds) (2014) Water Worlds: Human Geographies of the Ocean, Routledge, London, 196pp

 Boucquey, N., Fairbanks, L., St. Martin, K., Campbell, L. & McCay, B. (2016) The ontological politics of marine spatial planning: assembling the ocean and shaping the capacities of ‘community’ and ‘environment’, Geoforum , 75, 1-11. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.geoforum.2016.06.014

 Frazão Santos, C., et al. (2020) Integrating climate change in ocean planning. Nature Sustainability , 3(7) 505–516. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41893-020-0513-x 

 Jay, S. (2012) Marine space: manoeuvring towards a relational understanding, Journal of Environmental Policy and Planning, 14(1) 81-96. https://doi.org/10.1080/1523908X.2012.662383 

 Jay, S. (2018) The shifting sea: from soft space to lively space. Journal of Environmental Policy and Planning 20(4) 450-467. https://doi.org/10.1080/1523908X.2018.1437716 

 Jay, S. (2019). Measured as the water flows: the striated and smooth in marine spatial planning. Maritime Studies 19, 255–268. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40152-019-00158-x 

 Ritchie, H., McElduff, L. (2020) The whence and whither of marine spatial planning: revisiting the social reconstruction of the marine environment in the UK. Maritime Studies 19, 229–240. https://doi.org/10.1007/s40152-020-00170-6
 Schlüter, A., Van Assche, K., Hornidge, A. K., & Văidianu, N. (2020). Land-sea interactions in marine spatial planning: Redefining the role of coastal communities and environmental flows. Marine Policy 121, 104104. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.marpol.2020.104104

sessions.title: Engaging Everyday Spatialities: Mixed Methods for Visual, Narrative, and Designerly Approaches to Spatial Knowledge
sessions.description: This roundtable addresses the need for mixed methods to expand our understanding of mapping practices across disciplines. Moving beyond conventional cartographic approaches, it brings together perspectives that integrate visual techniques (such as GIS and critical cartography) with non-cartographic forms of mapping, including ethnographic and narrative practices, recognising that maps are never neutral but actively shape what—and whose—realities become visible. Our discussion will examine how these various approaches to mapping can and cannot be combined to produce rich spatial knowledge that represents everyday spatialities. How can these distinct approaches to mapping be combined to represent the positionalities of different stakeholders, hiding, revealing, and confronting what are, in fact, dynamic social and ecological relations? In what ways does mixing methods have the capacity to resolve the issue of particular communities, features, and territorialities being excluded? The roundtable will query how mixing methods can both enable and challenge researchers to combine quantitative and qualitative data to capture lived, embodied, and relational dimensions of space that are typically neglected in traditional cartography, yet are essential to just representations of everyday spatialities. Building on the collaborative work of the Leiden-Delft-Erasmus PortCityFutures Thematic Research Group “Mixed Methods and Design Strategies", the roundtable aims to foster an interdisciplinary dialogue among human geographers, anthropologists, and spatial scientists on innovative ways of multi-dimensional mapping, foregrounding questions of representation, power, ethics and responsibility in how spatial knowledge is produced.

sessions.title: Active Mobility to School: Geographies of Inequality, Justice, and Everyday Urban Movement (1)
sessions.description: Children’s journeys to and from school sit at the intersection of three global crises affecting youth: preventable road collisions, rising physical inactivity and worsening well-being. Yet research and policy remain fragmented across transport planning, public health, education and governance, with limited cumulative evidence on how to deliver andscale interventions in real cities. This session positions Active Mobility to School (AMTS), including walking, cycling andwheeling, as a developmental nexus that shapes autonomy, social inclusion and long-term travel habits, while influencing health and safety outcomes. We aim to convene an international forum that integrates social sciences, urban policy, health, and transport engineering. Through evidence from the Global North and South, this session brings transport geography into direct dialogue with debates on inequality and justice, focusing on AMTS, by examining how transport systems, policies, and street environments unevenly shape children’s mobility opportunities across different urban and regional contexts and identify practical pathways for institutionalisation and equitable scale-up.

papers.title: Negotiating independent mobility: road safety and everyday journeys for children with SEND
Whose Walk Counts? Active Mobility to School and Transport Justice in China’s Ecological Relocation
Drawing, Walking, Mapping Ways to School: Participatory and Arts-based Approaches to Children’s Everyday Mobilities

sessions.title: Rethinking Privilege in Transnational Migration from the Perspective of the Global South
sessions.description:
Privilege is about power, (in)equality and (in)justice (McIntosh, 2012). In transnational migration studies, privilege is commonly defined by the capacity to move across borders, migrants’ everyday embodied experiences, changes in structural positioning and meso- and macro-level migration infrastructures (Cranston & Duplan, 2023; Duplan, 2023; Hannam et al., 2006). Building upon existing debates, privilege is shaped through intersections of class, ethnicity, gender and the stratified power of states often rooted in colonial and postcolonial histories. As such, privilege is neither fixed nor universal, but geographically and temporally contingent, produced through specific socio-political contexts and spatial hierarchies (Chattoraj, 2025; Duplan & Cranston, 2023; Kunz, 2016). 

 While insightful, existing scholarship remains anchored in a Global North-centric teleology, leaving the nuanced power dynamics emerging within the Global South comparatively underexplored (Bommes & Morawska, 2017; Fiddian-Qasmiyeh, 2020; Pisarevskaya et al., 2020). This analytical bias also risks obscuring shifting global power and migration landscapes in which certain Global South countries, including BRICS states, are increasingly positioned as regional or global hubs. Consequently, privilege in these contexts may be reconfigured through domestic socio-political transformations within Global South countries as well as through evolving geopolitical relationships among them.

 This session invites geographers to rethink privilege in transnational migration from the perspective of the Global South, foregrounding its relational and context-sensitive character. Rather than treating the “Global South” as a fixed category, the session approaches it as a theoretical intervention and epistemic standpoint that helps challenging Western-centric assumptions in existing debates on privilege in transnational migration. This session welcomes theoretical, empirical and methodological contributions regarding, but not limited to, the following themes:

 - Unequal capacities for mobility in the Global South
- Passports, visas and citizenship as privilege-making regimes
- Racialised and nationalised hierarchies of privilege within Global South destinations
- Privileged and marginalised migrant categories in Southern contexts
- Gendered privilege in Global South migration
- Migrant agency and the strategic negotiation of privilege
- Postcolonial legacies of privilege in global mobility
- Privilege in Southern migration amidst globalisation or anti-globalisation

papers.title: Nigerian International Students and their Families Navigate Privilege and Precarity Across Transnational Social Fields
New Expatriates in the Global South: Privilege Negotiation for Taiwanese expatriates in Vietnam

sessions.title: Geographies of inequalities and public policy for urban vulnerable populations in Latin America (2)
sessions.description:
The lives of vulnerable populations in Latin American cities are shaped by deeply rooted social, economic and political inequalities. This session seeks to advance a collaborative and policy-oriented conversation about how public policy can enable more just urban environments for those experiencing vulnerability and living with disadvantages. Building on scholarship about urban informality, everyday resilience and (creative) responses to marginalisation (Fundación IDEA, 2017; Fernández et al., 2023), we welcome empirical and theoretical contributions that examine how public policies actively shape, intensify or mitigate urban vulnerability, and how vulnerable populations respond to, negotiate, comply with or contest these policy interventions in their everyday lives. We also welcome work that critically examines the limits of existing policy frameworks (e.g., social protection, housing, climate resilience, informal economy) and explores the institutional, financial, and bureaucratic barriers and enablers to effective and actionable policy change. 

 This session builds on the earlier “Creative Vulnerabilities in Latin America” panel and workshop (RGS-IBG 2025) and seeks to move the debate further into public-policy grounds, bringing together theory and practice to reflect on policy design, implementation and lived outcomes.

papers.title: Urban Wellbeing at the Margins: Structural Barriers to Wellbeing-Oriented Urban Policy in Lima, Peru
Knowledge Economies and Inequality: Rethinking Innovation-Led Growth in Medellín

sessions.title: “Species and Spaces of Learning”: more-than-human dimensions of learning and children's geographies
sessions.description:
This work-in-progress session invites early career researchers to share research exploring more-than-human dimensions of learning and children's geographies. We are particularly interested in research examining how children, young people, families, and educators relate to plant and animal species and spaces across formal schooling, informal learning environments, alternative education settings, and community-based pedagogies.

 Situated within the conference theme "Geographies of Inequalities," this session provides a space for querying the boundaries of educational research. We invite submissions that grapple with the methodological, ethical, and theoretical frictions of working with more-than-human worlds that resist easy categorisation.

 For researchers exploring transdisciplinary approaches to the more-than-human entanglements shaping spaces of learning, this session offers space and opportunity to think alongside others engaged in similar work. We welcome contributions across transversal themes, including: spatializing agency (how non-human actors shape educational geographies); inequalities of access (who or what is included or excluded from "legitimate" learning spaces); methodological frictions (doing research with rather than on participants); decentering the human (reimagining curriculum as ecological relation); and affective geographies (messy, embodied encounters in forming environmental subjectivities).

 The session seeks to build community among early career researchers through collaborative discussion of works-in-progress. Presenters will be able to share abstracts, research projects, or thesis excerpts and receive focused peer feedback to advance their work. We also aim to extend this support beyond the conference through an ongoing early career researcher network, fostering future collaborations and collective projects.

sessions.title: Robots, Drones and urban (in)justice: Automation, surveillance, inclusion and new geographies of (in)justice in more than human cities (1)
sessions.description:
Artificial Intelligences increasingly enter already technicised urban spaces, which they can occupy and affect materially through devices such as robots and drones. Progress in AI and the highly networked nature of current urban environments make it possible for robots and drones to operate in close proximity to humans in complex, unscripted environments including public spaces, at home, at work or in urban green spaces. As they do so, they have potential to impactfully reconfigure cities, automating tasks, transforming urban flows, and extending the reach of AIs designed to maximise specific objectives, metrics or utility functions. In this session we will explore the contours of the new forms of (in)justice relevant to emerging entanglements and relations in urban spaces shared by human, non-human and artificial intelligences: How and why are Geographers thinking about and engaging with (in)justice in urban futures which now include humans but also non-human intelligences and agencies embodied in drones and robots? What new forms of injustice may make themselves felt when robots and drones relate in various ways to urban space (or fail to do so)? 

 Papers in this session contribute to theoretical and empirical discussion of urban drones and robots, with attention to the new forms of justice or injustice potentially introduced as embodied AIs transform, augment, optimise or automate urban life.

papers.title: Robotic utopias and evil paradises: Utopia as a method to articulate the futures implied in smart machine strategies
Script Collision: Layered Operations and the Emergence of Urban  (In)Justice in Robotic Space

Robotic Urbanism and the Question of Justice: Towards a Critical Framework for Governing Automated Cities

sessions.title: Geospatial Artificial intelligence methods and applications (1)
sessions.description:
Over the past decade, significant advancements in deep learning have launched a new “AI spring,” reigniting research interest in artificial intelligence within GIScience and quantitative geography. While large language models have been making headlines in newspapers worldwide, a broader range of foundation models and architectures (e.g., CNN, GNN GAN, LSTM, Transformer) have sparked new work in geospatial artificial intelligence (Janowicz et al., 2020; De Sabbata et al., 2023, Mai et al., 2025) and representation learning (Liu et al., 2025; Klemmer et al, 2025). For example, vision models have been applied to street-view imagery to explore urban perceptions and infer socio-economic outcomes (Biljecki and Ito 2021; Law et al 2019). The transformer architecture has been used for natural language processing (Berragan et al., 2023), image analysis and machine vision (Li et al, 2022). Graph neural networks have been employed for geodemographic classifications and question-answering with geographic knowledge graphs (De Sabbata and Liu, 2023; Mai et al, 2020). More recently, geospatial foundation models (Janowicz et al., 2025) have emerged, leveraging large-scale geographic data to support multi-modal geographic analysis.

 This session aims to be a forum to discuss advances, opportunities, and challenges of the use of GeoAI in quantitative geography and geographic information science, showcasing the latest advancements in GeoAI theories, methods and applications within the realm of quantitative geographic studies.
 References

 - Berragan, C., Singleton, A., Calafiore, A., & Morley, J. (2023). Transformer based named entity recognition for place name extraction from unstructured text. International Journal of Geographical Information Science , 37 (4), 747-766.
- Biljecki, F., & Ito, K. (2021). Street view imagery in urban analytics and GIS: A review. Landscape and Urban Planning , 215 , 104217.
- De Sabbata, S., et al. (2023). GeoAI in urban analytics.* International Journal of Geographical Information Science*, 37(12), pp.2455-2463.
- De Sabbata, S., & Liu, P. (2023). A graph neural network framework for spatial geodemographic classification. International Journal of Geographical Information Science , 37(12), 2464-2486.
- Janowicz, K., at al. (2020). GeoAI: spatially explicit artificial intelligence techniques for geographic knowledge discovery and beyond. International Journal of Geographical Information Science , 34(4), pp.625-636.
- Janowicz, et al. (2025). GeoFM: how will geo-foundation models reshape spatial data science and GeoAI? International Journal of Geographical Information Science, 39(9), 1849–1865.
- Klemmer, K., et al (2025). Earth Embeddings: Towards AI-centric Representations of our Planet.
- Law, S., Paige, B., & Russell, C. (2019). Take a look around: using street view and satellite images to estimate house prices. ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST) , 10(5), 1-19.
- Li, W., & Hsu, C. Y. (2022). GeoAI for large-scale image analysis and machine vision: Recent progress of artificial intelligence in geography. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information , 11(7).
- Liu, Y., et al. (2025). Representation learning for geospatial data. Annals of GIS, 31(4), 557–583. 
- Mai, G., et al. (2020). SE‐KGE: A location‐aware knowledge graph embedding model for geographic question answering and spatial semantic lifting. Transactions in GIS , 24(3), pp.623-655.

sessions.title: Exhibiting Nations
sessions.description:
With 2026 marking the 75 th anniversary of the staging of the Festival of Britain (and the 175 th anniversary of the Great Exhibition), the papers in this session examine the interface of two long-standing and occasionally overlapping interests within cultural and historical geography and cognate fields: exhibitions; and nations. On the one hand, geographers have had a long-standing interest in both studying and organising exhibitions: analysing exhibitions and practices of exhibiting; generating exhibition and display content; and curating exhibitions. On the other hand, geographers have also held a long-standing concern with the narration and performance of nations and national identities. At times, the interface of these two themes has been explored, particularly in academic discussions of national museums, exhibitions, festivals, commemorations, and anniversary celebrations. However, practices of exhibiting extend into and show up in many different spheres, becoming incorporated into a vast array of ‘national’ performances, from the production of television history documentaries to the staging of historical pageants and world expositions.

 This session is open to scholars wishing to present on how nations are ‘exhibited’, including but not limited to the following: 

 - Critical studies of exhibitions that have been tied into discourses of the nation, from multinational world fairs/universal exhibitions, to nationally-focused museum and art exhibitions. 
- Studies of different exhibition themes in their entanglement with discourses of nationhood and national identity, including exhibitions relating to geographic, anthropological, historical, artistic and commercial themes. 
- Exhibitions composed in different media and formats, from living museums, to exhibitions of photography, maps, art, and anthropological objects.
- Studies of how nations are exhibited in a range of different media-spaces, from books and television programmes, to more performative modes such as operas, pageants, and plays.
- Studies focussing on the public reception as well as the staging and curation of exhibitions.
- Studies of seemingly banal and everyday performances of nations and national identities through exhibitions of different kinds, as well as traditionally ‘hot’ celebrations and commemorations.
- Studies of different kinds of spaces of exhibition, from village shows and local museums, to national museums and World fairs. 
- Studies exploring questions of justice and injustice, examining the inclusion and exclusion of particular subjects or themes from exhibitions, and the role of minorities, colonies and empires (and their ‘subjects’) in national narratives.

papers.title: Exhibiting Anticolonialism: Fleeting Visions of a Future Nation at the Annual Conferences of the Indian National Congress, 1924-47
Exhibiting Exploration: The Museum of The Royal Geographical Society
Exhibiting at Home: Curating Nation in the Everyday
Stepping through the national geobody: the case of nation-themed miniature parks
Exhibiting Britain, or Exhibiting Wales? The Dolhendre Hill Farm Scheme and the 1951 Festival of Britain

sessions.title: Rural Spatial Justice and Place-Making from a Planetary Perspective (3)
sessions.description:
Processes of rural place-making are closely connected to questions of inequalities and injustice. Disparities in the distribution of wealth, resources and services between rural and urban areas, as well as between rural places, shape trajectories of place development and the identities attached to places. Processes of place-making in turn shape perceptions of spatial injustice, or the unfair treatment, informing a ‘rural consciousness’ through which rural residents interpret politics (Cramer, 2016). Questions of rural spatial justice are also evident in the variegated capacity of individuals to participate in making rural places – a ‘right to the rural’ paralleling the ‘right to the city’. Moreover, approached as a normative concept, rural spatial justice can inspire proactive and progressive place-making (Woods, 2023).

 This session specifically calls for papers that address these dynamics between rural place-making and spatial justice from a planetary perspective. Following principles outlined in Wang et at’s (2025) call for planetary rural geographies, a planetary perspective highlights the making and remaking of rural places within unequal global relations and associated questions of economic and social justice; the more-than-human composition of place and the questions of multi-species justice embedded in rural places; the justice or injustice of knowledge claims for describing, knowing and representing rural places, from colonial scientific legacies to indigenous thinking; and rural place-making with respect to planetary crises, including issues of environmental justice in the remaking of rural places by climate change and in conflicts over the repurposing of rural land for renewable energy, rewilding or carbon forestry, for example.

 As such, we understand place-making as a relational, contested, and uneven process shaped by multi-scalar forces, including climate change, agro-ecological transitions, infrastructure expansion, extractivism, conservation, migration, and food sovereignty struggles. We are particularly interested in how justice claims emerge across scales, and how rural places are made, unmade, and remade through interactions among human and more-than-human actors.
 The session aims to foster dialogue between rural studies, critical agrarian studies, political ecology, and more-than-human geographies, advancing a relational and planetary understanding of rural spatial justice.

papers.title: The relational (Thai) village: re-centring the rural
Creative Place Making as an positive Effect on Globalization in rural Areas - and how to deal with negative perceptions
Scarred Landscapes: Extraction, Tourism, and “Resource Materialities” of Quarries in Wales and England
affiliations: Cardiff University School of Geography and Planning

sessions.title: Authors meet critics: Philippa Williams and Lipika Kamra's Privacy Techtonics: Feminist geopolitics of privacy, WhatsApp and democracy in India
sessions.description:
This panel will include reviews of Privacy Techtonics with responses from the authors.  
 About the book: Today, privacy is more than a personal choice, it is a terrain shaped by power, politics and everyday life. The global dominance of Meta’s WhatsApp and Big Tech’s recent turn towards ‘digital private spaces’ raises important questions about the balance between public and private interests in the digital age. Through a feminist geopolitics of privacy, Privacy Techtonics draws connections between Meta’s imagined private digital future and the everyday reality of surveillance, marginality and resistance. Focusing on India, WhatsApp’s largest market, it shifts attention away from western experiences of digital privacy to centre the ‘digital peripheries’ that have sustained WhatsApp’s expansion and corporate power.

 Privacy Techtonics shows how WhatsApp’s encrypted privacy architectures intersect with data colonialism, state politics and surveillance and social inequalities. It illustrates how privacy – as ideology, design, law and experience – is infused with power, from intimate life and everyday politics to the policies of state governments and the financial strategies of technology companies.

 By challenging western, technocratic and patriarchal notions of privacy as a universal good, Privacy Techtonics argues that privacy is socio-technical and geopolitical process shaped by the contradictory practices and struggles of Big Tech, the state, civil society and citizens. The book concludes that privacy is being used both to support and to erode democracy in today’s digital age.

sessions.title: “Zeitenwende”: Political–Economic Geographies of Militarization and Rearmament in Germany and Finland
sessions.description:
In light of Russia’s war against Ukraine and the dramatically shifting role of the United States in the global configuration of geopolitical alliances, Germany—similar to its EU partners—faces a new geopolitical security environment. In both EU-level foreign and security policy debates and domestic political discussions within Germany, calls for autonomous defense capabilities have become increasingly prominent.

 These emerging geopolitical discourses of (in)security intersect in Germany with a context in which the military has traditionally been viewed with skepticism since the Second World War and in which, particularly since the end of the Cold War, the reduction of arms production (“peace dividend”) has been especially pronounced. Against this background, the globally observable trend toward rearmament and the associated processes of militarization assume a particularly significant form in Germany and are characterized above all by extensive investments in the defense industry.

 The session analyzes these developments through three interrelated contributions. It examines both the discursive processes underpinning the current (re-)militarization of Germany and political-geographical as well as labor-geographical perspectives on their manifestations within the German defense industry.

papers.title: “Troubling Worlds of Work”: Workers’ Experiences of Conversion from Civilian to Military Production
Military geographies in the Sámi homeland in Finland
Emotional Geopolitics of Militarization: A Critical Geopolitical Analysis of Current Debates in the German Bundestag
Political-Geographical Perspectives on the Scaling Up of the German Defense Industry

sessions.title: Wither Human(ity)? (1)
sessions.description:
In a moment marked by militarised borders, racialised policing, authoritarian resurgence, environmental degradation, and genocidal violence, humanitarian language saturates public discourse. Appeals to “humanity” promise universality, dignity, and protection. Yet who is recognised as fully human within these appeals – and who is not? Responding directly to the conference theme Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places , this session examines how unjust places are organised through historically racialised constructions of the human itself.

 Rather than treating the human as a neutral and unquestioned foundation, the papers approach it as a stratified and contested category that distributes life, comfort, responsibilities, and vulnerabilities unevenly across spaces. Inequalities, in this view, are not only economically or institutionally (re)produced, but ontologically grounded.

 The session begins by revisiting more-than-human and new materialist debates that seek to destabilise Western humanism. While these approaches productively challenge anthropocentrism, they often sideline the socio-economic and racial logics that have historically determined who counts as human in the first place. By foregrounding racialisation and political economy, this intervention insists that moving “beyond” the human cannot substitute for confronting its racial foundations.

 Questions of spatial belonging emerge in an analysis of the “politics of comfort”. Home, dwelling, and spatial design appear neutral or technical, yet they presuppose a normative, secure, and propertied human subject. Comfort becomes a spatial technology that renders some bodies properly “at home” while marking others as excessive, out of place, or less-than-human. Inequality is embedded not only in policy but in spatial imaginaries themselves.

 The problem of responsibility then comes into focus. If poststructuralist and posthumanist critiques destabilise reason and subjecthood, on what grounds can ethical obligation be articulated? Rather than returning to human exceptionalism, this session explores whether a critical re-humanisation of geography might reconfigure responsibility in ways that remain reflexive while resisting indifference.

 Finally, drawing on Black radical and decolonial thought, being human is reframed as struggle rather than stable category. Humanitarian and human rights frameworks, far from neutral, can operate as racial ordering devices that distribute life and death along enduring colonial lines. Humanity emerges as a terrain of contestation rather than a settled fact.

 Together, these papers argue that working toward just places requires confronting how the category of the human has been historically produced through racial and colonial hierarchies. This conceptual interrogation forms the foundation for the linked session Wither Human(ity)? (II) , which turns to the concrete sites where these hierarchies are enacted, negotiated, and resisted in practice.

papers.title: The Politics of Comfort: Posthuman Being and the Making of Home
Human Being as Struggle: Toward Insurrectionist Ethics
Responsibility without Vernunft? Opportunities of a re-humanisation of cultural geography
Have we ever been social? On present absences in new materialisms

sessions.title: Investigating Inclusivity: Qualitative Approaches to Understanding Inclusion in Space, Place, and Practice
sessions.description: Inclusivity has become a central ambition in contemporary urban policy and planning, yet the ways in which inclusion and exclusion are experienced in everyday environments remain unevenly understood. This panel includes contributions that examine how qualitative research methods can illuminate lived experiences of inclusivity within urban spaces , with particular attention to public environments such as green spaces, streets, and neighbourhoods.Drawing on emerging research on  inclusive green space access and use , alongside projects exploring  age-friendly urban design and environments for older adults  and  climate justice approaches to nature-based urban heat adaptation , the session will foreground how qualitative approaches—including interviews, participatory methods, ethnography, and co-design—can reveal the complex social, spatial, and cultural dynamics that shape who feels welcome, safe, and able to participate in urban life.While policy frameworks often promote inclusivity as a universal goal, lived experiences of urban environments are shaped by factors such as age, mobility, gender, health, and socio-economic context. Qualitative methodologies provide crucial tools for capturing these situated experiences and for understanding how design, governance, and everyday practices influence access to urban amenities and public life.The panel seeks contributions that critically explore  inclusive design, public space, and urban accessibility , particularly those that foreground underrepresented perspectives or methodological innovation. By bringing together research on green infrastructure, climate adaptation, age-friendly environments, and inclusive urbanism, the session aims to open interdisciplinary dialogue on how cities can be designed and governed to support more inclusive and equitable urban futures.

sessions.title: Repoliticising Inequality: Organised Abandonment, Carceral Violence and Actively Working Towards Abolition (2): Body: struggles and resistance
sessions.description:
This experimental 1-day session at this year's RGS conference, invites creative and conventional contributions that centre around abolitionist understandings of “organised abandonment” (Gilmore 2022, Arefin and Khan 2024, Mei-Singh 2024). Mei-Singh (2024) (drawing on Gilmore 2008 and Bhandar 2018) offers a brief overview of the state of the concept:

 Existing scholarship on organized abandonment conveys how neoliberal state reorganization sacrifices particular places. This results in the flight of economic activity and jobs, environmental degradation, underemployment, the diminishment and privatization of public programs, and the disappearance of entire ways of life, all part and parcel of the disregard of those subjugated by a racial capitalist social order.

 The session(s) invite activists, scholars, artists, community workers and others (at all career stages and from diverse locations) to come together - in ways that are life-giving, creative and transcend academic convention - to continue ‘sharpening our focus of how such abandonment results from plans, as well as abdications of responsibility’ (Gilmore 2023). Arefin and Khan (2024) maintain that organised abandonment is ‘not a passive withdrawal, but an active plot’. They (Ibid:) go on to add that this ‘invites the same precision in our counterplot’.

 Therefore, the session(s) also invite us to continue sharpening our focus on how we counter and unmake organised abandonment. We see this countering and unmaking as ‘actively working towards abolition’ (Kaba nd) and finding, recovering and restoring lifeways that have been forcibly abdicated (Mei-Singh 2024). Sessions include papers and micro-workshop activities that help deepen how we understand organised abandonment and how we resist and restore in the wake of the ‘rupturing of life-giving relations’ (Mei-Singh 2024). This matters because grassroots activists continue to theorise abolition as an ethics, practice, struggle and way of being, that requires the development of “dual power” (Kaba and Ritchie 2021) where emergent and oppositional forms of resistance coexist and interdepend (Brown 2017, Ritchie 2023, Chhibber 2025, see also Hughes 2021). That is:

 understanding, opposing, fighting and unmaking carceral systems, organised abandonment and related ways of being 

 coexist and interdepend with 

 restoring, building and practicing life-affirming systems and ways of being that refuse to use organised abandonment and carcerality as solutions to entrenched social problems (Davis 2003, Kaba and Ritchie 2022, Ritchie 2023, Kass 2025). 

 ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀ ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

 We see this session not as a way of coming to a definitive disciplinary conclusion or resolution on what organised abandonment is but rather as a way of coming together with an openness for whatever emerges, be this contradictions, contingencies or interrelations. We encourage ‘acting and speaking in ways that are not about mastery, but rather about audacity or incompleteness, because [we] don’t think any of us can be complete in our understanding or in our pursuit of freedom on our own’ (Bhattachrya np 2025).

 ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

papers.title: Counter-Plots: A Collage and Thread-Mapping Activity
"This university course can lead to diverse careers in law-enforcement..."
Freedom Dreaming
Reading for refusal as abolitionist practice

sessions.title: Grounding nuclear imaginaries: Placing geographies of nuclear ‘progress’, protest, and care (2)
sessions.description:
The nuclear continues to preoccupy our minds. As energy, weapon, mineral, medicine, and waste, nuclear issues and the social imaginaries that underpin them continue to exert a shaping force on global and local affairs. This session invites scholars and practitioners to attend to these contemporary imaginaries: their modes of production, internal logics and inconsistencies, the possibilities they enable or foreclose, and the forms of power that they underpin. 

 Two questions are of particular interest. The first is the extent to which contemporary nuclear imaginaries are tied to or inherit from ideas, knowledges, and aesthetics of the past. Are these novel formations, or do they bear the traces and traumas (Schwab, 2020) of bygone periods like the Cold War (Faux, 2024)? To what extent are efforts to renew the nuclear (for example the efforts to brand civil nuclear power as ‘green’) an effective strategy in re-ontologising it? At the heart of this is a consideration of the nature of nuclear imaginaries as a potent mode of reproducing historical patterns (and violences) as well as a new set of tools to grasp the specificity of the present. The second centres the relationship between nuclear imaginaries and place. In the last two decades, important ethnographic and historical research have sought to articulate various ‘local’ nuclear imaginaries tied to particular lands, nation-states, governments, and scientific organisations: for example, an American post-Cold War nuclear imaginary (Masco, 2008), British nuclear cultures (Hogg, 2016; Hogg and Brown, 2019; Hughes, 2012), and civic epistemologies of the Indian elite classes (Anderson, 2010; Haines, 2019). Yet, outside of this corpus, scholars note a tendency in popular imaginaries of the nuclear to untether from place. The historian Lisa Yoneyama (1999, 2023) calls this tendency nuclear universalism, where the abstraction of nuclear imaginaries – especially into global imaginaries by way of an aesthetics of apocalypse – obscures the structural violence inflicted upon specific communities and places erased by these imaginaries (Hecht, 2012; Nixon, 2011; Voyles 2015). The disembedding of imaginaries from place has an obscuring function; it is only by concealing the long-term material and ecological burdens of nuclear mining, processing, and waste storage that its inherent promises (e.g. of cheap and endless energy) can be upheld (Pottin, 2024). These are nuclear imaginaries that ironically inhibit imagination itself, making it hard to think otherwise (Deckard, 2022) and posing a challenge to forms of anti-nuclear resistance (Faison and Fields, 2024; Urwin 2025). 

 Taken together, these two questions guide what promises to be a lively exchange on the forms and functions of nuclear imaginaries in yet another supposed ‘nuclear renaissance’. The session turns on the central provocation that it ought to matter where thought takes place and how thought makes place. Only by attending to the worlding capacities of imaginaries might we be able to consider ways to shift them (Carpenter, 2016). We invite papers that consider the recursive relationships between nuclear imaginaries and nuclear places.

papers.title: “The last forty years weren’t a lie!”: exploring contested nuclear imaginaries in British radiation monitoring bunkers
Imaginaries of Nuclear Justice in the Sea of Islands
Safe/unsafe bedrock: the nuclear underground as the site for competing deep time imaginaries
Performing Temporal Irresolution: Art, Temporality and the Rokkasho Reprocessing Plant
Railway, radiation, and ruins: archives of anti-nuclear protest on Arabunna Country, South Australia

sessions.title: Unfuturisms: Unsettling the Rhythms of Planetary Social Life (1)
sessions.description:
Progressive, modern, technological, alternative, utopian, socialist, planetary, radical, ecological, revolutionary, catastrophic, subaltern, sustainable, resilient, more-than-human, speculative, pluriversal, dystopian, in the ruins, from below: we’re awash in futures to come —always yet come— futures that are neither quite delivered upon nor ever quite given up on.

 Across human geography and the humanities and social sciences more broadly, extraordinary amounts of energy have been devoted to critically attending to the future’s unjust and unevenly distributed nature, and to the search for alternative futures in the Anthropocene. Defying pronouncements of the future’s imminent yet slow “cancellation,” the attempt has often been that of reprising and expanding the archive of “futurisms” (across geographies, political traditions, literary genres and forms of activism) to re-open possible futures for a planetary age. 

 And yet, the fundamental problem is that “the future” is not what it used to be (Savransky, 2024, 2025). It was modernity’s faith in progress that enabled one to envisage the future as something one could bring unto oneself, unto others, unto the earth . But in an age of irreversible planetary instability and climate chaos, what was once a source of hope and aspiration, and a problem of anticipation, imagination and redemption, has become precarious when not an outright extortion, a source of fear and dread, a problem of finitude and catastrophe, managed retreat and adaptation (Savransky & Lundy, 2022). 

 This panel is an invitation to gather around a provocation: not to envisage alternative futures but to envisage unfuturisms— alternatives to “the future” as the organising grammar and rhythm of planetary social life. What becomes of the idea of “the future” on an earth rendered forever unstable? What would it take to slip out of the future’s grip and stray out of the uneven and unjust striations through which it continues to dig and groove the terrain? What other rhythms of planetary life —human and more— might be rendered perceptible in the act of giving “the future” a pass?

papers.title: For Now For Ever: Notes on Unfuturism
affiliations: Institute of Language, Literature, and Anthropology (CSIC)

papers.title: Administrative Life After the Future: Staging the Lebenswasseragentur in a Beavered Berlin
Geocidal ethics: what is earth going to be?
Unsettling the Myth of Futurelessness: Living on after the End of the Atomgrad

sessions.title: World Café: At a Distance: Researching urban elsewheres
sessions.description:
With the world becoming increasingly urban, UK Higher Education Institutions have hired academics from diverse global contexts, keen to ensure leadership at the forefront of urban knowledge creation, theory building and pedagogy. Despite these intentions, however, Global North-based academics with knowledge and experience of research and home ‘elsewheres’ find their ability to contribute to curriculum development, theory-building and grant funding limited. 

 This Roundtable is part of a suite of work on Urban Elsewheres, funded by a GW4 grant and aims to establish a community of international researchers who are passionate about using their global expertise to strengthen scholarship on urban challenges. We wish to establish a community with the objectives to uncover the experiences of living in one context and researching elsewhere and collectively address the difficulties of integrating such work, particularly work undertaken in the Global Majority, into research in their institutions. The World Café would revolve around 3 questions:

 - What are the unique opportunities presented by living in one place and researching another?
- What challenges have you experienced as an ‘in-between’ researcher?
- In what ways would having an urban ‘elsewheres’ research network be useful in supporting your work?

 We propose to use a World Café style of engagement to set up 3 “stations” of discussion through which people rotate. This is followed by a plenary reporting back on the discussions and adding further thoughts.

 The World Café would have three core objectives: 

 - To connect and network international urban researchers who are often isolated within their departments or institutions, creating a foundation for future collaborations and potential joint funding applications. 

 - To identify the challenges these scholars face in leveraging, developing, and applying their global expertise within their institutions. 

 - To explore collaborative initiatives that could be developed to address these barriers.

sessions.title: Quantum Geographies / Geographies of Quantum
sessions.description:
Quantum mechanics revolutionised physics and is at the heart of many contemporary technological advancements. Despite occasional proposals to bring quantum mechanics into geographical theorisation (e.g., Peterman, 1994; Smith, 2016), and despite the radical possibilities for reconceptualising geographic concepts including space, time, and objectivity, most quantum social science has emerged from other disciplines. Wendt (2015) proposed a quantum mechanical concept of consciousness that has been applied in international relations (Der Derian and Wendt, 2022), whilst Barad (2007) developed agential realism through interdisciplinary engagement with the philosophy-physics of Niels Bohr. Drawing upon this agential realism, along with Black studies and Caribbean thought, Noxolo’s Black Quantum Creative Geographies (2025) is a welcome new beginning for research on quantum geographies.

 Alongside these disciplinary debates, the Second Quantum Revolution has generated a significant but understudied industry around quantum science and technology, which in the United Kingdom is entangling various spatial dynamics and geographies as well as social, ecological, and economic processes. In Autumn 2025, Kier Starmer and Donald Trump signed a £31 billion ‘Technology Prosperity Deal’ to promote US-UK cooperation in Artificial Intelligence, civil nuclear energy, and quantum technologies. In November of the same year, the Tony Blair Institute for Global Change, released a ‘New National Purpose’ paper stressing the centrality of the UK quantum science and technology industry to national sovereignty and economic growth, to be secured through ‘venture capital statecraft’ (Schwarz 2025). The UK National Quantum Strategy lays out a network of ‘Quantum Hubs’, anticipates the infrastructure for a ‘quantum city’, and implicates global resource and labour supply chains. The ways in which this quantum science and technology industry is being shaped by and altering existing political geographies across a variety of scales deserves attention and requires novel theoretical and methodological approaches to the study of geographies of science and technology (Mahony 2021). 
 In this session, we want to explore the role that quantum mechanics can play in geographical theorisation, geographical research into scientific research and technological development based on quantum mechanics, and the entanglement between the two.

papers.title: Barad’s brittlestars and the question of life
Geographies of Quantum
Quantum Black cultural geographies and the rise of Generative AI
Between Body and Elsewhere: Spatializing Non-Physical Place with Quantum Ontology

sessions.title: Emerging voices in economic geography (2)
sessions.description:
This session invites postgraduate researchers (PGRs) and early career researchers (ECRs) to share emerging research, ideas, and provocations in economic geography. We aim to create a supportive and inclusive space for scholars at the early stages of their academic journeys to showcase work in progress, explore new directions, and connect with others working across diverse traditions and contexts.

 We welcome contributions that engage with any aspect of economic geography, broadly defined. This may include (but is not limited to) research on:

 - Economic inequalities and uneven development
- Digital, platform, and creative economies
- Gender, race, identity and the economy
- Geographies of markets, production, exchange and consumption
- Geographies of money, finance and financialisation
- Labour, work, livelihoods, and care
- Housing, real estate, and the built environment
- Regional, urban, and rural economies
- Sustainability, climate, and green transitions
- Industrial change, innovation, and entrepreneurship
- Informal, alternative, and community economies
- Intellectual histories and futures of economic geography
- Theories and theorising in economic geography
- Methods and methodological innovation in research on the economy

 The session is intentionally open and exploratory. We particularly encourage:

 - Papers based on PhD research or postdoctoral projects
- Works-in-progress or presentation of preliminary findings
- Contributions that experiment with methods, theory, or interdisciplinary approaches
- Submissions from researchers who are new to the RGS–IBG conference or academic conferencing more broadly

 Our goal is to foster constructive discussion, peer support, and confidence-building in a friendly environment. Presenters will be encouraged to share not only findings, but also questions, challenges, and future directions.

papers.title: Searching for a Progressive Industrial Policy around Energy-Intensive Industries in Central Europe
From Local Drought to Global Shock: Quantifying the Amplification of Semiconductor Supply Disruptions through Global Supply Chains
affiliations: Department of Geography, National Taiwan University

sessions.title: Geographies of Intensity (2)
sessions.description:
Intensity appears to be a problem today. The crisis prone present seems to be characterised by too much of the wrong sort of intensity. Claims are made that subjects are too outraged or too disaffected. Whilst some are baited to outrage amid today’s digital economy, others lie flat as they feel a pernicious combination of expectation and economic downturn. Extended periods of stretched out waiting for fragile promises to materialise (Pettit 2023), coexist with hustle cultures of constant activity (Thieme 2025). Calls to foster passionate attachment to work coexist with quiet quitting (Hong 2022). Politics amid the extended end of neoliberalism and as mediated by digital capitalism is characterised as being full of uneasy mixtures of outrage and boredom. Maladies of intensity – from burnout (Han 2015) to overstimulation – appear to serve as the signature affects for a present characterised as at once too much and too little, both too intense and not intense enough. 

 But in addition to being key to diagnosing the present conjuncture in all its overdetermined complexity, intensity can be found across multiple geographies. Value is created through generating intensity, whether in new digital worlds, through branding and hype, or via entertainment forms (e.g. the Blockbuster film). Capitalism has frequently been understood to involve a logic of intensification as value is extracted from life, labour and land. Political forms offer and promise intensity, whether the quietness and disaffection of liberalism or the rage infused enjoyment of right-wing populism (Anderson & Secor 2025). Forms of power work through intensity. Foucault (1977), for example, writes of how sovereignty works through ‘visible scenes of intensity’ as he details the spectacle of the scaffold. Events that are apprehended as crises are mediated through intensities that can change how they become present in everyday life. Mediated in terms of the catastrophic, climate change might be met with boredom and other disaffected feelings, for some subjects. The ‘immigration crisis’ gathers intensities around it, as authoritarian intensities are performed through mediated spectacle of violent raids targeting racialised subjects. At the same time, intensity is central to the lived experience of place. Whether the buzz of a city, the density of a crowd (McFarlane 2016) or stillness of a landscape, particular kinds of good intensity are governed, desired, valued, and experienced. This set of sessions explores the cultures, politics and economies of intensity.

papers.title: Distraction as Urban Sensory Pollution
Intensities of Loss
Violent origins and diffuse impacts: understanding morphing geographies of intensity through the case study of British torture in Ireland

sessions.title: Inclusive Field Teaching
sessions.description: Fieldtrips, and specifically residential fieldtrips, are a fundamental component of geographical teaching and learning. The Royal Geographical Society describe fieldwork as the ‘jewel in the crown of geography’. Fieldtrips at undergraduate level are core learning spaces developing analytical research skills, real-world experiential learning, observation-based knowledge generation, as well as teamwork and cohort building opportunities (Herrick, 2010). Market driven trends have seen the rise in seemingly ‘exotic’ long-haul destinations (McGuinness and Simm, 2005), yet this has been tempered within Geography departments by discussions about the value (both in learning and financial terms) of such trips, and concerns around risk and risk-taking for students and staff (Herrick, 2010). More recently broader debates around decolonising both higher education, and geography specifically, have permeated discussions around the geography fieldtrip, particularly where fieldtrips involve travel to the Global South (Staddon et al., 2021; Noxolo, 2017). It is therefore imperative that both the teaching and educational experience provided by fieldtrips is diverse and inclusive for both students and staff.

papers.title: Rethinking teaching in the field: inclusion and building resilience through experience
affiliations: Department of Geography, King's College London

papers.title: Creative pedagogies for inclusive field trips and assessment in the age of AI

Teachers’ understanding of inclusivity in fieldwork: variations and implications
Flattening Scale in the Field: Rethinking Hierarchy Through Undergraduate Field Teaching

sessions.title: Just parks? Making urban green spaces more accessible and inclusive (1)
sessions.description: Urban green spaces are widely acknowledged as critically important amenities that provide a range of social and ecological benefits. But the provision, scale and quality of these spaces tends to vary considerably between and within different cities. Even in cities where there is adequate provision, green spaces are not necessarily welcoming, safe or usable for many disadvantaged or marginalised groups. The Landscape Geography Working Group and the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group invite papers that address the accessibility and inclusivity of urban green spaces. This co-sponsored session aims to bring together academics working in disparate fields, including leisure studies, urban planning, landscape architecture, urban sociology, political ecology, and health studies, and various strands of geography. The title of the session ‘just parks?’ emphasises that provision tends to be unjust but also suggests that part of the solution may lie in directing attention beyond conventional green spaces. Papers might address the obstacles that restrict access to urban green spaces including physical, financial, administrative and symbolic barriers. However, the session is framed positively, and we also want to include papers that suggest how progressive change may be achieved. We have used the term ‘making’ in the subtitle because we want to explore how urban green spaces might be designed, managed or programmed differently to make them more accessible/inclusive. The co-convenors have worked together after presenting at previous RGS-IBG Conferences and we aim to provide a forum through which future collaborations might emerge.

papers.title: From playing fields to a sporting hub – a template for inclusive greenspace?
Gendered leisure in Iran and the politics of segregation: Justice or state power? The case of women-only parks
‘They just want to have a safe space to express themselves’: a park design typology to promote adolescent inclusion
Designing Tower Hamlets’ Green Grid: reshaping urban green spaces through gender inclusive design
“I personally feel unhappy about it, because ** was a happy place”. Children’s visual reflections on a redesigned urban park

sessions.title: Heat-stress in and Beyond Humans: Critical Planetary Health Perspectives (1)
sessions.description:
Over 2.4 billion workers worldwide are exposed to excessive heat each year, resulting in nearly 19,000 deaths and tens of millions of occupational injuries and illnesses (ILO, 2025). Heat also affects more-than-human life, including the deaths of over 650,000 farm animals during the 2021 Pacific Northwest heatwave and thousands of chickens in India’s 2015 extreme heat events (Huber, 2022). These patterns position heat not simply as environmental exposure but as a lived health condition produced through intertwined climatic, ecological, and socio-economic transformations that unevenly distribute vulnerability across human and other-than-human life.

 This panel foregrounds heat as an embodied and relational health experience. Rather than treating climate change as a distant environmental driver, we approach heat as something encountered through working bodies, homes, infrastructures, care practices, and everyday survival strategies. Heat reshapes hydration, fatigue, sleep, cognition, pregnancy outcomes, and chronic illness, while also altering patterns of disease transmission, microbial activity, and multispecies cohabitation. Health, in this sense, emerges not from isolated bodies but from entangled environments.

 We place these dynamics in conversation with EcoHealth, One Health, and Planetary Health frameworks to examine how well-being and care are (re)produced across human and more-than-human relations (Cole & Deshpande, 2019; Hoogeveen et al., 2023). While these frameworks emphasize interconnected systems, lived experiences of heat reveal uneven burdens shaped by labour precarity, housing conditions, access to care, and social marginalisation. Occupational exposure often restricts possibilities for rest and recovery, making heat a workplace health and safety issue as well as a broader well-being concern (Kjellstrom et al., 2019).
 At the same time, responses to the climate–health nexus frequently remain technocratic and depoliticised, privileging metrics and thresholds over lived experience (Sim & Parsons, 2025). We instead consider heat harms as ethical and relational conditions shaping care, responsibility, and vulnerability across species (Celermajer et al., 2025). This perspective foregrounds everyday coping practices, cultural understandings of heat, and community-based adaptations as central to health. This panel invites papers that ask questions such as (but not limited to):
 - How is heat experienced as an embodied condition across human and more-than-human bodies in specific places and environments?
- In what ways does heat and its related indicators reorganise labouring conditions and occupational health for working bodies, including vulnerable human labour groups and more-than-human workers such as livestock and working animals, particularly where exposure cannot easily be avoided?
- What critical, methodological, or interdisciplinary interventions are needed to move beyond technocratic or Eurocentric framings of heat and health, thus moving towards multispecies just futures?

 As an extended and exploratory conversation, this panel aims to bring together scholars working across environmental, health, labour, and more-than-human geographies to think collectively about heat and its related forms as an embodied, relational, and multispecies condition, and to explore how more just planetary futures might be imagined under conditions of climate change.

papers.title: From Pleasant Weather to Planetary Risk Rethinking Heat in Temperate Contexts
Technologies for and beyond
Heat-stress in more-than-human labouring bodies: A case study from Cambodia
Affective Thermographies: Visualizing Embodied Heat Beyond Thermal Imaging in the Brazilian Amazon

sessions.title: Voluminous Futures (1) Conceptualising futurity through geographies of volume
sessions.description:
Amidst
 intensifying climate volatility, geopolitical contestation, and rapid 
technological acceleration, volume––across air, ice, water, and 
underground strata––have become critical terrains through which actors 
imagine, contest, and enact futures. Far from empty or inert, these 
environments are materially complex, unevenly known, and often only 
partially visible. Their indeterminacy not only complicates existing 
regimes of knowledge and power, but
 also makes them particularly potent for imagining and governing 
uncertain futures that exceed conventional planning or forecasting. 
Uncertainty in voluminous environments is, therefore, more than 
something to be pre-emptively managed and contained: it is a generative condition that enables multiple modes of future-making.

 Foregrounding the imagined futures of volume provides an opportunity to analyse how these spatialities open up
 particular forms of futurity whilst foreclosing others, and to 
interrogate the vocabularies––anticipation, speculation, pre-emption, 
projection––through which they are described and enacted. From the 
opening of Arctic passages, the remaking of oceanic territories through 
subterranean and undersea energy, data, and telecommunications 
infrastructures, to the expansion of aerial logistics and drone corridors, imaginaries of volume are reorganising
 territorial claims, political authority, resource frontiers, and forms 
of governance. These projected futures also shape everyday 
subjectivities, affective orientations, multi-species and non-human worlds.
 This Voluminous Futures session aims to initiate a dialogue between literature on volume and future(s) through two time slots. In this 100-minute session, the first hour will see four paper presentations, followed by a more in-depth discussion about the ideas raised in these papers amongst the presenters and audience, led by the convenors.

 Focusing on underground and aerial volumes, this session has gathered fascinating and cutting-edge contributions, from discussions of airspace futures in DJI drone discourse and the politics of breathing in high altitudes of the Tibetan plateau, to the mapping of the underground in historical Northeast India and Upper Burma and the extraction of rare earths in contemporary Laos.

papers.title: Mapping the Underground: Volumetric Geology in Northeast India and Upper Burma, 1850–1900
Indeterminate Strata: Rare Earths Extraction and Politics of Future in Lao Uplands
Engineering the Future Vertically: DJI Drones and the Urban Atmosphere of Speed and Terror
Thin Air: Exploring the Altitudinal Politics of Breathing

sessions.title: Uneven Coasts: Spatial Inequalities, Health, and Resilience across the Life Course
sessions.description:
Coastal communities in England are shaped by longstanding spatial inequalities that intersect with health, ageing, mental wellbeing and resilience. Despite growing policy attention, coastal places remain marginal within geographical debates on inequality and justice, often treated as peripheral rather than as sites of distinct and enduring disadvantage. This session brings together four papers 
that examine how coastal residence produces and sustains unequal life-course outcomes, integrating longitudinal population data with participatory, place-based research.

papers.title: Mapping frailty across place: coastal differences in older adult health in England
Coastal health gap in life expectancy
"Something to do and somewhere to go": adolescent leisure participation and adult mental health
Mapping Resilience in Coastal Communities: ARISE Focus Groups

sessions.title: Uneven and Contested Geographies of Data Centers (3)
sessions.description:
In recent years, data centres have become an increasingly common 
subject of public and political debate (Edwards, 2024). The focus and 
criticism in these debates are usually directed at the massive 
consumption of natural resources (Brodie, 2024; Hogan, 2015), their 
effect on local communities (Bridges, 2024), and the concentration of 
geopolitical power – especially with regards to large data centers that 
are currently being planned or built in the context of the hype 
surrounding artificial intelligence. As essential infrastructure 
for living in our increasingly datafied worlds and for organising 
digital capitalism, data centres also raise a variety of geographical 
questions and problems. These range from the conflict-ridden embedding 
of data centres in local environments (Rone, 2024), to their role in 
larger infrastructure constellations (Atkins, 2021; Velkova, 2016), 
urban settings, and geopolitical issues such as digital sovereignty, 
digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019) and data localisation (Rosa, 2022). 
This session brings together conceptual and empirical contributions to 
critical data centre studies and research on the geographies of digital 
infrastructures. Against this backdrop, our session brings together 
contributions from researchers in digital geography, political 
geography, urban studies, political ecology and science and technology 
studies, examining the socio-technical imaginations, material effects 
and geopolitical and infrastructural embeddings of data centres. The 
papers engage with topics such as data centers in the Global South / 
Global Majority regions, contested infrastructuralization of data 
centers in rural and urban areas, the embedding of data centers in 
geopolitical strategies, programs, and conflicts, sociotechnical 
imaginations of AI data centers and social movements mobilizing against 
the data centers.

 Literature Atkins, E. (2021). Tracing the ‘cloud’: Emergent political geographies of global data centres. Political Geography, 86.

 Brodie, P. (2024). Smarter, greener extractivism: Digital 
infrastructures and the harnessing of new resources. Information, 
Communication & Society, 1–20.

 Bridges, L. E. (2024). Competing digital capacities: Between 
state-led digital governance and local data center tradeoffs. 
Information, Communication & Society, 27(10).

 Edwards, D., Cooper, Z. G. T., & Hogan, M. (2024). The making of critical data center studies. Convergence. 

 Hogan, M. (2015). Data flows and water woes: The Utah Data Center. Big Data & Society, 2(2).

 Kwet, M. (2022). Digital colonialism and Infrastructure as dept. 
Frontiers in African Digital Research, 65–77.Rone, J. (2024). The shape 
of the cloud: Contesting date centre construction in North Holland. New 
Media & Society, 26(10) 

 Rosa, F. R. (2022). Code Ethnography and the Materiality of Power in Internet Governance. Qualitative Sociology, 45(3), 433–455.

 Velkova, J. (2016). Data that warms: Waste heat, infrastructural 
convergence and the computation trafficcommodity. Big Data & 
Society, 3(2).

papers.title: Data Centers within Europe’s Net-Zero Transition: The Dilemma between Digital Growth and Socio-Spatial Justice
Suturing Financialized Progress and Infrastructural Reality: A Decadal Analysis of Data Center Narratives and Energy Policy in China
Imaginaries of the Post-Carbon City. Data Center Development and the Urban Heating Transition in Berlin.

sessions.title: Unequal City: Rethinking Representation for a Just Urban Future
sessions.description:
Urban representations, unbuilt spaces, walkability, imaginaries, and idealized images fundamentally shape spatial justice, yet circulate unequally. This panel examines Beirut as a laboratory of Middle East and North Africa (MENA) geographies of inequalities, where these regimes collide with space and power, coloniality /modernity, and infrastructure politics, revealing the politics of city imaging toward just places.

 Practices of unbuilt urban space expose how demolition as violence (Fijalkow) erases collective memory in informal settlements' "lived spaces" (Fremont), replacing them with sanitized redevelopment amid resource politics and territoriality. Building on Gehl's public space practices and Whyte's "Social Life of Small Urban Places," we trace how visual disappearance rewrites urban history, displacing marginalized narratives and knowledge production.

 Beirut's car-centric urban design, rooted in coloniality/modernity legacies, prioritizes connectivity over local accessibility, spawning pedestrian reclamation initiatives. Yet mobility policies, key infrastructure policies, optimize for cars, not commuters, exacerbating citizenship divides. The urban imaginary (A. Bourdin) becomes a counter-discourse where pedestrians voice hopes and needs, challenging macro-scale planning with micro-scale (Secchi, Vigano) lived experience.

 Social media amplifies utopian city images where inequalities vanish; a "perfect just world" accessible to all. These circulating representations promise spatial justice but elide lived disparities; genocide echoes, war scars, and power asymmetries are functioning as ideological screens (Debord) that naturalize exclusion while claiming universality.

 Anchored in representation theory (Debord), spatial justice (Soja), and visual culture studies, this panel bridges architecture, urban sociology, and media analysis. Beirut's crises—informality, mobility inequity, digital utopianism—crystallize global MENA challenges of equitable city representation.

papers.title: Narratives of Vacant Unbuilt Spaces as Mirrors of Inequalities in Beirut City
Urban Morphologies of Mobility and the Production of Accessibility Inequalities: A case study of Beirut
Scopic Experience and Urban Imaginaries
