sessions.title: What cities does the Belt and Road lead to? Future directions and critical questions at the nexus of Global China, infrastructure and urbanism
sessions.description:
Twenty-first century urbanism is being fundamentally reshaped by the reconfiguration of global capital flows, geopolitical realignments, and new infrastructural networks that challenge Western-led development paradigms. China's Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) has emerged as perhaps the most consequential infrastructure-led development strategy of our time, producing what scholars have termed a "global force of urban transformation" (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024). Yet as the BRI enters a new phase—marked by intensifying US-China rivalry, shifting European approaches to Chinese capital, and starkly differentiated local responses ranging from enthusiastic embrace in Serbia and Hungary to recent reversals in Argentina—the theoretical and empirical tools for understanding BRI urbanism demand critical reassessment. How China "arrives" differs dramatically across places, yet scholarship has only begun to unpack the mechanisms, actors, and infrastructures through which these differentiated geographies are produced. This session engages with what we term the "urbanisation, localisation, and infrastructurisation" of the BRI and Global China agenda. Building on recent calls to understand the BRI as fundamentally an urban project (Shin et al., 2022; Andujar et al., 2024) and infrastructure as a key site for theorising contemporary urbanisation (Kanai and Schindler, 2022; Schindler et al., 2022), the session seeks to advance both theoretical frameworks and empirical research at the nexus of BRI/Global China, infrastructure, and urbanism. The session embraces contributions that move beyond geopolitical and macro-economic analyses to examine how the BRI materialises locally—through what infrastructures, involving what actors, producing what forms of urbanism, and with what consequences for governance, labour, and everyday life. It welcomes disciplinary perspectives from across geography's broad field with the aim of generating new vocabularies and comparative insights for understanding infrastructure-led urbanisation in an era of shifting global power. 
 The session will engage with the following themes:  Infrastructure and Urbanisation — What infrastructures are driving BRI-inspired urbanism? How do digital, financial, transport, logistics, and cultural infrastructures interact to reshape urban spaces?  
 Geopolitics, Capital, and Local Responses — How are changing geopolitical conditions reshaping the landscape of Chinese investments? What is the relationship between openness or closure to Chinese capital and local political formations? How do cities across respond to Chinese infrastructure investment? Actors, Governance, and Agency —  What actors are involved in BRI urbanism, and what is at stake for different stakeholders? What coalitions emerge, and what forms of local agency are exercised? Financial Mechanisms and Development Trajectories — What financial mechanisms drive BRI urbanism? How do investment strategies, public-private partnerships, and capital flows shape urban development trajectories?  Global Geographies and Comparative Perspectives — What is the global geography of BRI urbanism? How can comparative research help us understand the particularities of how Chinese infrastructure investment impacts different regions and localities? What patterns emerge across cases, and what context-specific dynamics demand attention? Theoretical Concepts and Methodological Advances — What new theoretical frameworks are needed to conceptualise BRI urbanism and its relationship to infrastructure-led development? How might the urbanisation, localisation, and infrastructurisation of the Global China agenda be better theorised? What methodological approaches can advance our understanding of these processes?

papers.title: Coordination or Fragmentation? Cross-Project Dynamics in City-Regions in Southeast Asia
Extended Urbanization through Corridors: Rethinking the Belt and Road Initiative in Chongqing
What Cities Do Special Economic Zones Build? Case Studies of China-inspired Urbanisation in India and Africa

sessions.title: Sensory Lives multi-sensory playhouse tent (2 - workshop)
sessions.description: The Sensory Lives project aims to better understand how homeless families with neurodivergent children experience living in Temporary Accommodation. In this workshop, learn about and experience a multi-sensory playhouse tent designed to communicate children’s lived experiences of B&Bs, hotels, and other forms of Temporary Accommodation. The playhouse tent hopes to re-centre children’s perspectives in discussions of homelessness and communicate neurological difference more inclusively in research and policy.

sessions.title: Engaging inequalities and injustice issues towards developing just educational spaces within and outside institutions
sessions.description:
This session foregrounds education as a spatial and social practice, and geography education as a potential vehicle to achieve social justice. Formal and informal educational spaces, such as classrooms, universities, digital platforms, museums, streets, and community organisations, are inherently geographically significant. These spaces are shaped by uneven power relations, institutional structures, cultural traditions and material conditions, constituting critical sites where inequalities are reproduced, negotiated, or resisted.

 Geographical research has extensively explored inequalities related to migration, climate change, development, health and geopolitics. However, the spatial politics of teaching and learning remain comparatively under-theorised within mainstream geographical debates. In other words, the role of geography in shaping public sayings and doings of equality and justice remains marginalised.

 We argue that conventional linear education models are increasingly insufficient to enable learners to critically engage with complex and intertwined knowledges of equality and justice (see: Kim, 2019; Sundaresan, 2020; Simm, Marvell & Mellor, 2021; Lambert, Béneker, Bladh & Mitchell, 2025). At the same time, the lack of communication and transformation between academic geography, school geography and public geographical imaginaries has raised urgent questions about who are able to access to geographical knowledge, whose voices are heard, and what role geography can play to make a difference beyond institutions.

 This session invites contributions that (re-)conceptualise educational spaces as contested geographies and explore how geography (as disciplines, pedagogy, and public practice) can advocate for developing just spaces and places. It especially seeks the works that bridge research, teaching, academic activism, and public engagement, amplifying emerging or marginalised voices.

papers.title: Collaborative and creative mapping as a peripatetic way to produce equal spaces for knowledge exchange outside the classroom: A case in Shanghai
Immersive geographies and the power of transformation in school spaces
Country-Led Pedagogies for a Different Future
Who Cares about Fieldwork? A Person-Centred Framework for a Compassionate Early-Career Fieldwork Pedagogy

sessions.title: Innovative and fair food communities : towards shared knowledge, shared hope and future resilience.
sessions.description:
This hybrid ession seeks to engage food communities who are challening global supply chains though a food justice and / or relocalisation lens to share learning outcomes from parterships and networks active accross boundaries. Our call especially seeks out participatory and community led and policy focussed research, in order to enable dissemination across geographical and dsiciplinary bundaries. Presentations will exemplify novel and innovative approaches to challening food networks and will bring forward new information and actions, possibly from localised projects e.g. addressing food in-acess. The accessible hybrid session will enrich broader academic understanding of unheard dialogues arising from unfair and unsustainable supply chains and place based contexts. 

 The session calls on researchers from marginalised group to participitate who may be focussing on a wide range, but not limited to themes such as

 - Decolonising food systems,
- Food rescue and redistribution,
- Access and rights to food campaigns,
- Growers campaigns and land availability
- Slow Food groups,
- Hopeful solutions,
- Local food hubs and partnerships
- Local procurement and food charters
- Innovative and entrepreneurial methdoologies and outcomes

 Early career researchers and outcomes of practitioner based research work involving building resileience and co-created solutions such as case studies and small scale international studies are very welcome.

papers.title: From Surplus to Social Infrastructure: Participatory Redistribution in Unequal Food Systems (Nigeria - UK)
Food Hub’s business models enabling shared value in SFSCs and supporting resilient rural food systems
Embedded, participatory research: co-producing a local food action plan for Gwynedd, Northwest Wales.

sessions.title: Research with, not on: A Workshop on Engaged Scholarship on the Geographies of Labour and Technology
sessions.description:
Research at the intersection of labour and technology has flourished in recent years, drawing attention to the exponential growth in precarious labour in the digital economy. Simultaneously, labour movements have erupted across geographies, sectors, and class positions to shape, contest, and even reverse its growth trajectories. Yet, despite these parallel developments, these movements and the scholarship around them remain disconnected, rarely intersecting in sustained or generative ways. Intervening in this disconnect, this session proposes an interactive workshop for critical geographers to collectively reflect on our roles within contemporary labour struggles in the digital economy. Specifically, this session will create a space for critical dialogue about engaged and non-extractive methodological practice at the intersections of technology, labour, and the geographies of work.

 Central to our discussion is the question of how scholars, working within structures of the neoliberal university, can extend radical solidarity to labour movements within the digital economy. We will examine the complexities of privilege and precarity across different geographical contexts, exploring how positionality shapes solidarity politics and the possibilities for meaningful praxis. We will explore principles of non-extractive research practice and strategies for building long-term relationships that challenge institutional complicity while supporting movement work.

 Facilitated by two PhD researchers drawing on their experiences of engaged research with platform worker unions in India, this interactive co-production workshop invites scholar-activists undertaking engaged projects to reflect collectively on our practice. Using hands-on materials such as maps, videos, photos, pamphlets, social media content, the workshop will prompt collaborative reflection on participants' own research trajectories. Participants will work in small groups, selecting from pre-identified topics and guiding questions that foreground the practical, political, and ethical dilemmas of scholar-activism.

 Rather than producing prescriptive "best practices" or toolkits that risk flattening complexities or reproducing epistemic violence, the workshop will work towards two outcomes. First, participants will collaboratively draft a research manifesto or statement of principles articulating shared commitments for politically engaged scholarship. Second, we will initiate a post-conference writing and dialogue network to support participants in conducting engaged research, and to develop the workshop’s reflections into collaborative outputs, extending this work beyond the conference setting.

sessions.title: Are the State and Market Still Relevant for the Urban Poor? Rethinking Housing in Contemporary Cities
sessions.description:
A sizeable share of the population in many cities in both the Global South and the North lives without proper shelter. The casual and market-oriented housing policies in many cities/countries have failed to ensure affordable housing for low-income groups forcing them to live in a sub-human environment. The enormous flow of capital into cities' real estate in recent years has not produced housing for all. In fact, market reforms and rising real estate prices have further increased the vulnerability of marginalised sections to housing. In many cities, prices have appreciated several times in recent years, while workers' real wages have grown slowly during the same period. Many cities have also experienced rising costs for other essential commodities and services. This indicates that income and affordability are not keeping pace with the rise in real estate prices. It can now be argued whether the State and the Market remain relevant for poorer sections of society in cities with respect to housing. 

 This session invites papers based on empirical case studies from both the Global South and the Global North, as well as conceptual contributions on how housing provisions and markets operate within cities and how marginalised groups negotiate with the state and the market regarding their shelter. In other words, the papers should foreground the urban realities and should address the following issues though not limited to, (i) How are the urban housing questions addressed by State and Market and what are chasm that are created between income of marginalised sections and market prices in the housing sector, (ii) How have been the growth of houseless population in cities and what are their coping mechanism, (iii) How do urban planning and policy frameworks (like smart city) shape the urban housing question and the location of the housing in the cities? (iv) How does the aestheticisation and infringement of citizenship rights/legalities lead to demolitions, displacements, gentrification, and socio-spatial exclusions of the marginalised sections? (v) What forms of alternative policies and planning can help in socio-economically just and inclusive urban housing in cities?

 Papers focusing on metropolitan cities are especially encouraged.

papers.title: Housing Ownership in India: Patterns and Regional Variation
Performative Compliance and Human Buffers: Negotiating the State and Market Chasm in Majuqiao, Beijing
Housing in Mumbai: Situation of the Poor between the State Apathy and Irrelevance of the Market
affiliations: Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai

sessions.title: 'Transport Governance for a Future' with Professor Kate Pangbourne
sessions.description: In this session Professor Kate Pangbourne will give a short presentation to introduce her innovative and interdisciplinary book tackling one of the 21st century’s most urgent challenges: steering transport systems toward a socially just, low-carbon future. Transport Governance for a Future is published by Edward Elgar in the TGRG series Transport, Mobilities and Spatial Change. She will introduce her CATI Transition Momentum Framework, designed to identify leverage points for accelerating change through systematic analysis of continuities, alignments, technologies and implementations. She will explain how her empirical work on transport governance structures and processes has informed its design. She will call academics and practitioners to action that consciously and collaboratively integrates diverse perspectives and knowledges across a range of fields not currently central to transport, so that modernising transport governance is fit for a world in polycrisis. Transport Governance for a Future is an essential resource for students and academics in transport studies and human geography, and has important insights for those in economics, politics and public policy, public administration and management and environmental studies. It is also a useful guide for practitioners and policymakers involved in global transport governance. Professor Karen Lucas will then chair an invited panel of 2/3 academics to critically engage with the book (who will have had an advanced electronic pre-print) and with Kate. Karen and each panellist will give a 5 minute response to the book before more general discussion and Q&A.

sessions.title: Critical Climate Geographies: Unsettling the Discipline (3) Disciplinary Trends
sessions.description:
Parsons et al. (2025) recently reminded us that “climate change is everywhere within geographical scholarship. Yet, it is not equally everywhere.” This tension sits at the heart of what it means to engage in critical climate geographies today. Climate change permeates the discipline, yet the power relations, epistemic architectures, and political imaginaries that shape it often remain unexamined or unevenly articulated. In line with the inequalities theme of this years’ RGS-IBG Annual Conference, this session takes seriously the provocation that to understand climate change and its related inequalities, we must also unsettle the discipline itself - its assumptions, its boundaries, and its inherited ways of knowing.

 Building on catalytic interventions across human geography (e.g. Parsons et al. 2025; Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Liverman, 2024; Sultana 2022; Mahony & Hulme 2018; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010), we foreground the diverse trajectories that have pushed climate scholarship into new conceptual and political terrain. The aforementioned articles collectively map a field in which climate epistemologies, decolonial critique, multi‑scalar governance, and speculative climate futures have become central to understanding how climate change is made and remade through power, knowledge, and lived experience.

 Within this expanding geographical landscape, scholarship on climate epistemologies has interrogated the politics of knowledge production, revealing how climate expertise is constructed, circulated, and contested (Mahony and Hulme 2018; Liverman, 2024; Parsons et al. 2025). Decolonial and anti‑racist climate geographies have exposed how colonial legacies, racial capitalism, and structural inequalities underpin climate vulnerability and shape global policy imaginaries (Liverman, 2024; Parsons, 2024; Sultana 2022; Yusoff 2018; Baldwin 2016). Meanwhile, work on climate governance, security, and multi‑scalar politics has traced the entanglements of climate change with human security, adaptation, and state power (Rothe et al., 2025; Kythreotis et al., 2024; Westman, 2024; O’Brien 2012), while analyses of anticipatory and speculative climate futures highlight how imaginaries of socio‑ecological transformation shape political action and the horizons of possibility (Bulkeley & McFarlane, 2024; Cox, 2024; Braun 2015; Anderson 2010).

 We invite papers that support, extend, challenge, or disrupt these trajectories. Contributions may address climate epistemologies and co‑production; decolonial, feminist, and anti‑racist approaches; digital and algorithmic climate governance; climate justice, migration, and security; embodied and affective climate geographies; urban and multi‑scalar governance; or speculative and transformative climate futures. We particularly welcome interdisciplinary, reflexive, and methodologically experimental work – ethnographic, creative, digital, speculative, or collaborative – that pushes the boundaries of what climate geography can be.

 If critical climate geographies are to meet the political and epistemic demands of the present moment, they must continue to unsettle the discipline: its categories, its hierarchies, and its claims to authority. This session aims to convene that unsettling.

papers.title: Transformation Without Catastrophe? Envisioning and enacting Climate Futures through Social and Solidarity Economies
Geographic Research in an Age of Climate System Breakdown
Using a Transdisciplinary Geography to Know Crude Oil Differently and Reimagine its Relations

sessions.title: Infrastructures of Social Reproduction and Political Subjectivation: Housing, Class, and Urban Inequalities (2)
sessions.description:
In the context of intersecting housing, climate, and cost-of-living crises, access to basic socio-technical infrastructures—water, energy, mobility, and telecommunication as well as housing itself—has become increasingly precarious for many. Yet these infrastructures are central for maintaining social reproduction understood as the everyday work of sustaining life and labour power (see, e.g., Gillespie and Hardy 2021; Madden 2025; Meehan 2025). In recent years, scholars have increasingly focused on the crises of social reproduction arising from precarious access to such structures – entailing exhaustion, health issues, and limited participation in wage labor as well as intensifying urban inequalities (see, e.g., Madden 2025; Protschky and Formella forthcoming; Rao 2021). Research shows that alienation and depletion of social reproduction often inhibit solidarity and political action by those affected - due to limited time, power-imbalances between tenants, landlords, unhoused people and service providers, shame for one’s own situation or fragmentation of groups affected by infrastructural precarity (Latocha 2024; Protschky 2023; Reichle and Bescherer 2021; Rinn et al. 2022). While precarious access to infrastructures of social reproduction thus effectively impedes engagement against this precarity, scholars also point to venues for enabling political action, including practical support in reproductive activities, raising class consciousness, and bridging divides between class fractions (Gehriger 2025; Uhlmann 2023).

 This curated session invites contributions that take seriously the material and political consequences of infrastructural precarity for reproductive life and political subjectivation. Bringing Social Reproduction Theory into dialogue with critical housing and infrastructure studies, we ask how housing and infrastructure regimes shape the embodied, affective, and relational capacities through which people endure, comply with and resist precarious conditions while organizing everyday life.

 We are particularly interested in contributions that address questions such as:

 • How do precarious housing and infrastructural arrangements reorganise the conditions under which people can sustain everyday life and reproduce labour power?

 • In what ways do exhaustion, alienation, shame, time poverty, and fragmentation inhibit solidarity and political organising—and how might they also become grounds for renewed political agency?

 • How do finance-dominated housing regimes and state regulation govern social reproduction, and with what classed, gendered, and racialised effects?

 • How can we theorise housing as an infrastructure and terrain of social reproduction—linking uneven materiality, lived experience, political governance, and struggle?

 • What practices (mutual aid, reproductive support, tenant organising, coalition-building) enable political action and bridge divides between class fractions, such as tenants and unhoused groups?

 We welcome creative, conceptual, and empirical contributions, including but not limited to:

 • Housing and socio-technical infrastructures (e.g., water, energy, mobility, telecommunications) as material conditions of social reproduction

 • Financialisation, commodification, and the governance of social reproduction

 • Tenancy, eviction, and the infrastructural politics of (un)housing

 • ‘Lived’ housing crises: depletion, alienation, and everyday survival strategies

 • Political subjectivation under infrastructural precarity: demobilisation and politicisation

 • Class (re)formation through housing relations; reproductive relations and urban inequality

 • Feminist political economy perspectives on dwelling, labour power, and urban class experience

 • Organising amid crisis: care infrastructures, mutual aid, and bridging fragmented publics

papers.title: Leasehold Flat-ownership without Home: Property Hegemony and Un‑Homing in a Hyper-high-density City
“Without Anita’s Place I would probably be dead”: Examining discourses of safety from the perspective of homeless encampment residents
affiliations: PhD candidate, Geography, Simon Fraser University

sessions.title: Mapping Intersecting Inequalities through Relief Maps: Scales, Processes and Lived Experience (2)
sessions.description: This session brings together a set of conceptual, empirical and methodological contributions that centre on mapping intersecting inequalities through the Relief Maps model. Drawing on findings from the ERC-funded INTERMAPS project, the session examines how inequalities are produced, lived, and negotiated across different places, scales, and everyday contexts. The central focus of the session is on mapping intersectional inequalities as lived experience. Relief Maps are mobilised across all papers as a shared feminist and intersectional framework that makes it possible to translate embodied experiences of inequality into spatial forms of analysis. Rather than treating mapping as a neutral act of representation, the session understands mapping as a situated and relational process through which inequalities can become visible. In this sense, the session draws on a multidimensional and relational understanding of place that goes beyond location alone. By engaging with place as the intersection of spatial settings, everyday practices, emotional attachments, and social positions, the papers show how intersecting power relations are materialised differently across homes, urban spaces, sites of activism, environmental contexts, and more-than-human relations. This approach allows for the analysis of inequalities across multiple scales, while remaining grounded in lived experience. By foregrounding intersectional mapping grounded in lived experience, the session contributes to geographical debates on spatial justice and shows how rethinking mapping practices can support the pursuit of more just places.

papers.title: Navigating Selective Belonging: Intersectional Experiences of Privileged Migrants in Barcelona
Monosexuality as Spatial Regime: Uneven Geographies of Belonging
Mapping Hostility with Relief Maps: Exploring Anti-Gender and Antifeminist Opposition in Spain
Dwelling with Climate Change: Exploring Impacts and Agency in Domestic Spaces with Climate Relief Maps

sessions.title: Mobilising care (2): exploring the imaginaries, expectations, politics and qualities of care in everyday travel
sessions.description:
Whilst time remains the dominant currency when thinking about what matters in everyday travel, it is increasingly clear that for many journeys outside labour relations, articulations of care sit at the heart of much travel decision-making (Ivins and Spinney 2024). As a result, time savings in particular, but also many other aspects of travel such as levels of comfort and safety are prioritized in the pursuit of care, rather than cost savings. Mobility and the ways we choose to travel are therefore central to the cultures and politics of care (Kent 2025). Pushing off from the premise that mobility contributes to the accomplishment of three broad forms caring relation (1) self-care: physical/ emotional work directed towards maintaining/ enhancing our personal wellbeing; (2) familial care: activities directed toward maintaining/ enhancing the wellbeing of others; and (3) environmental care: activities directed toward maintaining/ enhancing local and natural environments (Bowlby 2012:2101; Hobson 2006), this session seeks to expand and explore debates at the intersection of mobility and care. Against this backdrop the session seeks contributions engaging with (but not limited to) a range of themes and questions including: 

 - How is care accomplished through different mobilities; which mobilities (digital and physical) are seen to possess qualities that contribute to care, and which are not? 

 - To what extent does care underpin judgements and decisions about how we travel, what forms of care are evident, and where (self, others, environments) and when (towards immediate or future goals) is care directed? 

 - Caring expectations – what standards of care do we come to expect in relation to different mobilities, and how are these expectations shaped and ratcheted up? What role do different forms of mobility play in shaping standards of care and the qualities that are deemed important? 

 - How do different scales of mobility - encompassing daily travel and global mobility shape imaginaries and accomplishments of care? 

 - How do standards of care desired in mobilities differ within and across different groups in society, how do standards change over time and across regions and countries? 

 - How do particular embodiments arising from gender, age, labour, ethnicity, income, class, etc shape how we perceive and accomplish care, and the forms of mobility we choose to use? 

 - How do attempts to change travel behaviour towards more sustainable alternatives affect people’s ability to enact and accomplish care? What happens when care not be accomplished in ways and to standards of our choosing? 

 - What politics of care arise when considering the roles, contradictions, and potential ‘carelessness’ and harms of different mobilities in caring relations? 

 - How do physical and virtual mobilities intersect in the accomplishments of care?

sessions.title: Nature, cities, and environmental inequalities: exploring the intersections of environment, human health, and social justice (2)
sessions.description:
The current debate on inequalities has well acknowledged the systemic disparities that cut across health, social, environmental, and economic domains in both the Global South and the Global North (Dorling, 2023). Over the recent decades, there has been growing recognition of the interconnected nature of these systems (Ellis et al., 2025), particularly through the lenses of sustainability and ecological health (Barrett, 2022). Social and economic inequalities frequently translate into unequal access to natural resources, environmental amenities, and essential services, factors that play a critical role in shaping both physical and mental health outcomes (Bratman et al., 2019; Wang et al., 2026).

 Environmental inequalities in access to nature are closely related to broader social and economic inequalities, with clear implications for health and well-being (Fratesi et al., 2024; Roberts et al., 2021). Communities facing persistent socioeconomic disadvantage are more likely to experience limited availability and lower quality of green and blue spaces, alongside higher exposure to environmental degradation and risk. These patterns are shaped by structural factors, including siloed organisation of spatial planning, land-use, and infrastructure investment decisions, which systematically privilege and marginalise different communities (Mayen Huerta et al., 2025). An intersectional and place-based analytical approach is therefore essential to investigate the mechanisms through which environmental inequalities are produced and maintained, and to inform integrated policy responses that address environmental justice and health inequalities holistically (Cohen et al., 2024).

 Meanwhile, with less than four years remaining to deliver the targets of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development under the Global Biodiversity Framework, the gap between commitments and lived practice is becoming increasingly concerning (Obura et al., 2026). The narrowing policy window heightens the need for evidence-based research to inform place-sensitive interventions, particularly those addressing inequalities rooted in socioeconomic and geographic conditions. In the UK, such disparities are recognised as having implications for long-term national security (Defra, 2026).

 This session seeks to further continue an interdisciplinary dialogue on the intersections of nature, human health, and equality, exploring both the challenges and opportunities. We welcome theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions that address, but are not limited to, the following themes:

 The role of nature-based solutions, including green and blue spaces, in reducing health inequalities

 Innovative and community-based approaches to urban development and biodiversity integration

 Urban planning, environmental justice, and policies promoting equitable access to nature

 The impacts of climate change and environmental degradation on health inequalities

 Intersectional perspectives on environment, health, and social justice

 Digital technologies, AI, and smart-city tools in mapping and addressing health inequalities

 Nature, care, and social infrastructure in marginalised communities

sessions.title: Towards Just Blue Places: Blue Economies, Equitable Futures and Resilience in Times of Climate Change
sessions.description:
The rapid expansion of blue economies is widely framed as essential to sustainable development and economic growth by governments and industry (e.g. OECD, 2016). Oceans are increasingly constructed as frontiers of innovation, as sites for renewable energy, aquaculture, bioprospecting, seabed mining and marine conservation alongside traditional fisheries and harvesting of new species. Yet this raises urgent questions: Who benefits from these blue economies? Who is excluded? How are coastal landscapes and communities and ocean spaces being reassembled (e.g. Li, 2014), and with what consequences for justice, equity and place?

 While policy narratives often promise resilience, inclusion and sustainability, growing evidence suggests that Blue Economy transitions may reproduce or deepen existing inequalities along lines of gender, indigeneity, class, migration status, age and geography (Bennett et al., 2021; Fusco et al. 2022; Cisneros-Montemayor, A. M., et al. 2025). Processes such as “ocean grabbing” (Bennett et al., 2015) and the marginalisation of small-scale and Indigenous fisheries raise concerns about access, rights and recognition. At the same time, climate change is intensifying pressures on coastal communities, reshaping governance, livelihoods and senses of belonging.

 Geographical scholarship has begun to question how ocean-based industries are mapped as occurring at sea while remaining disconnected from onshore communities and dependent places (St. Martin & Hall-Arber, 2008), despite obvious overlaps in some industries, such as aquaculture (Martin et al.2021). Attending to the relationship between blue spaces (at sea) and blue places (onshore communities) opens up critical questions about spatial justice, governance and belonging. Emerging debates around Blue Justice (Jentoft et al., 2022) further highlight the need to rethink how ocean economies are assembled and for whom.

 This session focus on geographical engagements with the social, spatial and political dimensions of Blue Economy transitions and transformations. By foregrounding equity, place and power, this session aims to rethink Blue Economies and how they might be reimagined to support more just, resilient and sustainable coastal futures.

 References 

 Bennett NJ, Govan H and Satterfield T (2015) Ocean grabbing. Marine Policy 57 61–68.

 Bennett NJ, Blythe J, Cisneros-Montemayor AM, et al. (2021) Just transformations to sustainability. Marine Policy 125 104387.

 Cisneros-Montemayor, A. M., et al. "Social equity indicators for a Blue Economy: Guidelines for application." Marine Policy 180 (2025): 106815.

 Fusco, Leah M., et al. "Blueing business as usual in the ocean: Blue economies, oil, and climate justice." Political Geography 98 (2022): 102670.

 Jentoft S, Chuenpagdee R and Said A (eds) (2022) Blue Justice: Small-Scale Fisheries in a Sustainable Ocean Economy. Cham. Springer.

 Li TM (2014) What is land? Assembling a resource for global investment. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers 39(4) 589–602.

 Martin, Sarah J., et al. "‘Landing’ salmon aquaculture: Ecologies, infrastructures and the promise of sustainability." Geoforum 123 (2021): 47-55.

 OECD (2016) The Ocean Economy in 2030. Paris: OECD Publishing.

 St. Martin K and Hall-Arber M (2008) The missing layer: Geo-technologies, communities, and implications for marine spatial planning. Marine Policy 32(5) 779–786.

sessions.title: Geographies of design
sessions.description:
Design plays a fundamental role in shaping spatial relations and ordering environments. Despite underpinning several strands of geographical thought and research, however, the term ‘design’ has remained strangely peripheral, rarely itself becoming the central object of investigation. This appears to be changing. Geographers have recently explored the incorporation of the more-than-human realm into design (Japser, 2018; Oliver, 2025); the emergence of neo-organicist approaches to design (Krivý and Gandy, 2023); the aesthetics of contemporary ecological design (Brady and Prior, 2020; James, 2025); and the postcolonial and decolonial politics of design (Dang, 2021; Jazeel, 2017). 

 This session reflects upon these emerging though often discrete currents. It aims to explore the relationship between design and geographical inquiry as well as the possibilities for new lines of interdisciplinary connection. In line with the conference focus on the geographies of inequalities and just places, the session is principally concerned with papers that explore design as always-already political. Who designs? Through what modalities and for whom? What are the possibilities for counter-hegemonic forms of design that challenge rather than reinforce systemic inequalities? 

 Contributors might speak to one or more of the following themes:

 1. Historical and contemporary geographies of design: How has design historically ordered, staged, and transformed nature, space, and social relations? What continuities and ruptures exist between historical and contemporary design practices?

 2. Sites and scales: How does design differ across diverse scales and geographical contexts? What is lost and gained as practices of design travel? 

 3. Interdisciplinary engagements: What connections might be made with diverse literatures on design encompassing architectural criticism, urban studies, urban political ecology, the environmental humanities, aesthetic theory, and critical landscape studies? Is there a distinctive geographical contribution to be made within contemporary debates concerning design?

 4. Politics and power: How do design practices reflect and reproduce particular power relations? Conversely, how has design been mobilized towards counter-hegemonic ends?

 5. Aesthetics and atmospheres: Explorations of how design curates particular aesthetic experiences, affective atmospheres, and sensory encounters. 

 6. More-than/non-human design: How does design mediate human/non-human relations? How might contemporary approaches center non-human agencies, multispecies relations, and ecological processes? 

 References:

 Brady, E. and Prior, J., 2020. Environmental aesthetics: A synthetic review. People and Nature, 2(2), pp.254-266.

 Dang, T.K., 2021. Decolonizing landscape. Landscape Research, 46(7), pp.1004-1016. 

 James, Z., 2025. Staged ecologies: Aesthetics, nature and infrastructure in the late‐modern metropolis. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 50(4), p.e70004.

 Jasper, S., 2018. Sonic refugia: Nature, noise abatement and landscape design in West Berlin. The Journal of Architecture, 23(6), pp.936-960.

 Jazeel, T., 2017. Tropical modernism/environmental nationalism: The politics of built space in postcolonial Sri Lanka. Fabrications, 27(2), pp.134-152.

 Krivý, M. and Gandy, M., 2023. Zany beetroot: Architecture, autopoiesis, and the spatial formations of late capital. Environment and Planning D: Society and Space, 41(6), pp.1058-1074.

 Oliver, C., 2025. Dancing with foxes: More‐than‐human design in ‘the chicken city’. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, 50(2), p.e12695.

sessions.title: Active Mobility to School: Geographies of Inequality, Justice, and Everyday Urban Movement (2)
sessions.description: Children’s journeys to and from school sit at the intersection of three global crises affecting youth: preventable road collisions, rising physical inactivity and worsening well-being. Yet research and policy remain fragmented across transport planning, public health, education and governance, with limited cumulative evidence on how to deliver and scale interventions in real cities. This session positions Active Mobility to School (AMTS), including walking, cycling and wheeling, as a developmental nexus that shapes autonomy, social inclusion and long-term travel habits, while influencing health and safety outcomes. We aim to convene an international forum that integrates social sciences, urban policy, health, and transport engineering. Through evidence from the Global North and South, this session brings transport geography into direct dialogue with debates on inequality and justice, focusing on AMTS, by examining how transport systems, policies, and street environments unevenly shape children’s mobility opportunities across different urban and regional contexts and identify practical pathways for institutionalisation and equitable scale-up.

sessions.title: Political ecologies of the illicit and their nonhuman reconfigurations
sessions.description:
Informal economies and global supply chains, inclusive of unauthorised, illegal/illicit, and criminal/ised forms of production and trade are profoundly implicated in the commodification, wellbeing, and conservation of nonhuman life. Although illegal wildlife trade is perhaps the most well-known example of how illegal or illicit economies can have devastating consequences for species futures (Collard, 2021; Duffy, 2022), the scale of global farmed animal production, for example, is overwhelmingly in informal, illegal, and even criminal economies, and is often entangled with the racial persecution of those labouring in risky animal trades (Narayanan 2019). Further, nonhuman labour-capital is also imposed upon via indirect or secondary effects of illicit economies: the multispecies slavery-environment nexus implicated in illicit sandmining with devastating ecological consequences (Narayanan 2025), while even illicit trade in ornamental plants is leveraged by law enforcement actors to deepen the criminalization and further marginalization of already racialized populations excluded from mainstream economies (Hübschle and Margulies, 2024).

 Critically, state institutions are often directly or indirectly involved in scaffolding illicit economies and supply chains, (re)producing environmental harms and animal violence (McSweeney, 2023; Vasile and Iordăchescu, 2022) by exploiting racially vulnerable workforces. A growing body of scholarship on ‘political ecologies of the illicit,’ attends to both the uneven power and politics reshaping ecologies through illicit forms of trade, land use change, resource exploitation, “…and how responses to them produce and/or reify long-standing practices of exclusionary environmental protection and securitization bolstering military, state, and capitalist economic power and control” (Margulies et al., 2023: 1). But nonhuman life is not only impacted by illicit economies, it also (re)shapes and (re)configures them (Dickinson, 2022). To this end, papers will expand the remit and scholarship of political ecologies of the illicit attentive to both their entangled ecological and economic drivers and consequences and what sorts of institutions and actors benefit or lose out in these illicit configurations. Further, papers will consider the agentic role of nonhumans in (re)shaping illicit economies in thwarting, resisting, or otherwise troubling commodification and violent destruction of nonhuman life.

papers.title: Navigating the interface between legality and illegality: actors and networks of red coral trade in China
affiliations: School of Geography, University of Leeds

sessions.title: Atmospheres and Young People’s Lives: Sensory Worlds, Socio-Technical Environments, and Speculative Fiction
sessions.description:
This panel consolidates and extends atmospheric research within youth geographies by engaging atmospheres as conceptual, methodological, and political problematics rather than as discrete empirical objects. Although affective atmospheres have been extensively theorised in human geography (e.g. Ben Anderson; Derek McCormack; Kathleen Stewart), their analytic uptake in youth studies has been comparatively cautious. Youth geographies have tended to privilege voice, representation, and agency, often mobilising “youth” as a stable socio-political category shaped through governance and generational power. While this orientation has been vital for foregrounding young people as situated actors, it has constrained engagement with the diffuse, pre-personal, and more-than-human forces that condition their spatial experience (Kraftl and Horton, 2017).

 Bringing together work on sensory attunement, socio-technical milieus, and geo-social imaginaries, the panel repositions youth not as the origin of agency but as participants within distributed socio-material-technical assemblages. Young people’s lives are understood as emerging through atmospheres composed of rhythms, infrastructures, architectures, digital systems, environmental conditions, and embodied practices. Inquiry, in this framing, becomes an immersive and collaborative attunement to the intensities that shape how young people sense, inhabit, and imagine their worlds. Rather than capturing youth experience as already formed, atmospheric research attends to how young people are implicated in the ongoing composition of relational fields that exceed them yet remain intimate in their effects.

 Conceptually, this orientation shifts from fixed social categories toward an atmospheric politics aligned with affect theory (e.g. Nigel Thrift; Brian Massumi). Here, youth are neither sovereign agents nor passive subjects, but are caught up in circulations of feeling, density, and sensibility through which power operates. Inequality and possibility take shape in the modulation of the environments young people inhabit and in the anticipatory disabling urban environments (Leyman),  geological life and climate inheritance (Truman), and socio-technical infrastructures (Trafí-Prats) that press upon their present. By centring youth within these atmospheric dynamics, the panel advances a more-than-human analytics attentive to how young lives are composed, constrained, and opened within shifting environmental and affective conditions.

 Session Format 

 We propose a live-only in person paper session. It will begin with a 15-minute introduction by the chair, Samantha Wilkinson (Manchester Metropolitan University) framing the conceptual and methodological stakes of the panel. Each of the three papers will be allocated 15 minutes for presentation, followed by 5 minutes of focused discussion. The session will conclude with a 15-minute response from the discussant, EJ Renold (Manchester Metropolitan University), leaving an additional 5 minutes at the end for a brief collective exchange.

papers.title: Un/Common Sensory Worlds: Cosplay, Neurodiversity, and the Re Atmosphering of Urban Life
Geo-Atmospheres: Youth Speculative Writing about Place and the Future in Post Industrial Wales and Meridianed London
Youth, Technicity, and Urban Atmospheres: Reframing the Smartphone Debate

sessions.title: Spaces of belonging and barriers: the changing geographies of higher education inequalities
sessions.description: Higher education (HE) has long been positioned as a pathway to social mobility (Bathmaker et al., 2016; Breen and In, 2025), yet contemporary landscapes of HE are increasingly marked by deepening inequalities that are spatial, structural, and intersectional. This session invites contributions that critically examine the uneven geographies of access, participation, experience, and outcomes, and how these are produced, reproduced, and potentially transformed within contemporary HE systems. We encourage papers that interrogate inequalities across multiple scales – from the global to the local – and contexts that consider how structural forces, policy frameworks, marketisation, and socio‑cultural dynamics shape differentiated HE including student trajectories.

Bureaucracies of (non) belonging: The extended inequalities of being ‘international’ in UK HE
A New Era of Studenthood? Shifting Campus Geographies during a Cost-of-Living Crisis.

sessions.title: Robots, Drones and urban (in)justice: Automation, surveillance, inclusion and new geographies of (in)justice in more than human cities (2)
sessions.description:
Artificial Intelligences increasingly enter already technicised urban spaces, which they can occupy and affect materially through devices such as robots and drones. Progress in AI and the highly networked nature of current urban environments make it possible for robots and drones to operate in close proximity to humans in complex, unscripted environments including public spaces, at home, at work or in urban green spaces. As they do so, they have potential to impactfully reconfigure cities, automating tasks, transforming urban flows, and extending the reach of AIs designed to maximise specific objectives, metrics or utility functions. In this panel session we will explore the contours of the new forms of (in)justice relevant to emerging entanglements and relations in urban spaces shared by human, non-human and artificial intelligences: How and why are Geographers thinking about and engaging with (in)justice in urban futures which now include humans but also non-human intelligences and agencies embodied in drones and robots? What new forms of injustice may make themselves felt when robots and drones relate in various ways to urban space (or fail to do so)? 

 Panellists will engage in theoretical and empirical discussion of urban drones and robots, with attention to the new forms of justice or injustice potentially introduced as embodied AIs transform, augment, optimise or automate urban life.

sessions.title: Geospatial Artificial Intelligence methods and applications (2): Workshop
sessions.description:
Over the past decade, significant advancements in deep learning have launched a new “AI spring,” reigniting research interest in artificial intelligence within GIScience and quantitative geography. While large language models have been making headlines in newspapers worldwide, a broader range of foundation models and architectures (e.g., CNN, GNN GAN, LSTM, Transformer) have sparked new work in geospatial artificial intelligence (Janowicz et al., 2020; De Sabbata et al., 2023, Mai et al., 2025) and representation learning (Liu et al., 2025; Klemmer et al, 2025). For example, vision models have been applied to street-view imagery to explore urban perceptions and infer socio-economic outcomes (Biljecki and Ito 2021; Law et al 2019). The transformer architecture has been used for natural language processing (Berragan et al., 2023), image analysis and machine vision (Li et al, 2022). Graph neural networks have been employed for geodemographic classifications and question-answering with geographic knowledge graphs (De Sabbata and Liu, 2023; Mai et al, 2020). More recently, geospatial foundation models (Janowicz et al., 2025) have emerged, leveraging large-scale geographic data to support multi-modal geographic analysis.

 This session aims to be a forum to discuss advances, opportunities, and challenges of the use of GeoAI in quantitative geography and geographic information science, showcasing the latest advancements in GeoAI theories, methods and applications within the realm of quantitative geographic studies.

 References

 - Berragan, C., Singleton, A., Calafiore, A., & Morley, J. (2023). Transformer based named entity recognition for place name extraction from unstructured text. International Journal of Geographical Information Science , 37 (4), 747-766.
- Biljecki, F., & Ito, K. (2021). Street view imagery in urban analytics and GIS: A review. Landscape and Urban Planning , 215 , 104217.
- De Sabbata, S., et al. (2023). GeoAI in urban analytics.* International Journal of Geographical Information Science*, 37(12), pp.2455-2463.
- De Sabbata, S., & Liu, P. (2023). A graph neural network framework for spatial geodemographic classification. International Journal of Geographical Information Science , 37(12), 2464-2486.
- Janowicz, K., at al. (2020). GeoAI: spatially explicit artificial intelligence techniques for geographic knowledge discovery and beyond. International Journal of Geographical Information Science , 34(4), pp.625-636.
- Janowicz, et al. (2025). GeoFM: how will geo-foundation models reshape spatial data science and GeoAI? International Journal of Geographical Information Science, 39(9), 1849–1865.
- Klemmer, K., et al (2025). Earth Embeddings: Towards AI-centric Representations of our Planet.
- Law, S., Paige, B., & Russell, C. (2019). Take a look around: using street view and satellite images to estimate house prices. ACM Transactions on Intelligent Systems and Technology (TIST) , 10(5), 1-19.
- Li, W., & Hsu, C. Y. (2022). GeoAI for large-scale image analysis and machine vision: Recent progress of artificial intelligence in geography. ISPRS International Journal of Geo-Information , 11(7).
- Liu, Y., et al. (2025). Representation learning for geospatial data. Annals of GIS, 31(4), 557–583. 
- Mai, G., et al. (2020). SE‐KGE: A location‐aware knowledge graph embedding model for geographic question answering and spatial semantic lifting. Transactions in GIS , 24(3), pp.623-655.
- Mai, G., et al. (2025). Towards the next generation of Geospatial Artificial Intelligence. International Journal of Applied Earth Observation and Geoinformation , 136 , 104368.

sessions.title: Fear and Liking in the Fonds: Radical Archival Praxis
sessions.description:
In recent years, there has been a burgeoning attention paid to ‘community’ (Caswell 2014), ‘grassroots’ (Rawson 2009), ‘autonomous’ (Moore and Pell 2010), ‘interference’ (Sellie et al. 2015), or ‘radical’ (Schwenk 2011) archives, celebrated as spaces capable of accentuating the contingency of archival authority while preserving and mobilising marginalised histories and alternative urban politics (Burgum 2020, Beswick et. al. 2016). Work highlighting the social and collaborative nature of working with/in archives (Ashmore et. al. 2012) further develops the inherently political recognition that archives create histories as much as they contain them: an attention to what is presented and elided in archival collections demonstrates a ‘methodological commitment to how history’s exclusions are secured and made’ (Stoler 2009, 17). 
 We position the radical archive as a space of embodied and potentially prefigurative politics (Routon 2026), one which ‘is change-oriented and future-minded, insofar as it helps us imagine both a different way of archiving and a different world to be archived’ (Ghaddar and Caswell 2019, 72). That said, archival work is nuanced and depends upon one’s ‘angle of arrival’ (Ahmed 2014), capable of provoking affections, aversions, anxieties, and frustrations that resonate in researchers’ bodies as they encounter materials (Dwyer and Davies 2010, DeSilvey 2007, Steedman 2001). Methodological work on this point can itself be radical; several scholars have recently explored how marginalised bodies—Black, Trans, or disabled, for example (see Longley 2021, Rawson 2009, Brilmyer 2022)—experience and transform archival spaces. Meanwhile, other scholarship engages the difficulties of working with ‘unsavoury’ radical collections, such as those of racist or fascist movements (Ashe 2021). Working with hate in this way presents its own ethical challenges around positionality and researcher safety, drawing the political responsibilities of collections and archivists into sharp relief (see Jones 2024). At the same time, archival practice presents promising avenues to oppose resurgent radical nationalisms through forms of counter-history and ‘archival resistance’ (Orich 2020). 
 For this session, we welcome papers which engage with radical archival institutions and/or collections, encompassing empirical work and/or methodological reflections. Submissions could explore themes such as: 
 - The politics of access to radical, political, and/or atypical collections 
- Considerations of how different kinds of radical materials should be labelled, stored, and organised 
- Embodied experiences with/in archives, including questions around access and representation 
- Encounters with hateful, extreme, or other ‘unsavoury’ materials in the archive 
- Navigating contradictory commitments in archival work 
- Connections between radical archival materials and other research methodologies 
- Radical archives as sites of resistance to hegemonic political formations

papers.title: Collecting a White Power Archive: The Three Klans
The fascist intimate: Encountering poems, postcards, and expense receipts in the archives of the west German radical right
Archival co-curation as method
A Radical Archive of Radicals

sessions.title: Rural Politics of the Far Right
sessions.description:
In a multitude of regions across the globe, far-right political parties have demonstrated a notable degree of success, particularly in rural areas. This phenomenon is especially pronounced in areas that are economically disadvantaged and have experienced marginalization as well as stigmatization. However, the manner in which the far right intends to confront the challenges faced by these rural regions remains to be elucidated. Furthermore, it is imperative to assess whether far-right parties effectively address the specific challenges confronting these regions and communities.

 The session invites contributions to an analysis of the political activities of the far right in, for, or through rural areas. We posit that a more profound comprehension of the rural dimensions of far-right politics, their absence, contradictions, past development, and national differences can enhance the extant discourse on the political geographies of the far right. To that end, we invite submissions of papers from diverse empirical and geographical backgrounds on the following (or other related) issues:

 - How does the far right think about "the rural"? What are their ideas about the past and future of rural areas?

 - What problems are the far right identifying in their rural policies, and who do they blame for them?

 - What are far-right suggestions for regional policy to deal with problems in rural areas, such as economic and demographic decline or infrastructure decay? What are the far-right positions in other rural-related fields, such as agricultural politics?

 - What is the far right organized like in rural areas? Who is part of their network? What do the national parties of the far right have planned for rural areas?

 - What methodological tools could be useful for studying the political activities of the far right in rural areas?

 - What theoretical tools can help us understand the connection between the far right and rural places?

papers.title: “You can’t eat electricity”: A new reactionary ‘politics of the rural’
Rural-Urban Social Antagonisms and Political Polarity: A Marxist Perspective
Bureaucracy in agriculture as a subject of far-right mobilisation
Rural metapolitics – German far-right agricultural policy in the 21st century
Interstitial Radicalisation: the Far Right and Farmer Discontent in Europe

sessions.title: A Change in the Climate
sessions.description:
The climate crisis is affecting everyday lives worldwide. It is asking many of us to cope with more frequent extreme weather events, disrupted food and ecological systems, and a growing incidence of diseases that are food, water and vector borne. The urgency of these risks has led to a rise in climate ‘emergency’ declarations. While such declarations are intended as a call to action, they can evoke panic, compromising the time and attention required to prioritise responses rooted in justice, care and solidarity. In this session, we reflect on what it means to centre justice in responses to the climate crisis and to make space for alternative ways of knowing, being and living in a climate-changed world. 

 In the first part of the session, Dr Sarah Bell will be in conversation with Dr Liz Crow of Roaring Girl Productions. Liz has been leading ‘A Change in the Climate’; a multi-disciplinary visual arts project exploring the interweave of climate change and disability, echoing the nuanced fragility and strength within disabled bodies and non-human nature. The project has created a series of striking still and moving images, which speak to existential questions at the heart of social injustice and the climate crisis; who gets framed out and whose needs are centred, who gets to live and who gets discarded, and how do we make space for joy and remember why the world is worth fighting for? 

 Liz will introduce and discuss these pieces in conversation with Sarah, who is leading the Sensing Climate project; a five-year study, examining opportunities for responses to the climate crisis to build fairer societies, building on the priorities and insights of people who are disabled, d/Deaf, neurodivergent and people with long-term health conditions.

 In the second part of the session, Liz and Sarah will be joined by three panellists who will draw on their work to reflect on the questions raised by ‘A Change in the Climate’ in the context of wider social and climate justice issues:

 - Dr Harrie Larrington-Spencer, Research Fellow at the University of Westminster, whose work is rooted at the intersection of inclusive environmentalism, feminist theory and critical disability studies. 
- Dr Richard Scriven, Geographer at University College Cork, who will draw on his work in advocacy and human rights law to discuss the environmental concerns of disabled people and the need to strengthen the intersectional dimension of environmental justice. 
- Dr Danielle Johnson, Postdoctoral Researcher at Durham University, whose work spans experiences of marginalisation from climate action amongst Māori communities in Aotearoa New Zealand and, more recently, the risks of power outages in extreme weather amongst disabled and neurodivergent communities in northeast England. 

 Each panel member will have ten minutes to respond to the questions raised by ‘A Change in the Climate’ before opening the floor to questions from, and discussion with, the audience.

sessions.title: Wither Human(ity)? (2)
sessions.description:
If inequalities are embedded in the very definition of the human, how are these hierarchies lived, reproduced, and contested in everyday spaces? Building directly on the conceptual groundwork of Wither Human(ity)? (I) , this session turns to urban, environmental, securitised, and healthcare contexts in which access to humanity is actively negotiated.
 Engaging the conference theme Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places , the session demonstrates that struggles for justice are simultaneously struggles over who is recognised as fully human – and whose life, care, and voice are rendered expendable.

 In Rio de Janeiro, ethnographic research with K-9 security trainers reveals controversies around “humanisation”. Here, the boundaries between human and non-human are not abstract philosophical concerns but practical matters shaping training regimes and security practices. Situated cosmologies complicate academic moves beyond the human, revealing how colonial legacies and necropolitical conditions structure lived understandings of humanity.

 In the UK, racialised environmental injustice unfolds through exclusion from green spaces. Black and Black-mixed heritage communities describe material barriers and affective landscapes of fear and hesitation. At the same time, outdoor community groups cultivate alternative geographies of belonging and wellbeing, reclaiming nature as a space of dignity and collective presence. Nature becomes a site where humanity is denied and reasserted.

 Urban vacant spaces in Brussels offer another terrain of contestation. Framed alternately as ecological sanctuaries or as potential shelters for homeless and migrant populations, these spaces expose tensions between multispecies justice and claims to housing and refuge. Vacancy becomes a lens through which competing constructions of the human – and of justice – collide.

 In healthcare, analysis of NHS guidance and policy reveals how institutional discourse positions patients as passive recipients rather than epistemic partners. Migration, linguistic diversity, and socio-economic difference are flattened within standardised health literacy frameworks. Through datafication and professional authority, experiential knowledge is marginalised, producing epistemic injustice and reinforcing unequal access to care.

 Across these sites, hierarchies of life, care, and voice are spatially organised and institutionally reproduced. Just places cannot be achieved without confronting how institutional, urban, and policy regimes differentially recognise, protect, and deny humanity in practice.

 Together with Wither Human(ity)? (I) , this session advances a relational and anti-racist rethinking of humanity as both conceptual problem and lived struggle – linking ontological critique of inequalities to everyday geographies of injustice.

papers.title: The “Human” in more-than-human research: “humanization” in Rio de Janeiro’s K-9 security
Muted Voices In Health Literacy: How Discursive Power Produces Epistemic Injustice In Urban Healthcare
Urban Vacant Spaces: Refuges for Vulnerable Human and Non-Human Life?

sessions.title: Investigating the geopolitics of private satellite infrastructure: new pathways for connectivity and conflict
sessions.description:
Corporate satellite companies, such as Starlink and Amazon Leo, have promised to rewire global connectivity and communication through their new generation of low-earth orbit (LEO) constellations. The launch of thousands of these satellites into orbital space will have significant consequences on earth, as digital infrastructure begins to shift from terrestrial to space-based systems. The political, economic, and security impact of such a shift remains untold and untested. For example Starlink’s potential connectivity is creating new connectivities between and among scam economies, conflict zones and smuggling networks. At the same time, increased accessibility of satellite communication is challenging state-based monopolies and creating new opportunities for resilience and improvisation in the face of infrastructural failure. As power circulates from legacy and state-owned telecommunications giants to a club of private enterprises, new questions around state-sovereignty, technological independence, and illicit economies arise. This call for papers seeks submissions rooted in political geography that identify emergent patterns, themes, and issues related to the emergence of privately-owned planetary infrastructures.While not limited to these issues, panellists will discuss one or more of the following questions: 

 - How are privately-owned satellite companies, such as Starlink, reshaping geopolitical relations?

 - How do we trace the impacts of these innovations, are there particular geographical methods or approaches that need to be brought to the fore? 

 - To what extent are such innovations addressing or exacerbating existing global inequalities?

papers.title: The view from above: extra-terrestrial bordering and new scales of seeing
Materiality in motion: tracing the illicit deployment of space-based connectivity

sessions.title: Repoliticising Inequality: Organised Abandonment, Carceral Violence and Actively Working Towards Abolition (3): Heart: hope, imagination, futures
sessions.description:
This experimental 1-day session at this year's RGS conference, invites creative and conventional contributions that centre around abolitionist understandings of “organised abandonment” (Gilmore 2022, Arefin and Khan 2024, Mei-Singh 2024). Mei-Singh (2024) (drawing on Gilmore 2008 and Bhandar 2018) offers a brief overview of the state of the concept:

 Existing scholarship on organized abandonment conveys how neoliberal state reorganization sacrifices particular places. This results in the flight of economic activity and jobs, environmental degradation, underemployment, the diminishment and privatization of public programs, and the disappearance of entire ways of life, all part and parcel of the disregard of those subjugated by a racial capitalist social order.

 The session(s) invite activists, scholars, artists, community workers and others (at all career stages and from diverse locations) to come together - in ways that are life-giving, creative and transcend academic convention - to continue ‘sharpening our focus of how such abandonment results from plans, as well as abdications of responsibility’ (Gilmore 2023). Arefin and Khan (2024) maintain that organised abandonment is ‘not a passive withdrawal, but an active plot’. They (Ibid:) go on to add that this ‘invites the same precision in our counterplot’.

 Therefore, the session(s) also invite us to continue sharpening our focus on how we counter and unmake organised abandonment. We see this countering and unmaking as ‘actively working towards abolition’ (Kaba nd) and finding, recovering and restoring lifeways that have been forcibly abdicated (Mei-Singh 2024). Sessions include papers and micro-workshop activities that help deepen how we understand organised abandonment and how we resist and restore in the wake of the ‘rupturing of life-giving relations’ (Mei-Singh 2024). This matters because grassroots activists continue to theorise abolition as an ethics, practice, struggle and way of being, that requires the development of “dual power” (Kaba and Ritchie 2021) where emergent and oppositional forms of resistance coexist and interdepend (Brown 2017, Ritchie 2023, Chhibber 2025, see also Hughes 2021). That is:

 understanding, opposing, fighting and unmaking carceral systems, organised abandonment and related ways of being 

 coexist and interdepend with 

 restoring, building and practicing life-affirming systems and ways of being that refuse to use organised abandonment and carcerality as solutions to entrenched social problems (Davis 2003, Kaba and Ritchie 2022, Ritchie 2023, Kass 2025). 

 ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀ ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

 We see this session not as a way of coming to a definitive disciplinary conclusion or resolution on what organised abandonment is but rather as a way of coming together with an openness for whatever emerges, be this contradictions, contingencies or interrelations. We encourage ‘acting and speaking in ways that are not about mastery, but rather about audacity or incompleteness, because [we] don’t think any of us can be complete in our understanding or in our pursuit of freedom on our own’ (Bhattachrya np 2025).

 ✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀✿ ❊ 🌸 🌷 ❀

papers.title: Nature connection as making liminal space to access embodied knowledge
'Abolition is a group improvisation’: A Sound Workshop
Envisioning an autonomous, feminist and anti-racist data server

sessions.title: Grounding nuclear imaginaries: Placing geographies of nuclear ‘progress’, protest, and care (3)
sessions.description:
The nuclear continues to preoccupy our minds. As energy, weapon, mineral, medicine, and waste, nuclear issues and the social imaginaries that underpin them continue to exert a shaping force on global and local affairs. This session invites scholars and practitioners to attend to these contemporary imaginaries: their modes of production, internal logics and inconsistencies, the possibilities they enable or foreclose, and the forms of power that they underpin. 

 Two questions are of particular interest. The first is the extent to which contemporary nuclear imaginaries are tied to or inherit from ideas, knowledges, and aesthetics of the past. Are these novel formations, or do they bear the traces and traumas (Schwab, 2020) of bygone periods like the Cold War (Faux, 2024)? To what extent are efforts to renew the nuclear (for example the efforts to brand civil nuclear power as ‘green’) an effective strategy in re-ontologising it? At the heart of this is a consideration of the nature of nuclear imaginaries as a potent mode of reproducing historical patterns (and violences) as well as a new set of tools to grasp the specificity of the present. The second centres the relationship between nuclear imaginaries and place. In the last two decades, important ethnographic and historical research have sought to articulate various ‘local’ nuclear imaginaries tied to particular lands, nation-states, governments, and scientific organisations: for example, an American post-Cold War nuclear imaginary (Masco, 2008), British nuclear cultures (Hogg, 2016; Hogg and Brown, 2019; Hughes, 2012), and civic epistemologies of the Indian elite classes (Anderson, 2010; Haines, 2019). Yet, outside of this corpus, scholars note a tendency in popular imaginaries of the nuclear to untether from place. The historian Lisa Yoneyama (1999, 2023) calls this tendency nuclear universalism, where the abstraction of nuclear imaginaries – especially into global imaginaries by way of an aesthetics of apocalypse – obscures the structural violence inflicted upon specific communities and places erased by these imaginaries (Hecht, 2012; Nixon, 2011; Voyles 2015). The disembedding of imaginaries from place has an obscuring function; it is only by concealing the long-term material and ecological burdens of nuclear mining, processing, and waste storage that its inherent promises (e.g. of cheap and endless energy) can be upheld (Pottin, 2024). These are nuclear imaginaries that ironically inhibit imagination itself, making it hard to think otherwise (Deckard, 2022) and posing a challenge to forms of anti-nuclear resistance (Faison and Fields, 2024; Urwin 2025). 

 Taken together, these two questions guide what promises to be a lively exchange on the forms and functions of nuclear imaginaries in yet another supposed ‘nuclear renaissance’. The session turns on the central provocation that it ought to matter where thought takes place and how thought makes place. Only by attending to the worlding capacities of imaginaries might we be able to consider ways to shift them (Carpenter, 2016). We invite papers that consider the recursive relationships between nuclear imaginaries and nuclear places.

papers.title: Epistemic Culture and Necropolitical Violence of the Soviet Atomic Project
Stranded Fuel-Cycle Futures: West Valley, Barnwell, and the Promissory Afterlife of Reprocessing
“We need to keep the road open”: Enacting fuel supply-chain imaginaries against unruly nuclear
Atom in the Pacific: Tezuka Osamu’s Pacific critique of Japan’s “Atoms for Peace”
