sessions.title: Unfuturisms: Unsettling the Rhythms of Planetary Social Life (2)
sessions.description:
Progressive, modern, technological, alternative, utopian, socialist, planetary, radical, ecological, revolutionary, catastrophic, subaltern, sustainable, resilient, more-than-human, speculative, pluriversal, dystopian, in the ruins, from below: we’re awash in futures to come —always yet come— futures that are neither quite delivered upon nor ever quite given up on.

 Across human geography and the humanities and social sciences more broadly, extraordinary amounts of energy have been devoted to critically attending to the future’s unjust and unevenly distributed nature, and to the search for alternative futures in the Anthropocene. Defying pronouncements of the future’s imminent yet slow “cancellation,” the attempt has often been that of reprising and expanding the archive of “futurisms” (across geographies, political traditions, literary genres and forms of activism) to re-open possible futures for a planetary age. 

 And yet, the fundamental problem is that “the future” is not what it used to be (Savransky, 2024, 2025). It was modernity’s faith in progress that enabled one to envisage the future as something one could bring unto oneself, unto others, unto the earth . But in an age of irreversible planetary instability and climate chaos, what was once a source of hope and aspiration, and a problem of anticipation, imagination and redemption, has become precarious when not an outright extortion, a source of fear and dread, a problem of finitude and catastrophe, managed retreat and adaptation (Savransky & Lundy, 2022). 

 This session is an invitation to gather around a provocation: not to envisage alternative futures but to envisage unfuturisms— alternatives to “the future” as the organising grammar and rhythm of planetary social life. What becomes of the idea of “the future” on an earth rendered forever unstable? What would it take to slip out of the future’s grip and stray out of the uneven and unjust striations through which it continues to dig and groove the terrain? What other rhythms of planetary life —human and more— might be rendered perceptible in the act of giving “the future” a pass?

papers.title: Against the Future: Waqt, Temporal Sovereignty, and the Ethical density of the Now
Frameworks for Unfuturing: On “Fairbourne: a Framework for the Future”
affiliations: Institute for Language, Literature, and Anthropology (CSIC)

papers.title: Who wants to live forever? Corporate immortality and the territorial expansion of the deep future
Endgames, failing, and futures without disease

sessions.title: Utility natures: the financial lives of water and energy
sessions.description:
Amid tumbling wages and the spiralling cost-of-living crisis, utility poverty has emerged as a defining feature of contemporary life. New logics of accumulation and financialisation in both the energy and water sectors have deepened household indebtedness and are reorganising the relations of social reproduction in ways that overlap and compound precarity (Meehan 2025; Koutlou 2025; Purcell et al. 2020, 2025; Furlong 2021; Desvallées 2020; Loftus et al. 2019; Petrova 2018). Twinned affordability crises in energy and water are, at their heart, a question of politics and power. Yet while geographers have advanced critical understandings of financialisation and its pernicious effects on water and energy provision, attention to the ‘lived experiences’ of utility natures are often amiss and are seldom considered in tandem. What can we learn from the lived experiences of water and energy struggles? What shapes peoples’ experiences of utility poverty? What lines of commonalities and difference can we draw across these two sectors? And in an era of increasing inequality, wracked by crises in household social reproduction and (un)affordable living, what forms of struggle and political insurgency have emerged in response? This session seeks to centre the ‘lived experiences’ of utility natures by bringing together perspectives that reveal the interwoven relations of water, energy, and finance. We welcome presentations that advance critical theoretical and/or empirical knowledge about the experience, logics, governance, politics, and/or struggles of utility natures. At a minimum, we encourage papers that start to draw connections among different utility natures and consider the benefits and/or challenges of bringing these areas of scholarship together to understand, contest, and potentially transform the financial lives of water and energy. Topics of interest may include: 

 - Utility poverty (energy and/or water) and its lived experiences, especially within the cost-of-living crisis 
- Financial subjectivities and the multi-scalar role of fiscal infrastructures in the production of utility poverty
- Techniques of financial discipline — such as water, gas, or electricity shutoffs — and their impacts and repercussions on households 
- Utility shutoff moratoriums and their legal and critical geographies 
- State and private utility governing logics; how they address, produce, or reproduce new inequalities and exclusion
- Logics of value grabbing in utility natures and their impacts on households 
- Racial capitalism and utility natures; legacies of racialised and class-based urban planning and struggles in energy and/or water poverty 
- Platform capitalism and the role of digital technologies that mediate and govern utility natures
- Financialisation of life’s essential services and infrastructures, and the cascading effects on unaffordability and exclusion, profit, and power 
- Critical approaches to assistance programs and experiences of radical alternatives 
- Utility natures and household adaptation to climate change and extreme weather (heat, flooding, drought, cold) 

 ‘Utility Natures’ is modelled after a session we are running on the same topic at the POLLEN conference in Barcelona, July 2026. Subject to engagement and interest, we plan to develop key insights and contributions from both sessions into a special issue.
 This panel is intended to be in person only.

papers.title: Authoritarian neoliberalism, energy debt and the making of depletive enclosure
Of money, mobiles and off-grid solar
Historicizing energy utility debt beyond rising rates and unaffordability
Financing Inclusion Through Exclusion:  Debt, Utilities, and the Political Economy of Medellín’s Urban Transformation

sessions.title: Emerging voices in economic geography (3)
sessions.description:
This session invites postgraduate researchers (PGRs) and early career researchers (ECRs) to share emerging research, ideas, and provocations in economic geography. We aim to create a supportive and inclusive space for scholars at the early stages of their academic journeys to showcase work in progress, explore new directions, and connect with others working across diverse traditions and contexts.

 We welcome contributions that engage with any aspect of economic geography, broadly defined. This may include (but is not limited to) research on:

 - Economic inequalities and uneven development
- Digital, platform, and creative economies
- Gender, race, identity and the economy
- Geographies of markets, production, exchange and consumption
- Geographies of money, finance and financialisation
- Labour, work, livelihoods, and care
- Housing, real estate, and the built environment
- Regional, urban, and rural economies
- Sustainability, climate, and green transitions
- Industrial change, innovation, and entrepreneurship
- Informal, alternative, and community economies
- Intellectual histories and futures of economic geography
- Theories and theorising in economic geography
- Methods and methodological innovation in research on the economy

 The session is intentionally open and exploratory. We particularly encourage:

 - Papers based on PhD research or postdoctoral projects
- Works-in-progress or presentation of preliminary findings
- Contributions that experiment with methods, theory, or interdisciplinary approaches
- Submissions from researchers who are new to the RGS–IBG conference or academic conferencing more broadly

 Our goal is to foster constructive discussion, peer support, and confidence-building in a friendly environment. Presenters will be encouraged to share not only findings, but also questions, challenges, and future directions.

papers.title: Beyond Homogeneity: Differentiated Engagement with High- and Low-Risk Fringe Finance among People with Disabilities in Waterloo Region, Ontario, Canada
Permissibility, power and exclusion at the economic frontiers of vacant property in London
Performing Sustainability: Symbolic Governance and the State–Finance Nexus in Indonesia’s Green Transition
Exploring the extent of spatial mismatch between employment and housing in European cities

sessions.title: Integrating Economic and Place-Based Perspectives on Peripheral Development
sessions.description:
Contemporary non-metropolitan and peripheral regions are navigating a complex landscape of globalisation-driven pressures, rapid technological restructuring, and demographic shifts. While traditional models emphasise the primacy of urban agglomeration (Duranton & Puga, 2004), recent evidence (e.g. Pike et al., 2011; Rodríguez-Pose, 2013) indicates that many peripheral territories exhibit significant structural resilience and distinct evolutionary trajectories that challenge standard growth paradigms. An increasing number of studies and publications are focusing on innovation beyond agglomerations, and the focus on exploiting growth potential is being contested, and other types of goals as well-being (Randolph & Currid-Halkett, 2022), social and cultural values (Leick & Lang, 2018), are highlighted as important in a sustainable and resilient policy perspective (Syssner & Erlingsson, 2023).

 Current research, however, remains characterised by a degree of theoretical fragmentation. Research often bifurcates into siloed analyses: focusing either on economic drivers (e.g., innovation systems, human capital, labour market dynamics) or on more place-based dimensions (e.g., institutional governance, quality of life, and social cohesion).

 However, regional development processes are not just purely economistic processes, but rather a conflictual process in which different opinions or even divergent views emerge and compete through different language relationships (Calignano & Nilsen, 2024). This session, therefore, seeks to synthesise these perspectives by examining the multi-dimensional interactions between social, environmental, and economic processes. We specifically aim to explore how structural conditions intersect with localised agency, place-based identity, and spatially anchored capital to influence regional outcomes.

 By moving beyond deficit-based frameworks, this session invites contributions that investigate how non-metropolitan territories leverage territorial rationality and endogenous agency to secure their long-term economic trajectories. We welcome theoretical, empirical, and methodological papers that bridge the divide between economic and place-based scholarship to provide a more holistic understanding of development in the periphery.

 We welcome papers on topics such as:

 - endogenous agency in regional development

 - well-being as a policy goal in regional development

 - the geography of 'left-behind' places

 -  competing narratives between "growth-oriented" and "well-being-oriented" in regional policy

 - holistic understanding of sustanable regional development

papers.title: Beyond Growth-Led Development: The Semiconductor Industry and the Social Cost of Regionalism in Taiwan's Industrial Periphery
The twin transition: informed policy advice for a peripheral case
Firm Strategies and Regional Development in Norway
Putting Agents in Agency: The Role of Patriotic Capital in Local and Regional Development

sessions.title: Geographies of Intensity (3)
sessions.description:
Intensity appears to be a problem today. The crisis prone present seems to be characterised by too much of the wrong sort of intensity. Claims are made that subjects are too outraged or too disaffected. Whilst some are baited to outrage amid today’s digital economy, others lie flat as they feel a pernicious combination of expectation and economic downturn. Extended periods of stretched out waiting for fragile promises to materialise (Pettit 2023), coexist with hustle cultures of constant activity (Thieme 2025). Calls to foster passionate attachment to work coexist with quiet quitting (Hong 2022). Politics amid the extended end of neoliberalism and as mediated by digital capitalism is characterised as being full of uneasy mixtures of outrage and boredom. Maladies of intensity – from burnout (Han 2015) to overstimulation – appear to serve as the signature affects for a present characterised as at once too much and too little, both too intense and not intense enough.

 But in addition to being key to diagnosing the present conjuncture in all its overdetermined complexity, intensity can be found across multiple geographies. Value is created through generating intensity, whether in new digital worlds, through branding and hype, or via entertainment forms (e.g. the Blockbuster film). Capitalism has frequently been understood to involve a logic of intensification as value is extracted from life, labour and land. Political forms offer and promise intensity, whether the quietness and disaffection of liberalism or the rage infused enjoyment of right-wing populism (Anderson & Secor 2025). Forms of power work through intensity. Foucault (1977), for example, writes of how sovereignty works through ‘visible scenes of intensity’ as he details the spectacle of the scaffold. Events that are apprehended as crises are mediated through intensities that can change how they become present in everyday life. Mediated in terms of the catastrophic, climate change might be met with boredom and other disaffected feelings, for some subjects. The ‘immigration crisis’ gathers intensities around it, as authoritarian intensities are performed through mediated spectacle of violent raids targeting racialised subjects. At the same time, intensity is central to the lived experience of place. Whether the buzz of a city, the density of a crowd (McFarlane 2016) or stillness of a landscape, particular kinds of good intensity are governed, desired, valued, and experienced. This set of sessions explores the cultures, politics and economies of intensity.

papers.title: Imperfect Affective Infrastructures: Radical Housing, Inconvenience, and Political Intensity
Intensity, pathology, burnout
From regime to machine: Intensive eliminatory velocities in Palestine
Atmospheres and Intensities of Genocidal Weathering: Hypervigilance and Exhaustion in Gaza
When care runs flat: the intensities of emotional labouring in service work

sessions.title: Rethinking Educational Pedagogies and Practices in Unequal Contexts
sessions.description:
Education is frequently positioned as a vehicle for social mobility and justice, yet institutions are deeply uneven places, shaped by widening social, economic and spatial inequalities. This session invites contributions that critically examine student and/or staff experiences of teaching, learning and assessment within these unequal contexts. It invites contributions that explore how educational experiences, institutional resources and academic labour are geographically differentiated and unevenly valued.

 Contributions may focus on geographical disparities in education provision, for example widening participation, inequalities in academic careers and working conditions, in addition to broader spatial politics of internationalisation, and unequal impacts of neoliberal austerity, marketisation and institutional restructuring.

 The session also welcomes work on campus geographies, such as inclusive pedagogies, assessment practices and curriculum design, and how these are inflected by inequalities related to class, race, gender, disability, citizenship and place. Papers might also discuss digital divides, accessibility, caring responsibilities, precarity and institutional policies and practices shaping everyday educational experiences in classrooms, online spaces and the field.

 Crucially, the session seeks to move beyond diagnosis to consider resistance, reform and reimagining of power, privilege and exclusion. This includes reflections on policy interventions, collective action and alternative educational practices. Contributions may be empirical, conceptual or practice-based, and may engage with students, staff or institutional policy contexts. The session aims to create space for critical discussions about how educational spaces might become more just places of learning and work.

 Potential themes: 

 - Teaching, learning and pedagogy in unequal institutional contexts

 - Assessment practices, fairness and equity

 - Student experience, belonging and exclusion

 - Staff experience, workload, wellbeing and precarity

 - Widening participation, access and retention

 - AI, digital and hybrid learning practices and environments

 - Disability, accessibility and inclusive practices

 - Institutional policies, metrics and governance

 - Marketisation, austerity and institutional change

 - Resistance, reform and reimagining educational practices

papers.title: Pedagogies and Place: Spatial Inequalities and Blended Doctoral Education
Making Virtual Field Trips Inclusive in Geography Education: A Case Study of NE London
From Margins to Methods: Women of Colour Navigating Higher Education
Theorizing Self-Accommodation as Spatial Practice
Uneven pedagogical approaches to developing critical thinking skills among students between further and higher education

sessions.title: Just parks? Making urban green spaces more accessible and inclusive (2)
sessions.description: Urban green spaces are widely acknowledged as critically important amenities that provide a range of social and ecological benefits. But the provision, scale and quality of these spaces tends to vary considerably between and within different cities. Even in cities where there is adequate provision, green spaces are not necessarily welcoming, safe or usable for many disadvantaged or marginalised groups. The Landscape Geography Working Group and the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group invite papers that address the accessibility and inclusivity of urban green spaces. This co-sponsored session aims to bring together academics working in disparate fields, including leisure studies, urban planning, landscape architecture, urban sociology, political ecology, and health studies, and various strands of geography. The title of the session ‘just parks?’ emphasises that provision tends to be unjust but also suggests that part of the solution may lie in directing attention beyond conventional green spaces. Papers might address the obstacles that restrict access to urban green spaces including physical, financial, administrative and symbolic barriers. However, the session is framed positively, and we also want to include papers that suggest how progressive change may be achieved. We have used the term ‘making’ in the subtitle because we want to explore how urban green spaces might be designed, managed or programmed differently to make them more accessible/inclusive. The co-convenors have worked together after presenting at previous RGS-IBG Conferences and we aim to provide a forum through which future collaborations might emerge.

papers.title: Planning, Parks, and Housing Markets: Exploring Park Accessibility in Zurich-Schwamendingen Through the Lens of Housing
affiliations: ETH Wohnforum – ETH CASE, Zurich, Switzerland

papers.title: Multiple Sensory Twins:  Modelling Neurodiversity-Inclusive Urban Parks
Rethinking Urban Parks through Edible Intervention: Spatial Acceptability, Governance and the Conditions of Inclusion
Negotiated Publicness: Reconceptualising Rongxing Garden as Resilient Socio-spatial Infrastructure
Landscapes of identity: The case of Al-Istiqlal national Park, Palestine
Why Access Is Not Enough: Temporal and Everyday Barriers to Green Inclusion in Cardiff

sessions.title: Heat-stress in and Beyond Humans: Critical Planetary Health Perspectives (2)
sessions.description:
Over 2.4 billion workers worldwide are exposed to excessive heat each year, resulting in nearly 19,000 deaths and tens of millions of occupational injuries and illnesses (ILO, 2025). Heat also affects more-than-human life, including the deaths of over 650,000 farm animals during the 2021 Pacific Northwest heatwave and thousands of chickens in India’s 2015 extreme heat events (Huber, 2022). These patterns position heat not simply as environmental exposure but as a lived health condition produced through intertwined climatic, ecological, and socio-economic transformations that unevenly distribute vulnerability across human and other-than-human life.

 This panel foregrounds heat as an embodied and relational health experience. Rather than treating climate change as a distant environmental driver, we approach heat as something encountered through working bodies, homes, infrastructures, care practices, and everyday survival strategies. Heat reshapes hydration, fatigue, sleep, cognition, pregnancy outcomes, and chronic illness, while also altering patterns of disease transmission, microbial activity, and multispecies cohabitation. Health, in this sense, emerges not from isolated bodies but from entangled environments.

 We place these dynamics in conversation with EcoHealth, One Health, and Planetary Health frameworks to examine how well-being and care are (re)produced across human and more-than-human relations (Cole & Deshpande, 2019; Hoogeveen et al., 2023). While these frameworks emphasize interconnected systems, lived experiences of heat reveal uneven burdens shaped by labour precarity, housing conditions, access to care, and social marginalisation. Occupational exposure often restricts possibilities for rest and recovery, making heat a workplace health and safety issue as well as a broader well-being concern (Kjellstrom et al., 2019).

 At the same time, responses to the climate–health nexus frequently remain technocratic and depoliticised, privileging metrics and thresholds over lived experience (Sim & Parsons, 2025). We instead consider heat harms as ethical and relational conditions shaping care, responsibility, and vulnerability across species (Celermajer et al., 2025). This perspective foregrounds everyday coping practices, cultural understandings of heat, and community-based adaptations as central to health. This panel invites papers that ask questions such as (but not limited to):

 - How is heat experienced as an embodied condition across human and more-than-human bodies in specific places and environments?
- In what ways does heat and its related indicators reorganise labouring conditions and occupational health for working bodies, including vulnerable human labour groups and more-than-human workers such as livestock and working animals, particularly where exposure cannot easily be avoided?
- What critical, methodological, or interdisciplinary interventions are needed to move beyond technocratic or Eurocentric framings of heat and health, thus moving towards multispecies just futures?

 This session extended and exploratory conversation, with an aims to bring together scholars working across environmental, health, labour, and more-than-human geographies to think collectively about heat and its related forms as an embodied, relational, and multispecies condition, and to explore how more just planetary futures might be imagined under conditions of climate change.

 This is an extension to Part 1 of Heat-stress in and Beyond Human/

papers.title: Bodies and bricks in tandem: Weathering the more-than-human rhythms of brick moulding labour in Delhi
Heatwaves and Multiple Precarities: Lessons from the Weathered Experiences of the Contracted, Outdoor Laborers Working at Calcutta Electric Supply Corporation in Kolkata
affiliations: Bielefeld University and University of Bologna

papers.title: Heat, Labour, and the Politics of Habitability

sessions.title: Voluminous Futures (2) Conceptualising futurity through geographies of volume
sessions.description:
Amidst
 intensifying climate volatility, geopolitical contestation, and rapid 
technological acceleration, volume––across air, ice, water, and 
underground strata––have become critical terrains through which actors 
imagine, contest, and enact futures. Far from empty or inert, these 
environments are materially complex, unevenly known, and often only 
partially visible. Their indeterminacy not only complicates existing 
regimes of knowledge and power but
 also makes them particularly potent for imagining and governing 
uncertain futures that exceed conventional planning or forecasting. 
Uncertainty in voluminous environments is, therefore, more than 
something to be pre-emptively managed and contained: it is a generative condition that enables multiple modes of future-making. 

 Foregrounding the imagined futures of volume provides an opportunity to analyse how these spatialities open particular forms
 of futurity whilst foreclosing others, and to interrogate the 
vocabularies––anticipation, speculation, pre-emption, 
projection––through which they are described and enacted. From the 
opening of Arctic passages, the remaking of oceanic territories through 
subterranean and undersea energy, data, and telecommunications 
infrastructures, to the expansion of aerial logistics and drone corridors, imaginaries of volume are reorganising
 territorial claims, political authority, resource frontiers, and forms 
of governance. These projected futures also shape everyday 
subjectivities, affective orientations, multi-species, and non-human worlds. 

 This Voluminous Futures session aims to initiate a dialogue between literature on volume and future(s) through two time slots. In this 100-minute session, the first hour will see four to five paper presentations, followed by a more in-depth discussion about the ideas raised in these papers amongst the presenters and audience, led by the convenors.
 This time slot is bringing together brilliant and original contributions, that discuss voluminous futures across oceans and from Earth’s orbit. The selected contributions are indeed presenting empirical cases as varied as the tracing of the recent evolution of future-making in the depth of the Pacific Ocean, to the disruption of Euro-modern sovereignty by the employment of modern low-orbit satellites sensing, measuring and assessing states territories. Others are discussing the futures of disaster capitalism and their imaginations of varied volumes, both on and off-Earth.

papers.title: Signal Territory Beyond Earth: Technical-Trusteeship in Outer Space Communication
affiliations: Department of Bio-Industry Communication and Development, National Taiwan University

papers.title: 'Domain awareness' and the military territorialization of the volume
Geography of promise, or postponing oceanic futures? Unpacking the social construction of a ‘Thermal Dome’ in the Pacific Ocean off Central American

papers.title: Volume Versus Volatility: Subsurface Survivalisms and (Counter)Hegemonic Imaginaries

sessions.title: Geography and Area Studies in the current conjuncture
sessions.description:
Geography and area studies share a long and troubled history. Both have been deeply entangled with colonial and imperial projects, in which the production of regional knowledge was mobilised to govern, extract, and dominate. The imperative to “know the world” has too often been aligned with the reproduction of violent global orders. Yet this history has never been uncontested. Over recent decades—and with renewed urgency today—geographers and area studies scholars have sought to critically rework these traditions, developing approaches that foreground epistemic plurality, linguistic difference, and situated knowledges, and that challenge Eurocentric hierarchies of theory and method.

 In parallel, activists and scholars in regions such as Latin America/Abya Yala have long attempted to re-imagine themselves as anti-imperial projects grounded in solidarity, mutual aid, and alternative political imaginaries. However, these ambitions are increasingly constrained by the current global conjuncture: the resurgence of overt imperial practices, authoritarian nationalism, and geopolitical realignment. Across the Americas, the Middle East and North Africa, and beyond, external intervention, internal fragmentation, and the weaponisation of knowledge have reshaped the conditions under which area-based research is produced and circulated.

 This session asks: what is the value of area studies within geography today? What strategic possibilities does it offer for articulating anti-colonial, transnational, and dialogical research agendas? How might area studies contribute to the co-production of knowledge across epistemic, linguistic, and political differences—without reproducing extractive or exceptionalist logics? Equally, what could a critical geographical approach bring to area studies? and, finally, what challenges does the current conjuncture pose for such projects, and how might geographers respond?

 By revisiting area studies through a critical geographical lens, the session seeks to open a collective discussion on the futures of geographically grounded, politically engaged scholarship in an increasingly polarised world.

 The session will take the form of a roundtable dialogue between geographers working on Latin American and MENA and encourages active participation of all present.

sessions.title: Multiple readings of abandoned places: governing, living and researching in foreclosed futures
sessions.description:
Abandonment has increasingly emerged as a key concept for understanding contemporary geographies of inequality, marginalisation and urban restructuring. While geographical scholarship has long addressed abandonment through its material manifestations—such as depopulation, housing vacancy, infrastructural decay and territorial decline—these processes have often been framed as residual outcomes of economic restructuring or demographic change. This session instead approaches abandonment as an active, selective and temporally uneven mode of governance, through which institutions govern surplus lives and disposable spaces.

 Building on Agamben’s conceptualisation of abandonment as a relational condition of being simultaneously excluded from and subjected to the law (Agamben, 1998, 2017), and engaging with critical geographical debates (Pratt, 2005; Gilmore, 2007, 2022; Selmeczi, 2012; Leshem, 2017), abandonment is understood here not as absence or failure, but as an organised and normalised technique of power. This perspective foregrounds the intersection of institutional withdrawal, symbolic violence and material neglect, unfolding across long temporalities and embedding violence within everyday life (Bourdieu, 1998; Berlant, 2007; Povinelli, 2011).

 ​As a second analytical layer, the session examines the lived realities of abandonment through the concept of endurance. Endurance, according to the Cambridge dictionary, is “the ability to keep doing something difficult, unpleasant, or painful for a long time” and has been used by Povinelli (2011) and feminist scholars to focus on the mundane, everyday violence without transformation (Wilkinson & Ortega-Alcázar, 2019). Rather than reading endurance as hope, resistance or resilience, this session understands it as an ethical and political condition of living through abandonment—one that makes visible both survival practices and the structural cruelty that necessitates them.

 ​Crucially, the session also invites reflection on how abandonment and endurance are researched. Conducting research in abandoned spaces requires a certain degree of ambiguity and “staying with the trouble” (Haraway, 2016) to do justice to the injustices without resorting to romanticising, solutions or redemption (Dawney & Jellis, 2024). The session thus invites engagement with alternative narratives, pluralities and reading for difference (Gibson-Graham, 2008) that address these themes and critically reflect on how to conduct and present research on “chronic and cruddy” (Povinelli, 2011) everyday forms of violence that persist in abandoned places.

 ​We welcome papers that engage (broadly) with the following questions:

 - How does abandonment operate as a multidimensional and temporally uneven mode of governance across different contexts?

 - What everyday practices sustain life within conditions of abandonment, and at what costs?

 - How do institutional, symbolic and material dimensions of abandonment intersect with experiences of endurance?

 - What methodological and ethical challenges arise when researching and writing about spaces of abandonment and foreclosed futures?

 - How do scholars write about spaces where there are no clear solutions, no redemptive futures, and no easily narratable forms of resistance? How can critical scholarship avoid reproducing territorial stigma or, conversely, romanticising suffering as innovation or resilience?

papers.title: Enduring State Abandonment: Occupation and (Self)Care among Destitute Migrants
Dwelling in China’s Unfinished Homes
Decrepitude and Excess in the Realtor Colony: towards a political economy of urban abandonment
affiliations: University of Illinois in Urbana Champaign

papers.title: Abandonment, not absence: Cultural Infrastructures and Everyday Endurance in Tor Bella Monaca

sessions.title: Nuclear Latitudes: Positions on Landscape, Memory and the Invisible
sessions.description:
Nuclear landscapes are shaped as much by imaginaries—of progress, sacrifice, secrecy, and catastrophe—as by the material infrastructures they host. Yet these imaginaries often operate invisibly, abstracting nuclear processes from the places and communities that bear their consequences. This performance-led panel gathers artist-researchers whose work across sound, film, photography, text, curatorial practice, and site-specific intervention makes these invisible forces knowable. The panel foregrounds artistic practice as geographical method—equipped to render visible and audible what dominant modes of knowledge production leave unseen: the slow, embodied, and materially entangled.

 Convened by Jay Needham, Louise K Wilson, and Frederike Lange, the group of artist-researchers share a commitment to situated knowledge and nuclear placemaking as  subject and method. Their practices treat nuclear sites as constitutive space- places where memory, power, and ecology are produced. Collectively, they consider how issues of contested geographies and justice influenced their practices as well as the sites they have researched and inhabited.

 Through the affective capacity of the arts, the panel will engage with how nuclear imaginaries produce spatiotemporalities—zones, and places, and how these are interpreted, sustained, and challenged through artistic practice.

 Louise K. Wilson and Jay Needham create video and sound-based media art that reflects on nuclear imaginaries through post-memory and auto-ethnographic site visits. Each has dedicated their careers to the creation of performances and exhibitions that harness the capacity of art to express the complex aesthetic and cultural relations of toxic legacies both former and present.

 Frederike Lange ’s curatorial and research practice centres on visualizing the legacies of extractive industries. Her recent project on uranium mining heritage in the German-Czech borderlands combined exhibition-making, oral history, and artistic positions to trace nuclear pasts into the present.

 Gair Dunlop works in text, photography, and video, documentary forms through an artist’s eye to surface remnants of a fantasy military-scientific-pastoral nation. His practice features collaboration and critical-cultural interrogations of Britain’s nuclear imaginary.

 Etsuko Ichikawa ’s contemplative film Echo at Satsop , filmed inside a nuclear cooling tower, creates a space where memory and place  can be experienced. Shaped by the histories of Hiroshima and Fukushima across generations, her work resists explicit declaration in favour of open encounter.

 Harry Willis Fleming owns Bervie Brow Research Station, a former RAF ROTOR station and Cold War bunker in Scotland, secret and off-limits for seventy years. Now converted into a space for artists, it serves as an arena for working with nuclear environments and imagined futures.

 Ken Hollings presents Terminal Desert , a lecture tracing how atomic tests in the American Southwest transformed physical terrain into symbolic wastelands. From 1950s creature features to irradiated nightmares, Hollings reveals how horror thrives in relentless, unforgiving sunlight.

 Together, these contributions position artistic research as a mode of inquiry capable of surfacing what dominant nuclear narratives render invisible: the slow violence of contamination, the persistence of Cold War spatial logics, and the embodied experiences of communities living with and against nuclear infrastructure. The panel argues that art doesn't merely represent nuclear places but actively intervenes in how they are known, remembered, and imagined.

papers.title: Etsuko Ichikawa
Frederike Lange
Gair Dunlop
Dr. Harry Willis Fleming
Jay Needham
Dr. Louise K. Wilson
Ken Hollings

sessions.title: Leisure as an infrastructure of care and belonging in contexts of migration and displacement
sessions.description:
In the context of global migration, forced displacement, and transnational mobility, questions of belonging, wellbeing, and inclusion have become increasingly central (Minca et al., 2022; Phillimore, 2024). This session examines leisure as a collective infrastructure of care and belonging in contexts of migration and displacement, foregrounding how leisure is shaped through community leadership, collaboration, and voluntary labour involving migrant and refugee communities. Leisure spaces and experiences, such as festivals, community events, volunteering initiatives, and other immersive and embodied forms of leisure, are not simply sites of participation or consumption, but are actively produced through relational organising across community, institutional, and public domains. While such infrastructures can enable care, cultural continuity, and social connection (Atalay & Korkut, 2025; De Martini Ugolotti, 2022; Hassanli, Walters, & Williamson, 2021; Hassanli, Walters, & Friedmann, 2020; Kim et al., 2021; Murad & Versey, 2021; Spaaij et al., 2023; Stodolska, 2025; Zhang et al., 2022), they are also shaped by governance arrangements, funding precarity, and uneven distributions of labour and recognition, and may therefore both support wellbeing and reproduce social and spatial inequalities (De Martini Ugolotti, 2022; Hassanli, Walters, & Williamson, forthcoming; McGee & Pelham, 2018).

 Building on this framing, the session brings together leisure and tourism geographies to examine how leisure infrastructures are organised, governed, and sustained, and with what consequences for care and inequality. Leisure is approached through an infrastructural lens, understood as the interconnected networks of people, organisations, spaces, practices, and resources that enable leisure to take shape in relation to migrant and refugee communities. We call for contributions that move beyond individual participation (demand side) to examine who organises leisure, who performs the labour of care, and how organisational and governance conditions shape the capacity of leisure to function as collective care (supply side). In doing so, the session aligns closely with the conference theme Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Spaces.

papers.title: Watching Mardi Gras: Embodied observations on leisure, infrastructure and inequality
Hospidarity and leisure places of care
affiliations: University of Trento; Corvinus University of Budapest

sessions.title: Making Life Possible: Rethinking the South and ‘Unprecedented’ Climate Risk
sessions.description:
Amid accelerating climate breakdown and deepening socioecological crises, cities are increasingly at the forefront of what is being theorized as the urban South—not as a static geography, but as a relational condition of unevenly burdened climate risk, everyday repair practice and resistance under a global political economic system of finance capital. This session seeks to extend and critically engage with emergent debates that look to rethink Southern urbanism through the lens of repair practice (McElvain & de Coss-Corzo, 2025) mediated by finance and within emergent climate economies (Webber et al, 2022).

 Through empirically-grounded provocations, this panel explores how repair—understood as the entangled material, social, and political labour practices that make life possible—both reproduces and contests conditions wrought by climate-driven crises across Southern urban landscapes. We ask: 

 - How do communities in Southern cities improvise, endure, and organize in the face of "unprecedented" climate and political shocks? 
- How do climate breakdown and ‘unprecedented’ climate risk reconfigure the geographies of the urban South? 
- How do state and extra-state forces enforce uneven risk through financialized governance, militarized care, and speculative reconstruction? 
- And how might Southern urban experiences of repair inform reparative, decolonial, and abolitionist futures beyond Eurocentric models of resilience and development?

 References

 McElvain, B., & de Coss-Corzo, A. (2025). Rethinking the urban South? Centering “unprecedented” risk and repair. Urban Geography, 0(0), 1–15. https://doi.org/10.1080/02723638.2025.2561962

 Webber, S., Nelson, S., Millington, N., Bryant, G., & Bigger, P. (2022). Financing Reparative Climate Infrastructures: Capital Switching, Repair, and Decommodification. Antipode, 54(3), 934–958. https://doi.org/10.1111/anti.12806

sessions.title: ‘Ihsaan’ in the Urban Realm: Socio-spatial geographies of more-than-justice (1)
sessions.description:
What if justice is only the minimum condition for our collective urban futures — not the horizon? Much contemporary urban theory is organised around rights, recognition, redistribution, and repair. These frameworks remain vital. Yet across cities worldwide, everyday urban life is also sustained through quieter practices that exceed calculation, obligation, and contract: care, generosity, patience, mutual holding, and collective striving. These are not easily captured by policy categories — yet they keep cities liveable where institutions fall short. This session invites scholars working with “more-than justice” frameworks in the urban realm, geographies that resist capture. Answering a pluriversal call to bring alternative ontological infrastructure, we are using “ihsaan” as a conceptual holder to refer to forms of relating and acting that go beyond what is required—practices of giving, sustaining, and enabling life that are not reducible to legal mandate, policy design, or transactional ethics. It appears in the quiet infrastructures of everyday life: neighbours holding each other up, communities extending care beyond duty, and collective acts that make the city liveable. How can we theorise them otherwise? Building on recent theorisation of ihsaan in politics (Khan, 2019) and urban studies (Shafique, 2024), this session treats ihsaan not as abstract virtue, but as urban praxis. It directs attention to the gap between formal justice and lived socio-material reality, and to the everyday labour that quietly sustains urban life where institutions fall short. We welcome theoretical and empirical contributions that rethink urban ethics, politics, and practice beyond rights-based imaginaries. We ask: // What dimensions of urban life escape the language of justice, and why do dominant urban theories struggle to grasp everyday practices of care, generosity, and dignity? // What forms of urban ethics and value exist beyond justice — beyond entitlement, repair, and compliance — and how can these be theorised without collapsing into moral idealism? // How can we reconceptualise the city as shaped by affirmative flows of desire, generosity, and collective striving, rather than only by conflict, scarcity, claim-making, and protection — and what alternative urban logics of giving, sustaining, and enabling does this reveal? 
 If this resonates with your work, we warmly invite your contribution.

papers.title: Whose Public Space? Place Valuation, the Politics of Visibility, and Everyday Practices in Shanghai’s Xuhui Riverside

sessions.title: Urban inequalities and transformative interventions (1)
sessions.description: Cities are increasingly paradoxical spaces, concurrently representing vast wealth concentration and deeply embedded socio‑economic and spatial disparities. Set within a context of intensifying global crises – from climate change to financial precarity – traditional geographical perspectives are being encouraged to extend diagnosis to critically examine transformative interventions that can reshape urban policy and practice. This session brings together papers that not only identify urban injustices but also critically examine effective solutions to those injustices, thereby challenging the status quo. The core challenge addressed across the papers is how interventions translate from academic critique into tangible political or planning initiatives, thereby ‘shaping agendas’. Many of the papers draw innovative methodologies (for example, comparative longitudinal analysis, participatory action research, and advanced spatial modelling) to critically assess the effectiveness and scalability of these interventions. Ultimately, these papers collectively address the question: How can geographical research on inequality, through rigorous and forward‑thinking analysis, serve as a force for radical urban change, shaping legislative agendas and governance toward a more just urban future?

sessions.title: Just Rural Development, Innovative and Partnership Approaches : towards facilitating resilient communities and/or influencing policy and planning (1)
sessions.description:
Rural challenges persist in challenging acdemic research and practice based innovation though presenting wicked issues that often require or are best addressed though partnership and or place based local solutions and innovative approaches. This session calls for sharing of such examplar research that are currently involved in bringing about integrative and more just policy for rural areas as sustainably planned places. Often such work draws from an integrated acdemic – practitioner understanding of rural lived experience, policy contexts and stakeholder framimgs of the challenges faced. This may support activities and give voice to or representations of normally challenged and/or marginalised groups e.g. creative practice, community based Carbon descent, inclusive heritage, cultural innovations, digital communication, marginalised faming families, housing development, food hubs, rural training and skills innovations and hyper-local relocalisation and health-based and wellbeing projects.

 Papers are especially sought from collaborators involved in seeking just outcomes for rural communities. These may include community development organisations, local pilot case studies and evaluations and also wider academic-practitioner networks. Contributions from underpresented groups are warmly welcomed to enable knowledge sharing.

 The first session welcomes international case studies and calls on early career researchers working in this theme through innovative approaches, building local partnerships and / or influencing rural governance and funding. 

 The second hybrid panel session will invite members to engage in a facilitated lively discussion of innovative case studies, partnerships accressing rural wellbeing and good policy development.

sessions.title: Points of departure: just and sustainable agri-food system transitions across places, scales and temporalities
sessions.description:
Contemporary industrial food systems have a homogenising effect: extractive and exploitative modes of food production, processing, distribution, retail and consumption dominate globally, driving profound environmental harms alongside socio-cultural flattening, political-economic concentration, and deepening inequalities (Clapp 2020; Patel 2007). In response, alternative food movements have proliferated: from slow food, organic, regenerative and agroecological farming to vegan and locavore diets. Yet these initiatives remain marginal to dominant food regimes, often constrained by political economic conditions or absorbed into market-oriented and technocratic logics that blunt their transformative potential (McGregor et al . 2024; Bless 2025; Hurst and Pant 2025; Pant et al . 2025). Meanwhile, state-led responses to crises produced by industrial food systems are fragmented, depoliticised and reactive: outsourcing governance to markets, while lacking appropriate long-term visions. "Just transition" has emerged as a prominent concept to bridge this fragmentation, invoked across policy, civil society and grassroots movements alike as a framework for navigating the economic and social upheavals of food systems transformation. 

 Yet the rhetorical power of just transition has not been matched by clarity about how they are governed, by whom, through what processes, and in whose interests. This session interrogates the plurality of just transitions across five empirical contributions spanning distinct scales, temporalities, sectors and geographies: from gleaning networks in the US and grain networks in New York and Scotland, to civic food initiatives in Australia, dairy sector policy gaps, and epistemic authority in UK agri-food transformation networks.  

 The papers expose a common tension between the promise of participatory, place-specific alternatives and the structural constraints — bureacratic, economic, epistemic — that constrain their capacity for transformation. The panel foregrounds place-based and peripheral imaginaries as essential to navigating the impasse between macro-level policy and micro-level practice, interrgoating how just food alternatives operate beyond, and in friction with, dominant institutional norms (Tribaldos & Kortetmäki 2022; Coenen et al. 2012). A key concern throughout is how transitions can occur without undermining rural livelihoods or weakening the social and economic cohesion of communities that depend on existing food insutries, indicating that justice may require more robust relational infrastructures and flexible processes, rather than purely top-down, measurable outcomes. 

  This panel contends that just agri-food transitions cannot be forged through technical or managerial consensus but must be negotiated across the irreducibly plural and inherently messy visions that arise from differently situated places, scales and temporalities. Justice requires not solely more ambitious policy, but honest reckoning with whose perspectives are privileged and what futures are built into, or foreclosed by, prevailing transition imaginaries.

papers.title: ReGraining Ground: Exploring Regional Alternative Grain Networks in Scotland and New York
Cultivating Ecologies of Mattering: A Food Systems Transition Design Framework for Cultivating  Care Through Food
Who is the “our” in “Our recommendations”? A network analysis of knowledge synthesis in a UK agri-food systems governance initiative
Policy gaps for a just transition in the Australian Dairy Sector

sessions.title: Sustainable Future Making and its (Uneven) Geographies (1)
sessions.description:
As climate change gathers pace with distinctly uneven geographical impacts and a growing number of world regions find themselves confronted with the socio-economic vulnerabilities arising from intersecting planetary crises, calls gather pace to envisage and enact a distinct future that is climate sensitive, environmentally sustainable and socio-ecologically fairer and just. 

 In this context we have seen increasing attention being given to future oriented frameworks on matters of the environment, climate change and sustainability. This includes compelling or dystopic narratives of future strategically mobilised as desirable or inevitable (Arnall and Hilson, 2023; Kanarp et al.,2025; Peakock and Devine-Wright, 2025) and social action that builds on the symbolic and discursive power of spatial imaginaries to challenge low-carbon futures (Ryder et al., 2025). We also witness the triumph of anticipatory governance and planning (Anderson, 2010; Granjou et al., 2017) and of future oriented tools, including scenarios, deliberative mapping foresight, anticipatory gaming, visioning, experimentation, utilised in imagining sustainable futures (Bellamy et al., 2022; Candy and Dunagan, 2017; Hebinck et al., 2018; Muiderman et al., 2017; Oomen et al., 2021). Research also directs attention to more radical visions of the future, including prefigurative futuring practices (Burnett and Nunes, 2021; Jeffrey and Dyson, 2020) and counter-imaginaries emerging from activism (Matheney et al., 2024), alongside efforts to build abundant futures (Collard et al., 2015) and liberatory futures (Narayanan, 2023). As such, scholarship increasingly points to the emotions and affects involved in constraining, enabling, accelerating or delaying transitions to sustainable futures (Durnova et al., 2025; Martiskainen and Sovacool, 2021).

 This sensibility to futures, however, also encounter critical challenges in the form, for instance, of instances of resistance (Caris and Cao, 2024; Hilson and Arnall, 2024) or greenlash/backlash (Alter and Zürn, 2020; Wild et al., 2018); embedded forms of thinking and acting that can captivate otherwise possible futures (Haajer and Oomen, 2025); ongoing and unresolved tensions around exclusion and marginalisation that can spur feelings of futurelessness and stymie sustainable futures (Della Bosca and Gillespie, 2018; Lewis, 2024; Tutton, 2023); (ideological) power structures that perpetuate high carbon futures (Bedford et al., 2023) and geo-political risks, uncertainties, changes that can disrupt desirable futures (++). 

 It is within this urgent context that we propose this session as a space to think critically and collectively about this future turn on matters of environment, climate change and sustainability. We are interested in sharing and learning from a range of geographical contexts and sectoral domains and invite contributions that conceptually and empirically explore the notion of ‘sustainable future making’, sharpen the focus on its (uneven) geographies, and deepen our understanding of the generative potential of these processes. Possible topics include:

 - Imagining and Enacting Sustainable Futures
- Futures as Governing Devices, the Governance of Sustainable Futures
- Contested Sites and Negotiated Trajectories of Sustainable Futures
- Temporalities of Sustainable Future Making
- Co-producing sustainable futures: Methodological considerations and challenges
- (Un)making Sustainable Futures: Power and Inequalities
- Emotional geographies of sustainable future-making

papers.title: Sustainable Futures at the Frontlines: Navigating Complexity and Capacity in the Co-production of Equity-Centered Urban Transitions
Forests in focus, labour in the fine print: digital cattle tracking in the Brazilian Amazon

sessions.title: Environmental geography, invasion ecology and the politics of nature
sessions.description:
As human migration is becoming increasingly contested, so too is the ongoing redistribution of plant and animal species across the globe. In an era of accelerating ecological change, the issue of biological invasions has emerged as a pressing—and politically charged—environmental challenge. Non-native species that naturalize and spread disproportionately in new environments can dramatically reshape ecosystems, threaten biodiversity, disrupt economic activities and unsettle long-held assumptions about what belongs where.

 Yet invasion science is far from a purely environmental matter. Species movements have been fundamentally shaped by colonial expansion, global trade networks and contemporary regimes of mobility, as well as by Anthropocene's blurring of the boundaries of what we consider ‘natural’. What counts as invasive is often determined not just by ecological impact but by political priorities, economic interests, cultural values and historical contingencies. While the global circulation of species continues to intensify, the number of endemic species extinctions linked to invasive dynamics is also accelerating, raising urgent questions of responsibility, governance and justice.

 This complexity demands interdisciplinary inquiry. How do we navigate the tension between ecological urgency, environmental and social justice and the problematic politics of belonging? How do economic demands, social perceptions and regulatory frameworks shape contemporary conservation priorities? As climate change accelerates, how do we distinguish between adaptation, assisted migration and invasion without reproducing exclusionary or technocratic logics?

 This session seeks critical, interdisciplinary engagement with the complexities of the invasive species discourse to explore the conceptual entanglements between human and non-human mobility, the cultural work performed by invasion narratives, and the uneven geographies of environmental management and biosecurity. This panel seeks to present an open space of dialogue between empirical research, critical theory and policy analysis, and to contribute to ongoing debates on invasive species as a social and political issue.

papers.title: Nature, restoration and the invasive species dilemma

sessions.title: Restoration of Urban Nature as Living Labs: Advancing Mental Health, Heat Resilience, and Equity
sessions.description:
As climate change intensifies, urban heatwaves pose a critical threat to public health and ecological stability, disproportionately impacting low-income and marginalised communities. These environmental inequalities intersect with existing social vulnerabilities, creating compound disadvantages that demand urgent and just intervention. This session argues for a paradigm shift in urban climate adaptation: from top-down infrastructure projects to participatory, place-based experimentation through the restoration of vital blue-green spaces. 
 We invite contributions that critically examine how the restoration and co-management of urban wetlands, green spaces, rivers, and forests can serve as equitable interventions to mitigate heatwaves and enhance mental health. 

 This session positions these restored ecosystems not merely as final products, but as dynamic Urban Living Labs. This framework allows researchers, practitioners, and residents to collaboratively experiment with and monitor Nature-based Solutions (NbS), iteratively testing restoration methods and governance models in real-world contexts before scaling them to other sites. By focusing on spaces accessible to the urban poor, we move beyond the concept of green gentrification toward a model of 'just green enough'. 
 We seek to explore how these co-produced labs can foster ecological literacy, social cohesion, and psychological well-being, while simultaneously cooling urban heat islands and improving quality of life. 
 Furthermore, nature‑positive engineering plays a vital role by ensuring that infrastructure not only minimises harm but also actively restores and strengthens local ecosystems. Approaches such as green infrastructure, biodiversity‑sensitive design, water‑sensitive urban planning, and climate‑resilient materials help rebuild ecological health while centring equity. Embedding these principles enables cities to improve air quality, manage stormwater, reduce urban heat, and enhance community wellbeing—without displacing vulnerable residents or triggering green gentrification. 
 We welcome work that investigates the cross‑sector partnerships, policy frameworks, and long‑term stewardship models required to achieve regenerative, inclusive urban development that benefits those most affected by climate risks. Central to our inquiry is equity: 
 Who benefits from urban greening? 
 How can we ensure that restored rivers, wetlands, or forests become resources for thermal comfort and mental restoration for all, rather than amenities that displace vulnerable populations?  
 We will explore the tensions, successes, and failures of using these natural spaces as living labs to democratise climate action. By bringing together geographers, urban ecologists, planners, and community partners, this session aims to forge a new research agenda that places justice at the heart of nature-based experimentation, ultimately contributing to the development of climate-resilient and mentally healthy urban environments for everyone.

sessions.title: Unequal Perceptions: Sensuous Geographies and Ecologies of Sensing
sessions.description:
In our late-industrial and digitalised world, perception is no longer an exclusively human matter. Today, we find devices, buildings, infrastructures, and even landscapes as perceiving entities (Weizman et al. 2014), next to plants, animals, and other non-human beings. Through sensors, perception becomes a practice of media and technology, ubiquitously distributed over the planet and beyond – often unnoticed in its mode of operation, but generously involved in providing capacities for feeling new socio-spatial realities (Gabrys 2016; Hansen 2011).

 Even though human geography has long emphasised senses, perception, embodiment, experience, and meaning as central to place-making (Tuan 1977; Rodaway 2002), questions scrutinising the spatial production – and the producers – of perception itself remain overlooked yet fundamental. If we understand perception as practice, embedded in cultural nexuses, done by humans and non-humans, both bodily and technically, we must also consider spaces and places excluded from these productions – by accident or by design – remaining as sensory blind spots, staying unattended. This session brings these regimes of perception into focus, asking how sensuous, bodily ways of knowing place (Pink 2015; Sumartojo & Pink 2018) intersect and relate to technical and more-than-human forms of sensing (Benson 2010; Ballestero 2019).

 Tracing the inequalities these differing perceptual regimes may lead to, we seek to shift the attention towards the preconditions and configurations of environmental sensing, sensuous lifeworlds, and affective atmospheres. How and where are perceptual regimes produced? Who decides what is, or what may become, perceptible, measurable, and detectable? Technical sensing systems may translate environmental conditions into data, thresholds, and alerts, while everyday sensory experiences register feelings of discomfort, risk, attachment, or loss. These modes do not operate separately; they interact, reinforce, and contradict one another, forming their own ecologies of sensing (Parisi 2009) which shape how late-industrial places become liveable or unliveable; reinforcing or alleviating inequalities.

 We welcome a wide range of conceptual, empirical, and methodological contributions and case studies engaging with:

 - Sensuous and experiential ways of knowing place and the world
- Environmental sensing and monitoring as spatial practices
- Spaces and places of perception production
- Relations between lived experience, data, and technical sensation
- Perceptual politics of (in)visibility and ignorance
- Experimental and interdisciplinary approaches to sensuous and perceptual geographies in practice

papers.title: Digitally Mediated Embodiment: Sensing, Sense Checking and Disentangling from River Quality Data in Oxfordshire
What is it like to be a sensor medium? On perception and the epistemology of machine sensing
affiliations: CRC Media of Cooperation, University of Siegen, GER

sessions.title: Democratising the Economy (1)
sessions.description: People across the world are rethinking and reorganising their local economies towards more democratic, equitable, and sustainable forms in response to long-term (and uneven) economic and socio-spatial inequalities and multiple crises: from the global financial crisis and austerity policies decimating cultural provision and public capacity, the COVID-19 pandemic’s exposure of complex geographies of inequality and structural issues of health and social care under capitalism, and the climate emergency which too regularly affirms a crisis of essential infrastructures created by decades of underinvestment. Theoretically, a democratisation of economic relations is, of course, closely related to the concept of economic democracy (Cumbers et al 2020), but the vision of a democratic economy also has many ‘siblings’: diverse economies (Gibson-Graham 2008), approaches including the foundational, social and solidarity, and wellbeing economies, and tools such as circular and doughnut economics (Raworth 2019). In practice, we see efforts to democratise economic activity particularly at the local and regional level such as a return of public, collective, and diverse ownership forms through de-privatisation of (public) services, infrastructures, and assets across a wide range of sectors (Kishimoto et al 2020, Cumbers and Paul 2022, Bianchi and Russell 2026). Equally, the global cooperative movement’s long history of mutual economic development and alternative practice, and more recently, experimentation with post-growth and community wealth building is taking hold in cities and communities across the world (Guinan and O’Neill 2020, Savini 2023, Crisp et al 2024). This session seeks to gather theories, approaches, models, and case studies of alternative economic development and organisation that ‘democratise the economy’ to examine them as more than the sum of their parts. Deliberately inclusive, the session invites theoretical and/ or empirical contributions, including but not limited to public, collective, and community ownership, democratic governance, municipalism, (re)municipalisation and de-privatisation, cooperativism, questions of growth, and alternative imaginaries.

papers.title: Building solidarity economies: tracing the historical geographies of UK credit unions

sessions.title: Making Sense of Unstable Ground: Living On with Disaster (1)
sessions.description:
Environmental disasters are often approached as moments of rupture requiring technical intervention, governance, or recovery. Yet for those who live with ongoing environmental instability, disaster is also a condition of everyday life, one that must be interpreted, endured, and rendered meaningful in order for life to continue. This panel invites papers that examine how people make sense of unstable ground and persist in living with it, attending to the ethical, temporal, spiritual, pragmatic, political, and moral forms of reasoning through which environmental volatility come to be lived with, remains tolerable, or becomes inhabitable.

 We are particularly interested in empirically grounded work that explores how expectations of loss, recurrence, return, or repair shape everyday conduct in landscapes affected by flooding, landslides, erosion, drought, or seismic uncertainty. How are futures imagined, and obligations sustained amid prolonged instability? How do religious or sacred understandings, moral responsibilities, temporal orientations, and situated knowledges inform decisions about staying, moving, rebuilding, or enduring? What forms of reasoning emerge in contexts where disaster is neither exceptional nor resolved?

 The panel especially welcomes contributions from settings that sit at the physical, political, or epistemic margins of dominant disaster narratives, including postcolonial and Global South contexts. We invite papers that foreground lived reasoning and situated knowledge, and the practices through which people continue inhabiting environments marked by ongoing instability, including the thresholds, tensions, and decisions through which staying, adaptation, or departure are weighed and enacted, and that invite reflection on how Geography has approached disaster. Contributions may engage critically with but are not limited to themes such as:

 - Moral and ethical reasoning in disaster-affected lives

 - Religious, sacred, or cosmological interpretations of environmental instability

 - Temporalities of waiting, anticipation, recurrence, and endurance

 - Living with infrastructure, protection, or neglect

 - Practices of staying, return, repair, and inhabitation under conditions of ongoing risk, including the thresholds and tensions through which leaving or relocation may be considered

 - Disaster as a lived condition rather than a discrete event

 - Social contracts and imagined obligations in at-risk contexts, including how expectations, responsibilities, and claims are negotiated between residents, states, infrastructures, and other forms of authority

papers.title: All That Begins in Water Ends in Water: Mourning on the Brahmaputra Chars

sessions.title: Geographies of Care and Violence (1)
sessions.description:
Feminist, political, and social geographies have richly examined care as an ethical relation, a form of labour, an affective practice, and a political project (Bartos 2018; Bondi 2008; Gutiérrez Sánchez 2022; McDowell 2004; Power and Hall 2018; Raghuram et al. 2009; Raghuram 2016), fundamental to the ways lives, bodies, and worlds are sustained (Fisher and Tronto 1990). At the same time, geographers have extensively documented violence in its many forms—intimate, structural, slow, and spectacular (Christian and Dowler 2019; Pain and Cahill 2022). The session seeks to examine the relations between care and violence. 

 This session examines care and violence not as opposites, but as entangled and co-constitutive processes. The session is particularly interested in work that explores how violence is sustained, extended, or transformed through the absence, withdrawal, misrecognition, or uneven distribution of care, and how care may be complicit in or resistant to violence.

 Feminist geographies of care have argued that the relationship between caregiver and care receiver is not a simple or linear practice, unsettling assumptions that care is inherently benign, highlighting its ambivalences, exclusions, and conflicts, and calling for greater attention to the ways care and conflict are not mutually exclusive (Bartos 2018; Power 2019; Raghuram et al. 2009). 

 Building on these debates, this session foregrounds questions of carelessness, neglect, abandonment, and misaligned care, asking how harm persists not only through acts of violence but also through everyday relations, institutional practices, and political–economic arrangements that deny or foreclose care. By bringing care and violence into conversation, this session seeks to open space to explore how care might be reimagined as a political and geographical practice capable of interrupting violence.

papers.title: Geographies of care and violence: reflecting on research with men who are survivors of sexual violence

sessions.title: The overlooked spatial politics of the energy transition (1)
sessions.description: In the grand task of overhauling energy infrastructures to shift towards fossil fuel-free sources and reduce energy consumption, proponents such as municipalities and other public actors have generally foregrounded technology, policy, and cooperation. In this process, other – and perhaps less solutionist – dynamics have largely been overlooked, deemed minor, or have operated under the radar. This session invites contributions on dynamics in the energy transition that complement dominant dialogues. The session aims to (1) provide multi-scalar readings of the transition, (2) contribute to dialogues on the multiplicity of the transition process in various contexts, and (3) collect and share empirical contributions outlining how energy transitions are being materialized. We will discuss contributions that bring insights from diverse geographical contexts, alongside conceptual  discussions. The sub-topics include, but are not limited to: • the role of everyday maintenance and repair • geographies of waste and redundancy • social reproduction and the energy transition at the micro-scale (gender, households, unpaid labour, and more) • the role of the arts, creative expression, and storytelling • social infrastructure of "left behind" regions

sessions.title: Authors meet critics: Eda Beyazit and Karen Lucas's edited volume Gendered Mobilities in the Global South
sessions.description:
This book explores how gender shapes everyday mobilities across the Global South, bringing together insights from gender studies, mobilities research, and critical perspectives on infrastructure development. The book approaches mobility as a socio-spatial and political process shaped by power relations, cultural norms, and economic inequalities. It examines how gender intersects with class, race, age, and other identities to structure who moves, how, and under what conditions.

 Through a combination of theoretical reflections and empirical case studies from diverse contexts, including South Asia, Sub-Saharan Africa, and Latin America, the book highlights how mobility systems often reinforce gender inequalities, from unsafe public transport environments to unequal access to employment and education. At the same time, it records the everyday strategies women employ to navigate these constraints.

 In this Meet the Authors session, the editors, Dr Eda Beyazit (UWE Bristol) and Prof Karen Lucas (University of Manchester), will discuss the motivations behind the project, the challenges of researching gendered mobilities in the Global South, and the implications of the book for more equitable and inclusive transport policy and infrastructure planning. Professor Tim Schwanen (University of Oxford) will reflect on the book’s themes by situating them within broader debates on gender, mobility justice, and decolonising transport research, and will open the discussion to questions from the audience. Gendered Mobilities in the Global South, Edward Elgar Publishing,

sessions.title: The family as a site of environmental politics
sessions.description:
In recent years, families and family relations have been increasingly drawn into political discourse surrounding the environment and climate (e.g. birth strike for climate, XR families, Parents for Future, Fridays for Future), drawing attention to how climate change is viewed in an intergenerational frame. Amid concern that the effects of climate and ecological crisis will exacerbate the global care crisis (MacGregor et al 2022), there is a need to investigate how these crises add to and/or reshape caring activities and family practices, as well as the capacities to undertake these activities. Meanwhile, within the environmental policy process, there is a lack of attention to how climate change and care inequalities intersect, resulting in mitigation and adaptation strategies that are unresponsive to the needs of those who undertake care work. 

 Family relations of care and responsibility motivate protest in the struggle for resources and land around the world, with parental identity often forming deep emotional attachments to just futures, as well as offering a strategic narrative (Conradsen, 2016; Dombroski, 2016; Ramm and Gideon, 2020, 2020). The vital role mothers and other family members play in eco-political struggles has been highlighted; but how involvement in climate and environmental activism shapes the wider realities of family life is less well attended to (see (Howard, 2022). On the other hand, families are often the target of policy and campaigns aimed at reducing household energy use and resource consumption, with the affective and material burden of pro-environmental responsibilities falling disproportionately on (unremunerated) women caregivers (see MacGregor et al 2022). 

 Despite the emergence of significant work on the family as a site of politics and morality (Holloway, 1998; Hall, 2011; Jupp, 2016; Tarrant and Hall, 2020), geographers have paid little attention to how ethico-political dilemmas of ecological crisis surface and are negotiated within family relations, both within and across generations. This might seem an awkward time to turn the focus to families. Narratives of global ‘fertility collapse’ abound, in some instances attended by a barely veiled antipathy towards single, childless women in particular. The so-called ‘manosphere’ sells a regressive model of family/gender relations as aspirational and desirable, while the rise of ‘pronatalist’ advocacy appears to solidify the marrying of techno-solutionism with eugenics for the ‘baby bust’ era. Nonetheless, whilst family relations are not necessarily superior or more important than other relationships, ‘family remains of importance to people as a distinct, designated aspect of their everyday lives’ (Morgan, 2013). Equally, family is not a singular or neutral concept; it encompasses a multitude of settings and practices, including relations of neglect, uncare and violence. While family settings and relations are highly diverse and do not necessarily include children, family remains a regular touch point in social life, even in its absence. The idea of family continues to matter in different ways across the life course, and the ways in which it comes to matter is thoroughly political.

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Gender and Feminist Geography (1)
sessions.description:
The Gender and Feminist Research Group of the RGS (with the IBG) warmly encourages postgraduates and early career researchers to submit papers for our new and emerging research session. The session is ideal for researchers who would like an opportunity to present their research in a supportive and constructive academic environment, and for researchers at all stages of their careers who are interested in presenting papers that actively engage with discussions on current and emerging theoretical or methodological innovations in the field of feminist and gender geography.

 ‘Gender and Feminist Geographies’ is intended to cover a broad spectrum of research and papers are welcome from any area of feminist and gender geographical inquiry, with the aim of bringing together current and emerging themes, issues and approaches. Papers are especially welcome that connect with this year’s theme of “Geographies of Inequalities: Toward Just Places” at any stage in their research process are welcome, making the session a great opportunity for early career and postgraduate researchers to gain experience presenting their work. The sessions will also provide a forum to meet and discuss emerging ideas with other researchers in a friendly and relaxed environment, as well as opportunities to explore possibilities and relevance of engaging with feminist theory and methods within research.

 If you would like to submit a paper for consideration, please send a 300-word abstract to Rituparna Bhattacharyya, rituparna.bhattacharyya@accb.org.uk and Alkım Karaağaç, e.a.karaagac@bham.ac.uk, by Friday, 20th February 2026.
