sessions.title: Emerging and innovative pedagogies in geography higher education
sessions.description:
The evolving landscape of geography higher education demands pedagogical innovation that is responsive to the diverse needs of students and the challenges of the 21st century. This session invites paper contributions that explore emerging and transformative approaches to teaching, learning, and assessment, offering a platform to share ideas and discuss emerging and innovative HE pedagogies. 

 Central to the discussion is the integration of inclusive and equitable teaching practices that address the diverse needs of students. By rethinking traditional teaching and learning approaches and exploring alternative assessment models, educators are fostering accessible and engaging learning experiences. These practices are further enhanced by advances in technology, including the use of generative AI. Tools such as AI-driven writing aids, data analysis platforms, and virtual simulations are being leveraged to personalise learning, create innovative assessment opportunities, and prepare students for a digitally-literate world, while also prompting critical discussions about ethics and academic integrity.

 The session also explores how pedagogies adapted during the COVID-19 pandemic, such as digital and hybrid teaching, have evolved into powerful tools for the post-pandemic era. Innovative uses of digital platforms and immersive technologies, such as GIS, augmented reality, and online collaborative tools, are changing how students interact with geographical content and their peers and tutors. This transition to flexible and technology-rich learning environments is complemented by a renewed focus on authentic and sustainable fieldwork. By emphasising local contexts, virtual field experiences, and ethical community engagement, educators are balancing pedagogical rigor with social, environmental and financial responsibility.

 Recognising the holistic nature of education, this session also considers the affective dimensions of learning. Emotionally supportive pedagogies are emerging as crucial tools in fostering student and staff wellbeing, resilience, and motivation. Equally transformative are student-staff partnerships, where students are empowered as co-creators in curriculum design, assessment practices, and research, ensuring that educational practices remain inclusive, relevant, and reflective of diverse perspectives.

 By integrating these themes, the session aims to provide a collaborative space for sharing innovative practices that advance the discipline of geography, preparing students to navigate and address the complex challenges of a changing world.

sessions.title: Attuning to More-than-human Seas: Negotiating Multispecies Justice in the Anthropocene Ocean (1)
sessions.description:
“What would it mean to take the Anthropocene out to sea?” (Alaimo, 2017, p. 153). With this provocation, Alaimo highlights the persistent neglect of marine environments and species within the terra-centric Anthropocene discourse. Oceans are profoundly marked by human activity, yet continue to be framed as vast, remote, and seemingly inexhaustible spaces for exploitation. Over the past two decades, the ocean ‘out there’ has been promoted as the latest frontier for economic development and an unexplored playground for climate change mitigation, often under the banner of “blue growth” (Johnson and Braverman, 2019; Bennett et al., 2021; Buck, 2019). Critically engaging with Blue Economy ambitions (and their unintended consequences), this session explores how sustainable futures and multispecies justices might be realised in Anthropocene seas. Blue spaces beyond the coastline are typically uninhabited by humans, difficult to access and govern, and frequently located beyond the boundaries of national territories. Such spaces are nevertheless deeply entangled with global political economies, colonial histories, resource extraction, and uneven distributions of environmental risk, responsibility, and benefit. These dynamics increasingly render marginalised coastal communities and marine organisms vulnerable to the cascading impacts of human activities in the ocean. Put simply, Anthropocene seas are inherently unequal places and spaces. 
  Taking seriously the dynamic fluidity of the ocean requires moving beyond static separations between humans and nature, sea and land, or matter and mediation (Steinberg and Peters, 2015; Peters and Steinberg, 2019), to instead spotlight turbulent marine lifeworlds and more-than-human aquatic assemblages that are subjected to – yet also contest, challenge, and resist – unjust oceanic political economies. We thus invite papers that grapple with diverse notions of multispecies justice, blue justice, and blue degrowth to highlight overlooked oceanic geographies and to ask how Anthropocene seas can become sites for just and sustainable multispecies futures. 
  We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes: 

 - Extending critical Anthropocene scholarship to oceanic contexts 
- More-than-human engagements with the Blue Economy and blue (de)growth 
- Indigenous and decolonial perspectives on Anthropocene oceans 
- Afrofuturism, speculative ecologies and imagined ocean futures 
- More-than-human oceanic geopolitics 
- Affect and the ocean: embodied and emotional human-marine relations 
- Blue animal geographies 
- Multispecies marine methodologies

sessions.title: Geographies of inequalities and bias in AI
sessions.description:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) has entered our daily life and work, transforming how many people learn about the world, as well as the way we approach data analysis in human, digital and physical geographies (Ash et al., 2019; Bennett and De Sabbata, 2023; Gao, 2023; Janowicz et al., 2022; Osborne and Jones 2023). However, AI models and tools have their own geographies, shaped by their creation and deployment (Bender et al., 2021; Miceli and Posada, 2022; Weidinger et al., 2022), and which, in turn, generate and shape disinformation and misinformation, biases, and inequalities. The societal and ethical implications of AI are still underexplored in a continuously, rapidly changing technological and market environment. Crucially, many discussions are confined to (sub-)disciplinary silos, which hinder a broader understanding of this complex phenomenon.

 Aligning itself to this year’s Chair’s theme “Geographies of inequalities: towards just places” , this session aims to be an open forum to bridge critical analysis of AI based on theories in human and digital geographies (Sieber et al., 2025), and quantitative geographic analysis on AI safety and alignment (De Sabbata et al., 2025; Janowicz et al., 2025; Van de Weghe et al., 2025). 
 References

 - Ash, James, Rob Kitchin, and Agnieszka Leszczynski. 2019. Digital Geographies Edited by James Ash, Rob Kitchin, Agnieszka Leszczynski. Los Angeles: Sage.
- Bender, E.M., Gebru, T., McMillan-Major, A. and Shmitchell, S., 2021, March. On the dangers of stochastic parrots: Can language models be too big?. In Proceedings of the 2021 ACM conference on fairness, accountability, and transparency (pp. 610-623).

sessions.title: Cartographies of Everyday Inequalities (1)
sessions.description:
Cartographies of Everyday Inequalities: Spatial entanglements in urban spaces Dr. Kiran Bhairannavar and Vishavjeet Dhanda  Department of Geography, University of Delhi

 Key words : urban space, inequalities, just city, informalities, invisibilities, lived space

 Who gets stopped while walking? Who gets policed in public spaces? Whose vending is penalized and whose informal practices remain invisible? These questions raise important concerns about how authority is selectively exercised on certain bodies and practices that become targets of regulation, while similar practices by others evade scrutiny entirely. The session examines how everyday spaces—streets, markets, parks, public spaces, homes, neighbourhoods, digital spaces—become sites where spatial inequalities are produced, contested, and navigated (Martínez Rodríguez, 2019; Cohen & Knierbein, 2021). We focus on mapping these inequalities not as static distributions of disadvantage but as dynamic socio-spatial processes.

 There has been considerable work on urban inequalities as a key theme, however an everyday episodic lens largely remains distant (Ghertner, 2015; Massa, 2020; Jaffe, 2025). Taking this as a point of departure, we are interested in ‘everyday inequalities’ as emerging through intersecting spatial entanglements produced by state mechanisms, infrastructural arrangements, and socio-cultural hierarchies. (Simone, 2021; Kulz, 2025; Zsinka & Berger, 2025; Shafique, 2025). We seek to better understand how spatial practices further complicate these inequalities through differential toleration, penalization, or regularization depending on who occupies space, where and how; reinforcing hierarchies along lines of caste, class, gender, race, ethnicity, religion, sexuality and ability. 

 Foregrounding within these ideas, we are looking forward to contributions that help us further unpack the everyday nuances of inequalities. We seek answers to: 
How can we map everyday spatial inequalities beyond conventional approaches? What methods, tools and frameworks can reveal intersecting logics of agency, power, informalities, survival, and resistance? How do we foreground marginalized experiences ethically and non-extractively within the urban cultural episteme? How do everyday inequalities shape differential lived realities and futures? What role do affective and embodied experiences play in sustaining or challenging spatial hierarchies? How might everyday inequalities as a concept transform urban policy, planning practice, and governance frameworks toward more just cities and urban spaces?

 The session welcomes contributions addressing: 

 - Mapping everyday inequalities through banalities of access, waiting, affordability, and precarity

 - Everyday carceralities: discretion, extortion, selective enforcement, and penalization in routine practices

 - Public complicity and middle-class activism invoking/resisting state violence to securitize/secure everyday spaces

 - Embodied and emotional geographies of survival, policing, and displacement

 - Materialities and discourses: how everyday spaces are operationalized, contested, and reimagined.

papers.title: Fostering Belonging Despite Displacement: Shadow Care Infrastructure and Constellations in the City
Beyond (in)formal: Generative Practices, Planning Discourse, and the Making of Urban Inequality on Delhi’s Footpaths
affiliations: Department of Geography, University of Delhi

sessions.title: (Re)Imagining Just Climate Futures through Creative Approaches (1)
sessions.description:
Climate change is not only a technical or environmental challenge; it is also a struggle of imagination (Yusoff & Gabrys, 2011). Dominant climate futures are increasingly shaped by models, metrics and algorithmic projections, while questions of justice, care, power and responsibility are often sidelined. In line with the theme of the 2026 RGS-IBG conference, Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places, we invite contributions that interrogate these dominant imaginaries and experiment with creative techniques to (re)imagine just climate futures.

 Drawing on feminist ethics of care and a reparative orientation (Puig de la Bellacasa, 2017; Myers et al., 2024; Tuck, 2009), we approach future-making as situated, relational labour—work that foregrounds uneven burdens and asks how climate imaginaries may normalise loss and reproduce colonial hierarchies by privileging some lives, knowledges and places over others (Sultana, 2022). Creative methods are not simply alternative modes of presentation here; they are ways of knowing and intervening (e.g. Hawkins, 2015). Working with affect, embodiment and narrative, they can unsettle model- and metric-driven registers and make room for futures that challenge what counts as “realistic.” Contributions in this panel thus analyse and experiment with creative approaches—art–science collaboration, participatory speculation, speculative mapping and also drawing (see e.g. Davies, 2024; Perry, 2018) —while attending to the frictions, ethical dilemmas and power relations involved in imagining otherwise. 

 The session aims to offer an open space to share reflective and experimental contributions on futuring practices and future-oriented research in geography and allied fields. We welcome work that attends to research processes as much as outcomes, including limitations, frictions, failures, ethical dilemmas and political tensions that emerge when working with uncertain futures, communities and technologies. We encourage a variety of formats, from traditional papers to art-based and practice-based contributions and other experimental formats (e.g., performances, facilitated exercises, video screenings). 

 We are particularly interested in contributions addressing:

 - Futuring practices and future-oriented methodologies centering the role of conflicts, emotions and positionalities
- Reparative, caring and justice-oriented approaches to climate futures engaging with feminist and decolonial debates
- Art–science collaborations and interdisciplinary research
- Creative, participatory and experimental research methods including the use of drawing, illustration, comics and/or speculative cartography
- Critical perspectives on AI, data and modelling in climate governance
- Questions of inclusion, exclusion and power in processes of future-making

papers.title: More than visualisation : towards a typology of methods using comics for future studies
Creative Pedagogies for Undisciplined Explorations  of Urban Futures
Positive Cities: reparative futures through urban flow analysis and action
Amsterdam Decentraal
Harnessing Friction and Care: (Re)Imagining Just Climate Futures through AI
Prompting Future Visualizations in Performance Art: Intermedia, Liveness and AI in Reparative Climate Imaginaries

sessions.title: Author meets critics: Gwyneth Lonergan's Borders, Citizenship, and Pregnancy: Migrant women’s experiences of pregnancy and maternity care in the UK
sessions.description:
Borders, Citizenship, and Pregnancy explores migrant women’s experiences of pregnancy and maternity care in the north of England, and what these experiences reveal about the relationship between reproduction, citizenship, and bordering processes at this juncture. Based on interviews and focus groups with migrant mothers, third sector workers, and NHS staff, this book uses the analytical framework of reproductive justice to examine how racialised and gendered discourses and policies around health, austerity, belonging, motherhood, in conjunction with the expanding border regime, interact to create a highly stratified and complex system of exclusions, contributing to migrant women’s relatively poor maternal outcomes. 

 It has long been recognised that bordering processes are informed by anxieties around migrants’ reproductive practices, and serve to discipline these practices (c.f. Klug, 1989; Luíbheid 2013). For the nation-state to survive, citizens must reproduce themselves both biologically, by giving birth to the next generation of citizens; and socially, by inculcating their children with the values of ‘good citizenship.’ Anxieties around the national reproductive future shape which migrants are seen as un/desirable, and the conditions under which migrants are allowed to enter and remain in the UK, and access resources and state services. These anxieties target women especially, because as women are constructed as responsible for reproducing the nation-state (Yuval-Davis, 1997); moreover, because citizenship is hierarchical and multi-tiered, the impact of these policies vary considerably depending on the social location of individual women, and their perceived ability to reproduce the nation-state ‘correctly.’ However, the recent proliferation, multiplication, and intensification of bordering processes has strengthened the reach of the state in policing these practices, and new forms of reproductive injustice are emerging as a consequence. At the same time, as bordering processes expand to multiple sites, they encounter, and may be shaped, mediated, or even undermined by the practices and philosophies associated with these sites, and new possibilities for resistance may emerge. 

 This book breaks new ground in considering migrant women’s experiences of pregnancy as shaped by citizenship and bordering processes, and arguing that reproduction must be seen as central to expanding border regimes. In this panel, leading scholars of the geographies of bordering processes and/or reproduction will engage with the author in a discussion of Borders, Citizenship, and Pregnancy , and the ways in which bordering processes are informed by, and impact, discourses, policies, and experiences around pregnancy and reproduction, and the resulting patterns of injustice.
 Panel members include:
 Dr Gwyneth Lonergan, Northumbria University
 Professor Jen Bagelman, Newcastle University
 Professor Kathryn Cassidy, Northumbria University
 Professor Sarah Marie Hall, University of Manchester

sessions.title: Governing Transitions, Distributing Consequences: Locating Institutional (In)justice in Food Systems (1)
sessions.description:
Food geographies have generated rich insights into the spatial politics of production, consumption, and alternative food networks. Food systems scholarship, meanwhile, has advanced understanding of interconnections across scales and sectors. Yet both literatures tend to concentrate analytical attention at the "ends" of food systems, whether on farming landscapes and agrarian change or on ‘the food environment’ (retail environments, households, and eating practices). This session turns attention to the spaces in between: the processing facilities, distribution networks, wholesale markets, and public provisioning systems that constitute the "missing middle" of food system governance. These are places where (in)justice takes shape, yet they remain underexamined as sites of geographical inquiry.

 We invite contributions that examine how inequalities are produced, reproduced, and contested in these institutional spaces, attending to both the process and outcomes of food system transformation. How are transformation pathways governed, and whose interests shape them? And crucially, what happens after? Even transformations pursued in the name of justice generate consequences: shifts in where food is produced and processed, new winners and losers across global supply networks, and reconfigured relations between humans, animals, and environments. How are these consequences distributed across places, species, and countries, and how might governance arrangements anticipate and address the new inequalities that just transitions themselves produce?

 We particularly welcome contributions that extend justice frameworks beyond the human while remaining structurally and institutionally grounded. Rather than focusing on ethical consumption or community-based multispecies encounters, we seek analyses of how human-animal-environment relations and multispecies futures are shaped by institutional and non-human factors. Topics might include: public food procurement; wholesale, processing, and distribution governance; lock-in and path dependency; transition governance and its distributive effects; institutional arrangements for animal welfare; and anticipatory approaches to just transition.

papers.title: Delivering One Health in Wales Through Food Systems in Transition:  Infrastructure, Wholesalers, and Following The Money
Justice in the Missing Middle: Transformation Statements and Institutional (In)justice in Papua New Guinea’s Cocoa Supply Chain
Sustainability in the broiler chicken industry: trade-offs, lock-in, and path dependency
Historical transitions and anticipated futures for live bird chicken trading in India

sessions.title: Exploring the emerging geographies of fintech: from mobile banking to cryptocurrencies to Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDCs)
sessions.description:
There has been an increased interest among geographers in recent years in the rise of ‘fintech.’ Early attention has focused on private fintech innovations such as money transfer apps (Natile 2020), private payment apps for entrepreneurs (Baker 2021), private loan platforms (Langley and Leyshon 2021), and private mobile- enabled insurance products (Migozzi et al. 2021). This literature has variously found that fintech innovations such as mobile money and cryptocurrencies can ‘disrupt’ prevailing consumer markets (Zook and Grote 2024), reduce costs on sending remittances (Cirolia et al. 2022; Robins 2025), improve access to credit (Ding et al. 2018), and widen access to insurance products (Mhlanga 2023). They can in some circumstances however also usher in new forms of digital financial exclusion, surveillance, and indebtedness (Bhagat and Roderick 2020); while also arguably facilitating only a partial – and particularly neoliberalized – conception of ‘development’ (Girard 2022). 

 Though there has so far been less critical attention on state-led fintech innovations such as Central Bank Digital Currencies (CBDC) and Central Bank- managed instant payment platforms such as Brazil’s Pix , geographers have nonetheless opened up important conversations about these new innovations. This includes investigating the possible political and economic disruption, competition, and ‘disintermediation’ that state-led fintech innovations may usher in (Lai 2020; Huber 2023; Chia and Helleiner 2024), the potential of state-led fintech innovations to drive new spatial patterns of local and regional development (Chu et al. 2025), questions of culture and public acceptance (Baronchelli et al. 2022), and ethical questions about privacy and surveillance (Wenker 2022). 

 This session aims to build on these emerging theoretical and empirical insights by inviting presentations on geographically-informed papers on fintech innovations. We are particularly interested in those working on fintech from the perspective of the changing role of the state within economy and society. The session aims to address a range of questions about fintech innovations, including (but not limited to):

 - How and for whom are fintech innovations facilitating financial inclusion, and what are their limitations as financial inclusion tools?
- How are fintech innovations contributing to enhanced economic development, especially in overcoming prior market or spatial barriers to development?
- To what extent are fintech innovations proving to be a source of competition and disintermediation for incumbent finance actors and practices, such as commercial banks and other finance firms?
- How are fintech innovations reconfiguring the global geographies of money and finance, especially where they appear to challenge existing currencies and money transfer infrastructures such as SWIFT and BACS?
- How are fintech innovations being perceived and received by the public in different places, especially in the context of concerns over privacy and surveillance?

papers.title: From cryptocurrency to cryptofinance: FTX, disintermediation and the US state
Fintech path creation in China: an evolutionary economic geography perspective
Value extraction in Global Digital Networks
The Geography of the UK’s Fintech Ecosystem: The First Fifteen Years

sessions.title: Entanglements of Change: More-Than-Representational and Practice-Based Approaches to the Spatialities of Socio-Ecological Transformation
sessions.description:
Transformation toward a sustainable, post-fossil, and just society is not a master plan. Socio-ecological transformations unfold – and happen – in everyday life, in concrete spaces, through bodies, objects, emotions, and relations. Transformations are neither linear nor purely technological; rather, they are contradictory, contested, and spatially differentiated processes. They emerge within historically sedimented power relations, producing disturbances, detours, and losses, while simultaneously opening up new spaces of possibility. Transformation thus becomes a lived practice (Walker 2020; Görmar & Kinossian 2022), giving rise to diverse modes of subjectivation and agency. Building on this conceptualization, the session seeks to sharpen a more-than-representational, practice-theory, and spatial perspective that understands change as a situated relational entanglement. By foregrounding the political characteristics of space – friction and renegotiation, diversity and encounter, situatedness and relationality – together with the potentials of practice-theory approaches (Hui et al. 2017; Schäfer & Everts 2019), the session aims to make visible both the conflictive and differentiated nature of transformations as well as their creative dimensions. 

 Central to such an approach is a focus on the everyday dimensions of transformation (Schurr & Strüver 2016; Domínguez-Guzmán et al. 2023): routines, improvised practices, and modes of engaging with change that unfold “under the radar” of dominant regimes of attention. Equally important is the exploration of the emotional dimensions of transformation (Emery 2020; Martiskainen & Sovacool 2021), including unevenly distributed phenomena such as ‘transition pain’ (Bogner et al. 2024), climate emotions (Neckel & Hasenfratz 2021), as well as hope, and utopian imaginaries (Feola et al. 2023). Finally, a spatial and practice-oriented approach enables grasping diversity / difference within transformations, illuminating how categories such as gender, class, or migration background shape local negotiations of transformational movements, within which different subjects embody different relationships to change (Meier & Aytekin 2018; Braunger & Walk 2022). Hence, transformation is an ongoing re-negotiation of relationships between diverse subjects and groups, individuals and environments, self and other (Zhang 2024). 

 The session turns to several interrelated questions. It explores how transformation processes are made visible, tangible, and understandable in everyday life. What do differently situated subjects point to , and where do they lead us when they aim to show transformation(s) and its conflicts? Relatedly, the session asks about the relations between dominant transformation imaginaries, and lived experiences of everyday change. A spatial and practice-theory perspective is also sensitive to the experiences of multiple, overlapping changes. Moreover, the session situates the manifestations of transformation within local assemblages: transformation processes – their visibilities, imaginaries, subjectivities, and capacities – are shaped in and through a “thrown-togetherness” of heterogeneous elements (Massey 2005; Dihlmann & Helbrecht 2024 ). Transformation must be conceived as emerging from the messiness of everyday life. Such a perspective captures constellations of (in)visibility and (dis)attachment (Anderson 2022), viewing transformations as contentious assemblages.  Finally, the session conceives of transformation as a process that generates multiple uncertainties – including epistemological disruptions (Altstaedt, Fladvad & Hasenfratz 2022). It thus invites analyses of the ways in which subjects engage with the “geographies of uncertainty” (Senanayake & King 2021).

papers.title: Diffracting Democratic Spatialities with Practices of Making-with: On the entanglements of invited and invented spaces of participation in socio-ecological transformations in Luxembourg
“On the way” to sustainable last-mile: contested mobilities of using light electric freight vehicles

Sensing Traces on a Tomato Plant: A Neurodiverse Approach to Practicing Sensory Multispecies Ethnography

sessions.title: China and Beyond: the Contentious Politics of Infrastructure and Real Estates Development in Asia
sessions.description:
Research on the Belt and Road Initiative (BRI) has claimed that Global China has given shape to a new, groundbreaking, form of urbanisation (Apostolopoulou, 2020; Curtis and Klaus, 2024; Wiig and Silver, 2019). Drawing on research conducted in the framework of the program « Cities Along the New Silk Roads in Southeast Asia », this panel argues instead that Chinese investments in large infrastructure and real estates are « grafted » into a contentious politics of spatial and economic development that is path-dependent on nationally driven policies, processes of territorial transition, and national elites’ strategies of capital accumulation. The panel mirrors together cases from three Southeast Asian countries — Cambodia, Malaysia, and Thailand — to show how urban and territorial development in which Chinese transfers are involved accentuates patterns of spatial inequality that take root in decades-long processes of territorial transition. Grassroots movements have carved variable geometries of contestation space to those processes that determine how, and to what extent, alternatives for fairer development models can(not) take shape. The case of corridor development in India allows to question the specificity of corridor development as led by Chinese transfers. It also expands further the reflection on local agency, by looking at the governance mechanisms that may allow the expansion of public participation at the sub-national scale.

 Cited references:  
 Apostolopoulu, Elia. 2020. The uneven geographies of China’s Belt and Road Initiative: Authoritarian neoliberal urbanism, social inequality and grassroots resistance in London, Athens and Colombo. Antipode 53(3): 831-858.

 Curtis, Simon and Klaus, Ian. 2024. The Belt and Road City. Geopolitics, urbanization, and China’s search for a new international order. New Haven, London: Yale University Press. 

 Wiig, Alan and Silver, Jonathan. 2019. Turbulent presents, precarious putures: Urbanization and the deployment of global infrastructure. Regional studies 53(6): 912-923.

sessions.title: Foodscapes for healthy, just, and sustainable food systems?
sessions.description:
Foodscapes is a concept widely used in geography (1) and anthropology (2) to explore relationships between food, people, and place in everyday life (3). While it has generated rich insights into how food is materially situated and socially experienced, its potential contribution to the growing field of food systems science remains underexplored. Food systems science has emerged as an influential interdisciplinary field with a mission to support healthy, sustainable, and just food systems globally (4). However, prevailing approaches often rely on universalising narratives of desirable food systems that privilege particular forms of knowledge, values, and expertise, whilst marginalising others (5). What constitutes a desirable food system, and how problems are framed are deeply situated and context-specific. This session asks whether a foodscapes lens can help navigate the complexity of contemporary food systems by critically examining how food systems are defined, experienced, and valued in place.

 A foodscapes framework offers a pluralistic lens for understanding how desirable food systems are negotiated. It foregrounds the intersections between cultural meanings and representations with material and environmental contexts (6). Foodscapes also foreground the meaningful and often reciprocal relationships among humans and between humans and non-humans (7). By examining whose interpretations of sustainability, health, and justice are legitimised, and how these narratives are produced, circulated, and contested, a foodscapes lens enables critical engagement with questions about power, governance, and knowledge (8, 9).

 This session invites papers on the foodscapes concept and practice: what analytical value does it offer for understanding food systems; how can it help expose and challenge inequalities embedded in dominant food system narratives; and what possibilities does it open for reimagining sustainable and just food futures? We welcome empirical, conceptual, and methodological papers, and its implications for food systems research, policy, and practice.

papers.title: The multiple dimensions of urban foodscapes: an analysis of food markets and urban food policy in London
Inside the box: The lunchbox as a negotiation space for emotional practices of nutrition, parenting, and security

sessions.title: Leveraging education settings as sites of sustainability transition: Research for Transforming Practice into Policy
sessions.description:
Education settings are important sites of action on climate and nature recovery. With over 24,000 schools in England, the educational estate covers a significant area in terms of grounds and built environment, which alongside a student headcount in excess of 9m and staff workforce FTE close to 1m (GOV.UK, 2025) leads to significant environmental impact in terms of carbon emissions and wider impacts, as well as representing a significant potential leverage point for sustainability transition and policy lever for tackling national sustainability priorities.

 In England, the Department for Education’s Sustainability and Climate Change strategy, aims to unlock the potential of sustainability transformation of education settings, but this requires support in terms of expertise and resource to the vast majority of the 24,000 schools, plus early years settings and further education colleges across England. The Climate Ambassadors programme, funded by the Department for Education is a critical pillar in the delivery of the DfE’s Sustainability and Climate Change strategy, situated within a complex ecosystem of four other national-scale aligned programmes. The programme supports education settings (from early years through to further education colleges) in England to develop and implement climate action plans through linking support from volunteer ‘climate ambassadors’ with appropriate capacity and expertise, to the needs of individual education settings. Climate action plans take a whole-institution approach focused around the four pillars of Adaptation and resilience, Biodiversity, Climate education and green careers, and Decarbonisation. The programme is centrally co-ordinated and delivered through nine regional hubs, engendering place specific elements of delivery. 

 Alongside the delivery of the programme, the impact of the programme on education settings and climate ambassadors is being measured, while gaps in setting engagement based on geography, socio-economic characteristics and climate vulnerability and gaps in climate ambassador recruitment and engagement on the basis on geography and expertise are analysed on an ongoing basis. Therefore, in addition to the direct impact of the activities themselves on the settings, their learners and local communities, this programme and the data it is collecting has significant potential to inform future policy and practice on the role of education settings in driving sustainability transitions while tackling inequality.

 This panel aims to explore what the Climate Ambassadors programme can contribute to research and policy at the intersection of education settings and action on climate and nature recovery. Some of the questions to be explored include: What is the role of place in engagement with education settings and volunteers? How do localised climate vulnerabilities influence engagement? How do existing inequalities play out in settings’ engagement and impact? Are volunteers a sustainable model for support? How is climate action framed with other agendas and priorities? The audience is invited to contribute to further the research agenda setting at the intersection of practice, research and policy for action on climate and nature recovery through education settings.

sessions.title: Working with remainders: traces and spectrality in justice-making processes (1): analytics
sessions.description:
Session co-convenors: Annaclaudia Martini (University of Bologna) and Martina Tazzioli (University of Bologna)

 The contemporary cultural moment is characterized by a renewed interest towards evidence, and evidence-based epistemologies and methodologies. This concern has also permeated geographical debates, partly as a result of impact-driven analyses that declare the aim of recognising inequalities and fostering justice-making processes. This session challenges such a quest for evidence as justice seeking instrument and seeks to shift attention towards traces and spectrality as analytical lenses for re-thinking geographies and politics of justice.

 This session interrogates how the analytical lens of traces, haunting and spectres can be effectively used in geography for articulating critical knowledge in the current cultural and geopolitical conjuncture. We aim to explore how traces and spectrality might be productively mobilised as analytics or as methods on different phenomena such as wars, migrations, natural disasters and ecological loss, heritage and memory, collective struggles and mobilizations, taking traces and spectres seriously in their heterogeneity and incompleteness. In focusing on hauntings (Derrida, 1994) and on traces (Ginzburg, 2012), we complicate historical narratives implicitly dependent on the linear temporalities of progress, and justice-seeking processes exclusively grounded on the production of evidence. Such perspective shift exposes individuals' and collectives' fragmented experiences of memory, justice, the impact of colonial legacies on our present, and intergenerational trauma.

 We invite contributions that engage with traces and/or spectrality from a theoretical, methodological or empirical point of view, and that interrogate how these can be used for bringing into focus concealed and naturalised inequalities, and for envisioning justice-seeking processes in different fields. Such approach can expand and move beyond current epistemologies on traces (Didi-Huberman, 2017; McGeachan, 2018; Ogborn, 2005) and hauntology and spectrality (Derrida, 1994; Maddern & Adey, McCormack, 2010), interrogating how these not only allow us to enable novel understandings of past events and violations, but also unfold and activate research pathways for envisioning justice-making processes.

 References 

 Derrida, J. (1994). Specters of Marx: The State of the Debt, the Work of Mourning and the New International . London: Routledge.

 Didi-Huberman, G. (2017). Bark . MIT Press.

 Ginzburg, C. (2012). Threads and traces: True false fictive . University of California Press.

 Maddern, J. F., & Adey, P. (2008). Spectro-geographies. cultural geographies , 15(3), 291-295.

 McCormack, D.P. (2010). Remotely sensing affective afterlives: The spectral geographies of material remains. Annals of the Association of American Geographers , 100(3), 640-654.

 McGeachan, C. (2018). Historical geography II: Traces remain. Progress in Human Geography , 42(1), 134-147.

 Ogborn, M. (2005). Atlantic geographies. Social & Cultural Geography , 6(3), 379-385

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Food Geographies (1)
sessions.description:
In line with RGS-IBG 2026's conference theme of Geographies of inequalities: towards just places, we invite submissions on new and emerging research within Food Geographies. We particularly encourage submissions from early career (ECRs) and postgraduate researchers (PGRs), as well as scholars working in or on the Global South, including research that challenges dominant Euro-American framings of food geographies. This session aims to provide a supportive and constructive space to share work in progress, early findings, or new conceptual and methodological approaches and can be a wonderful opportunity to foster collaborative work, positive dialogue and advance research agendas within the field of food geographies and food justice (Deschact, 2021; Heynen et. al., 2012; Slocum, 2018; among others). 

 Food is deeply entangled with social, cultural, economic, racial, gendered, and environmental inequalities, from access to land and labour, to nutrition, health, and everyday food practices (see Gibson-Graham et. al. 2013; Cadieux & Slocum, 2015). Research in food geography has shown how food systems across scales often reinforce uneven power relations, while also offering sites of resistance, care, and alternative futures (Goodman et al., 2012; Holt-Giménez, 2017).

 For this session, we are particularly interested in papers that critically examine food-related inequalities and explore pathways that broaden the scope of justice and just spaces within Food Geographies. Papers can be innovative about their research aims/design, demonstrate creative methodologies and/or theoretical questions, or draw upon empirical findings. We especially welcome work that closes the gap between food production and consumption, attends to diversity within diets, advances a de/postcolonial approach to foodways, and engages with emerging practices of gastrodiplomacy and gastrosolidarity in digital and physical spaces (Low, 2021).

 Some themes include:

 - Food access, poverty, and everyday food inequalities

 - Labour, migration, and justice in food and agricultural systems

 - Race, gender, caste, and colonial injustices in food geographies

 - Indigenous, local, and alternative food knowledges

 - Environment, sustainability, and climate inequalities in food systems

 - Food, health, and uneven outcomes across places

 - More-than-human relations, care, and food justice

 - Resistance, activism, and food-based social movements

 - Affect, emotion, and embodied geographies of food

 - Gastrodiplomacy, gastrosolidarity, and food as soft power

 - Food, place-making, heritage, and cultural memory

 - Methodological innovations in researching food and inequality

 References 

 Cadieux, K. V., & Slocum, R. (2015). What does it mean to do food justice? Journal of political ecology , 22 , 1.

 Deschacht, L. (2021). What role do urban food strategies play in fostering justice? (Doctoral dissertation, Ghent University).

 Low, K. E. (2021). Gastropolitical encounters and the political life of sensation. The Sociological Review , 69 (1), 190-205.

 Gibson-Graham, J. K., Cameron, J., & Healy, S. (2013). Take back the economy: An ethical guide for transforming our communities . U of Minnesota Press.

 Goodman, D., DuPuis, E. M., & Goodman, M. K. (2012). Alternative food networks: Knowledge, practice, and politics . Routledge.

 Heynen, N., Kurtz, H. E., & Trauger, A. (2012). Food justice, hunger and the city. Geography compass , 6 (5), 304-311.

 Holt-Giménez, E. (2017). A foodie's guide to capitalism . NYU Press.

 Slocum, R. (2018). Must everything be called “food justice?”. Local Environment , 23 (11), 1103-1105.

sessions.title: Teaching migration in the contemporary world
sessions.description: With point of departure in the recently published book Migration: A Critical Introduction (published by Wiley (2026), as part of the Critical Geographies series), this panel brings together scholars to discuss the importance and challenges of teaching migration in the contemporary world. This panel provides a critical conversation about the challenges we face as educators interested in the geographies of migration, how those challenges have persisted or changed, and our strategies for responding to those challenges in the classroom and beyond. In doing so, this panel will explore critical geographical approaches to teaching migration and related topics including but not limited to borders, belonging, diaspora, displacement, global climate change, labor, and migration justice.

sessions.title: Twinning European Cities
sessions.description:
The
 development of ‘digital twins’ of objects and processes is emerging as a
 key practice in the current phase of the digital revolution. In the 
urban context, this trend has given rise to Urban Digital Twins (UDTs), 
aimed at representing, modelling and simulating entire cities. UDTs mark
 a new phase in discourses around smart cities, technology-driven 
urbanism, and AI-based technological solutionism. 

In
 the European context, a distinct UDT agenda is emerging, often 
emphasizing novel opportunities for participation, transparency, and 
reliability in urban decision-making, envisioned to reduce the gap 
between technical expertise and citizenship. In practice, research has 
shown how UDTs emerge within specific local contexts and 
techno-political trajectories, being entangled with specific geographies
 of power relationships. 

This
 call seeks to critically engage with these implications by gathering 
case studies of European UDTs through the prism of the conference’s 
theme of geographies of inequalities. Where and how do UDTs produce 
(in-)equalities? How do the topics of inequality and injustice look like
 through the lenses of the various different disciplines that have 
formed the UDT field? How can responsible European UDTs that actively 
take these aspects into account be designed in practice? We welcome both
 theories and practices related to UDTs; these examples should not only 
be considered from a technological point of view but be connected to the
 political, social, and ecological issues related to their development. 

 This panel includes contributions on European UDTs highlighting the multiple 
aspects that contribute to their design and implementation (technology, 
governance, politics, ecology, city branding, urban economics, among 
others) and from different perspectives, including but not limited to: 

- 
Urban Geography
- Science and Technology Studies
- Urban Planning
- Computational Social Sciences
- Philosophy of Technology
- Urban Sociology

Amoore, L. (2025). Because the twin is not a copy: On the politics of digital twins. new media & society, 27(8), 4549-4564. 

Ferré-Bigorra, J., Casals, M., & Gangolells, M. (2022). The adoption of urban digital twins. Cities, 131, 103905. 

Kitchin,
 R., & Dawkins, O. (2025). Digital twins and deep maps. Transactions
 of the Institute of British Geographers, 50(1), e12699. 

Knopf,
 S., Macq, H., & Wentland, A. (2025). Urban futures in the mirror of
 technology? The politics of urban digital twins. new media & 
society, 27(8), 4385-4401. 

Nochta,
 T., Wan, L., Schooling, J. M., & Parlikad, A. K. (2021). A 
socio-technical perspective on urban analytics: The case of city-scale 
digital twins. Journal of urban technology, 28(1-2), 263-287.

sessions.title: Unconventional Geographies of Solidarity (1): Ephemeral Alliances
sessions.description:
In Multitude 
(2004), political philosophers Michael Hardt and Antonio Negri proposed 
the possibility of the “multitude” as a “living alternative” that grows 
from within the biopolitical production of shared knowledges, 
relationships, sentiments, and collaborations that facilitates 
globalized networks of capitalism. Unlike political units of “the 
people,” “the masses”, and “the classes” which signify the possibility 
of organized collective action based on commonality (of race, history, 
economic status etc.), the “multitude” is constituted through 
difference; an elusive, unpredictable and fundamentally ungovernable 
collective of singularities that may synchronize temporarily for 
specific goals or movements (cultural, social, political or otherwise), 
but without ever becoming fully unified and/or articulable. 

 Twenty years later, the world facilitated by globalized networks like
 social media is ever more complex than when they wrote this book. At 
the same time as people across the world have become intensely 
connected, the world has also become fragmented by political 
polarization, identity politics, and growing social isolation. Yet, the 
conceptualization of the "multitude" still resonates with geographical 
scholarship over the last two decades that has questioned conventional 
understandings of social movements and drawn attention to relational, 
affective, and improvised forms of solidarity produced through everyday 
practices and encounters (Amin, 2004; Bayat, 2013; Featherstone, 2008; 
Simone and Rao, 2012).

 This session invites theoretical, empirical and methodological 
interventions that explore unconventional, fragile and often overlooked 
geographies of solidarity that fall outside established frameworks of 
social movements, activism, or institutional politics, yet permeate 
everyday life in subtle and unexpected ways (Neimanis and Walker, 2014; 
Tsing, 2015).

papers.title: The role of Urban food growing projects and associated food hubs in addressing local food vulnerability and strengthening social ties

sessions.title: Big Tech, the State and Emerging Governance Assemblages (2)
sessions.description:
Recent scholarship highlights the growing influence of technology corporations in shaping political, social, and state practices. Big Tech platforms and AI technologies are increasingly entangled with state power, authoritarian logics, and new forms of governance, raising fundamental questions about political authority, public accountability, and democratic life (Jarret et al, 2025). The concept of “Big Tech authoritarianism” (Akbari 2025) foregrounds how tech giants not only mediate information but also align with or reproduce authoritarian logics of power, surveillance, and control. Likewise, emerging frameworks of AI authoritarianism (Dekeyser et al. 2025) argue that key features of AI-such as scalability, algorithmic incorporation of difference, and techno-determinist ideologies - actively reshape political governance and authoritarian dynamics across geopolitical contexts.

 This session responds to a growing need to critically interrogate the political thrust of technological power and the growing political influence of tech capital at a time when digital platforms and algorithmic systems are increasingly central to shaping state practices, public discourse, citizen engagement, and the possibilities for resistance. Rather than treating “the digital” as a neutral domain, we invite geopolitically informed analyses that situate technology in the relational dynamics of power, space, and governance. This aims to build on existing scholarship theorizing data power (Thatcher & Dalton, 2021), technofascism (McElroy 2025; Coeckelbergh, 2026), or digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019), and aligns with recent calls to rethink governance frameworks in light of AI’s active modulation of political processes and the political synergies between Big Tech and state actors.

 This session explores how Big Tech and AI are implicated in reconfiguring the practices, techniques, and imaginaries of governance. We seek contributions that situate these developments within broader socio-spatial processes, attending to power, resistance, and the uneven geographies of digital infrastructure and political authority (Akbari 2026 -upcoming). We invite submissions to this paper session on issues related to:

 • Big Tech, political power, and emerging forms of governance

 • AI, platforms, and the reconfiguration of state authority

 • Uneven geographies of digital power

 • Practices and logics of digital authoritarianism

 • Democratic frictions and political contestation in digital governance

 • Manifestations of Big Tech or AI authoritarianism across distinct spheres of governance

 References

 Akbari, A. (2026-upcoming). Uneven datafication. Dialogues on Digital Society.

 Akbari, A. (2025). Big Tech authoritarianism: Political synergies of an emerging power. Dialogues on Digital Society, 1(3), 317-321.

 Coeckelbergh, M. (2026). Technofascism: AI, Big Tech, and the new authoritarianism. AI & SOCIETY, 1-14.

 Dekeyser, T., Lynch, C. R., & Maalsen, S. (2025). AI Authoritarianism: Towards an Analytical Framework. Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers, e70048.

 Jarrett, K., Kitchin, R., Hiah, J., & Knight Steele, C. (2025). Authoritarianism in the digital age. Dialogues on Digital Society, 1(3), 243-251.

 Kwet, M. (2019). Digital colonialism: US empire and the new imperialism in the Global South. Race & class, 60(4), 3-26.

sessions.title: Current and emerging research in transport (1): Inequalities
sessions.description:
This session responds to the theme of this year’s conference by considering how transport geographers can further integrate ideas of inequality, justice or equity analysis into the work they do. We welcome abstracts from across transport geography that respond to this theme. Below, we have looked to outline some questions to provoke discussion across qualitative, quantitative, and theoretical perspectives on inequalities.

 - How does access to transport vary based on age, gender, ethnicity, or other protected characteristics (and how can we correct these inequalities)?
- How does access to transport vary by mode, and how acceptable are the inequalities this invites? How can inequalities in transport be measured, such that they account for the diverse needs of individuals (and the wide-reaching impact transport has on societies)?
- Is GeoAI compatible with transport justice? Can machine learning assist with computing and uncovering transport inequalities?
- What is transport justice? How should it be theorised? What has the canon of transport justice missed to date?

 The session provide a relaxed, supportive atmosphere for postgraduates at any stage of their research to present work in progress and share ideas with one another. Nonetheless, all are welcome to attend.
 The TGRG traditionally rewards a postgraduate prize for the best postgraduate presentation in any TGRG session at the RGS-IBG Conference. All postgraduate presentations in this session will automatically be eligible for the prize.

sessions.title: Reimagining Water Geographies (1)
sessions.description:
Water is routinely invoked as a universal, connective substance. Yet the ways it is narrated and conceptualised in geography can also reproduce exclusion, erasure, and spatial injustice.

 In this session, we will discuss how critical geography might turn the tide by thinking with water as relation, medium, and more-than-human material force, while staying attentive to uneven power, deterritorialization, and colonial legacies. Contributions will revolve around works that reconnect conversations often kept apart (island/ocean studies and freshwater scholarship; maritime geopolitics and hydro-feminist approaches), and that develop concepts and methods capable of tracking entanglements across scales and watery environments.

 Participants will rework key concepts such as planetary waters, islands, coasts, basins, and watery bodies. We will engage with “water places” where injustice is produced or contested (infrastructure, extraction, conservation, borders, mobility/immobility, militarization, tourism, and disasters), presenting methods for analyzing water as relation: fresh–salt continuums, land–sea interfaces, multi-sited approaches, mapping/visual methods, more-than-human methodologies, and more. Finally, by exploring the plural material dimensions of water, its essentiality in human-made environments, and its relationship with infrastructure and climate change adaptation, we will address compelling dilemmas in epistemic politics: how water categories travel, whom they privilege, and what alternative, decolonial geographies they enable.

sessions.title: The Gendered Labours of Everyday Ecological Transformation (1): Governance and responsibility
sessions.description:
Across households, neighbourhoods, community projects and eco-homes, women and people identifying with feminised or non-cis-masculine roles disproportionately drive everyday ecological transformation. Cooking from scratch, zero-waste practices, community food projects, care work, and sustaining eco-homes or eco-communities all form part of the daily labour of ecological change. Yet these practices often reproduce entrenched gendered divisions of labour, assigning women responsibility for food, waste, childcare, emotional wellbeing, and household sustainability. A central question animates this session: Is ecological transformation dependent on gendered inequalities? 

 Feminist environmental thinkers have long shown that care is structurally devalued in capitalist and patriarchal systems (Tronto, 1993; Federici, 2019). Ecofeminists argue that ecological transformation demands confronting the patriarchal hierarchies that render feminised care invisible (Gaard, 1997; Shiva, 2000; Salleh et al., 2017). Gender and sustainability scholarship likewise highlights gendered labour divisions as central to socio-ecological change (Cohen, 2017). At the same time, development and political-economy research shows how women’s labour is often instrumentalised within neoliberal narratives of market expansion and ecological solutionism (Moeller, 2018). Everyday socio-ecological change is therefore deeply entangled with the politics of social reproduction.

 In many grassroots sustainability initiatives, ecological aspirations materialise through the everyday reorganisation of domestic practices—community kitchens, shared gardens, composting, and collective food provisioning. These are sites where ‘matters of care’ (Puig de la Bellacasa, 2017) become ecological commitments enacted through often-shared daily labour. Research on co-housing and intentional communities shows how shared spaces are imagined to redistribute labour and collectivise sustainability efforts (Williams, 2005; Lietaert, 2010). Yet gendered responsibilities frequently re-emerge, even within settings explicitly committed to equality and ecological ethics (Eräranta et al., 2009; Jarvis, 2013; Vestbro & Horelli, 2012; Pickerill, 2015). Care is often collectivised without being politicised, reproducing hidden hierarchies of gender, labour and power (Bhatia, 2022; Gerner, 2025).

 These tensions—between empowerment and burden, ecological commitment and gendered expectation—sit at the centre of this session. We seek to foreground the geographies of ambivalence that shape who performs ecological labour, how this labour becomes visible or invisible, and how spaces of sustainability can both challenge and reproduce inequality. We invite empirical, theoretical, and methodological contributions engaging with these tensions and the gendered labours of ecological transformation.

papers.title: Between Responsibility and Empowerment: Gendered Labour and Ecological Discourses in Rural China

sessions.title: Reclaiming street space from cars (1): new instruments and experiences with the socio-ecological transformation of urban neighbourhoods and cities for better liveability and more sustainable transport
sessions.description:
In recent years many cities worldwide have embarked on a transformation of the transport system to improve living conditions and reduce negative aspects of transport. Among other issues, central objectives of these transformations are to use urban space for non-car purposes to improve liveability and promote the use of non-motorized or shared modes of transport. Various cities have promoted concepts like the 15-min-city, superblocs or improved cycling and walking infrastructures. To implement these concepts, car spaces (parking, streets) need to be re-used for other purposes. Despite questionable overall effects of these new instruments on a city-wide level, the relevance and emergence of these types of policies and related instruments for urban transition processes are clear.

 The objective of this session is to assemble research evaluating and assessing transport, social, economic or liveability aspects of instruments and tools employed for transforming urban areas. The instruments might involve restructuring the built environment (including transport infrastructures), communication and participation strategies or relevant financial instruments. We also seek research addressing the availability and redistribution of parking spaces by financial or other instruments or by policy packages combining the instruments mentioned. Preferably, we are seeking empirical contributions using sound theoretical reflections on the effects and changes initiated by relevant policy instruments. Real world laboratories or other means of transdisciplinary research are highly encouraged to present their findings.

sessions.title: Burning Inequalities: Critical Geographies of Fire (1)
sessions.description:
Where and why fire impacts people and places is deeply intertwined with many facets of inequality and injustice. This session aims to contribute to discussions around the role of geographical perspectives in conceptualising and researching fire inequality. 

 The devastating fires of Grenfell Tower in 2017 and more recently at Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong in 2025 serve as stark reminders that we must understand and redress inequalities that have shaped exposure to fire. Fires like Grenfell do not exist in a vacuum, instead they can expose and uncover inequalities and injustice embedded within our societies (Marmot, 2020). Scholars who have explored the contexts surrounding Grenfell in particular, have asserted the importance of foregrounding the role of racial logics of neoliberal urban governance (Danewid, 2020), geographies of injustice and inequality (MacLeod, 2018) and, institutional negligence and evasion of accountability (Özbilgin, Erbil and Valsecchi, 2026) in fomenting fire inequalities. 

 Furthermore, as global warming intensifies wildfires, the devastating consequences of such disasters continue to be disproportionately felt by underserved, minority and poor communities (Thomas, 2024). Research has highlighted that these impacts follow the contours of existing economic, social and racial inequalities as a result of insufficient resources, exclusion from risk preparedness (Baker et al. , 2024) but also the lack of resources to rebuild after a wildfire (Davies et al. , 2018). This is not by any means an exhaustive list, but speaks to the various factors that shape vulnerability to fire, and the injustices that follow a fire incident.

 Geographical perspectives and geographical imaginaries can be harnessed to explore the spatiality of these overlapping and intersecting inequalities, and the mechanisms through which they emerge and are sustained. For example, how inequalities are produced and shaped at different scales and sites, from government decision making to geographies of the home. Critical perspectives on disasters have argued that they are not natural phenomena , therefore how can geographers contribute knowledge around the transformation of fire into a human disaster?

sessions.title: Climate Apartheid: meanings, contexts, politics, legitimacy (1)
sessions.description:
The lexicon of ‘justice’ is increasingly central to climate policy debates and interventions, framed through discourses such as climate, environmental or energy justice, and more broadly the ‘just transition’. These frameworks recognise that climate change disproportionately affects marginalised groups who bear little responsibility for emissions and lack influence in political negotiations (e.g. Schlosberg and Collins, 2014; Sultana, 2022). The scope of climate injustice has since expanded, encompassing not only the inequalities of climate change impacts , but also the ways in which climate mitigation and adaptation strategies also contribute to unequal processes and outcomes (e.g. Rice et al., 2022). This is increasingly referred to as climate apartheid. Initially deployed by activists as a provocative term to secure attention in global policy circuits (e.g. Bond 2016), the concepts of eco/climate apartheid are currently being developed by critical scholars as a framework to explain and critique how climate action by privileged individuals and groups (re)produces injustice (e.g. Tuana, 2019; Long et al., 2020; Rice et al., 2022; Long, 2024a, 2024b; Heron, 2024).

 The roots of the term are largely traced to South African Archbishop Desmond Tutu, who in 2008 introduced the idea of “adaptation apartheid” to highlight his concerns that “adaptation is becoming a euphemism for social injustice on a global scale” in which wealthy countries protect themselves from climate change impacts via infrastructure technology investments unavailable to poorer counterparts (UN, 2008:166). A decade later, Philip Alston warned the UN Human Rights Council that “we risk a ‘climate apartheid’ scenario where the wealthy pay to escape overheating, hunger and conflict while the rest of the world is left to suffer” (UN, 2019). While initially the concept lacked coherence, recent scholarship has produced a comprehensive definition of climate apartheid as “an emerging system of discrimination, segregation, and violence based on various axes of oppression and privilege (race, class, gender, sexuality) that is produced by the material effects of climate change, but also many responses to the crisis” (Rice et al, 2001:627). This definition also introduces the analytical binaries of climate privileged and climate precarious, to capture how climate action affords protection and benefits to some individuals and groups, whilst generating heightened vulnerability and exclusion for others. Nonetheless, there remain a wide range of uses and interpretations in circulation, applied across disciplines and case study contexts.

 Given the focus of the RGS/IBG2026 conference on exploring geographies of inequalities and just places, alongside the current wave of scholarship on climate apartheid, it seems an appropriate place and time to encourage further debate. As the concept and research field of climate apartheid is relatively new, we envisage this session as a platform for scholars whose research resonates with the concept of eco/climate apartheid and who are interested in engaging in debates related to the concepts’ meaning and development, politics and legitimacy, policy implications, as well as critical reflections on how climate apartheid functions ‘in practice’. Primarily we conceive this as a space to interrogate how the concept of climate apartheid is emerging conceptually and empirically.

sessions.title: Blue anxiety? Rethinking the promise of oceanic blue spaces
sessions.description:
The ocean has long been cast as cure. Its rhythms, immersions, and vertiginous horizons populate an expanding literature on therapeutic blue spaces with salutogenic promises – restorative gifts for the stressed, the depleted, the land-bound. But what happens when the cure is also the catastrophe? When the same water that heals also takes livelihoods, erodes sacred sites, deposits plastic on shores that cannot refuse it, and renders futures grammatically subjunctive? This session begins precisely where the therapeutic turn falters, insisting that “blue anxiety” – that particular dread born not merely of environmental hazard, but of the sea’s structural contradictions – is not the exception in the story of coastal wellbeing. It is, perhaps, its most honest chapter.

 The session maps this contradictory human condition across empirical, creative, and conceptual contributions spanning multiple geographies – from tropical small-island communities navigating ecological collapse to urban waterfronts caught between gentrification and climate risk; from island coastlines bearing the disproportionate moral weight of marine pollution to low-lying communities where climate denial and climate anxiety inhabit the same anxious body. Contributions engage with who gets to access the blue in the first place – asking how disability, race, class, and geography structure not just proximity to water but the very possibility of its restorative encounter. Together, they show that blue anxiety is not simply hazard-induced. It is produced: by collapsing ecosystems, by normative governance assumptions, by the slow violence of environmental injustice, and by the stubborn mismatch between the promise of coastal space and the unequal conditions of its inhabitation.

 Together, these contributions – empirical, creative, poetic, and methodologically restless – provincialise the therapeutic: they return a universal promise to its specific geographies of production, and in doing so, reveal it to have always been unevenly distributed. They think with disability as epistemic expertise, with ecological grief as political diagnostic, with creative and poetic forms of knowing as scholarly method in their own right. They ask not merely “what does the ocean do to us?” but “for whom, under what conditions, and at whose expense?” The session neither retreats to lament nor dissolves into critique without care. It insists on the dialectic: that wellbeing and anxiety, belonging and alienation, restoration and rupture are co-produced within dynamic water/land-scapes – and that holding this co-production in tension is the necessary precondition for any honest coastal justice. Our land-bound bodies breathe forever precarious on this blue planet. The theory, this session argues, must finally match the tide.

papers.title: The Sea That Heals and Kills: Wellbeing, Precariousness, and the Paradox of Tropical Blue Spaces
Citizens of a Changing Coast: Wellbeing at the Frontlines of Marine Plastic Pollution
Living with the sea: urban blue spaces, wellbeing and inequality
Unsettling the Therapeutic Coast: Disability, Risk and the Making of Blue Anxiety

sessions.title: Geographies of Impact
sessions.description:
How do research 'impact' agendas shape, and are shaped by, the questions geographers ask about power, politics, and inequality? Research impact has become central to contemporary academic institutions, shaping how research in geography and related disciplines is designed, evaluated, valued, and funded. Broadly understood as the positive effects of research on society, impact is increasingly treated as measurable, auditable, and policy-relevant, forming part of an impact culture or agenda (Chubb & Reed, 2018; Rogers et al., 2014). Research that delivers tangible benefits beyond the academy reflects geography’s commitment to justice, equity, and participatory approaches (Hopkins, 2021; Pain et al., 2011). However, dominant frameworks often prioritise particular forms of knowledge, reproduce inequalities, and incentivise research focused on outputs that are pre-defined, quantifiable, measurable (Darby, 2017; Gandy, 2014). In doing so, they can incentivise research oriented towards pre-defined, auditable outputs, while marginalising slower, relational, and less easily measurable forms of engagement (Paul et al., forthcoming).

 This session brings together scholars reflecting on the diverse geographies of impact within and beyond the academy. Rather than rejecting the notion of impact, this session creates space for critical and constructive dialogue about how impact is understood, enacted, and experienced across different contexts. We approach impact as a situated and contested practice, emerging through relationships between researchers, institutions, collaborators, and places. Engaging directly with the conference theme of Geographies of Inequalities, the session examines how contemporary impact agendas intersect with broader landscapes of power, exclusion, and marginalisation across academic research. Critics have argued that dominant framings of research impact valorise those impacts that are ‘substantial, visible, muscular, large-scale, auditable, heroic, and readily narratable’ (Horton, 2020, 4). Such framings privilege particular types of ‘impactful’ researcher and position participation as a strategic instrument, while more humble, emergent or relational forms of impact often remain obscured.

 The ‘Geographies of Impact’ session runs across two timeslots and emphasises active participation. This first session includes four fifteen-minute presentations selected through an open call, followed by a 40-minute panel discussion, facilitated by Dr. Caitlin Hafferty, with our authors to explore the diverse forms of impact raised in the papers.

 The contributions within our session explore diverse practices of research impact, from policy advisory roles within government to community-led relational collaborations and solidarity work in authoritarian contexts. Drawing on fieldwork with disabled communities, governmental engagement shaping health and transport policy, and documentation of racialised violence in authoritarian regimes, the papers examine how impact unfolds through both formal institutional channels and relational, care-full practices. Our presenters reflect on the possibilities of working inside policy spaces, weighing the benefits of proximity to decision-making against potential constraints on academic freedom, alongside the relational and emergent practices of impact to explore how meaningful change occurs through everyday interactions during research. The panel will create space for participatory reflection on these themes. Together, these discussions encourage critical dialogue about how diverse impacts are conceptualised, practised, and experienced across different contexts.

The Imperative for Engaged Research in the Face of Existential Politics: Against the Perverse Incentives of Modern Academia

sessions.title: Entanglements of Environmental (In)Justices in the Sea of Islands
sessions.description:
The Sea of Islands (Hau'ofa 1994) refers to the Pacific region where people live on islands but are connected through their common ocean. Western imaginaries of this region often focus on large linear distances on topographic maps, conceptualizing the islands as remote and isolated. They further deny and devalue the people living there by exoticizing and naturalizing them, and by focusing on their small populations rather than their rich cultural histories of resilience. The notion of “Large Ocean States” instead of “Small Islands Developing States,” promoted by political actors in the region, presents a counterpoint to these imaginaries. In order to relate to pacific epistemologies, geographic research on and about the Sea of Islands must emphasize connectivity—between diverse peoples, between human and more-than-human entities, land, sea, and sky, across generations—and thus employ relational approaches and a rather holistic way of thinking (Smith 2021). For mapping, a more relational, topological approach has been proposed (Kuper et al. 2024), which aligns with these epistemological perspectives by emphasizing connections and continuities over linear distances.
 Entangled with the misreading of the region—as part of colonial oppression—is the region’s exposure to a web of intertwined apocalyptic crises (Maurer 2024). Colonization and Christianization decimated populations through violence and imported diseases, while colonial exploitation transformed not just the socio-economic system but the very land —most notably through phosphate mining. Today, the deep-sea environments are threatened by intense resource extraction. The region's cultural neglect is contrasted by strategic militarization. Over 300 nuclear explosions were conducted from the 1940s to the 1990s in Māo’hi Nui/French Polynesia, the Marshall Islands, Kiribati, and Johnston Atoll/Kalama and have caused widespread contamination, environmental destruction, fisheries collapse, and forced resettlements. Meanwhile, climate change poses severe threats via rising sea levels, extreme weather events, and the loss of sustainable livelihoods.
 These crises are rooted in colonial histories, with powerful states denying the rights of indigenous populations. Pacific peoples advocate for environmental justice through diverse means, including art, poetry, international rallies, and panels. Their demands encompass recognition, compensation, truthtelling, nuclear disarmament, and genuine independence.
 Our session includes contributions  that integrate a focus on methodological approaches aligning with local epistemologies and realities with addressing environmental injustices, resistance, and resilience.

papers.title: Looking for lost nuclear territories
affiliations: Université Grenoble Alpes, CNRS, Sciences Po Grenoble, Pacte, Grenoble, France
