sessions.title: Chair's event: How to generate ‘thinking environments’
sessions.description:
How can we enable students, researchers, collaborators, and colleagues to do their very best thinking, for themselves? This session addresses this question by sharing the ‘time to think’ approach developed by Nancy Kline (1999, 2009, 2020).

 In this session, we will be joined by Time to Think Global Faculty member Claire Andrews who will introduce this approach and run through the ten components involved in creating a ‘thinking environment’. We will then focus on the practical applications of this including a live demonstration of a ‘time to think’ session in action. 

 Featuring Claire Andrews (independent), Professor Jen Bagelman, Renzo Szkwarok, and Professor Peter Hopkins (all Newcastle University).

sessions.title: Home–school borderlands: family, discipline, and the making of unequal childhoods
sessions.description:
Discipline and shame rarely stop at the school gate. They move through homework routines, attendance monitoring, fee reminders, parent–teacher meetings, messaging apps, and neighborhood reputational circuits—reworking who is held responsible for “good” behavior and “good” schooling, and with what emotional, social, and material consequences. These home–school borderlands can operate as infrastructures of inequality, enrolling families into regimes of respectability, compliance, and accountability while unevenly distributing blame, risk, and care across households, teachers, platforms, and institutions.

 Bringing together children’s, youth and family geographies with geographies of education and emotional/digital geographies, this session examines how unequal childhoods are produced and contested across home–school relations. We ask: How do discipline, shame, fear, and humiliation travel across school, home, and everyday urban space? What “infrastructures” (bureaucratic, spatial, and digital) intensify or redistribute educational pressure? How do these dynamics intersect with class, caste, gender, disability, migration status, language, and precarious labor—and how do children and families navigate, refuse, or rework them?

 The session connects four complementary papers. First, an ethnographic case study of low-fee private schooling in an “unauthorized” colony in Delhi examines shame-based discipline as a form of affective governance that extends into the home via parental threats and reputational pressure. Second, a Kerala-focused paper traces how legal reasoning can re-legitimate corporal punishment as “character formation,” contrasted with an alternative schooling model that rejects subjugation and adult authority as the basis of order. Third, an EdTech paper analyzes “coerced consent,” showing how school requirements and social pressure push families into educational apps while producing stratified privacy harms for children. Fourth, a China-based participatory study explores how smartphones and classed digital parenting practices reshape children’s mobility, supervision, and “affective inaccessibility” across the home–school borderland.

papers.title: ‘Beizzati’ beyond the school gate: shame-based discipline and home–school borderlands in a low-fee private school in Delhi
affiliations: Geographies of Children, Youth and Families Research Group

papers.title: “Dad Drives, I Scroll”: Smartphones, Parental Discipline, and the Making of Classed Childhoods across Urban China's Home–School Borderlands
Coerced Consent: Shame, Social Pressure, and the Erosion of Children's Privacy in Educational Technology
affiliations: Department of Politics and International Studies , Pondicherry University

sessions.title: ‘Ihsaan’ in the Urban Realm: Socio-spatial geographies of more-than-justice (2)
sessions.description: What if justice is only the minimum condition for our collective urban futures — not the horizon? Much contemporary urban theory is organised around rights, recognition, redistribution, and repair. These frameworks remain vital. Yet across cities worldwide, everyday urban life is also sustained through quieter practices that exceed calculation, obligation, and contract: care, generosity, patience, mutual holding, and collective striving. These are not easily captured by policy categories — yet they keep cities liveable where institutions fall short. This session invites scholars working with “more-than justice” frameworks in the urban realm, geographies that resist capture. Answering a pluriversal call to bring alternative ontological infrastructure, we are using “ihsaan” as a conceptual holder to refer to forms of relating and acting that go beyond what is required—practices of giving, sustaining, and enabling life that are not reducible to legal mandate, policy design, or transactional ethics. It appears in the quiet infrastructures of everyday life: neighbours holding each other up, communities extending care beyond duty, and collective acts that make the city liveable. How can we theorise them otherwise? Building on recent theorisation of ihsaan in politics (Khan, 2019) and urban studies (Shafique, 2024), this session treats ihsaan not as abstract virtue, but as urban praxis. It directs attention to the gap between formal justice and lived socio-material reality, and to the everyday labour that quietly sustains urban life where institutions fall short. We welcome theoretical and empirical contributions that rethink urban ethics, politics, and practice beyond rights-based imaginaries. We ask: // What dimensions of urban life escape the language of justice, and why do dominant urban theories struggle to grasp everyday practices of care, generosity, and dignity? // What forms of urban ethics and value exist beyond justice — beyond entitlement, repair, and compliance — and how can these be theorised without collapsing into moral idealism? // How can we reconceptualise the city as shaped by affirmative flows of desire, generosity, and collective striving, rather than only by conflict, scarcity, claim-making, and protection — and what alternative urban logics of giving, sustaining, and enabling does this reveal? If this resonates with your work, we warmly invite your contribution.

papers.title: Caring for each other: on practising Ihsaan as refusal of organized abandonment in the Dutch welfare state
Beyond Justice: Ihsaanic Urban Praxis and the Palestinian Urban Future in Gaza
Iftar Dastarkhwan as Ihsaan: Exploring the Socio-spatial Ethics of Free Communal Feeding During Ramadan in Islamabad

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Food Geographies (2)
sessions.description:
In line with RGS-IBG 2026's conference theme of Geographies of inequalities: towards just places, we invite submissions on new and emerging research within Food Geographies. We particularly encourage submissions from early career (ECRs) and postgraduate researchers (PGRs), as well as scholars working in or on the Global South, including research that challenges dominant Euro-American framings of food geographies. This session aims to provide a supportive and constructive space to share work in progress, early findings, or new conceptual and methodological approaches and can be a wonderful opportunity to foster collaborative work, positive dialogue and advance research agendas within the field of food geographies and food justice (Deschact, 2021; Heynen et. al., 2012; Slocum, 2018; among others). 

 Food is deeply entangled with social, cultural, economic, racial, gendered, and environmental inequalities, from access to land and labour, to nutrition, health, and everyday food practices (see Gibson-Graham et. al. 2013; Cadieux & Slocum, 2015). Research in food geography has shown how food systems across scales often reinforce uneven power relations, while also offering sites of resistance, care, and alternative futures (Goodman et al., 2012; Holt-Giménez, 2017).

 For this session, we are particularly interested in papers that critically examine food-related inequalities and explore pathways that broaden the scope of justice and just spaces within Food Geographies. Papers can be innovative about their research aims/design, demonstrate creative methodologies and/or theoretical questions, or draw upon empirical findings. We especially welcome work that closes the gap between food production and consumption, attends to diversity within diets, advances a de/postcolonial approach to foodways, and engages with emerging practices of gastrodiplomacy and gastrosolidarity in digital and physical spaces (Low, 2021).

 Some themes include:

 - Food access, poverty, and everyday food inequalities

 - Labour, migration, and justice in food and agricultural systems

 - Race, gender, caste, and colonial injustices in food geographies

 - Indigenous, local, and alternative food knowledges

 - Environment, sustainability, and climate inequalities in food systems

 - Food, health, and uneven outcomes across places

 - More-than-human relations, care, and food justice

 - Resistance, activism, and food-based social movements

 - Affect, emotion, and embodied geographies of food

 - Gastrodiplomacy, gastrosolidarity, and food as soft power

 - Food, place-making, heritage, and cultural memory

 - Methodological innovations in researching food and inequality

 References 

 Cadieux, K. V., & Slocum, R. (2015). What does it mean to do food justice? Journal of political ecology , 22 , 1.

 Deschacht, L. (2021). What role do urban food strategies play in fostering justice? (Doctoral dissertation, Ghent University).

 Low, K. E. (2021). Gastropolitical encounters and the political life of sensation. The Sociological Review , 69 (1), 190-205.

 Gibson-Graham, J. K., Cameron, J., & Healy, S. (2013). Take back the economy: An ethical guide for transforming our communities . U of Minnesota Press.

 Goodman, D., DuPuis, E. M., & Goodman, M. K. (2012). Alternative food networks: Knowledge, practice, and politics . Routledge.

 Heynen, N., Kurtz, H. E., & Trauger, A. (2012). Food justice, hunger and the city. Geography compass , 6 (5), 304-311.

 Holt-Giménez, E. (2017). A foodie's guide to capitalism . NYU Press.

 Slocum, R. (2018). Must everything be called “food justice?”. Local Environment , 23 (11), 1103-1105.

sessions.title: Just Rural Development, Innovative and Partnership Approaches : towards facilitating resilient communities and/or influencing policy and planning (2)
sessions.description:
Rural challenges persist in challenging acdemic research and practice based innovation though presenting wicked issues that often require or are best addressed though partnership and or place based local solutions and innovative approaches. This session calls for sharing of such examplar research that are currently involved in bringing about integrative and more just policy for rural areas as sustainably planned places. Often such work draws from an integrated acdemic – practitioner understanding of rural lived experience, policy contexts and stakeholder framimgs of the challenges faced. This may support activities and give voice to or representations of normally challenged and/or marginalised groups e.g. creative practice, community based Carbon descent, inclusive heritage, cultural innovations, digital communication, marginalised faming families, housing development, food hubs, rural training and skills innovations and hyper-local relocalisation and health-based and wellbeing projects. 

 The second hybrid panel session will invite members to engage in a facilitated lively discussion of innovative case studies, partnerships accressing rural wellbeing and good policy development.
 Invited Panel Members 
 Prof Mike Woods       Aberystwyth University and Local Partnership for Innovation and                                             Policy (LPIP) Cymru Wledig Lead

 Jessie Buchan            Together for Change, Pembs  (Panel Chair)

sessions.title: Just Transitions in and beyond China (1)
sessions.description:
The global climate and biodiversity crises cannot be addressed without ‘just transitions’ – profound and far-reaching social reconfigurations that place well-being and social justice at their core. While just transition narratives resonate with activists, scholars, and policy makers across geographies, the concept has gained significantly less traction in China. This represents a critical oversight. First, China is the largest emitter of greenhouse gases (IEA, 2023) and leading nation in renewable electricity capacity additions at the global level (IEA, 2025). China’s increasing influence – economically, politically and ecologically – places the nation in a position to significantly shape global transition trajectories. Second, ongoing and planned environmental reform within China is already producing social impacts (e.g., through the phase-out of fossil fuel industries (Gong and Lewis, 2024) and local, uneven effects of environmental conservation (Wang and Lo, 2022), only likely to deepen as transitions unfold. Third, Chinese flows of investment shape patterns of economic development and socio-spatial configurations in locations around the world (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024), with highly uneven and uncertain outcomes. Finally, theoretical frameworks on ‘justice’ (social, environmental, political, and beyond) may require re-conceptualisation in relation to China’s socio-cultural and political context. Mainstream understandings remain shaped by liberal-democratic traditions, which may constrain examinations of transitions in China and China-connected spaces (Wang and Lo, 2021).

 Mapping and exploring how just transitions unfold within and beyond China is therefore an urgent research agenda. This panel engages with multiple dimensions of these dynamics, including but not limited to:

 - Social justice and socio-economic impacts of transition-related policies and reforms in China , including uneven outcomes of industrial restructuring, labour displacement, infrastructure investment, and urban-rural inequality. This includes studies on environmental justice dimensions of transitions, such as uneven impacts of environmental policies and programmes of ecological conservation.

 - Just transition outcomes of Chinese overseas investment , such as renewable energy projects, forestry/afforestation, and mining of critical minerals. We especially invite contributions that unpack the complex dynamics at play in global supply chains and the decoupling of impacts across locations of resource extraction and product deployment. 

 - Chinese discourse of justice and just transition , including studies that explore contemporary policy narratives and public debates and their approaches to justice, as well as critiques of Anglophone conceptions of these debates. We also welcome work that engages wider and diverse forms of justice and just transition issues across China and China-connected spaces.

papers.title: Whose Justice and Justice for Whom? Reinterpreting Environmental Justice in Contemporary China
Just Transition in China: Silences and Discourses
Greening the Gentrification Trajectory: State-Led Ecological Transformation and Socio-Spatial Justice in Shanghai
Rural Revitalisation as Spatial Transition? Land Reform and Justice in Guangzhou, China

sessions.title: Sustainable Future Making and its (Uneven) Geographies (2)
sessions.description:
As climate change gathers pace with distinctly uneven geographical impacts and a growing number of world regions find themselves confronted with the socio-economic vulnerabilities arising from intersecting planetary crises, calls gather pace to envisage and enact a distinct future that is climate sensitive, environmentally sustainable and socio-ecologically fairer and just. 

 In this context we have seen increasing attention being given to future oriented frameworks on matters of the environment, climate change and sustainability. This includes compelling or dystopic narratives of future strategically mobilised as desirable or inevitable (Arnall and Hilson, 2023; Kanarp et al.,2025; Peakock and Devine-Wright, 2025) and social action that builds on the symbolic and discursive power of spatial imaginaries to challenge low-carbon futures (Ryder et al., 2025). We also witness the triumph of anticipatory governance and planning (Anderson, 2010; Granjou et al., 2017) and of future oriented tools, including scenarios, deliberative mapping foresight, anticipatory gaming, visioning, experimentation, utilised in imagining sustainable futures (Bellamy et al., 2022; Candy and Dunagan, 2017; Hebinck et al., 2018; Muiderman et al., 2017; Oomen et al., 2021). Research also directs attention to more radical visions of the future, including prefigurative futuring practices (Burnett and Nunes, 2021; Jeffrey and Dyson, 2020) and counter-imaginaries emerging from activism (Matheney et al., 2024), alongside efforts to build abundant futures (Collard et al., 2015) and liberatory futures (Narayanan, 2023). As such, scholarship increasingly points to the emotions and affects involved in constraining, enabling, accelerating or delaying transitions to sustainable futures (Durnova et al., 2025; Martiskainen and Sovacool, 2021).

 This sensibility to futures, however, also encounter critical challenges in the form, for instance, of instances of resistance (Caris and Cao, 2024; Hilson and Arnall, 2024) or greenlash/backlash (Alter and Zürn, 2020; Wild et al., 2018); embedded forms of thinking and acting that can captivate otherwise possible futures (Haajer and Oomen, 2025); ongoing and unresolved tensions around exclusion and marginalisation that can spur feelings of futurelessness and stymie sustainable futures (Della Bosca and Gillespie, 2018; Lewis, 2024; Tutton, 2023); (ideological) power structures that perpetuate high carbon futures (Bedford et al., 2023) and geo-political risks, uncertainties, changes that can disrupt desirable futures (++). 

 It is within this urgent context that we propose this session as a space to think critically and collectively about this future turn on matters of environment, climate change and sustainability. We are interested in sharing and learning from a range of geographical contexts and sectoral domains and invite contributions that conceptually and empirically explore the notion of ‘sustainable future making’, sharpen the focus on its (uneven) geographies, and deepen our understanding of the generative potential of these processes. Possible topics include:

 - Imagining and Enacting Sustainable Futures
- Futures as Governing Devices, the Governance of Sustainable Futures
- Contested Sites and Negotiated Trajectories of Sustainable Futures
- Temporalities of Sustainable Future Making
- Co-producing sustainable futures: Methodological considerations and challenges
- (Un)making Sustainable Futures: Power and Inequalities
- Emotional geographies of sustainable future-making

papers.title: (Un)fulfilled promises of the past and (im)possible futures in Sweden’s Green Industrial Revolution
(Un)making Sustainable Futures: Thinking (with) ‘Sustainability Flashpoints’
Governance Tools for Local Authority Decision-Making
affiliations: Tyndall Centre for Climate Change Research, University of Manchester

papers.title: “Curious about the past, enthusiastic about the future”: everyday cultural participation and sustainable future-making in a post-industrial place
Real-world labs as spaces of sustainable future making: prefigurative experimentation in times of planetary crisis

sessions.title: Queer Arab Geographies – Navigating Inequalities Towards Just Spaces
sessions.description:
This proposed session, “Queer Arab Geographies: Navigating Inequalities Towards Just Spaces,” seeks to explore the multifaceted intersections of queerness, Arab identities, and spatial dynamics within the broader context of geographical inequalities. Aligning with the 2026 RGS-IBG Annual International Conference theme, “Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places,” the session examines how queer Arabs negotiate, resist, and reimagine spaces marked by exclusion, peril, and privilege. From urban nocturnes in Gulf cities to diasporic migrations and historical temporalities, the session aims to highlight the ways in which queer experiences in Arab contexts challenge heteronormative structures, fostering pathways to more equitable and just geographies. 
 Queer Arab geographies remain under-researched in mainstream geographical scholarship, often overshadowed by Western-centric queer theories or Orientalist framings that exoticize or pathologize non-normative sexualities in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA). Drawing on works like Joseph Massad’s Desiring Arabs (2007) and its critiques, this session interrogates how colonial legacies, state surveillance, and global Islamophobia shape queer spatial practices. For instance, research on expatriate gay men in Dubai reveals “nocturnal performative geographies” where privilege intersects with vulnerability—spaces of temporary inclusion amid legal precarity, as expatriates leverage Western passports to navigate underground nightlife while local queer Arabs face heightened risks (Centner, 2021). Such dynamics underscore inequalities not just in access to space but in the very legibility of queer existence. 
 The session will also address migration and diaspora as key sites of queer Arab spatial politics. Queer Middle Eastern refugees often encounter “ambivalent geographies” in host countries, where asylum narratives demand performative authenticity—recounting trauma to fit Western notions of the “true self”—while erasing complex home ties (Jabr, 2021). In diasporic contexts, organizations like Al-Fatiha demonstrate coalitional possibilities, bridging queer Muslim identities across borders to counter Islamophobia and advocate for asylum without reinforcing imperial agendas (Rouhani, 2009). These migrations highlight how queer Arabs “undo belonging” to forge hybrid spaces, resisting binary notions of home/exile and contributing to just places through reimagined communities. 
 I invite contributions across formats: empirical papers on queer urbanism in cities like Beirut or Amman; theoretical explorations of queer theory’s integration into Arab geographies (e.g., clitocentrism and unfulfilled needs in queer studies); or creative interventions on digital spaces where queer Arabs build solidarity. The session aims for two timeslots (1 hour 40 minutes each), potentially online to include voices from restricted regions, ensuring inclusive engagement through live Q&A, pre-recorded inputs, and post-session sharing of materials. By centering queer Arab perspectives, this session advances towards just places, amplifying marginalized voices in geographical discourse.

sessions.title: Map Room Conversations (2): Foresting Landscapes
sessions.description: Maps establish conversations between spaces, bringing into dialogue locations, symbols, meaning, and people. The relation between mapping as practice and maps as objects has long been the subject of theoretical and methodological debate within and beyond the discipline of geography. In the sessions below we use the RGS-IBG map collection to continue this debate about maps and mapping through two types of conversation (which build on the first sessions of this type at the 2024 conference, see https://rgs-ibg.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/toc/10.1111/(ISSN)1475-4762.map-room-conv). The first is between two researchers who use maps from the collection to explore a shared interest from different perspectives. The second conversation will be between the researchers, who will present some of the maps they work with, and the participants in these interactive sessions. We invite you to take this opportunity for close encounters and conversations with the Society’s map collection.

Organising the forest space in Colonial Bombay, India

sessions.title: Dialogues in Human Geography Conversation Forum: The psyche, sense and the psychoanalytic: a conversation about the self in geographic thought.
sessions.description: How is the ‘self’ being thought in contemporary human geography? Somewhat vague perhaps, indistinct, fuzzy, a bit bewildered? Between now established work on embodiment, more recent moves to affect and emotion, and a resurgence of interest in psychoanalysis, questions of the self, of self-hood, of the psyche - of what is denoted with the adjectives ‘I’ or ‘you’ - are once again open questions. Yet there is a long tradition of engaging with these issues outside our contemporary frames, including through psychologism, humanism, phenomenology, and of course traditional metaphysics. The aim of this panel is to foster a conversation on the different ways in which the psyche might be approached, deliberated and perhaps even defined. Is the psyche a locus-point of conscious and unconscious thought? A site of motivation and instinct? A substrate of perception? An ephemeral effect of inhuman forces? A focus of affective of emotive response? Bringing together scholars in different conceptual traditions, the panel seeks to explore what the psyche is or could be, and how different framings can potentially shape our understanding of the human, human spatiality and community in geography.

papers.title: Psychoanalysis, Geography and the Psyche
On cringing and creatureliness
Elemental phenomenologies
Co-constituting expanded selves: affects and others

sessions.title: Democratising the Economy (2)
sessions.description: People across the world are rethinking and reorganising their local economies towards more democratic, equitable, and sustainable forms in response to long-term (and uneven) economic and socio-spatial inequalities and multiple crises: from the global financial crisis and austerity policies decimating cultural provision and public capacity, the COVID-19 pandemic’s exposure of complex geographies of inequality and structural issues of health and social care under capitalism, and the climate emergency which too regularly affirms a crisis of essential infrastructures created by decades of underinvestment. Theoretically, a democratisation of economic relations is, of course, closely related to the concept of economic democracy (Cumbers et al 2020), but the vision of a democratic economy also has many ‘siblings’: diverse economies (Gibson-Graham 2008), approaches including the foundational, social and solidarity, and wellbeing economies, and tools such as circular and doughnut economics (Raworth 2019). In practice, we see efforts to democratise economic activity particularly at the local and regional level such as a return of public, collective, and diverse ownership forms through de-privatisation of (public) services, infrastructures, and assets across a wide range of sectors (Kishimoto et al 2020, Cumbers and Paul 2022, Bianchi and Russell 2026). Equally, the global cooperative movement’s long history of mutual economic development and alternative practice, and more recently, experimentation with post-growth and community wealth building is taking hold in cities and communities across the world (Guinan and O’Neill 2020, Savini 2023, Crisp et al 2024). This session seeks to gather theories, approaches, models, and case studies of alternative economic development and organisation that ‘democratise the economy’ to examine them as more than the sum of their parts. Deliberately inclusive, the session invites theoretical and/ or empirical contributions, including but not limited to public, collective, and community ownership, democratic governance, municipalism, (re)municipalisation and de-privatisation, cooperativism, questions of growth, and alternative imaginaries.

papers.title: The Offers and Needs Market as a Rapid Community Wealth Building Practice
Caring regions: Towards a regional imaginary based on alternative economies, economic democracy and care?
Economic Democracy in Alternative Food Networks: the case of Denmark
The Community Economies Livelihood Framework: building the knowledge base for ethical economies
affiliations: Te Herenga Waka - Victoria University of Wellington

sessions.title: Making Sense of Unstable Ground: Living On with Disaster (2)
sessions.description:
Environmental disasters are often approached as moments of rupture requiring technical intervention, governance, or recovery. Yet for those who live with ongoing environmental instability, disaster is also a condition of everyday life, one that must be interpreted, endured, and rendered meaningful in order for life to continue. This panel invites papers that examine how people make sense of unstable ground and persist in living with it, attending to the ethical, temporal, spiritual, pragmatic, political, and moral forms of reasoning through which environmental volatility come to be lived with, remains tolerable, or becomes inhabitable.

 We are particularly interested in empirically grounded work that explores how expectations of loss, recurrence, return, or repair shape everyday conduct in landscapes affected by flooding, landslides, erosion, drought, or seismic uncertainty. How are futures imagined, and obligations sustained amid prolonged instability? How do religious or sacred understandings, moral responsibilities, temporal orientations, and situated knowledges inform decisions about staying, moving, rebuilding, or enduring? What forms of reasoning emerge in contexts where disaster is neither exceptional nor resolved?

 The panel especially welcomes contributions from settings that sit at the physical, political, or epistemic margins of dominant disaster narratives, including postcolonial and Global South contexts. We invite papers that foreground lived reasoning and situated knowledge, and the practices through which people continue inhabiting environments marked by ongoing instability, including the thresholds, tensions, and decisions through which staying, adaptation, or departure are weighed and enacted, and that invite reflection on how Geography has approached disaster. Contributions may engage critically with but are not limited to themes such as:

 - Moral and ethical reasoning in disaster-affected lives

 - Religious, sacred, or cosmological interpretations of environmental instability

 - Temporalities of waiting, anticipation, recurrence, and endurance

 - Living with infrastructure, protection, or neglect

 - Practices of staying, return, repair, and inhabitation under conditions of ongoing risk, including the thresholds and tensions through which leaving or relocation may be considered

 - Disaster as a lived condition rather than a discrete event

 - Social contracts and imagined obligations in at-risk contexts, including how expectations, responsibilities, and claims are negotiated between residents, states, infrastructures, and other forms of authority

papers.title: Living on or letting go: diffuse decision-making under uncertain climate futures in Southern Louisiana
Contingency and Constrained Choice: Planning Life on Unstable Administrative Ground in Post-Earthquake Antakya
Loss, adaptation, and ‘business as usual’ at the frontline of coastal change
Community involvement in wildfire risk reduction through a social contract lens: a case study in Yellowknife, Northwest Territories, Canada
affiliations: Priestley Centre for Climate Futures, School of Earth, Environment and Sustainability, School of Geography, Faculty of Environment, University of Leeds

sessions.title: Geographies of Care and Violence (2)
sessions.description:
Feminist, political, and social geographies have richly examined care as an ethical relation, a form of labour, an affective practice, and a political project (Bartos 2018; Bondi 2008; Gutiérrez Sánchez 2022; McDowell 2004; Power and Hall 2018; Raghuram et al. 2009; Raghuram 2016), fundamental to the ways lives, bodies, and worlds are sustained (Fisher and Tronto 1990). At the same time, geographers have extensively documented violence in its many forms—intimate, structural, slow, and spectacular (Christian and Dowler 2019; Pain and Cahill 2022). The session seeks to examine the relations between care and violence.

 This session examines care and violence not as opposites, but as entangled and co-constitutive processes. The session is particularly interested in work that explores how violence is sustained, extended, or transformed through the absence, withdrawal, misrecognition, or uneven distribution of care, and how care may be complicit in or resistant to violence.

 Feminist geographies of care have argued that the relationship between caregiver and care receiver is not a simple or linear practice, unsettling assumptions that care is inherently benign, highlighting its ambivalences, exclusions, and conflicts, and calling for greater attention to the ways care and conflict are not mutually exclusive (Bartos 2018; Power 2019; Raghuram et al. 2009).

 Building on these debates, this session foregrounds questions of carelessness, neglect, abandonment, and misaligned care, asking how harm persists not only through acts of violence but also through everyday relations, institutional practices, and political–economic arrangements that deny or foreclose care. By bringing care and violence into conversation, this session seeks to open space to explore how care might be reimagined as a political and geographical practice capable of interrupting violence.

papers.title: Community care as a resistance strategy against structural and legislative violences. Abortion mobilities and digital support system in the US.
Care in Crisis: Involuntary psychiatric detention as a violent practice of care

Enforced transnationalism from an ethics of care perspective: barriers to living ‘care-filled lives’ and resistance tactics among ‘forced’ transnational families in the UK
Entanglements of care and violence in the lives of refugee medical professionals

sessions.title: The overlooked spatial politics of the energy transition (2)
sessions.description: In the grand task of overhauling energy infrastructures to shift towards fossil fuel-free sources and reduce energy consumption, proponents such as municipalities and other public actors have generally foregrounded technology, policy, and cooperation. In this process, other – and perhaps less solutionist – dynamics have largely been overlooked, deemed minor, or have operated under the radar. This session invites contributions on dynamics in the energy transition that complement dominant dialogues. The session aims to (1) provide multi-scalar readings of the transition, (2) contribute to dialogues on the multiplicity of the transition process in various contexts, and (3) collect and share empirical contributions outlining how energy transitions are being materialized. We will discuss contributions that bring insights from diverse geographical contexts, alongside conceptual discussions. The sub-topics include, but are not limited to: • the role of everyday maintenance and repair • geographies of waste and redundancy • social reproduction and the energy transition at the micro-scale (gender, households, unpaid labour, and more) • the role of the arts, creative expression, and storytelling • social infrastructures of "left behind" regions

sessions.title: Feminist political ecologies of the agrarian-forest interface
sessions.description:
Feminist political ecology has long foregrounded how inequalities in resource access and use, politics of representation, and ways of knowing shape human-environment relations (Elmhirst 2015, Rocheleau et al. 2013). More recent political ecology research from feminists and beyond has sought to explicitly link justice frameworks to understand how multiple forms of inequalities in terms of distributional, procedural, and recognitional justice are reproduced, contested, or subverted through social (in)actions (Svarstad & Benjaminsen 2020, San Martin & Wood 2022, Sultana 2021). These concerns are especially acute at the agrarian-forest interface where communities reliant on natural resources for their livelihoods struggle to survive amidst threats from land grabs, technocratic conservation, capitalist-patriarchal systems, and adverse incorporation to capitalist markets (Elias et al. 2021, Martin et al. 2016). Yet agrarian–forest interfaces are also sites of possibility, where struggles for justice take shape through Indigenous and women’s land rights movements, food sovereignty initiatives, and climate justice organizing (Deere 2017, Guerrero 2013, Radcliffe 2014). 

 In this session we invite contributions that use feminist political ecology to critically analyze politics of inequality and justice across diverse agrarian-forest interfaces. We are particularly interested in research that attends to intersectional difference—including but not limited to class, caste, gender, sexuality, and able-bodied-ness—and traces how injustices operate across political-economic, epistemic, embodied, and intimate scales. We welcome empirically grounded and theoretically engaged papers, as well as contributions that feature novel or innovative methodological approaches to studying these issues or elevate movements for justice and healing in agrarian-forest landscapes.

 Works Cited 

 Deere, C. D. (2017). Women's land rights, rural social movements, and the state in the 21st‐century Latin American agrarian reforms. Journal of Agrarian Change 17(2), 258-278.

 Elias, M., Joshi, D., & Meinzen-Dick, R. (2021). Restoration for whom, by whom? A feminist political ecology of restoration. Ecological Restoration , 39 (1-2), 3-15.

 Elmhirst, R. (2015). Feminist political ecology. In The Routledge handbook of political ecology (pp. 519-530). Routledge.

 Guerrero, M. A. J. (2013). Civil rights versus sovereignty: Native American women in life and land struggles. In Feminist genealogies, colonial legacies, democratic futures (pp. 101-122). Routledge.

 Martin, A., Coolsaet, B., Corbera, E., Dawson, N. M., Fraser, J. A., Lehmann, I., & Rodriguez, I. (2016). Justice and conservation: The need to incorporate recognition. Biological conservation , 197 , 254-261.

 Radcliffe, S. A. (2014). Gendered frontiers of land control: indigenous territory, women and contests over land in Ecuador. Gender, Place & Culture , 21 (7), 854-871.

 Rocheleau, D., Thomas-Slayter, B., & Wangari, E. (2013). Feminist political ecology: Global issues and local experience . Routledge.

 San Martin, W., & Wood, N. (2022). Pluralising planetary justice beyond the North-South divide: Recentring procedural, epistemic, and recognition-based justice in earth-systems governance. Environmental Science & Policy , 128 , 256-263.

 Sultana, F. (2021). Political ecology 1: From margins to center. Progress in human geography , 45 (1), 156-165.

 Svarstad, H., & Benjaminsen, T. A. (2020). Reading radical environmental justice through a political ecology lens. Geoforum , 108 , 1-11.

sessions.title: Author meets critics: Thomas Dekeyser's Techno-Negative: A Long History of Refusing the Machine
sessions.description:
This panel is an Author Meets Critics session focused on Thomas Dekeyser's recent book, Techno-Negative: A Long History of Refusing the Machine (2026, University of Minnesota Press). See the book's description below.  

 A radical history of technology told through acts of resistance, not progress 
 A radical history of technology told through acts of resistance, not progressThe
 history of technology is often told as a history of progress, moving 
optimistically and inevitably from one emancipatory invention to the 
next. Techno-Negative turns this story on its head, taking us on a
 journey to the critical junctures where people have pointedly rejected 
and tried to undo, rather than adopt, new technologies. Beginning with 
Archimedes’s decision to destroy his own war machines, this book 
explores the will to negate technology as a deep—but persistently 
condemned—current in history.

 As he presents a new theory of 
technological power, Thomas Dekeyser argues that technologies, never 
neutral, operate as “ontological policing,” drawing the boundaries of 
humanness as they are unequally leveraged by select groups. Looking 
beyond the Luddites to medieval monks banning tools, seventeenth-century
 loom burners, revolutionary lantern smashers, and computer arsonists, 
Dekeyser shows how people have long recognized and resisted the machine 
as a violent, sometimes deadly force implicated in defining who counts 
as human and whose lives (and ways of life) are worth saving.

 Against
 the ubiquitous demands to reform or accelerate technological 
“advancement” that have failed to disrupt our present, Dekeyser proposes
 a spirited alternative: abolition. He challenges us to rethink the 
terms of our technological present and future. In a time when Big Tech 
grows increasingly enmeshed with authoritarian control, Techno-Negative is a conceptual declaration, and source of inspiration, for those searching for a new paradigm of technological politics.

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Gender and Feminist Geography (2)
sessions.description:
The Gender and Feminist Research Group of the RGS (with the IBG) warmly encourages postgraduates and early career researchers to submit papers for our new and emerging research session. The session is ideal for researchers who would like an opportunity to present their research in a supportive and constructive academic environment, and for researchers at all stages of their careers who are interested in presenting papers that actively engage with discussions on current and emerging theoretical or methodological innovations in the field of feminist and gender geography.

 ‘Gender and Feminist Geographies’ is intended to cover a broad spectrum of research and papers are welcome from any area of feminist and gender geographical inquiry, with the aim of bringing together current and emerging themes, issues and approaches. Papers are especially welcome that connect with this year’s theme of “Geographies of Inequalities: Toward Just Places” at any stage in their research process are welcome, making the session a great opportunity for early career and postgraduate researchers to gain experience presenting their work. The sessions will also provide a forum to meet and discuss emerging ideas with other researchers in a friendly and relaxed environment, as well as opportunities to explore possibilities and relevance of engaging with feminist theory and methods within research.

 If you would like to submit a paper for consideration, please send a 300-word abstract to Rituparna Bhattacharyya, rituparna.bhattacharyya@accb.org.uk and Alkım Karaağaç, e.a.karaagac@bham.ac.uk, by Friday, 20th February 2026.

sessions.title: Reparative Geographies of Education: Children’s politics, visions and future-making
sessions.description:
Reparative approaches call on us to surface the interconnections between the past, present and futures in both the formation of educational injustice and its repair. They recognise how the ‘afterlives’ of historical injustice shape interests and action within the spatial infrastructures of education systems today. Structures of deprivation and advantage can be inherited as history traces across generations as well as sediments in place. However, injustice does not have to be predetermined, totalising or inevitable: redress and repair can offer new possibilities towards the building of more hopeful futures. 

 This session makes a case for reparative geographies of education and explores how we might harness its future-focused and action-orientated commitments. We do so through a collection of papers based on our work on the Reparative Futures of Education (Repair-Ed) project, a five-year research project based at the University of Oxford. The project involves place-based research with 10 schools and their surrounding communities in the city of Bristol (UK), examining past and present racial and classed injustices in experiences of primary schooling. It explores what reparative action for these injustices might look like, aiming to foster cross-city dialogue towards the collective recognition and responsibility for redress.

 The session draws together the theoretical and methodological dimensions of our work on Repair-Ed, and introduces our development of a spatial-reparative praxis that is experimental and responsive to the lived histories and materialities of schooling in Bristol. Our praxis calls for responsiveness to how structural injustice is sedimented in place and carried in the body, and how repair necessitates opening creative spaces of participation, dialogue and exchange. Through a multi-scalar approach, our session brings together critical reflections on the possibilities and challenges of building a reparative praxis across different threads of our work on the project.

 In recognition that children’s voices are central to reimagining reparative futures of education, we explore what a spatial-reparative praxis might look like in the context of our participatory research with primary school children. This area of our research addresses the question: how do children make sense of past and present injustices in schooling and how do they conceptualise educational justice for future children? The session will introduce reparative approaches to working with children as researchers and co-creators of knowledge through a range of approaches, from Youth Participatory Action Research to photo-walk methods. It will invite discussion on how children make sense of the spatiality of injustice in their schools, local communities and the wider city. It will also open reflection on their ideas and actions towards reimaging school spaces, educational infrastructures and their relational connections to their locals and the wider city.

papers.title: How do children imagine just futures of education? Creating reparative futures of education through Youth Participatory Action Research
The Futures We Inherit: Children, Space, and the Reparative Politics of Education
Reciprocal Encounters: Photo-Walking and Reparative Visual Pedagogies
Towards just educational futures: Experimenting with a spatial-reparative praxis

sessions.title: Elemental thinking: Shaping worlds, shaping lives
sessions.description:
Elemental thinking: shaping worlds, shaping lives responds to a growing appetite among scholars, students, professionals, and practitioners fascinated by the interplay between the elements and our lives. That appetite is underpinned by people’s desire to care for Earth, for others, and for themselves in ways that account for the planet’s dynamism, power, and agency. This, then, will be a wide-ranging panel discussion for those who want to explore how we live, think, feel, act and are shaped by elemental forces. Such forces include but are not limited to earth, air, fire, water, metal, wood, ice, or void. Among other topics, panellists will consider the idea that elements actively co-constitute bodies, subjectivities, relations, settings, and environments. The session should appeal to those interested in examining this idea in diverse and generative ways that extend our understanding of being in the world.

 The core argument to be explored is that elements are not passive entities that support human life from the outside; they are dynamic participants in shaping what lives are possible and how those lives unfold in worlding processes. By attending to how elements form, constrain, and enable bodily, emotional, spiritual, cultural, political and other experiences, the discussion will offer a framework for understanding diverse and divergent worlds and selves. In doing so, it will invite participants to rethink the elements as central to questions of identity, power, knowledge, survival, and our capacities to flourish. 
 Importantly, the panel will also be an opportunity to launch the Palgrave Macmillan Series in Elemental Thinking. Members of the core editorial team will be on hand to listen to and learn from prospective authors and editors of collections about your ideas for possible book proposals. We warmly encourage seasoned and nascent elemental thinkers to join us for this event.

sessions.title: Attuning to More-than-human Seas: Negotiating Multispecies Justice in the Anthropocene Ocean (2)
sessions.description:
“What would it mean to take the Anthropocene out to sea?” (Alaimo, 2017, p. 153). With this provocation, Alaimo highlights the persistent neglect of marine environments and species within the terra-centric Anthropocene discourse. Oceans are profoundly marked by human activity, yet continue to be framed as vast, remote, and seemingly inexhaustible spaces for exploitation. Over the past two decades, the ocean ‘out there’ has been promoted as the latest frontier for economic development and an unexplored playground for climate change mitigation, often under the banner of “blue growth” (Johnson and Braverman, 2019; Bennett et al., 2021; Buck, 2019). Critically engaging with Blue Economy ambitions (and their unintended consequences), this session explores how sustainable futures and multispecies justices might be realised in Anthropocene seas. 

 Blue spaces beyond the coastline are typically uninhabited by humans, difficult to access and govern, and frequently located beyond the boundaries of national territories. Such spaces are nevertheless deeply entangled with global political economies, colonial histories, resource extraction, and uneven distributions of environmental risk, responsibility, and benefit. These dynamics increasingly render marginalised coastal communities and marine organisms vulnerable to the cascading impacts of human activities in the ocean. Put simply, Anthropocene seas are inherently unequal places and spaces. 

 Taking seriously the dynamic fluidity of the ocean requires moving beyond static separations between humans and nature, sea and land, or matter and mediation (Steinberg and Peters, 2015; Peters and Steinberg, 2019), to instead spotlight turbulent marine lifeworlds and more-than-human aquatic assemblages that are subjected to – yet also contest, challenge, and resist – unjust oceanic political economies. We thus invite papers that grapple with diverse notions of multispecies justice, blue justice, and blue degrowth to highlight overlooked oceanic geographies and to ask how Anthropocene seas can become sites for just and sustainable multispecies futures. 

 We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes: 

 - Extending critical Anthropocene scholarship to oceanic contexts 
- More-than-human engagements with the Blue Economy and blue (de)growth 
- Indigenous and decolonial perspectives on Anthropocene oceans 
- Afrofuturism, speculative ecologies and imagined ocean futures 
- More-than-human oceanic geopolitics 
- Affect and the ocean: embodied and emotional human-marine relations 
- Blue animal geographies 
- Multispecies marine methodologies

sessions.title: Political discontent and ‘left-behind’ people and places: perceptions, narratives, and regional futures
sessions.description:
The uneven impacts of global megatrends – technological change and digitalisation, demographic shifts such as urbanisation, ageing and migration, and climate-related transformations – have increasingly interacted with recent economic and geopolitical shocks, notably rising energy costs and persistent cost-of-living pressures. Together, these processes have intensified spatial inequalities across regions and social groups in many advanced economies (OECD, 2022).

 While much existing research has conceptualised political discontent through the lens of left-behind regions, recent work suggests that this framing risks oversimplifying a far more granular geography of disadvantage. Growing attention has therefore turned to how these uneven transformations are experienced, interpreted, and narrated within specific places. Emerging evidence shows that experiences of deprivation, declining opportunity, and weakened social infrastructure are often highly localised, cutting across rural and urban settings and appearing even within otherwise prosperous regions. Left-behindness, in this sense, is not a fixed territorial category but a place-specific condition,shaped by combinations of access to services, demographic change, labour-market dynamics, and social fabric (e.g. RodríguezPose, 2018; Houlden et al., 2024; de Vega Perancho et al., 2025).

 This session builds on the literature on the ‘geography of discontent’ (Los et al., 2017; McCann, 2018; 2019), but explicitly moves beyond explanations centred solely on socio-economic decline or compositional disadvantage. Instead, it foregrounds the perceptual, emotional, and narrative dimensions through which spatial inequalities are understood and mobilised, and examines how these dimensions interact with policy responses and regional development strategies.

 We invite contributions that explore how political discontent is shaped by spatial imaginaries, collective memories, and local identities, and how these processes vary across places and scales. The session pays particular attention to the implications for place-based policy: how policies are perceived by local actors, how they may unintentionally reinforce feelings of neglect or mistrust, and how alternative approaches might better engage with the lived experiences of socalled ‘left-behind’ places.

 Themes and questions the session seeks to address include (non-exhaustive):

 - How are experiences of left-behindness constructed, perceived, and narrated at local scales?

 - What do recent multidimensional and granular approaches reveal about hidden or unexpected geographies of disadvantage?

 - How do perceptions of neglect and misrecognition interact with long-term economic and demographic change?

 - In what ways does political discontent function as a spatial feedback loop, reinforcing territorial inequalities?

 - How can concepts such as liveability and the foundational economy enrich understandings of political discontent?

 - What kinds of place-based policies can engage with everyday experiences of inequality without reproducing mistrust or territorial stigma?

 Conceptual, theoretical, and empirical contributions – qualitative or quantitative – are welcome. We particularly encourage papers that connect political geography, regional studies, and public policy, and that speaks to contemporary debates on democracy, spatial inequality, and regional futures.

 Submission details

 If you are interested in participating in this special session, please submit an abstract via the general abstract submission system of the conference by the stated deadline (03/03/2026).

 For any questions, please contact the session organiser

sessions.title: Cartographies of Everyday Inequalities (2)
sessions.description:
Cartographies of Everyday Inequalities: Spatial entanglements in urban spaces Dr. Kiran Bhairannavar and Vishavjeet Dhanda Department of Geography, University of Delhi

 Key words : urban space, inequalities, just city, informalities, invisibilities, lived space

 Who gets stopped while walking? Who gets policed in public spaces? Whose vending is penalized and whose informal practices remain invisible? These questions raise important concerns about how authority is selectively exercised on certain bodies and practices that become targets of regulation, while similar practices by others evade scrutiny entirely. The session examines how everyday spaces—streets, markets, parks, public spaces, homes, neighbourhoods, digital spaces—become sites where spatial inequalities are produced, contested, and navigated (Martínez Rodríguez, 2019; Cohen & Knierbein, 2021). We focus on mapping these inequalities not as static distributions of disadvantage but as dynamic socio-spatial processes.

 There has been considerable work on urban inequalities as a key theme, however an everyday episodic lens largely remains distant (Ghertner, 2015; Massa, 2020; Jaffe, 2025). Taking this as a point of departure, we are interested in ‘everyday inequalities’ as emerging through intersecting spatial entanglements produced by state mechanisms, infrastructural arrangements, and socio-cultural hierarchies. (Simone, 2021; Kulz, 2025; Zsinka & Berger, 2025; Shafique, 2025). We seek to better understand how spatial practices further complicate these inequalities through differential toleration, penalization, or regularization depending on who occupies space, where and how; reinforcing hierarchies along lines of caste, class, gender, race, ethnicity, religion, sexuality and ability.

 Foregrounding within these ideas, we are looking forward to contributions that help us further unpack the everyday nuances of inequalities. We seek answers to: How can we map everyday spatial inequalities beyond conventional approaches? What methods, tools and frameworks can reveal intersecting logics of agency, power, informalities, survival, and resistance? How do we foreground marginalized experiences ethically and non-extractively within the urban cultural episteme? How do everyday inequalities shape differential lived realities and futures? What role do affective and embodied experiences play in sustaining or challenging spatial hierarchies? How might everyday inequalities as a concept transform urban policy, planning practice, and governance frameworks toward more just cities and urban spaces?

 The session welcomes contributions addressing: 

 - Mapping everyday inequalities through banalities of access, waiting, affordability, and precarity
- Everyday carceralities: discretion, extortion, selective enforcement, and penalization in routine practices
- Public complicity and middle-class activism invoking/resisting state violence to securitize/secure everyday spaces
- Embodied and emotional geographies of survival, policing, and displacement
- Materialities and discourses: how everyday spaces are operationalized, contested, and reimagined.
