sessions.title: (Re)Imagining Just Climate Futures through Creative Approaches (2): World Cafe
sessions.description:
Climate change is not only a technical or environmental challenge; it
 is also a struggle of imagination (Yusoff & Gabrys, 2011). Dominant
 climate futures are increasingly shaped by models, metrics and 
algorithmic projections, while questions of justice, care, power and 
responsibility are often sidelined. In line with the theme of the 2026 
RGS-IBG conference, Geographies of Inequalities: Towards Just Places, we
 invite contributions that interrogate these dominant imaginaries and 
experiment with creative techniques to (re)imagine just climate futures.

 Drawing on feminist ethics of care and a reparative orientation (Puig
 de la Bellacasa, 2017; Myers et al., 2024; Tuck, 2009), we approach 
future-making as situated, relational labour—work that foregrounds 
uneven burdens and asks how climate imaginaries may normalise loss and 
reproduce colonial hierarchies by privileging some lives, knowledges and
 places over others (Sultana, 2022). Creative methods are not simply 
alternative modes of presentation here; they are ways of knowing and 
intervening (e.g. Hawkins, 2015). Working with affect, embodiment and 
narrative, they can unsettle model- and metric-driven registers and make
 room for futures that challenge what counts as “realistic.” 
Contributions in this panel thus analyse and experiment with creative 
approaches—art–science collaboration, participatory speculation, 
speculative mapping and also drawing (see e.g. Davies, 2024; Perry, 
2018) —while attending to the frictions, ethical dilemmas and power 
relations involved in imagining otherwise. 

 The session aims to offer an open space to share reflective and 
experimental contributions on futuring practices and future-oriented 
research in geography and allied fields. We welcome work that attends to
 research processes as much as outcomes, including limitations, 
frictions, failures, ethical dilemmas and political tensions that emerge
 when working with uncertain futures, communities and technologies. We 
encourage a variety of formats, from traditional papers to art-based and
 practice-based contributions and other experimental formats (e.g., 
performances, facilitated exercises, video screenings). 

 We are particularly interested in contributions addressing:

 - Futuring practices and future-oriented methodologies centering the role of conflicts, emotions and positionalities
- Reparative, caring and justice-oriented approaches to climate futures engaging with feminist and decolonial debates
- Art–science collaborations and interdisciplinary research
- Creative,
 participatory and experimental research methods including the use of 
drawing, illustration, comics and/or speculative cartography
- Critical perspectives on AI, data and modelling in climate governance
- Questions of inclusion, exclusion and power in processes of future-making

sessions.title: Transport and Mobility Justice: Current Trajectories and Future Directions
sessions.description:
Transport and mobility justice scholarship has long been concerned with uneven access and opportunity. Early work treated accessibility as a social indicator for evaluating public policy (Wachs and Kumagai, 1973), while the spatial mismatch hypothesis exposed how racialised housing discrimination and uneven automobility constrained access to suburbanising employment (Kain, 1968). These contributions established distributional inequality as a central concern and positioned transport as constitutive of social participation rather than a neutral technical domain.

 Over time, the field deepened and diversified. Feminist research revealed the spatial containment and entrapment experienced by women across racialised and classed positions (Inwood et al., 2015), linking everyday mobility constraints to labour markets and care responsibilities (Gilow, 2020). Studies of transport subsidies, infrastructure provision and appraisal practices demonstrated how benefits and burdens are unevenly allocated, often legitimised through ostensibly neutral metrics (Pereira et al., 2019). Work on transport disadvantage and social exclusion conceptualised mobility constraints as both cause and consequence of marginalisation (Lucas et al., 2016). In parallel, mobilities scholarship reframed the debate, introducing concepts such as motility and interrogating the discursive and material production of uneven mobility regimes (Kaufmann et al., 2004). Normative interventions, notably Martens’ (2016) sufficiency-based framework and Sheller’s (2018) articulation of mobility justice across scales, sharpened questions of justice, power and political economy.

 Taken together, this body of work has substantially reoriented transport scholarship. It has displaced narrow performance metrics, foregrounded distributional and recognitional claims, and situated everyday mobility within wider regimes of power, political economy and environmental change. Transport and mobility justice are now recognised as constitutive of social and spatial ordering, not marginal correctives to technical planning.

 This roundtable seeks to broaden and pluralise debates on transport and mobility justice by bringing transport and mobilities scholars into sustained dialogue. In particular, it invites reflection on how transport and mobility justice can engage more substantively with decolonial thought and Global South urbanisms, and what it would mean to take intersectionality and care seriously at the level of method and institutional practice. As decarbonisation and digitalisation reshape infrastructures and mobility regimes, the session will also consider how justice claims should be negotiated across scales, from local accessibility to transnational circulations, without reducing political contestation to technocratic metrics. The objective is to expand the conceptual, geographical and normative horizons of the field while consolidating a forward-looking research agenda.

 References 

 Gilow, M.(2020). “It's work, physically and logistically”. JoTG , 85 ,102693.

 Inwood, J.F., et al.(2015). "Where Do We Go From Here”. Southeastern Geographer , 55 (4),417-433.

 Kain, J.F.(1968). Housing segregation, negro employment, and metropolitan decentralization. TQJoE , 82 (2),175-197.

 Kaufmann, V., et.al. (2004). Motility. IJURR , 28 (4), 745-756.

 Lucas, K., et.al. (2016). Transport poverty and its adverse social consequences. PICET , 169 (6),353-365.

 Martens, K. (2016). Transport Justice . Routledge.

 Pereira, R. H., et.al. (2019). Distributional effects of transport policies on inequalities in access to opportunities in Rio de Janeiro. JTLU , 12 (1),741-764.

 Sheller, M. (2018). Mobility Justice . Verso.

 Wachs, M., & Kumagai, T.G. (1973). Physical accessibility as a social indicator. SEPS , 7 (5),437-456.

sessions.title: Governing Transitions, Distributing Consequences: Locating Institutional (In)justice in Food Systems (2)
sessions.description:
Food geographies have generated rich insights into the spatial politics of production, consumption, and alternative food networks. Food systems scholarship, meanwhile, has advanced understanding of interconnections across scales and sectors. Yet both literatures tend to concentrate analytical attention at the "ends" of food systems, whether on farming landscapes and agrarian change or on ‘the food environment’ (retail environments, households, and eating practices). This session turns attention to the spaces in between: the processing facilities, distribution networks, wholesale markets, and public provisioning systems that constitute the "missing middle" of food system governance. These are places where (in)justice takes shape, yet they remain underexamined as sites of geographical inquiry.

 We invite contributions that examine how inequalities are produced, reproduced, and contested in these institutional spaces, attending to both the process and outcomes of food system transformation. How are transformation pathways governed, and whose interests shape them? And crucially, what happens after? Even transformations pursued in the name of justice generate consequences: shifts in where food is produced and processed, new winners and losers across global supply networks, and reconfigured relations between humans, animals, and environments. How are these consequences distributed across places, species, and countries, and how might governance arrangements anticipate and address the new inequalities that just transitions themselves produce?

 We particularly welcome contributions that extend justice frameworks beyond the human while remaining structurally and institutionally grounded. Rather than focusing on ethical consumption or community-based multispecies encounters, we seek analyses of how human-animal-environment relations and multispecies futures are shaped by institutional and non-human factors. Topics might include: public food procurement; wholesale, processing, and distribution governance; lock-in and path dependency; transition governance and its distributive effects; institutional arrangements for animal welfare; and anticipatory approaches to just transition.

papers.title: Enabling the sustained effective use of foresight to improve food system sustainability in regulatory and policy contexts
The “Right to Grow” for Food and Nature: an analysis of policy and practice through a multispecies justice lens
Institutional (In)justice in Swiss Food Governance: Labour, Land, and the Missing Middle
affiliations: Faculté des géosciences et de l'environnement;  Institut de géographie et durabilité; PLANH is the Planning and Housing Research Group

papers.title: Who grows, who knows? Framing sustainable food futures

sessions.title: Environmental Geography without AMOC (Atlantic Meridional Overturning Current)
sessions.description:
What happens to environmental geography if the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Current—AMOC—significantly weakens or collapses? It is a scenario of increasing importance for everybody concerned with how this critical current in the Atlantic Ocean, and which transports warm water to northwestern Europe, is affected by a confluence of anthropogenic forces, of which climate change is a principal driver. What happens to environmental geography—and what is happening already—when irreversible shifts to AMOC are examined, contemplated, and speculatively engaged?
 This panel identifies the potential collapse of AMOC as a significant moment across geography, and especially for environmental geographers. The scale, significance, and systemic impacts of shifts in AMOC throw together oceanic, coastal, and terrestrial human-environment relations in ways that do not fit easily with either human or physical geography approaches to them. Yet issues around how futures are imagined or foreclosed, and how scholarship before, during, or after critical tipping points are crossed press for responses. In this context, this panel discusses the ways that AMOC, and its potential collapse, demand conceptual vocabularies and empirical categories sufficient to gain purchase on complex and potentially rapidly shifting human-environment relationships.

papers.title: Futures thinking and the politics of anticipation
The future of human–coastal coupled systems
Ice and the AMOC
Geography with AMOC
What is a current? AMOC and the semiotics of flow

sessions.title: Centering intersectional feminist approaches in mobility planning (1)
sessions.description:
The lack of free, safe, and accessible mobility limits access to opportunities and restricts full participation in urban life. In response, transforming transport systems towards more sustainable and just models has become urgent, guided by principles of equity and inclusion that underpin “just mobility transitions” (Oliveira Soares & Glaser, 2025; Schwanen, 2021; Sheller, 2018). Within this process, the gender equality perspective has been crucial in making visible the structural inequalities faced by women and other historically marginalised groups in urban planning and mobility. However, while some cities worldwide have implemented gender-sensitive urban mobility policies, the transformative potential of intersectional feminist planning as an integral part of just mobility transitions is yet to be realised. Gender-focused/gender-mainstreaming policies targeting women have been a key starting point for addressing existing inequalities (Jaimurzina et al., 2017; Sánchez de Madariaga, 2013). However, without an intersectional dimension and integral approach, such policies may have a narrow focus and limited impact, thus benefitting some groups more than others or be at risk of being watered down or even hijacked by populist agendas. Additionally, even within feminist movements and organisations pursuing mobility justice for various groups, intersectional thinking is sometimes not integrated, leading to exclusions, omissions and missed opportunities for demanding a broader change. A more radical revision of mobility planning regimes that intersectional feminist lens could inspire is yet to happen. This revision would challenge assumptions and values underpinning mobility planning beyond just making selected services and places ‘women friendly’. Instead, applying intersectional feminist lens to rethinking mobilities could inspire centering care, accessibility for all and combatting intersectional exclusions of multiple marginalized groups, linked to income, disability, ethnic and religious background, gender identity, sexual orientation, etc. (Castañeda et al., 2024; Jirón et al., 2022; Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025). 
 This challenge resonates with what epistemic justice scholarship in mobility planning has highlighted: the epistemic frameworks that shape urban and transport planning remain largely gender-insensitive (Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025), thus making it difficult to effectively integrate this approach. Conceptual frameworks, tools, and indicators that guide planning policies are still anchored in dominant ways of producing data, designing policy, and evaluating mobility systems. These technocratic and androcentric paradigms prioritise quantitative evidence, productive (work-related) mobility, and a conception of the human being as a rational, utility-maximising agent. In doing so, they marginalise relational, collective, and everyday experiences of mobility, care-related and recreational mobilities, and erase the diverse positionalities from which the city is experienced. Additionally, politics and geographies of academic and professional knowledge production and circulation do not offer an even ground for ideas, practices and activist efforts to receive exposure.  

 Bringing intersectional feminist and epistemic justice perspectives together, the session interrogates dominant gender-insensitive planning paradigms – quantitative, productivity-centred, androcentric, and oriented around the rational, utility‑maximising individual – that systematically marginalise care, relational, recreational and collective everyday (im)mobilities. It asks how knowledge hierarchies in academia, policy, and professional practice shape what counts as evidence, which actors and geographies are heard, and whose experiences of the city remain invisible.

papers.title: Challenging the epistemic foundations of mobility planning from an intersectional feminist perspective in Amsterdam, Barcelona and Santiago de Chile
Care, Control, and Capability: Challenging the Epistemic Foundations of Mobility Planning
Planning as Care: Reimagining Transport Through Relational Mobilities
Rendering Mobility of Care (In)Visible: Epistemic Injustice in Mobility Data
Between Mapping and Meaning: Intersectional Feminist Reflections on Spatialising Urban Safety

sessions.title: Care in Crisis Systems: Engaged research in and of the health system
sessions.description:
Renditions of NHS crisis are a normalised feature of public discourse in Britain. Entrenched funding, staffing and infrastructure shortages intersect within narrations of this ‘critical’ crisis (Darzi, 2024; Herrick, 2025). Yet viewing healthcare solely through the lens of cuts can occlude analysis of the shifting institutional arrangements austerity brings: private sector interests increasingly enmeshed in care delivery; technological investments; the promotion of community care models (NHS England, 2025). Considering Herrick’s (2025) spotlighting of the NHS as a vital concern for critical geographers, we ask what role ethnographic and engaged research can play in elucidating situated experiences of institutional reconfigurations.

 This session builds on important analyses of discriminatory patterns of work and recruitment (Batnitzky & McDowell, 2011; Irons, 2024), as well as calls from feminist, trans geographers and disability scholars as to the pernicious, potentially violent logics enacted through health system thresholds and dismissals (Bailey et al., 2020; Kavanagh et al., 2021; Todd, 2023; Dhairyawan, 2024). We draw too on analyses which examine prolonged experiences of waithood, which characterise care in a ‘holding pattern’ (Kiely 2021, p. 717), alongside research attending to gatekeeping and threshold practices that compel care-seekers to articulate deservingness (Kiely & Warnock, 2022; Muir & Hawking, 2024).

 We are especially interested in research which charts– through ethnography, engaged practices or biographies– how people’s experiences of the health system are entangled with wider quests for good care, both within and beyond it. Across such contexts, this session will explore how resilience is made as an affective and ethical demand differentially imposed on people and communities. Attending to the uneven conditions under which refusal and non-use of care, for instance, become possible foregrounds how unjust systems distribute not only harm and protection, but also the very possibility of contestation (Benjamin, 2016).

papers.title: Possibilities for storytelling as transgressive care in and beyond systems of medico-legal abortion provision
PrEP as Socio-Medical-Spatial Infrastructure: Delivery and Access Pathways
Medication Access Networks: A Research Agenda for Informal Pharmaceutical Flows
Suturing Care: The transnational pursuit of elective weight loss surgery
Under Pressure: Distress, Obligation and Care in Post-Welfare UK

sessions.title: Cartooneering your research
sessions.description:
An opportunity to learn and try out a simple but powerful method for thinking creatively about your research. No drawing skills required – just a willingness to give it a go.

 In this workshop, participants will be invited to sketch out their research in cartoon format, aiming to create short stories from their research during the session. The workshop is an interactive opportunity to practice ‘cartooneering’ your research in a fun and engaging manner. There will also be opportunity to reflect on how cartooneering could be used as a tool for planning your research, or for telling the story of your research to a range of audiences. Please bring your own pen/pencil if possible.

sessions.title: NGOs and Sustainable Fashion Transitions: Challenges, Strategies and Success Stories (1)
sessions.description:
It is well-established that the fashion industry is one of the most environmentally and socially unsustainable industries in the world (Bick et al., 2018; Niinimäki et al., 2020; United Nations Environment Programme, 2023). Despite these impacts being well known, the industry is resistant to change and, with the acceleration of ultra-fast fashion, may be getting worse. While there is an urgent need for change, the question remains: how will this occur?

 Transforming the industry requires intervention from multiple levers concurrently, including government legislation, consumer shopping behaviours, manufacturing processes, and brands’ decision making. Non-governmental organisations (NGOs) are frequently positioned as key agents of change due to their ability to operate across scales and engage diverse actors. However this reliance on NGOs warrants critical examination, particularly regarding their capacity, legitimacy, accountability, and potential co-optation within existing power structures. 

 This session is interested in understanding the strategies and role of NGOs influencing the pro-socio-environmental behaviours that value chain actors (i.e. raw materials producers, manufacturers, brands, consumers, recyclers) are pursuing, including challenges and enablers. For the purpose of this session, NGOs are defined as organisations of any size and prominence, involved in activities which push for positive environmental and social change in the fashion industry such as setting certifications and standards, developing industry schemes, campaigning, and reporting on industry activities. Examples include Textile Exchange, Fashion Revolution, Oeko-Tex, Zero Discharge of Hazardous Chemicals (ZDHC), Oxfam, Clean Clothes Campaign, Fair Wear Foundation, Laudes Foundation, Sustainable Apparel Coalition, and Or Foundation. 

 In this session, the fashion industry is used as a lens to analyse ethical labour and production, product design and materials, recycling and disposal, and over-consumption. This session brings together research from different contexts to critically examine how NGOs influence socially and environmentally sustainable practices across fashion value chains, including limitations, potentially unintended consequences, and success stories to inspire across geographies. Particular attention is given to how NGOs navigate tensions between collaboration and confrontation, voluntary standards and regulatory change, and incremental improvements versus systemic industry transformation.

papers.title: The Work of Hybrid Intermediation in Sustainable Fashion Transitions: NGOs Between System Coordination and Cultural Value Construction
Co-creation as method to develop more ambitious climate goals for a just transition in fashion
Governing Hazardous Chemicals in the Australian Fashion Industry: The Role of NGOs
affiliations: Institute for Sustainable Futures, University of Technology Sydney

sessions.title: Working with remainders: traces and spectrality in justice-making processes (2): epistemologies
sessions.description:
Session co-convenors: Annaclaudia Martini (University of Bologna) and Martina Tazzioli (University of Bologna)

 The contemporary cultural moment is characterized by a renewed interest towards evidence, and evidence-based epistemologies and methodologies. This concern has also permeated geographical debates, partly as a result of impact-driven analyses that declare the aim of recognising inequalities and fostering justice-making processes. This session challenges such a quest for evidence as justice seeking instrument and seeks to shift attention towards traces and spectrality as analytical lenses for re-thinking geographies and politics of justice.

 This session interrogates how the analytical lens of traces, haunting and spectres can be effectively used in geography for articulating critical knowledge in the current cultural and geopolitical conjuncture. We aim to explore how traces and spectrality might be productively mobilised as analytics or as methods on different phenomena such as wars, migrations, natural disasters and ecological loss, heritage and memory, collective struggles and mobilizations, taking traces and spectres seriously in their heterogeneity and incompleteness. In focusing on hauntings (Derrida, 1994) and on traces (Ginzburg, 2012), we complicate historical narratives implicitly dependent on the linear temporalities of progress, and justice-seeking processes exclusively grounded on the production of evidence. Such perspective shift exposes individuals' and collectives' fragmented experiences of memory, justice, the impact of colonial legacies on our present, and intergenerational trauma.

 We invite contributions that engage with traces and/or spectrality from a theoretical, methodological or empirical point of view, and that interrogate how these can be used for bringing into focus concealed and naturalised inequalities, and for envisioning justice-seeking processes in different fields. Such approach can expand and move beyond current epistemologies on traces (Didi-Huberman, 2017; McGeachan, 2018; Ogborn, 2005) and hauntology and spectrality (Derrida, 1994; Maddern & Adey, McCormack, 2010), interrogating how these not only allow us to enable novel understandings of past events and violations, but also unfold and activate research pathways for envisioning justice-making processes.

 References 

 Derrida, J. (1994). Specters of Marx: The State of the Debt, the Work of Mourning and the New International . London: Routledge.

 Didi-Huberman, G. (2017). Bark . MIT Press.

 Ginzburg, C. (2012). Threads and traces: True false fictive . University of California Press.

 Maddern, J. F., & Adey, P. (2008). Spectro-geographies. cultural geographies , 15(3), 291-295.

 McCormack, D.P. (2010). Remotely sensing affective afterlives: The spectral geographies of material remains. Annals of the Association of American Geographers , 100(3), 640-654.

 McGeachan, C. (2018). Historical geography II: Traces remain. Progress in Human Geography , 42(1), 134-147.

 Ogborn, M. (2005). Atlantic geographies. Social & Cultural Geography , 6(3), 379-385

papers.title: Community, Haunting and the Rhythm of Mining Life
The body as moving archive: Migrant disappearances and performative searches across borders
What remains, what was never: ghosts of remembrance and forgetting in post-disaster Tohoku, Japan
The City Haunted by its Traumatic Past

sessions.title: Out of Office: Geographies of Leave
sessions.description:
While paid and unpaid working lives have long been a central concern within Geography (Bürkner and Lange 2020, Dutta 2016, Herod 1997, Strauss 2020), periods of absence from paid work remain comparatively under-examined. Yet leave is a critical site through which attachments to work are made, challenged, and reconfigured, particularly in neoliberal contexts where paid employment is often closely tied to identity, productivity, and self-worth (Jones 2025, Pettit 2024, Thieme 2017). Periods of leave often involve complex transitions into and out of working life, the renegotiation of temporal and spatial boundaries, and contested expectations around care, rest, recovery, and responsibility. 

 Geographers have explored work through lenses of labour, care, precarity, temporality, and everyday life (Bissell 2022, Carswell 2016, Marotta 2021, Richardson 2018, Worth 2016 ) . Building on this work, this session foregrounds leave as a distinctive analytical focus, attending to how time away from work is lived, (under)valued, and spatio-temporally organised. Drawing particularly on feminist and economic geographies, we seek contributions that attend to the uneven distribution of leave, the gendered and relational labour it entails, and its entanglement with labour markets, welfare regimes, and household economies. 

 This session explores the relationship between paid work and leave, broadly framed as periods of time away from paid employment. Leave is taken here to encompass a wide range of experiences, including maternity, paternity and adoption leave, sickness absence, bereavement leave, furlough, unemployment, retirement and un-retirement, and the spatio-temporal limitations placed around work and leave by migrant visas.

papers.title: Between Many Worlds: Precarious Positionalities Out of Office
Work, Identities, Transition: feminist geographies of parental leave
“Out of Rhythm” – An Exploration of the Disorientation Felt by Workers Transitioning Into and Out of Leave
“Leave not more than 180-days outside the UK”: An Autoethnography of Maternity Leave on a Skilled Worker Visa

sessions.title: Shadow Geographies of Care and Migration
sessions.description:
We are living through intensely un-caring times. And yet, perhaps in reaction to this, we are seeing increased interest in the concept and practice of care across disciplines. 

 In relation to migration, research on care has tended to focus on care work by migrants. However care as a ‘species activity’ (Fisher and Tronto 1990) extends beyond migrants working as carers. It involves a range of practices that create interconnections across different scales, concerned with caring about, caring for, caregiving and care receiving (Tronto 1998), and with questions of “how the world ought to be”, and “how we could imagine our shared world through radical ethics of care” (Raghuram 2016:514; Clark-Kazak 2022).

 While anti-immigrant activity tends to be highly visible, much care of people on the move, by both migrants themselves and by non-migrants, happens in the shadows, for reasons of safety or legality, or simply through quiet and everyday ways of countering hostile environments and policies. We are interested in these hidden or ‘shadow’ geographies of care, the often invisible, and sometimes extra-legal, forms of care developed by migrants themselves, and by individuals, groups and organisations that support them in various ways. 

 We are also interested in work which takes into account the ‘plurality of care knowledges’ (Sheringham 2025), challenging understandings of care, beyond western or European norms, or rethinking what care means for different people. We would like to hear from scholars researching in this area, but also activists, practitioners and artists who are working with/challenging ideas of care and migration.

papers.title: Negotiating, navigating and circulating care within pathways of inclusion in Scotland
MobiliseCare: Reframing the migration debate through building a ‘care-tography’
Quiet Resistances: Migrant Women’s Care Practices and Counter-Infrastructures in the City
affiliations: Urban Studies Foundation Research Fellow, Department of Geography, University of Manchester

papers.title: Sharing Food, Building Belonging: Food-centred Methodologies in Understanding Belonging and Care in Migrant Provision Spaces
Creating space, holding care: fostering solidarity and connection through creative encounters with refugees and people seeking asylum

sessions.title: Unconventional Geographies of Solidarity (2): Counter-Narratives
sessions.description:
In Multitude 
(2004), political philosophers Michael Hardt and Antonio Negri proposed the possibility of the “multitude” as a “living alternative” that grows from within the biopolitical production of shared knowledges, relationships, sentiments, and collaborations that facilitates globalized networks of capitalism. Unlike political units of “the people,” “the masses”, and “the classes” which signify the possibility of organized collective action based on commonality (of race, history, economic status etc.), the “multitude” is constituted through difference; an elusive, unpredictable and fundamentally ungovernable collective of singularities that may synchronize temporarily for specific goals or movements (cultural, social, political or otherwise), but without ever becoming fully unified and/or articulable. 

 Twenty years later, the world facilitated by globalized networks like social media is ever more complex than when they wrote this book. At the same time as people across the world have become intensely connected, the world has also become fragmented by political polarization, identity politics, and growing social isolation. Yet, the conceptualization of the "multitude" still resonates with geographical scholarship over the last two decades that has questioned conventional understandings of social movements and drawn attention to relational, affective, and improvised forms of solidarity produced through everyday practices and encounters (Amin, 2004; Bayat, 2013; Featherstone, 2008; Simone and Rao, 2012).
 This session invites theoretical, empirical and methodological interventions that explore unconventional, fragile and often overlooked geographies of solidarity that fall outside established frameworks of social movements, activism, or institutional politics, yet permeate everyday life in subtle and unexpected ways (Neimanis and Walker, 2014; Tsing, 2015).

papers.title: Platform workers’ solidarities: from everyday digital-urban encounters to an intersectional and transnational movement
Complicating and Complicated Solidarity with Chickens
Weird multitudes: Exploring ontological incommensurability through literature

sessions.title: Current and emerging research in transport (2): Innovations
sessions.description:
This session amplifies contributions from postgraduate students conducting research in any aspect of transport geography . This year, we have received abstracts across transport policy management, modelling for transport justice, and co-creation of transport futures.
 The session provide a relaxed, supportive atmosphere for postgraduates at any stage of their research to present work in progress and share ideas with one another. Nonetheless, all are welcome to attend. 

 The TGRG traditionally rewards a postgraduate prize for the best postgraduate presentation in any TGRG session at the RGS-IBG Conference. All postgraduate presentations in this session will automatically be eligible for the prize.

sessions.title: Reimagining Water Geographies (2)
sessions.description:
Water is routinely invoked as a universal, connective substance. Yet the ways it is narrated and conceptualised in geography can also reproduce exclusion, erasure, and spatial injustice.

 In this session, we will discuss how critical geography might turn the tide by thinking with water as relation, medium, and more-than-human material force, while staying attentive to uneven power, deterritorialization, and colonial legacies. Contributions will revolve around works that reconnect conversations often kept apart (island/ocean studies and freshwater scholarship; maritime geopolitics and hydro-feminist approaches), and that develop concepts and methods capable of tracking entanglements across scales and watery environments.

 Participants will rework key concepts such as planetary waters, islands, coasts, basins, and watery bodies. We will engage with “water places” where injustice is produced or contested (infrastructure, extraction, conservation, borders, mobility/immobility, militarization, tourism, and disasters), presenting methods for analyzing water as relation: fresh–salt continuums, land–sea interfaces, multi-sited approaches, mapping/visual methods, more-than-human methodologies, and more. Finally, by exploring the plural material dimensions of water, its essentiality in human-made environments, and its relationship with infrastructure and climate change adaptation, we will address compelling dilemmas in epistemic politics: how water categories travel, whom they privilege, and what alternative, decolonial geographies they enable.

papers.title: The Analytics and Politics of Indigenous Water Mobilities
Who Owns the Water? - Land Rush for water & policy response in Iceland
From scarcity to crime: a systematic literature review of water ‘theft’ definitions
Islands and their seas: Charting the politics of islandness in the archipelagic cities of Hong Kong and beyond
affiliations: The Chinese University of Hong Kong

papers.title: Rivers into Megawatts: Hydropower and the Remaking of Rivers in Nepal’s Arun Valley

sessions.title: The Gendered Labours of Everyday Ecological Transformation (2): Reproduction and maintenance
sessions.description:
Across households, neighbourhoods, community projects and eco-homes, women and people identifying with feminised or non-cis-masculine roles disproportionately drive everyday ecological transformation. Cooking from scratch, zero-waste practices, community food projects, care work, and sustaining eco-homes or eco-communities all form part of the daily labour of ecological change. Yet these practices often reproduce entrenched gendered divisions of labour, assigning women responsibility for food, waste, childcare, emotional wellbeing, and household sustainability. A central question animates this session: Is ecological transformation dependent on gendered inequalities? 

 Feminist environmental thinkers have long shown that care is structurally devalued in capitalist and patriarchal systems (Tronto, 1993; Federici, 2019). Ecofeminists argue that ecological transformation demands confronting the patriarchal hierarchies that render feminised care invisible (Gaard, 1997; Shiva, 2000; Salleh et al., 2017). Gender and sustainability scholarship likewise highlights gendered labour divisions as central to socio-ecological change (Cohen, 2017). At the same time, development and political-economy research shows how women’s labour is often instrumentalised within neoliberal narratives of market expansion and ecological solutionism (Moeller, 2018). Everyday socio-ecological change is therefore deeply entangled with the politics of social reproduction.

 In many grassroots sustainability initiatives, ecological aspirations materialise through the everyday reorganisation of domestic practices—community kitchens, shared gardens, composting, and collective food provisioning. These are sites where ‘matters of care’ (Puig de la Bellacasa, 2017) become ecological commitments enacted through often-shared daily labour. Research on co-housing and intentional communities shows how shared spaces are imagined to redistribute labour and collectivise sustainability efforts (Williams, 2005; Lietaert, 2010). Yet gendered responsibilities frequently re-emerge, even within settings explicitly committed to equality and ecological ethics (Eräranta et al., 2009; Jarvis, 2013; Vestbro & Horelli, 2012; Pickerill, 2015). Care is often collectivised without being politicised, reproducing hidden hierarchies of gender, labour and power (Bhatia, 2022; Gerner, 2025).

 These tensions—between empowerment and burden, ecological commitment and gendered expectation—sit at the centre of this session. We seek to foreground the geographies of ambivalence that shape who performs ecological labour, how this labour becomes visible or invisible, and how spaces of sustainability can both challenge and reproduce inequality. We invite empirical, theoretical, and methodological contributions engaging with these tensions and the gendered labours of ecological transformation.

papers.title: Critical Reading of Gender in the Climate Im/mobilities: A Case of Sundarbans Delta, India
affiliations: Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai

sessions.title: Reclaiming street space from cars (2): new instruments and experiences with the socio-ecological transformation of urban neighbourhoods and cities for better liveability and more sustainable transport
sessions.description:
In recent years many cities worldwide have embarked on a transformation of the transport system to improve living conditions and reduce negative aspects of transport. Among other issues, central objectives of these transformations are to use urban space for non-car purposes to improve liveability and promote the use of non-motorized or shared modes of transport. Various cities have promoted concepts like the 15-min-city, superblocs or improved cycling and walking infrastructures. To implement these concepts, car spaces (parking, streets) need to be re-used for other purposes. Despite questionable overall effects of these new instruments on a city-wide level, the relevance and emergence of these types of policies and related instruments for urban transition processes are clear.

 The objective of this session is to assemble research evaluating and assessing transport, social, economic or liveability aspects of instruments and tools employed for transforming urban areas. The instruments might involve restructuring the built environment (including transport infrastructures), communication and participation strategies or relevant financial instruments. We also seek research addressing the availability and redistribution of parking spaces by financial or other instruments or by policy packages combining the instruments mentioned. Preferably, we are seeking empirical contributions using sound theoretical reflections on the effects and changes initiated by relevant policy instruments. Real world laboratories or other means of transdisciplinary research are highly encouraged to present their findings.

sessions.title: Burning Inequalities: Critical Geographies of Fire (2)
sessions.description:
Where and why fire impacts people and places is deeply intertwined with many facets of inequality and injustice. This session aims to contribute to discussions around the role of geographical perspectives in conceptualising and researching fire inequality.

 The devastating fires of Grenfell Tower in 2017 and more recently at Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong in 2025 serve as stark reminders that we must understand and redress inequalities that have shaped exposure to fire. Fires like Grenfell do not exist in a vacuum, instead they can expose and uncover inequalities and injustice embedded within our societies (Marmot, 2020). Scholars who have explored the contexts surrounding Grenfell in particular, have asserted the importance of foregrounding the role of racial logics of neoliberal urban governance (Danewid, 2020), geographies of injustice and inequality (MacLeod, 2018) and, institutional negligence and evasion of accountability (Özbilgin, Erbil and Valsecchi, 2026) in fomenting fire inequalities.

 Furthermore, as global warming intensifies wildfires, the devastating consequences of such disasters continue to be disproportionately felt by underserved, minority and poor communities (Thomas, 2024). Research has highlighted that these impacts follow the contours of existing economic, social and racial inequalities as a result of insufficient resources, exclusion from risk preparedness (Baker et al. , 2024) but also the lack of resources to rebuild after a wildfire (Davies et al. , 2018). This is not by any means an exhaustive list, but speaks to the various factors that shape vulnerability to fire, and the injustices that follow a fire incident.

 Geographical perspectives and geographical imaginaries can be harnessed to explore the spatiality of these overlapping and intersecting inequalities, and the mechanisms through which they emerge and are sustained. For example, how inequalities are produced and shaped at different scales and sites, from government decision making to geographies of the home. Critical perspectives on disasters have argued that they are not natural phenomena , therefore how can geographers contribute knowledge around the transformation of fire into a human disaster?

sessions.title: Climate Apartheid: meanings, contexts, politics, legitimacy (2)
sessions.description:
The lexicon of ‘justice’ is increasingly central to climate policy debates and interventions, framed through discourses such as climate, environmental or energy justice, and more broadly the ‘just transition’. These frameworks recognise that climate change disproportionately affects marginalised groups who bear little responsibility for emissions and lack influence in political negotiations (e.g. Schlosberg and Collins, 2014; Sultana, 2022). The scope of climate injustice has since expanded, encompassing not only the inequalities of climate change impacts , but also the ways in which climate mitigation and adaptation strategies also contribute to unequal processes and outcomes (e.g. Rice et al., 2022). This is increasingly referred to as climate apartheid. Initially deployed by activists as a provocative term to secure attention in global policy circuits (e.g. Bond 2016), the concepts of eco/climate apartheid are currently being developed by critical scholars as a framework to explain and critique how climate action by privileged individuals and groups (re)produces injustice (e.g. Tuana, 2019; Long et al., 2020; Rice et al., 2022; Long, 2024a, 2024b; Heron, 2024).

 The roots of the term are largely traced to South African Archbishop Desmond Tutu, who in 2008 introduced the idea of “adaptation apartheid” to highlight his concerns that “adaptation is becoming a euphemism for social injustice on a global scale” in which wealthy countries protect themselves from climate change impacts via infrastructure technology investments unavailable to poorer counterparts (UN, 2008:166). A decade later, Philip Alston warned the UN Human Rights Council that “we risk a ‘climate apartheid’ scenario where the wealthy pay to escape overheating, hunger and conflict while the rest of the world is left to suffer” (UN, 2019). While initially the concept lacked coherence, recent scholarship has produced a comprehensive definition of climate apartheid as “an emerging system of discrimination, segregation, and violence based on various axes of oppression and privilege (race, class, gender, sexuality) that is produced by the material effects of climate change, but also many responses to the crisis” (Rice et al, 2001:627). This definition also introduces the analytical binaries of climate privileged and climate precarious, to capture how climate action affords protection and benefits to some individuals and groups, whilst generating heightened vulnerability and exclusion for others. Nonetheless, there remain a wide range of uses and interpretations in circulation, applied across disciplines and case study contexts.

 Given the focus of the RGS/IBG2026 conference on exploring geographies of inequalities and just places, alongside the current wave of scholarship on climate apartheid, it seems an appropriate place and time to encourage further debate. As the concept and research field of climate apartheid is relatively new, we envisage this session as a platform for scholars whose research resonates with the concept of eco/climate apartheid and who are interested in engaging in debates related to the concepts’ meaning and development, politics and legitimacy, policy implications, as well as critical reflections on how climate apartheid functions ‘in practice’. Primarily we conceive this as a space to interrogate how the concept of climate apartheid is emerging conceptually and empirically.

papers.title: ‘This explosive planetary drama’: Examining the representational politics of climate mobilities in popular science books through the lens of climate apartheid
Shifting Up, Scaling Diverse: Governing Plural Affluent Ways of Life for Urban Just Transitions
Climate apartheid at the borders: exploring the securitisation of ‘climate migration’
The mind of climate apartheid

sessions.title: Urban inequalities and transformative interventions (2)
sessions.description: Cities are increasingly paradoxical spaces, concurrently representing vast wealth concentration and deeply embedded socio‑economic and spatial disparities. Set within a context of intensifying global crises – from climate change to financial precarity – traditional geographical perspectives are being encouraged to extend diagnosis to critically examine transformative interventions that can reshape urban policy and practice. This session brings together papers that not only identify urban injustices but also critically examine effective solutions to those injustices, thereby challenging the status quo. The core challenge addressed across the papers is how interventions translate from academic critique into tangible political or planning initiatives, thereby ‘shaping agendas’. Many of the papers draw innovative methodologies (for example, comparative longitudinal analysis, participatory action research, and advanced spatial modelling) to critically assess the effectiveness and scalability of these interventions. Ultimately, these papers collectively address the question: How can geographical research on inequality, through rigorous and forward‑thinking analysis, serve as a force for radical urban change, shaping legislative agendas and governance toward a more just urban future?

sessions.title: Geographies of Justice: mapping legal, carceral and civil societal notions of an elusive concept
sessions.description:
Justice is inextricably linked to all our lives. It is an undercurrent that affects our experiences in myriad of ways from personal interactions to our understanding of legal systems as well as global politics. At the same time today's interconnected world is increasingly affected by multiple crises: an interplay of intertwined and mutually reinforcing challenges – including climate change, economic uncertainty, war, displacement and the erosion of democratic institutions. This is accompanied by a changing understanding of justice and a close interconnection and centralisation of these crises in the field of justice. At the heart of these matters lies a philosophical question of how justice is interpreted, including whether justice is a universal human condition or instead a more localised form of solidarity (Jeffrey 2013). In relation to a moral geography on how we ought to or want to live, what constraints we experience and how these are negotiated (Smith 1998, 2000; Proctor 1998), justice sits alongside other conceptual ideas like trust and confidence. 

 One such area in which questions of justice converge is the legal domain (Blomley 1994; Delaney 2010): in court many people are confronted with justice in particularly personal but also wider institutional ways in the sense that they are seeking justice, delivering justice or find themselves at the receiving end (Schliehe & Jeffrey 2023). For the ones who are convicted and find themselves in the justice system, be it in prison or on probation often see themselves confronted not only with their own emotional geography of justice but with a changing sense of trust in the justice system, questioning the legitimacy of institutions and one’s own sense of trust in the world at large. It is at this intersection of legal and carceral geographies, that justice often takes a sharper form (Schliehe & Jeffrey 2023). These questions in turn also affect society at large and our sense of (mis-)trust in the state or level of confidence in its institutions.
 This session is addressing questions and concerns in fields ranging from geographies of moral philosophy and its bearing on our understandings of justice to emotional-geographical and legal-geographical lenses and mechanisms used to address injustice and resolve conflict.

papers.title: Enduring collectively: Spatialities of transformative justice in German (post-)autonomous movement contexts
Right to be included in the community: intersections of disability rights and geographies of justice
Crafting Justice: How Girls Persist to Resist Violence through Community Zine-making
On spatial justice: loitering under Public Space Protection Orders

sessions.title: Digital participation: a route to reducing inequalities?
sessions.description:
This panel critically explores the extent to which digital participation can serve as a route to reducing inequalities in place-making and knowledge production. 

 The UK’s recently published Industrial Strategy Plans (DTI, 2026) emphasise that digital technology and ‘createch’ approaches will be at the forefront of research and investment in coming decades. At the same time, UKRI reflect the ‘participatory turn’ across their funding schemes, emphasising the importance of public involvement through co-production and citizen science. Digital tools are increasingly used to inform policy, planning, and research, yet the challenge remains of how to do digital participation without recreating existing inequalities (British Academy, 2022; Fung, 2025; Maming et al, 2025; Robinson et al, 2015).

 It is well established that participatory methods allow people to represent their own realities rather than being spoken for (for example Aczel et al 2023; Halstead, 2025; Peimani and Kalamipour, 2025) and the hope as been that digital iterations of these approaches can capture lived, experiential, and place-based knowledge  (Maming et al, 2025; Stempeck, 2025). Yet our experiences as researchers using digital participatory methods suggests that the affordances of digital participation are finely balanced against its limitations.

 We welcome short papers, creative/interactive presentations exploring these issues. We offer this panel as an opportunity for researchers, practitioners and research participants to reflect on (amongst other things):

 - Ways that digitised participatory approaches are shaped by and can counteract intersecting inequalities
- Emerging, unexpected or hard-to-handle insights from digital participatory projects
- The potential for digital participation to foreground “other” kinds of data about places and how this can be mobilised to create just places

 Contact k.smith7@hull.ac.uk or sarah.gambell@glasgow.ac.uk for submissions. 

 References

 Aczel, M., Makuch, K.E.(2023) Climate change, young people, and the IPCC: The role of citizen science. Elementa: Science of the Anthropocene; 11 (1): 00029. doi: https://doi.org/10.1525/elementa.2022.00029
 British Academy (2022), Understanding digital poverty and inequality in the UK, http://doi.org/10.5871/digital-society/understanding-digital-poverty 

 Fung, A. (2015), Putting the Public Back into Governance: The Challenges of Citizen Participation and Its Future. Public Administration Review, 75: 513-522. https://doi.org/10.1111/puar.12361 

 Halstead, F., Parsons, K. J., Vo, T., Lloyd Williams, A., Jones, L., Le, H., Nguyen, A., Hackney, C., Parsons, D. (2025). Place-based and intergenerational storytelling for youth climate action: moving beyond participation and towards momentum. Children’s Geographies, 1–8. https://doi.org/10.1080/14733285.2025.2519027

 Maming, J. B., Escarilla, C., Taño-an, E. L., & Fare, N. (2025). Technology-enabled public participation: opportunities and challenges of participatory research. Psychology and Education: A Multidisciplinary Journal, 39(6), 723–731. https://doi.org/10.70838/pemj.390602

sessions.title: Gender and Feminist Geographies Research Group (GFGRG) Walking Tour- Designed to be forgotten: women in South Kensington
sessions.description:
Designed to be forgotten: women in South Kensington Join Iya London for a thought-provoking walking tour exploring the hidden contributions of Victorian women to the architecture and design of South Kensington.

 Discover how they helped shape this renowned arts and science district—and why their stories remain largely untold.

 You'll also explore the differing representations of men and women in the area’s public spaces. This walk will change the way you see South Kensington!

 What You’ll See

 We'll focus on the Victoria and Albert Museum, the Royal School of Mines, the Memorial to the 1851 Great Exhibition, the Royal Albert Hall and the Albert Memorial.

 About Your Guide

 Joanna Oyediran is a qualified Open City guide. She regularly leads walks round White City for Open City as part of their year-round tours programme. Her other clients have included the Royal College of Art, the LSE, the Great Exhibition Road Festival, and Planning Aid for London.

 Joanna is the founder of Iya London, which unearths and shares London’s overlooked stories, past and present.
 Meeting Point
 Outside the main entrance of the RGS
 Booking a place
 This will be organised nearer the time

 Timetabling note: Joanna is available for the slot on Thursday 3 rd September- 11.10am-12.50pm

sessions.title: Chair's event: Challenging racism and racial inequalities
sessions.description:
This panel session focuses on the important work being done in geography and related fields to tackle racism and to challenge racial inequalities.

 Drawing on different international examples – and speaking from diverse positions - the panel focuses on how research is exposing the harm being done by racism in different geographical contexts, the ways in which researchers and their collaborators are working to resist this, and how different approaches to antiracism feature within this work.

 Featuring Professor Laura Pulido (London School of Economics and Political Science), Professor Claire Hancock (Paris), Dr James Carr (University of Limerick), Dr Rachel Sharples (University of Western Sydney), Dr Margaret Byron (University of Leicester), and chaired by Dr Alanna Kamp (University of Western Sydney).

sessions.title: Clearing roadblocks: a workshop for interdisciplinary collaboration in transport geography
sessions.description:
This workshop is primarily designed for early careers researchers and postgraduate students .
 At the TGRG, we recognise that transport geography is one of the most interdisciplinary strands of research. Our committee is composed of engineers, qualitative researchers, social theorists, planners and data scientists, matching the breadth of the sector itself. However, we recognise a degree of stigma exists across this ‘broad church’, caricatured as qualitative researchers scorning what they see as pure positivism, or quantitative researchers deriding what they see as work with insufficiently big data. The truth, as always, is a shade of grey, a missed opportunity, an inequality that stands to be corrected.
 Please join us for this TGRG sponsored workshop, where we will aim to b ring together researchers across the discipline , encouraging us to see the value in our different methodological and theoretical approaches, and hopefully working to make transportation research more diverse and more accessible.
 After all, if one sub-discipline is going to be talking about research accessibility - surely it should be the transport geographers.
 Feel free to bring your lunch along to this session.

sessions.title: Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa Keynote Lecture
sessions.description:
Geographies of the Middle East and North Africa research group are honoured to have Dr Munira Khayyat as the inaugural keynote speaker for the research group. Khayyat is an anthropologist focused on life in war, intimate genealogies of empire, and theory from the South. Her first book, A Landscape of War: Ecologies of Resistance and Survival in South Lebanon (University of California Press 2022) examines resistant ecologies in a world of perennial warfare. Drawing on long-term fieldwork in frontline villages along Lebanon’s southern border with Israel, she examines war not only as a place of death and destruction, but also necessarily, as an environment of living.

 Khayyat is currently working on a second book that fleshes out the complex heart of empire in Saudi Arabia. Heart of Black Gold draws on a personal archive meticulously created by her maternal grandfather, who was among the first Arabian employees of ARAMCO, the Arab American Oil Company. How has oil — its extractive, shiny infrastructures, camps, big men, politics and corporations, its global ecologies — shaped lived environments? Insisting on a feminist and multidisciplinary rearranging of the archive, the book inhabits history-in-the-making as it unfolds in domestic scenes, lived quarters, the affective terrains of oil.
 Title and Abstract TBC

papers.title: The Fire Will Never Cease: Defiant Ecologies in South Lebanon

sessions.title: Film screening and discussion: Saodat: Another History of the Soviet Empire (2026)
sessions.description: This Author meets Critics session centres on the new 21-minute mixed-media film Saodat: Another History of the Soviet Empire , written and directed by feminist geographers and sociologists Negar E. Behzadi and Lucia Direnberger, with animation direction by Kate Jessop. Combining filmed interviews, archival footage and photography, and animation, the film tells the story of Saodat, a woman from Tajikistan whose social and professional mobility was supported by the Soviet system in its borderlands. Through Saodat’s life and intergenerational memories, the co-authored film offers an embodied and situated history of Soviet colonialism in Central Asia. It traces how her life was intertwined with broader geopolitical transformations while also revealing the forms of ‘embodied violence’ embedded in the Soviet colonial project. The film foregrounds how Saodat navigated and embraced norms of Soviet modernity as a pathway to advancement, situating her experience within the wider history of empire. Building on this work, Behzadi and Direnberger have also developed a research article ‘A Feminist Geopolitics of Soviet Colonialism’, which draws on Saodat’s story to explore the production of ‘borderland bodies’. The session will include a screening of the film followed by a discussion with three invited scholars. Critics will explore how the filmic project challenges epistemological and ontological inequalities in histories and geographies of colonialism, particularly the marginalisation of Soviet colonialism in post- and decolonial debates. They will also consider the role of feminist situated histories and visual methodologies in decolonising geographical knowledge and contributing to the making of more just places through a praxis of visibilisation and reparation.

sessions.title: Feminist Geographies of Heat, Health and Thermal Inequality (1)
sessions.description:
Feminist Geographies of Heat, Health and Thermal Inequality 

 Climate change is increasingly recognised as a defining health challenge of our time, yet its harms are profoundly uneven. Extreme and chronic heat, in particular, is often framed through biomedical thresholds and behavioural risk models, obscuring how exposure, endurance and recovery are structured through gendered labour, racialised housing markets, care infrastructures and political economy. This session intervenes by bringing feminist geography into direct conversation with critical health geography to reposition heat as a form of slow violence, an embodied, relational and deeply political determinant of health.

 Building on feminist care ethics (Tronto), intersectionality (Crenshaw), and climate justice scholarship, we conceptualise heat as a form of thermal violence: a slow, cumulative force that reorganises daily rhythms, constrains rest and hydration, intensifies unpaid care work, exacerbates reproductive and mental health vulnerabilities, and exposes workers in precarious sectors to disproportionate risk. Who is able to cool, rest and recover? Whose suffering is rendered visible in heat metrics, and whose remains normalised?

 We invite theoretical, methodological and empirical contributions from across the Global South and North that engage with themes such as:

 - Gendered and intersectional heat-health inequalities

 - Cooling infrastructures, energy poverty and thermal justice

 - Labour, care and everyday endurance under chronic heat

 - Housing, informality, mobility and public space in warming cities

 - Feminist political ecology and climate-health governance

 - Innovative feminist and mixed methods for researching heat-health

 - Mapping heat hot spots and vulnerability using geospatial approaches

 These topics are illustrative. We encourage any contributions that critically engage the nexus of gender, health, and environmental change. 

 The session will run in a mixed format combining paper presentations with a facilitated discussion designed to surface shared questions and future directions. We warmly encourage Early Career Researchers and scholars working across disciplines to submit.

 Submission Details: Given the short timeline, abstracts (max 250 words) are requested promptly by 4 th March 2026 . We welcome work in progress, conceptual interventions and empirically grounded studies. We will review submissions on a rolling basis due to the tight deadline. Accepted presenters will be notified quickly in order to meet the RGS-IBG programme deadline of 6 March 2026.

 Beyond the conference , this session aims to open space for an ongoing collaborative conversation. Participants will be invited to join a developing feminist heat-health reading group to continue dialogue and explore where this agenda might lead.

 Contact: If you are interested in contributing, please get in touch with session organisers: Dr Mildred O. Ajebon ( m.o.ajebon@durham.ac.uk) and Dr Caitlin Robinson ( caitlin.robinson@bristol.ac.uk ).

 Let’s use this moment to sharpen the debate and build something lasting.

Living through thermal injustice: Longitudinal insights into housing and energy vulnerability
Embodying Thermal Inequality: Feminist Geographies of Chronic Heat in Lagos

sessions.title: Reimagining Health, Wellbeing and Inclusion: Responsible Food Geography Research and Innovation
sessions.description:
This session aims to examine how food systems research engages with health, wellbeing, inclusion, and equitable access to food as they are shaped by intersecting inequalities, including racialised labour relations, gendered food work, and uneven access to healthy and culturally appropriate food. Food geography as an emerging research area, professional practice, and agri-food movements addresses questions of multiple justice, inequality, sustainability, identity, and multispecies relations (Pradhan and Pant, 2026). As the field grows, so does the need for research, policy, and practices that are co-designed, co-created, ethically grounded, inclusive, and attentive to power and politics (Pant, 2019; Shaw et al., 2024).

 The session invites participants to present research results while critically examining their own positionality, reflecting on the ethical dimensions of their methods, and considering how food geography research can support more just and sustainable food system futures.

 While we are open to novel ideas, the following are indicative topics participants may consider.

 - Participatory and community‑led research, co-design, and co-production addressing the triple burden of malnutrition (under/over nutrition, nutritional deficiency)
- Decolonial and anti‑oppressive methodologies in food systems research
- Multispecies and more‑than‑human food geographies
- Food justice, food security, sovereignty, and labour politics
- Reflexivity, positionality, and responsible food systems research and innovation

 References 

 Pant, L.P. (2019). Responsible innovation through conscious contestation at the interface of agricultural science, policy, and civil society . Agriculture and Human Values 36, 183–197. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10460-019-09909-2

 Pant et al. (2025). A global scoping review of alternative food movements calls for food justice and justice beyond individual humans. Global Food Security, 46, p.100877. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.gfs.2025.100877

 Pradhan, P. and Pant, L. P., Eds. (2026). The Elgar Companion on Food System Transformation for Sustainable Development . Edward Elgar, Cheltenham, UK

sessions.title: “Are you doing your work?” Care-full methods and ethics of care in (and beyond) the field (1)
sessions.description:
Care is now firmly on the agenda in human geography. Yet too often, it appears as a topic to be studied, rather than an ethic that is actively practiced (Middleton & Samanani, 2021). When Audre Lorde asked the question, “Are you doing your work?” She was referring to the introspection and deeper conversations that need to be had to confront how structures of power shape embodied experiences. The understanding of care not simply as a social practice or as a concept but as an ethic has been taken up by Lawson (2007, 2009), who argues programmatically for geographers to take up a “critical care ethics for our epistemological, ontological, methodological, and daily life practices (as professionals and citizens)” (2007, p. 3). Caring is hard work. As an ethic, it forces us to become entangled and complicates our relationships in the field: with communities, interlocutors/friends, research assistants, and ourselves. Sometimes it is discomfiting. Omer Aijazi (2024, p. 178) asks, “what does thinking from the heart offer? What if we see the world this way?” In this panel, we examine how treating care as both an ethic and a form of relational labour reshapes the everyday work of research and fieldwork.

 This session asks a simple, uncomfortable, hopeful, and necessary set of questions: Are we doing our work with care? What is an ethics of care, and how might it show up in the everyday practices of fieldwork, writing, and collaboration? What might it enable (or constrict) when we foreground an ethics of care in our methodological and personal approaches? When does care become a method? Can it disrupt ‘extractivist’ models of research? What do researchers refuse in the name of care? And what refusals are rendered impossible? What happens when care is betrayed? We invite papers that examine the practical, emotional, and moral consequences of care in the field and beyond, focusing on the relationships through which care is enacted: with research assistants, interlocutors/friends, family, community members, and other collaborators involved in the research process. We highly encourage contributions from scholars working in or with contexts in the Majority World and from precarious academic contexts. Particular attention will be paid to works grounded in feminist, decolonial, queer, anti-caste, Southern, and indigenous approaches. 

 This session is conceived as the foundation for a special issue on care-full methodologies . Contributors will be invited to develop their papers into a collective publication that advances practical, ethical, and critical approaches to conducting research with care.

sessions.title: Big Tech, the State and Emerging Governance Assemblages (1)
sessions.description:
Recent scholarship highlights the growing influence of technology corporations in shaping political, social, and state practices. Big Tech platforms and AI technologies are increasingly entangled with state power, authoritarian logics, and new forms of governance, raising fundamental questions about political authority, public accountability, and democratic life (Jarret et al, 2025). The concept of “Big Tech authoritarianism” (Akbari 2025) foregrounds how tech giants not only mediate information but also align with or reproduce authoritarian logics of power, surveillance, and control. Likewise, emerging frameworks of AI authoritarianism (Dekeyser et al. 2025) argue that key features of AI-such as scalability, algorithmic incorporation of difference, and techno-determinist ideologies - actively reshape political governance and authoritarian dynamics across geopolitical contexts. 

 This session responds to a growing need to critically interrogate the political thrust of technological power and the growing political influence of tech capital at a time when digital platforms and algorithmic systems are increasingly central to shaping state practices, public discourse, citizen engagement, and the possibilities for resistance. Rather than treating “the digital” as a neutral domain, we invite geopolitically informed analyses that situate technology in the relational dynamics of power, space, and governance. This aims to build on existing scholarship theorizing data power (Thatcher & Dalton, 2021), technofascism (McElroy 2025; Coeckelbergh, 2026), or digital colonialism (Kwet, 2019), and aligns with recent calls to rethink governance frameworks in light of AI’s active modulation of political processes and the political synergies between Big Tech and state actors. 

 This session explores how Big Tech and AI are implicated in reconfiguring the practices, techniques, and imaginaries of governance. We seek contributions that situate these developments within broader socio-spatial processes, attending to power, resistance, and the uneven geographies of digital infrastructure and political authority (Akbari 2026 -upcoming). We invite submissions to this paper session on issues related to:

 - Big Tech, political power, and emerging forms of governance
- AI, platforms, and the reconfiguration of state authority
- Uneven geographies of digital power
- Practices and logics of digital authoritarianism
- Democratic frictions and political contestation in digital governance
- Manifestations of Big Tech or AI authoritarianism across distinct spheres of governance

sessions.title: Orbiting and Hovering: Critical Remote Sensing and Volumetric Regimes
sessions.description:
The use of remote sensing technologies, including drones and earth observation sensors, is increasingly becoming part of how we map, monitor, track, measure and regulate various environmental and social challenges. Accompanying this growing relevance of remote sensing have been calls for work critiquing the epistemological foundations and the use of remote sensing technologies (Kroth 2025; Lappe et al. 2025). Critical remote sensing (CRS), as such an approach is called, accounts for the politics of sensing practices and sensing infrastructures. CRS attends to how both shape inequalities and injustices and how they remote sensing can be used to counter them. A growing number of scholars appeal to critical remote sensing by questioning the politics and everyday implications of the volumetric regimes that earth observation and other remote sensing techniques produce (Goodchild et al. 2022). Situated at the crossroads of political geography and many other fields including (urban) security studies (Klauser 2021), environmental modelling (Braun 2024), land use management (Paris 2025), biodiversity research (Milner et al. 2023) and many more, critical remote sensing is currently marked by disparate networks scattered across numerous domains.

 This RGS-IBG session brings together critical remote sensing scholars to proactively collaborate and network. The session features scholars whose work examines and critically evaluates the use of remote sensing data and infrastructures. Questions that papers link to include, for example:

 - What role does remote sensing play in amplifying and silencing different knowledges?

 - What epistemological foundations is remote sensing built on and what is missing?

 - How does remote sensing contribute to processes of modulating environments?

 - How can we study and make sense of volumetric regimes that link technologies in earth's orbit to local practices of sense making?

 - How does remote sensing play into ongoing crisis narratives and what can we learn from this about sustainability and digital transitions?

 - What would practices of counter-remote sensing look like and how could they be realized?

 - What are the geopolitical dimensions and implications of remote sensing?

 - What role do remote sensing technologies play in creating new scientific ties between public, private and academic partners?

 References: 

 Braun, A. C. (2024). More accurate less meaningful?  Progress in Physical Geography: Earth and Environment , 48 (3), 343–367. https://doi.org/10.1177/03091333241248055

sessions.title: Just Transitions in and beyond China (2)
sessions.description:
The global climate and biodiversity crises cannot be addressed without ‘just transitions’ – profound and far-reaching social reconfigurations that place well-being and social justice at their core. While just transition narratives resonate with activists, scholars, and policy makers across geographies, the concept has gained significantly less traction in China. This represents a critical oversight. First, China is the largest emitter of greenhouse gases (IEA, 2023) and leading nation in renewable electricity capacity additions at the global level (IEA, 2025). China’s increasing influence – economically, politically and ecologically – places the nation in a position to significantly shape global transition trajectories. Second, ongoing and planned environmental reform within China is already producing social impacts (e.g., through the phase-out of fossil fuel industries (Gong and Lewis, 2024) and local, uneven effects of environmental conservation (Wang and Lo, 2022), only likely to deepen as transitions unfold. Third, Chinese flows of investment shape patterns of economic development and socio-spatial configurations in locations around the world (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024), with highly uneven and uncertain outcomes. Finally, theoretical frameworks on ‘justice’ (social, environmental, political, and beyond) may require re-conceptualisation in relation to China’s socio-cultural and political context. Mainstream understandings remain shaped by liberal-democratic traditions, which may constrain examinations of transitions in China and China-connected spaces (Wang and Lo, 2021).

 Mapping and exploring how just transitions unfold within and beyond China is therefore an urgent research agenda. This panel engages with multiple dimensions of these dynamics, including but not limited to:

 - Social justice and socio-economic impacts of transition-related policies and reforms in China , including uneven outcomes of industrial restructuring, labour displacement, infrastructure investment, and urban-rural inequality. This includes studies on environmental justice dimensions of transitions, such as uneven impacts of environmental policies and programmes of ecological conservation.

 - Just transition outcomes of Chinese overseas investment , such as renewable energy projects, forestry/afforestation, and mining of critical minerals. We especially invite contributions that unpack the complex dynamics at play in global supply chains and the decoupling of impacts across locations of resource extraction and product deployment.

 - Chinese discourse of justice and just transition , including studies that explore contemporary policy narratives and public debates and their approaches to justice, as well as critiques of Anglophone conceptions of these debates. We also welcome work that engages wider and diverse forms of justice and just transition issues across China and China-connected spaces.
