sessions.title: Sustainable Future Making and its (Uneven) Geographies (3)
sessions.description:
As climate change gathers pace with distinctly uneven geographical impacts and a growing number of world regions find themselves confronted with the socio-economic vulnerabilities arising from intersecting planetary crises, calls gather pace to envisage and enact a distinct future that is climate sensitive, environmentally sustainable and socio-ecologically fairer and just. 

 In this context we have seen increasing attention being given to future oriented frameworks on matters of the environment, climate change and sustainability. This includes compelling or dystopic narratives of future strategically mobilised as desirable or inevitable (Arnall and Hilson, 2023; Kanarp et al.,2025; Peakock and Devine-Wright, 2025) and social action that builds on the symbolic and discursive power of spatial imaginaries to challenge low-carbon futures (Ryder et al., 2025). We also witness the triumph of anticipatory governance and planning (Anderson, 2010; Granjou et al., 2017) and of future oriented tools, including scenarios, deliberative mapping foresight, anticipatory gaming, visioning, experimentation, utilised in imagining sustainable futures (Bellamy et al., 2022; Candy and Dunagan, 2017; Hebinck et al., 2018; Muiderman et al., 2017; Oomen et al., 2021). Research also directs attention to more radical visions of the future, including prefigurative futuring practices (Burnett and Nunes, 2021; Jeffrey and Dyson, 2020) and counter-imaginaries emerging from activism (Matheney et al., 2024), alongside efforts to build abundant futures (Collard et al., 2015) and liberatory futures (Narayanan, 2023). As such, scholarship increasingly points to the emotions and affects involved in constraining, enabling, accelerating or delaying transitions to sustainable futures (Durnova et al., 2025; Martiskainen and Sovacool, 2021).

 This sensibility to futures, however, also encounter critical challenges in the form, for instance, of instances of resistance (Caris and Cao, 2024; Hilson and Arnall, 2024) or greenlash/backlash (Alter and Zürn, 2020; Wild et al., 2018); embedded forms of thinking and acting that can captivate otherwise possible futures (Haajer and Oomen, 2025); ongoing and unresolved tensions around exclusion and marginalisation that can spur feelings of futurelessness and stymie sustainable futures (Della Bosca and Gillespie, 2018; Lewis, 2024; Tutton, 2023); (ideological) power structures that perpetuate high carbon futures (Bedford et al., 2023) and geo-political risks, uncertainties, changes that can disrupt desirable futures (++). 

 It is within this urgent context that we propose this session as a space to think critically and collectively about this future turn on matters of environment, climate change and sustainability. We are interested in sharing and learning from a range of geographical contexts and sectoral domains and invite contributions that conceptually and empirically explore the notion of ‘sustainable future making’, sharpen the focus on its (uneven) geographies, and deepen our understanding of the generative potential of these processes. Possible topics include:

 - Imagining and Enacting Sustainable Futures
- Futures as Governing Devices, the Governance of Sustainable Futures
- Contested Sites and Negotiated Trajectories of Sustainable Futures
- Temporalities of Sustainable Future Making
- Co-producing sustainable futures: Methodological considerations and challenges
- (Un)making Sustainable Futures: Power and Inequalities
- Emotional geographies of sustainable future-making

sessions.title: Centering intersectional feminist approaches in mobility planning (2)
sessions.description:
The lack of free, safe, and accessible mobility limits access to opportunities and restricts full participation in urban life. In response, transforming transport systems towards more sustainable and just models has become urgent, guided by principles of equity and inclusion that underpin “just mobility transitions” (Oliveira Soares & Glaser, 2025; Schwanen, 2021; Sheller, 2018). Within this process, the gender equality perspective has been crucial in making visible the structural inequalities faced by women and other historically marginalised groups in urban planning and mobility. However, while some cities worldwide have implemented gender-sensitive urban mobility policies, the transformative potential of intersectional feminist planning as an integral part of just mobility transitions is yet to be realised. Gender-focused/gender-mainstreaming policies targeting women have been a key starting point for addressing existing inequalities (Jaimurzina et al., 2017; Sánchez de Madariaga, 2013). However, without an intersectional dimension and integral approach, such policies may have a narrow focus and limited impact, thus benefitting some groups more than others or be at risk of being watered down or even hijacked by populist agendas. Additionally, even within feminist movements and organisations pursuing mobility justice for various groups, intersectional thinking is sometimes not integrated, leading to exclusions, omissions and missed opportunities for demanding a broader change. A more radical revision of mobility planning regimes that intersectional feminist lens could inspire is yet to happen. This revision would challenge assumptions and values underpinning mobility planning beyond just making selected services and places ‘women friendly’. Instead, applying intersectional feminist lens to rethinking mobilities could inspire centering care, accessibility for all and combatting intersectional exclusions of multiple marginalized groups, linked to income, disability, ethnic and religious background, gender identity, sexual orientation, etc. (Castañeda et al., 2024; Jirón et al., 2022; Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025). 

 This challenge resonates with what epistemic justice scholarship in mobility planning has highlighted: the epistemic frameworks that shape urban and transport planning remain largely gender-insensitive (Nikolaeva & Shakthi, 2025), thus making it difficult to effectively integrate this approach. Conceptual frameworks, tools, and indicators that guide planning policies are still anchored in dominant ways of producing data, designing policy, and evaluating mobility systems. These technocratic and androcentric paradigms prioritise quantitative evidence, productive (work-related) mobility, and a conception of the human being as a rational, utility-maximising agent. In doing so, they marginalise relational, collective, and everyday experiences of mobility, care-related and recreational mobilities, and erase the diverse positionalities from which the city is experienced. Additionally, politics and geographies of academic and professional knowledge production and circulation do not offer an even ground for ideas, practices and activist efforts to receive exposure. 

 Bringing intersectional feminist and epistemic justice perspectives together, the session interrogates dominant gender-insensitive planning paradigms – quantitative, productivity-centred, androcentric, and oriented around the rational, utility‑maximising individual – that systematically marginalise care, relational, recreational and collective everyday (im)mobilities. It asks how knowledge hierarchies in academia, policy, and professional practice shape what counts as evidence, which actors and geographies are heard, and whose experiences of the city remain invisible.

sessions.title: Chris Philo's Geographies: A Reflection and Celebration
sessions.description: This panel reflects on and celebrates the work of Chris Philo, marking his retirement this year. Chris's work has made central contributions to a range of geographical questions and concerns. Over several decades, he has produced pivotal work across social and cultural geography, historical geography, rural geography, children's geography, animal geography, carceral geography, population geography, the geographies of madness and mental health, and the history of geography, as well as key contributions to how we work with thinkers from Foucault to Adorno. He has been a passionate advocate for geography as a discipline and has exemplified a spirit of intellectual generosity and curiosity. The panel brings together a range of geographers to reflect on and celebrate different aspects of his work and contributions.

sessions.title: Democratising the Economy (3)
sessions.description: People across the world are rethinking and reorganising their local economies towards more democratic, equitable, and sustainable forms in response to long-term (and uneven) economic and socio-spatial inequalities and multiple crises: from the global financial crisis and austerity policies decimating cultural provision and public capacity, the COVID-19 pandemic’s exposure of complex geographies of inequality and structural issues of health and social care under capitalism, and the climate emergency which too regularly affirms a crisis of essential infrastructures created by decades of underinvestment. Theoretically, a democratisation of economic relations is, of course, closely related to the concept of economic democracy (Cumbers et al 2020), but the vision of a democratic economy also has many ‘siblings’: diverse economies (Gibson-Graham 2008), approaches including the foundational, social and solidarity, and wellbeing economies, and tools such as circular and doughnut economics (Raworth 2019). In practice, we see efforts to democratise economic activity particularly at the local and regional level such as a return of public, collective, and diverse ownership forms through de-privatisation of (public) services, infrastructures, and assets across a wide range of sectors (Kishimoto et al 2020, Cumbers and Paul 2022, Bianchi and Russell 2026). Equally, the global cooperative movement’s long history of mutual economic development and alternative practice, and more recently, experimentation with post-growth and community wealth building is taking hold in cities and communities across the world (Guinan and O’Neill 2020, Savini 2023, Crisp et al 2024). This session seeks to gather theories, approaches, models, and case studies of alternative economic development and organisation that ‘democratise the economy’ to examine them as more than the sum of their parts. Deliberately inclusive, the session invites theoretical and/ or empirical contributions, including but not limited to public, collective, and community ownership, democratic governance, municipalism, (re)municipalisation and de-privatisation, cooperativism, questions of growth, and alternative imaginaries.

sessions.title: Tracing Symbolic Boundaries among Islands, Rivers and Borderlines : Improvising Embodied Inequalities in a Fluid World
sessions.description:
Migration studies are often narrated through water. Yet in a world marked by rising seas and hardening borders, water does not simply connect—it differentiates. For some, rivers are lifelines of mobility; for others, they become barriers of containment. This workshop interrogates water as a geography of inequity. We ask: How do waterlines—coastlines, rivers, oceanic routes—create and reinforce social inequalities? How are these inequalities embodied, felt differently across islands, borderlands, and vertical landscapes. 
 Moving beyond water as mere metaphor, this session engages participants in improvising embodied inequalities. Through a participatory improvisation workshop, we will explore how the ability to cross water (or be stopped by it) is unequally distributed; how ecological degradation and climate uncertainty deepen existing vulnerabilities; and how diasporic memory holds both trauma and resilience. Bridging political ecology, Island geographies, feminist geography, post-humanism, and postcolonial critique, we examine water as a site where power flows unevenly.

sessions.title: Geographies of Care and Violence (3)
sessions.description:
Feminist, political, and social geographies have richly examined care as an ethical relation, a form of labour, an affective practice, and a political project (Bartos 2018; Bondi 2008; Gutiérrez Sánchez 2022; McDowell 2004; Power and Hall 2018; Raghuram et al. 2009; Raghuram 2016), fundamental to the ways lives, bodies, and worlds are sustained (Fisher and Tronto 1990). At the same time, geographers have extensively documented violence in its many forms—intimate, structural, slow, and spectacular (Christian and Dowler 2019; Pain and Cahill 2022). The session seeks to examine the relations between care and violence.

 This session examines care and violence not as opposites, but as entangled and co-constitutive processes. The session is particularly interested in work that explores how violence is sustained, extended, or transformed through the absence, withdrawal, misrecognition, or uneven distribution of care, and how care may be complicit in or resistant to violence.

 Feminist geographies of care have argued that the relationship between caregiver and care receiver is not a simple or linear practice, unsettling assumptions that care is inherently benign, highlighting its ambivalences, exclusions, and conflicts, and calling for greater attention to the ways care and conflict are not mutually exclusive (Bartos 2018; Power 2019; Raghuram et al. 2009).

 Building on these debates, this session foregrounds questions of carelessness, neglect, abandonment, and misaligned care, asking how harm persists not only through acts of violence but also through everyday relations, institutional practices, and political–economic arrangements that deny or foreclose care. By bringing care and violence into conversation, this session seeks to open space to explore how care might be reimagined as a political and geographical practice capable of interrupting violence.

papers.title: Benevolent Containment: Micro-Geographies of Violence in Aged Care

sessions.title: Geographies of Food Insecurity: Spatialities, Precarity and the Pursuit of Just Food Places (1)
sessions.description:
Food insecurity is rarely a simple matter of food supply or financial poverty; it is a deeply spatialised phenomenon. As the 2026 conference theme highlights, entrenched inequalities are often reinforced by specific city, rural, and embodied landscapes. This session seeks to explore how a geographical lens can re-theorise food insecurity by moving beyond "food deserts" or income-centric models toward a more nuanced understanding of how space and place produce—and potentially resolve—food security and ensure that these resolutions are also socially just.

 Papers presented in this session interrogate the "more-than-financial" aspects of food insecurity and responses that engage directly with social justice. We focus on how geographical contexts create unique vulnerabilities for specific groups, how multi-scalar crises (such as the climate emergency or geopolitical shifts) translate into everyday precarity, and how place-based interventions can foster greater capability for socially just food security. By bringing together diverse theoretical and empirical approaches, this session aims to chart a path toward more "just places" in our global, national and local food systems.

sessions.title: Living Maps of Cultural Landscapes
sessions.description:
We engage the conference theme of Geographies of inequalities: toward just places  by positioning mapping cultural landscapes as a critical practice for advancing spatial and environmental justice. We approach cultural landscapes not merely as physical settings, but as socially produced environments shaped by memory, migration, labour, resistance, and everyday life. Mapping, in this context, becomes a method for revealing how justice—and injustice—are embedded in space.

 Conventional cartographies often reproduce dominant territorial impositions privileging state boundaries, property regimes, and extractive economies. In contrast, culturally grounded mapping practices foreground community knowledge, intangible heritage, and lived experience. By emphasising relational connections—between people and place, past and present, mobility and rootedness—mapping becomes both analytical and interpretive. It becomes a tool for preservation, empowerment, and dialogue. Through this approach, landscapes are understood not as static backdrops, but as dynamic and evolving cultural fields. 

 We ask,  how can cultural mapping expose spatial inequities or histories of dispossession? In what ways can participatory, decolonial, or digital methodologies contribute to more just representations of place? How might mapping practices help reclaim marginalised landscapes or reframe dominant narratives of territory and belonging? By centring cultural landscapes within debates on justice, this session positions mapping as an ethical and political act—one that reshapes how places are understood, governed, and imagined. Through interdisciplinary dialogue, we seek to explore how cultural cartographies can contribute to the making of more equitable and inclusive geographies, by positioning mapping not simply as representation, but as a dynamic process of knowledge production that reshapes how we understand territory, identity, and belonging

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Gender and Feminist Geography (3)
sessions.description:
The Gender and Feminist Research Group of the RGS (with the IBG) warmly encourages postgraduates and early career researchers to submit papers for our new and emerging research session. The session is ideal for researchers who would like an opportunity to present their research in a supportive and constructive academic environment, and for researchers at all stages of their careers who are interested in presenting papers that actively engage with discussions on current and emerging theoretical or methodological innovations in the field of feminist and gender geography.

 ‘Gender and Feminist Geographies’ is intended to cover a broad spectrum of research and papers are welcome from any area of feminist and gender geographical inquiry, with the aim of bringing together current and emerging themes, issues and approaches. Papers are especially welcome that connect with this year’s theme of “Geographies of Inequalities: Toward Just Places” at any stage in their research process are welcome, making the session a great opportunity for early career and postgraduate researchers to gain experience presenting their work. The sessions will also provide a forum to meet and discuss emerging ideas with other researchers in a friendly and relaxed environment, as well as opportunities to explore possibilities and relevance of engaging with feminist theory and methods within research.

 If you would like to submit a paper for consideration, please send a 300-word abstract to Rituparna Bhattacharyya, rituparna.bhattacharyya@accb.org.uk and Alkım Karaağaç, e.a.karaagac@bham.ac.uk, by Friday, 20th February 2026.

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Historical Geography (1)
sessions.description:
This session provides a space for postgraduates and early-career scholars undertaking research in Historical Geography to present at a major conference in a supportive and relaxed setting. Building upon past successful HGRG postgraduate session, our intention is to foster a supportive environment in which not only postgraduates, but everyone interested in historical geography connects with the wider historical geography community. 

 We welcome papers from postgraduates / early -career scholars at any stage of their research, that discuss initial research aims/design; engage with methodological and/or theoretical questions; or that draw upon empirical findings. There is no chronological or geographical limit to papers and the sessions will be designed to encourage audience questions, as well as the sharing of experience and advice. In short, this session will demonstrate the vibrancy of postgraduate contributions to Historical Geography.

sessions.title: Geography in Action: Revealing and Reducing Health Inequalities at the UK Health Security Agency (UKHSA)
sessions.description:
Public health outcomes are hugely influenced by place. From access to healthcare and green space to exposure to environmental hazards and infectious diseases, geography is an important determinant of health equity. This session explores how spatial thinking, geospatial analytics, and place‑based approaches can help identify disparities, design targeted interventions, and inform equitable public health policy. Through four speaker presentations followed by an interactive panel discussion, participants will gain insight into how geographic perspectives can transform decision‑making and reduce inequity across communities. This session will highlight Health Equity and Inclusion Health at the UK Health Security Agency.

 Learning Objectives 

 - Highlight how UKHSA are addressing Health Inequalities through strategic frameworks, visualisation, geospatial analysis and collaborative research

 - Describe how geography influences public health inequalities , including environmental, social, and infrastructural determinants

 - Recognise the value of geospatial data and tools in assessing, visualising, analysing and addressing disparities.

 - Identify collaborative opportunities between geospatial experts, public health professionals, and community partners.

 Session Structure  

 - Welcome & Introduction - James Lewis, Head of Geospatial, UKHSA

 - Speaker Presentations 
- Panel Discussion with All Speakers  
- Audience Q&A  
- Closing Remarks - James Lewis, Head of Geospatial, UKHSA

sessions.title: Attuning to More-than-human Seas: Negotiating Multispecies Justice in the Anthropocene Ocean (3)
sessions.description:
“What would it mean to take the Anthropocene out to sea?” (Alaimo, 2017, p. 153). With this provocation, Alaimo highlights the persistent neglect of marine environments and species within the terra-centric Anthropocene discourse. Oceans are profoundly marked by human activity, yet continue to be framed as vast, remote, and seemingly inexhaustible spaces for exploitation. Over the past two decades, the ocean ‘out there’ has been promoted as the latest frontier for economic development and an unexplored playground for climate change mitigation, often under the banner of “blue growth” (Johnson and Braverman, 2019; Bennett et al., 2021; Buck, 2019). Critically engaging with Blue Economy ambitions (and their unintended consequences), this session explores how sustainable futures and multispecies justices might be realised in Anthropocene seas. 

 Blue spaces beyond the coastline are typically uninhabited by humans, difficult to access and govern, and frequently located beyond the boundaries of national territories. Such spaces are nevertheless deeply entangled with global political economies, colonial histories, resource extraction, and uneven distributions of environmental risk, responsibility, and benefit. These dynamics increasingly render marginalised coastal communities and marine organisms vulnerable to the cascading impacts of human activities in the ocean. Put simply, Anthropocene seas are inherently unequal places and spaces. 

 Taking seriously the dynamic fluidity of the ocean requires moving beyond static separations between humans and nature, sea and land, or matter and mediation (Steinberg and Peters, 2015; Peters and Steinberg, 2019), to instead spotlight turbulent marine lifeworlds and more-than-human aquatic assemblages that are subjected to – yet also contest, challenge, and resist – unjust oceanic political economies. We thus invite papers that grapple with diverse notions of multispecies justice, blue justice, and blue degrowth to highlight overlooked oceanic geographies and to ask how Anthropocene seas can become sites for just and sustainable multispecies futures.
 We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes: 

 - Extending critical Anthropocene scholarship to oceanic contexts 
- More-than-human engagements with the Blue Economy and blue (de)growth 
- Indigenous and decolonial perspectives on Anthropocene oceans 
- Afrofuturism, speculative ecologies and imagined ocean futures 
- More-than-human oceanic geopolitics 
- Affect and the ocean: embodied and emotional human-marine relations 
- Blue animal geographies 
- Multispecies marine methodologies

papers.title: Infrastructural repair and maintenance at sea: more-than-human entanglements in ocean techno-sciences
The Wet Plantationocene: Seaweed Farming and the Making of Marine Plantation Frontiers
Mapping Genetic Extractivism in the High Seas

sessions.title: Writing successfully for the Journal of Geography in Higher Education
sessions.description: This session is designed to encourage and enable new academics/postgraduates that have undertaken pedagogic research to get their work published and disseminated to the rest of the geography community. After discussing the mission of the Journal of Geography in Higher Education (JGHE), panelists will explain the submission, review, and publication processes of the JGHE. Topics will include the nature of material appropriate for submission, the types and level of evidence necessary to support findings, the recommended length of manuscripts, advice about writing for an international readership, and JGHE's citation index. In this practical workshop, prospective authors will interact with panelists through an interactive paper review session and to discuss issues specific to manuscripts they are planning or writing. Practical guidance and advice will be provided to potential authors. The session will end with a plenary around the demands of getting published. It should be noted that the proposed running order of the session needs to be flexible to accommodate the needs and level of response from prospective.

sessions.title: Towards just places through children’s geographies in, of, and for, education (1)
sessions.description:
This session invites discussion about the value of, and possibilities for, further engagement with children's geographies in, and of, education, policy and practice. We focus on education as integral to children's everyday lives and their understanding of the world. From lecture halls to forest schools, education experiences unfold across diverse (in)formal and (un)structured spaces. Education spaces serve not only as sites of teaching and learning, but also as spaces of play, socialisation, resistance, and identity formation (McKendrick & Hammond, 2020; Kraftl, 2022; Potter & Cowan, 2020). 
 Yet these educational experiences are shaped by structural forces that create and maintain educational inequalities. School design, curriculum priorities, accountability measures and the legacies of residential schools often work to (re)create and (re)produce (in)equity and (in)justice (Catling & Martin, 2011; Kulz, 2017). We are particularly interested in how structural and institutional conditions and mechanisms intersect and compound across multiple scales, from embodied and everyday experiences in classrooms to regional, national, and global patterns of exclusion and marginalization. 

 To explore these matters further, we welcome contributions examining possibilities for children’s geographies  in  education, considering for example, where children are present or absent in curricula or engaged with through pedagogy across different phases and spaces of education (Catling, 2005; Hammond, 2022; Owens et al., 2022). We also welcome contributions that consider children’s geographies  of  education, exploring for example how children - with different identities - experience and imagine material and social spaces of education spaces and systems (schools, homeschooling-pods, forest schools etc) (Holloway et al., 2010; English, 2021; Brown, 2022; Kraftl et al., 2022; Brundrett, Malone & Rowley, 2025). This includes how children navigate and experience unequal access across and as shaped by digital, physical, and environmental factors. We invite contributions from those researching and teaching in higher education, as well colleagues working in K-12 or alternative education spaces. We would be particularly delighted to hear from children and young people themselves through research or direct contribution.   Through this session, we aim to strengthen connections between children's geographies scholarship and educational practice, working toward more just and equitable educational futures.

papers.title: A Feathered Foundation: Children’s Connections with Local Species, Implications for Research and Education
Mental struggles as embodied intersecting violence: a case study in Nepal
Girls in the woods: The influence of Forest School on primary aged girls’ perceptions of (invisible) girlhood

sessions.title: Towards Geographies of the Middle (1)
sessions.description:
This session explores the middle as a productive, open, and under-theorised conceptual vantage point in geography. It emerges in dialogue with recent calls in the geographies of ageing literature to attend more closely to the middle (Barron, 2025) and middle-age by extension (Lulle, 2024). However, the middle is pertinent far beyond age and ageing literature. This session takes this insight as a starting point, opening the conversation to ‘the middle’ in all its forms and to all (inter)disciplinary vantage points.

 The middle appears in social classifications (mid-career, medium-educated, middle-class) and in spatial logics of scale, proportion, and measurement. It is also profoundly processual . To be in the middle of something (a journey, a text, a negotiation, a household task) is to inhabit unfolding processes, unfinished projects, and open present horizons. The middle is the thick present in which pasts accumulate and futures are actively crafted.

 Theoretically, scholars have often approached the middle indirectly, through concepts such as liminality, waiting, impasse, or transition, foregrounding affective states and moments of change. Methodologically, too, the middle is ever-present but rarely named. Research turns occur mid-project; insights emerge through interruption. Fieldwork, analysis, and writing repeatedly unfold in unsettled mid-states that shape knowledge production in important ways.

 Our aim is to think critically and creatively about what a geography of the middle might look like. We invite abstracts that explore any geography of the middle, including (but not limited to):

 - Life-course and biographical middles, including midlife and middle age, and approaches that think through the middle to reframe youth or later life
- Social, political, and classed middles, such as centrism and political ambivalence, partial citizenship, precarity, mobility, and being ‘caught in the middle’
- Transitional and processual middles, including gender transitions, decarbonisation and socio-technical change, recovery, waiting, and suspension
- Affective and relational middles, such as ambivalence, burnout, care responsibilities, sandwich generations, and negotiated belonging
- Everyday and temporal middles, such as midweek, midday, mid-career, and other rhythms of the ‘thick present’
- Methodological middles, including research conducted mid-project, through interruption, partial findings, or reorientation

 References: 

 Barron, A. (2025). Making sense of ‘middle-age’: thinking from and through the middle. Social & Cultural Geography , pp.1-20.

 Lulle, A. (2024). Midlife Geographies: Changing lifecourses across generations, spaces and time . Bristol. Bristol University Press.

papers.title: Bodies in the Middle: the menopause as a socio-spatial experience
Menopause and the Geographies of the Middle: Everyday Experiences of Reproductive Ageing
Making sense of ‘middle-age’: thinking from and through the middle
Affective Futurity of Middle Age:  Strength Training as Everyday Futurity-Work and Anticipated Ageing

sessions.title: Writing Minor: Nonrepresentational Inequalities of Asian Diasporas (1)
sessions.description: This session explores the non-representational aspects of Asian diaspora inequalities in two directions. First, we examine existing media portrayals, political discourse, and cultural stereotypes that reduce the polyvocality of Asian diasporic lives and reproduce minority lives as unequally 'minor' (Hong 2020). We look particularly closely at the uneven representations and territorialisations of emotionality, sensibility, and viscerality along lines of race, nationality, and citizenship. Second, we seek alternative, irregular, and 'minor' ways of representing diasporic lives and experiences that challenge these existing discourses and theoretical frameworks. Affirming the force of the minor, we explore creative forms of expression, narratives, and world-making practices that exist in the spaces made within and transcending inequality (Yapp 2021). Overall, this session examines the importance of representational politics while encouraging alternative ways of writing geographical research on diaspora, with special attention to fluid, affective, and experimental approaches. We encourage submissions that engage with Asian diasporas globally, including but not limited to East Asian, Southeast Asian, South Asian, and Central Asian contexts, and welcome comparative and transnational approaches. We also welcome diverse formats of writing, including traditional papers, visual essays, creative-critical hybrids, and collaborative presentations that push the boundaries of conventional academic formats. This session aims to explore directions including, but not limited to: •     Diasporic home and world-making efforts in the interstices of unequal representations •     Diasporic geographies of senses, bodies, and intimacy •     Critical analysis and/or creative re-animations of unequal media representations of diaspora •     Alternative ways of theorising and representing the diaspora as and beyond 'minor' •     Intersectional experiences of inequality within and across Asian diasporic communities (including dimensions of class, gender, sexuality, generation, and ability) •     Historical and contemporary perspectives on Asian diasporic nonrepresentation References Hong, C.P. (2020) Minor feelings: a reckoning on race and the Asian condition . London: Profile Books. Yapp, H. (2021) Minor China: method, materialisms, and the aesthetic . Durham: Duke University Press.

sessions.title: The ‘rural crisis’ and social inequalities in housing and other services
sessions.description:
The rural dimensions of the contemporary ‘housing crisis’ in the UK are arguably under-stated in national media and political discourses, despite commentators raising serious concerns of rural housing affordability and exclusion (e.g. Colomb and Gallent, 2022; Korden and Naumann, 2024; Shucksmith, 2025; Woods, 2025). Over the last decade, the unaffordability of rural housing for many local social groups has been exacerbated by processes of rural gentrification (Sherman, 2023); increasing second/multiple home ownership (Gallent et al., 2023 ); the proliferation of short-term rentals, including AirBnBs and holiday lets, and; the rising availability of agricultural properties for conversion to residential housing (as farms increasingly wind-down due to harsh economic conditions) (Franks, 2022). The predilection for rural living has also been intensified by opportunities for affluent groups to counterurbanise and work-from-home, and reduce commuting (Wilson et al., 2025). At the same time, state-led pressures for more new-build housing have led to many rural spaces on the fringes of metropolitan, provincial and small market towns, as well as villages and hamlets, being transformed. Although this may provide an opportunity to provide more affordable housing, these new estates often comprise relatively high cost, exclusive housing, with limited proportions of ‘affordable housing’. These processes of rural change are giving rise to widening social inequalities between and within many rural places in the UK, which is also having a cascading impact on the supply and demand for rural services, such as pubs, post offices, health centres and retail. In this session, we seek papers that connect with the broad issue of rural housing and social inequalities, and which examine factors and outcomes associated with growing inequalities in rural housing markets and rural places.

 References 

 Colomb, C., & Gallent, N. (2022). Post-COVID-19 mobilities and the housing crisis in European urban and rural destinations. Policy challenges and research agenda. Planning Practice & Research , 37 (5), 624-641.

 Franks, J. R. (2022). UK Agriculture at a Crossroads. Outlook on Agriculture , 51 (4), 448-459.

 Gallent, N., Stirling, P., & Hamiduddin, I. (2023). Pandemic mobility, second homes and housing market change in a rural amenity area during COVID-19–The Brecon Beacons National Park, Wales. Progress in Planning , 172 , 100731.

 Kordel, S., & Naumann, M. (2024). The rural housing crisis: analytical dimensions and emblematic issues. Housing Studies , 39 (12), 3029-3046.

 Sherman, J. (2023). “Please don’t take this”: Rural gentrification, symbolic capital, and housing insecurity. Social Problems , 70 (2), 491-510.

 Shucksmith, M. (2025). The exclusive countryside post‐pandemic. The Geographical Journal , 191 (4), e12524.

 Wilson, R., Noble, C., & Currie, M. (2025). The changing rural idyll and the ideal migrant: The case of Scotland during COVID-19. Journal of Rural Studies , 114 , 103497.

 Woods, M. (2025). Rural recovery or rural spatial justice? Responding to multiple crises for the British countryside. The geographical journal , 191 (4), e12541.

sessions.title: Epistemic Injustice: Geographical Data and the Urban Pedagogy
sessions.description:
This panel examines the implication of justice in the circulation of the knowledge in urban space; focusing particularly on the gathering, processing, archiving, and teaching of data. It asks: What is the role of epistemic justice in the creative urban pedagogies toward the just city? With the everchanging geographical landscape of economic, socio-political, and environmental conditions affecting urban life, ‘data’ has an inevitable role in how urban life is lived, governed and taught. Nonetheless, persistent questions remain about how ‘data’ is utilised in geographical and urban practices, such as defined and produced (by experts, institutions, algorithms, residents), taught and circulated (through curricula, maps, policy documents), stored and archived (what is preserved, forgotten, or erased), and mobilised and contested (for planning, governance, advocacy, or resistance).

 Several layers of questions arise when working with ‘data’ in practice. Data can be described as inherently biased; however, it can also be argued that data is a formless alienated object awaiting human interpretation, while human interpreters are themselves inevitably biased. How then can more representative, less biased, and pluralistic approaches to data collection be achieved? How can data be processed and incorporated into policy making without undermining local wisdom, empirical data, experiential knowledge, and embodied mappings? How can human emotions and intangible motivations be archived without rendering them static and fixed? In the educator–student relationship, on what basis do you trust educators to ‘know better’? Whose data is collected, heard, preserved, and taught? How do systematic exclusions along lines of gender, age, race, nationality, and shifting judgments about what counts as credible or verifiable knowledge shape this?

 This enquiry is also challenging the way we see justice. What is the limitation of justice itself? And what does epistemic justice truly mean in an urban space? Is genuine democratised and pluralised data even achievable? Can we imagine a world with an alternative way of seeing data and/or without data? Concerning Epistemic Injustice in an Urban Context, this panel seeks a deeper discussion on the temporality, practicality, and limitation of data gathering, processing, and application. It invites geographers and beyond to rethink how our practices engage with the non-tangible and invisible elements of the data, and how the nature of such data shapes their ways of seeing justice.

papers.title: Shaping Just Cities through Digital Urban Heritage Narratives: Mobilising Local Knowledge under Epistemic Constraints
Towards Epistemic Justice: Image Theatre as Embodied Urban Data
Drawing London, Writing Belgrade: Life Maps and Poetry as Counter-Cartographies of Transnational Diasporic Belonging
From Heterotopia to Utopian Order: Epistemic Construction and Exclusion in the Urban Public Art of Putrajaya and Kuala Lumpur
affiliations: Academy of Malay Studies, Universiti Malaya

papers.title: Counting What Shapes the City: Capital-Flow Data, Assetized Streets, and the Politics of Enclosure

sessions.title: Putting people, place and power at the centre of just transition debates in the UK
sessions.description:
Mainstream approaches to climate policy in the UK tend to fixate on individual consumer choice, ignore social and spatial injustices, and put social and environmental needs in competing siloes. Top-down, one-size-fits-all strategies fail to change dominant power relations or reduce carbon emissions at the pace and scale needed, while bottom-up approaches that just assume communities can step in when the state does not (eg 'the Big Society') are inadequate. It is well past time for a paradigm shift. 

 Place-sensitive, collective strategies are the best hope for moving toward a fairer and more democratic low carbon future. These strategies must meet climate ambitions at the same time as tackling myriad problems caused by socio-economic inequalities in one of the most spatially unequal countries in the world. But to facilitate this shift, we need not only to understand what works where, how and for whom -- and what could work in different circumstances or with more or different resources -- but also how to communicate positive examples that have broad public resonance. 

 We propose a conference panel dedicated to discussing the role of place-sensitive, participatory research in realising these urgent needs. How can co-productive research demonstrate the transformative potential of an inclusive and just transition to a lower carbon society? What is needed to enable citizens to collectively decide what a just climate future means for them? Which stories and whose voices should be amplified to rebuild trust and counter the rising resistance to net zero, especially from the populist right? 

 In this proposed panel, researchers in the ESRC-funded Centre for Joined-Up Sustainability Transformations (JUST) will attempt to answer these questions. We will discuss findings from our place-sensitive case study research with communities in five areas of North of England, highlighting  the substantive themes (including principles of justice, social infrastructure, social and solidarity economies, democratic innovations and policy, governance and change) and the methodological challenges and creativity that have emerged so far. 

 The panel's framing themes and questions will be introduced by the Director of the JUST Centre (MacGregor, chair of the session), before five ‘snapshot’ presentations about case studies in West Cumbria, Merseyside, Greater Manchester, West Yorkshire and the North East. There will then be a chaired discussion with all panellists and session participants, which invites additional examples and reflections on how participatory, co-produced research can contribute to the development of hopeful narratives that put people, place and power at the centre of just transition debates. 

 We plan to fund five early career researchers who have been working on place-based case studies to attend the RGS Conference to give short presentations (not papers so they are not submitting individual paper proposals). Case studies include: Cumbria Citizen's Climate Assembly, Todmorden Learning Centre, Dwellbeing Shieldfield, Growing Together in Levenshulme, and Kindred (Liverpool).

sessions.title: Just Urban Transitions: Geographies of Inequalities and Governance Pathways to Sustainable Futures (1)
sessions.description:
Urban sustainability transitions are urgent, uneven, and deeply political (Goh, 2021; Gonçalves et al., 2025). As cities are increasingly reframed from sites of environmental degradation to key arenas for climate mitigation and adaptation (Parnell, 2016), governments and communities face profound challenges in balancing ecological limits with socio-spatial justice and the creation of liveable urban futures (Kaufmann et al., 2024). These challenges are particularly acute in cities shaped by entrenched inequality, informality, and infrastructural precarity, where climate crises intersect with longer histories of colonial dispossession and uneven development.

 This session critically engages with the concept and practice of Just Urban Transitions. While the term “just transition” emerged from labour struggles linked to energy transitions (McCauley & Heffron, 2018), a growing body of scholarship calls for a more expansive urban lens that foregrounds the full range of resources, services, and opportunities required for urban life, including water, sanitation, housing, mobility, and land (Hughes & Hoffmann, 2020; Maboye et al., 2025).

 Bringing together empirically grounded and conceptually rich contributions, the session examines how just urban transitions are co-produced, governed, contested and lived across diverse urban contexts. Particular attention is given to the governance dilemmas and “complex trade-offs” (van Aswegen & Matamanda, 2025) that emerge as climate mitigation and adaptation efforts intersect with existing forms of precarity and privilege. The session highlights how decision-making draws on both political and technical forms of knowledge across multiple levels and actors, shaping access to basic services and urban opportunities.

 Centred on cities, particularly in the Global South, the session explores urban spaces as complex and dynamic arenas where governance is enacted through state and non-state actors, and where crises of energy, water, housing, employment and mobility are deeply entangled.

 Themes addressed include governance and multi-scalar coordination; justice in climate adaptation and mitigation; everyday practices, informality and hybrid infrastructures; methodological innovations; and rethinking metrics and imaginaries of sustainable urban futures.

papers.title: Growth Before Justice? Economic Rationales and Urban Climate Governance in Sri Lanka
Negotiating informality and urban resilience: implications for equity
Governing a Just Urban Transition

sessions.title: Dis/En/Abling mobility: Mobile labour and (in)justices in access to mobility
sessions.description:
Mobility is a fundamental prerequisite for everyday life in all kinds of spatial patterns. While cities are often portrayed as hubs of movement and accessibility, rural regions are increasingly shaped by a growing necessity for mobility as public services and infrastructures continue to decline. At the same time, all spaces remain deeply dependent on mobility to sustain work, care, and social life.
 Yet, what frequently remains hidden is where, how, and whose mobility is enabled. Put differently, who is mobile for whom —and at what cost—so that others can move faster, farther, or more conveniently across spatial scales? What spatial disparities characterize access to mobility? Mobility services and infrastructure are neither evenly distributed, nor just in the ways they are built and used. Rather, mobility systems are produced, maintained, and enabled by political decisions and the labour, time, and bodies of others, while mobility itself also depends on individuals’ capabilities and resources.
 This session therefore foregrounds frictions and tensions in the provision of and access to mobility services and infrastructures, drawing attention to the often invisible labour, spatial arrangements and social relations that are the backbone of mobility provision systems. From bus drivers who enable everyday accessibility, to platform-based delivery cyclists and couriers whose work is an integral part of cities’ transport logistics, mobility is continuously produced. In rural contexts, access to essential services, beyond those available locally, often depends on community-based, informal, and private transport provision. All of these mobilities are essential but frequently undervalued, precarious, and unevenly recognized. Whilst questions of accessibility and mobility provision have given rise to new approaches to urban planning, they are even more acute in rural areas, where being mobile is even more a necessity.
 In this session we critically explore the intersections of who is mobile, by whom mobility is enabled, and resulting socio-spatial inequalities and injustices. We examine not only mobility practices themselves, but also the hidden power relations, inequalities, and dependencies embedded in the (re-)production of mobility systems. In doing so, the session seeks to contribute to ongoing debates on mobility justice (Sheller, 2018) and mobility poverty (Lucas, 2016) across different spatial scales.

 Planned contributions:

 1. Wojciech Kębłowski, Monika Maciejewska, Merlin Gillard: Making transport work under material scarcity: lessons from Santiago de Cuba

 2.  Amrutha Viswanath: Missing Mobilities: Investigating Last Mile Connectivity to Public Transport in Underserved Neighbourhoods of Shivaji Nagar, Mumbai and Clichy-sous-Bois, Greater Paris Region

 3. Dhita de La Roche: From Access to Dependence: Informal Mobility Systems and the Reproduction of Transport Unfairness for Women with Disabilities in Yogyakarta, Indonesia

 4. Sarah Davidson: Immobilising the Marginalised: Transport Infrastructure and Mobility Injustice in Belfast

 5. Samuel Nello-Deakin, Guillem Viñas Salvador: Exploring the equity of urban traffic fines: Disciplining dangerous or vulnerable transport modes?

sessions.title: Citizen science and co-production as routes to plurality and justice
sessions.description:
In this panel we discuss papers that outline and analyse non-extractive research methods to examine innovative forms of research design that better attend to plural knowledges, land-based struggles and community inclusion. We are particularly interested in how principles of citizen science or co-production can “promote inclusion (and) interrogate power relations and hegemonic knowledge systems (Vincent, 2022:730; see also Kesby et al., 2013: Kesby, 2007; Kindon et al., 2007). 

 In short, we will learn from projects that prioritize collaborative, participatory, co-productive methodological work and seek papers on methods designed to support “self-determination, decolonization and social justice” (Smith, 1999:4).

sessions.title: NGOs and Sustainable Fashion Transitions: Challenges, Strategies and Success Stories  (2)
sessions.description:
It is well-established that the fashion industry is one of the most environmentally and socially unsustainable industries in the world (Bick et al., 2018; Niinimäki et al., 2020; United Nations Environment Programme, 2023). Despite these impacts being well known, the industry is resistant to change and, with the acceleration of ultra-fast fashion, may be getting worse. While there is an urgent need for change, the question remains: how will this occur?

 Transforming the industry requires intervention from multiple levers concurrently, including government legislation, consumer shopping behaviours, manufacturing processes, and brands’ decision making. Non-governmental organisations (NGOs) are frequently positioned as key agents of change due to their ability to operate across scales and engage diverse actors. However this reliance on NGOs warrants critical examination, particularly regarding their capacity, legitimacy, accountability, and potential co-optation within existing power structures. 

 This session is interested in understanding the strategies and role of NGOs influencing the pro-socio-environmental behaviours that value chain actors (i.e. raw materials producers, manufacturers, brands, consumers, recyclers) are pursuing, including challenges and enablers. For the purpose of this session, NGOs are defined as organisations of any size and prominence, involved in activities which push for positive environmental and social change in the fashion industry such as setting certifications and standards, developing industry schemes, campaigning, and reporting on industry activities. Examples include Textile Exchange, Fashion Revolution, Oeko-Tex, Zero Discharge of Hazardous Chemicals (ZDHC), Oxfam, Clean Clothes Campaign, Fair Wear Foundation, Laudes Foundation, Sustainable Apparel Coalition, and Or Foundation. 

 In this session, the fashion industry is used as a lens to analyse ethical labour and production, product design and materials, recycling and disposal, and over-consumption. This session brings together research from different contexts to critically examine how NGOs influence socially and environmentally sustainable practices across fashion value chains, including limitations, potentially unintended consequences, and success stories to inspire across geographies. Particular attention is given to how NGOs navigate tensions between collaboration and confrontation, voluntary standards and regulatory change, and incremental improvements versus systemic industry transformation.

papers.title: Governing fashion sustainably: What hope for NGOs?
Stitching for Change: Negotiating Power and Livelihoods through NGO-led Skills Upgrading in India’s Informal Fashion/Garment Sector
Sustainability Intelligence (SI): NGOs as Systemic Enablers of Sustainable Fashion Business Transformation – A Case Study of a Netherlands-based Denim Industry Collective

sessions.title: The Missing Building Workshop
sessions.description:
This session uses making to explore inequality, belonging, and spatial justice. It is informed by feminist theory, which understands knowledge as lived, situated, and shaped through everyday experience. It draws on The Missing Building, a participatory research framework that invites participants to design a small model of a building they feel is missing in their community. The objective is not to highlight design solutions, but to use the process of making (ABR) as a constructive dialogue between people, nature, and the city, and to attend to what these absences reveal about the places we live and about ourselves as living within them. The session approaches the built environment as relational and practiced, shaped through movement, care, and unequal conditions of access and recognition. It is informed by bell hooks’ engaged pedagogy and her understanding of hope as a lived practice, where making is understood as an ethical and political act. In a world marked by intensified struggles over land, ownership, displacement, and the right to claim space, absence becomes a way of listening differently, attending to who belongs, who decides, and who is left out, while resisting top-down approaches to place and decision-making.
 The workshop will start with a brief introduction to The Missing Building , a participatory research project taking place in the UK, the Netherlands, Portugal, Canada, and Mozambique, which invites people to consider which buildings or spaces are missing in their communities. Delegates will spend time creating a small conceptual model of a building they wish existed in their neighbourhood or city. We will finish with a group discussion, reflecting together on belonging, inequality, care, and spatial justice. The session is designed as a space for shared inquiry and making, with opportunities for ongoing collaboration after the conference.

sessions.title: Podcasting for Urban Studies
sessions.description:
Podcasting is an increasingly common form of audio media in popular culture and media alike. Urban researchers and educators have made clear calls for attention to this medium over the past decade. This Urban Studies- sponsored roundtable convenes a variety of established and new initiatives putting podcasting at the heart of urban research and dissemination. Building on experiences like City Road , the Urban Radar , USJ’s Walking the City With… or The Urban Political , the roundtable reflects on the challenges and opportunities for podcasting to advance and shape our (inter)disciplinary futures. Can we formalise podcasting as method, reading it as input and format in scholarship production not just dissemination? Could we revalue slow podcasting, and is there still time for “thick time” podcasting in today’s ever fast academia? Should podcasting be institutionalised or stand independently of stringent academic structures? To unpack these urban podcast hosts go behind the scenes of their experiences, dialogues and innovations.

 Participants: 

 Michele Acuto (University of Bristol, chair); Caitlin Morrissey (University of Manchester); Beth Perry (University of Sheffield and Urban Radar); Ayona Datta (University College London); Tom Goodfellow (University of Manchester and Urban Radar); Dallas Rogers (University of Sydney and City Road Podcast); Hanna Hilbrandt (University of Zurich and Urban Political).

sessions.title: Scaling Conservation, Scaling Injustice? Power, Values, and Equity in Expanding Nature-based Credit Market Interventions
sessions.description:
Nature-based credit markets have been positioned as a solution to financing conservation interventions needed to meet the fast-approaching targets of the Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework and the Paris Agreement (Swinfield et al., 2024; IPCC, 2023; CBD, 2022). Nature-based credits are quantified units of ecosystem services (e.g. carbon) that can be sold in voluntary or compliance markets. Their rapid expansion - often associated with the commodification of nature, standardisation of land governance and management approaches, and enduring colonial legacies - raises pressing questions about equity, power, and justice (Milner-Gulland, 2024; McAfee, 2023; Chausson et al., 2023).

 This session explores the trade-offs between urgency, scalability, and justice in nature-based credit markets. We ask: Whose values, knowledge, and priorities shape how these markets are designed and implemented? Can conservation be scaled equitably through credit-based mechanisms? If credits fall short, what alternative tools and mechanisms might help address the climate and biodiversity crises within the constraints of current political and economic systems?

 Focusing on the current evidence, the first half of the session draws on academic presentations and critical provocations that present:

 (i) empirical case studies (including multi-scalar analyses and first-hand practitioner accounts) that explore trade-offs and synergies between scaling conservation through credit-based mechanisms and advancing social justice and equity. 

 (ii) reflections on adjustments to, or alternatives beyond, nature-based credits that seek to redistribute power, embed equity, and sustain place-based justice. This may include adaptations to the design of credit-based mechanisms (e.g. community-owned carbon projects and locally defined monitoring frameworks), as well as other market-based instruments (e.g. green bonds) and non-market or redistributive approaches (e.g. rights of nature, unconditional cash transfers). 

 These contributions will inform a facilitated discussion during the second half of the session. Speakers and participants will collectively reflect on the cases presented, comparing experiences across contexts and critically assessing the potential and limitations of proposed adjustments and alternatives to credit-based markets. The aim is to create a shared space to explore common patterns and design challenges, and to identify priority areas for future research and practice.

 Discussion themes may include:

 - How credit-based interventions reshape rights and power relations among actors, and how the associated risks and responsibilities are distributed as these interventions expand (e.g. among NGOs, governments, local communities, Indigenous Peoples, businesses, and financial institutions).

 - How standards, metrics, and monitoring systems reconfigure what counts as “good” conservation, including whose values and forms of knowledge are prioritised.

 - How different strategies for “working at scale” influence equity in recognition, decision-making, and benefit-sharing, including the role of governance arrangements (e.g. centralised versus networked) and implementation strategies (e.g. replicating standard models versus adapting to local contexts).

 - What political, legal, and institutional conditions are necessary for credit-based (or other market-based) mechanisms to avoid reinforcing existing power asymmetries. 

 - Under what conditions non-market or redistributive approaches may be able to deliver at the scale and speed implied by the urgency of the biodiversity and climate targets.

sessions.title: The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route (1)
sessions.description:
This panel session brings together contributions from the ERC-project
 “The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan
 Route” to present its preliminary findings and foster critical 
discussion and exchange. The project constitutes the first 
transnational, multi-sited, and extensive qualitative study of the 
so-called "Balkan Route", which is the most important overland informal 
migration corridor in Europe, taken by thousands of refugees every year.
 Linking Greece to Western Europe, across Bulgaria, Romania, Albania, 
North Macedonia, Kosovo, Montenegro, Serbia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, 
Slovenia, and Croatia, the Route is a complex geography of formal and 
informal, visible and invisible sites, an assemblage of diverse actors, 
camps, borders, violence, solidarity, and a multiplicity of 
micro-routes, constantly adapting and shifting. Across and between these
 interconnected spatialities, refugees forge their trajectories towards 
Europe through "The Game", the term they use to refer to their 
clandestine journeys and border crossings. Through The Game, refugees 
themselves produce a counter-geography that consists of a constellation 
of strategically organised refugee spatialities and imaginative 
geographies, marked by the hopes and ambition of seeking a better life 
in Europe as well as by the pain and fear experienced through perilous 
journeys. Across the precarious, ambivalent, and ever-changing 
circumstances of the Balkan Route, an archipelago of formal and 
makeshift camps has emerged, hosting thousands of refugees as they make 
repeated attempts to enter the EU. Key in producing and sustaining The 
Game, these sites serve as temporary shelters, nodes of services and 
information, where refugees meet smugglers, wait, and plan the next 
move. 

 Drawing on the project team’s rich, multi-sited ethnographic 
research, this panel session interrogates the complex 
counter-geographies of the Balkan Route by investigating its distinct 
spatialities, archipelago of camps, informal mobilities, ambivalent 
legal geographies, and border geopolitics.

 The panel is organised into two sessions. The first session brings 
together contributors from the edited volume “Thinking Like a Route: 
Counter-geographies of Informal Migration in the Balkans”, who will 
reflect on their chapters while outlining the subsequent developments of
 their research. The second session features empirically grounded 
presentations by four researchers of the project conducting fieldwork at
 key junctures along the Balkan Route, including Austria, Greece, North 
Macedonia, and Slovenia.

papers.title: The silent 'politics of the refugee ban' along the Balkan Route
(Counter-)Geographies of Route Thinking
Beyond Implementation: EU Enlargement, Asylum Governance, and the Limits of Legal Alignment along the Balkan Route

sessions.title: Co-creating Coastal Resilience: Participation, collaboration, and cross-sector working for just coastal futures (1)
sessions.description:
Coastal risk is not simply the outcome of sea-level rise, erosion, flooding or pollution. It is also produced through the politics of governance – who defines risk, whose knowledge counts, who is allowed in the room, and how responsibility is allocated, shared or quietly offloaded across sectors and scales. As coastal hazards increasingly intersect with housing shortages, ageing infrastructure, habitat loss, public health crises and fragile local economies, collaborative working has become a central expectation within shoreline management and flood risk governance (Sayers et al., 2022; Lane et al., 2011). Yet in practice, participation and partnership is often expensive, slow, fragile and messy – shaped, and sometimes derailed, by uneven capacity, institutional inertia, funding constraints and histories of mistrust (Blunkell, 2017).

 This pair of linked sessions brings together researchers, partners and community collaborators involved in the Resilient Coastal Communities and Seas (ReCCS) programme, a £15m UKRI–Defra programme involving 100+ co-leads and charged with doing something ambitious: generating knowledge, action and resilience for UK coastal communities and seas in just 5 years. At the programme’s midpoint, these sessions offer space not for polished narratives, but for honest, critical and constructive reflections on what it really means to attempt participatory coastal research at scale and across sectors with diverging priorities, constraints and cultures.

papers.title: Relational Resilience: Impact and Learning in Transdisciplinary Settings
affiliations: Centre of Environment Fisheries and Aquaculture Science (Cefas)

Building Coastal Community Resilience in the East of England: Reflecting on an Asset-Based Approach

sessions.title: Interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility: the challenges of a cross-cutting theme (1)
sessions.description:
A wealth of literature has developed in Anglo-Saxon and European countries over the past 20 years on the interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility. It has highlighted the importance of a systemic approach that combines housing and transportation costs at the household level (Haas and Osland, 2013; Coulombel, 2018). Some studies have adopted a macroscopic approach to show the central role of the state of socio-technical systems, including a political economy of car dependency that enables space-consuming and energy-intensive land use patterns (Le Goff et al., 2025). Other more sociological approaches have focused on households’ overall residential strategies (Desvallées and Haine, 2025, Zanolini and Morency, 2025). Finally, most of this research has focused on the social issues raised by this double expense. They highlight the interconnection between vulnerabilities in housing and mobility, which overlap in households facing a combination of socioeconomic difficulties and spatial marginalisation (Mattioli et al., 2018; Simcock et al., 2021).

 In addition to this social issue, which can be further explored through changes in lifestyles (e.g., teleworking, electric cars), the topic of household spending on housing and mobility is now giving rise to new avenues of research, broadening and enriching the questions being asked. 

 1) Firstly, from a methodological point of view, statistical data on housing and mobility are very often constructed in thematic silos, and the development of statistical tools to observe these expenditures is currently very limited. Some recent work has attempted to overcome this limitation, either by using databases that had not been exploited from this perspective until now (Makarewics et al., 2020), or by developing original collection methods (Nicolas et al., 2025).

 2) Still on the issue of the cross-cutting nature of the topic, the treatment of mobility-housing interactions raises key questions about spatial planning and public policy choices in terms of anticipating and controlling urban sprawl. It therefore seems important to examine public policy measures in order to diagnose the persistence of the siloed treatment of these two issues, but also to report on the concerns of local authorities regarding the role of this double expenditure on regional planning.

 3) Exploring the environmental dimension of these interactions broadens the discussion beyond social issues to include impacts in terms of emissions and shifts the focus to other audiences, potentially moving from low-income households to the residential footprint of wealthy households. It also questions the tools used to represent the interactions between transportation and urban planning in situations of strong urban dynamics.

 4) Finally, there is a wide variety of fields that have been explored from this angle of interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility, with opportunities to cross-reference different spatial contexts. The idea is to offer regional perspectives on North and South America, Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Pacific region/

 The session we are proposing will therefore be an opportunity to take stock of recent academic work on the theme, with the intention of launching a collective reflection in the social sciences, engineering sciences, environmental sciences, and health sciences.
