sessions.title: Reclaiming street space from cars (3): new instruments and experiences with the socio-ecological transformation of urban neighbourhoods and cities for better liveability and more sustainable transport
sessions.description:
In recent years many cities worldwide have embarked on a transformation of the transport system to improve living conditions and reduce negative aspects of transport. Among other issues, central objectives of these transformations are to use urban space for non-car purposes to improve liveability and promote the use of non-motorized or shared modes of transport. Various cities have promoted concepts like the 15-min-city, superblocs or improved cycling and walking infrastructures. To implement these concepts, car spaces (parking, streets) need to be re-used for other purposes. Despite questionable overall effects of these new instruments on a city-wide level, the relevance and emergence of these types of policies and related instruments for urban transition processes are clear. 

 The objective of this session is to assemble research evaluating and assessing transport, social, economic or liveability aspects of instruments and tools employed for transforming urban areas. The instruments might involve restructuring the built environment (including transport infrastructures), communication and participation strategies or relevant financial instruments. We also seek research addressing the availability and redistribution of parking spaces by financial or other instruments or by policy packages combining the instruments mentioned. Preferably, we are seeking empirical contributions using sound theoretical reflections on the effects and changes initiated by relevant policy instruments. Real world laboratories or other means of transdisciplinary research are highly encouraged to present their findings.

papers.title: Residential Parking in Practice: An Exploration of Everyday Parking Routines in Residential Neighbourhoods through the Lens of Practice Theory
Charging the Imaginary: Electric Vehicles and Spatial Imaginaries of Car-Home Proximity
Car restriction policies in European cities: a comparative analysis

sessions.title: QMRG Annual Lecture in collaboration with The Geographical Journal
sessions.description:
This flagship event aims to celebrate cutting-edge work in quantitative geography while fostering connection and dialogue across the diverse methodological traditions that coexist within the RGS-IBG community.

 It directly addresses the persistent gap between methodological communities in human geography by offering a platform for dialogue for researchers across the RGS-IBG. It will foreground research that combines quantitative and theoretical insight, showing how geographers can use diverse forms of evidence to engage with questions of inequality, space, and environment.

papers.title: Whose Numbers Count? The Contested Politics of Data in National Policy and Planning
affiliations: School of Geography and Environmental Sciences at the University of Southampton

sessions.title: Extended urbanisation in South Africa's former homelands
sessions.description:
South Africa’s former bantustans (or apartheid-era ethnic homelands) present a dilemma for post-apartheid policy making and governance. The ten bantustans were central to the apartheid plan for racial spatial engineering, and it was a fair expectation that they would wither away with their full reintegration into South Africa at the end of apartheid. However, bantustan geographies proved to be resilient, with a longstanding scholar of their histories, William Beinart, commenting in 2012 that it is “surprising how many people stayed in the former ‘homelands’ and dense rural settlements”. Yet not even Beinart could have anticipated the scale of a seemingly incongruous transformation currently underway. 

 South Africa’s first three post-apartheid censuses indicate that the population of almost all former bantustan areas remained static or even declined in the fifteen years after apartheid. But the latest Census 2022 results show a reversal – between 2011 and 2022 the former bantustans together had a population growth rate higher than the rest of the county. Some were growing significantly faster than metropolitan municipalities, including crisis-ridden Johannesburg. This growth is being accompanied by dramatic changes in the built environment. Particularly significant is the recent surge of homebuilding on land administered by traditional authorities. These investments are at a scale and intensity, and of a character, that seem incongruous with areas imagined to be rural or represented in policy and planning as rural. This residential construction, in styles and sizes typically associated with middle-class or even luxury suburbs and gated communities in metropolitan cities, is not isolated. The trend has been observed across many parts of the country and is increasingly accompanied by related developments such as local- and regional-scale malls.

 This session combines four papers that explore this extraordinary change in direction in South Africa urbanisation path. The papers are all located within the theoretical and analytical frame of extended urbanisation – the processes by which different forms of ‘the urban’ spread beyond conventionally defined cities into territories conventionally understood as ‘rural’, often under the aegis of wider urban forces that may be planetary in scale and scope. Combined, the papers are both informed by, and speak back to inform, the theory of extended urbanisation. 

 The first paper considers the scale of demographic and spatial change, seeking to account for what is going on in the conjuncture of multiple, overlapping, and mutually reinforcing social, economic, and cultural processes playing out nationally. The second points at regional and local variations across the former bantustans and looks to explain differences in terms of spatial considerations and historical legacies. A third paper looks closely at extended urbanisation in Bushbuckridge, a very rapidly growing ex-bantustan area outside the commuting range of any large urban centre, and accounts for growth in terms of a three dimensional framework of material transformations, regulatory practices, and the production of new experiences and meanings. A fourth paper examines extended urbanisation in areas of dual land governance on the ‘inherited periphery’ of the Gauteng City-Region and eThekwini.

papers.title: Differentiated patterns of change across South Africa’s former Bantustans: A closer look at the Limpopo, Mpumalanga and Eastern Cape provinces
‘Homeland’ legacies in South Africa’s city-region peripheries: emerging, consolidating and stagnating urban territories in Gauteng and eThekwini
What is Bushbuckridge? Extended urbanisation, displaced urbanism, or new urban territories in South Africa
affiliations: Chair of Urban and Spatial Development, Centre for Urbanisation and Peripheralization, Technical University of Munich (Munich, Germany)

papers.title: Extended urbanisation in the former bantustans: Extent and underlying processes

sessions.title: Touching Visions: Touch, Embodiment and the Sensory for More Just and Caring Cities - Discussion and workshop
sessions.description:
Cities are encountered through bodies that sense, feel, and are touched by their environments, yet much of this everyday felt sensing is excluded from urban knowledge systems. In this session we seek to explore how the body “becomes a site of possibility” (Merleau-Ponty, 2002) in the caring city (Power & Williams, 2020). Puig de la Bellacasa (2017) invites us to think of the “webs of care through sensorial materiality, as chains of touch that link and remake worlds” (p.120). Drawing in the sensory encountering of the city we explore the often neglected senses of urban everyday life through the lens of care. In particular we draw on Puig de la Bellacasa’s ‘touching visions’, which refer to ways of knowing and imagining the world that are shaped by care, proximity, and response-ability , rather than by detached, distanced, or objective observations. They are ethico-onto-epistemic orientations that emphasise how knowledge is always situated, relational, and affectively “touched” by what and whom it engages with. 

 Yet bodies are not touched equally by the city. Sensory exposures, vulnerabilities, and capacities to care are unevenly distributed along lines of gender, race, class, disability, age, and caregiving relations. Approaching urban health through “touching visions” therefore opens up a way of thinking about inequalities as embodied, affective, and sensorial; enacted through everyday encounters with urban spaces.

 Situated at the intersection of urban geography, feminist geographies of care, sensory geographies, and critical geographies of health, this session explores how touching visions might reframe understandings of urban health and care. We are particularly interested in how gendered, racialised, disabled, and caregiving bodies experience and navigate urban environments; how infrastructures of care shape access to health and wellbeing; and how more-than-human relations; with air, heat, water, plants, and urban ecologies; participate in chains of touch that sustain or undermine health and wellbeing.

 Part 1 – Touching provocations (10 minutes each) 

 We invite scholars to prepare short, conceptually focused provocations that critically engage with how urban life is sensed, touched, and cared for (or not) through bodies, environments, and infrastructures. Presentations may be empirical, theoretical, or methodological, but should foreground how in/equalities are experienced through embodied and sensory relations of touch, proximity, and exposure. In keeping with the session’s emphasis on sensorial materiality, we welcome multimodal or sensory elements in presentations, including sound, images, maps, audio recordings, movement, video, or other media that can communicate how the urban is felt and lived.

 Part 2 – Arts-based, sensory mapping workshop (touching visions in practice) 

 The second half of the session will be an interactive, arts-based workshop in which participants collaboratively map the speakers’ provocations through drawing, collage, or diagramming. Rather than treating each paper as separate, the workshop will explore how different in/equalities touch one another across bodies, spaces, and infrastructures, tracing resonances, tensions, and unexpected connections across speakers’ and session participants’ lived experiences. This collective mapping will enact touching visions as a method: a way of knowing that is situated, relational, and responsive.

papers.title: Haptic recall: Sensing familiarity in fieldwork
Holding hands at the crossroads: child-parent touching interdependencies on the move
Cycling from Frame to Frame: Communicating Sensory Urban Experiences through Graphic Storytelling
Fostering Waste Care: Touch and Sensory Encounters in Community Composting Initiatives
Situated Sensing: Crip Celebration and Caring Co-Creation for Urban Mobility

sessions.title: ‘On mission?’: Understanding the geographies of clean energy growth and development (1)
sessions.description:
Building on a long period of cross-party political consensus in the UK, which included establishing its world leading legally binding net zero 2050 target, in 2024 Prime Minister Starmer announced that ‘Making Britain a Clean Energy Superpower’ was one of the six milestones for his new mission-led government (UK Govt 2024). Alongside delivering energy security, reducing consumer bills and reaching net zero, the Clean Energy Superpower Mission promised to drive “investment, good jobs and growth across the country” (UK Govt 2024a). 
 Like the preceding Conservative Government’s Clean Growth (2017) and Powering Up Britain (2023) strategies, clean energy is seen as offering not only significant national economic returns but also the promise of more regionally balanced growth (Curran et al 2022). Such rebalancing, ‘levelling-up’, or ‘realising potential’ will, by its very nature, require “leveraging the clean energy transition to turbocharge growth” (UK Govt 2025b) across poorer and more peripheral areas of the UK. In particular, industrial heartlands and coastal communities have been identified as areas with “enormous opportunity to build the energy industries of the future and create the good jobs at good wages that people have long demanded” (UK Govt 2025b p.4). Consequently, clean energy has become central to the visions and economic development policy lexicon of many peripheral and old industrial regions. 

 This session uses the bold ambitions of the UK’s mission-led approach, as a point of departure, to reflect on what progress we have made, and what challenges still exist, in understanding the geographies of clean energy growth and their contributions to regional development. Considerable conceptual and empirical contributions have been made to this agenda in recent years from work within and across inter alia energy geographies (e.g. Bridge and Gailing 2019); EEG and Green Path Development (e.g. Dawley et al 2025); Green Innovation and Technological Diversification (e.g. Moreno and Ocampo-Corrales 2022); Geographies of Sustainability Transitions (e.g. Binz et al 2025); Regional Innovation Systems (e.g. Jakobsen et al 2022) and Global Production Networks (e.g. Coe and Gibson 2023). However, how do these promising contributions help us understand, or even inform, the geographical opportunities and challenges facing supra-national (e.g. EU); national; and local and regional clean energy growth visions, policies and strategies (e.g. Coenen et al 2021; Trippl 2025). Equally, as the political consensus surrounding Clean Power and Net Zero comes under greater challenge internationally, including within the UK itself, we also look for insights into how we can understand the implications for localities and regions facing increased uncertainty and risk around clean energy investment from both state and industry. 

 Themes could include, but are not limited to, international perspectives and policy insights related to: 

 - What forms of local and regional development are emerging around clean power ? How are these being manifested in local and regional visions and futures? 

 - To what extent does the growth of clean power disrupt or reinforce existing forms of uneven development?

 - How effective are existing and emerging forms of governance in delivering the development of clean power across, and within, regions and localities?

papers.title: The regional promise of UK solar: Imaginaries, fixes, and contradictions of green accumulation regimes
Policy Mixes and State Capacities in Regional Path Development: Low Carbon and Renewable Energy in Northern England
Regional implications of private network ownership and power the UK’s clean energy transition
affiliations: UCL Institute for Innovation and Public Purpose

papers.title: Reframing UK energy policy towards wellbeing within planetary boundaries
affiliations: Science Policy Research Unit, University of Sussex

papers.title: Powering regional paths of development? 25 Years of Offshore Wind in the UK

sessions.title: Contesting Housing Injustice: Mobilising Materials of Concern (1)
sessions.description:
Mould has recently become a potent symbol of domestic inequality in postwelfare states (Kane, 2023; Shurety, 2025; Storer et al., 2025). Similar to other uncharismatic intrusions, mould reveals a material dimension to longstanding geographical concerns for the domestic as a site of struggle, permeated by, and very much a part of, discriminatory ideologies and sociopolitical forces (Blunt and Dowling, 2006; Brickell, 2012; Massey, 1994; Sibley, 1995; Speer 2017). But mould is not just a site of despair, it is also political – this substance has simultaneously been mobilised by activists to articulate reform to discriminatory and neglectful modes of housing provisioning (NEF, 2024). Within the still unfolding mould epidemic, materiality bleeds into metaphor –mould renders visible structural and historical processes of marginalisation lost in technocratic and austere planning regimes (Simpson et al., 2025). 

 Mouldy struggles not only reveal any bounded notion of home to be a bourgeois fantasy (Kaika, 2004), but politicise the unequal risks and labours sprung from disrupted socio-natural boundaries over which tenants have little control. Whilst Kaika writes of water, we might add concerns relating to a multiplicity of transgressive organic and chemical matter – mould, pests, pollutants – all which enter, grow with, leak from the lining of, and cohabitate our homes at markedly uneven rates. Indeed, precursors to the politicisation of mould can be found in struggles for housing justice which centre cladding (Smith, 2023), lead (Fennel, 2015), dust (Noterman, 2023), and pollution (Schneller et al., 2022). These non-human and uncharismatic intrusions reshape inhabitation, carrying the potential to destabilise security and even to unmake the home under regimes of precarity (Baxter and Brickell, 2014). 

 A growing chorus of empirical studies draws our attention to racialised-classed logics which shape the distribution of matter out of place (Douglas, 1966). This panel is interested in how people live with, and contest uncharismatic, toxic, or ‘feral’ non-humans and materials. It explores the interface between lived experience and the work of contesting potentially harmful materials – in the practical and evidentiary practices through which matter becomes concerning within and beyond domestic space. 

 We are interested in the processes through which people, communities and activists ‘scale’ with uncanny domestic intrusions to confront grinding forces of in-situ dispossession. We likewise welcome coalitional scholarship which traces political contestations which form around materials – both in early struggles for recognition where harm is often contested and contentious (Murphy, 2006), and where later, following the “designation of the unseeable” (ibid: 9), matter comes to be used to make claims against hostile and dismissive states, landowners, and institutions for fairer and juster habitation. We hope to draw together scholars engaged in activist work, or studying the quieter work of persevering in conditions of housing distress amidst biomedical, legal and regulatory structures which limit claims and compensation.

papers.title: Mobilising Mould in the UK Hostile Environment
Lifestyle Takes Place: Dwelling, Mould and the Moralisation of Everyday Life in Social Housing
Resisting mould in the face of dispossession: Experiences of poor conditions on regenerating housing estates in London
Contesting Mould and the Politics of Responsibility
Fifty Years of Campaigning Against Dampness
affiliations: London School of Economics and Political Science

sessions.title: Geopolitics: borders, resources, territories

sessions.title: Digital Geographies Annual Lecture
sessions.description: The Digital Geographies Annual Lecture engages with this year's Chair's Theme of 'Geographies of inequalities: toward just places' by turning to the everyday ways in which digital power and control is enacted. Our panellists - Azadeh Akbari, Julian Brigstocke and Philippa Williams - will share insights from their respective research and work from a range of contexts. sessions.title: Feminist Geographies of Heat, Health and Thermal Inequality (2)
sessions.description:
Climate change is increasingly recognised as a defining health challenge of our time, yet its harms are profoundly uneven. Extreme and chronic heat, in particular, is often framed through biomedical thresholds and behavioural risk models, obscuring how exposure, endurance and recovery are structured through gendered labour, racialised housing markets, care infrastructures and political economy. This session intervenes by bringing feminist geography into direct conversation with critical health geography to reposition heat as a form of slow violence, an embodied, relational and deeply political determinant of health.

 Building on feminist care ethics (Tronto), intersectionality (Crenshaw), and climate justice scholarship, we conceptualise heat as a form of thermal violence: a slow, cumulative force that reorganises daily rhythms, constrains rest and hydration, intensifies unpaid care work, exacerbates reproductive and mental health vulnerabilities, and exposes workers in precarious sectors to disproportionate risk. Who is able to cool, rest and recover? Whose suffering is rendered visible in heat metrics, and whose remains normalised?

 We invite theoretical, methodological and empirical contributions from across the Global South and North that engage with themes such as:

 - Gendered and intersectional heat-health inequalities

 - Cooling infrastructures, energy poverty and thermal justice

 - Labour, care and everyday endurance under chronic heat

 - Housing, informality, mobility and public space in warming cities

 - Feminist political ecology and climate-health governance

 - Innovative feminist and mixed methods for researching heat-health

 - Mapping heat hot spots and vulnerability using geospatial approaches

 These topics are illustrative. We encourage any contributions that critically engage the nexus of gender, health, and environmental change. 

 The session will run in a mixed format combining paper presentations with a facilitated discussion designed to surface shared questions and future directions. We warmly encourage Early Career Researchers and scholars working across disciplines to submit.

 Beyond the conference , this session aims to open space for an ongoing collaborative conversation. Participants will be invited to join a developing feminist heat-health reading group to continue dialogue and explore where this agenda might lead.

 Let’s use this moment to sharpen the debate and build something lasting.

papers.title: Heat-related Health Risks and Inequalities in Urban Everyday Life: Insights from Household Surveys in German Cities
Energy Justice in Incremental Social Housing: Everyday Negotiations of Vulnerability and Care in Chile
Chinese women’s embodied experience in Wilderness Trekking
affiliations: School of Hotel and Tourism Management, The Hong Kong Polytechnic University

papers.title: Uncovering Emotions and Hope for Justice in Urban Adaptation to Future Heat Extremes

sessions.title: Memory, Memorialization, and Resilience in Displaced Lives
sessions.description:
Memories are sites where identity, belonging, and resistance are enacted. For refugees and displaced communities, memory sustains connections to their lost homes and once celebrated cultural heritage. In contexts of displacement, where social and cultural identities are under constant pressure, memory becomes a vital resource to preserve their history. Through shared acts of remembering, they come together to memorialize their struggles and reconstruct collective histories to bridge the gaps between remembered places and present realities. They come together to make a place in the out-of-places to resist their erasure. 

Yet, their memory also carries the weight of trauma. Their experience of violence can also produce silence that tends to forget the painful events. It raises critical questions about memorialization. Could memorialization be a selective process of drawing boundaries between self and the other to marginalize certain experiences, legitimize social hierarchies, and reinforce or challenge dominant narratives? 

We begin from the assumption that memory is neither a straightforward resource for resilience nor a passive terrain of suffering. Instead, we seek to invite papers that acknowledge the complex reality that memory work among displaced communities can be simultaneously generative and contested; healing and fragmenting; collective and exclusionary. As forced displacement reaches unprecedented levels globally, we seek papers that aim to understand how memory can operate as a terrain of survival and as a site of power struggles. In other words, we are interested in examining memory as an active, ongoing political process that seeks to define not just belonging but possible futures as well. We welcome empirical, theoretical, and methodological studies that make critical interventions to existing scholarship drawing from a broad range of memory practices and contexts, including storytelling, material objects, rituals, poetry, cultural performance, among others. 

The papers may specifically questions including but not limited to the following: 

- When there is so much trauma involved in their lived experience, how do 
occluded memories find ways in their places? 
- Can memory and memorialization foster resilience by creating spaces for 
recognition, continuity, and collective healing? 
- How memory is practiced, produced, and mediated by power? 
- Who gets to remember publicly, and whose memories remain (in)visible - How do everyday practices—storytelling, rituals, material objects, and cultural performances—trace and sustain memory over time?

papers.title: Hope is Home: Land, Memory, and the Politics of Counter-Mapping
Memory as Spatial Struggle: Displacement, Reconstruction, and Contested Futures in Sur and Dêrsim
Refugee Camp as Memoryscape
Journalistic Witnessing- Testimony Gathering and Collection in Mediterranean Border Zones

sessions.title: Interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility: the challenges of a cross-cutting theme (2)
sessions.description:
A wealth of literature has developed in Anglo-Saxon and European countries over the past 20 years on the interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility. It has highlighted the importance of a systemic approach that combines housing and transportation costs at the household level (Haas and Osland, 2013; Coulombel, 2018). Some studies have adopted a macroscopic approach to show the central role of the state of socio-technical systems, including a political economy of car dependency that enables space-consuming and energy-intensive land use patterns (Le Goff et al., 2025). Other more sociological approaches have focused on households’ overall residential strategies (Desvallées and Haine, 2025, Zanolini and Morency, 2025). Finally, most of this research has focused on the social issues raised by this double expense. They highlight the interconnection between vulnerabilities in housing and mobility, which overlap in households facing a combination of socioeconomic difficulties and spatial marginalisation (Mattioli et al., 2018; Simcock et al., 2021).

 In addition to this social issue, which can be further explored through changes in lifestyles (e.g., teleworking, electric cars), the topic of household spending on housing and mobility is now giving rise to new avenues of research, broadening and enriching the questions being asked.

 1) Firstly, from a methodological point of view, statistical data on housing and mobility are very often constructed in thematic silos, and the development of statistical tools to observe these expenditures is currently very limited. Some recent work has attempted to overcome this limitation, either by using databases that had not been exploited from this perspective until now (Makarewics et al., 2020), or by developing original collection methods (Nicolas et al., 2025).

 2) Still on the issue of the cross-cutting nature of the topic, the treatment of mobility-housing interactions raises key questions about spatial planning and public policy choices in terms of anticipating and controlling urban sprawl. It therefore seems important to examine public policy measures in order to diagnose the persistence of the siloed treatment of these two issues, but also to report on the concerns of local authorities regarding the role of this double expenditure on regional planning.

 3) Exploring the environmental dimension of these interactions broadens the discussion beyond social issues to include impacts in terms of emissions and shifts the focus to other audiences, potentially moving from low-income households to the residential footprint of wealthy households. It also questions the tools used to represent the interactions between transportation and urban planning in situations of strong urban dynamics.

 4) Finally, there is a wide variety of fields that have been explored from this angle of interactions between residential location, housing, and mobility, with opportunities to cross-reference different spatial contexts. The idea is to offer regional perspectives on North and South America, Europe, Asia, Africa, and the Pacific region/

 The session we are proposing will therefore be an opportunity to take stock of recent academic work on the theme, with the intention of launching a collective reflection in the social sciences, engineering sciences, environmental sciences, and health sciences.

papers.title: Residential Location, Housing Precarity, and Mobility Constraints: A Cross-Cutting Framework for Identifying Dual Energy-Mobility Cost Burden
The Impact of Short-Term Rentals (STRs) and STR Regulation on Neighborhood Change in New Orleans
Double Energy Vulnerabilities in France: When Residential Choices Lock Households into Patterns of Energy Dependence
affiliations: Laboratoire Transitions Énergétiques et Environnementales (UMR TREE 6031), Université de Pau et des pays de l'Adour / E2S UPPA, CNRS

sessions.title: Religious Nationalisms and the spatial politics of the Far Right
sessions.description:
‘The far right’, argue Strømmel and Schmiedel, ‘has a theology... and we need to respond to it theologically’ (2020: 146). From the US Christian Nationalist right to Hindutva ideology in India, the combination of religion with extreme forms of nationalism has become increasingly prominent in global politics. Nominally secular parties and political movements of the far right – such as in Turkey, Germany, or the UK – have also sought to enlist and build alliances with ultraconservative elements of religions to strengthen their causes.

 These developments are arguably producing both new geopolitical orderings at global and macro-regional scales, alongside (often-violent) changes in the everyday life of places and communities affected by them. Such groups draw on a variety of techniques with distinctly spatial characteristics: well-established geographical imaginaries related to religious war and the clash of civilisations over geographical ‘pivot’ points; palingenetic odes to national rebirth through theocratic piety and purification of national space; policing strict social hierarchies by making claims to a ‘natural’ order and enforcing those claims with crusader-like, militaristic men at its apex.

 This session brings into dialogue scholarship on the changing geographies of religion (Kong et al., 2025; Hopkins, 2025), religious geopolitics (Albrecht and Sturm, 2025), anti-fascist geographies (Pries, 2019; Santamarina, 2020; Ince, 2019), and work on the affective politics of authoritarianism and right-wing populism (Luger, 2022; 2024; Anderson and Secor, 2025). The present conjuncture requires critical reflection on how an effective, pluralistic anti‑fascist politics might operate. As far‑right actors mobilise religious affects, practices, and imaginaries, resistance must engage the theological and emotional registers through which these movements acquire meaning and force. At the same time, this moment opens out new ethical and political possibilities - interfaith solidarities, religious activism, and postsecular collaborations across ideological and religious difference (Cloke et al 2019) - both to contest far‑right religious politics and cultivate alternative moral and political futures.

 We invite papers that attend to the geographies and spatial politics of religious nationalisms, in any context, and/or diverse efforts to confront, evade, and exceed them.

papers.title: Faith, folk and the far right: racist and anti-racist Heathenry and Occultism in Britain
Rise and Fall of Dimes Square: How Tech Money Attempted to Create a Fascist Catholic Subculture in Pandemic-Era New York
Temple corridors and the spatial politics of religious nationalism in India
Negotiating Constraints and Practising Resistance: Lives of Muslim Girls and Women in Mumbai
affiliations: School of Geographical Sciences, University of Bristol

sessions.title: Organizing (in-)justice through infrastructures
sessions.description:
Infrastructures have long been understood as a foundational condition of social, economic, and political life. In geography and across other disciplines, infrastructures are increasingly conceptualized not merely as technical systems or material backbones, but as powerful socio-material assemblages. They organize life spatially while simultaneously being shaped and maintained, but also contested by social interaction and individual practice. Infrastructure research already emphasized the need to jointly consider material and social dimensions of infrastructural productions and operations (Jaglin & Rateau, 2020; Lawhon et al., 2018; Rosen & Gribat, 2025). However, while this insight is widely acknowledged, empirical and methodological approaches have often struggled to specify how exactly materiality, social practices, institutions, networks and discourses interact in concrete settings. As a result, the complex relationship of infrastructures as organizing and organized elements of life with its underlying power-relations, materialities and socialities have not yet been explored sufficiently. This becomes particularly evident in processes of negotiation and conflict over infrastructures, highlighting disbalances of resources such as (political) power, money or time, contributing to the production of (in-)justices.

 Newer theoretical and empirical insights that focus on the entanglements of infrastructures and (in-)justices offer approaches to address this still existing gap in research on infrastructures, while also highlighting the complex relationships of structural power imbalances and individual rationalities of actors. These debates draw attention to the role of infrastructures in producing and reproducing spatial inequalities with its violent effects and the structural production of vulnerabilities and inaccessibilities along lines of class, race, gender and (dis-)ability. Further, infrastructures are often part of extractivist projects and material processes which, often irrevocably, alter environments, affecting certain communities more than others and causing “feral” effects in the more-than-human world (Tsing et al. 2024). These processes have, among other concepts, been captured as planetary urbanization (Brenner 2013) or slow violence (Nixon 2013).

 The session aims to address these infrastructurally organized inequalities as injustices. This points towards moments of resistance that allow to oppose dominant forms of infrastructures with different and more just practices of infrastructuring (Volmar 2023) organized by practices of care, commoning and intimacy (Berlant 2016; Zechner 2021). Research within this field often challenges not only the dominant notions of infrastructural organization but also reflects on scientific knowledge production as infrastructure itself addressing ethical questions about research, theory and the use of knowledge (Puig de la Bellacasa 2017, Berlant 2016). These debates come with a range of theoretical and methodological contributions for the challenges of research on infrastructures, highlighting the importance of connecting different strands of thought to further develop theory and practice in the field.

papers.title: Infrastructuring Care: Unequal Social Infrastructures and the Politics of Care Innovation
Inclusive infrastructure under pressure: Building the case for GEDSI in LMIC energy and transport development
affiliations: United Nations Office for Project Services (UNOPS)
University College London; University of Oxford

sessions.title: Working towards Equality in Indigenous Tourism
sessions.description:
Indigenous peoples have struggled to gain recognition of their ownership of their lands and knowledge systems. Despite this, the past 20+ years are showing the growth of independent Indigenous-owned tourism ventures and also an increased interest in Indigenous tourism. Indigenous peoples steward 25% of the earth’s land and most tourism experiences around the world take place on Indigenous territories, yet their role is little acknowledged. Indigenous tourism can help protect sacred places, strengthen sustainable practices, and preserve land, culture, and languages. It can also support Indigenous communities in their pursuit of recognition and self-determination. So how do we work together toward a more inclusive and equitable future?

 We have personal and professional experience with Indigenous communities around the globe, including work focused in the social geographies of injustice and inequality, bridging spaces, time, and cultures. While Luis Felipe (Puruhá) explains the overarching conditions for Indigenous peoples across the globe, focused on economic inequities, Sébastien (Wendat) will highlight how the Indigenous-led tourism mark of excellence and authenticity, the Original Original, will help consumers and destinations better interact with Indigenous experiences as well as how global Indigenous tourism data is guiding Indigenous tourism worldwide. Juan Carlos (Aymara) will share examples of the real challenges faced by many Indigenous communities in regions with fewer financial resources, limited infrastructure, and lower levels of tourism development.

 The discussion will be moderated by Amy, who works on inclusive cultural initiatives with Indigenous communities, including capacity-building in museums and tourism with both Luis Felipe’s and Juan Carlos’s communities in the Andes.

 Moderator: 

 Amy Van Allen (USA), PhD Geography (Leicester), Fulbright Scholar, senior project manager Smithsonian's National Museum of the American Indian

 Panelists:

 Sébastien Desnoyers-Picard (Canada), President and Chief Executive Officer, Destination Original Indigenous Tourism (DO-IT), former Vice-President Indigenous Tourism Canada

 Luis Felipe Duchicela (USA/Ecuador), CEO ISP Global, former Global Indigenous Program Advisor to the World Bank and USAID

 Juan Carlos Patón (Bolivia), legal representative ASOREMUC-QŃ and Qhapaq Ñan Tours

sessions.title: From Camps to Island: The Politics of Protection for Rohingya Genocide Survivors
sessions.description:
Bhasan Char has become one of the most consequential and contested spaces in contemporary refugee governance. It is also inseparable from the fact that the Rohingyas are genocide survivors, subjected to systematic violence, persecution, serious crimes against humanity and forced displacement from Myanmar. Many first sought refuge in Bangladesh and were concentrated in the Cox’s Bazar camps. More recently, a substantial number have been relocated to Bhasan Char, a newly developed island settlement in the Meghna estuary. This trajectory, from genocidal expulsion to camp settlements and then to island relocation, provides a critical lens for the 2026 RGS conference theme, Geographies of inequalities: towards just places , because it foregrounds how extreme violence and displacement are translated into spatial arrangements that can reproduce or reconfigure injustice.

 This session examines Bhasan Char as a managed geography of inequality in which material improvements in shelter and basic services can coexist with serious human rights concerns, including restricted freedom of movement, limited livelihood opportunities, uneven access to education and healthcare, protection risks, and the social harms associated with separation from kinship networks and familiar community infrastructures. The panel offers an empirically grounded, conceptually rigorous discussion of how inequality is produced through governance practices, infrastructural design, and the politics of evidence in a context shaped by genocide and its aftermath.

 The contributions develop three integrated lines of inquiry. First, the session foregrounds lived experiences and the gap between official assurances and everyday realities, drawing on field-based interviews and narratives that document how refugees assess safety, housing, rations, healthcare, education, cultural and religious life, and the dignity of everyday practice. These accounts illuminate how inequalities become embedded within apparently administrative decisions about services, permissions, and everyday regulation, often with uneven effects across age, gender, and household position.

 Second, the panel examines livelihood, capability, and agency under constraint. Using the capability approach and related frameworks, contributions explore informal economic activities and coping strategies that create modest autonomy, skill development, and dignity, while remaining shaped by market isolation, regulatory uncertainty, and mobility restriction. This strand directly addresses a central concern for “just places”: the conditions under which resilience becomes a genuine expansion of freedom and opportunity rather than a rhetorical substitute for enforceable rights.

 Third, contributions analyse communicative ecologies, showing how mobile phones and social media can function as coping infrastructures while also reflecting inequalities in safety, affordability, and access. Environmental and geomorphological perspectives assess settlement sustainability and risk transfer within a dynamic delta, including cyclone and storm-surge exposure, erosion and accretion processes, and climate variability. A further contribution draws on micro-narratives of gendered violence and vulnerability, conceptualising harm as a continuum from genocide through displacement to camp and relocation settings and highlighting how structural and cultural violence may persist even where physical security appears improved.

 The session concludes by proposing a practical agenda towards just places for genocide-displaced populations: rights-centred mobility arrangements, accountable risk governance, ethically designed livelihood pathways, and evidence practices that amplify refugee voices while minimising harm.

papers.title: Habitation after Genocide in a Changing Delta: Geomorphology, Climate Risk, and the Spatial Inequalities of Rohingya Relocation to Bhasan Char
Livelihood, Agency and Capabilities: Informal Economic Activities of Rohingya Refugees in Bhasan Char
Beyond Paradise and Prison: Reimagining Bhasanchar Through the Lens of the Right to Freedom of Movement and Access to Livelihoods
affiliations: Centre for Peace and Justice, BRAC University

sessions.title: Geographies of Food Insecurity: Spatialities, Precarity, and the Pursuit of Just Food Places (2)
sessions.description:
Food insecurity is rarely a simple matter of food supply or financial poverty; it is a deeply spatialised phenomenon. As the 2026 conference theme highlights, entrenched inequalities are often reinforced by specific city, rural, and embodied landscapes. This session seeks to explore how a geographical lens can re-theorise food insecurity by moving beyond "food deserts" or income-centric models toward a more nuanced understanding of how space and place produce—and potentially resolve—food security and ensure that these resolutions are also socially just. 

 Papers presented in this session interrogate the "more-than-financial" aspects of food insecurity and responses that engage directly with social justice. We focus on how geographical contexts create unique vulnerabilities for specific groups, how multi-scalar crises (such as the climate emergency or geopolitical shifts) translate into everyday precarity, and how place-based interventions can foster greater capability for socially just food security. By bringing together diverse theoretical and empirical approaches, this session aims to chart a path toward more "just places" in our global, national and local food systems.

papers.title: Social practice misalignment in food places. How more-than-financial use of food support sites creates contested and transferred practices
Food insecurity in rural places: invisibilities, local foodscapes and socialities
Developing a community-led, city-level Disaster Risk Plan for Food Security
Food access as lens to rural inequality: An ethnographic inquiry into food insecurity experiences among rural families with young children
Tending the Plot: Living Otherwise while the Plantation Recalibrates

sessions.title: Author meets critics: Joe Gerlach's Spinoza's Geographical Ethics
sessions.description:
Spinoza’s Geographical Ethics by Joe Gerlach is the first book length treatment of Spinoza’s work in geography. It offers a careful, inventive and always spirited reading of how Spinoza offers a re-writing of earth and world. Gerlach brings this geographical version of Spinoza to bear on the problem of how to practice a geographical ethics today, in the midst of twenty-first century societal, environmental and political transformations and violent upheavals. As such, Spinoza’s Geographical Ethics grapples with issues long at the heart of geography, including cartography, rights and hope, re-encountering and re-working them through a geographical Spinoza. 

 The panellists will draw out themes, propositions and positions from Spinoza: A Geographical Ethics , connecting it with their work, and opening a conversation about Spinoza in geography, ways of thinking spatially in the early twenty first century, and how to conceptualise and practice a geographical ethics.

sessions.title: Finding Equality in Coastal and Marine Litter Research and Governance: Learning from Lived Experience on Scotland’s West Coast
sessions.description:
Plastic pollution has mobilised the academy across disciplines yet, while its sources are globally shared, its socio-ecological impacts are not. To understand these uneven impacts requires considered integration of lived experience into plastic pollution research. However, such knowledge remains underutilised within scholarly and political landscapes that preferentially pursue the new, the novel, and the international over the current, the everyday, and the local. 

 Through sustained engagement with communities along Scotland’s west coast, our research has foregrounded the expertise of local knowledge holders working to understand, monitor, and manage marine plastic pollution along their coastlines. While these communities have welcomed academic engagement, they have also challenged dominant research approaches that disconnect marine debris and its impacts from people, overlooking heritage, place-based relationships, and daily life. 

 Community partners identify standardised scientific methodologies, complex industrial systems, and legislative constraints as key limitations to effective environmental stewardship and sustainable solutions. These limitations are further confounded by practical challenges of time, distance, and money in which this largely volunteer workforce operates. Consequently, the published record presents an environmental summary of marine litter along Scotland’s west coast that is juxtaposed to the lived experiences of those who share their local environments with marine litter year-round.

 This panel session responds to this disconnect by centring community perspectives as critical geographical knowledge, providing a platform for community partners to share the spatial, temporal, and political challenges that shape their engagement with plastic pollution on heavily-littered, geographically-distant coastlines. These lived experiences will be situated alongside discussions of research and governance gaps that researchers and CMRG members can contribute to, shaping more just futures for coastal communities. Grounded in principles of ecological citizenship and blue justice, the session positions community partners as panellists and the academy as its audience, fostering dialogue for how geography can better support equitable coastal stewardship.

sessions.title: New and Emerging Rural Researchers
sessions.description:
This Rural Geography Research Group session, held annually, is a vibrant platform for exploring cutting-edge concepts in rural geography and for showcasing the endeavours of postgraduate and early-career researchers. 
 Given recent transformative shifts in rural landscapes, our focus aligns with the overarching theme of “Geographies of inequalities”, which examines spatial inequalities and injustices across society, culture, politics, the economy, community, climate and the environment. In the evolving narrative of rural dynamics, landscapes have undergone significant transformations in response to global and national shifts. 
 This session aims to discuss some of the inequalities facing rural areas and how they have responded to these inequalities, such as the climate crisis and COVID-19, to name a couple of examples.
 We invite you to delve into the diverse landscape of rural geography, and we are interested in hearing from a diverse range of research, including how these inequalities have been mapped, monitored and tracked, for example. 
 The aim is to stimulate insightful discussions centred on inequalities in rural geographies, thereby fostering a nuanced understanding of the inherent complexities within the rural landscape.
 This in-person session will provide a friendly and encouraging forum for researchers to present their ideas and results and discuss them constructively with all attendees.

papers.title: More Rain, More Water, Uneven Benefits: Irrigation Access and Agrarian Livelihoods in Semi-Arid Eastern India

sessions.title: New and Emerging Research in Historical Geography (2)
sessions.description:
This session provides a space for postgraduates and early-career scholars undertaking research in Historical Geography to present at a major conference in a supportive and relaxed setting. Building upon past successful HGRG postgraduate session, our intention is to foster a supportive environment in which not only postgraduates, but everyone interested in historical geography connects with the wider historical geography community.

 We welcome papers from postgraduates / early-career scholars at any stage of their research, that discuss initial research aims/design; engage with methodological and/or theoretical questions; or that draw upon empirical findings. There is no chronological or geographical limit to papers and the sessions will be designed to encourage audience questions, as well as the sharing of experience and advice. In short, this session will demonstrate the vibrancy of postgraduate contributions to Historical Geography.

Our intention is to foster an environment where connections between Masters, PhD students, Early Career Researchers, and the wider community can be made.

 Papers will discuss initial research aims/design, engage with methodological and/or theoretical questions, or that draw upon empirical findings. The sessions will be designed to encourage audience questions, as well as the sharing of experience and ideas. By showcasing the dynamic contributions of postgraduate researchers, we aim to create an inclusive and collaborative space for academic growth.

sessions.title: Restorative in/justice for human and more-than-human entities through past, present and uncertain climate futures
sessions.description:
Recognising and resolving harms and losses associated with climate change and climate in/action is essential for restorative justice. Indeed, both mitigation and adaptation decisions pose a number of justice dilemmas that can lead to unavoidable losses; for example, communities may need to be permanently relocated, development and land rights may need to be restricted in certain locations, and ecosystems may be sacrificed to make way for climate initiatives (Juhola et al., 2022). Yet, despite being essential to the transformative climate justice agenda (See et al., 2024), restorative justice has remained the least studied dimension of climate justice. 

 With empirical research still in its infancy, crucial theoretical and practical questions remain unanswered (Newell et al., 2021). How is restorative in/justice experienced and articulated? How is restorative in/justice re/constructed through multi-scalar governance structures and power dynamics? How should restorative in/justices be redressed, for whom, by whom and through what mechanisms (e.g. reconciliation and repair)? How should the agents of injustice and non/agency be considered in these debates? How are restorative matters mis/recognised and de/legitimised through anticipated futures? How do articulations of, or speculations upon, future action shape our politics of the present? With calls to decentre the human and stand in solidarity with the more-than-human world (Tschakert, 2020; Chao et al., 2022; Sebo, 2022), how might restorative justice for 
nature be voiced and enacted in just transitions? Each of these questions raises interesting ethical and moral questions, as well as theoretical, methodological and empirical challenges. Moreover, the concept draws attention to the temporalities of justice; while ‘re’ evokes retrospective concern with the past, restorative justice should also enable proactive, forward-looking approaches that anticipate and circumvent future harms (See et al., 2024). 

 Engaging with these critical questions, this session will present theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions that advance restorative justice debates from human, non- and more-than-human perspectives, allowing for open discussions afterwards.

papers.title: The Art of Justice? Restorative Futures in the Peruvian and Chilean Ecological Avant-Garde
Literature and the 1953 North Sea Flood: Searching for justice in Hubert Nicholson’s Sunk Island (1956)
Balancing restorative justice for more-than-humans in allocating land for climate adaptation
affiliations: Institute of Landscape Planning, BOKU University

Narrating non-human restorative in/justices on the Norfolk coast
What is a just managed retreat? Future imaginaries and restorative justice in coastal adaptation
Restorative in/justice in urban climate adaptation – reflections from a review of justice frameworks and inclusive adaptation planning in practice

sessions.title: Journal of Geography in Higher Education Lecture: Teaching Place Name Justice Under Pressure: Curriculum Hacking and Infrastructures of Care Between Critical Geography and K–12 Classrooms

sessions.title: Towards just places through children’s geographies in, of, and for, education (2)
sessions.description:
This session invites discussion about the value of, and possibilities for, further engagement with children's geographies in, and of, education, policy and practice. We focus on education as integral to children's everyday lives and their understanding of the world. From lecture halls to forest schools, education experiences unfold across diverse (in)formal and (un)structured spaces. Education spaces serve not only as sites of teaching and learning, but also as spaces of play, socialisation, resistance, and identity formation (McKendrick & Hammond, 2020; Kraftl, 2022; Potter & Cowan, 2020). 

 Yet these educational experiences are shaped by structural forces that create and maintain educational inequalities. School design, curriculum priorities, accountability measures and the legacies of residential schools often work to (re)create and (re)produce (in)equity and (in)justice (Catling & Martin, 2011; Kulz, 2017). We are particularly interested in how structural and institutional conditions and mechanisms intersect and compound across multiple scales, from embodied and everyday experiences in classrooms to regional, national, and global patterns of exclusion and marginalization.

 To explore these matters further, we welcome contributions examining possibilities for children’s geographies  in  education, considering for example, where children are present or absent in curricula or engaged with through pedagogy across different phases and spaces of education (Catling, 2005; Hammond, 2022; Owens et al., 2022). We also welcome contributions that consider children’s geographies  of  education, exploring for example how children - with different identities - experience and imagine material and social spaces of education spaces and systems (schools, homeschooling-pods, forest schools etc) (Holloway et al., 2010; English, 2021; Brown, 2022; Kraftl et al., 2022; Brundrett, Malone & Rowley, 2025). This includes how children navigate and experience unequal access across and as shaped by digital, physical, and environmental factors. We invite contributions from those researching and teaching in higher education, as well colleagues working in K-12 or alternative education spaces. We would be particularly delighted to hear from children and young people themselves through research or direct contribution.   Through this session, we aim to strengthen connections between children's geographies scholarship and educational practice, working toward more just and equitable educational futures.

papers.title: Place Based Education: Mapping our landscape to engage with our present and past
Gender differences in activities and occupations across traditional and egalitarian European countries.
Restoring Sense-Making to Young People (Cyborgs) in Our Techno-Social-Natural World
On the importance of taking young people as political actors seriously in school geography

sessions.title: Towards Geographies of the Middle (2)
sessions.description:
This session explores the middle as a productive, open, and under-theorised conceptual vantage point in geography. It emerges in dialogue with recent calls in the geographies of ageing literature to attend more closely to the middle (Barron, 2025) and middle-age by extension (Lulle, 2024). However, the middle is pertinent far beyond age and ageing literature. This session takes this insight as a starting point, opening the conversation to ‘the middle’ in all its forms and to all (inter)disciplinary vantage points.

 The middle appears in social classifications (mid-career, medium-educated, middle-class) and in spatial logics of scale, proportion, and measurement. It is also profoundly processual . To be in the middle of something (a journey, a text, a negotiation, a household task) is to inhabit unfolding processes, unfinished projects, and open present horizons. The middle is the thick present in which pasts accumulate and futures are actively crafted.

 Theoretically, scholars have often approached the middle indirectly, through concepts such as liminality, waiting, impasse, or transition, foregrounding affective states and moments of change. Methodologically, too, the middle is ever-present but rarely named. Research turns occur mid-project; insights emerge through interruption. Fieldwork, analysis, and writing repeatedly unfold in unsettled mid-states that shape knowledge production in important ways.

 Our aim is to think critically and creatively about what a geography of the middle might look like. We invite abstracts that explore any geography of the middle, including (but not limited to):

 - Life-course and biographical middles, including midlife and middle age, and approaches that think through the middle to reframe youth or later life
- Social, political, and classed middles, such as centrism and political ambivalence, partial citizenship, precarity, mobility, and being ‘caught in the middle’
- Transitional and processual middles, including gender transitions, decarbonisation and socio-technical change, recovery, waiting, and suspension
- Affective and relational middles, such as ambivalence, burnout, care responsibilities, sandwich generations, and negotiated belonging
- Everyday and temporal middles, such as midweek, midday, mid-career, and other rhythms of the ‘thick present’
- Methodological middles, including research conducted mid-project, through interruption, partial findings, or reorientation

 References: 

 Barron, A. (2025). Making sense of ‘middle-age’: thinking from and through the middle. Social & Cultural Geography , pp.1-20.

 Lulle, A. (2024). Midlife Geographies: Changing lifecourses across generations, spaces and time . Bristol. Bristol University Press.

papers.title: Co-ops, landlords, homeowners, and tenants: transitional precarity and the afterlives of London squatting
“Nobody knows when it will end, it’s already part of our daily life”: The spatio-temporalities of being in the middle of constructing Tel Aviv’s Light Rail
Smartphones as unfinished archives: insights from fieldwork with refugees

sessions.title: Writing Minor: Nonrepresentational Inequalities of Asian Diasporas (2)
sessions.description: This session explores the non-representational aspects of Asian diaspora inequalities in two directions. First, we examine existing media portrayals, political discourse, and cultural stereotypes that reduce the polyvocality of Asian diasporic lives and reproduce minority lives as unequally 'minor' (Hong 2020). We look particularly closely at the uneven representations and territorialisations of emotionality, sensibility, and viscerality along lines of race, nationality, and citizenship. Second, we seek alternative, irregular, and 'minor' ways of representing diasporic lives and experiences that challenge these existing discourses and theoretical frameworks. Affirming the force of the minor, we explore creative forms of expression, narratives, and world-making practices that exist in the spaces made within and transcending inequality (Yapp 2021). Overall, this session examines the importance of representational politics while encouraging alternative ways of writing geographical research on diaspora, with special attention to fluid, affective, and experimental approaches. We encourage submissions that engage with Asian diasporas globally, including but not limited to East Asian, Southeast Asian, South Asian, and Central Asian contexts, and welcome comparative and transnational approaches. We also welcome diverse formats of writing, including traditional papers, visual essays, creative-critical hybrids, and collaborative presentations that push the boundaries of conventional academic formats. This session aims to explore directions including, but not limited to: •     Diasporic home and world-making efforts in the interstices of unequal representations •     Diasporic geographies of senses, bodies, and intimacy •     Critical analysis and/or creative re-animations of unequal media representations of diaspora •     Alternative ways of theorising and representing the diaspora as and beyond 'minor' •     Intersectional experiences of inequality within and across Asian diasporic communities (including dimensions of class, gender, sexuality, generation, and ability) •     Historical and contemporary perspectives on Asian diasporic nonrepresentation This second session will include a collective workshop in the end, mapping out common themes and future potentials. References Hong, C.P. (2020) Minor feelings: a reckoning on race and the Asian condition . London: Profile Books. Yapp, H. (2021) Minor China: method, materialisms, and the aesthetic . Durham: Duke University Press.

papers.title: Rethinking Migration in the Half-Second Gap: Affective Engineering, Sonic Denarrativization, and the Asian Diaspora
Collective Workshop: mapping out common themes and future potentials.
affiliations: School of Geographical Sciences Univeristy of Bristol

papers.title: Whose absence haunts the diaspora spaces? : Geographies of non-representation among Indian diaspora in Ireland.
Towards diasporic affect
