sessions.title: Elections and electoral geography in turbulent times
sessions.description:
Despite growing political volatility and far-right advances across many parts of the world, geographers have had surprisingly little to say about what remains the most obvious manifestation and mechanism of these political shifts: democratic elections. This session seeks to stimulate a debate on the significance and dynamics of elections in the current global conjuncture.

 Some twenty years ago, following a period in which the number of nominally democratic systems had dramatically expanded, Barnett and Low (2004) observed “how little processes of democratisation, or democracy as a broader theme, have had on research agendas in human geography”. It is striking that as many democratic systems come under strain today, democracy, at least understood as a set of organised institutions and processes – including elections –, continues to be conspicuously absent from contemporary geographic debates.

 The subdiscipline of electoral geography in particular still struggles to shake off its reputation of an “unrepentant positivism” (Johnson & Warf, 2025), contributing to its status as a minority and largely siloed pursuit within political geography. However, the interactions between social geographies and electoral systems have been extensively theorised, ranging from established debates around the role of “context” and place in moulding political attitudes (Agnew, 1996; Johnston & Pattie, 2004) to more recent exploration of how institutional actors seek to enable or suppress the representation of different groups (Forest, 2018). Meanwhile, some scholars have sought to bring frameworks and themes of broader interest to bear on electoral geography, from gender (Secor, 2004; McGing, 2015) and sexuality (Chapman, 2011) to emotional (Schurr, 2013) and digital geographies (Temple, 2023). While Anglophone electoral geography largely maintains a provincial focus on global North, there has been growing attention to electoral geographies elsewhere (Gethin et al., 2021; Halvorsen, 2020; Richmond & McKenna, 2024).

 Meanwhile, the widespread electoral turbulence of the last decade has drawn attention to the uneven and shifting geographies of support for different political projects. Attention has focused most emphatically on what Rodríguez-Pose (2018) has called “the revenge of places that don’t matter” – the apparent drift of neglected post-industrial regions towards support for nativist parties, embodied paradigmatically by the “revolt of the rustbelt” in the US (McQuarrie, 2017) and the fall of the Labour Party’s “red wall” in the Midlands and North of England (Mackinnon, 2020). However, far more complex and varied shifts in voting patterns can be observed across different contexts, from support for diverse “populisms” across different regions of Italy (Agnew & Shin, 2017; Rossi, 2018) to electoral surges of both right and left in Texas’ border region (Davis, 2020). While geographers have explored the spatial imaginaries and affective registers of populist and far-right movements (Lizotte & Kallio, 2023; Anderson & Secor, 2022), far more could be said about how they interact with elections, understood as routine democratic exercises with their own institutionalised, lived and imagined geographies.

 This session invites contributions that analyse contemporary electoral geographies and geographical insights into the study of elections.

papers.title: Advancements in effectively measuring the Global Rural-Urban Electoral Divide
Exploring Women Candidates’ Experiences on the Campaign Trail: Campaign Ethnographies in the 2024 UK General Election
Gambling on Political Futures: Geographies of Election Betting
Socio-spatial inequality and urban electoral geography: The cases of São Paulo and Rio de Janeiro

sessions.title: Failed practices and places of failure – on the peripheralization of failure
sessions.description:
Failure seems to be pushed to the periphery. Things go wrong, people fail, but this should rather not happen where it matters. What does this reveal about how normality and professionality are thought of and placed? In this session, we build on recent research in geography and beyond that highlights the role of failure as a social phenomenon, as a part of practice, and of place-related normativities. Together, we will explore preconditions and manifestations/localisations of failure, along with its trajectories and (re)presentations. In this session we ask:

 • What role do situational circumstances, structural characteristics and institutions play in the production of failure of people in a marginalized areas?

 • What resources are needed to not fail at the periphery or how could failure be addressed in movements between individual care and systemic resistance?

 • How and in what ways are the lived experiences and multiplicities of failure as a localised and/or mobile phenomenon expressed in everyday practices – including or especially in socio-spatial areas (professional or institutional contexts) that do not appear to have failed, but rather seem to be or present themselves as exemplary or successful?

 • What are potential advantages/disadvantages of failure happening in or being pushed to the margins?

papers.title: Failed Places – On the Production of “places of failure” as a form of territorial stigmatization
When Problems Do (Not) Become Failures: The Peripheralization of Recognition and Accountability
Unpacking The Logistics Town: speculation on warehouses, land and labour
The Renegotiation of Failed Materialities: Ethnographic Insights into Reuse Practices, Deconstruction-sites and Asbestos.

sessions.title: Who cares? What does it mean to care in critical geopolitics?
sessions.description:
"Who cares?" This simple question asks whether people care at all, who is expected or allowed to care, and which issues, places, and lives are rendered worthy of care. As care is unevenly distributed, valued, and governed, it plays a central role in how injustice and inequality are (re)produced across time and space. The notion of care has conventionally been examined within social and healthcare scholarship, focusing on physical health and emotions within caregiving (Skinner & Herron, 2020). Feminist geographical work foregrounded the gendered organisation of care labour and its entanglement with social reproduction and power (McEwan & Goodman, 2010). Subsequent research across moral, social, and rural geographies has extended care ethics to interrogate ethical consumption, human rights, intergenerational relations, the restructuring of care under neoliberal political economies, and collective practices of care as mutual aid during the pandemic (Olson, et al., 2020).

 Despite this rich body of work, care has been less explicitly theorised and explored within critical geopolitics. Political geographers have deeply cared about marginalised spaces and people, precarious lives, and unequal power relations (Campos-Delgado & Côté-Boucher, 2024; McDowell, 2022). Yet little research has explained why people care (or not) about politics and/or political events. In a world marked by the ongoing genocide in Palestine, pandemic afterlives, the rise of right-wing nationalism and anti-migration rhetoric, and Trump’s ‘ego-politics,’ care is unevenly mobilised, fatigued, displaced, or refused. 

 Care in (geo)politics is also deeply affective. While some forms of care are mediated through attentiveness, empathy, and responsibility (Holton, 2025), others emerge through disaffection, boredom, indifference, resignation, or emotional exhaustion (Anderson & Secor, 2025). Not caring, in this sense, is not simply a moral failure but an affective condition shaped by media saturation, crisis fatigue, and structural distance. How would the world continue if geopolitical issues are often perceived as too distant, overwhelming, or normalised to care? At the same time, environmental change foregrounds forms of care that extend beyond human–human relations into more-than-human relational worlds (Krzywoszynska, 2019). Algorithms, artificial intelligence, digital infrastructures, and cybersecurity, raise new ethical questions about care, responsibility, and governance in socio-technical worlds (Maalsen, 2023).

sessions.title: Just Urban Transitions: Geographies of Inequalities and Governance Pathways to Sustainable Futures (2)
sessions.description:
Urban sustainability transitions are urgent, uneven, and deeply political (Goh, 2021; Gonçalves et al., 2025). As cities are increasingly reframed from sites of environmental degradation to key arenas for climate mitigation and adaptation (Parnell, 2016), governments and communities face profound challenges in balancing ecological limits with socio-spatial justice and the creation of liveable urban futures (Kaufmann et al., 2024). These challenges are particularly acute in cities shaped by entrenched inequality, informality, and infrastructural precarity, where climate crises intersect with longer histories of colonial dispossession and uneven development.

 This session critically engages with the concept and practice of Just Urban Transitions. While the term “just transition” emerged from labour struggles linked to energy transitions (McCauley & Heffron, 2018), a growing body of scholarship calls for a more expansive urban lens that foregrounds the full range of resources, services, and opportunities required for urban life, including water, sanitation, housing, mobility, and land (Hughes & Hoffmann, 2020; Maboye et al., 2025).

 Bringing together empirically grounded and conceptually rich contributions, the session examines how just urban transitions are co-produced, governed, contested and lived across diverse urban contexts. Particular attention is given to the governance dilemmas and “complex trade-offs” (van Aswegen & Matamanda, 2025) that emerge as climate mitigation and adaptation efforts intersect with existing forms of precarity and privilege. The session highlights how decision-making draws on both political and technical forms of knowledge across multiple levels and actors, shaping access to basic services and urban opportunities.

 Centred on cities, particularly in the Global South, the session explores urban spaces as complex and dynamic arenas where governance is enacted through state and non-state actors, and where crises of energy, water, housing, employment and mobility are deeply entangled.

 Themes addressed include governance and multi-scalar coordination; justice in climate adaptation and mitigation; everyday practices, informality and hybrid infrastructures; methodological innovations; and rethinking metrics and imaginaries of sustainable urban futures.

papers.title: Pragmatic Infrastructural Governance: Informal Water Connections as a Pathway to Water Security in Khayelitsha
Just transitions at the urban periphery? Examining the implementation of rainwater harvesting to tackle Mexico City’s water crisis
An accidental Just Urban Transition

sessions.title: Driven Out: Municipal displacement and the health consequences of urban restructuring
sessions.description:
How can we make just places when we start from no place? Displacement is a long-established artefact of urban gentrification and is one that is central to any understanding of health inequalities and urban social justice. Displacement as a process is inimical to any notion of justice, involving the classed removal and reordering of people within and beyond given urban settings. Not only is the process in itself unjust, but notwithstanding its long pedigree in the urban studies literature, it continues to exist as a matter of epistemological injustice for practical reasons. We know much of how places change over time, but we know less of the trajectories of individuals who are displaced from them. This has always been an empirical challenge for research but matters now more than ever for the ways in which an increasingly spatially-polarised country is leading to more and more extreme variants of the phenomenon. Across the western world rolling programmes of austerity under successive governments have hit deindustrialised urban areas hardest whilst (globalised) flows of wealth have continued to residualise in increasingly unliveable and unsustainable core metro-regions. This context is also leading to new forms of long-range displacement as local authorities in London and the south-east look to ‘offload’ unwanted populations elsewhere as surplus urban human capital.

 This session will address the manifold and evolving dimensions of displacement, with a focus on the health impacts of various forms of displacement, taking a diversity of stances which will consider both more orthodox short-range and evolving longer-range forms of displacement through both qualitative and quantitative approaches, the contemporary and the historic. We pose the question here: if elective socio-spatial mobility needs to be considered a portable form of capital for the individual, how can we better account for both the personal and collective effects of (forced) displacement on marginalised people and the further marginalised spaces they are always decanted to?

papers.title: "People are just being pushed further and further out": displacement, slow violence, and health inequalities in four English coastal towns
Health narratives and territorial stigma in the gentrification of two neighbourhoods in Middlesbrough, UK
“If you can afford to move, you move.” Residential mobility and regional health inequalities in England
Displacement beyond class: Urban class instability as demographic crisis

sessions.title: Genomic Politics: Precision Breeding, Genetic Engineering, and the Future of a Resilient Food System.
sessions.description:
Conveners : 

 Theo Stanley, University of Southampton 

 Emma Roe, University of Southampton

 Damian Maye, University of Gloucestershire 

 Panel Abstract 

 Recent scientific and biotechnological breakthroughs in genomic sequencing, gene editing, and microbial engineering are driving innovation in industrial food systems. Advances have rendered animal and plant genomes more knowable and more manipulable than ever before. Genetic engineering techniques such as CRISPR promise to unlock higher yields, pest and disease resistance, enhanced nutrition, and greater productivity. This session asks: 

 - What is the potential role of these applied genomic technologies in the food system? 

 - How might they transform the dynamics and interactions which constitute it? 

 - What impact might they have on consumption patterns, environmental degradation, public health, and national food system resilience? 

 Applied genomic technologies are routinely legitimised, naturalised, and normalised through the narratives that they are speeding up “natural” selection and evolution. Genetic engineering is thus framed as a softer and far more politically palatable approach than genetically modified organisms (GMOs) in ethical and regulatory debates. In England (and notably not in Wales and Scotland), the 2023 Precision Breeding Bill distinguishes many organisms produced through precision breeding from GMOs, emphasising that the technology is “a key tool for UK food security”.

 This session critically interrogates how genomic technologies reshape food geographies by asking what kinds of harms, risks, vulnerabilities, and opportunities are produced through a growing reliance on genomic technologies. We wish to understand more about the obscurity and visibility of different genomic technologies – including but not only genetic engineering - that are in use or potentially could come into use in different parts of the food system.

 We welcome papers on a range of topics, including (but not limited to) issues of: biopolitics, metabolic governance, climate and disease resistance, enzymes, animal feed and supplements, precision breeding, GMOs, biosecurity, and the politics of ‘precision’ in contemporary food systems. We encourage applicants to think about how these themes relate to inequality across human and more-than-human divides.

papers.title: Millets, Biofortification, and the Persistence of Nutritionism in Anti-Hunger Politics
Climate Emergency and the Politics of Speed: Gene Editing in Temporal Perspective
Metabolic Resilience in the UK Poultry Industry: A New Frontier in Genomic Biotechnologies
Decoding differently: Gene sequencing and Simondon’s genetic method

sessions.title: Making Home Over Time: How does time and temporality reshape our understanding of home?
sessions.description:
Home is often seen as a stable place, yet it is an unfinished project made through time (Brickell, 2012). Homemaking unfolds through rhythms and routines, life-course transitions, and the shifting work of attachment and repair long emphasised in critical geographies of home (Boccagni, 2014; Merla & Smit, 2023). However, this domestic time is increasingly fractured. It is shaped by migration temporalities that stretch, suspend, or intensify family life across borders (Walsh, 2011; Li, 2025), and by housing trajectories that connect lives across unequal spaces (Brickell et al., 2024; Born et al., 2025). Increasingly, domestic time is mediated by digital systems, from smart home technologies (Gram-Hanssen & Darby, 2018) to rental platforms (Maalsen & Gurran, 2022). These temporal processes shape who can settle, stay, move, or reunite, and under what conditions home can be sustained.

 This session asks: What does it mean to treat time not merely as a backdrop to homemaking, but as a key medium through which home is made and governed? How do housing infrastructures and bordering practices produce uneven temporal conditions for dwelling (e.g., conditions of waiting, interruption, repetition, or acceleration), and how are these temporalities lived, contested, and negotiated in everyday homemaking?

 We use time-making as a broad lens and welcome papers that connect domestic temporal experience to wider political-economic, legal, infrastructural, and technological formations. We also welcome contributions that extend the discussion through life-course perspectives and emerging work on platformed/algorithmic infrastructures. We seek to convene presentations and discussions surrounding:

 - Lifecourse, Age, and Intergenerational Homemaking
- Home as Temporality: Rhythms, Ruptures, and Domestic Time
- Migration, Mobility, and Distributed Homemaking
- Hope, Futures, and the Temporal Politics of Homemaking
- Affective, Gendered, and Embodied Home Spaces

papers.title: How overseas returned talents negotiate multiple identities in everyday home-making in Shanghai China?
affiliations: East China University of Science and Techonology

papers.title: Between Tides and Clock Time: Reconfiguring Domestic Temporality among Boat-Dwellers’ Resettlement in Coastal China
Home is where the hope is: a Photovoice study uncovering women’s experiences of homelessness in London
'Home' at the dinner table: Food and the simultaneity of homes
'Sailing' through Precarity: Home-making of Single Chinese Female Professionals in Tokyo and Shenzhen

sessions.title: Just Transitions in and Beyond China (3)
sessions.description:
The global climate and biodiversity crises cannot be addressed without ‘just transitions’ – profound and far-reaching social reconfigurations that place well-being and social justice at their core. While just transition narratives resonate with activists, scholars, and policy makers across geographies, the concept has gained significantly less traction in China. This represents a critical oversight. First, China is the largest emitter of greenhouse gases (IEA, 2023) and leading nation in renewable electricity capacity additions at the global level (IEA, 2025). China’s increasing influence – economically, politically and ecologically – places the nation in a position to significantly shape global transition trajectories. Second, ongoing and planned environmental reform within China is already producing social impacts (e.g., through the phase-out of fossil fuel industries (Gong and Lewis, 2024) and local, uneven effects of environmental conservation (Wang and Lo, 2022), only likely to deepen as transitions unfold. Third, Chinese flows of investment shape patterns of economic development and socio-spatial configurations in locations around the world (Apostolopoulou et al., 2024), with highly uneven and uncertain outcomes. Finally, theoretical frameworks on ‘justice’ (social, environmental, political, and beyond) may require re-conceptualisation in relation to China’s socio-cultural and political context. Mainstream understandings remain shaped by liberal-democratic traditions, which may constrain examinations of transitions in China and China-connected spaces (Wang and Lo, 2021).

 Mapping and exploring how just transitions unfold within and beyond China is therefore an urgent research agenda. This panel engages with multiple dimensions of these dynamics, including but not limited to:

 - Social justice and socio-economic impacts of transition-related policies and reforms in China , including uneven outcomes of industrial restructuring, labour displacement, infrastructure investment, and urban-rural inequality. This includes studies on environmental justice dimensions of transitions, such as uneven impacts of environmental policies and programmes of ecological conservation.

 - Just transition outcomes of Chinese overseas investment , such as renewable energy projects, forestry/afforestation, and mining of critical minerals. We especially invite contributions that unpack the complex dynamics at play in global supply chains and the decoupling of impacts across locations of resource extraction and product deployment.

 - Chinese discourse of justice and just transition , including studies that explore contemporary policy narratives and public debates and their approaches to justice, as well as critiques of Anglophone conceptions of these debates. We also welcome work that engages wider and diverse forms of justice and just transition issues across China and China-connected spaces.

papers.title: Remaking Verticality: Repair, Temporality, and Urban-Rural Inequality in Kangding, Southwestern China
affiliations: London School of Economics and Political Science

papers.title: Rethinking neighborhood participation through everyday practice and participation infrastructure: evidence from Chengdu
Exploring the role of temporary urbanism in urban regeneration within the context of China’s urban transformation: the shift from a focus on quantity to a focus on quality

sessions.title: Platform tourism and the making of home: reproductive labour, domestic imaginaries and digital lives
sessions.description:
Short-term
 rental platforms represent one of the most consequential tourism 
phenomena over the last two decades. Research has linked platforms such 
as Airbnb, VRBO, and Booking.com to a range of socio-spatial 
inequalities, particularly affecting housing affordability and 
availability. However, many aspects of temporary renting are often 
neglected, particularly those that affect its impact on the institution 
of home and reproductive labour. 

 Short-term
 rental platforms are a multiscalar and home-making phenomenon that 
actively shape domestic spaces. These practices engage emotions, values,
 and discourses related to home, with particular emphasis on the 
gendered organisation of everyday life. They reflect a fantasy of “being
 at home” worldwide and commodify homely experiences and atmospheres. 
More research is needed to understand how platforms influence 
perceptions of home, inhabitation and reproductive labour. 

 The
 session examines the intersection of short-term rental platforms with 
labour, the home and the everyday lives of those who inhabit them. It 
explores how digital platforms mediate private spheres and shape 
domestic imaginaries. Drawing on feminist and digital geographies, it 
aims to extend critical scholarship on short-term rental platforms.

papers.title: When Homes Become Hotels: Platform Economies and the Material Transformation of Domestic Spaces
‘Don’t forget, you are guests’: Airbnb, the homely and the ‘uncanny’.
Platform Tourism and the Making of Home in Central São Paulo - Brazil
Negotiating Hospitality and Boundary Work in the Airbnb Home

sessions.title: Social and Cultural Geographies in Policy and Practice: A Practical Workshop
sessions.description:
In November 2025, the Social and Cultural Geographies Research Group (SCGRG) held an online event to explore the translation of academic social and cultural geography into policy and practice. This workshop at AC2026 seeks to continue this conversation, inviting participants to respond to the reflections that emerged from our November event and contribute their own. No experience of having worked at the policy or practice interface is required in order to participate, and you don’t need to have attended the first event; rather, our aim in facilitating this ongoing conversation is to distil key guidance for social and cultural geographers seeking to engage policymakers or practitioners in future. 

 This workshop will incorporate a World Café in which participants will be invited to review and comment on the guidance emerging from the conversation so far. Participants will also have the opportunity to apply their learning from the session by planning their own preliminary steps in engaging with policy or practitioner audiences. Our aim as session convenors is to learn from, and with, participants in order to inform development of resources to support social and cultural geographers at all career stages with policy and practitioner engagement. 

 Workshop discussion will cover topics including:

 - How to begin thinking about how to connect your research with policy or practice
- How to bridge methodological divides between contemporary geographical research and external contexts with different epistemological cultures
- How your research might play a constructive part in critiquing policy or practice, or challenging its processes or parameters, in ways that might ultimately improve them
- How to ‘speak the language’ of policy and practice

sessions.title: The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route (2)
sessions.description:
This panel session brings together contributions from the ERC-project “The Game: Counter-mapping Informal Refugee Mobilities along the Balkan Route” to present its preliminary findings and foster critical discussion and exchange. The project constitutes the first transnational, multi-sited, and extensive qualitative study of the so-called “Balkan Route”, which is today the most important overland informal migration corridor in Europe, taken by thousands of refugees every year. Linking Greece to Western Europe, across Bulgaria, Romania, Albania, North Macedonia, Kosovo, Montenegro, Serbia, Bosnia-Herzegovina, Slovenia, and Croatia, the Route is a complex geography of formal and informal, visible and invisible sites; an assemblage of diverse actors, camps, borders, violence, solidarity; and a multiplicity of micro-routes, constantly adapting and shifting. Across and between these interconnected spatialities, refugees forge their trajectories towards Europe through The Game, the term they use to refer to their clandestine journeys and border crossings. Through The Game, refugees themselves produce a counter-geography that consists of a constellation of strategically organised refugee spatialities and imaginative geographies, marked by the hopes and ambitions of seeking a better life in Europe as well as by the pain and fear experienced through perilous journeys. Across the precarious, ambivalent, and ever-changing circumstances of the Balkan Route, an archipelago of formal and makeshift camps has emerged, hosting thousands of refugees as they make repeated attempts to enter the EU. Key in producing and sustaining The Game, these sites serve as temporary shelters, nodes of services and information, where refugees meet smugglers, wait, and plan the next move. 

 Drawing on the project team’s rich, multi-sited ethnographic research, this panel session interrogates the complex counter-geographies of the Balkan Route by investigating its distinct spatialities, archipelago of camps, informal mobilities, ambivalent legal geographies, and border geopolitics.

 The panel is organised into two sessions. The first session brings together contributors from the edited volume “Thinking Like a Route: Counter-geographies of Informal Migration in the Balkans”, who will reflect on their chapters while outlining the subsequent developments of their research. The second session features empirically grounded presentations by four researchers of the project conducting fieldwork at key junctures along the Balkan Route, including Austria, Greece, North Macedonia, and Slovenia.

papers.title: Navigating the Eastern Aegean Border Archipelago: Methodologies of encounter on the move
Nomoscapes of Crisis: Governing Invisible Mobilities along the Balkan Route
Walking methodologies and “jungles” on the Balkan Route
Where does the ‘Balkan Route’ provisionally end? The spatial politics of (im)mobility in Vienna.

sessions.title: Co-creating Coastal Resilience: Participation, collaboration, and cross-sector working for just coastal futures (2)
sessions.description:
Coastal risk is not simply the outcome of sea-level rise, erosion, flooding or pollution. It is also produced through the politics of governance – who defines risk, whose knowledge counts, who is allowed in the room, and how responsibility is allocated, shared or quietly offloaded across sectors and scales. As coastal hazards increasingly intersect with housing shortages, ageing infrastructure, habitat loss, public health crises and fragile local economies, collaborative working has become a central expectation within shoreline management and flood risk governance (Sayers et al., 2022; Lane et al., 2011). Yet in practice, participation and partnership is often expensive, slow, fragile and messy – shaped, and sometimes derailed, by uneven capacity, institutional inertia, funding constraints and histories of mistrust (Blunkell, 2017).

 This pair of linked sessions brings together researchers, partners and community collaborators involved in the Resilient Coastal Communities and Seas (ReCCS) programme, a £15m UKRI–Defra programme involving 100+ co-leads and charged with doing something ambitious: generating knowledge, action and resilience for UK coastal communities and seas in just 5 years. At the programme’s midpoint, these sessions offer space not for polished narratives, but for honest, critical and constructive reflections
on what it really means to attempt participatory coastal research at scale and across sectors with diverging priorities, constraints and cultures.

papers.title: Engaging with coastal communities and waste legacies: Reflections from an interdisciplinary research team
Coast-R Panel Discussion
Learning from Past Marine Energy Transitions: Insights for Just Coastal Futures
Coastal Legacy Landfills: perceptions, management, engagement and risks behind the UK’s silent climate timebomb. An overview of a PhD project
Shifting Shorelines: A Serious Game to Capture Qualitative and Lived Experiences for Inclusive Coastal Resilience Planning

sessions.title: What does landscape mean in my research? Can it be captured in a photograph?
sessions.description:
In this session, we will convene a roundtable and open discussion about the use of photography in landscape geography research. As ideas of landscape are often considered or interpreted as visual elements, we ask how we as researchers use photography as a means of communicating the research itself, the subject matter, or as some other form of representation or methodological approach. We will also explore if the visual nature of representing our research – and how this changes – is something that we as landscape geographers need to strengthen in our toolkit?
 Members of the Landscape Geography Working Group will convene this roundtable. discussion Dr Nicola Dempsey (University of Sheffield), Dr Meredith Whitten (University College London), Dr Daniel Bos (University of Chester) will be in conversation with Jamie Owen from RGS. 

 Calling on examples from specific projects and practices, we will explore how researchers can consider aesthetics, ethics and the methodological underpinnings of photography in our research. We will also gain insight from Jamie who organises the Society’s international photography competition, Earth Photo. 

 The discussion will consider aspects and use of photography in research such as involving (or not) research participants or co-creators. This might be photo-elicitation based on our own photographs taken as a researcher or based on those of participants. It might be photography as a means of documentation, record-making or evaluation combined with activities such as walking, watching or monitoring e.g. seasonal change. How researchers consider the representation and symbolic dimensions of photography will be explored to consider how we acknowledge our practices and processes of image creation and production. 

 The session will take place in person. The session is linked to the Landscape Geography Working research group photography exhibition when the entries for the competition will be displayed, discussed as part of a wider roundtable discussion. The session will end with the announcement of the competition winners.

 Some reading

 Oldrup, H.H. and Carstensen, T.A. (2012) Producing geographical knowledge through visual methods, Geografiska Annaler: Series B, Human Geography, 94:3, 223-237, DOI: 10.1111/j.1468-0467.2012.00411.x

 Rose, M., Heddon, D., Qualmann, C., Wilson, H., & Maggie, O. (2022) The Walkbook: Recipes for Walking and WellBeing. https://walkcreate.gla.ac.uk/the-walkbook/

sessions.title: “Are you doing your work?” Care-full methods and ethics of care in (and beyond) the field (2)
sessions.description:
Care is now firmly on the agenda in human geography. Yet too often, it appears as a topic to be studied, rather than an ethic that is actively practiced (Middleton & Samanani, 2021). When Audre Lorde asked the question, “Are you doing your work?” She was referring to the introspection and deeper conversations that need to be had to confront how structures of power shape embodied experiences. The understanding of care not simply as a social practice or as a concept but as an ethic has been taken up by Lawson (2007, 2009), who argues programmatically for geographers to take up a “critical care ethics for our epistemological, ontological, methodological, and daily life practices (as professionals and citizens)” (2007, p. 3). Caring is hard work. As an ethic, it forces us to become entangled and complicates our relationships in the field: with communities, interlocutors/friends, research assistants, and ourselves. Sometimes it is discomfiting. Omer Aijazi (2024, p. 178) asks, “what does thinking from the heart offer? What if we see the world this way?” In this panel, we examine how treating care as both an ethic and a form of relational labour reshapes the everyday work of research and fieldwork.

 This session asks a simple, uncomfortable, hopeful, and necessary set of questions: Are we doing our work with care? What is an ethics of care, and how might it show up in the everyday practices of fieldwork, writing, and collaboration? What might it enable (or constrict) when we foreground an ethics of care in our methodological and personal approaches? When does care become a method? Can it disrupt ‘extractivist’ models of research? What do researchers refuse in the name of care? And what refusals are rendered impossible? What happens when care is betrayed? We invite papers that examine the practical, emotional, and moral consequences of care in the field and beyond, focusing on the relationships through which care is enacted: with research assistants, interlocutors/friends, family, community members, and other collaborators involved in the research process. We highly encourage contributions from scholars working in or with contexts in the Majority World and from precarious academic contexts. Particular attention will be paid to works grounded in feminist, decolonial, queer, anti-caste, Southern, and indigenous approaches. 

 This session is conceived as the foundation for a special issue on care-full methodologies . Contributors will be invited to develop their papers into a collective publication that advances practical, ethical, and critical approaches to conducting research with care. This is the second part of a 2 part discussion.

papers.title: To Care and Be Cared For: Gendered Relational Labour in Ethnographic Fieldwork with Women Workers in Delhi
Co-creative Care: Exploring Complexities and Possibilities through Reflexive, Relational Practice
Beyond positionality, between researcher/practitioner: Embodied modes of capturing, analysing, representing, and translating dance
Shared Rhythms, Situated Ethics: Learning to Care with “Dancing Aunties” in Shanghai
Additive Research Methodologies: A Prefigurative Care-Full Approach to Participatory Research Design
Khyaal Rakhna Unka [Take Care of Him]: Care-full relations and writing with Care

sessions.title: Cities of hope: Urban citizenship and social solidarity in troubled times
sessions.description:
As cities around the world find themselves under threat from attacks on civil liberties, authoritarian violence and even complete destruction in the case of Rafah and Jabalya, while the impacts of global pandemics such as Covid-19 continue to be felt especially among the poorest and most vulnerable urban communities, how do everyday practices of humanity, dignity and solidarity help to forge a collective sense of hope for a better future?

 From the streets of Minneapolis to Mumbai hope signals the motivation for ethical activity in moments of crisis and breakdown (Zigon, 2018). It has, thus, been understood as not only about looking forward to a better future but as Les Back (2021:5) writes, hope “is an attention to the present and the anticipation that something unexpected will happen and emerge from its ruins”. Hope is a set of dispositions, including the ensemble of knowledge, embodied inclinations, and affective investment that condition practices and are conditioned by them (Jansen, 2016). In operationalising hope as the collective transformation of the urban through new solidarities and imagined communities (Roy et al 2022), hope can be seen as both relational and institutional - drawing on individual emotions and aspirations as well as a collective (re)envisioning of the future alongside state-centred demands on the right to the city.

 A geographical re-imagining of hope could focus attention on “those moments when, regardless of status or substance, subjects constitute themselves as citizens” (Isin & Nielsen, 2008: 18). These reflections on hope and citizenship assume poignant intellectual and practical significance in the context of the troubled times in which cities and their residents find themselves.

papers.title: City without Civility:  Being Outcastes in Ahmedabad
Making Time: Enchronic Practices and Urban Belonging in Athens
Migration, Hope, and Resilience: Migrant Workers in Kochi, Kerala
Exiting authoritarianism for London's surplus of hope: Trajectories of out-migrants from Turkish metropoles to London

sessions.title: ‘On mission?’: Understanding the geographies of clean energy growth and development (2)
sessions.description:
Building on a long period of cross-party political consensus in the UK, which included establishing its world leading legally binding net zero 2050 target, in 2024 Prime Minister Starmer announced that ‘Making Britain a Clean Energy Superpower’ was one of the six milestones for his new mission-led government (UK Govt 2024). Alongside delivering energy security, reducing consumer bills and reaching net zero, the Clean Energy Superpower Mission promised to drive “investment, good jobs and growth across the country” (UK Govt 2024a). 

 Like the preceding Conservative Government’s Clean Growth (2017) and Powering Up Britain (2023) strategies, clean energy is seen as offering not only significant national economic returns but also the promise of more regionally balanced growth (Curran et al 2022). Such rebalancing, ‘levelling-up’, or ‘realising potential’ will, by its very nature, require “leveraging the clean energy transition to turbocharge growth” (UK Govt 2025b) across poorer and more peripheral areas of the UK. In particular, industrial heartlands and coastal communities have been identified as areas with “enormous opportunity to build the energy industries of the future and create the good jobs at good wages that people have long demanded” (UK Govt 2025b p.4). Consequently, clean energy has become central to the visions and economic development policy lexicon of many peripheral and old industrial regions.

 This session uses the bold ambitions of the UK’s mission-led approach, as a point of departure, to reflect on what progress we have made, and what challenges still exist, in understanding the geographies of clean energy growth and their contributions to regional development. Considerable conceptual and empirical contributions have been made to this agenda in recent years from work within and across inter alia energy geographies (e.g. Bridge and Gailing 2019); EEG and Green Path Development (e.g. Dawley et al 2025); Green Innovation and Technological Diversification (e.g. Moreno and Ocampo-Corrales 2022); Geographies of Sustainability Transitions (e.g. Binz et al 2025); Regional Innovation Systems (e.g. Jakobsen et al 2022) and Global Production Networks (e.g. Coe and Gibson 2023). However, how do these promising contributions help us understand, or even inform, the geographical opportunities and challenges facing supra-national (e.g. EU); national; and local and regional clean energy growth visions, policies and strategies (e.g. Coenen et al 2021; Trippl 2025). Equally, as the political consensus surrounding Clean Power and Net Zero comes under greater challenge internationally, including within the UK itself, we also look for insights into how we can understand the implications for localities and regions facing increased uncertainty and risk around clean energy investment from both state and industry.

 Themes could include, but are not limited to, international perspectives and policy insights related to: 

 - What forms of local and regional development are emerging around clean power ? How are these being manifested in local and regional visions and futures?
- To what extent does the growth of clean power disrupt or reinforce existing forms of uneven development?
- How effective are existing and emerging forms of governance in delivering the development of clean power across, and within, regions and localities?

papers.title: Going Beyond Clusters?: The challenge of reconfiguring energy landscapes for Carbon Capture and Storage (CCS) dispatchable power
Sectoral Tectonics: Energy Governance Logics and the Electrification of Transport
affiliations: Institute for Transport Studies, University of Leeds

sessions.title: Contesting Housing Injustice: Mobilising Materials of Concern (2)
sessions.description:
Mould has recently become a potent symbol of domestic inequality in postwelfare states (Kane, 2023; Shurety, 2025; Storer et al., 2025). Similar to other uncharismatic intrusions, mould reveals a material dimension to longstanding geographical concerns for the domestic as a site of struggle, permeated by, and very much a part of, discriminatory ideologies and sociopolitical forces (Blunt and Dowling, 2006; Brickell, 2012; Massey, 1994; Sibley, 1995; Speer 2017). But mould is not just a site of despair, it is also political – this substance has simultaneously been mobilised by activists to articulate reform to discriminatory and neglectful modes of housing provisioning (NEF, 2024). Within the still unfolding mould epidemic, materiality bleeds into metaphor –mould renders visible structural and historical processes of marginalisation lost in technocratic and austere planning regimes (Simpson et al., 2025).

 Mouldy struggles not only reveal any bounded notion of home to be a bourgeois fantasy (Kaika, 2004), but politicise the unequal risks and labours sprung from disrupted socio-natural boundaries over which tenants have little control. Whilst Kaika writes of water, we might add concerns relating to a multiplicity of transgressive organic and chemical matter – mould, pests, pollutants – all which enter, grow with, leak from the lining of, and cohabitate our homes at markedly uneven rates. Indeed, precursors to the politicisation of mould can be found in struggles for housing justice which centre cladding (Smith, 2023), lead (Fennel, 2015), dust (Noterman, 2023), and pollution (Schneller et al., 2022). These non-human and uncharismatic intrusions reshape inhabitation, carrying the potential to destabilise security and even to unmake the home under regimes of precarity (Baxter and Brickell, 2014).

 A growing chorus of empirical studies draws our attention to racialised-classed logics which shape the distribution of matter out of place (Douglas, 1966). This panel is interested in how people live with, and contest uncharismatic, toxic, or ‘feral’ non-humans and materials. It explores the interface between lived experience and the work of contesting potentially harmful materials – in the practical and evidentiary practices through which matter becomes concerning within and beyond domestic space. 

 We are interested in the processes through which people, communities and activists ‘scale’ with uncanny domestic intrusions to confront grinding forces of in-situ dispossession. We likewise welcome coalitional scholarship which traces political contestations which form around materials – both in early struggles for recognition where harm is often contested and contentious (Murphy, 2006), and where later, following the “designation of the unseeable” (ibid: 9), matter comes to be used to make claims against hostile and dismissive states, landowners, and institutions for fairer and juster habitation. We hope to draw together scholars engaged in activist work, or studying the quieter work of persevering in conditions of housing distress amidst biomedical, legal and regulatory structures which limit claims and compensation.

papers.title: Airy infrastructure: Decarbonising residential densification
"A horrible dank ... rotting": Renting with Mould in London
Caring with and against the breakdown. Maintenance and repair under conditions of neglect
‘London housing means mice’: contesting the everyday injustice of domestic pest infestation

sessions.title: Knowing the City: A Dialogues in Urban Research Panel on the Epistemological Turn in Urban Geography
sessions.description:
Over the past two decades, urban geography has been confronting a range of epistemological questions. Scholars have routinely questioned core concepts, critiqued where and by whom knowledge is produced, debated the kinds of knowledge that should be generated, and argued over how best to reconcile diverse approaches within the sub-discipline. In response, several recent efforts have sought to reconstruct urban geography’s approach to knowledge production (e.g. conjuncturalism, fragmentation, singularity). These debates have unfolded alongside broader societal shifts that have undermined trust in academic knowledge and devalued the idea of truth itself. The stakes of current epistemological work in urban geography are therefore high. This panel brings together scholars to reflect on the epistemological orientations shaping urban geography today.

 Questions to be discussed include:

 - Do our epistemological concerns make the present moment exceptional?

 - How should urban geographers respond to the so-called “post-truth” moment?

 - Are there ways to bring pluralistic epistemological projects into dialogue—and is this necessary or desirable?

 - What have urban geographers turned away from, and how should we think about intellectual inheritance and legacy at this moment?

 - How should urban geographers navigate a world in which evidence and affect are both central to claim-making?

 - Is some degree of epistemological alignment necessary for a sub-discipline to function?

 The session invites panelists to reflect on their own navigation of the epistemological turn and to consider how their work can contribute to the collective project of producing geographical knowledge about “the urban.”

papers.title: How to know the digital city?
Who knows the city?
The New Urban Aesthetic: the experiential turn and power in the digitally-mediated city
The epistemic turn in urban studies
Knowing in fragments

sessions.title: Journal of Geography in Higher Education Reception: Celebrating 50 years of JGHE
sessions.description:
Come and join members of the editorial board and friends of the Journal of Geography in Higher Education to celebrate 50 years promoting, enhancing and sharing geography learning and teaching in all institutions of higher education throughout the world. 
 The journal provides a forum for geographers and others, regardless of their specialisms, to discuss common educational interests, to present the results of educational research, and to advocate new ideas.
 The reception follows the Journal of Geography in Higher Education, Teaching Place Name Justice Under Pressure: Curriculum Hacking and Infrastructures of Care Between Critical Geography and K–12 Classrooms ’ by Derek H. Alderman, Department of Geography & Sustainability, University of Tennessee, Knoxville USA

sessions.title: Authors meet critics: Geoffrey DeVerteuil and Andrew Power's Social Geographies: Relationalities, Encounters and Hope
sessions.description: This session brings together the authors of Social Geographies: Relationalities, Encounters and Hope (Geoffrey DeVerteuil and Andrew Power) for an evening “author meets critics” discussion of the book’s key themes and contributions. The book explores how relational thinking, everyday encounters, and practices of care and solidarity shape social geographies, while also reflecting on the possibilities for wellbeing and hope in contemporary social life. Drawing on diverse empirical and theoretical contributions, it considers how relationships between people, places and environments are continually made and remade through everyday interactions.Following a short overview of the book by the authors, invited discussants Eleanor Jupp and Gavin Andrews will offer critical reflections on the book’s themes, before opening the discussion to the audience. The session aims to generate a lively conversation about the future directions at the intersection between social and health/wellbeing geographies and the role of relational approaches in understanding contemporary challenges in people's lives.

sessions.title: The State of Geography in HE UK
sessions.description: This panel builds on the State of Geography in UK HE report (published February 2026). The report summarises key findings from a snapshot ‘State of Geography’ survey, of all institutions in the UK who offer Higher Education geography degrees. It identified that smaller non-Russell Group institutions are encountering the greatest pressure, large Russell Group institutions in London or the south are expanding, there are uneven student admission changes, and very few institutions have closed their geography programmes but two thirds of geography programmes are reducing the number of optional modules available to students. Many institutions are struggling to retain field class teaching and operating with reduced academic staff numbers, and reduced Professional Service staff and support. A majority of institutions reported increased academic staff workloads, though many are managing to retain research time (albeit in practice research time is shrinking). In this panel we will explore (a) what can we take away from the results that are important for the discipline? and (b) what collective action, response, ways forward are there that will support the future of geography in the UK?

sessions.title: Geographies of Reparatory Justice for Historical Enslavement
sessions.description:
This session seeks to examine reparatory justice for historical enslavement from explicitly geographical perspectives. As Araujo’s (2017) recent book, Reparations for Slavery and the Slave Trade: A Transnational and Comparative History, 
makes clear, there has been a long history of claims for reparation for historical enslavement. While highlighting the range and diversity of these claims, the book at the same time reveals further opportunities to examine their historical geographies. The 21 st century resurgence of interest in reparatory justice emphasises that the issue is far from in the past. A whole series of popular, academic and political developments, informed by on-going enslaved descendant activism, has brought reparatory justice into the spotlight once more. These range from critiques of celebratory commemorations of abolition (Bressey, 2009) and the Black Lives Matter movement’s protests against racial injustice, to calls for reparatory dialogue and action from governments of nations negatively impacted by European chattel enslavement, notably CARICOM (2014) and Commonwealth leaders in 2024. A spectrum of reparatory justice scholarship exists, examining different meanings and dimensions of reparatory thought (Schwarz, 2022) and action (Beckles, 2013; Shepherd and Reid, 2019), with ‘reparatory history’ practice set out by Hall (2018) as an important strand of reparatory justice. At the academic institutional level, reparatory justice has been supported through the Universities Studying Slavery (USS) consortium established in early 2016, a network with US roots which has expanded more recently to Europe (Beckles 2019; Zacek, 2025).

 This session will explore understanding of the ways in which geography matters to reparatory justice, including situated and institutional understandings of reparatory justice past and present, historical geographies of reparatory justice, geographies of reparatory justice in practice and universities and reparatory justice in international context. 
 References 

 Araujo, A L (2017). Reparations for Slavery and the Slave Trade : a transnational and comparative history. Bloomsbury Academic.

 Beckles, H M (2013) Britain’s Black Debt: Reparations for Caribbean slavery and native genocide. University of the West Indies Press.

 Beckles, H M (2019) The Reparation Movement: Greatest Political Tide of the Twenty-first Century. Social and Economic Studies , 68(3&4) 11-30.

 Bressey, C (2009) The Legacies of 2007: Remapping the Black Presence in Britain. Geography Compass , 3(3) 903–917. 

 CARICOM (2014) CARICOM Ten Point Plan for Reparatory Justice. https://caricom.org/caricom-ten-point-plan-for-reparatory-justice/

 Hall, C (2018) Doing Reparatory History: Bringing ‘race’ and slavery home. Race & Class , 60 (1), 3–21.

 Schwarz, K (2022) Reparations for Slavery in International Law: Transatlantic Enslavement, the Maangamizi, and the Making of International Law . New York, NY: Oxford University Press.

 Shepherd, V A & Reid, A (2019) Women, Slavery and the Reparation Movement in the Caribbean. Social and Economic Studies , 68(3&4) 31–59.

 Zacek, N (2025) The Price of Knowledge: universities and slavery in Anglo-American perspective. American Nineteenth Century History 
26(1) 83-101.

sessions.title: Uneven Development: Decolonial, Feminist and Alternative Perspectives (1)
sessions.description:
In the face of daunting global challenges – including climate change, biodiversity loss, disruptive technologies, the ascendency of authoritarianism and political cynicism, and geopolitical tensions and conflicts – the orthodox paradigm of development based on growth and modernization, driven by innovation and technological advancement, is increasingly challenged. More and more, the fundamental question is raised whether this model of development is leading us astray, instead of offering a way forward, and is actually an important part of the causes for our current predicament (ss Morales, Evenhuis and Sariego-Kluge, 2025).

 This is especially true for the challenge of uneven development and extensive inequities across space. With the orthodox model of development came a belief that the benefits drawn from growth and ongoing modernization will eventually reach the wider society. Yet, this “trickle down” promise has not materialised. Instead, spatial inequalities are widening across the globe along many dimensions (Lobao and Hooks, 2025), starkly visible in many parts of the Global South, and increasingly exposed within the Global North, revealing its multiple “souths”. These inequalities are reshaping political landscapes, fuelling exclusionary and destabilising political movements and harming the progress of sustainable transformations.

 Yet, geographers have long shown that the “trickle down” effect is unlikely to occur without intentional intervention (Hudson, 2007; Werner, 2016; Peck, 2019). Moreover, geographical research reveals that “the economy” is not a singular, coherent entity. Rather, it comprises diverse practices, relations and arrangements that coexist and interact across places. Therefore, understanding spatial uneven development requires perspectives that go beyond standard economic metrics. To better understand spatial inequality, and imagine meaningful interventions, we need approaches that reconfigure or expand how we conceptualise the relationship between economy and space.

 Current ecological, social, and geopolitical shifts invite a renewed perspective on how economic processes interact with spatial structures and how these interactions contribute to the production and persistence of spatial inequalities. These contexts highlight the need to examine the diverse ways economic activity relates to place, to consider multiple economic logics operating across space, and to explore a wider set of conceptual tools for analysing these dynamics. This includes approaches that foreground feminist, decolonial, ecological, and other alternative understandings of economic life and its spatial expressions. Therefore, we invite papers that:

 - Reimagine the relationship between economy and space through alternative, feminist, decolonial, or other lenses
- Examine how spatial inequalities are created, reproduced, and transformed from the points of view of decolonial, feminist and alternative approaches
- Examine plural or non-capitalist economic practices and their spatial implications
- Offer conceptual, methodological, or empirical insights into how economies operate differently across places
- Explore the ethical, political, or ecological dimensions of economic activities and propose new frameworks for analysing or intervening in spatial inequality

sessions.title: Challenging racism in challenging times: geographies of anti-racism (1)
sessions.description:
This panel examines the geographies of racism and anti-racism in the context of widening inequalities and ongoing struggles for just places. 

 Racism remains a deeply spatialised and systemic form of inequality, shaping access to education, health, wellbeing, housing, mobility, safety, and civic participation. Experiences of racism also undermine social inclusion, trust, and senses of belonging, producing unjust places in which equality of opportunity and dignity are unevenly distributed. From neighbourhoods and institutions to online spaces and national imaginaries, racism is embedded in the social, political, and spatial organisation of everyday life. 

 At the same time, emerging racial justice and dignity frameworks provide new ways of conceptualising and evaluating anti-racism efforts—foregrounding whether people feel that they belong, feel safe and respected in the places they inhabit. These approaches align closely with geographical concerns about place-making, power, and justice.

 However, we are currently experiencing challenging times for anti-racism work. Heightened geo-political tensions, political leadership that normalises or sanctions racism and prejudice, and the growing influence of populist and right-wing discourses have reshaped public debate and policy landscapes. New technologies, digital platforms, and online cultures have super charged the spread of racist and extremist attitudes, while strained relationships across cultural groups and with key institutions has weakened allyship and solidarity. Critical, place-based, and action-oriented anti-racism scholarship can make racism more difficult. This will improve community relations, expand citizenship, and build public confidence in diversity and immigration.

 This session will examine the challenges facing anti-racism work , while also offering insights into effective, innovative, or hopeful forms of anti-racist action across different spatial, institutional, and political contexts. Collectively, the session aims to advance geographical understandings of racism and anti-racism, and to contribute to debates on how more just, inclusive, and dignified places might be imagined and enacted.

papers.title: Whiteness and Anti-Asian Racism as Entangled Global Force: Media Discourses in the US, Australia, and Germany
Allyship and the activation of anti-racist geographies: prodding the state into action
Advancing the Measurement of Structural Racism in Housing: A Conceptual Framework
Hoping for Tremors: Doing Anti-Racism Work in the Neoliberal University
Attitudes and behaviours that inform white victimhood narratives, white privilege and claims of anti-white racism

sessions.title: Beyond Access: Lived Experience and Disability Justice in Tourism and Leisure Spaces (1)
sessions.description:
Tourism provides significant potential for well-being, social connection, and leisure engagement. However, these benefits are not equally distributed or accessible across the population. This session moves “beyond access” to centre the rich diversity of lived experience and the transformative framework of disability justice within tourism and leisure geographies. Directly addressing the conference theme, “Geographies of Inequality,” this session explores what it truly means to establish tourism and leisure spaces that are not simply compliant, but are genuinely welcoming, equitable, and inclusive of human diversity.

 This session aims to bridge a connection between scholarship on embodiment and affect andcritical principles of disability justice. Drawing on this framework that demands not just entry, but also equitable participation, intersectional analysis, and collective liberation, we invite papers that prioritize the knowledge and experiences of disabled people to explore how leisure spaces are navigated, contested, and re-imagined. We are particularly interested in research that moves beyond a singular focus on physical impairment to embrace neurodiversity, sensory worlds, and the complex interplay of multiple social identities.

 Potential topics include, but are not limited to: • Critiques of checklist-style accessibility and the pursuit of belonging, dignity, and joy. • The sensory, affective, and embodied geographies of navigating, negotiating, or avoiding tourist destinations. • Intersectional analyses where disability meets race, gender, queerness, and class in leisure contexts. • Disability activism, community-led tourism, and the co-design of just and inclusive spaces. • Re-imagining heritage and nature-based tourism through a disability justice lens. • The role of digital platforms and media in fostering justice.

sessions.title: Social Infrastructures for Net Zero: Enabling just place-based decarbonisation (1)
sessions.description:
As a particular political and economic project, Net Zero prioritises market-based mechanisms and large-scale technological interventions whilst leaving the social infrastructures essential for collective climate action under-resourced. This continues a longer trajectory whereby decades of austerity, privatisation, and the extension of market logics have systematically dismantled the spaces, resources, and time necessary for collective organising—rendering invisible the care work, skill-sharing, and relational practices upon which sustainable transitions fundamentally depend.

 This panel challenges these narrow framings by foregrounding social infrastructure as a critical yet marginalised dimension of sustainability transitions. We conceptualise social infrastructure not merely as physical spaces that support collective action, but as relational processes constituted through labour, care, and collective actions. Social infrastructures operate bidirectionally: they enable the collective action necessary for decarbonisation, and are co-produced with Net Zero agendas. Community energy projects, retrofit programmes, and grassroots climate organising all depend upon—and simultaneously create—networks of care, skill-sharing, resource exchange, and collective mobilisation. 

 The panel explores the multi-scalar relationships between decarbonisation and justice, examining how these operate from household to neighbourhood to national levels. We consider how existing community mobilisations can be supported and enhanced, whilst ensuring that place-based needs and diverse forms of community organising are recognised and resourced. Crucially, we examine: What are the social infrastructures of just, rapid and deep decarbonisation, what enables and sustains it? By making visible the gendered, classed, and racialised relations upon which decarbonisation depends, we advance a more radical approach that centres people, places, and power relations in just transitions.
 Speakers in our sessions will share findings from their research (Session 1) and provocations for discussion (Session 2) on the following questions: What are the social infrastructures of just, rapid and deep decarbonisation, what enables and sustains them? Which forms of labour, care and social relations sustain Net Zero infrastructures? Who does the work of Net Zero, under what conditions, and for whom? How do social infrastructures of decarbonisation operate across scales, from households to neighbourhoods, cities, and national policy frameworks? What types of social infrastructure matter most in advancing Net Zero? How is knowledge about decarbonisation produced, shared, and legitimised within and across different social infrastructures?

papers.title: The relationship between decarbonisation, social infrastructure and wellbeing

sessions.title: Rural Events Between Community Cohesion and Tourism Mobilities: Rethinking Rurality Through Events
sessions.description:
Events are commonly studied within urban contexts, where they are embedded in dense cultural, economic, and social infrastructures. In contrast, events in rural settings—remote, peripheral, or insular—have received significantly less scholarly attention, often framed through deficit-based narratives. This session proposes a critical reconsideration of “rural events” by interrogating the very notion of rurality and its associated stigmas and inequalities. Rather than treating the rural as a simple spatial or demographic descriptor, we explore how rurality is socially constructed, negotiated, and contested, and how events offer a lens for reimagining these processes.

 Existing research on rural events frequently emphasises what rural areas “lack”—infrastructure, funding mechanisms, professionalization—risking the reproduction of simplistic stereotypes and overlooking the resilience and innovation exhibited by rural communities. At the same time, rural events are widely recognized as key social, cultural, and political infrastructures, strengthening community cohesion, generating belonging, and supporting local identity. Increasingly, they also intersect with tourism mobilities, becoming drivers of place marketing, economic diversification, and cultural commodification. This duality raises critical questions about how rural events navigate tensions between community-oriented values and tourism-driven agendas, and how such dynamics shape local identities, power relations, and social change.

 This session invites contributions that critically examine the complex relationality between events, rurality, community life, and tourism flows. By exploring events through their rurality, we aim to uncover how they sustain community cohesion while simultaneously responding to broader mobilities, demographic shifts, and development and cultural pressures.

papers.title: Strategic, Practical and Aesthetic Reflections on the rural in events.
Negotiating Social Licence to Operate through community transition: A rural festival case
Development of craft beer events and community changes in rural Japan
Art Events as narratives of belonging and distinctiveness in insular space: The case of the Northern Aegean islands

sessions.title: Designing Pluriversality in Times of Crisis: Envisioning Spatial Justice Through Alternative Cartographies (1)
sessions.description:
What happens when crises become mappable? 

 Contemporary societies are increasingly shaped by crisis as an everyday condition rather than an exceptional event. Environmental contamination, climate risks, infrastructural failures, humanitarian displacements - to mention a few - unfold through a mix of visible emergencies and less visible, slow, and unevenly distributed forms of harm. These crises are not only experienced on the ground; they are continuously identified, stabilised, and rendered governable through visual systems.

 Maps, dashboards, and visual models have become central to how crises are recognised, communicated, and acted upon. From pollution alerts and flood forecasts to humanitarian risk maps and urban performance indicators, crisis cartographies translate complex, uncertain, and often contested conditions into spatial forms that can circulate across institutions, policy arenas, and publics. In doing so, they do more than depict crises. They shape how crisis is defined, where it is located, whose experiences are counted, and which responses appear legitimate or necessary.

 These mapping practices are deeply implicated in the production and governance of inequality. Crisis cartographies rely on classificatory schemes, thresholds, and visual conventions that privilege certain forms of knowledge and render others marginal or invisible. While often mobilised in the name of evidence-based decision-making, dominant visual approaches can flatten lived experiences, obscure historical responsibility, and reproduce epistemic and spatial injustices.

 We approach mapping as a political, ethical, and relational practice rather than a neutral technique. The session highlights how cartographies operate not only as representations but also as methods, infrastructures, and interventions that influence how crises and inequalities are understood and addressed. It asks how geographers might rethink mapping practices to support more just and plural spatial futures, within and beyond institutional frameworks.

 We welcome empirical, conceptual, and methodological papers engaging with themes including:

 • Crisis cartographies as visual objects, examining how maps, dashboards, and visual models shape what is seen, valued, and governed

 • Crisis cartographies as methods, focusing on mapping as a process of sensing, translating, and maintaining crisis data and experiences, including relational or low tech approaches

 • Crisis cartographies as interventions, exploring how mapping participates in political struggles, institutional decision making, or collective action

 • Crisis cartographies as infrastructures, attending to the socio technical systems, platforms, policies, and labour that sustain mapping practices over time

 • How mapping practices shape the recognition and governance of inequality, and whose experiences are made visible or left out

 • Alternative cartographic practices, such as participatory, feminist, decolonial, artistic, or speculative mapping, that rework dominant visual orders and open possibilities for spatial justice.

sessions.title: Reproductive Justice: Inequalities in Becoming and Being Parents (1) - Structures of Inequality
sessions.description:
Parenting unfolds amid intersecting social, legal, political, biological, and economic inequalities across diverse spaces, encompassing the making and breaking of families (Smietana, Thompson, and Twine 2018). This panel interrogates the geographies of reproductive—defined as a) the right to have children, b) the right not to have children, c) the right to raise children amid supportive circumstances (Ross and Solinger 2017), and the right to sexual autonomy (Ross 2021). Specifically, we are interested in how people become and live as parents —or fail to do so—amid relational and structural inequities within the "global intimate space of reproduction" (Schurr et al. 2025, 10).

 We invite contributions critically examining the “inequalities of becoming and being parents” among marginalised groups across intersectional axes, transcending simplistic dichotomies of invisibilisation/stigmatisation versus happiness/joy. We seek empirically grounded, conceptually rich papers illuminating the lived realities of (intended) parents’ fluid identities and shifting family arrangements in diverse settings. Contributions from the Majority World highlighting unequal experiences—along dimensions of race, ethnicity, indigeneity, class, gender, sexuality, disability, chronic illness, infertility, neurodiversity, religion, or citizenship—are especially welcome. Possible themes include (but are not limited to):

 - (Im)possibilities of parenting amid crises (e.g., poverty, climate change, war), 
- Family separations (e.g., due to prisons, borders, militarized zones) and transnational parenting
- Queer reproductive justice
- Social parents and chosen families
- Access barriers to parenthood/parenting support for disabled individuals
- Everyday challenges of neurodivergent parents
- Migrantised and racialised experiences

 We encourage case studies, comparative analyses, and methodological reflections that critically engage with reproduction and parenting in diverse contexts. We expect 15-minute in-person presentations, with ample time for discussion.
 References 

 Ross, Loretta. 2021. "Reproductive Justice. Ein Rahmen für eine anti-essentialistische und intersektionale Politik." In Mehr als Selbstbestimmung! Kämpfe für reproduktive Gerechtigkeit , edited by Kitchen Politics, 17-60. Münster: edition Assemblage.

 Ross, Loretta, and Rickie Solinger. 2017. Reproductive justice: An introduction . Oakland: University of California Press.

 Schurr, Carolin, Susanne Schultz, Nora Komposch, Nina Etter, Laura Perler, and Yolinliztli Pérez-Hérnandez. 2025. "Geographies of reproductive justice." Progress in Human Geography 0 (0):1-21. doi: 10.1177/03091325251338823.

 Smietana, Marcin, Charis Thompson, and France Winddance Twine. 2018. "Making and breaking families–reading queer reproductions, stratified reproduction and reproductive justice together." Reproductive Biomedicine & Society Online 7:112-130.

sessions.title: No Space Like Home? Space inequalities, property development and urban injustice (1)
sessions.description: Organised by Ruth Neville (CASA, UCL), Katherine Brickell (Geography, KCL); Helen Carr (Law, University of Southampton); Ella Harris (Geography, KCL); Phil Hubbard (Geography, KCL), Iliana Ortega-Alcazar (Geography & Planning, University of Sheffield); Jon Reades (CASA, UCL) and Eleanor Wilkinson (Geography & Planning, University of Sheffield). Housing systems around the world are being reshaped by affordability pressures, demographic changes, and shifting policies, with many of the latter favouring densification and the delivery of new housing at pace. While debates around housing often focus on the rate and price at which additional housing units are provided, less attention has been paid to the unequal distribution of housing space itself. This means we lack understanding of how much domestic space different social groups and populations have access to, where this is located, and how this enhances, or diminishes their quality of life. This is despite the consensus that the dimensionality of housing helps determine whether it functions not simply as a shelter, but as a space that feels like home. The session aims to advance understandings of how housing space is produced, valued, contested, and lived, and how it might be governed or redistributed in support of more just and liveable housing futures. We particularly welcome contributions that explore, amongst others, the following: ⚡ The measurement, mapping or modelling of inequalities in housing space, quality, and affordability using different data sources ⚡ The use of qualitative, creative or sensory methods to explore the affective, emotional and relational dimensions of domestic space ⚡ Health discrimination and the well-being impacts of social overcrowding and space insufficiency ⚡ The environmental and social sustainability of different allocations and distributions of housing space ⚡ The role of planning, policy, and regulatory frameworks in encouraging equality of access to housing space ⚡ The significance of prop-tech, platform economies and finance in the changing distribution of housing space
