Thresholds to Nowhere

sessions.title: Lost in Post-Fossil Transformations
sessions.description: Socio-ecological transformations towards more sustainable, post-fossil, and just societies currently unfold through contentious and ongoing negotiations. Transformation is never only about turning towards something new; it also entails turning away from established practices, infrastructures, and ways of life. The escalating climate crisis and associated phase-outs and policy shifts foreground processes of disappearance, in which certain domains must be dismantled or discontinued. In this sense, transformation produces experiential forms of loss (Reckwitz 2024) and creates the conditions for emerging societal modes of farewell, letting go, or dealing with “transition pain” (Bogner et al. 2024). Developing further debates on emotional dimensions of sustainability transitions, this session focuses on how practices, affects, politics, and spaces of loss are implicated in transformative contexts, shaping socio-political relations and future pathways. Loss has been discussed in geography, for example in relation to ecological loss and damage in a climate-changed world (Cunsolo & Landmann 2017; Hepach & Hertz 2023), as well as in studies of deindustrialisation, ruination, and vacancy (Meier 2012; Rohse et al. 2020; Zhang 2024). Only very recently have the first explicit attempts been formulated to conceptualize the “geographies of loss” (Runkel & Schramm 2024). To delimit an otherwise broad field, the session focuses on current socio-ecological transformations, including sustainability transitions, climate policies, and shifts away from fossil-based ways of being. At the same time, it approaches transformation more broadly as a situated and lived experience, and therefore resists tying transformation-related losses too narrowly to fixed objects or dominant narratives such as deindustrialisation or ecological collapse. Instead, the session foregrounds loss as dispersed, unevenly materialising, and socially differentiated, emerging within wider societal shifts and power relations. Contributions may explore how loss accompanies transformation from the outset—anticipated when change is envisioned or engineered, reactivated through past experiences, or expressed as mourning for futures that never materialised. Against the backdrop of dominant narratives of progress, spaces for collective engagement with loss are often limited (Elliott 2018; Reckwitz 2024). This session therefore asks how practices and spaces of loss might enable social participation in transformation, and whether engaging with loss can open ethical and political perspectives for shaping just transitions. Loss raises difficult questions about the legitimacy of holding on and about how politically problematic sentiments—such as claims that “things were better before”—can be addressed without slipping into nostalgic or reactionary positions, or arguing about the moral order of societal ‘losers’ and what legitimacy groups have for trying to “hold on”. Yet if loss can function as a corrective to one-sided logics of progress, or as a critique of sustainability’s tendency toward preservation (rather than toward exploring progressive forms of letting go; Elliott 2018), we invite contributions that explore how the perspective of loss might be mobilised in critical and progressive ways.

sessions.title: Beyond local conditions: conceptualising ‘place’ for local climate action
sessions.description:
Geographers have been at the forefront of research into local responses to climate change for over two decades. Recent geographical work on local conditions for local climate policy, local transformative capacities, the role of small enterprises or communities and social movement activism, all allude to the importance of local factors, networks and processes in the development and implementation of local climate policies. Surprisingly, however, the notion of ‘place’ is rarely foregrounded in these studies, producing a crucial omission taken the importance of ‘place’ as one core concept in human geography debates.

 Therefore, the aim of this session is to strengthen the conception of ‘place’ in work on local climate action, and to establish a dialogue with classical conceptions of place within human geography. Works of John Agnew, Doreen Massey, Anssi Paasi and Tim Creswell, to name a few, put forward a relational understanding of place, targeting the dynamics how places are becoming constructed, and contested, through different layers of social relations. Importantly, this implies moving beyond conceptions of place as fixed and solely local, stressing the embeddedness of place and place-making processes in developments across locations and scales.

 Speaking to different contexts, this session seeks to clarify how (1) given material and political structures, (2) emotive underpinnings and (3) agency and power dynamics serve or impede the development of capacities for climate action; and how place features are, in turn, reshaped in collaborations and conflicts around tackling climate change, including struggles over what constitutes a “just place” in a climate action context. We contend that by unearthing the dynamics around inclusion and exclusion, and accounting for linkages across places, a critical perspective on place will open new pathways for conceptualising issues of justice in local climate action and beyond.

sessions.title: Coming Into View: Emerging Voices and Expanding Horizons in Animal Geographies (1)
sessions.description:
Animal geography has developed into a vibrant and diverse field of inquiry at the frontiers of critical geographical scholarship. Researchers continue to advance efforts to decolonise and globalise animal geographies (Hovorka 2017), refine ethical and political engagements with animal life and its study (Gibbs, 2024), and push the subdiscipline’s limits through attention to uncharismatic, unconventional and previously overlooked nonhuman animals (Pille-Schneider, 2024; Philip, 2024; Oliver and Dickinson, 2024). Important work nonetheless remains for animal geographers, as they bring to fore new regions, relations, knowledges, and critters that have so far been relatively underexplored in the field (Philo et al., 2024). 

 We seek to showcase the work of emerging scholars – postgraduate and early career researchers – who are forging new empirical, conceptual, and methodological paths forward for animal geographies. In light of this year’s conference focus on geographies of (in)equalities, we especially welcome contributions that explore how more just places for animals might be realised, as well as how animal geography can further do justice to the ethical, political, and epistemic promises of its agenda. 

 Alongside conventional paper presentations, we encourage works that use exploratory, arts-based, and experimental ways of presenting research.

papers.title: “Don’t worry, he’s friendly”: Canine mediations of urban safety, danger, and stigma in Amsterdam

sessions.title: Pessimist Geographies (1)
sessions.description:
From pessimist-philosopher Arthur Schopenhauer’s declaration (2000: 309) that “there is no measure or end of suffering” to the afro-pessimist insistence on the need for “the end of this world” (Wilderson, 2018), pessimism has manifold lives. Whether as a psychological disposition or collective affect, an indifference to the world or a call to arms, pessimism names the absence or loss of an “orientation toward a future, toward something always yet to come, conceived as bestowing a value on life by way of the future anterior” (Berlant and Edelman, 2014: 3).

 Despite their habits of depleting and exhausting, pessimisms appear, currently, to be flourishing, attaching themselves to subjects, narratives, and places, for better or for worse. The central questions this session is concerned with is: How do (and which) times and spaces appear to invite pessimism, and which exclude it? What kinds of (non)relations, destitutions, or affects come to matter when something, or someone, enters into a pessimist relationship to others, the self, or the world as a whole? How do pessimisms come to structure or fracture subjects, places, communities, politics, and temporalities?

 An expert in “raising problems without solutions,” (Thacker, 2015: 5) the pessimist often becomes the problem, and their pessimism becomes simply a matter of failure: a failure to see the light at the end of the tunnel, a failure of political and philosophical thinking, a failure to locate and embrace the joys hidden in the abundant present, a failure to pick oneself up, a failure to build in a crisis-ridden present. Without feeling the need to ascribe pessimism such a moral value or a prescriptive ethics (e.g. of transformation, of rehabilitation), we invite examinations that stay with pessimism, how it comes to take hold, and how it (re)shapes our sense of self, the social, politics, and the world. Paying attention to pessimism brings us not only into its own labour, but also into that of optimism as that which commonly propels, and prescribes, our social relations, political narratives, collective dreams, personal projects, and societal organisations.

papers.title: Misaligning pessimism: discursive constructions of global and positive tipping points

sessions.title: Accessing the Carceral Continuum: Success, Denial and Negotiation (1)
sessions.description: Carceral spaces, despite their multiple mobilities and porous patchwork boundaries, remain inherently difficult for researchers to access. Whilst the intricacies of entry differs according to different institutional and national contexts, common to most access attempts is the need to liaise with institutional actors via personal connections or lengthy formal applications. Such negotiations see scholar’s access gatekept, restricted and often denied under the guise that projects are unaligned to official interests or compromise organisational security. Despite having to navigate such access conditions, reflections on such processes are often overlooked within carceral geographic scholarship, with papers instead favouring the sharing of research findings. This session creates space for further discussion around the underexplored experiences of negotiating, gaining and being denied access to carceral space-times. Uncovering how the carceral apparatuses we (attempt) to work alongside operate, govern and control not only those who are incarcerated but also those who study carceral spaces. Engaging with the conference theme of geographies of inequalities, such reflections allow us to address the inequalities which emerge when attempting to do carceral research, and to acknowledge how research restrictions (re)produce inequalities in knowledge regarding the lived experience of incarcerated people.

papers.title: Negotiating prison officers’ role in delivering healthcare: Reflections from a Canadian qualitative study
Autoethnography as Access: Bypassing the Gatekeeper Through Lived Experience

Denial and Distance: Reflecting on Carceral Research  Restrictions and Access for Early Career Researchers

sessions.title: Participation in the City: How Urban Participatory Innovations Are Reshaping Power and Democracy
sessions.description:
Cities are crucial arenas where democracy is contested, reimagined, and reshaped, innovating new practices and institutions of participation. Yet we still lack a clear understanding of how urban life anddemocratic practice.. This panel examines what we term “urban participatory innovations” (UPIs): hybrid, collective efforts to reconfigure decision-making, transform social reproduction, and reshape power relations in urban contexts.

 Drawing on insights from our forthcoming book Participation in the City , the panel brings together interdisciplinary research from seven cities across four regions—Buenos Aires, Cape Town, Lyon, New York, São Paulo, Toronto, and Warsaw—developed by a team of over 25 scholars. Through a comparative lens, we investigate how UPIs create new democratic ecosystems, challenge existing hierarchies, and produce new solidarities amid crises of urban inequality and democratic legitimacy.

 Rather than reducing participation to either institutional reform or grassroots mobilization, we explore its complex interplay with urban governance, informality, digital engagement, and struggles over territory. In line with RGS 2026’s theme “Geographies of inequalities: towards just places.,” we ask: How do UPIs cultivate (in) justice in the city? How does participation impact urban inequalities? What ruptures in power and governance do UPIs enable? And how might they prefigure more just urban futures?

 This panel offers new conceptual frameworks and empirical insights that not only advance scholarly debates but also inform practitioners, policymakers, and activists seeking to harness urban spaces as arenas of democratic renewal.

papers.title: From Urban Democracy to Urban Power: Reframing Participation in the City
Cities as Saviours of Democracy and Trust?
Urban Participatory Innovation: Relational Comparison at the Current Conjuncture
(In)formal Urban Democracy
Participation Under Pressure: Crisis, Emergency, and Inequality

sessions.title: Emerging Methods and Approaches for Researching Internet Infrastructures
sessions.description: In recent years, various academic disciplines have developed a renewed interest in the geographies of Internet infrastructures. This has drawn attention to the often overlooked infrastructural conditions of data flows and their political embeddedness. For example, studies on the geographies of digital network interconnection discuss the importance of urban (colocation) data centres for international data traffic (Monstadt & Saltzman, 2025; Rosa, 2021), work on the geographies of submarine cables, landing stations and Internet shutdowns describe postcolonial path dependencies in transcontinental data flows (Dambrosio et al., 2025; Grover, 2023; Mwema & Birhane, 2024;), studies on the geographies of network topologies reveal state measures of national data isolation or control (Douzet et al., 2022; Limonier et al., 2021; Salamatian et al., 2021), and work on the geographies of digital platforms discuss the close future of a spatially fragmented and simultaneously centralised (post-)Internet (Huston, 2023; Stocker et al., 2021). At the same time, one of the greatest challenges for critical social science research continues to be the challenging empirical and methodological approaches to these often „invisible“ geographies (Furlong, 2021). In this session, we therefore would like to bring together specific methodological and methodological-conceptual approaches for further research on Internet infrastructures. We are interested in both more ‘quantitative’ methods, such as those related to computer sciences and Internet measurements, and more ‘qualitative’ and ethnographic approaches, as well as methods for mapping physical Internet infrastructures. In addition, we welcome ideas for contributions that address broader methodological reflections or establish disciplinary historical references to research methods on Internet infrastructures.

papers.title: Framing Emerging Methods for Researching Internet Infrastructures
How infrastructure affects digital network proximity - Insights into EllaLink's historical effect on data routing
Beacons in the wires: putting a spotlight on the key digital infrastructures with NSA’s indirect assistance

sessions.title: Who knows? Inequality and Intersectionality in Knowledge Production (1)
sessions.description:
This panel interrogates the intersectionality of gendered, classed, racialized and institutional dynamics shape the production, circulation, and definition of knowledge and technology. Feminist scholarship has shown how knowledge has been unequally produced to both entrench socio-economic inequalities and challenge institutional hegemony (Murphy 2012; Massey, 1995). The inequality of knowledge is further reproduced based on the dynamics of power and imaginations, through the accumulation of history and contemporary practices co-constitutive with spatial processes of differentiation and the exclusion of subject and object (Gregory, 1998; Liebman et al. 2023, Datta, 2023). While the (re)production of knowledge reproduces a hierarchy that further intensifies economic, health and social inequalities, people negotiate through everyday practices to challenge the existing regime of knowledge (Qureshi et al, 2017).

 This session advances critical approaches to historical and contemporary topics grounded in the intersections of gender, race and class in knowledge (re)production. Such topics include, conflicts, appropriations and negotiations between informality and expertise, autonomy and epistemic in/justices, transnational and (de)colonial knowledge production/management, intimate technologies and domestic labour and bottom-up strategies.

 Accordingly, this session will discuss diverse cases of knowledge, technology, power and in/justice to grapple with these complexities and identify modes of intervention into such inequalities as geographers. Enriching feminist geographic scholarship on the gendered politics of knowledge production and information circulation, this session brings together case studies across different time periods and geographical areas to focus on the inequality in the geography of knowledge, and the conflicts and negotiation in formal and informal practices. Who is qualified as the subject of knowledge and who defines it? What conflicts and collaborations occur in the process of negotiation, appropriation and translation of knowledge? How does time and space influence the dynamics of knowledge?

 This panel is the first part of two panels. The first panel features four papers. The second session will feature the last two papers. A roundtable with all authors from both panels will close the session.

papers.title: Recognising knowledge gained from experience of poverty. Towards an intersectional geography of epistemic injustices
'Green' extractivism and the politics of myths and rumours in Kalaallit Nunaat (Greenland)

Care and Coercion in Psychosocial Knowledge(s)

sessions.title: Interventions in and with environmental publics: Creative approaches to renegotiating (un)just places (1)
sessions.description:
In policy and practice across diverse sectors, environments are abstracted from material realities and lived experiences, enabling environmental and socioeconomic injustices. In academia, place-based and participatory creative approaches have emerged as counter-hegemonic attempts to ground ways of understanding people, places, and the more-than-human (McLean, 2022). In recent years, calls within both Geography (Basile, 2025) and the Environmental Humanities (Van Dooren et al., 2024) for scholarship to engage with publics have brought such approaches into dialogue with policy and practice (e.g. Davis and de Andre, 2025). For scholars, introducing participatory creative methods into applied contexts promises ‘more inclusive and empowering socioenvironmental possibilities’ (Van Dooren et al., 2024, p.7), while also presenting methodological and epistemological challenges. 

 This session invites interdisciplinary contributions and asks:

 - How can participatory creative methods translate local knowledge into actionable policy and practice interventions that contribute to the creation of more just places? 
- How do the affective, relational, and reflexive ways of knowing environments enabled by participatory creative methods challenge dominant frameworks in policy and practice? 
- How can these methods centre collaboration and facilitate reciprocity with marginalised communities? 
- What are the ethics and politics of representing environments and their inhabitants through these methods? 

 In a time of increasing socioenvironmental inequalities, this session responds to the need for more situated, pluralistic, and textured understandings of place beyond the academy.

papers.title: Beating the Bounds: Reimagining the Anglo-Saxon Tradition of boundary walking through eco-poetics and embodied practice.
Wilding community: walking, mapping, and sensing together
Imaging from below with NatureScot’s community landscape monitoring projects

sessions.title: Advancing Geographies of Wellbeing: Critical, Contextual and Qualitative Perspectives
sessions.description:
In recent years, geographical work has sought to conceptualise wellbeing as relational, emergent and processual: as an embodied, affective and atmospheric experience, always located in time and space. Drawing on non-representational and posthumanist approaches, this scholarship has significantly expanded how wellbeing is understood. This work responds to earlier critiques of dominant wellbeing research, including its individualism and reliance on narrow quantitative parameters, as well as its assumption that wellbeing is knowable, attainable and able to be increased. There have also been critical evaluations of positive psychology, given its focus on acquiring skills for happiness, and the neoliberal ideologies that shift blame and responsibility from systemic processes to individuals.  

 Responding to these observations, the papers in this panel advance geographies of wellbeing in several ways, drawing on critical, contextual and qualitative perspectives. At a high level, we ask whether wellbeing remains a useful concept, given the lack of definitional consensus, while considering whether its semantic malleability might increase its utility.  We also recognise that for many marginalised groups – including asylum seekers, migrants, prisoners and other minorities – struggles over rights, recognition, safety and material survival often take precedence over abstract or normative accounts of ‘living well’ or ‘happiness’. In such contexts, the language of wellbeing risks obscuring structural violence, depoliticising inequality, or imposing exclusionary and culturally specific ideals. In the contexts of extreme inequality and marginalisation, discussions of ‘wellbeing’ may seem disconnected from lived experience and of little immediate relevance. 

 Through the papers and related discussion, we will engage critically with the ontology and epistemology of wellbeing, considering what is at stake in retaining, reformulating, and even moving beyond the term altogether.  We will explore novel methodologies for researching and evaluating wellbeing, and for studying embodied, relational and more-than-human experiences. We will also examine the potential of creative approaches for understanding what it means to be well and how we, as researchers, can apply these methods with sensitivity.

papers.title: Queer(ing) health geography
Cultivating wellbeing in the post-disaster city: youth experiences of an urban community farm
How can wellbeing be explored through creative engagement with a marginalised community? How can creative activities open conversations about what wellbeing means in everyday life in a secular group?
Reflecting on co-producing evaluation tools that capture embodied and sensory experiences of wellbeing with people seeking asylum

sessions.title: Power, Class and Politics in Green and Blue Spaces: Geographies of Dissonance and Resistance in Leisure and Recreational Activities (1)
sessions.description:
Contemporary engagements with nature, whether in forests, coastlines, rivers, parks, or urban green corridors, are highly political. Leisure and recreation in green and blue spaces are often celebrated as places of wellbeing, connection, and sustainability. However, they are also spaces where power, exclusion, and class relations are reinforced. This session explores how practices such as walking, swimming, surfing, fishing, boating, climbing, camping and community gardening reveal tensions between access and enclosure, tradition and innovation, privilege and marginalisation (Aquino et al., 2022).

 We aim to highlight the dissonance that arises when social inequalities intersect with leisure in natural environments, and to analyse the resistances carried out by communities that challenge dominant narratives about who belongs in these spaces and how these spaces should be used (Bates & Moles, 2022; Wheaton et al., 2019). Contributions might explore themes such as the commodification of recreational landscapes; exclusions from blue- and green-leisure; policing and surveillance of public nature spaces; disputes over conservation and “rewilding”; or grassroots movements reclaiming access and reimagining outdoor practices.

 By focusing on power, class, and politics in discussions of leisure, this session encourages critical reflection on how green and blue spaces are governed, contested, and transformed (O’Connor et al., 2022) within the wider context of the Anthropocene (Clark & Szerszynski, 2021). We invite interdisciplinary contributions from geography, sociology, cultural studies, political ecology, and related fields that showcase empirical, theoretical, and methodological innovations for examining dissonance and resistance within leisure and recreational life (Olive & Wheaton, 2020; Waitt et al., 2009).
 References 

 Aquino, K., Wise, A., Velayutham, S., Parry, K. D., & Neal, S. (2022). The right to the city: Outdoor informal sport and urban belonging in multicultural spaces. Annals of Leisure Research , 25 (4), 472–490.

 Bates, C., & Moles, K. (2022). Bobbing in the park: wild swimming, conviviality and belonging. Leisure Studies , 43 (6), 887–899. https://doi.org/10.1080/02614367.2022.2085774

 Clark, N. and Szerszynski, B. (2021). Planetary Social Thought: The Anthropocene Challenge for the Social Sciences. Cambridge: Polity. 

 O’Connor, P., Evers, C., Glenney, B., & Willing, I. (2022). Skateboarding in the Anthropocene: Grey spaces of polluted leisure. Leisure Studies , 42 (6), 897–907. https://doi.org/10.1080/02614367.2022.2153906

 Olive, R., & Wheaton, B. (2020). Understanding Blue Spaces: Sport, Bodies, Wellbeing, and the Sea. Journal of Sport and Social Issues , 45 (1), 3–19. https://doi.org/10.1177/0193723520950549

sessions.title: Geographies of neighbourhood poverty and deprivation: Examining compounding and intersecting insecurities
sessions.description:
Geographers have played an important role in examining the spatial differentiation of poverty and deprivation in a variety of urban areas. Popularised concepts such as ‘leftbehind’ neighbourhoods (Houlden et al, 2024), ‘neighbourhood effects’ (Bauder, 2002), ‘contextual poverty’ (Andersson et al, 2023) and ‘urban marginality’ (Thieme, Lancione & Rosa, 2017) illustrate how poverty and deprivation are often spatially concentrated at the neighbourhood scale, often intensifying in hyper-local pockets, such as streets or estates. These neighbourhoods are shaped by processes of marginalisation and their intersections with different spatial scales and contexts, such as wider institutional and policy environments (Petrović et al, 2021). They are often characterised by low-income households who experience limited socio-economic outcomes and life-chances. In this session, we will explore diverse geographies of poverty and deprivation at the neighbourhood and hyper-local scale. We are particularly keen to examine the compounding and intersecting insecurities in such geographies. 

 In such neighbourhoods, people often experience compounding (accumulating) and intersecting (overlapping) insecurities that go beyond living on a low-income (Bucelli et al, 2025; Ragin & Fiss, 2016). One insecurity can lead to another, with the impacts intensifying and multiplying. Systemic and socio-economic factors that compound and intersect include employment insecurity, precarious housing, inadequate infrastructure, welfare restrictions, and limited access to public services. Socio-cultural factors can include stigma, racism, social cohesion/fragmentation, and limited social infrastructures. 

 In this session, we aim to explore why and how compounding and intersecting insecurities emerge and persist and the impacts they have on people’s everyday lives and opportunities. We are interested in examining how neighbourhoods of poverty and deprivation are made and how people’s experiences are shaped by multiple spatial scales and contexts. Additionally, we are keen to examine how people and local organisations respond to, negotiate and aim to mitigate against compounding and intersecting insecurities. Responses may be more top-down, such as interventions from local and national governments to more locally led ones, such as interventions from charities, voluntary and community organisations and religious groups. We also want to examine how people, on anindividual level, negotiate insecurities in their everyday lives. At the heart of this session, we want to explore the granular details of disadvantaged places and how insecurities and inequalities intersect, compound and intensify.

papers.title: Neighbourhood experiences and responses to compounding insecurities in Sunderland, UK
Measuring Multiple Deprivation in the Canton of Zurich
Life Course Stratification in Space and Time: Opportunity Structures and the Reproduction of Inequality
Multidimensional hardship; contextualising faith-based responses to inequality and insecurity in one northern-English neighbourhood
Inscribing justice at scale: Stainforth plans its neighbourhood

sessions.title: Cities and the Disinformation Age?
sessions.description:
Since the late 1990s the shift from ‘industrialism’ to ‘informationalism’ ushered in proclamations that a new world of ‘knowledge capitalism’ had dawned, in which knowledge and information was the resource for economic competitiveness and societal development (May and Perry 2018). Cities were seen at the vanguard of this shift, whether as active proponents or sites where alternative imaginaries for a more democratic ‘knowledge society’ could emerge. These opposing struggles – for knowledge as a tool to extract value or as a lever for spatial justice – nonetheless aligned in framing ‘knowledge’ and ‘information’ as (relatively) unproblematic categories. Orthodox scholarship was oriented to how cities could enable spillovers and agglomeration economies to leverage profitable advantage, whilst critique focused on spatial inequalities (Madanipour 2011) resulting from the intensification of techno-capitalist extractions from the ‘urban field’ (Moisio and Rossi 2025).

 Are we now in a disinformation age or ‘infodemic’ (Stephens et al. 2023)? Has the promise of the information age (Bell 1979; Castells 2010) turned sour in the midst of the contemporary geopolitical landscape – where what counts as ‘knowledge’ and ‘information’, as much as what we do with it, is contested and politicised? Disinformation is not new, but the speed, scale and intensity of changes raises fresh questions, given the intersections between rising authoritarianism, democratic backsliding and technologies and tools to ‘control the capital’ (Goodfellow and Jackman 2023), digital and technological innovations (Balayan and Tomin 2024), their re-appropriation as mechanisms for urban control (Schlumberger et al. 2024) and the geopolitical restructuring of global relations. There is emerging but underdeveloped scholarship – often outside urban geography - on the role of different local populations and institutions in resisting ‘truth decay’ (Jackson 2025) in the context of diminishing trust in democracy.

 This session brings together scholars within and outside geography and urban studies who are deploying and challenging extant ideas and discourses of 'disinformation' in different cities: from AI hype and disinformation in making future cities in Hyderabad (India), infopolitics in Abidjan (Cote d’Ivoire), the political economy of disinformation in metropolitan regions in India, everyday information access between and within communities in Tripoli (Lebanon), Patna (India) and Arua (Uganda), and narratives of disinformation in Dhaka (Bangladesh). Together, we will explore traditional and popular manifestations of dis (and mis)information, recent phenomena and underpinning crises of information access, and the relations between cities and urban infrastructures in the disinformation age.

papers.title: AI Hype and the Speculative City: Notes from Hyderabad
Organised Disinformation: Infopolitics of Uncertainty in Abidjan, Cote d’Ivoire
Crises, Communications and Inequalities: Analyses of Social Media Siloes and Resource Access from Lebanon, India and Uganda

sessions.title: Author meets critics: Hanno Brankamp's Occupied Refuge: Humanitarian Colonization and the Camp in Kenya
sessions.description: This author-meets-critics session addresses   Occupied Refuge: Humanitarian Colonization and the Camp in Kenya (Duke University Press: 2026) by Hanno Brankamp. Drawing on rich and original ethnographic research in the Kakuma refugee camp in Kenya, this book provides insight into the much broader and persistent political project of humanitarian colonization, and the racialized violence of refuge. Panelists Claudio Minca, Martina Tazzioli, and Yolanda Weima will discuss the book. Following a response by the author, time will also be allotted to audience questions and further exchange.

sessions.title: Bridging Urban (and) Food Studies through Infrastructure and Governance Research
sessions.description:
Urban food has become a key area of inquiry and a strategic lever in academic and practitioner communities seeking to transform food systems. Recent compilations in the field identify three major advancements: more systemic engagement with the food system, greater attention to scalar complexity, and a stronger focus on relational governance dynamics; while also pointing out the need to further strengthen conceptual clarity around the urban, governance and policy, and issues of power and inequality (Clark et al., 2021; Hayden & Martín, 2025; Moragues-Faus et al., 2024; Moragues-Faus & Battersby, 2021).

 As urban food scholarship continues expanding, there has been increasing interest in the interactions between food systems and other socio-ecological systems shaping food security and sustainability outcomes as well as urban life (Battersby et al., 2024). Addressing these dynamics requires greater dialogue between urban and food studies, building on their transdisciplinary foundations. One emerging contribution to this effort is urban food infrastructures research - inspired by the infrastructural turn in urban studies (see Amin, 2014; Campbell et al., 2022; McFarlane and Rutherford, 2008). This work highlights the central role of infrastructures in shaping urban food systems and conditioning their potential transformations and outcomes (LOGIC et al., 2024). However, the field remains emergent, with no settled conceptualisation of urban food infrastructures, ranging from studies focused on physical systems to broader assemblage approaches incorporating social dimensions (Middha, 2022; Myers & Caruso, 2016; Rut & Davies, 2024; Williams & Tait, 2023). Research on urban food infrastructures offers an opportunity to establish a dialogue across food and urban studies and to advance research that addresses urban food inequalities in their material, spatial, and socio-political dimensions.

 In this panel, we use infrastructures as an entry point to establish a dialogue on cities' powers to transform socio-ecological systems and enact positive change, from health to climate change. The panel will draw on insights from different geographies, theoretical, methodological, and empirical contributions to reflect on how urban food infrastructures and urban powers are conceptualised and mobilised as means to understand and transform cities and food.

sessions.title: Territories, places, and practices of freedom in transatlantic perspective
sessions.description: If ‘freedom is a place’ (Gilmore, 2022), what are the geographies of its social relations (Massey, 1994)? How might freedom be understood not only as a location, but also as a practice, a process, or a mode of inhabiting the world? How can we bring together a ‘progressive sense of place’, as ‘articulated moments in networks of social relations and understandings’ ( Massey, 1994) , with territorialised projects of self-liberation and the aesthetic representation of freedom? In this session, we seek to place the lived spaces — and the conceptual, aesthetic and cosmopolitical practices — of the quilombo , the palenque, and the maroon community into conversation with wider projects of abolition, histories of the commune, and dreams of utopia. Interrogating the political and philosophical commitments, lived realities, and aesthetic politics of territories of freedom in the Atlantic world, we approach them not only as fixed or bounded formations, but also as provisional, mobile, and relational forms of inhabitation (Glissant, 2009; Harney & Moten, 2014). This session seeks to open up a conversation about the spatial and representational dimensions of freedom in comparative and transatlantic perspective.

sessions.title: Understanding the Spatial Inequalities of Health and Wellbeing in the North East of England: Local Authority Perspectives (1)
sessions.description:
Health inequalities are widely understood and felt at a geographical level. The wider determinants of health - or the building blocks of health - are intrinsically linked to the qualities of the places in which our communities live, work and access services. Local authorities are uniquely positioned to see and respond to these spatial inequalities at both local and hyper-local levels. 

 Evidence-informed practice and decision-making within Local Authorities has been strengthened by a growing network of research infrastructure, including NIHR Health Determinants Research Collaborations (HDRCs), Local Authority Research Practitioners (LARPs), and support services such as the Research Support Service (RSS). Understanding how this growing practice is being used to explore, understand and address spatial inequalities in health and wellbeing remains a critical challenge.

 Health inequalities in the North East of England are worse and widening compared with the rest of England (Corris, V. et al., 2020). Tackling these inequalities is a shared priority for all Local Authorities in the region and an important focus for applied, place-based research. 

 This panel session brings together those doing research in this area across local authorities, voluntary, community, and social enterprise (VCSE) organisations, and academic partners, to share the work they have been doing and the challenges they face.

sessions.title: The Ambiguous Geographies of Digital Dis/Connection (1)
sessions.description: In this session, we invite speakers to start from the provocation that relations of digital dis/connection are necessarily ambiguous. Digital technologies are now central to how people interact with the state, access welfare, navigate borders, and maintain everyday life more generally. Technologies such as the smartphone (or other ‘smart’ devices, platforms, or apps) are often presented as tools of connection: making services more efficient, improving communication, and increasing access to support. However, digital systems are rarely experienced as simply connective or helpful; especially for those governed through complex political systems such as asylum seekers, refugees, irregular migrants, welfare claimants, or those living through war and genocide. Negotiating these tensions, our speakers each explore how ambiguity becomes a (conceptual, methodological, practical) tool of doing geographical research at the intersection of dis/connection.

sessions.title: Geodatafication: Socio-technical Infrastructures, Economies and (Geo-)Politics of Datafied Space (1)
sessions.description:
The digital transformation is fundamentally spatial. Contemporary forms of digital capitalism, platform economies, and data-driven governance depend on the continuous translation of space, spatial relations, and territorial knowledge into machine-readable geospatial data. At the same time, geospatial data are a strategic resource in geopolitical competition, territorial surveillance, and contemporary warfare. From real-time logistics and location-based services to satellite infrastructures, drone operations, border surveillance, and military targeting, geodatafication lies at the heart of how spatial power is exercised, contested, and operationalized in the digital age.

 While recent debates in geography and the social sciences have addressed datafication, platformization, digital infrastructures, and geopolitics, the specific role of geospatial data and its infrastructures remains under-explored and under-theorized. This is particularly striking given that geospatial data are central to

 - the coordination and optimization of circulation, logistics, and value chains in digital capitalism,

 - the territorialization of digital platforms and markets,

 - the automation of surveillance, border regimes, and predictive governance,

 - the conduct of contemporary wars, hybrid conflicts, and geopolitical struggles over sovereignty and territory,

 - and the practices of digital humanitarianism through open and collaborative geospatial data.

 Therefore this session foregrounds geodatafication as a foundational process of the digital transformation. We conceptualize geodatafication as the socio-technical translation of space and spatial relations into geospatial data, enabling the calculability, governability, and operationalization of territory, circulation, and mobility. Geodatafication not only allows digital information to be anchored in physical space; it also provides the infrastructural, economic, and political basis for the spatial logistics of digital capitalism and for emerging forms of geopolitical control, surveillance, and warfare.

 The session invites papers that critically examine the political, economic, infrastructural, and everyday dimensions of geodatafication, with particular attention to its dual role in locating digital capitalism and operationalizing geopolitics and conflict.

 We welcome contributions that engage with, but are not limited to, the following themes:

 - Geodatafication and the spatial logics of digital capitalism (platforms, logistics, circulation, labor)

 - Geospatial data as infrastructure of territorial control, surveillance, and security

 - Geodatafication, geopolitics, and geo-economic competition

 - Open geospatial data, digital commons, and their incorporation into geopolitical, humanitarian and commercial strategies

 - Satellite systems, positioning infrastructures (GPS, Galileo), and geospatial data sovereignty

 - Geodatafication, GeoAI, and automation in military, security, and border regimes

 - Mapping, targeting, and geospatial intelligence in contemporary warfare and hybrid conflicts

 - Dual-use geospatial technologies and civil–military entanglements

 - Geodatafication, human rights documentation, and counter-mapping in conflict zones

 - Infrastructures, standards, and formats of geospatial data as sites of power and contestation

 The session welcomes conceptual, theoretical, and empirical contributions from across human geography and allied disciplines, including critical GIS, digital geography, political economy, political geography, STS, critical infrastructure studies, and geopolitics. Contributions may draw on qualitative, quantitative, mixed, or experimental methods.

 By bringing together perspectives on digital capitalism and geopolitics through the lens of geodatafication, this session aims to advance a more integrated understanding of how datafied spatial infrastructures shape contemporary power relations—from everyday platform practices to territorial surveillance and war.

papers.title: (Inter-)Faceing the Critique – A Socio-technical Perspective on Humanitarian Mapping with the HOT TM
Geodatafication: Rethinking the Spatial Foundations of Digital Transformation
Geodatafication of tacit knowledge in the smart city – Optimising emergency mobilities and rescue chains selectively?
Geodatafication and the spatial politics of heat-health risk platforms: From population-level to personalized approaches
Beyond the Binary: Ethnographic Projection and the Protective Translation of Sensitive Urban Geodata

sessions.title: Geographies of Subsumption (1): Social Reproduction and Urbanisation
sessions.description:
In recent work philosopher Andrés Saenz de Sicilia declares subsumption “the concept of capitalist domination…key to thinking the structure and development of capitalist societies” (2025: 115). As a conceptual tool able to parse the highly differential capture and command of labour, and wildly divergent appearance of exploitation in capitalist societies, subsumption has become invaluable to heterodox attempts to understand the volatile and crisis-ridden character of capitalism.

 In opposition to theoretical approaches that conceptualise capitalism as a homologous and inert social artefact of the mid-19th century, as well as those who take the morbid and dysfunctional character of contemporary capitalism as a prompt to jump across the postcapitalist horizon, subsumption provides us with a resource to trace the anxious and contradictory character of capitalist power in the present conjuncture (Cowan 2026).

 In this regard subsumption has been drawn upon by heterodox scholars in recent years to chart capital’s heterogeneity, informing an analytic of capitalist value able to account for diverse forms of capitalist exploitation, expropriation and coercion and capital’s untidy relation to social reproduction, racialisation and informality (Campbell, 2016; Chhabria, 2023; Clare 2019; Liu, 2022; Mezzadri, 2021; Tadiar, 2022). And yet despite the concept experiencing something of renaissance within heterodox theory more broadly, critical and marxist geographers (with some notable exceptions) have yet to fully engage with the spatial and geographical implications and characteristics of subsumption.

 This session will explore the geographies of capitalist subsumption through an engagement with social reproduction and forces of urbanisation.

 The concept of subsumption invites us to consider the sharply political grounds of capitalist settlement, and so we invite contributors too to consider how the terms of subsumption help shape both the emergence of new alliances and discords between capitalists and anticapitalists, and trace the fault-lines and leverage points of capitalist and anticapitalist power.

papers.title: Subsuming Informality: Informal Landed Property and the Geographies of Differential Subsumption in Peripheral Brazil
Subsumed Landscapes: The Unruly ‘Flesh’ of Capitalist Urbanization
Emigration Processing Zones: Migration Industries and the Social Reproduction of Labour in Punjab
Subsumption in Circulation: Platform Capital and Domestic Wage Labour in Urban India
"These Crazy Downpours": Social Reproduction, State Power, and the Volatile Geographies of Subsumption in Mexico City's Rainwater Market

sessions.title: Visualising the city: Urban imaginaries and aesthetics in a digital world (1)
sessions.description:
Urban imaginaries (Lindner & Meissner, 2019) play a key role in framing aspirations in planning, branding, infrastructure and movements for change within urban settings. Familiar tropes such as ‘world class city’, ‘eco city’, ‘smart city’, ‘clean city’ are underpinned by the circulation of aesthetic forms expressing achievement, desire and possibility, or demanding accountability when these are found wanting. 
 In the last two decades, with increased access to technologies such as smart phones and social media platforms, urban imaginaries have been digitised (Degen & Rose, 2024). The contemporary urban imaginary is rendered through a plethora of multimodal digital media such as selfies, memes, reels and AI generated content. Visualising the city is no longer confined to political authorities, planning experts or speculative developers and now includes anyone (residents, netizens and tourists alike) with access to the internet and the means to produce digital media.We invite papers that explore these aesthetic forms of the urban imaginary with suggested themes including, but not limited to

 - Image making practices by citizen led initiatives
- The urban imaginary in social media (possibility, accountability, nostalgia)
- Intersectional urban imaginaries
- AI generated urban imaginaries
- Image making practices by governments
- Aesthetic imaginaries of speculative developments

papers.title: Visioning the Digital Future: Understanding Contemporary Digital City Imaginaries through Ford’s Michigan Central Campus
Data Centres are Not City Centres: Imaginaries and Realities of an AI Urbanism
Cartotopia - AI Imaginaries and the Cartography of the City
Keeping it real: Experimenting with renderings for fictitious infrastructures

sessions.title: Actually Existing ‘Green Transformations’: Between Straightforward Plans and the Messy Realities of Everyday Processes (1)
sessions.description:
While ‘transition’ is an alluring term promising a more environmentally conscious future, the ‘green transition’ has faced significant hurdles in recent years. Although climate change remains a pressing global crisis, international solidarity is fraying and sustainable policies are under threat in many regions. Consequently, the ‘actually experienced’ green transition has become far less certain than the linear change of conditions the term ‘transition’ proclaims.

 In this sense, the green transition mirrors other major historical shifts. For instance, following the fall of the Berlin Wall, the ‘transitologist’ discourse of the 1990s anticipated a straightforward shift from state-centred systems toward democratic, market-driven societies. However, the lived experience of post-socialism was considerably less clear-cut. Striking analogies exist between these two narratives: both are defined as large-scale, disruptive changeovers (Healy & Barry, 2017) promising a new reality—whether integration into a Western capitalist future or a sustainable, livable one.

 Regarding the 1990s, anthropologists and geographers highlighted the fragmentations and hybridizations of supposedly ‘clear-cut’ shifts, which proved much messier than political elites anticipated (Verdery, 1996; Stark & Bruszt, 1998; Pickles & Unwin, 2003; Sýkora & Bouzarovski, 2012; Hann, 2012). Similar observations are increasingly valid for contemporary green transitions (White, 2025). Exploring ‘actually existing’ transformations is necessary to uncover the multiplicity of simultaneous changes and the injustices inherent in these processes. For example, introducing technological fixes without revising consumption practices may exacerbate inequalities or rely on carbon-intensive extraction (Skrzypek et al., 2022). Ultimately, a holistic ecological transition may require moving beyond the capitalist imperative of growth toward a degrowth logic prioritizing ‘care, solidarity, mutual aid, and empathy’ (Buch-Hansen et al, 2024).

 Much as in the story of post-socialism, a heuristic shift from transition to transformation offers a more nuanced insight into sustainability. While ‘transition’ often implies a managed shift between known states, ‘transformation’ suggests open-ended, turbulent, and conflicting processes occurring simultaneously in different directions.

 This session views the ‘green transition’ as a complex societal transformation and invites scholars to reflect on the conflicts and contradictions of these processes as they emerge in practice. We aim to explore ‘messy realities’ through critical readings of contemporary changes, drawing inspiration from historical transformative processes across the Global North, South, and East.

papers.title: Circular consumption in the CE transition: everyday practices and experiences within Irish and Welsh community initiatives
affiliations: Cardiff University School of Geography and Planning

sessions.title: Children's Legal Geographies (1)
sessions.description: Navigating legal landscapes can be challenging for young people as they work out where they belong and what spaces they have access to. This ‘working out’  involves intentional and unintentional transgression and an ongoing questioning of legal boundaries that can challenge parents, teachers, strangers, lawmakers and property owners to reflect on the taken-for-grantedness of law. It can also create openings to refigure legalised socio-spatial relations, especially as young people increasingly become involved in legal advocacy. This first of two paper sessions explores what centering young people’s perspectives about and experiences with the law can tell us about how we develop and normalise hegemonic legal geographies, and the possibilities of imagining and enacting alternatives. In doing so, it aims to develop discussion around children’s legal geographies (Noterman and Blomley 2024) – or how young people encounter, understand, and participate in law-making and breaking through their everyday spatial practices and imaginaries.

papers.title: Between hegemony and advocacy: Researching children’s right to a legal identity
Learning property: navigating children's legal geographies of 'real' estate
Sonic rights for homeless neurodivergent children living in temporary accommodation
Where are the children in the lawscape? A discussion on children's visibility in the urban space

sessions.title: Play, Learn, Navigate: Exploring the role of serious games for a sustainable Ocean (1)
sessions.description:
Serious games, designed for educational purposes rather than just for entertainment, have long been used in geography curriculum from primary school to higher education (Robinson et al., 2021), in leadership training and for engaging policymakers (Sabherwal et al., 2024). They are increasingly recognised as powerful tools to enhance ocean literacy and promote environmental education related to sustainable ocean management, both within and outside of formal educational systems (e.g. engaging stakeholders or conducting research) (e.g. FishBanks, GoJelly, Coastcraft). These serious games encompass various formats, including tabletop games, screen-based games (such as online games or apps), and role-playing games (RPGs). Such games allow players to gain a better understanding of complex issues and experiment with different choices, encouraging critical, long-term and holistic thinking (Carr, 2025). They can also be essential for public engagement and informing decision-making. However, navigating these resources can be challenging, and it is often unclear how to measure their success, i.e. their influence on desired behavioural change, and the knowledge-action gap.

 This session will explore empirical, methodological, and conceptual contributions that focus on the use of games and simulations to promote ocean literacy and support education on sustainable ocean and coastal development and management, including, but not limited to, questions, such as:

 - How can serious games be tailored to address issues of equity and justice in environmental education?
- How do different formats of serious games impact player engagement and learning outcomes?
- What metrics can be used to measure the effectiveness of serious games in promoting ocean literacy and sustainable ocean management?

 This workshop will not only include presentations but will also offer participants the opportunity to immerse themselves in a range of serious games and reflect on the connections between theory and practice. The session will explore the design principles of serious games that engage people in interacting with complex systems behaviours.

papers.title: Classroom formats and entrepreneurial mindset development in Marine Sciences: designing a serious game in Brazil
From Puzzles to Protection: Immersive Escape Rooms Driving Ocean Literacy
The Living Twin: A Relational Paradigm for Integrated Ocean Governance
Shall we play at marine spatial planning? Experience and insights from the MSP Challenge board game
affiliations: Ministry of Infrastructure and Water Management

sessions.title: Towards Just Geography Departments
sessions.description:
As a discipline, Geography is underpinned by notions and practices of justice and equity; it seeks to understand and respond to a changing world punctuated by complex and intersecting issues and crises. In recognition that such crises also touch down in the spaces and life worlds of academia, geographers have turned attention to the ‘state’ of the discipline, from the wide-ranging pressures shaping UK Universities to how these are embodied in their workforce through, for example, the rise and normalisation of precarious working conditions (see for example Mason and Megoran, 2021, 2023; Pickerill, 2026; Simpson, 2022; Squire et al., 2025) and the ways in which intersectional factors inform and shape these experiences (see for example Desai, 2017; Evans et al., 2025; Hughes, 2021; Maddrell et al., 2019; Tolia-Kelly, 2017). Such work has prompted critical questions about the future of the discipline (Roast, 2024; Rose-Redwood et al., 2024). 

 Thinking with such scholarship, this panel pauses with the conference’s theme - Geographies of inequalities: toward just places - by specifically spotlighting the experiences and spaces of the Geography Department. Exploring how questions of justice and injustice (re)make and (re)shape, as well as break, our places of work, it is interested in issues including:

 - Embodied experiences of workplace (in)justice in academia;
- Everyday and mundane places and spaces of workplace (in)justice; 
- Individual and collective strategies colleagues employ to work toward more just workplace futures;
- Opportunities for imagining alternative, more just, and equitable workplace futures;
- The possibilities and impossibilities of justness in the contemporary academy;
- The effects of workplace (in)justice on the future of geographical research.

 References: 

 Desai, V. (2017) Black and Minority Ethnic (BME) student and staff in contemporary British Geography. Area, 49(3), 320-323.

 Evans, B., Allam, A., Ruddock, A., Hale, C. (2025) Creating more inclusive workplaces for academics with energy limiting conditions. University of Liverpool. https:// doi.org/10.17638/03195126 

 Hughes, S.M. (2021) ‘Wait for a permanent contract’: The temporal politics of (in) fertility as an early career researcher. Environment and Planning C: Politics and Space, 39(8), 1725-1736.

 Maddrell, A., Thomas, N.J., Wyse, S. (2019) Glass ceilings and stone ﬂoors: an intersectional approach to challenges UK geographers face across the career lifecycle. Geografiska Annaler, 101(1), 7-20.

 Mason, O., Megoran, N. (2023) Geography as a vocation? Becoming a geographer under neoliberalism. Geografiska Annaler: Series B, Human Geography, 105(1), 17-37. 

 Mason, O., Megoran, N. (2021) Precarity and dehumanisation in higher education. Learning and Teaching, 14(1), 35-59. 

 Pickerill, J. 92026) The State of Geography in UK Higher Education. https://doi.org/10.55203/SXPZ4450 

 Roast, A. (2024) To whom does geography owe a future? Lessons from urban studies. Dialogues in Human Geography, 14(2), 288-291.

 Rose-Redwood, R. et al. (2024) Re-imagining the futures of geographical thought and praxis. Dialogues in Human Geography, 14(2), 177–191.

 Simpson, S. (2022) Feeling redundancy. The Geographical Journal, 188(1), 125-131.

sessions.title: Future-Making, Future-Faking, Future-Breaking? The politics of deception and the power of absurdity around large infrastructure and development projects
sessions.description:
Large-scale development and infrastructure projects have historically been driven and shaped through compelling narratives of the future (Anand, Gupta, & Appel, 2018; Larkin, 2018). Promises of growth, competitiveness, national renewal or sustainability are mobilised to secure public consent, unlock finance, and stabilise political coalitions. Yet these futures are often knowingly partial, exaggerated, or impossible to realise (Flyvbjerg, 2007). Rather than failures of planning or implementation, such practices may constitute a normalised political strategy: future-faking, a practice of fixing unfulfilled promises not through real improvements, but instead by an infinite series of new, (absurd) promises. We understand future-faking not simply as misrepresentation, but as a form of power that actively reshapes anticipatory behaviour, reallocates public resources, and produces uneven geographies of risk, hope, and dispossession.

 This session examines how actors strategically mobilise infrastructural futures, developmental visions, and grand narratives that they know will not materialise as presented, as well as the political, social, and spatial consequences of doing so, and the ways in which affected people navigate faked futures and resist them. With this, it contributes to the chair’s theme of ‘Geographies of inequality’, and their production through deceptive politics. Further, this session features presentations on both past and current large-scale projects, with an additional focus on exploring the absurd: visions that are as improbable as they are grandiose. Through critical examination, we delve into examples of faked futures; plans that, despite their absurdity, generate tangible and far-reaching material effects.

papers.title: Resisting fake hydrogen futures: unbuilt infrastructure, energy colonialism and future-making in resistance to the H2Med pipelines
affiliations: University of Bonn, Humboldt-Universität zu Berlin
University of Bonn, United Nations University Institute for Environment and Human Security

sessions.title: Uneven Development: Decolonial, Feminist and Alternative Perspectives (2)
sessions.description:
In the face of daunting global challenges – including climate change, biodiversity loss, disruptive technologies, the ascendency of authoritarianism and political cynicism, and geopolitical tensions and conflicts – the orthodox paradigm of development based on growth and modernization, driven by innovation and technological advancement, is increasingly challenged. More and more, the fundamental question is raised whether this model of development is leading us astray, instead of offering a way forward, and is actually an important part of the causes for our current predicament (ss Morales, Evenhuis and Sariego-Kluge, 2025).

 This is especially true for the challenge of uneven development and extensive inequities across space. With the orthodox model of development came a belief that the benefits drawn from growth and ongoing modernization will eventually reach the wider society. Yet, this “trickle down” promise has not materialised. Instead, spatial inequalities are widening across the globe along many dimensions (Lobao and Hooks, 2025), starkly visible in many parts of the Global South, and increasingly exposed within the Global North, revealing its multiple “souths”. These inequalities are reshaping political landscapes, fuelling exclusionary and destabilising political movements and harming the progress of sustainable transformations.

 Yet, geographers have long shown that the “trickle down” effect is unlikely to occur without intentional intervention (Hudson, 2007; Werner, 2016; Peck, 2019). Moreover, geographical research reveals that “the economy” is not a singular, coherent entity. Rather, it comprises diverse practices, relations and arrangements that coexist and interact across places. Therefore, understanding spatial uneven development requires perspectives that go beyond standard economic metrics. To better understand spatial inequality, and imagine meaningful interventions, we need approaches that reconfigure or expand how we conceptualise the relationship between economy and space.

 Current ecological, social, and geopolitical shifts invite a renewed perspective on how economic processes interact with spatial structures and how these interactions contribute to the production and persistence of spatial inequalities. These contexts highlight the need to examine the diverse ways economic activity relates to place, to consider multiple economic logics operating across space, and to explore a wider set of conceptual tools for analysing these dynamics. This includes approaches that foreground feminist, decolonial, ecological, and other alternative understandings of economic life and its spatial expressions. Therefore, we invite papers that:

 - Reimagine the relationship between economy and space through alternative, feminist, decolonial, or other lenses
- Examine how spatial inequalities are created, reproduced, and transformed from the points of view of decolonial, feminist and alternative approaches
- Examine plural or non-capitalist economic practices and their spatial implications
- Offer conceptual, methodological, or empirical insights into how economies operate differently across places
- Explore the ethical, political, or ecological dimensions of economic activities and propose new frameworks for analysing or intervening in spatial inequality

papers.title: Decolonising development to reimagine a planetary geography of development
Towards challenge  and justice oriented regional innovation systems for transformative climate adaptation
Who is setting the Agenda? Diversifying Geographies of Place Based Leadership

sessions.title: Challenging racism in challenging times: geographies of anti-racism (2)
sessions.description:
This panel examines the geographies of racism and anti-racism in the context of widening inequalities and ongoing struggles for just places.

 Racism remains a deeply spatialised and systemic form of inequality, shaping access to education, health, wellbeing, housing, mobility, safety, and civic participation. Experiences of racism also undermine social inclusion, trust, and senses of belonging, producing unjust places in which equality of opportunity and dignity are unevenly distributed. From neighbourhoods and institutions to online spaces and national imaginaries, racism is embedded in the social, political, and spatial organisation of everyday life.

 At the same time, emerging racial justice and dignity frameworks provide new ways of conceptualising and evaluating anti-racism efforts—foregrounding whether people feel that they belong, feel safe and respected in the places they inhabit. These approaches align closely with geographical concerns about place-making, power, and justice.

 However, we are currently experiencing challenging times for anti-racism work. Heightened geo-political tensions, political leadership that normalises or sanctions racism and prejudice, and the growing influence of populist and right-wing discourses have reshaped public debate and policy landscapes. New technologies, digital platforms, and online cultures have super charged the spread of racist and extremist attitudes, while strained relationships across cultural groups and with key institutions has weakened allyship and solidarity. Critical, place-based, and action-oriented anti-racism scholarship can make racism more difficult. This will improve community relations, expand citizenship, and build public confidence in diversity and immigration.

 This session will examine the challenges facing anti-racism work , while also offering insights into effective, innovative, or hopeful forms of anti-racist action across different spatial, institutional, and political contexts. Collectively, the session aims to advance geographical understandings of racism and anti-racism, and to contribute to debates on how more just, inclusive, and dignified places might be imagined and enacted.

papers.title: Mapping Racialised Childhood Harm Across Institutional Places: Practice Based Reflections Using AI, Art and Satire
The Hand that Feeds: Victorian Anti-Racism and Geographies of Cohesion
Beyond Words: Language, Power and Racial Justice
Challenging Racism in Sporting Places: Digital Intervention, Structural Inequality and Anti-Racist Action

sessions.title: Gender Inequalities: What Geographic Data Can (and Cannot) Tell Us
sessions.description:
Over the past decade, growing attention has been given to gender data gaps across multiple domains. In response, an increasing number of studies have sought to address these gaps and produce more inclusive forms of knowledge strengthening our understanding of existing gender inequalities. Geographic data, in particular, offer powerful tools for understanding the spatial dimensions of gender inequalities and their intersections with other socio-spatial inequalities.

 At the same time, despite living in a data-rich society, gender-disaggregated data remain far from standard practice. When available, such data often rely on binary classifications that fail to capture the full complexity of gendered experiences.

 This session aims to bring together scholars who use geographic data to examine gender inequalities. We seek to foster discussion around new empirical evidence, unexplored potential, and persistent gaps and limitations in existing data and approaches.

papers.title: The intersectional nature of mobility of care in São Paulo, Brazil
Mobilising Gender Data
Running in Place? Gender and the Urban Fitness Divide
Mapping progress: findings from the Gender Equality Index
Capturing Gendered Pedestrian Mobility at Night: An ABM of Perceived Safety and Route Choice Behaviour

sessions.title: Social Infrastructures for Net Zero: Enabling just place-based decarbonisation (2)
sessions.description:
This world café discussion continues dialogue from Session 1, inviting a broad audience to share their experiences and reflections on enabling just place-based decarbonisation.
 Social infrastructures are vital to the achievement of Net Zero, and are simultaneously co-produced with Net Zero agendas. We conceptualise social infrastructures as relational processes constituted through labour, care, and collective actions. Social infrastructures operate bidirectionally: they enable collective action for decarbonisation, and they are affected by Net Zero policies, funding and activity. Community energy projects, retrofit programmes, sustainable transport movements, community food intiatives and grassroots climate organising all depend upon - and simultaneously create - networks of care, skill-sharing, resource exchange, and collective mobilisation. Our discussants will share perspectives from different socio-technical systems and geographic settings: 
 - Jorge Ortiz-Moreno, University of Manchester (water), ‘What forms of care work are needed to advance decarbonisation at the household level? Lessons from the adoption of rainwater harvesting facilities in Mexico’.
- Patricia Aelbrecht, Public Space Observatory Research Centre, Geography and Planning School, Cardiff University. ‘What London’s Libraries Teach Us About Social Value and Net Zero Goals’.
- Stefan Bouzarovski, Geography, University of Manchester, ‘The hidden labour(s) of social infrastructure’.
- Katy Roelich, Sustainability Research Institute, School of Earth, Environment and Sustainability, University of Leeds. ‘What social infrastructure is needed to decarbonise transport?’
- Sarah Mander, (community resilience) CAST and Tyndall Centre, University of Manchester. ‘Stronger together: building resilience through community conversations’.

 Our facilitators will then convene a World Café discussion to explore the following questions: 

 - What is the social infrastructure of decarbonisation, what enables and sustains it?
- How do capacities and inequalities shape participation in and/or visibility of social infrastructure?
- What types of social infrastructure matter most in advancing Net Zero agendas? How should this be cultivated to enable just, place-based decarbonisation?

 We welcome participation from all involved in just place-based decarbonisation activity, whether through research, policy or practice.

sessions.title: Negotiating Dignity: Rethinking Justice in Geographical Research (1)
sessions.description:
Papers in this session place dignity at the centre of geographical research on justice. Over the past two decades, justice has become a core concern across multiple subdisciplines, including critical urban geography, environmental and climate justice, feminist and care geographies, Black and anti-racist geographies, political geography, and mobilities and transport geography. Much of this work has productively moved beyond distributive frameworks to address recognition, participation, embodiment, affect, and everyday power (Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). Yet the normative and lived stakes of these debates are often articulated indirectly. Dignity helps name what is frequently at issue but insufficiently theorised: the ability to inhabit space without humiliation, erasure, or disposability, and to exercise autonomy and social standing in the face of structural inequality. At the same time, dignity is a concept with a complex and contested history. Its universalist claims—long embedded in law, bioethics, and humanitarian governance—have often failed to confront colonial, racialised, gendered, and caste-based hierarchies, and at times have actively reproduced them (Misztal, 2013). Recent geographical scholarship has responded by advancing relational, contextual, and politically grounded understandings of dignity, attentive to how it is produced, denied, and negotiated through everyday encounters with institutions, infrastructures, and publics (Grossmann and Trubina, 2022; Kariũki and Martens, 2026; Plyushteva, 2023; McIlwaine et al., 2025). Taken together, dignity-oriented research extends geographical debates on justice. It draws attention to the affective and ethical dimensions of inequality that are often muted in distributive or procedural accounts. In doing so, it highlights how injustice is reproduced through routine practices of degradation and erasure. 
 References 

 Grossmann, K., & Trubina, E. (2022). Dignity in urban geography: Starting a conversation. Dialogues in Human Geography , 12 (3), 406-426.

 Hopkins, P. (2021). Social geography III: Committing to social justice. Progress in Human Geography , 45 (2), 382-393.

 Kariũki, W., & Martens, K. (2026). Dignified transport: The case of paratransit. Travel Behaviour and Society , 42 , 101140.

 McIlwaine, C., Peppl, R., & Cal Angrisani, C. (2025). Reclaiming dignity through creative encounters among Brazilian women survivors of violence in London. cultural geographies , 14744740251319039.

 Misztal, B. A. (2013). The idea of dignity: Its modern significance. European Journal of Social Theory , 16 (1), 101-121.

 Plyushteva, A. (2023). Negotiating dignity in public geography: The ethics of public engagement in pandemic times. Area , 55 (1), 53-61.

 Przybylinski, S. (2022). Where is justice in geography? A review of justice theorizing in the discipline. Geography Compass , 16 (3), e12615.

papers.title: Negotiating Dignity on the Road: Mobility, Slow Violence, and Everyday Life Under Occupation in Palestine
Cast(e)ing off Materials: Understanding the Changing Nature of Social Hierarchies and Relations Among Sanitation Workers and Waste Pickers in Delhi
Dignity in space: the psycho-spatial politics of everyday life in unemployment
Reclaiming dignity in the rental crisis: tenant counterpublics, rights-learning, and the making of ‘shelter as dignity’
Affective Geographies of Shame: Feminist Reflections on Ethnographic Encounters and Knowledge Production

sessions.title: From Inequalities to Injustices: Perinatal Health and the Experience of Place
sessions.description:
This panel examines how the study of perinatal inequalities can take form through the lens of geographies of dwelling and livelihood (Ingold, 2000). It approaches socio-spatial inequalities through processes of spatial appropriation linked to residential precarity, as well as through the logics of sorting and fragmentation of public policies, which directly produce vulnerability, particularly among women, gender minorities, and more broadly marginalized groups. By adopting a population-based approach focused on so-called “vulnerable” or “precarious” individuals and on their spatial practices, the panel analyzes the social production of inequalities as it materializes in forms of constraints on dwelling and spatial invisibilization.

 The French territory provides a particularly fertile ground for critical reflection within the geography of inequalities, as it clearly reveals the close relationship between socio-spatial inequalities and deteriorating health outcomes. Maternal mortality rates illustrate this pattern: the Paris metropolitan area records the highest rate in mainland France, while former French colonies that have been departmentalised experience rates several times higher (Deneux-Tharaux & Saucedo, 2024). An institutional context structured around “fragmentation and temporariness as a regulatory norm of poverty” (Gardella, 2016), combined with the saturation of reception facilities, generates a genuine “geography of wandering” for individuals and families facing housing-related precarity (Iasagkasvili, 2025).

 Research has shown that care work generates health inequalities that may extend to violence against minoritized and racialized groups (Crear-Perry, J., et al. 2021.). These inequalities also affect care providers themselves, particularly medical and paramedical professionals. Through task delegation processes, institutions frequently assign them to so-called “dirty work,” thereby reproducing structural inequalities within the labor market (Avril & Ramos Vacca, 2020). These inequalities become embodied in places and people, reflecting how structural policies create what Davis (2019) calls 'reproductive stratification', therefore determining whose reproduction is valued and supported, and whose is constrained or endangered.

 Finally, the panel explores the experiences of “affected” researchers (Favret-Saada, 1990) who adopt a dual positioning as doctoral researchers and healthcare professionals. By conducting research within their own work context, these researchers occupy a situated position that requires sustained observation and prolonged interaction while directly participating in “ordinary representations, routine practices, and their emic meanings” (Olivier de Sardan, 1995). Rather than adhering to approaches that claim researcher neutrality (Bernardie-Tahir & Schmoll, 2012), these experiences actively contribute to debates on the geographies of inequality and health, as well as on their role in the production of spaces of (in)justice (Soja, 2013).
