sessions.title: Producing Marginality in Motion: Questions of ‘Social Justice’ in the Public Transport System of the Global South
sessions.description:
Public transport is often viewed as a key component of urban mobility. But its needless to mention that there has been a declining trend in its modal share globally. In developing countries inadequate and poor public transport infrastructure due to lack of investment by local or national government, high cost of fuel, poorly connected road-network have been encouraging rapid transformation of urban mobility vis-à-vis consumers’ preference for timely, hassle-free direct travel. The complex interaction between sprawled urban growth and transport connectivity have fueled the enormous growth of private transport facilities. As observed in other developing countries, in India also rise in car ownership has not been significant. Rather car rental services and later internet-based ride hailing services became extremely popular among a major section of the population. In spite of its importance as a suitable multimodal travel option for sustainable urban development, recent research have been indicating that such services have been causing social exclusion, aggravating inequalities, particularly among low-income groups, Deb 2021, Young 2022, Oviedo et al 2025. Poorer sections of the population who live in the fringe areas with weaker transport facilities have been paying more to reach their workplace than the urban dwellers, Hine & Mitchell 2001. In emerging economies, such internet-based services have made urbanization-induced employment opportunities easily accessible to young generations, but the perceived risks are often overlooked, Mageto & Luke 2025. From a broader perspective certain ‘costs’ of such services rarely get estimated as significant factors, so candidly in the daily struggle of the young commuters. 

 At this backdrop, this paper aims to conceptualize the idea of ‘poverty’ as an exacerbation of ‘marginalisation’; resulting from inequalities in everyday urban mobility due to the growing popularity of ride-hailing services in Kolkata.

 Based on experiences among commuters of varying age and socioeconomic backgrounds, the paper explores the interrelation between urbanization, transport disadvantage and transport poverty as experienced in everyday life. This remains an understudied issue. There is an urgent need to bring forth these aspects of ‘marginalisation’ as pertinent issues often unaddressed in mainstream sustainable mobility planning. 

 References

 Deb, R. (2021). Ride-hailing and social exclusion: A study of low income neighbourhoods in Bogotá (Doctoral dissertation, UCL Discovery)

 Hine, J., & Mitchell, F. (2001). The role of transport in social exclusion in urban Scotland . Scottish Executive

 Mageto, J., & Luke, R. (2025). Perceived risks and intention to use ride-hailing services: Insights from an emerging market. Journal of Transport and Supply Chain Management , 19 , 1173.

 Muchlisin, M., Soza-Parra, J., Susilo, Y. O., & Ettema, D. (2024). Unraveling the travel patterns of ride-hailing users: A latent class cluster analysis across income groups in Yogyakarta, Indonesia. Travel Behaviour and Society , 37 , 100836.

 Oviedo, D., Sabogal-Cardona, O., & Scholl, L. (2025). Transport and social (dis) advantage in ride-hailing. Area Development and Policy , 10 (2), 295-325.

 Young, M., & Farber, S. (2022). Ride-hailing platforms are shaping the future of mobility, but for whom. The Platform Economy and the Smart City: Technology and the Transformation of Urban Policy , 84-107

sessions.title: Map Room Conversations (3): Re-Thinking Indian Map Traditions
sessions.description: Maps establish conversations between spaces, bringing into dialogue locations, symbols, meaning, and people. The relation between mapping as practice and maps as objects has long been the subject of theoretical and methodological debate within and beyond the discipline of geography. In the sessions below we use the RGS-IBG map collection to continue this debate about maps and mapping through two types of conversation (which build on the first sessions of this type at the 2024 conference, see https://rgs-ibg.onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/toc/10.1111/(ISSN)1475-4762.map-room-conv). The first is between two researchers who use maps from the collection to explore a shared interest from different perspectives. The second conversation will be between the researchers, who will present some of the maps they work with, and the participants in these interactive sessions. We invite you to take this opportunity for close encounters and conversations with the Society’s map collection.

sessions.title: Nigel Curry: a lifetime contribution to rural geography
sessions.description:
This Rural Geography Research Group-sponsored panel session is held in memory of Professor Nigel Curry, who passed away on 25th October 2025.

 The session will honour Nigel’s contributions to the discipline and his wider work, and provide colleagues with an opportunity for reflection. If you would like to share your memories of Nigel during the session, please contact Darren (D.P.Smith@lboro.ac.uk) and Aimee (amorse1@glos.ac.uk).

sessions.title: Reproductive Justice: Inequalities in Becoming and Being Parents (2) - Lived Experiences
sessions.description:
Parenting unfolds amid intersecting social, legal, political, biological, and economic inequalities across diverse spaces, encompassing the making and breaking of families (Smietana, Thompson, and Twine 2018). This panel interrogates the geographies of reproductive—defined as a) the right to have children, b) the right not to have children, c) the right to raise children amid supportive circumstances (Ross and Solinger 2017), and the right to sexual autonomy (Ross 2021). Specifically, we are interested in how people become and live as parents —or fail to do so—amid relational and structural inequities within the "global intimate space of reproduction" (Schurr et al. 2025, 10).

 We invite contributions critically examining the “inequalities of becoming and being parents” among marginalised groups across intersectional axes, transcending simplistic dichotomies of invisibilisation/stigmatisation versus happiness/joy. We seek empirically grounded, conceptually rich papers illuminating the lived realities of (intended) parents’ fluid identities and shifting family arrangements in diverse settings. Contributions from the Majority World highlighting unequal experiences—along dimensions of race, ethnicity, indigeneity, class, gender, sexuality, disability, chronic illness, infertility, neurodiversity, religion, or citizenship—are especially welcome. Possible themes include (but are not limited to):

 - (Im)possibilities of parenting amid crises (e.g., poverty, climate change, war),
- Family separations (e.g., due to prisons, borders, militarized zones) and transnational parenting
- Queer reproductive justice
- Social parents and chosen families
- Access barriers to parenthood/parenting support for disabled individuals
- Everyday challenges of neurodivergent parents
- Migrantised and racialised experiences

 We encourage case studies, comparative analyses, and methodological reflections that critically engage with reproduction and parenting in diverse contexts. We expect 15-minute in-person presentations, with ample time for discussion.

 References 

 Ross, Loretta. 2021. "Reproductive Justice. Ein Rahmen für eine anti-essentialistische und intersektionale Politik." In Mehr als Selbstbestimmung! Kämpfe für reproduktive Gerechtigkeit , edited by Kitchen Politics, 17-60. Münster: edition Assemblage.

 Ross, Loretta, and Rickie Solinger. 2017. Reproductive justice: An introduction . Oakland: University of California Press.

 Schurr, Carolin, Susanne Schultz, Nora Komposch, Nina Etter, Laura Perler, and Yolinliztli Pérez-Hérnandez. 2025. "Geographies of reproductive justice." Progress in Human Geography 0 (0):1-21. doi: 10.1177/03091325251338823.

 Smietana, Marcin, Charis Thompson, and France Winddance Twine. 2018. "Making and breaking families–reading queer reproductions, stratified reproduction and reproductive justice together." Reproductive Biomedicine & Society Online 7:112-130.

papers.title: Emotions Between Legality and Clandestinity: Abortion Testimonies in Brazil and France

(Im)possibilities of Parenting amid Crises: In-Work Poverty, Care, and Reproductive Justice in Switzerland
Margins and Alliances: A Grounded Approach to Queer Reproductive Justice in  Transnational Surrogacy Based on the Experiences of French Fathers in Colombia

sessions.title: No Space Like Home? Space inequalities, property development and urban injustice (2)
sessions.description:
Organised by Ruth Neville (CASA, UCL), Katherine Brickell (Geography, KCL); Helen Carr (Law, University of Southampton); Ella Harris (Geography, KCL); Phil Hubbard (Geography, KCL), Iliana Ortega-Alcazar (Geography & Planning, University of Sheffield); Jon Reades (CASA, UCL) and Eleanor Wilkinson (Geography & Planning, University of Sheffield).

 Housing systems around the world are being reshaped by affordability pressures, demographic changes, and shifting policies, with many of the latter favouring densification and the delivery of new housing at pace. While debates around housing often focus on the rate and price at which additional housing units are provided, less attention has been paid to the unequal distribution of housing space itself. This means we lack understanding of how much domestic space different social groups and populations have access to, where this is located, and how this enhances, or diminishes their quality of life. This is despite the consensus that the dimensionality of housing helps determine whether it functions not simply as a shelter, but as a space that feels like home.

 The session aims to advance understandings of how housing space is produced, valued, contested, and lived, and how it might be governed or redistributed in support of more just and liveable housing futures. 

 We particularly welcome contributions that explore, amongst others, the following:

 ⚡ The measurement, mapping or modelling of inequalities in housing space, quality, and affordability using different data sources

 ⚡ The use of qualitative, creative or sensory methods to explore the affective, emotional and relational dimensions of domestic space

 ⚡ Health discrimination and the well-being impacts of social overcrowding and space insufficiency

 ⚡ The environmental and social sustainability of different allocations and distributions of housing space

 ⚡ The role of planning, policy, and regulatory frameworks in encouraging equality of access to housing space

 ⚡ The significance of prop-tech, platform economies and finance in the changing distribution of housing space

sessions.title: Sustaining Cultural Tourism Places: Heritage Value, Livelihoods, and Long-Term Governance
sessions.description:
Across diverse regions and institutional settings, the coupling of culture and tourism is reshaping local development trajectories. From historic cities in Europe, to cultural tourism towns in China, and tourism villages and community destinations in Southeast Asia, heritage resources, cultural practices, and visitor economies are increasingly mobilised through place branding, regeneration, and wider processes of economic restructuring. Yet sustainability in these places rarely emerges as an automatic outcome. More often, it is produced through ongoing negotiations and trade-offs among growth, conservation, liveability, and environmental limits, negotiations that may reproduce as well as challenge existing geographical inequalities. Tourism expansion can generate income and employment while intensifying housing pressures, crowding public space, and producing precarious and seasonal labour that disproportionately affects marginalised groups. Heritage protection can support cultural continuity and local identity, but it can also sharpen tensions around commodification and contested notions of authenticity. Meanwhile, infrastructure capacity, ecological constraints, risk governance, and climate adaptation raise further questions about what can be sustained, by whom, and at what cost.

 This session brings together scholars and practitioners working on cultural tourism places and welcomes theoretical and empirical contributions that engage with cultural, social, economic, and environmental dimensions of sustainability across contexts. We particularly encourage work that advances international and interdisciplinary dialogue and methodological innovation, including:

       • Ethnography and in-depth interviews

       • Policy and institutional analysis

       • Spatial, digital, and data-driven approaches

       • Participatory evaluation and community-based methods

       • Sustainability assessment frameworks and indicator systems

 Possible themes include, but are not limited to:

       • Benefit distribution and local livelihoods

       • Resident wellbeing, social justice, and everyday liveability

       • Heritage authorisation, cultural participation, and authenticity politics

       • Overtourism, regeneration, and rural or urban transformation

       • Platformisation, tourism work, and labour precarity

       • Carrying capacity, resource governance, and environmental pressures

       • Crisis resilience and climate adaptation

       • Long-term maintenance, public service provision, and institutionalised governance beyond project cycles

 The session aims to create a cross-regional conversation around a shared question: in culture- and tourism-led development, what is being sustained, for whom, through which mechanisms, and how can places build workable long-term balances between growth, heritage, and everyday life?

sessions.title: Imaginations of Just Transitions – Visualizations of (in)justice, politics and (im)mobility in socio-ecological transformations
sessions.description:
Climate change can hardly be perceived from a purely scientific perspective only, as it has deep-seated consequences for how our societies are organized socially, economically, and culturally (Horn, 2024). This has been recognized within multiple studies that focus on the way climate change is visualized on social media, in film and series, in photography, and in the visual arts. In times when we often no longer have the language to describe the changes we are facing, these visual representations of change are becoming increasingly powerful as they can help us to understand the materiality of climate change (Rusca, 2024).

 In this session, we are keen to explore what role visual artefacts (tangible or digital) play within the perceptions and imaginations of possible socio-ecological transformations needed to address climate change. Geographers criticize the frequent use of apocalyptic scenarios and images that convey uncertainty (Beuret, 2024). They also criticize the stoking of fears, such as the fear of climate migration, and the externalization of the vulnerabilities and consequences of climate change to the Global South (Manzo, 2020; Johnson, 2011). 

 Durand-Delacre (2022) therefore argues that it is time to find new visualisations and imaginations if we do not want to run the risk of shutting down alternatives for just socio-ecological transformations. Rusca (2024) argues similarly, showing that we no longer need simple disaster scenarios to alert people to the looming disaster of climate change, but rather politically nuanced visions of the future that address issues of power and inequality to achieve just transformations. This also includes asking who is given space to develop new imaginations and what forms of knowledge are taken into account (Braun, 2015; Harris, 2022). Possible questions for the session thus include:

 How is climate change adaptation and transformation depicted and interpreted visually?

 How are issues such as (in)justice and (im)mobility addressed within such visual representations?

 Which motives dominate the visual discourse of climate change adaptation and transformation, and what effect do they have?

 How can these representations be broken down and critically questioned? Which methods are suitable for this?

 How can geographers support the development of critical imaginations and visualisations of just socio-ecological transformations in the context of climate change? 

 Literature:

 Beuret, N. (2024). Mapping the catastrophic imaginary: The organisation of environmental politics through climate change. In: Environment and Planning E: Nature and Space 7 (1), S. 252–270. DOI: 10.1177/25148486231168395.

 Braun, B. (2015). Futures: Imagining Socioecological Transformation—An Introduction. Annals of the Association of American Geographers, 105(2), 239-243.

 Durand-Delacre, D. (2022). (Mis)representing climate mobilities: lessons from documentary filmmaking. Journal of Ethnic and Migration Studies, 48(14), 3397-3415.

 Harris, D. M. (2021). Storying climate knowledge: Notes on experimental political ecology. Geoforum, 126, 331-339.

 Horn, E. (2024). Klima. Eine Wahrnehmungsgeschichte. Fischer.

 Johnson, H. L. (2011). Click to Donate: visual images, constructing victims and imagining the female refugee. Third World Quarterly, 32(6), 1015-1037.

 Manzo, K. (2010). Imaging vulnerability: the iconography of climate change. Area, 42(1), 96-107.

 Rusca, M. (2024). Towards a future-oriented political ecology of climate change. Geoforum, 153, 103921.

sessions.title: Emerging voices in Political Geography (2)
sessions.description: This panel brings together exciting research in political geography from doctoral and early career researchers. This second panel includes two methodological papers that centre the questions of care and ethics in political geography research. They draw from their field research experience and comment of doing political geography in contested locations and with vulnerable groups, and the ethical need for centring positionality and justice. The other two papers offer a Southern perspective on geopolitics and political culture, again expanding our understanding of political geography in practice.

sessions.title: Food transition pathways, politics of feeling and the geographies of inequality
sessions.description:
Cultural political geographers Anderson and Secor (2025) describe a new politics of feeling – one where the increasing influence of populism in socio-political discourse, runs alongside progressive and neoliberal politics. This session examines how these political affects are carrying agency in various ways across the food system, specifically in terms of what the food system should deliver for all of us (human and non-human), including those in positions of inequity, poverty and precarity.

 The UK Government has ambitions for a healthy, more affordable, sustainable and resilient food system. The European Commission has a vision for agriculture and food that fosters trust and dialogue across the entire value chain, prioritising food security and sovereignty. These ambitions are politically strategic as they attempt to find meaningful engagement, if not agreement, about the how across all aspects of the food system. And yet the populous is a heterogeneous beast that mobilises politically in incoherent, cross-cutting and divergent ways within the food system. We have seen, for example, how the cost-of-living crisis is pushing those struggling to afford their food shopping baskets to protest against mainstream political parties. Knife-edge farming businesses feel ignored as not all farmers are alike in terms of resources, assets and business model. (Seasonal) labour challenges across the food sector are attracting political attention, and with that further shifts towards automation.

 Such mobilisations signify a ‘new food politics’ (Maye et al 2025) that infuses into everyday life, involving producers, eaters, citizens, carers, workers, politicians, activists and virtual platforms (social media). This politics intersects with feelings of nationalism, identity, community, optimism, care, despair, hope, etc. It challenges norms, consensus frameworks and re-politicises political decision-making and social democracy. The affects of these contemporary ‘political atmospheres’ in turn reshape present and future everyday geographies of food, justice and care.

 The session invites contributors to engage critically in this new food politics and in particular with how the emergent geographies of political atmospheres, found variously across diverse communities, are shaping the contours of food system transformation pathways. We welcome empirical, conceptual and methodological papers.

 Below is an indicative list of topics:

 - Affective politics of food justice and geographies of inequality

 - Crisis, resilience, atmospheres and new politics of optimism and recognition

 - Just food futures and the voices that need to be re/presented

 - Redefining food system resilience through politics and affect

 - Food system visioning and nationalism in new political atmospheres

 - Justice and political perspectives of food system actors

 - Impacts of populism on consensus frameworks and deliberative democracy

 - The intersection between progressivism, neoliberalism and populism in the politics and geography of food system transformation

 - Geographies of protest, symbolic and soft power and links to food and politics

 - Cases and voices from everyday food geographies: eaters, workers, farmers, carers, citizens, non-humans

 - Perceptions of politics, food-making and justice from rural and urban communities

 - Metabolic food politics and intersections with political ideologies and place-making

 - Which places, nations and regions get left behind in new political atmospheres but equally how do communities respond and provide comfort?

papers.title: Hometown Ties in the Agricultural Producer Organizations and Inequality
Does eating meat divide urban and rural areas?  A comparative study of urban-rural patterns in meat consumption across EU countries
Food systems, transformation pathways, politics: examining nexus relations, asking uncomfortable questions
Eating at the Margins: Food Transition Pathways, Politics of Feeling, and the Geographies of Inequality in Ethnic Minority Food Systems

sessions.title: Coming Into View: Emerging Voices and Expanding Horizons in Animal Geographies (2)
sessions.description:
Animal geography has developed into a vibrant and diverse field of inquiry at the frontiers of critical geographical scholarship. Researchers continue to advance efforts to decolonise and globalise animal geographies (Hovorka 2017), refine ethical and political engagements with animal life and its study (Gibbs, 2024), and push the subdiscipline’s limits through attention to uncharismatic, unconventional and previously overlooked nonhuman animals (Pille-Schneider, 2024; Philip, 2024; Oliver and Dickinson, 2024). Important work nonetheless remains for animal geographers, as they bring to fore new regions, relations, knowledges, and critters that have so far been relatively underexplored in the field (Philo et al., 2024). 

 We seek to showcase the work of emerging scholars – postgraduate and early career researchers – who are forging new empirical, conceptual, and methodological paths forward for animal geographies. In light of this year’s conference focus on geographies of (in)equalities, we especially welcome contributions that explore how more just places for animals might be realised, as well as how animal geography can further do justice to the ethical, political, and epistemic promises of its agenda. 

 Alongside conventional paper presentations, we encourage works that use exploratory, arts-based, and experimental ways of presenting research.

papers.title: Fluid Alliances: Human, Technical, and Exotic Aquatic Actors in the Making of the Aquarium in Germany from 1870 to 1914
The Cat’s Eye: Reflections of a student multispecies ethnographer
Flow Country poems
A field can be a wall

sessions.title: Pessimist Geographies (2)
sessions.description:
From pessimist-philosopher Arthur Schopenhauer’s declaration (2000: 309) that “there is no measure or end of suffering” to the afro-pessimist insistence on the need for “the end of this world” (Wilderson, 2018), pessimism has manifold lives. Whether as a psychological disposition or collective affect, an indifference to the world or a call to arms, pessimism names the absence or loss of an “orientation toward a future, toward something always yet to come, conceived as bestowing a value on life by way of the future anterior” (Berlant and Edelman, 2014: 3).

 Despite their habits of depleting and exhausting, pessimisms appear, currently, to be flourishing, attaching themselves to subjects, narratives, and places, for better or for worse. The central questions this session is concerned with is: How do (and which) times and spaces appear to invite pessimism, and which exclude it? What kinds of (non)relations, destitutions, or affects come to matter when something, or someone, enters into a pessimist relationship to others, the self, or the world as a whole? How do pessimisms come to structure or fracture subjects, places, communities, politics, and temporalities?

 An expert in “raising problems without solutions,” (Thacker, 2015: 5) the pessimist often becomes the problem, and their pessimism becomes simply a matter of failure: a failure to see the light at the end of the tunnel, a failure of political and philosophical thinking, a failure to locate and embrace the joys hidden in the abundant present, a failure to pick oneself up, a failure to build in a crisis-ridden present. Without feeling the need to ascribe pessimism such a moral value or a prescriptive ethics (e.g. of transformation, of rehabilitation), we invite examinations that stay with pessimism, how it comes to take hold, and how it (re)shapes our sense of self, the social, politics, and the world. Paying attention to pessimism brings us not only into its own labour, but also into that of optimism as that which commonly propels, and prescribes, our social relations, political narratives, collective dreams, personal projects, and societal organisations.

papers.title: Sunstruck pessimism: when heat distorts reality in an era of hallucination
‘You will want for nothing’. Fantasy, negation, and the death drive.
Pessimism in spaces of professionalised climate activism
Logics of Void

sessions.title: Accessing the Carceral Continuum: Success, Denial and Negotiation (2)
sessions.description:
Carceral spaces, despite their multiple mobilities and porous patchwork boundaries, remain inherently difficult for researchers to access. Whilst the intricacies of entry differs according to different institutional and national contexts, common to most access attempts is the need to liaise with institutional actors via personal connections or lengthy formal applications. Such negotiations see scholar’s access gatekept, restricted and often denied under the guise that projects are unaligned to official interests or compromise organisational security. Despite having to navigate such access conditions, reflections on such processes are often overlooked within carceral geographic scholarship, with papers instead favouring the sharing of research findings. This session creates space for further discussion around the underexplored experiences of negotiating, gaining and being denied access to carceral space-times. Uncovering how the carceral apparatuses we (attempt) to work alongside operate, govern and control not only those who are incarcerated but also those who study carceral spaces. Engaging with the conference theme of geographies of inequalities, such reflections allow us to address the inequalities which emerge when attempting to do carceral research, and to acknowledge how research restrictions (re)produce inequalities in knowledge regarding the lived experience of incarcerated people. 
 This second session will take a workshop format, and will collectively explore the challenges associated with researcher access in carceral contexts, building upon the reflections traced in the paper presentations prior. Specifically, we will ask attendees to consider three themes: success, denial, and negotiation. This will be aimed at all career stages, with the intention of bringing different researchers together to share experiences, knowledges, and solutions to these questions of access. This workshop therefore helping to address the inequalities experienced by different people attempting to do carceral research. As knowledge about access to carceral spaces is often limited, reproducing inequalities for incarcerated people, more collective understanding about access processes, and what a lack of access can tell us, can enable us to work towards more just spaces. We hope after the session to produce a resource that collates our collective reflections, to be used as a valuable tool for carceral scholars going forward. We will make this resource available through the Carceral Geographies Working Group network.

sessions.title: Emerging frontiers of urban popular economies
sessions.description: There has been renewed attention to the plurality of economic forms through which people organise their livelihoods in economic geography and related fields. Concepts such as variegated capitalism (Peck and Theodore, 2007) or diverse economies (Gibson-Graham, 2008; Cameron and Gibson-Graham, 2022) have emerged to describe and analyse the actually existing economy, characterised by a diverse, heterogeneous, and often hybrid set of practices that sustain everyday life. One of such notions is the concept of popular economies , which has a longer genealogy rooted in the Latin American critique of the informal economy. Rather than a marginal sector destined to disappear, the popular economy was conceptualised as an alternative way of organising the economy (Coraggio, 1992; Razeto et al., 1983). Central to this perspective are the articulation between economic production and social reproduction, and the coexistence of cooperation and solidarity with competition. More recently, the concept has been reworked to account for how popular economies are reshaped by, and actively reappropriate, neoliberal rationalities such as financialisation, entrepreneurialism, and market expansion (Gago, 2017). Increasingly, the concept has been taken up more widely and applied to urban geographies (Simone, 2019), travelling beyond Latin America. This call is motivated by the recognition of emerging dynamics in urban popular economies, such as their growing transnationalisation, alongside other transformations and shifting frontiers that are reshaping urban life. Late Asian industrialisation and rise of Global China have fuelled urban popular economies with cheap goods, expanding their territorial reach into globalised economic circuits, as well as introducing new agents across different transnational spaces. Additionally, intensified migration has transformed the lived realities of these urban economies. Many migrants find livelihoods on the street and in popular markets, whereas the coexistence of diverse groups generates new opportunities for conflict and cross-cultural cooperation and solidarity. Also intertwined with popular economies are other major contemporary trends, such as the rise of platform capitalism and e-commerce, the centrality of illicit economies in many urban contexts, and the rise of political extremism and hostility against immigrants and ethnic minorities. How are these overlapping and often contradictory dynamics reshaping popular economies, their territories of operation, their moral logics, and their political possibilities? This session invites contributions that engage critically with Urban Popular Economy Frontiers , understood as sites of expansion, tension, and struggles. We welcome papers that explore, challenge, or rethink the concept of urban popular economies, its geographical extension, new empirical applications (in and beyond Latin America) and emerging methodological strategies in light of contemporary transformations.

papers.title: Unpacking Urban Popular Economies in India: The political economy of India’s “e-rickshaw” revolution
affiliations: National Law School of India University

papers.title: Methodological Frontiers
Popular Economies, Public Space, and Urban Change: Street Vendors' Livelihoods in San Diego and Emerging Frontiers of Gentrification in Mexico City
affiliations: San Diego State University / UC Santa Barbara

papers.title: From popular economies to outlaw capital: urban policy, Chinese immigration, and the making of the popular mall model in Belo Horizonte, Brazil

sessions.title: Designing Pluriversality in Times of Crisis: Envisioning Spatial Justice Through Alternative Cartographies (2)
sessions.description:
What happens when crises become mappable? 

 Contemporary societies are increasingly shaped by crisis as an everyday condition rather than an exceptional event. Environmental contamination, climate risks, infrastructural failures, humanitarian displacements - to mention a few - unfold through a mix of visible emergencies and less visible, slow, and unevenly distributed forms of harm. These crises are not only experienced on the ground; they are continuously identified, stabilised, and rendered governable through visual systems.

 Maps, dashboards, and visual models have become central to how crises are recognised, communicated, and acted upon. From pollution alerts and flood forecasts to humanitarian risk maps and urban performance indicators, crisis cartographies translate complex, uncertain, and often contested conditions into spatial forms that can circulate across institutions, policy arenas, and publics. In doing so, they do more than depict crises. They shape how crisis is defined, where it is located, whose experiences are counted, and which responses appear legitimate or necessary.

 These mapping practices are deeply implicated in the production and governance of inequality. Crisis cartographies rely on classificatory schemes, thresholds, and visual conventions that privilege certain forms of knowledge and render others marginal or invisible. While often mobilised in the name of evidence-based decision-making, dominant visual approaches can flatten lived experiences, obscure historical responsibility, and reproduce epistemic and spatial injustices.

 We approach mapping as a political, ethical, and relational practice rather than a neutral technique. The session highlights how cartographies operate not only as representations but also as methods, infrastructures, and interventions that influence how crises and inequalities are understood and addressed. It asks how geographers might rethink mapping practices to support more just and plural spatial futures, within and beyond institutional frameworks.

 We welcome empirical, conceptual, and methodological papers engaging with themes including:

 • Crisis cartographies as visual objects, examining how maps, dashboards, and visual models shape what is seen, valued, and governed

 • Crisis cartographies as methods, focusing on mapping as a process of sensing, translating, and maintaining crisis data and experiences, including relational or low tech approaches

 • Crisis cartographies as interventions, exploring how mapping participates in political struggles, institutional decision making, or collective action

 • Crisis cartographies as infrastructures, attending to the socio technical systems, platforms, policies, and labour that sustain mapping practices over time

 • How mapping practices shape the recognition and governance of inequality, and whose experiences are made visible or left out

 • Alternative cartographic practices, such as participatory, feminist, decolonial, artistic, or speculative mapping, that rework dominant visual orders and open possibilities for spatial justice.

papers.title: Alter-Mapping Crisis and Latency. Territorial delay, infrastructural hierarchies and socio-ecological futures
affiliations: Ecole Nationale Supérieure d'Architecture de Strasbourg

sessions.title: Who knows? Inequality and Intersectionality in Knowledge Production (2)
sessions.description:
This panel interrogates the intersectionality of gendered, classed, racialized and institutional dynamics shape the production, circulation, and definition of knowledge and technology. Feminist scholarship has shown how knowledge has been unequally produced to both entrench socio-economic inequalities and challenge institutional hegemony (Murphy 2012; Massey, 1995). The inequality of knowledge is further reproduced based on the dynamics of power and imaginations, through the accumulation of history and contemporary practices co-constitutive with spatial processes of differentiation and the exclusion of subject and object (Gregory, 1998; Liebman et al. 2023, Datta, 2023). While the (re)production of knowledge reproduces a hierarchy that further intensifies economic, health and social inequalities, people negotiate through everyday practices to challenge the existing regime of knowledge (Qureshi et al, 2017).

 This session advances critical approaches to historical and contemporary topics grounded in the intersections of gender, race and class in knowledge (re)production. Such topics include, conflicts, appropriations and negotiations between informality and expertise, autonomy and epistemic in/justices, transnational and (de)colonial knowledge production/management, intimate technologies and domestic labour and bottom-up strategies.

 Accordingly, this session will discuss diverse cases of knowledge, technology, power and in/justice to grapple with these complexities and identify modes of intervention into such inequalities as geographers. Enriching feminist geographic scholarship on the gendered politics of knowledge production and information circulation, this session brings together case studies across different time periods and geographical areas to focus on the inequality in the geography of knowledge, and the conflicts and negotiation in formal and informal practices. Who is qualified as the subject of knowledge and who defines it? What conflicts and collaborations occur in the process of negotiation, appropriation and translation of knowledge? How does time and space influence the dynamics of knowledge?

 This panel is the second part of two panels. The first panel features four papers. The second session will feature the last two papers. A roundtable with all authors from both panels will close the session.

sessions.title: Interventions in and with environmental publics: Creative approaches to renegotiating (un)just places (2)
sessions.description:
In policy and practice across diverse sectors, environments are abstracted from material realities and lived experiences, enabling environmental and socioeconomic injustices. In academia, place-based and participatory creative approaches have emerged as counter-hegemonic attempts to ground ways of understanding people, places, and the more-than-human (McLean, 2022). In recent years, calls within both Geography (Basile, 2025) and the Environmental Humanities (Van Dooren et al., 2024) for scholarship to engage with publics have brought such approaches into dialogue with policy and practice (e.g. Davis and de Andre, 2025). For scholars, introducing participatory creative methods into applied contexts promises ‘more inclusive and empowering socioenvironmental possibilities’ (Van Dooren et al., 2024, p.7), while also presenting methodological and epistemological challenges. 

 This session invites interdisciplinary contributions and asks:

 - How can participatory creative methods translate local knowledge into actionable policy and practice interventions that contribute to the creation of more just places? 
- How do the affective, relational, and reflexive ways of knowing environments enabled by participatory creative methods challenge dominant frameworks in policy and practice? 
- How can these methods centre collaboration and facilitate reciprocity with marginalised communities? 
- What are the ethics and politics of representing environments and their inhabitants through these methods?

 In a time of increasing socioenvironmental inequalities, this session responds to the need for more situated, pluralistic, and textured understandings of place beyond the academy.

papers.title: A reflection of the implications of real-world (messy) implementation challenges for Tactical Urbanism
Climate activism and temporal disorientation: Navigating tensions in time using collaborative textile-mapping
Towards meaningful consultation: Engaging local community members, policymakers, and decision-makers in creative audiovisual practice in Grangemouth, Scotland
Between participatory and partnership-based action research, towards an inclusive design for a district heat network

sessions.title: Beyond post-colonial approaches in climate change adaptation: a critical look at decolonisation-induced inequalities
sessions.description:
Decolonisation has increasingly become a buzzword of post-colonial scholars and institutions but, how approaches promoted by this concept enhance inclusiveness is more challenging and less understood. This is based on critically addressing how current climate change adaptation governance continues to largely exploit and marginalise vulnerable nations and communities in the Global South and North. 

 Decolonising climate governance is critical and urgent to create alternative narratives, institutions, and practices that challenge the dominant ideology and power structures. However, it is fundamentally naive to assume that all decolonising or Indigenous-led climate scholarship and practice represents or benefits all members of traditional marginalised indigenous or local groups. Decolonisation comes with the risk of societal fragmentation and widening inequalities. This happens when initiatives are captured by special interests and elites, as a result of unequal power relations. This could lead to unintended consequences such as the exclusion or disempowerment of marginalised or less powerful groups.

 This session contributes to a small but growing body of work that attends to multiple subjectivities within marginalised indigenous and local communities. The session seeks to engage in scholarship that examines how power differentials at the intersection of different positionalities and intersectionalities produce differing experiences of climate change adaptation from a decolonised perspective. Contributions from both Global South and Global North contexts, and from diverse methodological approaches, are especially welcome. To ensure that decolonisation in climate change adaptation remains relevant to Indigenous and local people, this session explores intersectionalities in decolonised climate change adaptation by welcoming contributions that explore:

 - Empirical cases of decolonisation in climate change adaptation within the domains of water, energy, food and health.
- Types of collective interests sustained and perpetuated following decolonisation processes.
- Inequalities and exclusions produced and reproduced in the process of adaptation.
- How individuals and communities are resisting inequalities created by decolonisation processes.

papers.title: A decolonial perspective on disability, climate risk, and adaptive practices in Sri Lanka
‘Goon-Paansu”( Literally translated: Maroon agricultural and agri-food heritage): Research on Maroon traditional knowledge and practices on cultivation their land
From Commons to Commodity: How decolonisation in climate resilience backfires in fishing practices in Ganvie on Lake Noukoue, Southern Benin
Decolonizing Heat Stress Adaptation? Unequal Burdens and Power in Urban Climate Responses
The illusion of Decolonial Governance and the gendered (adaptation) trap: a case of Dhaka

sessions.title: Diaspora Diplomacy: Crafting a More-Than-State Geopolitics?
sessions.description:
The questions this session asks are the following: how do diasporas develop their own diplomacies and participate in a broader geopolitical worldmaking beyond the state? And what are the geographies of their diplomatic practices?

 While the new US administration and its ‘Donroe Doctrine’ are shaping the world towards what has been defined as “hemispheric geopolitics” (Dodds, 2026), the rise of global diasporas (Cohen, 2023) in international politics seems to keep blurring the boundaries of territorial sovereignty. Their growing involvement in international politics has led scholars to speak of both human geopolitics (Gamlen, 2019) and diasporic geopolitics (Adamson and Han, 2024), arguing that they will be a key element in the future international order. Diasporas constitute a terrain for competition between states, but also for discovering opportunities to rethink diplomatic practices and reshape geopolitics within and beyond the state.

 Due to their liminal subjectivity emerging from participation in multiple worlds (McConnell, 2017), today, diasporas act as proactive participants in bilateral, multilateral, and polylateral diplomacy alongside other non-state actors, while also being increasingly subject to extraterritorial governance and surveillance by their home states (Gamlen et al., 2019).

 This consolidation in diaspora activism and governance has given rise to a new term, debated in both policy and academic circles: diaspora diplomacy . Geographers have built on earlier critiques of state-centric approaches that framed diaspora populations as passive agents of homeland development and foreign policy, instead focusing on forms of diplomacy enacted by diasporas (Boyle & Ho, 2017; Ho & McConnell, 2019). The field of diaspora diplomacy explores unconventional geographies and practices of worldmaking, diplomatic relations, diplomatic training, and internationalist solidarities and institutions, interweaving state and non-state actors.

 Examining the role of diasporas in diplomacy can shed light on how complex international relations operate in practice. In particular, a focus on diaspora diplomacy can highlight innovative modes and instruments for building a more cooperative transnational space and for giving voice to those who are often marginalized in the international arena. Diaspora diplomacy compels us to challenge rigid assumptions and categories – such as the internal/external and territorialization/deterritorialization dichotomies – while also prompting a rethinking of both statehood and non-statehood. 

 This session welcomes theoretical and empirical contributions that help explore diaspora diplomacy, particularly through critical approaches.

papers.title: Making diaspora diplomacy through sacred space: publics, advocacy and mediation across the UK and India
Everyday Diplomats in the Global East: Ghanaian Migrants and the Rewriting of African Foreign Relations
Multiscalar diaspora diplomacy in China's state-led diaspora mobilisation
Diplomacy Through Diaspora: Comparing Top-Down Engagement in Northern Ireland and the Republic of Ireland (1998–2026)
Deepening advocacy in post-statist diaspora diplomacy: The alliance-building of the Kurdish Freedom Movement in Europe

sessions.title: Power, Class and Politics in Green and Blue Spaces: Geographies of Dissonance and Resistance in Leisure and Recreational Activities (2)
sessions.description:
Contemporary engagements with nature, whether in forests, coastlines, rivers, parks, or urban green corridors, are highly political. Leisure and recreation in green and blue spaces are often celebrated as places of wellbeing, connection, and sustainability. However, they are also spaces where power, exclusion, and class relations are reinforced. This session explores how practices such as walking, swimming, surfing, fishing, boating, climbing, camping and community gardening reveal tensions between access and enclosure, tradition and innovation, privilege and marginalisation (Aquino et al., 2022).

 We aim to highlight the dissonance that arises when social inequalities intersect with leisure in natural environments, and to analyse the resistances carried out by communities that challenge dominant narratives about who belongs in these spaces and how these spaces should be used (Bates & Moles, 2022; Wheaton et al., 2019). Contributions might explore themes such as the commodification of recreational landscapes; exclusions from blue and green leisure; policing and surveillance of public nature spaces; disputes over conservation and “rewilding”; or grassroots movements reclaiming access and reimagining outdoor practices.

 By focusing on power, class, and politics in discussions of leisure, this session encourages critical reflection on how green and blue spaces are governed, contested, and transformed (O’Connor et al., 2022) within the wider context of the Anthropocene (Clark & Szerszynski, 2021). We invite interdisciplinary contributions from geography, sociology, cultural studies, political ecology, and related fields that showcase empirical, theoretical, and methodological innovations for examining dissonance and resistance within leisure and recreational life (Olive & Wheaton, 2020; Waitt et al., 2009).

papers.title: “For everyone, forever”: (Re)imagining access to nature in three National Trust Green Corridors in the North of England.
Family, friends, and forgetting in a blue green place
“There’s no management of the caravans”: Campervan Tourism and the Politics of Countryside Access in Scotland
Tourism as a Biodiversity Gatekeeper: Whose Nature Is Being Preserved?
Governing mountain access in Switzerland: tourism transition and the politics of outdoor leisure

sessions.title: Governing the AI-urban-environment nexus
sessions.description:
Artificial Intelligence (AI) is increasingly positioned within technocratic narratives as a pathway toward sustainable and resilient urban futures, from air-quality monitoring and heat-risk prediction to flood-warning systems and strategic visions for climate change mitigation. Yet, this framing simultaneously reshapes how environmental governance is both enacted and imagined in cities, privileging data-driven, anticipatory, and predictive decision-making for managing urban ecological uncertainty and environmental risks (Cugurullo and Xu, 2025; Marvin, 2025), while sidelining and obscuring more diverse, adaptive, context-specific approaches (Das et al., forthcoming).

 AI does not merely subject the urban environment to new forms of algorithmic control; it also materially transforms it (Bratton, 2021). The expansion of AI infrastructures demands significant energy resources, generates heat, and produces large volumes of e-waste (Cugurullo et al., 2025). These environmental impacts are unevenly distributed, reflecting and reinforcing existing social, geopolitical, and infrastructural inequalities (Fard, 2020). The integration of AI into urban environmental governance thus presents a paradox: while promoted as a technological solution to environmental issues, AI simultaneously introduces new layers of ecological degradation and environmental vulnerability (Das and Woods, forthcoming). From the cooling of data centres to the energy consumed by AI models, the entanglements of AI and environmental governance unfold across multiple dimensions of urban life. In this context, it remains unclear whether AI technologies can be actually capable of improving urban sustainability. Nonetheless, lured by the promises of the ideology of AI, city managers from all around the world are investing considerable amount of resources in the construction of large AI infrastructures supposed to make urban living more sustainable (Cugurullo, 2025).

 Moreover, AI-enabled environmental governance can reproduce or intensify socio-spatial inequalities. Overreliance on AI and algorithmic decision-making can overlook context-sensitive knowledge, reinforce data biases, and further marginalize vulnerable communities (Smith, 2020; Arora et al., 2023) particularly in Global South cities where lack of access to resources, uneven impacts of climate change, and infrastructural gaps are already pronounced. Consequently, the design, deployment, and governance of AI technologies must consider environmental and social impacts, especially at the local scale where environmental governance can shape how AI is being integrated into urban contexts.
 This session will explore both conceptual and empirical contributions that examine how AI deployment is increasingly shaping, and being shaped by, urban environmental governance. It will critically analyze the applications, imaginaries, infrastructures, environmental impacts, governance frameworks, and emergent frictions at the intersection of AI and urban environmental governance.

papers.title: Frictions and Fictions of Urban Environmental AI: On digital twins, urban governance, and the impossibility of a finished map (but the useful appeal therein)
Data-driven cities and the paradox of the sustainability fix
The conception of fantaspaces and the environmental limits of urban AI
affiliations: National University of Singapore and Trinity College Dublin

sessions.title: Climate and extraction: Political ecology of inequality and responsibility
sessions.description:
Convenors:  

 - Prof. Anja Nygren, University of Helsinki, (corresponding convenor), anja.nygren@helsinki.fi
- Associate Prof. Dinah Rajak , University of Sussex, d.r.rajak@sussex.ac.uk

 Discussant:  
 - Senior Research Fellow, Dr. Gediminas Lesutis , University of Berlin

 A crucial question in confronting climate change and social inequality is how to repair ruined environments and drastically altered livelihoods and lifeworlds in areas affected by large-scale resource extraction. This panel focuses on efforts at repair and claims for responsibility at extractive frontlines, suffering from multifaceted environmental and social effects of resource exploitation. Some of these effects occur gradually, cumulating over time; others in the aftermath of sudden disasters such as oil spills, forest fires, or devastating floods.

 We argue that novel approaches are needed to bridge the analyses of environmental restoration initiatives with perspectives that confront the sociopolitical challenges of repair, including unequal distribution of benefits and burdens, uneven power relations, dispossession, traumas of loss, and relations of mistrust. Our panel seeks to contribute to the political ecology of repair and responsibility in three ways. First , we acknowledge the risks involved in neoliberal efforts at double-valuing nature – by extracting it and by repairing it – through accumulation by restoration. The political ecology of repair and responsibility indicates integrated attention to restoration of damaged environments and recovery from dispossession. 

 Second , recovery from environmental-social harms is interlinked to questions of responsibility, including who should be held accountable for them and take responsibility of repair. We emphasize the struggles for responsibility as multitemporal processes that are historically rooted and temporally grounded, meanwhile entailing forward-thinking for creating new forms of political engagement and institutional recognition. This requires paying attention to how legacies of extraction condition possibilities for change; analysing both the forms of selective remembering and creative reinvention in the wake of extraction and quest for transformative responsibility.

 Third, more attention needs to be paid to the efforts of extraction-affected people to reimagine their lifeworlds, reclaim their resource rights and restitch their livelihoods in the pursuit of justice and hope. These efforts need to be examined within the wider political-economic conditions, where dominant discourses and politics often erase alternative ways of thinking and living. Only by linking the analyses of the effects of extractivism with the multi-scalar efforts at restitution it is possible to understand the prospects and constraints involved.

 Our session convenes presentations that examine the following topics:

 -  the ways in which people living at the extractive frontlines reimagine and recraft their altered environments, livelihoods, and lifeworlds
- new avenues for understanding responsibility
- novel modes of engaging in politics and regaining justice
- multi-temporal and multi-scalar analyses of new modes of living and liveability in environments damaged by degradation and dispossession

 We have plans for a joint publication related to the key topics of this session.

papers.title: Un/making futures: Rivers, ruination, and the multi-temporalities of repair in Cambodia
Corporate Narratives of Harm & the Abdication of Accountability in the Extractive Industries
The Politics of Repair: Responsibility
Repairing the desert? The use of desalinated water in mining in the context of green transition in Chile

sessions.title: Food on the move (1): geographies of dietary change and (re)valuation
sessions.description: Efforts to improve the sustainability and resilience of Western food systems will ultimately entail eating differently. Whether adopting new food sources, refraining from others, or returning to earlier ways of eating, dietary change of some sort appears unavoidable. This raises important questions for those seeking to improve Western food systems. How do things become appreciated and popularised as food? How, in fact, do things become regarded as food in the first place? Food geographers have made important contributions towards answering such questions, particularly across topics such as production-consumption linkages, ‘alternative’ ways of organising and producing food, and explorations of how food (and food waste) are enacted and categorised in practice. However, there is still much to learn about how foods circulate and become (re)valued within different geographies of production and consumption, knowledge around which is essential if we are to organise Western food systems differently. This session aims to advance debates in these areas. It asks: how do foods move around, both physically (e.g. foods travelling to different places) and socioculturally (e.g. foods becoming de-/re-valued by different eaters)? What does this tell us about how Western food systems might be organised in a more just, resilient and sustainable way?

sessions.title: Politics of valuation: Financial flows in the political geography of resources
sessions.description:
Resource extraction is a highly capital-intensive industry, sustained by a variety of financial flows. Whether expressed as equity investment, derivatives, bank loans, capital markets' bonds or public finance instruments (subsidies, guarantees, etc.), these financial flows are not simply movements of capital: they shape the political geography of resources. In fact, they structure power relations (dependence, risk mitigation, governance of uncertainty, control of information, capture of value, etc.) and determine the materialization of extractive activities in space (investment locations, connections between territories, political and environmental governance, local distribution of revenues etc.).
 This panel proposes to examine how financial flows and financial instruments shape the political geography of resources. Drawing on constructivist approaches (Raffestin 1980; Bridge 2009), we understand resources as processes of continuous construction at the intersection of material substances, political and economic contexts, technological availability, legal frameworks, but also financial mechanisms and instruments. These mechanisms and instruments operate at different scales and are networked to different places (i.e. financial centers, offshore centers, regional markets etc). Hence construction processes of resources are entangled in financial valuation processes.
 This approach to resources places great importance on the notion of value . Drawing on pragmatic approaches to valuation (Hercelin 2025), "the" value of a resource, of an economic project, or of a territory is shaped by the particular rationalities of financial instruments and models (Doganova 2024) - such as expected profitability, commodification, risk management, etc. Such financial valuation practices are contested by other valuation regimes - such as inalienability of land, competiting land use, the sanctity of a substance, etc. Instruments and models themselves can also be employed in or even act as sites of resource conflicts, for example for computing the distribution of mining rents or environmental compensation.
 Approaching resources through the lens of financial geography raises methodological challenges. The financial world has proven difficult to access. In this panel, we wish to give space to methodological considerations: on the one hand, how can we access financial data and actors? And on the other hand, how can the tools of financial geography inform research in the political geography of resources?

papers.title: The Power to Price: Transformations in the Cobalt GPN at the Intersection of Financial and Physical Markets
The Strategy of the Government of the State of Mecklenburg–Western Pomerania to Shield Nord Stream 2 from U.S. Sanctions

sessions.title: Beyond Access: Lived Experience and Disability Justice in Tourism and Leisure Spaces (2)
sessions.description:
Tourism provides significant potential for well-being, social connection, and leisure engagement. However, these benefits are not equally distributed or accessible across the population. This session moves “beyond access” to centre the rich diversity of lived experience and the transformative framework of disability justice within tourism and leisure geographies. Directly addressing the conference theme, “Geographies of Inequality,” this session explores what it truly means to establish tourism and leisure spaces that are not simply compliant, but are genuinely welcoming, equitable, and inclusive of human diversity.

 This session aims to bridge a connection between scholarship on embodiment and affect andcritical principles of disability justice. Drawing on this framework that demands not just entry, but also equitable participation, intersectional analysis, and collective liberation, we invite papers that prioritize the knowledge and experiences of disabled people to explore how leisure spaces are navigated, contested, and re-imagined. We are particularly interested in research that moves beyond a singular focus on physical impairment to embrace neurodiversity, sensory worlds, and the complex interplay of multiple social identities.

 Potential topics include, but are not limited to: • Critiques of checklist-style accessibility and the pursuit of belonging, dignity, and joy. • The sensory, affective, and embodied geographies of navigating, negotiating, or avoiding tourist destinations. • Intersectional analyses where disability meets race, gender, queerness, and class in leisure contexts. • Disability activism, community-led tourism, and the co-design of just and inclusive spaces. • Re-imagining heritage and nature-based tourism through a disability justice lens. • The role of digital platforms and media in fostering justice.

sessions.title: Spaces of Inequalities: Using Insights from Geography to Understand Contemporary Educational Inequalities
sessions.description:
Research on educational inequalities has deep historical roots and spans areas such as unequal access to educational institutions and stratification in educational transitions. Such research has also examined varied prospects for navigating norms within educational institutions, as well as other reproductive boundaries related to class, ethnicity, gender, and migration. Indeed, inequality remains a significant concern for scholars worldwide, as gaps between groups persist and, in some areas, steadily increase, especially in new geographies of gentrification and globalization. Further, some patterns of inequality, such as those relating to social class, have become more entrenched over time, while others emerge and disappear, as struggles over social, material, and symbolic goods, as well as political decision-making, change and take new shapes (see van Zanten 2009; Zancajo & Bonal 2020; Yoon et al. 2025). In this proposed panel, we focus on emerging spaces of inequalities within sociological and geographical research on education at local and global scales. 

 This panel will bring together an international group of scholars with expertise in educational mobility, segregation, choice, and marketization, as these have become key areas in contemporary educational change, reform, and policy-making, shaping inequalities. Focus will range from structural-level analysis to specific groups (e.g., elites, middle class, working class, racial and ethnic minorities). Further, topics will include current developments, such as global trends of student mobility, education marketization across various educational sectors, changing politics of access and educational policies that affect precarious groups, changing societal conditions and increasing structural inequalities, and emerging socio-spatial configurations of urban contexts contributing to educational inequalities. This panel will also explore where policymakers and institutional decision-makers have endeavoured to create greater inclusion for historically underrepresented groups. In doing so, our goal is to highlight the significance of place and space in the intersections of (re)production and resistance to educational inequalities across several sites included in this panel. 

 Four panellists will elaborate on the changing geographies of inequalities and how they are intertwined with educational inequalities. This geographical emphasis will provide new insights into current debates and discussions on educational disparities at local and global scales, as well as their interrelated connections. A panellist from England will share her research on student mobility opportunities and inequalities in post-Brexit. Another panellist will focus on how school types, racial composition, and patterns of white enrollment have changed across major U.S. cities since 2000 to assess how gentrification shapes educational inequalities. Lastly, two panellists will share their collaborative research on mixed-methods approaches to provide a more nuanced understanding of the changing geographies of increasingly unequal programs of choice offered in public (i.e., state) schools in Canada. All panellists will be invited to reflect further on the challenges and contributions of their chosen research approaches, both to the object of study and to the broader field of socio-spatial research on educational inequalities. We believe this year’s conference theme aligns well with our panel’s focus on studying the inequalities of spaces, with an emphasis on education, which has become ever more critical to shaping long-term social inequalities.

papers.title: Everyday Micro-Geographies of Schooling: Performance and Belonging in Delhi’s Government Schools
affiliations: Nirantar Trust - A Centre for Gender and Education

sessions.title: Understanding the Spatial Inequalities of Health and Wellbeing in the North East of England: Local Authority Perspectives (2): World Cafe
sessions.description:
Health inequalities are widely understood and felt at a geographical level. The wider determinants of health - or the building blocks of health - are intrinsically linked to the qualities of the places in which our communities live, work and access services. Local authorities are uniquely positioned to see and respond to these spatial inequalities at both local and hyper-local levels. 

 Evidence-informed practice and decision-making within Local Authorities has been strengthened by a growing network of research infrastructure, including NIHR Health Determinants Research Collaborations (HDRCs), Local Authority Research Practitioners (LARPs), and support services such as the Research Support Service (RSS). Understanding how this growing practice is being used to explore, understand and address spatial inequalities in health and wellbeing remains a critical challenge.

 Health inequalities in the North East of England are worse and widening compared with the rest of England (Corris, V. et al., 2020). Tackling these inequalities is a shared priority for all Local Authorities in the region and an important focus for applied, place-based research. 

 Building on the connected panel session, Understanding the Spatial Inequalities of Health and Wellbeing in the North East of England: Local Authority Perspectives (1), the world café session aims to bring together those carrying our research on health inequalities in local authority and VCSE settings with those in academic settings, for discussion, collaboration, and problem solving. During the world café representatives from North East of England local authority areas will each host a table discussion to promote collaborative thinking and problem-solving around a research question or challenge they are currently working on. 

 The session will begin with lighting talks from all table facilitators to showcase the topic they will be discussing at their table then attendees will be given the opportunity to select tables of interest and sit at four in total throughout the session. 

 Proposed format:

sessions.title: The Ambiguous Geographies of Digital Dis/Connection (2)
sessions.description: In this session, we invite speakers to start from the provocation that relations of digital dis/connection are necessarily ambiguous. Digital technologies are now central to how people interact with the state, access welfare, navigate borders, and maintain everyday life more generally. Technologies such as the smartphone (or other ‘smart’ devices, platforms, or apps) are often presented as tools of connection: making services more efficient, improving communication, and increasing access to support. However, digital systems are rarely experienced as simply connective or helpful; especially for those governed through complex political systems such as asylum seekers, refugees, irregular migrants, welfare claimants, or those living through war and genocide. Negotiating these tensions, our speakers each explore how ambiguity becomes a (conceptual, methodological, practical) tool of doing geographical research at the intersection of dis/connection.

sessions.title: Geodatafication: Socio-technical Infrastructures, Economies and (Geo-)Politics of Datafied Space (2)
sessions.description:
The digital transformation is fundamentally spatial. Contemporary forms of digital capitalism, platform economies, and data-driven governance depend on the continuous translation of space, spatial relations, and territorial knowledge into machine-readable geospatial data. At the same time, geospatial data are a strategic resource in geopolitical competition, territorial surveillance, and contemporary warfare. From real-time logistics and location-based services to satellite infrastructures, drone operations, border surveillance, and military targeting, geodatafication lies at the heart of how spatial power is exercised, contested, and operationalized in the digital age.

 While recent debates in geography and the social sciences have addressed datafication, platformization, digital infrastructures, and geopolitics, the specific role of geospatial data and its infrastructures remains under-explored and under-theorized. This is particularly striking given that geospatial data are central to

 - the coordination and optimization of circulation, logistics, and value chains in digital capitalism,

 - the territorialization of digital platforms and markets,

 - the automation of surveillance, border regimes, and predictive governance,

 - the conduct of contemporary wars, hybrid conflicts, and geopolitical struggles over sovereignty and territory,

 - and the practices of digital humanitarianism through open and collaborative geospatial data.

 Therefore this session foregrounds geodatafication as a foundational process of the digital transformation. We conceptualize geodatafication as the socio-technical translation of space and spatial relations into geospatial data, enabling the calculability, governability, and operationalization of territory, circulation, and mobility. Geodatafication not only allows digital information to be anchored in physical space; it also provides the infrastructural, economic, and political basis for the spatial logistics of digital capitalism and for emerging forms of geopolitical control, surveillance, and warfare.

 The session invites papers that critically examine the political, economic, infrastructural, and everyday dimensions of geodatafication, with particular attention to its dual role in locating digital capitalism and operationalizing geopolitics and conflict.

 We welcome contributions that engage with, but are not limited to, the following themes:

 - Geodatafication and the spatial logics of digital capitalism (platforms, logistics, circulation, labor)

 - Geospatial data as infrastructure of territorial control, surveillance, and security

 - Geodatafication, geopolitics, and geo-economic competition

 - Open geospatial data, digital commons, and their incorporation into geopolitical, humanitarian and commercial strategies

 - Satellite systems, positioning infrastructures (GPS, Galileo), and geospatial data sovereignty

 - Geodatafication, GeoAI, and automation in military, security, and border regimes

 - Mapping, targeting, and geospatial intelligence in contemporary warfare and hybrid conflicts

 - Dual-use geospatial technologies and civil–military entanglements

 - Geodatafication, human rights documentation, and counter-mapping in conflict zones

 - Infrastructures, standards, and formats of geospatial data as sites of power and contestation

 The session welcomes conceptual, theoretical, and empirical contributions from across human geography and allied disciplines, including critical GIS, digital geography, political economy, political geography, STS, critical infrastructure studies, and geopolitics. Contributions may draw on qualitative, quantitative, mixed, or experimental methods.

 By bringing together perspectives on digital capitalism and geopolitics through the lens of geodatafication, this session aims to advance a more integrated understanding of how datafied spatial infrastructures shape contemporary power relations—from everyday platform practices to territorial surveillance and war.

papers.title: The City Multiple: Comparing the Platform Geographies of Google Maps, Instagram, Facebook, and TikTok
Conservation software as tools of domination and resistance: EarthRanger, power, and biodiversity in northern Kenya
Geospatial Artificial Intelligence: automated Geodatafication at scale?

sessions.title: Contesting Tourism Growth: Critical Geographies, Spatial Inequalities, and Just Alternatives (1)
sessions.description:
This panel brings together critical perspectives on tourism growth as a spatially uneven and politically contested process. Across diverse geographical contexts, tourism is frequently positioned as a development solution, yet its expansion often intensifies inequalities in housing, labour, mobility, infrastructure, and access to decision-making—particularly in tourism-dependent and geographically constrained places such as islands, rural regions, and peripheral urban areas.

 The panel is structured around two complementary themes that speak to ongoing debates in critical geography, urban studies, and political economy.

 1: Urbanisation, Governance, and Spatial Inequality examines how tourism growth is planned, governed, and contested, and how it reshapes urban and regional space. Contributions in this theme explore the governance arrangements, policy frameworks, and institutional dynamics through which tourism-led development produces uneven outcomes, including displacement, housing pressures, precarious labour, and differentiated mobilities. Taken together, the papers and presentations situate tourism within broader processes of urbanisation, territorial restructuring, and spatial injustice.

 Panel B: Contestation, Alternatives, and Post-Growth Futures turns attention to challenges to growth-oriented tourism models and to emerging alternatives. Drawing on degrowth, regenerative, post-capitalist, and place-based approaches, this theme foregrounds grassroots, community-led, and Indigenous practices that reimagine tourism beyond extraction and expansion. Papers and presentations will explore how alternative tourism futures are articulated, negotiated, and sometimes institutionalised, and who has the power to define what constitutes a “just” tourism geography across different spatial and political scales.

 Together, the panel offers a critical and forward-looking examination of tourism’s role in shaping space, inequality, and possibility. It will be of interest to scholars and practitioners working on urbanisation, governance, political ecology, degrowth, and spatial justice, as well as those seeking to engage with post-growth debates and transformative approaches to tourism and place-making.

 This session will be held in-person in at the RGS conference London, with a hybrid option available for 2.   This session is sponsored by the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group (GLTRG) at the RGS conference.

papers.title: "See It Before It’s Gone": Last Chance Tourism and Nature Imaginaries in the Canadian Rockies
