sessions.title: Geographies of Subsumption (2): Nature and Labour
sessions.description:
In recent work philosopher Andrés Saenz de Sicilia declares subsumption “the concept of capitalist domination…key to thinking the structure and development of capitalist societies” (2025: 115). As a conceptual tool able to parse the highly differential capture and command of labour, and wildly divergent appearance of exploitation in capitalist societies, subsumption has become invaluable to heterodox attempts to understand the volatile and crisis-ridden character of capitalism.

 In opposition to theoretical approaches that conceptualise capitalism as a homologous and inert social artefact of the mid-19th century, as well as those who take the morbid and dysfunctional character of contemporary capitalism as a prompt to jump across the postcapitalist horizon, subsumption provides us with a resource to trace the anxious and contradictory character of capitalist power in the present conjuncture (Cowan 2026).

 In this regard subsumption has been drawn upon by heterodox scholars in recent years to chart capital’s heterogeneity, informing an analytic of capitalist value able to account for diverse forms of capitalist exploitation, expropriation and coercion and capital’s untidy relation to social reproduction, racialisation and informality (Campbell, 2016; Chhabria, 2023; Clare 2019; Liu, 2022; Mezzadri, 2021; Tadiar, 2022). And yet despite the concept experiencing something of renaissance within heterodox theory more broadly, critical and marxist geographers (with some notable exceptions) have yet to fully engage with the spatial and geographical implications and characteristics of subsumption.

 This session will explore the geographies of capitalist subsumption through an engagement with questions of capitalist nature and labour. we invite examinations of the geographies of capitalist subsumption, exploring the social, spatial and political dynamics of the differential capture of nature, land, labour, bodies, and technology into capital’s metabolic system. The concept of subsumption invites us to consider the sharply political grounds of capitalist settlement, and so we invite contributors too to consider how the terms of subsumption help shape both the emergence of new alliances and discords between capitalists and anticapitalists, and trace the fault-lines and leverage points of capitalist and anticapitalist power.

papers.title: Subsumed by Debt: Finance Capital and the Transformation of Rural Cambodia
‘You need a union’:  Subsumption, ‘Eavy Metal’ and the warren, and becoming ludic labour.
Technopolitical bifurcation in an era of polyalignment: Formal and real subsumption of nature in Indonesia’s nickel sector
Producing with nature: subsumption in Peru´s scallop frontier

sessions.title: Visualising the city: Urban imaginaries and aesthetics in a digital world (2)
sessions.description:
Urban imaginaries (Lindner & Meissner, 2019) play a key role in framing aspirations in planning, branding, infrastructure and movements for change within urban settings. Familiar tropes such as ‘world class city’, ‘eco city’, ‘smart city’, ‘clean city’ are underpinned by the circulation of aesthetic forms expressing achievement, desire and possibility, or demanding accountability when these are found wanting. 

 In the last two decades, with increased access to technologies such as smart phones and social media platforms, urban imaginaries have been digitised (Degen & Rose, 2024). The contemporary urban imaginary is rendered through a plethora of multimodal digital media such as selfies, memes, reels and AI generated content. Visualising the city is no longer confined to political authorities, planning experts or speculative developers and now includes anyone (residents, netizens and tourists alike) with access to the internet and the means to produce digital media.We invite papers that explore these aesthetic forms of the urban imaginary with suggested themes including, but not limited to

 - Image making practices by citizen led initiatives
- The urban imaginary in social media (possibility, accountability, nostalgia)
- Intersectional urban imaginaries
- AI generated urban imaginaries
- Image making practices by governments
- Aesthetic imaginaries of speculative developments

papers.title: The Aesthetic Performance of Smartness: How Political Rationales and Temporal Contestation Within the State Shape Pune Smart City
Garbage spots to selfie points: aesthetic imaginaries of the ‘cleanest city’
Speculative Urbanism: Film, Imagination and Algorithmic Governance in London
The materialisation of digital mobility platforms in Amsterdam: bodies and infrastructures in motion between physical and virtual space

sessions.title: Actually Existing ‘Green Transformations’: Between Straightforward Plans and the Messy Realities of Everyday Processes (2)
sessions.description:
While ‘transition’ is an alluring term promising a more environmentally conscious future, the ‘green transition’ has faced significant hurdles in recent years. Although climate change remains a pressing global crisis, international solidarity is fraying and sustainable policies are under threat in many regions. Consequently, the ‘actually experienced’ green transition has become far less certain than the linear change of conditions the term ‘transition’ proclaims.

 In this sense, the green transition mirrors other major historical shifts. For instance, following the fall of the Berlin Wall, the ‘transitologist’ discourse of the 1990s anticipated a straightforward shift from state-centred systems toward democratic, market-driven societies. However, the lived experience of post-socialism was considerably less clear-cut. Striking analogies exist between these two narratives: both are defined as large-scale, disruptive changeovers (Healy & Barry, 2017) promising a new reality—whether integration into a Western capitalist future or a sustainable, livable one.

 Regarding the 1990s, anthropologists and geographers highlighted the fragmentations and hybridizations of supposedly ‘clear-cut’ shifts, which proved much messier than political elites anticipated (Verdery, 1996; Stark & Bruszt, 1998; Pickles & Unwin, 2003; Sýkora & Bouzarovski, 2012; Hann, 2012). Similar observations are increasingly valid for contemporary green transitions (White, 2025). Exploring ‘actually existing’ transformations is necessary to uncover the multiplicity of simultaneous changes and the injustices inherent in these processes. For example, introducing technological fixes without revising consumption practices may exacerbate inequalities or rely on carbon-intensive extraction (Skrzypek et al., 2022). Ultimately, a holistic ecological transition may require moving beyond the capitalist imperative of growth toward a degrowth logic prioritizing ‘care, solidarity, mutual aid, and empathy’ (Buch-Hansen et al, 2024).

 Much as in the story of post-socialism, a heuristic shift from transition to transformation offers a more nuanced insight into sustainability. While ‘transition’ often implies a managed shift between known states, ‘transformation’ suggests open-ended, turbulent, and conflicting processes occurring simultaneously in different directions.

 This session views the ‘green transition’ as a complex societal transformation and invites scholars to reflect on the conflicts and contradictions of these processes as they emerge in practice. We aim to explore ‘messy realities’ through critical readings of contemporary changes, drawing inspiration from historical transformative processes across the Global North, South, and East.

sessions.title: Children's Legal Geographies (2)
sessions.description: Navigating legal landscapes can be challenging for young people as they work out where they belong and what spaces they have access to. This ‘working out’ involves intentional and unintentional transgression and an ongoing questioning of legal boundaries that can challenge parents, teachers, strangers, lawmakers and property owners to reflect on the taken-for-grantedness of law. It can also create openings to refigure legalised socio-spatial relations, especially as young people increasingly become involved in legal advocacy. This second of two paper sessions explores what centering young people’s perspectives about and experiences with the law can tell us about how we develop and normalise hegemonic legal geographies, and the possibilities of imagining and enacting alternatives. In doing so, it aims to develop discussion around children’s legal geographies (Noterman and Blomley 2024) – or how young people encounter, understand, and participate in law-making and breaking through their everyday spatial practices and imaginaries.

papers.title: Livelihood aspirations, forest based everyday practices and law enforcement encounters among recent rural-urban migrant young people in Malawi

sessions.title: Play, Learn, Navigate: Exploring the role of serious games for a sustainable Ocean (2)
sessions.description:
Serious games, designed for educational purposes rather than just for entertainment, have long been used in geography curriculum from primary school to higher education (Robinson et al., 2021), in leadership training and for engaging policymakers (Sabherwal et al., 2024). They are increasingly recognised as powerful tools to enhance ocean literacy and promote environmental education related to sustainable ocean management, both within and outside of formal educational systems (e.g. engaging stakeholders or conducting research) (e.g. FishBanks, GoJelly, Coastcraft). These serious games encompass various formats, including tabletop games, screen-based games (such as online games or apps), and role-playing games (RPGs). Such games allow players to gain a better understanding of complex issues and experiment with different choices, encouraging critical, long-term and holistic thinking (Carr, 2025). They can also be essential for public engagement and informing decision-making. However, navigating these resources can be challenging, and it is often unclear how to measure their success, i.e. their influence on desired behavioural change, and the knowledge-action gap.

 This session will explore empirical, methodological, and conceptual contributions that focus on the use of games and simulations to promote ocean literacy and support education on sustainable ocean and coastal development and management, including, but not limited to, questions, such as:

 - How can serious games be tailored to address issues of equity and justice in environmental education?

 - How do different formats of serious games impact player engagement and learning outcomes?

 - What metrics can be used to measure the effectiveness of serious games in promoting ocean literacy and sustainable ocean management?

 This workshop will not only include presentations but will also offer participants the opportunity to immerse themselves in a range of serious games and reflect on the connections between theory and practice. The session will explore the design principles of serious games that engage people in interacting with complex systems behaviours.

sessions.title: Chair's event: Migrant justice and border abolition
sessions.description:
This panel reflects on peoples’ struggles against systems of bordering – from border crossings, detention, and deportation to raids and everyday policing of migrant communities.

 Through a focus on radical practices, histories, and geographies of migrant justice and border abolition, this conversation explores the challenges, joys, and benefits of imagining, planning and practicing a world without borders – a world where people have the freedom to move, the freedom to stay, and the freedom to return.

 Join this panel session to learn about inspiring research and current debates on migrant justice and border abolition.

 Featuring Dr Geoff Boyce (University College Dublin), Dr Martina Tazzioli (University of Bologna), Gracie Mae Bradley (University of Glasgow), Dr Anish Chhibber (Queen Mary University of London), and chaired by Dr Malene Jacobsen (Newcastle University).

sessions.title: Chair's event: Towards care-full geographies of disability, health, well-being, and care
sessions.description:
This panel session focuses on geographies of disability, health, wellbeing and care with contributions from leading researchers working on these topics

 This panel showcases recent developments and emerging intersections within and between these subdisciplines and amplifies interdisciplinary work on the lived experiences of health inequalities, including those relating to chronic illnesses, learning disabilities, caregiving, body-mind differences, and other key issues connected to the complex and unequal geographies of health, care, and wellbeing.

 Featuring Professor Bethan Evans (University of Liverpool), Dr Ed Kiely (Queen Mary University of London), Professor Elizabeth Olson (UNC Chapel Hill), Professor Andrew Power (University of Southampton), and chaired by Dr Poppy Budworth (University of Liverpool.

sessions.title: Power, inequalities and participation: revisiting food citizenship and beyond (1)
sessions.description:
In a food system wrought by crises and inequalities, ‘food citizenship’ emerged alongside ‘food democracy’ as one way of re-politicising food. The concept re-imagines consumers as active citizens who participate in shaping more just food systems. Fundamental questions of participatory justice are at stake: who is able to participate in food governance, on what terms, and with what consequences for social and ecological futures?

 Despite its growing traction, limited attention has been paid to intersectionality, situatedness and embodied experiences of ‘food citizenship’, and examining how food system participation is structured by gender, race, class, disability, age and other axes of power. The figure of a ‘citizen’ is never neutral: normative expectations around ‘active citizenship’ are prevalent in food citizenship debates and can reproduce exclusion by privileging particular capacities, temporalities and modes of engagement. Critiques of citizen participation highlight potential to reinforce neoliberal dynamics and subjectivities. Socio-spatial injustices also constrain participation; participation doesn’t inherently ‘fix’ systemic injustices.

 Taking food citizenship as an imperfect but productive starting point, this session will critically interrogate the methodologies, geographies and politics of subject formation, participation and justice in the democratisation of food governance across scales and places/territories:

 - Who is (imagined as) a ‘food citizen’? What visions of food citizenship circulate across different spaces and scales, what political work do they perform, and for whom?
- Who is included or excluded from these imaginaries and practices, and how is power and participation structured through gender, class, race, ethnicity, disability or age?
- How does embodied food citizenship intersect with food sovereignty, food justice and other debates?
- Which alternative language, theoretical perspectives or methodological approaches can help reinvigorate participatory justice values and ethical commitments within food citizenship debates and practice?

 Four short papers will be followed by a world cafe-style discussion.

papers.title: Rethinking food citizenship through the lens of childhood
The making of food citizens as classed and gendered ‘experts by experience’
Food Democracy in Practice: On the Frictions and Conflicts in Organising Solidarity Kitchens
World-cafe discussion
affiliations: Department of Geography and Environmental Science, University of Reading

sessions.title: Legal Animal Geographies: Towards Multispecies Justice in Policy and Practice (1)
sessions.description:
There is a vast array of work that explores governance, regulation, and legislation of animals in a range of different spaces, including work examining the role of responsibility in dog walking in the Scottish Outdoor Access Code (Brown and Dilley, 2012; Brown, 2015), regulation of ‘street’ and ‘stray’ dogs in the UK and India (Srinivasan, 2013), the legal blurriness of horseshoe crabs in animal welfare policy (Gorman, 2024), the poorly suited regulations and welfare guidance for fish in UK scientific research (Greenhough et al. 2024), social license to operate in Australian horse racing and horse welfare (Duncan et al., 2018; McManus, 2023), empathy and ethics in the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons Code of Professional Conduct (Donald, 2019), pet’s right to the city in China (Yan, 2025), and the different framings of dog-related policy in the UK (Allen et al., 2025). Bringing these works together raises three key questions:

 (1) How can we take the care of animals seriously in current legal systems?(2) How can we contest, challenge, and resist unjust legal systems for animals? (3) How can we create just multispecies worlds? This session explores how multispecies legal justice might be realised.

 Taking animals seriously in legal systems requires challenging the anthropocentrism inherent in our understandings of animals as property, tools, and objects (Deckha, 2021; Allen et al., 2025), to instead highlight the ways in which animals’ own subjectivities shape space and everyday life. As Braverman (2016) argues we need to consider the range of ethical and political issues of what it means to govern more-than-human life.

 We invite papers that not only highlight current multispecies injustice, but seek to challenge, resist, and contest these systems to create more just multispecies worlds and futures. We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes:

 Animals and abuse; Animals and housing; Animals and inequality; Animals and law; Animals and policy (change); Animal campaigning; Animals, history, and policy; Animals, policy, and space; Animals and everyday policing; Human-animal governance; Multispecies justice; Wildlife crime

 To submit, please send your paper title and abstract (of up to 250 words) to Daniel Allen (d.allen@keele.ac.uk) and Jamie Arathoon (j.arathoon1@yorksj.ac.uk) by Friday 28th February (This allows session organisers time to review proposals and submit the final session to the RGS-IBG portal by the official deadline of Friday 6th March 2026.)

 Please also let us know your contact details, affiliation, and any accessibility requirements.

papers.title: Deterring avian life: policy, property, and entangled justice
Leveraging Tourism to Reduce Animal Suffering

Rights of nature, jurisdictional geographies and the legal (il)legibility of wildlife

sessions.title: Living with Urban Inequality: Ageing, Well-being, and Mobility (1)
sessions.description:
Urban inequality is lived not only through income disparities or housing outcomes, but through everyday experiences of ageing, care, health, and movement across the city (Lefebvre, 1991; Harvey, 2008). As urban populations age amid widening socio-spatial inequalities, access to mobility, services, social infrastructure, and meaningful participation in urban life becomes increasingly uneven (WHO, 2007). These inequalities are shaped by intersecting relations of class, gender, ethnicity, migration status, and health, and are embedded in neighbourhood change, welfare restructuring, and urban governance (Soja, 2010).

 This session brings together urban geographical research that examines how inequality is produced, experienced, and negotiated through the everyday mobilities and immobility of ageing populations. Mobility is understood broadly, encompassing physical movement, access to urban services, social participation, and connections across neighbourhood, city, and transnational scales. The session foregrounds well-being as relational and spatially contingent, shaped by both structural conditions and everyday practices of care, support, and belonging (O’Neil, 2020).

 We invite papers that engage empirically and/or theoretically with urban inequality through the lenses of ageing, well-being, and mobility. Relevant themes include but are not limited to: 

 - Ageing in unequal urban environments
- Health, well-being, and spatial inequality
- Everyday mobilities, immobility, and access to the city
- Migration, transnational ageing, and urban belonging
- Gendered, racialised, and classed experiences of later life
- Care, social infrastructure, and neighbourhood support systems
- Urban policy, governance, and the uneven provision of care and services
- Methodological approaches to researching ageing and inequality in cities

 The session welcomes qualitative, comparative, and critical contributions that situate lived experience within wider urban processes, contributing to long-standing debates in urban geography around inequality, social reproduction, and uneven development.

papers.title: Urban environments’ impacts on  aging in post-industrial cities:  reviewing the pathways to  healthy aging
affiliations: University of the West of Scotland

sessions.title: East and West in Israel/Palestine: Urban Inequalities in Rehabilitation, Demolition, and Infrastructure
sessions.description:
The disputed landscape of Israel/Palestine, shaped by modernization and colonial contestation, reveals deep-rooted inequalities embedded in urban planning and spatial design. This session explores the complexities of urban transformation, including planning and infrastructure, which often lead to rehabilitation in some areas and destruction in others. Through diverse spatial analyses by geographers, historians, archaeologists, and urban planners, we investigate the interactions among modernization, urban erasure, urban heritage, and social inequality in cities such as Jerusalem, Jaffa, and Lod/Lid.

 In "Defining East: The Rehabilitation of East Jerusalem before and after 1967," Noah Hysler Rubin and Abigail Jacobson examine the role of the East Jerusalem Development Society (EJDS) in rehabilitating inner-city neighborhoods before and after the 1967 war. They analyze urban planners' priorities regarding reconstruction efforts and the definition of urban heritage, and challenge traditional definitions of East and West Jerusalem.

 In "From East to East: Displacement, Housing, and the Spatial Reorganization of Tel Aviv-Jaffa," Elia Etkin presents the process of large-scale housing construction initiated by the Halamish company in Jaffa during the 1960s. She situates the development of new housing within broader urban renewal policies while also highlighting residents' resistance to urban clearance and the social ramifications of displacement.

 In "Urban Erasure and Unequal Development: Planning, Demolition, and Spatial Inequality in Lod / Lid," Tawfiq Da'adli analyzes how modern urban planning in Lod has perpetuated spatial inequality and urban erasure following the Nakba. His paper reveals how post-1948 planning practices have reshaped the city through processes of demolition and redevelopment, resulting in a fragmented urban landscape marked by disparities among Palestinian and Jewish communities.

 Finally, in "Cumulative Infrastructural Injustice: The Longue Durée of Telecommunications in Palestine/Israel," Shira Wilkof traces the development of telecommunication infrastructure in urban and rural Palestine/Israel from British colonial rule to contemporary Israel. She demonstrates how modern infrastructural systems have materially organized the dynamics of colonialism, sovereignty, and contested citizenship, offering the concept of "cumulative infrastructural injustice" as a crucial research paradigm for studying diverse mechanisms of exclusion.

 Together, the papers in this session engage with the emergence of urban inequalities through modern planning, urging an exploration of the power relations guiding allegedly mundane planning and infrastructure in the context of Israel/Palestine.

papers.title: From East to East: Displacement, Housing, and the Spatial Reorganization of Tel Aviv-Jaffa
Cumulative Infrastructural Injustice: The Longue Durée of Telecommunications in Palestine/Israel
Urban Erasure and Unequal Development: Planning, Demolition, and Spatial Inequality in Lod / Lid
Defining East: The Rehabilitation of East Jerusalem before and after 1967 through the East Jerusalem Development Society

sessions.title: Rural Transformations and Fire
sessions.description:
Land use change is widely recognised as a key driver of changing fire regimes (UNEP 2022). Across much of the world, such changes are deeply entangled with ongoing rural transformations that reshape how land is used, managed, inhabited, and governed. In this panel, we ask: how are rural transformations reconfiguring fire regimes in the twenty-first century?
 We approach agrarian change as not simply a process of rural abandonment or a shift away from agriculture. Rather, across geographies, it unfolds unevenly, often involving partial and incomplete shifts away from traditional livelihood practices. Agrarian change is better understood as a constellation of transformations in land use, labour relations, market integration, household composition, and rural demography (Li 2014; Bernstein 2021). Contemporary rural transformations may involve livelihood diversification, seasonal migration, new conservation regimes, climate mitigation policies, rewilding initiatives, or intensified commercial agriculture. Together, these shifts reconfigure the social and ecological conditions under which fire burns. Shifts in rural labour through migration and off-farm employment can alter the timing, frequency, and collective coordination of controlled burning (Yadav 2026). With fewer people living and working on the land, everyday fire stewardship may decline, leading in some contexts to fuel accumulation and heightened wildfire risk (Mathews and Malfatti 2024). In others, intensified state regulation, conservation, or carbon-oriented climate interventions redefine legality, marginalise traditional burning practices, and reinforce fire as a problem to be suppressed (Smith, Perkins and Mistry 2022; Eilenberg 2022).
 We are particularly interested in how changing configurations of land, labour, and livelihood strategies affect:

 - The continuity or transformation of traditional fire use practices
- Collective systems of fire management
- Intergenerational transmission of fire knowledge
- The capacity of communities and states to respond to unwanted or catastrophic fires
- The politics of blame, responsibility, and governance in changing rural contexts

 We welcome contributions from both the Global North and the Global South that attend to the uneven, messy, and often contradictory micro-processes through which agrarian change unfolds on the ground and how shifting rhythms of rural life are actively reshaping the conditions under which fire is used, suppressed, politicised, or allowed to burn.
 References:
 United Nations Environment Programme (UNEP) (2022) Spreading like Wildfire: The Rising Threat of Extraordinary Landscape Fires . Nairobi: UNEP.
 Li, Tania Murray (2014) Land’s End: Capitalist Relations on an Indigenous Frontier . Durham, NC: Duke University Press.Bernstein, Henry (2021) Class Dynamics of Agrarian Change . Halifax & Winnipeg: Fernwood Publishing.
 Yadav, Kapil (2026) ‘Agrarianising the forest fire crisis: rethinking forest fires from grassroots in the Uttarakhand Himalaya’, The Journal of Peasant Studies , 1–22. https://doi.org/10.1080/03066150.2025.2606880
 Mathews, Andrew S. & Malfatti, Federico (2024) ‘Wildfires as legacies of agropastoral abandonment: Gendered litter raking and managed burning as historic fire prevention practices in the Monte Pisano of Italy’, Ambio , 53, pp. 1065–1076. https://doi.org/10.1007/s13280-024-01993-x
 Smith, C., Perkins, O. & Mistry, J. (2022) ‘Global decline in subsistence-oriented and smallholder fire use’, Nature Sustainability , 5(6), pp. 542–551. https://doi.org/10.1038/s41893-022-00867-y
 Eilenberg, Michael (2022) ‘The last enclosure: smoke, fire and crisis on the Indonesian forest frontier’, The Journal of Peasant Studies , 49(5), pp. 969–998. https://doi.org/10.1080/03066150.2021.1907355

sessions.title: Plants and Empires: The Botanical Stories of Critical Geographies of Race and Indigeneity
sessions.description: Plants are alive and so is empire. Botanical extractions, repositories, sciences, and speculations were, and continue to be, central to imperial projects and relations. In this session, contributors examine the relationships between plants and empire through creative angles, in particular posing analysis through a critique of the garden. Several papers in this session juxtapose the palatable nature of the garden magazine form with the brutal colonial political economic processes laden within forms of “the garden”. The garden is a valorized cultural form in a white supremacist imaginary of human-environment relations. The garden is a space of recreation, of social reproduction, an outside to/escape from the drudgery of late capitalism. But it is an inescapably imperial form. The garden engendered imperial occupation and dispossession and empire engendered the niceness, valorization, and the aesthetics of the garden as we know it. Through the framing of the “garden”, we examine and unpack the themes: living empires, imperial and decolonial possibilities of botanical collections, and the co-constituted agency of colonized peoples and their plant relations. Contributors in this session draw on methods and approaches from geographies of race, Indigenous geographies, Black geographies and Black ecologies as well as materially-grounded geographical critique. Doing so we offer an examination of large scale processes of political economic transformation and place-specific resistance to those processes by conducting our analyses through specific plants and the aesthetic logics and forms of “the garden.” In this session, contributors touch on some of the following: How did pine trees make much of the movement of British empire possible? How does the cochineal insect that only grows on cactus in a small region of Mexico color and clothe empires the world over? What should we make of ornamental camellias that traveled alongside tea plants on East India Company ships and became quintessential in English gardens? How might we restore Indigenous knowledge and relationships to the plant materials held in drawers and bottles and vials in Kew’s Economic Botany collection, and repurpose them intellectually for de-imperialized futures? This session touches on these questions and much more on the imperial relations of plants and botanical resistances to empire across the earth.

sessions.title: Flipping the script: Exploring the possibilities of applied theatre for bringing Indigenous, vernacular and place-based knowledges into climate change adaptation
sessions.description:
Finding meaningful ways to blend scientific knowledges of the physical effects of climate change as measured through tools and modelling advancements, and local knowledges of at-risk communities about the availability and success of place-based adaptation options is an urgent challenge. Hosted by the Cl imate Collaboratorium Project, this session explores the transformative potential of creative, participatory and emergent approaches such as applied theatre, design thinking, improvisation and futures labs, in supporting place-based responses to climate change adaptation in communities exposed to environmental risks. Bringing together international perspectives, it pays particular attention to situated emergences across a diverse range of local examples. 

 Keen to amplify and connect the - often hidden - communities, projects and practitioners engaging in interdisciplinary arts-based approaches to climate change adaptation, Flipping the script is designed as a participatory session that shares the framing, processes and practices of work in different settings. We seek contributions from projects working directly with communities, and our aim is to broaden the voices, perspectives and approaches represented in this work. We encourage submissions that involve community voices, creative practitioner and artist perspectives and those that use exploratory, arts-based, interactive and experimental ways of investigating issues and presenting ideas.
 Each contribution will have 10 minutes, followed by a creatively facilitated plenary, supporting questions, discussion and new connections.

papers.title: Severn StoryLab: Developing an emergent applied theatre process to promote hopeful adaptive thinking as a place-based response to climate change

sessions.title: Inequality, religion, faith, and spirituality: towards a just world? (1)
sessions.description: Religion, faith and spirituality are affected by and affect inequalities. They can inspire and contribute towards the building of just places, and they can create tension. This session welcomes papers from scholars of all career stages who wish to explore the intersection of inequalities and religion/faith/spiritualities within their research. Recent engagements with the religious-spiritual have tended to focus on the lived realities, incorporating examinations of embodied identities, everyday practices, diversifying beliefs, and emotional and affective dimensions of religion. These operate on scales from the local through to the national and international. Much work in this sub-discipline has considered the Christian faith. We both welcome contributions focussing on Christianity and encourage papers focussing on other faiths and spiritualities.

sessions.title: Housing, Population Change, and Spatial Inequality (1)
sessions.description: Housing is a central area through which social and spatial inequalities are (re)produced and experienced. Processes of population change, including migration, ageing, and urban growth, are deeply intertwined with housing systems. This session explores how housing both reflects and structures population change across different geographical contexts, contributing toward spatial injustice. We welcome papers that examine the relationship between housing and population change, including but not limited to, housing affordability and population change; gentrification and displacement; migration, refugees, and housing precarity; population growth and housing supply; and the impacts of housing policy on population dynamics. Papers presented in this session may draw on diverse theoretical perspectives, quantitative or qualitative methods, and varied geographic scales.

sessions.title: Infrastructural Deaths (1): The Geographies and Temporalities of Infrastructural Deaths
sessions.description:
“[T]here is no ‘end’ to infrastructure”, state Ramakrishnan et al. (2021: 682) in their call for researchers to move beyond temporal assumptions of infrastructures that transition linearly from creation to afterlife and focus instead on ongoing transformations in infrastructural relations. However, recent events such as the political crisis around the irreversible shut down of the UK’s Scunthorpe steelworks also reveal that infrastructural ‘deaths’ – real or perceived – can have extraordinary political salience and should not be ignored. 

 To date, infrastructural deaths have been examined in a limited way in the interdisciplinary literature on infrastructural politics. Research has explored infrastructural disruptions and failures (Graham, 2010; Ranganathan, 2016; Silver, 2016), decay and ruination (Gupta, 2018; Ramakrishnan et al. 2021; Silver, 2021), infrastructural afterlives (Evans, 2021; Morris, 2021; Pike, 2019), urbicide and the strategic military ‘killing’ of infrastructure (Coward, 2008; Graham, 2005), the effects of infrastructural absences/disconnections (Kinder, 2016; Young & Keil, 2010), the (death) tolls of infrastructural labour (Addie, 2021; Tyner, 2019), and the decline of infrastructures through deindustrialisation (Emery, 2019; Pulido, 2016; Zhang, 2024). Surprisingly little social research has examined infrastructural decommissioning and retirement. What does exist has emerged in other fields such as energy geographies (De Laurentis & Windemere, 2024; McCauley, 2018; Windemere, 2019), engineering (Invernizzi et al. 2020), and law (Dike, 2017).

 Within this work, deaths have often been described in singular forms (urbicide), have been presented implicitly as temporary conditions (disruptions and failures), or have already happened (afterlives). In other cases, the political significance of death as a total, irrevocable, or inevitable phase state has been overlooked (decay/ruination), or the uniqueness of infrastructure as an object of analysis has been sidelined (deindustrialisation). Further research is therefore needed to conceptualise the political salience of infrastructural deaths, their geographies and temporalities, and the ways in which infrastructural constellations become reconfigured in relation to infrastructural deaths. 

 This session presents reflections on: 

 - What it means for an infrastructure to ‘die’ and the forms deaths take
- Where infrastructural deaths happen, over what timescales
- How infrastructural deaths are imagined, anticipated, contested, and managed, and the ways in which they recast the political relations surrounding infrastructures
- The labour required to anticipate, facilitate, mitigate, or prevent infrastructural deaths
- (Uneven) experiences of infrastructural deaths and the inequalities that produce/are reproduced through them
- The conditions of possibility infrastructural deaths create for new forms of social justice

papers.title: German Lignite Coal Infrastructure Between Burial and Exhumation; Death and Resurrection.
What has driven onshore wind farm closure in the UK and what happens next? Understanding end-of-life pathways

sessions.title: The ‘unlikely’ geography of climate leadership and innovation (1): Anticipatory governance
sessions.description:
Place-based climate leadership is usually associated with 
economically successful cities and regions, which have strong 
institutional capacity, fiscal resources, and favourable structural 
conditions (e.g. van der Heijden, 2019). In contrast, economically 
disadvantaged, peripheral and non-metropolitan places are often cast as 
vulnerable territories that are further peripheralised by net-zero, 
energy and green transitions (e.g. Golubchikov and O’Sullivan, 2020). 
This aligns with economic and political geography scholarship that 
emphasises the political constraints and institutional deficits faced by
 ‘left-behind’ places (e.g. Rodríguez-Pose, 2018). However, evidence 
also emerges that some of the most economically disadvantaged places 
become national leaders in climate and net-zero policy and governance 
(Golubchikov et al., 2026). Undoubtedly, economic agglomeration and 
favourable structural conditions matter, but they are not deterministic 
(Haupt et al., 2023). Motivated local actors – often working through 
coalitions spanning local government, civil society and businesses – can
 shape strong climate vision and action (Homsy, 2018; Haupt et al., 
2025). The lesson is that climate and net-zero leadership is a story of a
 more distributed geography than is often assumed. 

 Understanding this ‘unlikely geography’ of climate leadership remains
 limited, yet it is important for ensuring a genuinely just transition. 
First, recognising leadership and innovation beyond economically 
successful regions is essential for epistemic justice: it challenges who
 is seen as a legitimate source of innovation, governance and 
experimentation. Second, the experiences of less wealthy and peripheral 
places are potentially more transferable to the other regions facing 
similar constraints than the more celebrated cases. Two further key 
issues, however, are economic impact and political durability. Can such 
‘peripheral’ climate leadership translate into wider economic and social
 opportunities for these places? And can it be sustained over time amid 
persistent institutional and financial limitations, alongside 
intensifying backlash and contestation around climate policy?

 This session includes contributions that investigate this ‘unlikely’ 
geography of climate leadership – exploring how poorer and peripheral 
places are not only sites of risk and vulnerability, but also potential 
innovators, leaders, and agenda-setters in the net-zero transition.

papers.title: The unlikely geographies of climate governance: what makes ‘left-behind’ areas lead?
Net zero by stealth: multi-solving and the unlikely geography of climate leadership in rural Wales
The local government challenges of compatible land-based targets for biodiversity and climate change
An ‘unlikely’ green window of opportunity to reconfigure uneven fertilizer geographies through Kenya’s green fertilizer ambitions
Innovating here or imitating there? Spatial subjectivities of renewable energy innovation related to a Rwandan technical college

sessions.title: Geographies of claims-making in international law (1)
sessions.description:
There is near-universal agreement—in policy circles, academic debate, and public discourse—that international law is undergoing a moment of profound crisis. Whether through the erosion of long-standing multilateral institutions, escalating geopolitical fragmentation, or disputes over the legitimacy of international legal norms, commentators frequently diagnose a system under strain, if not experiencing outright rupture (e.g. Kinstler 2025; Tzouvala 2025). Yet paradoxically, this crisis has not diminished claims on and to international law. If anything, the actors who invoke, contest, and rework international law have multiplied. An increasingly diverse array of participants including states, social movements, Indigenous Peoples, corporations, NGOs and transnational advocacy networks turn to international legality—sometimes strategically and sometimes aspirationally—as a register through which to assert political claims, demand accountability, and imagine alternative political futures. The contemporary landscape is therefore marked by an expansion, rather than a retreat, of claims-making in international law. This expansion, in turn, raises important questions for geographers. Where, how, and by whom is international law being articulated, negotiated, and spatialised? With what social, material, and political effects? And how should these phenomena be reconciled with the challenges the international legal order currently faces?

 This session brings together scholars to examine the geographies of international legal claims-making. In doing so, we speak to the relative neglect of international law within legal geography scholarship (Jones and Smith 2015; Delaney 2017), while also encouraging greater dialogue between legal, political, and historical geographers (McGeachan 2017; Braverman et al. 2014; Graham and Bartel 2016).

 We invite contributions engaging with (but not limited to) the following questions:

 - What geographical imaginaries underpin and are produced through practices of international legal claims-making?
- How do sites such as courts and treaty bodies, in addition to ‘alternative’ sites of legal mobilisation, shape the forms and possibilities of international legal claims-making?
- What spatial practices (e.g. mapping, documenting, witnessing, archiving) underpin the construction of international legal claims?
- How do international legal claims circulate across local, national, regional, and global scales, and how are they transformed in the process?
- What material infrastructures support or constrain international legal claims-making?
- How does engaging in international legal claims-making contribute to the formation or transformation of geopolitical subjectivities?
- How do practices of international legal claims-making interact with, reinforce, or challenge domestic legal regimes?
- How can attention to historical forms of international legal claims-making illuminate the dynamics of the present global conjuncture?
- What role can geographers and geographical knowledge play in the construction of international legal claims?
- What methods are best suited to the study of international legal claims-making

sessions.title: The social reproduction of platform economies (1)
sessions.description:
The social reproduction of platform economies

 Organisers: Sam Nowak (Durham University) and Carolyn Prouse (Queen’s University) 

 Amidst the ongoing crisis of care and in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, there has been a resurgence of interest in the geographies, infrastructures and labour of social reproduction (Rodríguez-Rocha, 2021; Prouse, 2025). In parallel, geographers have highlighted how all manner of social processes are now increasingly intermediated through digital platforms, powered by big data, and shaped by algorithms (Langley and Leyshon, 2017). This session invites papers concerned with these converging trends, exploring the social reproduction of platform economies.

 While it has become axiomatic that platform firms generate value through interlinked processes of data capture (Langley and Leyshon, 2017), labour exploitation (van Doorn and Badger, 2020), and rent extraction (Sadowski, 2020), we suggest that thinking through social reproduction helps move beyond universalisations of ‘the platform’ to consider the everyday practices, social infrastructures, and racialised and gendered labour regimes that underpin, and are reproduced through, platform economies. Recent scholarship has highlighted the shortfalls of universalising and economistic theories of the platform through a focus on: how on-demand domestic work economies sometimes fail (Dattani, 2021); the marketisation, management, and monitoring of domestic space through various forms of ‘caring’ surveillance (Sadowski, Strengers & Kennedy, 2021); the differential encoding of bodies for access to food and other means of social reproduction (Akbari, 2025); the collectivisation practices of urban gig workers to manage occupational risk (Kwan, 2022; Nowak, 2023); and the variegated gendered effects/affects of platform work and digital economies (James, 2024; Hardcastle & Loomis, 2025). 

 In this session we build on this work to ask: How are digital technologies and platform monopolies re-configuring communities of care, mutual aid labor, and infrastructures of social reproduction? What conceptualisations of social reproduction are useful at this moment to reveal the extractive nature of digital capitalism and the re-making of vulnerability/precarity/life/care? How might a focus on ‘life’s work’ reshape how we think about the digitally-mediated city and urban life?

 This session invites papers that seek to answer these questions. We are interested in work that includes, but is not limited to:

 - The reconfiguration of urban spaces and processes of social reproduction through platform and gig economy work;
- The (re)production of gendered, racialised, classed, migrant and caste precarity through platform economies; 
- Dependence on Big Tech infrastructure for everyday social reproduction, and/or the collectivised contestations of this dependence; 
- Glitch politics and digital infrastructural failure
- The relationship between social reproduction, the digital commons, and enclosure
- The affective allure of predictive analytics, digital surveillance, and genAI in everyday spaces of social reproduction 
- The platformisation of care and domestic work
- The platformisation of housing, education, and health systems and technologies
- Socio-technical networks of mutual aid, platform cooperatives, and/or social movements that make life in the shadow of fascism
- Digital and platform technologies of biological reproduction, including femtech and reprotech

papers.title: Between calculability and absences: The platformisation of paid domestic work in Santiago de Chile
Platformised domestic labour
Deregulating the City: Platform work, food cultures, and the shifting urban landscape in Bangalore
Delivering and caring from the driver’s seat: automobility and social reproduction in platform food delivery

sessions.title: Researching the Digitalising State (1)
sessions.description: Across the world, the state is undergoing deep, fundamental shifts in how it produces data, manages information and digitises bureaucratic workflows. The everyday work of the state – to govern - is now enmeshed with technological appendages that have created what Sandra Braman so eloquently calls a ‘Change of State’ (Braman 2006). There is now a range of complex forms of ‘stateness’ (Jones 2002) arising from the last few decades of state restructuring, accelerated by the proliferation of digital/geospatial platforms and the increasing presence of private-sector actors within state offices. While these developments promise efficiency, transparency, and accountability in the state, they also reconfigure state power in ways that are often opaque, uneven, and difficult for critical geographers to study (Datta and Reyes Carranza 2026). This panel will bring together methodological contributions that interrogate the ‘digitalising state’ (Datta 2023) – a dynamic assemblage of state power across spaces, scales and actors, across public, private and multilateral organisations, across paper and digital information infrastructures, and across political-technological terrains of data, code and platforms. While the state has been notoriously hard to research, there have been significant contributions to investigating the ‘everyday state’ (Hull 2012; Gupta 2012; Dunleavy et al. 2006) from anthropology and organisation studies. What might critical geographers learn from these approaches, and how might we deploy a geographical lens to studying the digitalising state?

papers.title: Working on and ‘working with’ AI in researching digitalising states
An Experience of Using Social Network Analysis and Sankey Diagrams to Decipher Digital Regional Governance in China’s Greater Bay Area
The Digital Turn in Dutch and Danish Ports:  A Strategic Narrative and Ontological Security Lens to Technology Adoption
Methodological challenges in charting the data terrain of housing, property and planning in Ireland
From crisis to governmental fix: Platformisation-as-negotiation in authoritarian Vietnam

sessions.title: Artificial Intelligence and its Fascist Geographies
sessions.description:
As we write this abstract, a standoff between tech giant Anthropic and the US government has made very stark the stakes in an emergent set of formations where technocapitalism and Artificial Intelligence are increasingly the ground for growing fascism globally. In this case, Anthropic has presented itself as the ethical bulwark against the US state’s demand for its AI infrastructure as the backbone for new surveillance, and “fully autonomous weapons”, i.e., arms that will not even require the gamified agencies that drone operators exercise. This pushes us to question the role of AI-led technocracy in circumscribing and attending to neoliberal, authoritarian, and fascist futures.

 Tech companies are far from reliable ethical counterweights to fascist regimes. This is evident in the role that Generative AI tech has played in rising authoritarianism globally, not only in the US and the UK, but also in India, Israel, and beyond. These same surveillance, facial recognition, and monitoring technologies increasingly also enact racial capitalist pre-crime regimes, regulate the availability of healthcare, education, and supermarket products, and privatise access to previously public forms of knowledge (i.e. archival material) (Fathallah, 2026; Yang, 2025). Additionally, the datacenters required to power and house such technologies are reproducing fossil fuel energy regimes and exacting new and compounding forms of health and environmental burden on marginalised communities they occupy. Indeed, scholars increasingly argue that with this interpenetration of technologically dense infrastructures of surveillance in everyday life, states across the world are reneging on their social function to instead enact militarised forms of governance (Colón Vargas, 2025, Marie Hampton, 2023). This is the context within which we position our work, which interrogates the relationship between the rapid and often unquestioned normalisation of Generative AI in social and political life, and the pervasive spread of authoritarian and fascist tendencies among states globally. 

 Specifically, in this panel, we ask how neoliberal regimes, driven by the protection of private capital interests over social democracy have led to AI-led technocratic governance. We ask how Artifical Intelligence has intensified racial capitalist and settler colonial regimes of land dispossession, surveillance, carcerality, and genocide across landscapes in Europe, North America, and the Global South. We are interested in the gendered, racialised, and class politics of Generative AI, and the normalised violence it enables in its everyday use.

papers.title: Datacenters as Carceral Geographies? Datacenter Proliferation, Environmental Injustice, and 21st Century Fascism in Atlanta
“There’s nothing I can do”: AI and the coercion of apathy amongst Canadian public sector workers
Im/material Girlfriends: The Political Concept of AI Girlfriends
A Luddite Manifesto, or against the Taylorisation of Intellectual Labour
Policing Intimacies, Colonial Roots, and Intertwined Pipelines of Militarised Surveillance: Tracing the Political Economy of Military AI

sessions.title: New Research in the History and Philosophy of Geography 2026
sessions.description:
This session invites geographers working in diverse contexts across the discipline to share how their research connects or contributes to the history and philosophy of geography in some way. In particular the session welcomes postgraduate and early-career researchers who may wish to share the historical and/or theoretical contexts underpinning their research. The session is intended to provide a relaxed and friendly forum for researchers across and beyond the discipline of geography to share what they are working on with others.

 We welcome papers relating to the history of the discipline of geography, the history of geographical knowledge broadly construed, as well as histories and biographies of geographers and geographical thinkers.

 We also welcome papers relating to the philosophy of geography, philosophies used within geographical and adjacent disciplines, critical theory broadly construed, the development of geographical theories and the proposal of novel theories within geography and adjacent disciplines, as well as contributions exploring the theoretical contexts underpinning geographical or adjacent research.

 The session welcomes cross-, trans-, and multi-disciplinary research that may not straightforwardly or easily fit within the received categories of history and philosophy of geography. In addition, we welcome contributions which emphasise the politics of theory and the relevance of the history and/or philosophy of geography for addressing social, political, and economic challenges.

papers.title: A crack in everything: disruption in sustainability
The Relevance of Paul Ricouer’s Narrative Theory to Scholarship in Environmental Governance
Spatialising the intellectual: reflections from Doreen Massey’s archive
Institutional Inertia and Disciplinary Authority: Academic Cartography in Post-Soviet Russia

sessions.title: Un/translatable Geographies: querying the politics and ontologies of knowledge production in Latin America
sessions.description:
Gambiarra, jeitinho, mestizaje, machismo, territorio, buen vivir – sumak kawsay. In recent years, scholars publishing in English language have increasingly turned to specific notions emerging from fieldwork to contribute to geographical and broader academic debates. From marginal words in brackets or buried in fieldnotes, untranslatable notions have become centrepieces of empirical works and conceptual propositions. They are used to name and explain everyday practices and the complex social, material, and historical configurations in which they emerge, reflecting efforts to provincialise and decolonise knowledge (Yates and Nuñez 2021; Sultana 2025). 

 However, the circulation of these concepts is not uncontested. While situated concepts can pluralise notions of being and knowing the world, they can also repeat problematic patterns of exclusion and appropriation in the production of geographical knowledge. When moved across contexts, these concepts can shift, misalign, or even thicken. As they travel and become entangled in English-speaking geographies, these concepts’ textures can flatten; their singularity, irreducibly situated (Jazeel, 2019), might become domesticated. This risks reproducing epistemic hierarchies across locales, between theory and “case,” and between concepts that travel easily and those that remain marginal.

 This session invites scholars to think with the untranslatable in geographical research. Staying with the contradictions, paradoxes and dilemmas of knowledge production in English-speaking, Northern academia, it asks whether conceptual travel can generate productive misalignments, unsettling the status quo of knowledge production. Thinking with situated concepts, which are, for the lack of a better word, chi’ixi (Rivera Cusicanqui 2012), the session queries whether the mobilisation of concepts beyond their original contexts can open analytical, political, and relational horizons: energizing connection without sameness, and theory that remains accountable to place.

 Following the short interactive paper format (short presentations of five minutes each, followed by a chaired interactive discussion involving all the presenters and the audience to explore common themes from the papers),  presenters will tell a story about their engagement with one particular ‘untranslatable’ concept and how this speaks to three topics: analytical and political power; epistemic and geo-political hierarchies; and the ontological. Our goal is to have a discussion grounded in ethnographic material that engages critically with the un/translatable , asking not only what is lost, but also what becomes possible when concepts move.

sessions.title: Reimagining rural mobility: Everyday experiences, practices and opportunities in low-density regions (1)
sessions.description:
Rural transport disadvantages are often overlooked in academic and policy debates despite increasing evidence of inequalities linked to rural living, such as participation in education, labour and social life, as well as access to health. The majority of households have at least one car in rural areas of high-income economies such as the UK and a car is seen as essential to meet basic daily needs (Smith et al., 2012). Nevertheless, there are significant groups of people in rural areas who do not have car access (Chatterjee et al, 2019) or struggle financially to run a car (Mattioli et al, 2018). There is limited knowledge of the mobility experiences and practices of these groups. Buses are the main alternative for rural residents without a car, but the withdrawal of bus services in the UK risks increasing car dependence. There is strong interest currently in whether emerging mobility options such as demand responsive transport, car sharing and e-bikes can fill the gap and transform rural mobility. 

 These double sessions on rural mobility explore rural mobilities through empirical evidence from around the world, including:

 - Everyday mobility practices and coping strategies in low-density contexts, such as lift-giving, informal carpooling, and mobility rationing
- The consequences of limited mobility for access to education, employment, healthcare, care responsibilities, and social participation
- The intersectional dimensions of rural mobility disadvantage, including gender, age, disability, income, ethnicity, migration status, and caring roles
- The relationship between transport disadvantage, deprivation, poverty, and spatial inequality
- Longitudinal changes in mobility in response to policy interventions, such as bus service withdrawal or the introduction of demand-responsive transport
- Community-led, informal, or alternative mobility solutions and their limitations
- Alternative visions for the future of rural mobility

papers.title: Recognising Rural Mobility Practices: A systematic scoping review into user perspectives on shared and electric mobility
Ageing, residential mobility and access to healthcare in Aotearoa New Zealand
Car dependence, place and mobility in later life: Longitudinal evidence from NZPATHS
From rural mobility patterns to rural proofing: advancing decarbonisation and mobility justice
Understanding Trasport Disadvantage and User Experiences of Demand Responsive Transport in Rural Areas of England
affiliations: University of the West of England

sessions.title: Understanding Neighbourhoods from the Global South: Inside-Out Perspectives on Sustainable, Healthy and Learning Cities (1)
sessions.description:
Urban neighbourhoods – often the primary scale at which residents experience, organise and negotiate urban life – are increasingly recognised as critical sites for advancing sustainability, health and learning agendas. However, dominant theories, datasets and policy frameworks in neighbourhood studies are largely shaped by Global North perspectives (Baffoe and Kintrea, 2023), often adopting top-down approaches and externally defined models of urban development (UN-Habitat, 2014). These frameworks often fail to capture the socio-spatial complexities, informal systems and context-dependent everyday practices that characterise many neighbourhoods in the Global South (Parnell and Robinson, 2012). 

 This session advances an inside-out approach to neighbourhood studies in the Global South, highlighting residents' lived experiences, everyday practices and locally embedded knowledge systems. It builds on emerging comparative, place-based and co-produced approaches to understanding neighbourhoods (Lees et al., 2016), as well as neighbourhood-scale analysis from the Sustainable, Healthy and Learning Cities and Neighbourhoods project (Wang et al., 2025). We explore how neighbourhoods are planned, designed and managed under conditions of rapid urbanisation, informality, socio-economic inequality, and institutional complexity. 

We invite papers that critically examine planning approaches, urban design practices, and neighbourhood management and governance practices across cities in the Global South. The session welcomes contributions from scholars at all career stages and encourages submissions from researchers and practitioners working in, or in close collaboration with, communities in the Global South. Contributions may examine formal and informal planning systems, community-led and hybrid governance arrangements, neighbourhood design and public space, neighbourhood sustainability practices, service provision, mobility, health and learning environment, and data-driven neighbourhood interventions. We also welcome methodological papers that have used advanced quantitative or qualitative research approaches in examining neighbourhoods in the Global South. 

 References

 Baffoe, G. and Kintrea, K. (2023) 'Neighbourhood research in the Global South: What do we know so far?', Cities, 132, p. 104077. 
Lees, L., Shin, H. B. and López-Morales, E. (2016) Planetary gentrification. John Wiley & Sons. 
Parnell, S. and Robinson, J. (2012) '(Re) theorizing cities from the Global South: Looking beyond neoliberalism', Urban geography, 33(4), pp. 593-617. 
UN-Habitat (2014) A New Strategy of Sustainable Neighbourhood Planning: Five principles – Urban Planning Discussion Note 3. [Online]. Available at: https://unhabitat.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/05/5-Principles_web.pdf. 
Wang, Y. P., Kintrea, K., Everatt, D. and Kundu, D. (eds.) (2025) New Drivers of Division: Urbanisation and Spatial Inequality in Africa and Asia. Springer Nature.

sessions.title: Contestations of green transitions in UK agriculture (1)
sessions.description:
The green agricultural transition in the UK is at a fractious, contested, and pivotal moment. Post-Brexit changes to agri-environmental schemes have yet to realise significant success in minimising the environmental impact of agriculture and have led to farmers' protests across the UK. While much of the UK's net-zero plans rely on support  from rural (and farming) communities, reactionary groups have exploited farmers' feelings of uncertainty, frustration, and a lack of trust in the government to promote anti-net-zero sentiments . The notion of a just transition is key to any future green transition in UK agriculture, yet how this transition can be realised, and what a "just future" even looks like, remains unclear.

 This session explores the possibilities threats, opportunities, limitations of, and resistances to green agricultural transitions within the UK. Themes include but are not limited to:

 - Farmers' perspectives on climate change and the green transition
- The role of experiential knowledge in a achieving a just transition in agriculture
- Farmers' protests and the attempted co-option of these protests by reactionary groups
- Policy or financial barriers to agricultural transitions
- Visions for a "just future" of farming

papers.title: Mother nature: an intersectional analysis of environmental engagement in Kentish farm women
Land-Based Greenhouse Gas Removal and a Just Transition in UK Agriculture: Biochar Stakeholder Perspectives on Challenges and Opportunities
Imagining and realising a just future for farming: visions from farm women across England

sessions.title: Agricultural land and the Urban Periphery (1)
sessions.description:
Urban scholars argue that the periphery must take center stage in urban enquiry, especially in the Global South where most urbanisation occurs through the conversion of agricultural lands (Gururani, 2020; Kennedy & Sood, 2016a). Far from being a residual or marginal space, it has become a key terrain for mega-projects, world-class smart cities, and speculative real estate (Goldman, 2011; Kennedy et al., 2014; Raman, 2016). Periphery is shaped through the continuous interactions between rural and urban actors, institutions, and processes (Balakrishnan & Gururani, 2021). The promised idea of development through conversion of agricultural lands is often portrayed as future-oriented, and marketised neatly in form of planned development, smart cities, high-rise gated residential neighbourhoods, and shopping centres. However, this future oriented imagination is often complex and layered with historical past, and present. This is particularly relevant in context of periphery, where land, memory, and kinship remain deeply interwoven in form of colonial land regimes, ancestral claims to land, common lands, or historical associations to land (Simpson, 2014). This also sometimes becomes a subtle form of resistance to the future-oriented disciplining of planned development projected through policies and master plans. 

 The inherited inequalities due to differential power relations because of social, political, spatial, and economic relations gets refigured during these transitions of agricultural lands at the urban periphery. This largely aligns with the theme of the conference focusing on complexities of spatial inequalities at the periphery. Through this call for proposal, we seek to invite papers across various geographies undergoing transformation of agricultural lands on the periphery. We are looking for papers that engage conceptually, methodologically, or empirically grounded with the themes including (not limited to): temporality, contestation of land use changes, role of state, landownership and tenure, and social and cultural relations associated with agricultural lands. This could include exploring questions around:

 - What are the different forms of practices rooted in past or present that actors engage on agricultural lands? 
- How do these practices interact with the future oriented planned development envisioned through the conversion of agricultural lands? 
- Do these practices contest, resist, or further widen the inherited power asymmetries associated with agricultural lands? 
- What are the methods that can evoke the past relation or absences and compel the actors to push forward or construct the image of lands in transition against the future oriented maps or images produced through policies or plans?
- How do we capture the emotions, the visuals, and the spatial-temporal changes taken place at the periphery that weaves together the past, present, and future?

papers.title: Everyday Practices and Land Speculation in Delhi’s Agrarian Periphery
Fragmented Urban Frontiers: Topologies of Power and the Making of Uneven Land and Housing Markets in Sonipat District
Unfinished State: Agricultural Land and the Urban Periphery in East England

sessions.title: Cultivating Just Community Food Systems through Food Sharing (1)
sessions.description:
Socio-economic, environmental and spatial inequalities are central to how Food Sharing Initiatives (FSIs), from community gardens and landed kitchens, to seed saving initiatives and community composting, develop, organize, and operate. These inequalities shape the landscapes that FSIs are embedded in, as well as their strategies for inclusion, advocacy, and transformation.

 Spatial inequalities in land access and governance, for example, directly influence the ways in which collective growing initiatives access and govern land, and which populations they are accountable to. For example, in cities across the US racialized disinvestments in black neighborhoods through red lining are linked to an abundance of vacant land and the demand for community gardens. At the same times, spatial and socio-economic inequalities that manifest in gentrification and land speculation can make community gardens unwitting accomplices in green gentrification. Under conditions of climatic change, multilayered riskscapes increasingly affect adaptive capacities around community food initiatives internationally, with community gardens rebranded as urban nature-based solutions in some locations.

 The COVID 19 pandemic revealed the fragility of the capitalist food system, and the resilience of the community food system – where countless forms of mutual aid (from community fridges to community kitchens) mobilized to rescue and redistribute food and/or access food from local growers, to feed to those in need, alongside more established food banks and food aid mechanisms. Socio-economic and power inequalities are central to how food sharing is organized, beginning from the economic inequalities that create the need for food assistance, to the power inequalities between food donors and receivers, to social inequalities and stigmas related to charity and surplus food.

 In this session we invite food sharing scholars and community-based food practitioners to reflect on the role of food sharing initiatives in creating and sustaining just places. We challenge contributors to address inequalities and injustices that shape how these initiatives develop and operate, and what injustices might still need to be overcome for FSIs to be successful in meeting their mission, and creating more just community food systems and places. We are interested in approaches and tools developed to be a counterweight to inequalities and the extent to which food sharing initiatives collaborative with other actors in their efforts to do this. We are keen to hear about diverse community food imaginaries and research exploring tensions between these and those expressed by public or private sector actors.

 We welcome theoretically and empirically driven papers (or more creative interventions that can be shared in 15 minutes) that address topics such as:

 Art and citizen engagement

 Serious Games

 Food sharing

 Food governance

 Food Infrastructure

 Food Commons

 Food sustainability

 Care

 Conviviality

 Community cooking & eating

 Community growing

 Surplus Food Redistribution

 Agroecological urbanism

 Landed Community Kitchens

 Communities of food practice

 Critical food mapping

 Community food imaginaries

 Collective food action

papers.title: Infrastructures of Care and Food Agency: Reconfiguring Collective Food Practices in Valencia

sessions.title: Historicising Urban Labour Regimes: In/Visibility, Infrastructural Labour, and Resistance (1)
sessions.description:
The relationship between urbanisation and (semi)proletarianisation reflects distinct historical conjunctures shaped by colonialism, racial capitalism, and the uneven insertion of economies into global circuits of capital. These processes have produced urban labour regimes in which informality, precarity, household-based production, and circular migration are not transitional anomalies but enduring and politically consequential forms of urban life.

 Despite precarious workers' contributions to urban reproduction through 'essential' infrastructural labour (Fredericks, 2014, 2021; Gidwani, 2015; Gidwani and Chari, 2004; Stokes and De-Coss Corzo, 2023), worker histories and historical struggles remain underexplored in geographical debate. In the archival register, we find scant information of worker lives in records of the colonial and postcolonial state, and in the ethnographic register, the social reproductive realm and unwaged or ‘hidden’ labour are often treated as less important or accessible relative to the politics of waged work and the worksite. Increasingly precarious conditions—exacerbated by climate stress, privatization, debt, xenophobia, authoritarianism and surveillance—make tracing these histories and contemporary realities ever more pressing.

 This panel invites historically grounded and empirically diverse contributions across geographies and sectors that foreground comparative approaches to urban labour regimes. It asks: how have different historical pathways of urbanisation shaped labour relations and struggles in southern cities across class, gender, caste and race? We invite reflections on whether Southern urban labour histories might illuminate contemporary Northern urban conditions marked by platform economies, migrant labour preponderance, and social reproduction infrastructure transformations. However, we also question the risks of universalising labour categories and temporal narratives. How might historicising urban labour differently enable more situated, worker-centred theories of urban development?

papers.title: State-labour relations and the making of (post)colonial cities: The case of informal transport workers in Dar es Salaam, Tanzania
The Urban in Circuits of Indenture: A Critical Review
Urban Inequalities, Street Markets, and Grassroots Labour Regimes in Hong Kong
Migrant Care Labour and Regimes of In/Visibility in Cyprus and Singapore

sessions.title: Doing diplomacy beyond national jurisdiction (1)
sessions.description:
Environmental diplomacy is inherently geographical (see Kuus, 2016). It takes place; it is geographically placed (in specific nations, cities, and then in particular buildings (Touloumi, 2024), etc., as well as online (i.e. Vadrot et al. 2021)), and it involves hierarchies of socio-political place, rendered through power dynamics (i.e. ‘weaker’ states vs. ‘powerful’ ones, global north and south, ‘developing’, ‘developed’, ‘small island’ and so on). Diplomacy, then, involves inequalities of access, across different scales, from restricted institutional decision-making spaces (such as the UN) to places beyond national jurisdiction that are being discussed (i.e. the High Seas). 

 This session will pick apart the practices of researching in spaces of environmental diplomacy and bring to the fore difficulties encountered in undertaking research by actively engaging in spaces of environmental diplomacy (Hughes and Vadrot, 2023). In doing so, it also addresses how researchers might have to ‘do’ research diplomatically. It also considers the access points for those involved directly in diplomacy and decision-making. 

 In proposing this session, we take inspiration from the recent ratification of the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction (BBNJ Treaty), the ongoing negotiation of a Mining Code for the seabed beyond national jurisdiction by the International Seabed Authority (ISA), and a year of yet more global level environmental conferencing (i.e. COP and United Nations Ocean Conference (UNOC)). We invite submissions that deal with all iterations of environmental diplomacy, however, with a focus on the governance of geographies beyond national jurisdiction. 

 Organisations such as the United Nations (UN) and annual meetings, including the Conference of the Parties (COP) and the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic and the Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), rely on individual nations to act as host countries. Meeting attendance is subject to certain delegate status, from visa costs and approvals, security guardrails, to adhering to the host country’s governance regimes. Membership and participation in meetings are often exclusive, resource-intensive, multilevelled, and deeply uneven across delegations and events, with convoluted processes for gaining access and limitations on speaking rights. We thus ask who gets to make (legally-binding) decisions, and on behalf of whom, as many of these negotiations tackle issues that concern us all.

papers.title: Invisible badge(s) and diplomatic agenda(s). How does (in)access shape our understanding of ‘One Ocean’ diplomacy at the Third United Nations Ocean Conference?

sessions.title: Justice in Tourism Geographies (1)
sessions.description:
Despite a long-held concern with issues of justice within geography, in recent years we have witnessed a growing recognition that justice is a core concern among tourism geographers (cf. Paddison & Hall, 2022; Tomassini & Lamond, 2023; Wan et. al., 2024). And yet, despite its high usage, there is often limited direct discussion from tourism geographers regarding the substance of justice. Geographers, including those working in tourism studies, have often been hesitant to articulate what they mean by ‘justice,’ concerned that doing so imposes a normative ideal of fairness. Instead, they tend to favour practice-based, pluriversal, ‘bottom-up’ understandings of justice (Barnett, 2016); a move that constructs a universal/pluriversal binary.

 In the past decade, however, geographers have cautioned that there is a requirement to openly discuss the substance of justice, to consider the specificities of claims in relation to another, avoid extreme relativism whereby all claims to justice are equally valid without grounds for critique, and steer clear of any reductions in the concept’s political and analytical utility (Barnett, 2016; Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). We draw on this critical commentary to call for open dialogue within the sub-discipline of tourism geographies, to the conditions through which injustices are recognised and addressed, to make sense of ‘how and why observable patterns of inequity, discrimination, or unevenness are actually unjust’ (Barnett, 2016, pp.112).

papers.title: Justice as a Constitutive Principle of Tourism Governance: A Metagovernance Perspective on Structural Inequality
