sessions.title: Movements, memories and moments of democratic localism (1)
sessions.description:
Localist experiments have a long, varied and ambivalent lineage (Beveridge and Cochrane 2023), connecting from the escapist commune, through different municipalist waves including the Greater London Council and Barcelona en Comu, to antagonistic and alternative provision practices of parallel institutions, such as public pharmacies (Gonnet et al, 2025), solidarity economies (Arampatzi 2022) and urban agriculture (Traill 2023). The new municipalist moment, apparently stalled and deemed in crisis (Roth et al, 2023), may be the loudest recent iteration of this. However, when contextualised in a broader swathe of more ordinary municipalisations across the globe (Cumbers and Paul 2020), the persistence of local practices of mutualism, experimentation and self-organisation becomes apparent; often operating within as well as outwith formal policy processes.

 In this session, we invite papers that attend to the persistence of what we term "democratic localism": an umbrella term we use to denote a global and often interlinked paradigm of locally-scaled political interventions, both in, against and beyond the state, that reimagine everyday life from the ground up. Often such projects draw inspiration and elements of practice from a global elsewhere, with a circulation of policies akin to the movement of policy – but driven by a more heterodox set of actors, less a set of jet-setting policy elite and more rooted in everyday action and activism. Such a hypothesized movement (loosely conceived) draws on social and professional networks, histories of activism and the reification of iconic places like Barcelona or Rojava as metonyms for a set of often participatory practices that are under-explored.

 Whilst the current political moment is increasingly framed by rising authoritarianisms and entrenched crises, these movements persist. How they do so, in inhospitable times, remains a provocative question.

papers.title: Advancing Democratic Localism: inhabitance, the politics of place and radical municipalism in Scotland and beyond

sessions.title: Food on the move (2): geographies of dietary change and (re)valuation
sessions.description: Efforts to improve the sustainability and resilience of Western food systems will ultimately entail eating differently. Whether adopting new food sources, refraining from others, or returning to earlier ways of eating, dietary change of some sort appears unavoidable. This raises important questions for those seeking to improve Western food systems. How do things become appreciated and popularised as food? How, in fact, do things become regarded as food in the first place? Food geographers have made important contributions towards answering such questions, particularly across topics such as production-consumption linkages, ‘alternative’ ways of organising and producing food, and explorations of how food (and food waste) are enacted and categorised in practice. However, there is still much to learn about how foods circulate and become (re)valued within different geographies of production and consumption, knowledge around which is essential if we are to organise Western food systems differently. This session aims to advance debates in these areas. It asks: how do foods move around, both physically (e.g. foods travelling to different places) and socioculturally (e.g. foods becoming de-/re-valued by different eaters)? What does this tell us about how Western food systems might be organised in a more just, resilient and sustainable way?

sessions.title: Walking on Uneven Ground: Access, Belonging and Environmental Change on Trail Landscapes (1)
sessions.description:
Trail-based activities such as hiking, trail running and off-road cycling are often imagined as accessible and inclusive practices, particularly within rural landscapes that are framed as shared, equal and open spaces for all. Yet geographers and social scientists increasingly demonstrate that they are not socially or environmentally neutral. Rather, they are infrastructures through which uneven relations of power, belonging, and responsibility are organised (Cloke & Little, 1997; Neal, 2002; Chakraborti & Garland, 2004; Shrubsole, 2019; Fowler, 2020).

 Across footpaths, rights of way, bridleways, access land, and National Trails, everyday practices of movement produce differentiated experiences of comfort, entitlement, surveillance, and exclusion along lines of race, class, gender, disability, age, and nationality. At the same time, trails are increasingly shaped by environmental pressures associated with climate change, biodiversity loss, and landscape degradation. These changes are often encountered not abstractly, but sensorially and affectively: through eroded paths, altered seasonal rhythms, flood-damaged routes, or transformed ecologies. As such, trails operate as sites where inequalities of access and belonging intersect with questions of care, responsibility, and human–environment relations.

 Whilst outdoor recreation has been examined through mobilities (Snead et al., 2009) leisure (Lanclus, 2023), tourism (Timothy & Boyd, 2015), wellbeing (Pomfret et al., 2024) and heritage (Svensson et al., 2022), trails themselves remain comparatively under-theorised as spacial formations that structure both social relations and environmental engagements. Frequently marketed as universally welcoming and resilient spaces, trails are none-the-less shaped by legal frameworks, governance, historical narratives and affective atmospheres that privilege particular bodies, practices, and modes of environmental engagement while marginalising others.

 This session brings together work that critically examines trails as uneven and unsettled landscapes of movement. The first timeslot will focus on issues of racialized belonging on trails. Elisa Sisto’s paper deals with the uneasy coexistence between histories of refuge and contemporary regimes of surveillance in the Alps and Pyrenees. Elsa Mertala’s contribution foregrounds lived experiences of Black hikers in the Scottish outdoors while Mohammed Dhalech looks at racialized belonging and care along the Hadrian’s Wall trail. The first timeslot concludes with Tom Bennett’s paper which interrogates how national trails in England, often framed as a rural idyll, are in fact entangled with wider geographies of deprivation. Taken together, these papers unsettle assumptions about who belongs on the trail, foregrounding the social, political and affective negotiations embedded in seemingly neutral landscapes.

sessions.title: The Human, Unevenly: Rethinking Human(ist) Geographies of Embodiment, Difference, and Everyday Inequality (2): Sensing Margins
sessions.description: Humanism is often treated in contemporary geography as something already overcome, yet much research continues to rely on assumptions about embodied subjects, situated meaning-making, and ordinary practices of everyday life. This session asks what it means to take that reliance seriously, attending to the uneven afterlives of humanist traditions as they have been critiqued, reshaped, and reworked through feminist, post-colonial, and post-phenomenological interventions among others. Rather than returning to or rejecting humanism, we invite reflection on how these inheritances persist—quietly and imperfectly—in geographical theory, method, and engagements with inequality across places as much as the life course. The session is particularly interested in research focused on marginal, liminal, and everyday lives: moments of transition, care, exhaustion, precarity, ageing, or maintenance; sites where inequality is felt materially and unevenly; and spaces where justice is negotiated through embodied relations with institutions, infrastructures, environments, and objects. We welcome reflections on methods and practices - whether ethnographic, narrative, participatory, interpretive - that continue to rely on situated and embodied knowledges and meanings, even where theoretical vocabularies have shifted. Rather than calling for a return to classical humanism or a rejection of post-humanist thought, this session opens space to ask how we are already working with the human, unevenly and imperfectly, and with what consequences for feminist, justice-oriented geographical research and debates about just places. In this session, instead of seeking theoretical resolution or disciplinary closure, we invite contributions that linger with limits: of bodies, of concepts, of methods, or ontologically. In doing so, it asks what kinds of geographical work become possible when the human is not treated as a problem to be overcome, but as something lived, negotiated, and unevenly shared.

papers.title: Tracing migration as a lived spatial practice: reflection from researching migration in Poland
Geographies of Pleasure: A New & Critical Framework
Bittersweet Sympathy: Desires for Nonhuman Auralities
affiliations: Royal Holloway University of London, Centre for Geohumanities

sessions.title: Climate justice, creativity and humour: learning with and from children and young people (1)
sessions.description:
Recent years have seen growing acknowledgement of the essential role of humour in communicating about climate change, including in education (Gerlofs and Zheng, 2025; Russell et al., 2023). This may seem at odds with such a serious topic. However, humour is a recognised tool of social justice (Borum Chattoo and Feldman, 2020) and may offer new and engaging approaches for climate change education (CCE) in the face of the climate fatigue and despair reported among many young people (Hickman et al., 2021; Sanson and Bellemo, 2021).

 As scholars and practitioners conducting research with children and young people on climate-related topics, humour has become an intriguing aspect of our work. We are interested to hear from others who are similarly interested in the ways that fun, creativity, irony, irreverence and other comedic devices have 
and could become tools of climate justice, complementing or disrupting more traditional pedagogic methods. 

 We welcome traditional, creative or interactive presentations exploring children and young people’s own use of humour, their responses to others’ use of humour in CCE (or in wider climate communication), and especially research that involves children and young people in a participatory process, emphasizing co-production. The use of comedic devices in presentation delivery is warmly welcomed but not required! 

 Questions to be explored include, but are not limited to:

 · Can there be fun in CCE? What role can humour play in CCE?

 · Why is humour important when working in CCE?

 · How can humour open serious questions of climate justice? 

 · How does humour enter CCE, through children and educators alike?

 · How can or could humour work across language, culture and generational divides to support climate solidarity?

 · How are humour and creativity mutually supporting?

 · What are the ethics of using humour in research with children on climate change?

papers.title: Playfulness, Humour, and Care in an Online Vietnamese Youth Climate Organisation
Creative and humorous forms of everyday resistance in the youth-led climate justice movement in the Philippines
Messing around with messy challenges
Children’s humour in their exploration of climate change and creation of eco-stories
Just Joking: Humour as Epistemic Practice in Climate Resilient Playground Co-Design

sessions.title: Geographies of Authoritarianism (1)
sessions.description:
State authoritarian practices are in the ascendency around the world and have been for several years (Diamond et al 2016). Geographers have an important role to play in understanding and mapping their development, analysing their causes and consequences, and identifying tactics that slow and reverse their progress (Koch 2022). The stakes are high: individual and collective freedoms are being eroded in economic, social, cultural, institutional, political, digital, intellectual, spiritual, reproductive and corporeal spaces (see Regilme 2021).

 This session features papers that use geographical approaches and insights to speak to the philosophy, epistemology, and empirical nature of authoritarian practices. Philosophically, we need to understand how human freedom has become devalued - there is a need to comprehend the modes of thought that make authoritarianism a seductive state strategy. Epistemologically, we need to confront the reality that authoritarianism is hard, and sometimes dangerous, to study owing to the vested interest of states in concealing it – perspectives that establish principles for the safe and effective study of authoritarianism in the current era of increasingly constrained academic freedom are needed. Empirically, we need approaches that connect and traverse insights from the geographical sub-disciplines in recognition of the cross-cutting nature of authoritarian practices. We also need to reflect on the balance between defense and critique of ostensibly liberal practices and institutions that claim to seek to uphold freedom and human rights.

 Whilst evidence and argument points towards the generalized pattern of increasing authoritarianism at a global scale (Diamond et al 2016) – a sense in which a similar set of authoritarian discourse, practice, and rule is taking place across the globe – it remains true that these authoritarianisms look and feel different in different places (Dahlmann 2022). There is not just one kind of authoritarianism which operates in the same way in different places. What then, can a distinctly geographical approach to authoritarianism contribute?

papers.title: Geopolitical Imaginaries and the Spatialisation of Illiberalism: Hungary Post-2010 as a Hybrid Regime

sessions.title: Accounting for nature, or holding nature to account? Critical geographies of environmental accounting (1)
sessions.description:
This session stages a critical exploration of the links between geography, and cognate disciplines, and questions of environmental accounting. Over the last two decades, the environment has not only been increasingly taken into account - with its social and economic benefits measured, quantified, valued and priced - but also, more recently, held to account. While the former is often presented as a route to environmental improvement, it warrants scrutiny: environmental accounting is invariably partial, shaped by power relations, and uneven in its effects. The latter development is more troubling. As nature is ledgered and administered like any other asset, it becomes fungible, replaceable even, through accounting logics. This session will explore the Janus-faced nature of environmental accounting, where, on the one hand, accounting practices enable regimes of governance and place nature on the public balance sheet, on the other, they serve to demand a justification for nature’s existence via economic reasoning. 

 Carbon accounting brought something as nebulous as entities’ relations with the atmosphere onto the books, to assume (selective) carbon accountability, benchmark and rank progress, enable carbon markets and offsetting practices, and forecast decarbonisation pathways of benefit or minimal detriment to ‘business as usual’. Similarly, nature accounts, forming the basis of natural capital, biodiversity net gain, and the restoration economy, have further extended ‘stock-taking’ financial logics into the environmental domain. Governments, business, NGOs, and universities have largely acquiesced to the need for cost-benefit analyses in environmental decision-making, to the common sense of holding nature itself to account. The influence of economists, accountants, financial consultants and investors has significantly grown as a result, challenging that of ecologists and conservation bodies, at the fuzzy intersection of science, politics, and economics. 

 Beyond professionals, everyone can provide their own account of ‘the environment’. But amidst its popular conflation with economic accounting, it is often reserved as a practice for experts and technocratic management, delimiting who gets to speak for the environment. In response, geographers, STS scholars, and others have sought to foreground the potential of overlooked perspectives, including radical, decolonial, artistic, and activist backgrounds. This session invites contributions to contemplate and discuss the threats, limits and affordances of balance sheet nature.

papers.title: The Outsized Influence of Big Tech on Metrics, Standards, and Benchmarks in Carbon Accounting
AI for SEEA: Practices and technologies of natural capital accounting
Holding rivers to account
Insetting the Record Straight: Environmental credits, commensuration, and the politics of carbon accounting

sessions.title: Negotiating Dignity: Rethinking Justice in Geographical Research (2)
sessions.description:
Papers in this session place dignity at the centre of geographical research on justice. Over the past two decades, justice has become a core concern across multiple subdisciplines, including critical urban geography, environmental and climate justice, feminist and care geographies, Black and anti-racist geographies, political geography, and mobilities and transport geography. Much of this work has productively moved beyond distributive frameworks to address recognition, participation, embodiment, affect, and everyday power (Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). Yet the normative and lived stakes of these debates are often articulated indirectly. Dignity helps name what is frequently at issue but insufficiently theorised: the ability to inhabit space without humiliation, erasure, or disposability, and to exercise autonomy and social standing in the face of structural inequality. At the same time, dignity is a concept with a complex and contested history. Its universalist claims—long embedded in law, bioethics, and humanitarian governance—have often failed to confront colonial, racialised, gendered, and caste-based hierarchies, and at times have actively reproduced them (Misztal, 2013). Recent geographical scholarship has responded by advancing relational, contextual, and politically grounded understandings of dignity, attentive to how it is produced, denied, and negotiated through everyday encounters with institutions, infrastructures, and publics (Grossmann and Trubina, 2022; Kariũki and Martens, 2026; Plyushteva, 2023; McIlwaine et al., 2025). Taken together, dignity-oriented research extends geographical debates on justice. It draws attention to the affective and ethical dimensions of inequality that are often muted in distributive or procedural accounts. In doing so, it highlights how injustice is reproduced through routine practices of degradation and erasure. 
 References 

 Grossmann, K., & Trubina, E. (2022). Dignity in urban geography: Starting a conversation. Dialogues in Human Geography , 12 (3), 406-426.

 Hopkins, P. (2021). Social geography III: Committing to social justice. Progress in Human Geography , 45 (2), 382-393.

 Kariũki, W., & Martens, K. (2026). Dignified transport: The case of paratransit. Travel Behaviour and Society , 42 , 101140.

 McIlwaine, C., Peppl, R., & Cal Angrisani, C. (2025). Reclaiming dignity through creative encounters among Brazilian women survivors of violence in London. cultural geographies , 14744740251319039.

 Misztal, B. A. (2013). The idea of dignity: Its modern significance. European Journal of Social Theory , 16 (1), 101-121.

 Plyushteva, A. (2023). Negotiating dignity in public geography: The ethics of public engagement in pandemic times. Area , 55 (1), 53-61.

 Przybylinski, S. (2022). Where is justice in geography? A review of justice theorizing in the discipline. Geography Compass , 16 (3), e12615.

sessions.title: Human Presence Beyond Earth: Critical Geographies of Outer Space (1)
sessions.description:
After 54 years, humans are returning to extraterrestrial environments, namely around and on the Moon. Unlike the Apollo missions, which focused on exploration and return, today's lunar programs envision permanent settlement—transforming the Moon from a destination for brief visits into a new human territory. In February 2026, for instance, NASA plans to launch Artemis II, which will send four humans beyond Earth’s orbit for the first time since 1972 as they slingshot around the moon. As humans prepare to return to Earth’s only natural satellite, how can geographers critically engage with this development while challenging uneven power relations? Building on geographers' long-standing inquiry into outer space and recent publications debating human space activities (Armstrong & Klinger, 2025; Bennett and Cudney, 2025; Klinger, 2021; Squire et al., 2021), this session continues this line of investigation by exploring critical geographies of outer space.

 Contemporary space exploration differs fundamentally from the Apollo era, unfolding within a broader transformation of global geopolitics (Daniels & Krige, 2022) and commercialization (Denis et al., 2020). The bipolar order of the Cold War has given way to a multipolar landscape structured by technological rivalry, particularly between the United States and China, shaping these countries' space imaginations and missions (Puente-Lozano & Herring-Bazo, 2024; Wijeyeratne, 2026). Space exploration is no longer dominated by nation-states alone, but increasingly shaped by commercial–state partnerships, private capital, and hybrid governance arrangements emerging from shifting geopolitical configurations (Utrata, 2024). Proposals for "Moon villages," permanent infrastructures, and energy systems—including nuclear power and data centers—signal a move from exploration to settlement, raising urgent questions about sovereignty, territorialization, and control beyond Earth (Au, 2024; Vermeylen & Njeri, 2023).

 Earth’s orbital space is becoming increasingly crowded and integrated into social, political, and economic dynamics both on Earth and beyond (Bowen, 2020; Dunnett et al., 2019). Critical scholars have questioned who is accessing outer space and how these space missions are enabled through uneven, racialized, and even colonial exploitation on Earth (Armstrong & Klinger, 2025; Klinger et al., 2025; Redfield, 2000). Research has also explored how human perception and sensory experience shape engagements with outer space, as scholars develop and reflect on research methods for studying space and space-related practices (Praet & Pitrou, 2025; Timko et al., 2022; Vertesi, 2015). Outer space is not simply another research project: it is interwoven with terrestrial dynamics through the spacecraft, infrastructure, imagination, and legal systems that connect humanity to space (Messeri & Olson, 2025). As outer space assumes greater importance in geopolitical power and geo-economic competition, questions arise about how space infrastructure and its entanglement with Earth's social, cultural, political, and colonial histories reproduce existing uneven power relations beyond our planet.

 As outer space becomes increasingly entangled with Earth-based political economies, geographers are well positioned to interrogate this spatial reordering. This session examines how outer space is grounded in terrestrial infrastructures, extractive economies, and geopolitical power, revealing that the space frontier is deeply embedded in Earthly histories and inequalities.

papers.title: Grounding Heaven: The Luigi Broglio Space Centre and the Geopolitics of Space Infrastructure
Space Rock Materialities: Deep Pasts, Extractive Presents and Speculative Futures
Meteorites and the Making of a Terrestrial Space Economy
Space Summiting in Singapore: Traces of an Emergent Astropolitics
Diverse perspectives about the impacts of the future spaceport in Talara, Peru

sessions.title: Transactions of the Institute of British Geographers Panel: Geographers and 'justice work'
sessions.description: This TIBG plenary panel invites exploration of the vital and diverse contributions of geographers whose research, teaching, and praxis are deeply committed to justice work within and beyond the discipline. What is ‘justice work’ and how does it intervene in conditions of ‘injustice’? As a form of labour, how is it is organised, valued, and governed within the academy and beyond? How can ‘justice work’ help us to understand and build towards deeper engagements with justice that go beyond what Don Mitchell (2024) diagnoses as ‘commonsense justice-as-fairness’ which dominates the discipline today? At a moment of intensifying authoritarianism and political backlash against critical scholarship, what are the risks, costs, and constraints of justice work? How do geographers engaged in justice-oriented research, teaching, and public engagement navigate what can be profound risks and ethical stakes, both for scholars and for the communities with whom they work? In opening up these questions for discussion, the panel aims to critically examine justice work as a situated, uneven, and contested form of geographical labour, and to foster collective reflection on how such work might be sustained, protected, and reimagined in increasingly authoritarian political and institutional contexts.

sessions.title: Power, inequalities and participation: revisiting food citizenship and beyond (2)
sessions.description:
In a food system wrought by crises and inequalities, ‘food citizenship’ emerged alongside ‘food democracy’ as one way of re-politicising food. The concept re-imagines consumers as active citizens who participate in shaping more just food systems. Fundamental questions of participatory justice are at stake: who is able to participate in food governance, on what terms, and with what consequences for social and ecological futures?

 Despite its growing traction, limited attention has been paid to intersectionality, situatedness and embodied experiences of ‘food citizenship’, and examining how food system participation is structured by gender, race, class, disability, age and other axes of power. The figure of a ‘citizen’ is never neutral: normative expectations around ‘active citizenship’ are prevalent in food citizenship debates and can reproduce exclusion by privileging particular capacities, temporalities and modes of engagement. Critiques of citizen participation highlight potential to reinforce neoliberal dynamics and subjectivities. Socio-spatial injustices also constrain participation; participation doesn’t inherently ‘fix’ systemic injustices.

 Taking food citizenship as an imperfect but productive starting point, this session will critically interrogate the methodologies, geographies and politics of subject formation, participation and justice in the democratisation of food governance across scales and places/territories:

 - Who is (imagined as) a ‘food citizen’? What visions of food citizenship circulate across different spaces and scales, what political work do they perform, and for whom?
- Who is included or excluded from these imaginaries and practices, and how is power and participation structured through gender, class, race, ethnicity, disability or age?
- How does embodied food citizenship intersect with food sovereignty, food justice and other debates?
- Which alternative language, theoretical perspectives or methodological approaches can help reinvigorate participatory justice values and ethical commitments within food citizenship debates and practice?

 Four short papers will be followed by a world cafe-style discussion.

papers.title: Visualising food citizenship: multimodal ethnography and the uneven geographies of participation through the Food Citizens? i-doc
World-cafe discussion
affiliations: Department of Geography and Environmental Science, University of Reading

sessions.title: Legal Animal Geographies: Towards Multispecies Justice in Policy and Practice (2)
sessions.description:
There is a vast array of work that explores governance, regulation, and legislation of animals in a range of different spaces, including work examining the role of responsibility in dog walking in the Scottish Outdoor Access Code (Brown and Dilley, 2012; Brown, 2015), regulation of ‘street’ and ‘stray’ dogs in the UK and India (Srinivasan, 2013), the legal blurriness of horseshoe crabs in animal welfare policy (Gorman, 2024), the poorly suited regulations and welfare guidance for fish in UK scientific research (Greenhough et al. 2024), social license to operate in Australian horse racing and horse welfare (Duncan et al., 2018; McManus, 2023), empathy and ethics in the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons Code of Professional Conduct (Donald, 2019), pet’s right to the city in China (Yan, 2025), and the different framings of dog-related policy in the UK (Allen et al., 2025). Bringing these works together raises three key questions:

 (1) How can we take the care of animals seriously in current legal systems?(2) How can we contest, challenge, and resist unjust legal systems for animals? (3) How can we create just multispecies worlds? This session explores how multispecies legal justice might be realised.

 Taking animals seriously in legal systems requires challenging the anthropocentrism inherent in our understandings of animals as property, tools, and objects (Deckha, 2021; Allen et al., 2025), to instead highlight the ways in which animals’ own subjectivities shape space and everyday life. As Braverman (2016) argues we need to consider the range of ethical and political issues of what it means to govern more-than-human life.

 We invite papers that not only highlight current multispecies injustice, but seek to challenge, resist, and contest these systems to create more just multispecies worlds and futures. We welcome conceptual, empirical, methodological, and creative contributions that include, but are not limited to, the following themes:

 Animals and abuse; Animals and housing; Animals and inequality; Animals and law; Animals and policy (change); Animal campaigning; Animals, history, and policy; Animals, policy, and space; Animals and everyday policing; Human-animal governance; Multispecies justice; Wildlife crime

 To submit, please send your paper title and abstract (of up to 250 words) to Daniel Allen (d.allen@keele.ac.uk) and Jamie Arathoon (j.arathoon1@yorksj.ac.uk) by Friday 28th February (This allows session organisers time to review proposals and submit the final session to the RGS-IBG portal by the official deadline of Friday 6th March 2026.)

 Please also let us know your contact details, affiliation, and any accessibility requirements.

sessions.title: Living with Urban Inequality: Ageing, Well-being, and Mobility (2)
sessions.description:
Urban inequality is lived not only through income disparities or housing outcomes, but through everyday experiences of ageing, care, health, and movement across the city (Lefebvre, 1991; Harvey, 2008). As urban populations age amid widening socio-spatial inequalities, access to mobility, services, social infrastructure, and meaningful participation in urban life becomes increasingly uneven (WHO, 2007). These inequalities are shaped by intersecting relations of class, gender, ethnicity, migration status, and health, and are embedded in neighbourhood change, welfare restructuring, and urban governance (Soja, 2010).

 This session brings together urban geographical research that examines how inequality is produced, experienced, and negotiated through the everyday mobilities and immobility of ageing populations. Mobility is understood broadly, encompassing physical movement, access to urban services, social participation, and connections across neighbourhood, city, and transnational scales. The session foregrounds well-being as relational and spatially contingent, shaped by both structural conditions and everyday practices of care, support, and belonging (O’Neil, 2020).

 We invite papers that engage empirically and/or theoretically with urban inequality through the lenses of ageing, well-being, and mobility. Relevant themes include but are not limited to:

 - Ageing in unequal urban environments
- Health, well-being, and spatial inequality
- Everyday mobilities, immobility, and access to the city
- Migration, transnational ageing, and urban belonging
- Gendered, racialised, and classed experiences of later life
- Care, social infrastructure, and neighbourhood support systems
- Urban policy, governance, and the uneven provision of care and services
- Methodological approaches to researching ageing and inequality in cities

 The session welcomes qualitative, comparative, and critical contributions that situate lived experience within wider urban processes, contributing to long-standing debates in urban geography around inequality, social reproduction, and uneven development.

sessions.title: Sand Crisis or Sandthropocene? Geo(morpho)logizing urbanization
sessions.description:
The contemporary world is built on sand. It is a foundational material for construction and development, underpinning both land use change and rapid urbanization. A key ingredient of concrete, as well as a granular fill material for land reclamation and soil improvement, sand is geologizing, and geomorphologizing urbanization and economic development  in increasingly dynamic, unpredictable, and disastrous ways (Dawson, 2021; Jamieson, 2024). In recent years, extraction has accelerated so far beyond replacement rates that environmental scientists have posed the possibility of an impending sand crisis and ‘tragedy of the commons’ (Torres et al, 2017). However, geographers have also problematised this  invocation of crisis, whose designation obscures the complexity of sand mining and its relationship to economic development (John, 2021; Lamb, 2023), evident from the recurrence of black markets in sand across the world, which, for such a cheap and voluminous resource, demands further scrutiny.
 This session seeks to explore sand in its relationship to urbanization, economic development and the underlying geological and geomorphic processes it is both product and mediator of. The effects of sand mining are keenly felt at the scale of everyday life, foreclosing livelihoods and uprooting ecologies, while its economic role is intimately bound to the most delirious visions of speculative urbanism and state capitalism. It asks whether we are seeing the beginning of a sand crisis, or if we now find ourselves in the Sandthropocene, with sand’s ubiquitous presence in urban and infrastructural form constituting a novel geosocial formation wherein urban and geomorphic systems are rendered interdependent, challenging the terrestrial and geological bias of Anthropocene theory.

papers.title: Minerals, Magic, and Mining: The Politics of Animism in the Age of Critical Minerals
From Riverbed to Real Estate: Sand and the Granular Neoliberal Urban Production in Phnom Penh, Cambodia
Sands of Destruction
Towards a Sandthropocene

sessions.title: Contesting Tourism Growth: Critical Geographies, Spatial Inequalities, and Just Alternatives (2)
sessions.description:
This panel brings together critical perspectives on tourism growth as a spatially uneven and politically contested process. Across diverse geographical contexts, tourism is frequently positioned as a development solution, yet its expansion often intensifies inequalities in housing, labour, mobility, infrastructure, and access to decision-making—particularly in tourism-dependent and geographically constrained places such as islands, rural regions, and peripheral urban areas.

 The panel is structured around two complementary themes that speak to ongoing debates in critical geography, urban studies, and political economy.

 1: Urbanisation, Governance, and Spatial Inequality examines how tourism growth is planned, governed, and contested, and how it reshapes urban and regional space. Contributions in this theme explore the governance arrangements, policy frameworks, and institutional dynamics through which tourism-led development produces uneven outcomes, including displacement, housing pressures, precarious labour, and differentiated mobilities. Taken together, the papers and presentations situate tourism within broader processes of urbanisation, territorial restructuring, and spatial injustice.

 2: Contestation, Alternatives, and Post-Growth Futures turns attention to challenges to growth-oriented tourism models and to emerging alternatives. Drawing on degrowth, regenerative, post-capitalist, and place-based approaches, this theme foregrounds grassroots, community-led, and Indigenous practices that reimagine tourism beyond extraction and expansion. Papers and presentations will explore how alternative tourism futures are articulated, negotiated, and sometimes institutionalised, and who has the power to define what constitutes a “just” tourism geography across different spatial and political scales.

 Together, the panel offers a critical and forward-looking examination of tourism’s role in shaping space, inequality, and possibility. It will be of interest to scholars and practitioners working on urbanisation, governance, political ecology, degrowth, and spatial justice, as well as those seeking to engage with post-growth debates and transformative approaches to tourism and place-making.

 This session will be held in-person in at the RGS conference London, with a hybrid option available for 2. This session is sponsored by the Geographies of Leisure and Tourism Research Group (GLTRG) at the RGS conference.

sessions.title: Inequality, religion, faith, and spirituality: towards a just world? (2)
sessions.description: Religion, faith and spirituality are affected by and affect inequalities. They can inspire and contribute towards the building of just places, and they can create tension. This session welcomes papers from scholars of all career stages who wish to explore the intersection of inequalities and religion/faith/spiritualities within their research. Recent engagements with the religious-spiritual have tended to focus on the lived realities, incorporating examinations of embodied identities, everyday practices, diversifying beliefs, and emotional and affective dimensions of religion. These operate on scales from the local through to the national and international. Much work in this sub-discipline has considered the Christian faith. We both welcome contributions focussing on Christianity and encourage papers focussing on other faiths and spiritualities. The session will conclude with a whole group Q&A, led by discussant Justin Tse.

sessions.title: Housing, Population Change, and Spatial Inequality (2)
sessions.description: Housing is a central area through which social and spatial inequalities are (re)produced and experienced. Processes of population change, including migration, ageing, and urban growth, are deeply intertwined with housing systems. This session explores how housing both reflects and structures population change across different geographical contexts, contributing toward spatial injustice. We welcome papers that examine the relationship between housing and population change, including but not limited to, housing affordability and population change; gentrification and displacement; migration, refugees, and housing precarity; population growth and housing supply; and the impacts of housing policy on population dynamics. Papers presented in this session may draw on diverse theoretical perspectives, quantitative or qualitative methods, and varied geographic scales.

sessions.title: Infrastructural Deaths (2): Enacting and Contesting Infrastructural Deaths
sessions.description:
“[T]here is no ‘end’ to infrastructure”, state Ramakrishnan et al. (2021: 682) in their call for researchers to move beyond temporal assumptions of infrastructures that transition linearly from creation to afterlife and focus instead on ongoing transformations in infrastructural relations. However, recent events such as the political crisis around the irreversible shut down of the UK’s Scunthorpe steelworks also reveal that infrastructural ‘deaths’ – real or perceived – can have extraordinary political salience and should not be ignored. 

 To date, infrastructural deaths have been examined in a limited way in the interdisciplinary literature on infrastructural politics. Research has explored infrastructural disruptions and failures (Graham, 2010; Ranganathan, 2016; Silver, 2016), decay and ruination (Gupta, 2018; Ramakrishnan et al. 2021; Silver, 2021), infrastructural afterlives (Evans, 2021; Morris, 2021; Pike, 2019), urbicide and the strategic military ‘killing’ of infrastructure (Coward, 2008; Graham, 2005), the effects of infrastructural absences/disconnections (Kinder, 2016; Young & Keil, 2010), the (death) tolls of infrastructural labour (Addie, 2021; Tyner, 2019), and the decline of infrastructures through deindustrialisation (Emery, 2019; Pulido, 2016; Zhang, 2024). Surprisingly little social research has examined infrastructural decommissioning and retirement. What does exist has emerged in other fields such as energy geographies (De Laurentis & Windemere, 2024; McCauley, 2018; Windemere, 2019), engineering (Invernizzi et al. 2020), and law (Dike, 2017).

 Within this work, deaths have often been described in singular forms (urbicide), have been presented implicitly as temporary conditions (disruptions and failures), or have already happened (afterlives). In other cases, the political significance of death as a total, irrevocable, or inevitable phase state has been overlooked (decay/ruination), or the uniqueness of infrastructure as an object of analysis has been sidelined (deindustrialisation). Further research is therefore needed to conceptualise the political salience of infrastructural deaths, their geographies and temporalities, and the ways in which infrastructural constellations become reconfigured in relation to infrastructural deaths. 

 This session accordingly presents reflections on: 

 - What it means for an infrastructure to ‘die’ and the forms deaths take
- Where infrastructural deaths happen, over what timescales
- How infrastructural deaths are imagined, anticipated, contested, and managed, and the ways in which they recast the political relations surrounding infrastructures
- The labour required to anticipate, facilitate, mitigate, or prevent infrastructural deaths
- (Uneven) experiences of infrastructural deaths and the inequalities that produce/are reproduced through them
- The conditions of possibility infrastructural deaths create for new forms of social justice

papers.title: The Liminality of Infrastructural Deaths: Remembering, Mourning, and Contemplating the Material Environments of the (Post-)Fossil City
affiliations: Just Transition Centre, Martin Luther University Halle-Wittenberg

papers.title: The mines that won’t die

sessions.title: The ‘unlikely’ geography of climate leadership and innovation (2): Participatory governance
sessions.description:
Place-based climate leadership is usually associated with 
economically successful cities and regions, which have strong 
institutional capacity, fiscal resources, and favourable structural 
conditions (e.g. van der Heijden, 2019). In contrast, economically 
disadvantaged, peripheral and non-metropolitan places are often cast as 
vulnerable territories that are further peripheralised by net-zero, 
energy and green transitions (e.g. Golubchikov and O’Sullivan, 2020). 
This aligns with economic and political geography scholarship that 
emphasises the political constraints and institutional deficits faced by
 ‘left-behind’ places (e.g. Rodríguez-Pose, 2018). However, evidence 
also emerges that some of the most economically disadvantaged places 
become national leaders in climate and net-zero policy and governance 
(Golubchikov et al., 2026). Undoubtedly, economic agglomeration and 
favourable structural conditions matter, but they are not deterministic 
(Haupt et al., 2023). Motivated local actors – often working through 
coalitions spanning local government, civil society and businesses – can
 shape strong climate vision and action (Homsy, 2018; Haupt et al., 
2025). The lesson is that climate and net-zero leadership is a story of a
 more distributed geography than is often assumed. 

 Understanding this ‘unlikely geography’ of climate leadership remains
 limited, yet it is important for ensuring a genuinely just transition. 
First, recognising leadership and innovation beyond economically 
successful regions is essential for epistemic justice: it challenges who
 is seen as a legitimate source of innovation, governance and 
experimentation. Second, the experiences of less wealthy and peripheral 
places are potentially more transferable to the other regions facing 
similar constraints than the more celebrated cases. Two further key 
issues, however, are economic impact and political durability. Can such 
‘peripheral’ climate leadership translate into wider economic and social
 opportunities for these places? And can it be sustained over time amid 
persistent institutional and financial limitations, alongside 
intensifying backlash and contestation around climate policy?

 This session includes contributions that investigate this ‘unlikely’ 
geography of climate leadership – exploring how poorer and peripheral 
places are not only sites of risk and vulnerability, but also potential 
innovators, leaders, and agenda-setters in the net-zero transition.

papers.title: Engaging ‘unlikely’ participants in hands-on climate action to develop more inclusive climate policy?
affiliations: Centre for Consumer Society Research, University of Helsinki

papers.title: Latrobe Valley of Victoria, Australia as a case study of ‘unlikely geography’ of climate leadership: Voices of the Valley’s (VOTV) emergence and evolution as a community action group for renewable energy transition
Learning from non-pioneering municipalities. Inclusive climate governance and the mobilisation of ‘unlikely’ mitigation potential
From unlikely to likely climate leadership: how partnerships create value in energy access innovation ecosystems

sessions.title: Geographies of claims-making in international law (2)
sessions.description:
There is near-universal agreement—in policy circles, academic debate, and public discourse—that international law is undergoing a moment of profound crisis. Whether through the erosion of long-standing multilateral institutions, escalating geopolitical fragmentation, or disputes over the legitimacy of international legal norms, commentators frequently diagnose a system under strain, if not experiencing outright rupture (e.g. Kinstler 2025; Tzouvala 2025). Yet paradoxically, this crisis has not diminished claims on and to international law. If anything, the actors who invoke, contest, and rework international law have multiplied. An increasingly diverse array of participants including states, social movements, Indigenous Peoples, corporations, NGOs and transnational advocacy networks turn to international legality—sometimes strategically and sometimes aspirationally—as a register through which to assert political claims, demand accountability, and imagine alternative political futures. The contemporary landscape is therefore marked by an expansion, rather than a retreat, of claims-making in international law. This expansion, in turn, raises important questions for geographers. Where, how, and by whom is international law being articulated, negotiated, and spatialised? With what social, material, and political effects? And how should these phenomena be reconciled with the challenges the international legal order currently faces?

 This session brings together scholars to examine the geographies of international legal claims-making. In doing so, we speak to the relative neglect of international law within legal geography scholarship (Jones and Smith 2015; Delaney 2017), while also encouraging greater dialogue between legal, political, and historical geographers (McGeachan 2017; Braverman et al. 2014; Graham and Bartel 2016).

 We invite contributions engaging with (but not limited to) the following questions:

 - What geographical imaginaries underpin and are produced through practices of international legal claims-making?
- How do sites such as courts and treaty bodies, in addition to ‘alternative’ sites of legal mobilisation, shape the forms and possibilities of international legal claims-making?
- What spatial practices (e.g. mapping, documenting, witnessing, archiving) underpin the construction of international legal claims?
- How do international legal claims circulate across local, national, regional, and global scales, and how are they transformed in the process?
- What material infrastructures support or constrain international legal claims-making?
- How does engaging in international legal claims-making contribute to the formation or transformation of geopolitical subjectivities?
- How do practices of international legal claims-making interact with, reinforce, or challenge domestic legal regimes?
- How can attention to historical forms of international legal claims-making illuminate the dynamics of the present global conjuncture?
- What role can geographers and geographical knowledge play in the construction of international legal claims?
- What methods are best suited to the study of international legal claims-making

papers.title: The battle over the European Convention of Human Rights and the right private life
From Living Ecology to Legal Personhood: Mapping Rights of Rivers in Sri Lanka
Diversifying the geographies of international legal personality: the UNPO’s ‘Universal Declaration of the Rights of Peoples’
Geographies of Claims Making in International Law: Dreaming of Justice

sessions.title: The social reproduction of platform economies (2)
sessions.description:
The social reproduction of platform economies

 Organisers: Sam Nowak (Durham University) and Carolyn Prouse (Queen’s University) 

 Amidst the ongoing crisis of care and in the wake of the COVID-19 pandemic, there has been a resurgence of interest in the geographies, infrastructures and labour of social reproduction (Rodríguez-Rocha, 2021; Prouse, 2025). In parallel, geographers have highlighted how all manner of social processes are now increasingly intermediated through digital platforms, powered by big data, and shaped by algorithms (Langley and Leyshon, 2017). This session invites papers concerned with these converging trends, exploring the social reproduction of platform economies.

 While it has become axiomatic that platform firms generate value through interlinked processes of data capture (Langley and Leyshon, 2017), labour exploitation (van Doorn and Badger, 2020), and rent extraction (Sadowski, 2020), we suggest that thinking through social reproduction helps move beyond universalisations of ‘the platform’ to consider the everyday practices, social infrastructures, and racialised and gendered labour regimes that underpin, and are reproduced through, platform economies. Recent scholarship has highlighted the shortfalls of universalising and economistic theories of the platform through a focus on: how on-demand domestic work economies sometimes fail (Dattani, 2021); the marketisation, management, and monitoring of domestic space through various forms of ‘caring’ surveillance (Sadowski, Strengers & Kennedy, 2021); the differential encoding of bodies for access to food and other means of social reproduction (Akbari, 2025); the collectivisation practices of urban gig workers to manage occupational risk (Kwan, 2022; Nowak, 2023); and the variegated gendered effects/affects of platform work and digital economies (James, 2024; Hardcastle & Loomis, 2025). 

 In this session we build on this work to ask: How are digital technologies and platform monopolies re-configuring communities of care, mutual aid labor, and infrastructures of social reproduction? What conceptualisations of social reproduction are useful at this moment to reveal the extractive nature of digital capitalism and the re-making of vulnerability/precarity/life/care? How might a focus on ‘life’s work’ reshape how we think about the digitally-mediated city and urban life?

 This session invites papers that seek to answer these questions. We are interested in work that includes, but is not limited to:

 - The reconfiguration of urban spaces and processes of social reproduction through platform and gig economy work;
- The (re)production of gendered, racialised, classed, migrant and caste precarity through platform economies; 
- Dependence on Big Tech infrastructure for everyday social reproduction, and/or the collectivised contestations of this dependence; 
- Glitch politics and digital infrastructural failure
- The relationship between social reproduction, the digital commons, and enclosure
- The affective allure of predictive analytics, digital surveillance, and genAI in everyday spaces of social reproduction 
- The platformisation of care and domestic work
- The platformisation of housing, education, and health systems and technologies
- Socio-technical networks of mutual aid, platform cooperatives, and/or social movements that make life in the shadow of fascism
- Digital and platform technologies of biological reproduction, including femtech and reprotech

papers.title: ‘Banking the gig economy’: Workers, fintechs, and social reproduction
The financial technologies of ‘debt urbanism’
Platform-as-commons for infectious disease surveillance: Contestations over genomic and viral sovereignty as social reproductive practice
Digital Devices, Everyday Decisions: Inter/mediating Young People’s Financial Lives

sessions.title: Researching the Digitalising State (2)
sessions.description: Across the world, the state is undergoing deep, fundamental shifts in how it produces data, manages information and digitises bureaucratic workflows. The everyday work of the state – to govern - is now enmeshed with technological appendages that have created what Sandra Braman so eloquently calls a ‘Change of State’ (Braman 2006). There is now a range of complex forms of ‘stateness’ (Jones 2002) arising from the last few decades of state restructuring, accelerated by the proliferation of digital/geospatial platforms and the increasing presence of private-sector actors within state offices. While these developments promise efficiency, transparency, and accountability in the state, they also reconfigure state power in ways that are often opaque, uneven, and difficult for critical geographers to study (Datta and Reyes Carranza 2026). This panel will bring together methodological contributions that interrogate the ‘digitalising state’ (Datta 2023) – a dynamic assemblage of state power across spaces, scales and actors, across public, private and multilateral organisations, across paper and digital information infrastructures, and across political-technological terrains of data, code and platforms. While the state has been notoriously hard to research, there have been significant contributions to investigating the ‘everyday state’ (Hull 2012; Gupta 2012; Dunleavy et al. 2006) from anthropology and organisation studies. What might critical geographers learn from these approaches, and how might we deploy a geographical lens to studying the digitalising state?

papers.title: Researching ‘embodied’ digitalising states in the Global South
Studying Opaque Bureaucracies: A Methodological Inquiry into the Home Office’s Digitalisation
Researching ‘embodied’ digitalising states in the Global South
Encountering the digitalising state: Methodological reflections from Singapore
Following the Land: Land Topology as a Methodological Approach to Researching the Digitalising State

sessions.title: Agriculture and chemicalization: Perspectives in a rapidly changing field
sessions.description:
Global agriculture has been transformed in recent decades through processes of agricultural intensification, corporate restructuring and consolidation, and deepening chemical dependence. Within this transformation, pesticides have ascended from mere farm inputs to occupy a central role in modern agri-food systems. They now function not only as technical inputs but also as key commodities for capital accumulation, state power, and integration into volatile global markets (Berndt et al., 2025; Mansfield et al., 2023). Existing research has highlighted how these processes are unevenly embedded across regions and scales, reshaping agrarian livelihoods, ecological relations, and agrarian politics, while often reproducing or intensifying social, economic, and environmental inequalities (Werner, Berndt and Mansfield, 2022). Global agrochemical value chains have been reconfigured, shifting corporate concentration and reinforcing asymmetric power relations between farmers, states, and markets. The ongoing chemicalization of agriculture, combined with these power dynamics, renders the so-called 'global pesticide complex' an ever-evolving and rapidly transforming field (Mansfield et al., 2023; Werner, 2025). The session will also explore alternative agricultures, food sovereignty, and agroecological proposals that have been contesting the imperative of corporate profit. 

 Reference List

 Berndt, C., Werner, M., Mempel, F., Shattuck, A., & Dunivin, Z. O. (2025). The Generics Revolution and the New Economic Geography of the Global Pesticide Industry. Journal of Agrarian Change, n/a(n/a), e70007. https://doi.org/10.1111/joac.70007

 Mansfield, B., Werner, M., Berndt, C., Shattuck, A., Galt, R., Williams, B., Argüelles, L., Barri, F. R., Ishii, M., Kunin, J., Lapegna, P., Romero, A., Caicedo, A., Abhigya, Castro- Vargas, M. S., Marquez, E., Ojeda, D., Ramirez, F., & Tittor, A. (2023). A new critical social science research agenda on pesticides. Agriculture and Human Values. https://doi.org/10.1007/s10460-023-10492-w

 Werner, M., Berndt, C., & Mansfield, B. (2022). The glyphosate assemblage: Herbicides, uneven development, and chemical geographies of ubiquity. Annals of the American Association of Geographers, 112(1), 19–35. https://doi.org/10.1080/24694452.2021.1898322

 Werner, M. (2025). Global pesticide complex. The Journal of Peasant Studies, 1–18. https://doi.org/10.1080/03066150.2025.2547373

sessions.title: Author meets critics: Sam Halvorsen’s Territorializing Democracy: Strategies of Popular Participation in Buenos Aires
sessions.description:
Scholars are invited to share critical reflections on Sam Halvorsen’s book Territorializing Democracy: Strategies of Popular Participation in Buenos Aires (University of Georgia Press).

 Territorializing Democracy argues that the political control of space is key to understanding political participation in the city. Drawing on a decade of research in Buenos Aires, this book shows that participation is not simply a response to innovations in urban governance; it is a strategy rooted in the relational context of territory. Examining key sites of activism over recent years—campaigning for a people’s park, upgrading an informal settlement, the “national-popular” movement of Kirchnerism, and struggles over urban redevelopment—the analysis contributes to pressing democratic debates around autonomy and self-management, populism and clientelism, and democratic innovation and “participatory articulations.” Through the lens of space and geography, this book offers a relational analysis of popular participation, working between multiple neighborhoods and scales, across different struggles, and between the streets and political institutions, activists, and politicians. In doing so, Halvorsen proposes a dual understanding of the territorialization of democracy: a reflection of the changing political conditions shaping cities today and a tool for assessing how democratic practices emerge from specific, grounded struggles over space.

 Discussants:

 - Simone Veglio (Malmo University) 
- Yu-Shan Tseng (University of Southampton)
- Fiona Anciano (University of Western Cape)

 Chair: Cristina Temenos (University of Manchester)

sessions.title: Reimagining rural mobility: Everyday experiences, practices and opportunities in low-density regions (2)
sessions.description:
Rural transport disadvantages are often overlooked in academic and policy debates despite increasing evidence of inequalities linked to rural living, such as participation in education, labour and social life, as well as access to health. The majority of households have at least one car in rural areas of high-income economies such as the UK and a car is seen as essential to meet basic daily needs (Smith et al., 2012). Nevertheless, there are significant groups of people in rural areas who do not have car access (Chatterjee et al, 2019) or struggle financially to run a car (Mattioli et al, 2018). There is limited knowledge of the mobility experiences and practices of these groups. Buses are the main alternative for rural residents without a car, but the withdrawal of bus services in the UK risks increasing car dependence. There is strong interest currently in whether emerging mobility options such as demand responsive transport, car sharing and e-bikes can fill the gap and transform rural mobility. 

 These double sessions on rural mobility explore rural mobilities through empirical evidence from around the world, including:

 - Everyday mobility practices and coping strategies in low-density contexts, such as lift-giving, informal carpooling, and mobility rationing
- The consequences of limited mobility for access to education, employment, healthcare, care responsibilities, and social participation
- The intersectional dimensions of rural mobility disadvantage, including gender, age, disability, income, ethnicity, migration status, and caring roles
- The relationship between transport disadvantage, deprivation, poverty, and spatial inequality
- Longitudinal changes in mobility in response to policy interventions, such as bus service withdrawal or the introduction of demand-responsive transport
- Community-led, informal, or alternative mobility solutions and their limitations
- Alternative visions for the future of rural mobility

Estimation of Social Value from DRT use
affiliations: University of the West of England

sessions.title: Understanding Neighbourhoods from the Global South: Inside-Out Perspectives on Sustainable, Healthy and Learning Cities (2)
sessions.description:
Urban neighbourhoods – often the primary scale at which residents experience, organise and negotiate urban life – are increasingly recognised as critical sites for advancing sustainability, health and learning agendas. However, dominant theories, datasets and policy frameworks in neighbourhood studies are largely shaped by Global North perspectives (Baffoe and Kintrea, 2023), often adopting top-down approaches and externally defined models of urban development (UN-Habitat, 2014). These frameworks often fail to capture the socio-spatial complexities, informal systems and context-dependent everyday practices that characterise many neighbourhoods in the Global South (Parnell and Robinson, 2012).

 This session advances an inside-out approach to neighbourhood studies in the Global South, highlighting residents' lived experiences, everyday practices and locally embedded knowledge systems. It builds on emerging comparative, place-based and co-produced approaches to understanding neighbourhoods (Lees et al., 2016), as well as neighbourhood-scale analysis from the Sustainable, Healthy and Learning Cities and Neighbourhoods project (Wang et al., 2025). We explore how neighbourhoods are planned, designed and managed under conditions of rapid urbanisation, informality, socio-economic inequality, and institutional complexity. We invite papers that critically examine planning approaches, urban design practices, and neighbourhood management and governance practices across cities in the Global South. The session welcomes contributions from scholars at all career stages and encourages submissions from researchers and practitioners working in, or in close collaboration with, communities in the Global South. Contributions may examine formal and informal planning systems, community-led and hybrid governance arrangements, neighbourhood design and public space, neighbourhood sustainability practices, service provision, mobility, health and learning environment, and data-driven neighbourhood interventions. We also welcome methodological papers that have used advanced quantitative or qualitative research approaches in examining neighbourhoods in the Global South.

 References

 Baffoe, G. and Kintrea, K. (2023) 'Neighbourhood research in the Global South: What do we know so far?', Cities, 132, p. 104077. Lees, L., Shin, H. B. and López-Morales, E. (2016) Planetary gentrification. John Wiley & Sons. Parnell, S. and Robinson, J. (2012) '(Re) theorizing cities from the Global South: Looking beyond neoliberalism', Urban geography, 33(4), pp. 593-617. UN-Habitat (2014) A New Strategy of Sustainable Neighbourhood Planning: Five principles – Urban Planning Discussion Note 3. [Online]. Available at: https://unhabitat.org/wp-content/uploads/2014/05/5-Principles_web.pdf. Wang, Y. P., Kintrea, K., Everatt, D. and Kundu, D. (eds.) (2025) New Drivers of Division: Urbanisation and Spatial Inequality in Africa and Asia. Springer Nature.

sessions.title: Contestations of green transitions in UK agriculture (2)
sessions.description:
The green agricultural transition in the UK is at a fractious, contested, and pivotal moment. Post-Brexit changes to agri-environmental schemes have yet to realise significant success in minimising the environmental impact of agriculture and have led to farmers' protests across the UK. While much of the UK's net-zero plans rely on support from rural (and farming) communities, reactionary groups have exploited farmers' feelings of uncertainty, frustration, and a lack of trust in the government to promote anti-net-zero sentiments . The notion of a just transition is key to any future green transition in UK agriculture, yet how this transition can be realised, and what a "just future" even looks like, remains unclear.

 This session explores the possibilities threats, opportunities, limitations of, and resistances to green agricultural transitions within the UK. Themes include but are not limited to:

 - Farmers' perspectives on climate change and the green transition
- The role of experiential knowledge in a achieving a just transition in agriculture
- Farmers' protests and the attempted co-option of these protests by reactionary groups
- Policy or financial barriers to agricultural transitions
- Visions for a "just future" of farming

sessions.title: Agricultural land and the Urban Periphery (2)
sessions.description:
Urban scholars argue that the periphery must take center stage in urban enquiry, especially in the Global South where most urbanisation occurs through the conversion of agricultural lands (Gururani, 2020; Kennedy & Sood, 2016a). Far from being a residual or marginal space, it has become a key terrain for mega-projects, world-class smart cities, and speculative real estate (Goldman, 2011; Kennedy et al., 2014; Raman, 2016). Periphery is shaped through the continuous interactions between rural and urban actors, institutions, and processes (Balakrishnan & Gururani, 2021). The promised idea of development through conversion of agricultural lands is often portrayed as future-oriented, and marketised neatly in form of planned development, smart cities, high-rise gated residential neighbourhoods, and shopping centres. However, this future oriented imagination is often complex and layered with historical past, and present. This is particularly relevant in context of periphery, where land, memory, and kinship remain deeply interwoven in form of colonial land regimes, ancestral claims to land, common lands, or historical associations to land (Simpson, 2014). This also sometimes becomes a subtle form of resistance to the future-oriented disciplining of planned development projected through policies and master plans. 

 The inherited inequalities due to differential power relations because of social, political, spatial, and economic relations gets refigured during these transitions of agricultural lands at the urban periphery. This largely aligns with the theme of the conference focusing on complexities of spatial inequalities at the periphery. Through this call for proposal, we seek to invite papers across various geographies undergoing transformation of agricultural lands on the periphery. We are looking for papers that engage conceptually, methodologically, or empirically grounded with the themes including (not limited to): temporality, contestation of land use changes, role of state, landownership and tenure, and social and cultural relations associated with agricultural lands. This could include exploring questions around:

 - What are the different forms of practices rooted in past or present that actors engage on agricultural lands? 
- How do these practices interact with the future oriented planned development envisioned through the conversion of agricultural lands? 
- Do these practices contest, resist, or further widen the inherited power asymmetries associated with agricultural lands? 
- What are the methods that can evoke the past relation or absences and compel the actors to push forward or construct the image of lands in transition against the future oriented maps or images produced through policies or plans?
- How do we capture the emotions, the visuals, and the spatial-temporal changes taken place at the periphery that weaves together the past, present, and future?

papers.title: Narratives of dispossession: agrarian (counter)reform and Mexico's urban peripheries

sessions.title: Cultivating Just Community Food Systems through Food Sharing (2)
sessions.description:
Socio-economic, environmental and spatial inequalities are central to how Food Sharing Initiatives (FSIs), from community gardens and landed kitchens, to seed saving initiatives and community composting, develop, organize, and operate. These inequalities shape the landscapes that FSIs are embedded in, as well as their strategies for inclusion, advocacy, and transformation.

 Spatial inequalities in land access and governance, for example, directly influence the ways in which collective growing initiatives access and govern land, and which populations they are accountable to. For example, in cities across the US racialized disinvestments in black neighborhoods through red lining are linked to an abundance of vacant land and the demand for community gardens. At the same times, spatial and socio-economic inequalities that manifest in gentrification and land speculation can make community gardens unwitting accomplices in green gentrification. Under conditions of climatic change, multilayered riskscapes increasingly affect adaptive capacities around community food initiatives internationally, with community gardens rebranded as urban nature-based solutions in some locations.

 The COVID 19 pandemic revealed the fragility of the capitalist food system, and the resilience of the community food system – where countless forms of mutual aid (from community fridges to community kitchens) mobilized to rescue and redistribute food and/or access food from local growers, to feed to those in need, alongside more established food banks and food aid mechanisms. Socio-economic and power inequalities are central to how food sharing is organized, beginning from the economic inequalities that create the need for food assistance, to the power inequalities between food donors and receivers, to social inequalities and stigmas related to charity and surplus food.

 In this session we invite food sharing scholars and community-based food practitioners to reflect on the role of food sharing initiatives in creating and sustaining just places. We challenge contributors to address inequalities and injustices that shape how these initiatives develop and operate, and what injustices might still need to be overcome for FSIs to be successful in meeting their mission, and creating more just community food systems and places. We are interested in approaches and tools developed to be a counterweight to inequalities and the extent to which food sharing initiatives collaborative with other actors in their efforts to do this. We are keen to hear about diverse community food imaginaries and research exploring tensions between these and those expressed by public or private sector actors.

 We welcome theoretically and empirically driven papers (or more creative interventions that can be shared in 15 minutes) that address topics such as:

 Art and citizen engagement

 Serious Games

 Food sharing

 Food governance

 Food Infrastructure

 Food Commons

 Food sustainability

 Care

 Conviviality

 Community cooking & eating

 Community growing

 Surplus Food Redistribution

 Agroecological urbanism

 Landed Community Kitchens

 Communities of food practice

 Critical food mapping

 Community food imaginaries

 Collective food action

papers.title: Governance modes and contested resilience: Economic, social, and environmental value of food sharing in Barcelona, Milan, and Utrecht
Troubling Inequities: Temporal, Anticolonial, and More Than Human Approaches to Community Composting
Collective food responses to climate disasters: food solidarity networks after the Valencia DANA floods
Moving beyond consumption: competing worlds of worth for urban food system transformation

sessions.title: Historicising Urban Labour Regimes: In/Visibility, Infrastructural Labour, and Resistance (2)
sessions.description:
The relationship between urbanisation and (semi)proletarianisation reflects distinct historical conjunctures shaped by colonialism, racial capitalism, and the uneven insertion of economies into global circuits of capital. These processes have produced urban labour regimes in which informality, precarity, household-based production, and circular migration are not transitional anomalies but enduring and politically consequential forms of urban life.

 Despite precarious workers' contributions to urban reproduction through 'essential' infrastructural labour (Fredericks, 2014, 2021; Gidwani, 2015; Gidwani and Chari, 2004; Stokes and De-Coss Corzo, 2023), worker histories and historical struggles remain underexplored in geographical debate. In the archival register, we find scant information of worker lives in records of the colonial and postcolonial state, and in the ethnographic register, the social reproductive realm and unwaged or ‘hidden’ labour are often treated as less important or accessible relative to the politics of waged work and the worksite. Increasingly precarious conditions—exacerbated by climate stress, privatization, debt, xenophobia, authoritarianism and surveillance—make tracing these histories and contemporary realities ever more pressing.

 This panel invites historically grounded and empirically diverse contributions across geographies and sectors that foreground comparative approaches to urban labour regimes. It asks: how have different historical pathways of urbanisation shaped labour relations and struggles in southern cities across class, gender, caste and race? We invite reflections on whether Southern urban labour histories might illuminate contemporary Northern urban conditions marked by platform economies, migrant labour preponderance, and social reproduction infrastructure transformations. However, we also question the risks of universalising labour categories and temporal narratives. How might historicising urban labour differently enable more situated, worker-centred theories of urban development?

papers.title: The Brahmanical Panopticon: History of Reproduction of Caste and Sanitation Labour in Bombay
Men in the Sun: Subaltern Workers, and the Ecological Labour of Urban Modernity in Singapore and Qatar
“We unite despite oppression”: Tracing Unseen Labour Histories in the Making and Maintenance of ‘Sanitary’ Towns and Cities in East Bengal
How did barber Anees become a gig-worker? An ethnography of an urban village in Delhi, and how it organises labour and capital under Hindu majoritarianism

sessions.title: Doing diplomacy beyond national jurisdiction (2)
sessions.description:
Environmental diplomacy is inherently geographical (see Kuus, 2016). It takes place; it is geographically placed (in specific nations, cities, and then in particular buildings (Touloumi, 2024), etc., as well as online (i.e. Vadrot et al. 2021)), and it involves hierarchies of socio-political place, rendered through power dynamics (i.e. ‘weaker’ states vs. ‘powerful’ ones, global north and south, ‘developing’, ‘developed’, ‘small island’ and so on). Diplomacy, then, involves inequalities of access, across different scales, from restricted institutional decision-making spaces (such as the UN) to places beyond national jurisdiction that are being discussed (i.e. the High Seas). 

 This session will pick apart the practices of researching in spaces of environmental diplomacy and bring to the fore difficulties encountered in undertaking research by actively engaging in spaces of environmental diplomacy (Hughes and Vadrot, 2023). In doing so, it also addresses how researchers might have to ‘do’ research diplomatically. It also considers the access points for those involved directly in diplomacy and decision-making. 

 In proposing this session, we take inspiration from the recent ratification of the Agreement on Marine Biological Diversity of Areas beyond National Jurisdiction (BBNJ Treaty), the ongoing negotiation of a Mining Code for the seabed beyond national jurisdiction by the International Seabed Authority (ISA), and a year of yet more global level environmental conferencing (i.e. COP and United Nations Ocean Conference (UNOC)). We invite submissions that deal with all iterations of environmental diplomacy, however, with a focus on the governance of geographies beyond national jurisdiction. 

 Organisations such as the United Nations (UN) and annual meetings, including the Conference of the Parties (COP) and the Commission for the Conservation of Antarctic and the Marine Living Resources (CCAMLR), rely on individual nations to act as host countries. Meeting attendance is subject to certain delegate status, from visa costs and approvals, security guardrails, to adhering to the host country’s governance regimes. Membership and participation in meetings are often exclusive, resource-intensive, multilevelled, and deeply uneven across delegations and events, with convoluted processes for gaining access and limitations on speaking rights. We thus ask who gets to make (legally-binding) decisions, and on behalf of whom, as many of these negotiations tackle issues that concern us all.

papers.title: Oceanising Traditional Knowledge: Indigenous Presence and the Politics of Access at OOSC and UNOC
affiliations: French National Research Institute for Sustainable Development (IRD), Laboratory of Environmental Marine Sciences (Lemar)
Université Côte d’Azur, UMR ESPACE — Institut Universitaire de France
