sessions.title: Justice in tourism geographies (2)
sessions.description:
Despite a long-held concern with issues of justice within geography, in recent years we have witnessed a growing recognition that justice is a core concern among tourism geographers (cf. Paddison & Hall, 2022; Tomassini & Lamond, 2023; Wan et. al., 2024). And yet, despite its high usage, there is often limited direct discussion from tourism geographers regarding the substance of justice. Geographers, including those working in tourism studies, have often been hesitant to articulate what they mean by ‘justice,’ concerned that doing so imposes a normative ideal of fairness. Instead, they tend to favour practice-based, pluriversal, ‘bottom-up’ understandings of justice (Barnett, 2016); a move that constructs a universal/pluriversal binary.

 In the past decade, however, geographers have cautioned that there is a requirement to openly discuss the substance of justice, to consider the specificities of claims in relation to another, avoid extreme relativism whereby all claims to justice are equally valid without grounds for critique, and steer clear of any reductions in the concept’s political and analytical utility (Barnett, 2016; Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). We draw on this critical commentary to call for open dialogue within the sub-discipline of tourism geographies, to the conditions through which injustices are recognised and addressed, to make sense of ‘how and why observable patterns of inequity, discrimination, or unevenness are actually unjust’ (Barnett, 2016, pp.112).

papers.title: Geographies of Affect and the Spectres of Colonial Expansion and Tourism
Climate Vs Tourism Mobilities in the Sundarbans Delta: Through a Mobility Justice Approach
affiliations: Tata Institute of Social Sciences, Mumbai

Tourism with a heart: the politics of care in national strategic governance

sessions.title: Movements, memories and moments of democratic localism (2)
sessions.description:
Localist experiments have a long, varied and ambivalent lineage (Beveridge and Cochrane 2023), connecting from the escapist commune, through different municipalist waves including the Greater London Council and Barcelona en Comu, to antagonistic and alternative provision practices of parallel institutions, such as public pharmacies (Gonnet et al, 2025), solidarity economies (Arampatzi 2022) and urban agriculture (Traill 2023). The new municipalist moment, apparently stalled and deemed in crisis (Roth et al, 2023), may be the loudest recent iteration of this. However, when contextualised in a broader swathe of more ordinary municipalisations across the globe (Cumbers and Paul 2020), the persistence of local practices of mutualism, experimentation and self-organisation becomes apparent; often operating within as well as outwith formal policy processes.

 In this session, we invite papers that attend to the persistence of what we term "democratic localism": an umbrella term we use to denote a global and often interlinked paradigm of locally-scaled political interventions, both in, against and beyond the state, that reimagine everyday life from the ground up. Often such projects draw inspiration and elements of practice from a global elsewhere, with a circulation of policies akin to the movement of policy – but driven by a more heterodox set of actors, less a set of jet-setting policy elite and more rooted in everyday action and activism. Such a hypothesized movement (loosely conceived) draws on social and professional networks, histories of activism and the reification of iconic places like Barcelona or Rojava as metonyms for a set of often participatory practices that are under-explored.

 Whilst the current political moment is increasingly framed by rising authoritarianisms and entrenched crises, these movements persist. How they do so, in inhospitable times, remains a provocative question.

papers.title: Rethinking Municipalism from the Mountain: Toward an Ecological Civic Democracy
Model Places, the Politics of Utopia and the Imagination of Democratic Localism
Massey goes to Moscow: thinking conjunctures spatially
Mapping Democratic Localism: Knowledge Circulation in the Fearless Cities Network

sessions.title: Walking on Uneven Ground: Access, Belonging and Environmental Change on Trail Landscapes (2)
sessions.description:
Trail-based activities such as hiking, trail running and off-road cycling are often imagined as accessible and inclusive practices, particularly within rural landscapes that are framed as shared, equal and open spaces for all. Yet geographers and social scientists increasingly demonstrate that they are not socially or environmentally neutral. Rather, they are infrastructures through which uneven relations of power, belonging, and responsibility are organised (Cloke & Little, 1997; Neal, 2002; Chakraborti & Garland, 2004; Shrubsole, 2019; Fowler, 2020).

 Across footpaths, rights of way, bridleways, access land, and National Trails, everyday practices of movement produce differentiated experiences of comfort, entitlement, surveillance, and exclusion along lines of race, class, gender, disability, age, and nationality. At the same time, trails are increasingly shaped by environmental pressures associated with climate change, biodiversity loss, and landscape degradation. These changes are often encountered not abstractly, but sensorially and affectively: through eroded paths, altered seasonal rhythms, flood-damaged routes, or transformed ecologies. As such, trails operate as sites where inequalities of access and belonging intersect with questions of care, responsibility, and human–environment relations.

 Whilst outdoor recreation has been examined through mobilities (Snead et al., 2009) leisure (Lanclus, 2023), tourism (Timothy & Boyd, 2015), wellbeing (Pomfret et al., 2024) and heritage (Svensson et al., 2022), trails themselves remain comparatively under-theorised as spacial formations that structure both social relations and environmental engagements. Frequently marketed as universally welcoming and resilient spaces, trails are none-the-less shaped by legal frameworks, governance, historical narratives and affective atmospheres that privilege particular bodies, practices, and modes of environmental engagement while marginalising others.

 This session brings together work that critically examines trails as uneven and unsettled landscapes of movement. The second timeslot will focus on senses of place and embodied experiences on trails. Felicitas Kuebler’s paper delves into the limits of enjoyment and the negative atmospheres by approaching hiking in the Alps as embodied micro-practices. Dave McLaughlin explores the extent to which walking the International Appalachian Trail works to create space which holds across the transatlantic ‘Appalachian Divide’. By examining the trail-walking experiences at the Mogao Caves in Dunhuang, China, Chang Guo reveals how top-down heritage preservation influences perceptions of landscape and place. The second timeslot concludes with Eilis Lanclus’ paper which draws on sensory ethnographic work with trail sport participants in the UK and Belgium to unpack the duality between the growing desire of moving through ‘wild(er)’ landscapes and the ecological pressures these activities put on those landscapes. Taken together, these papers reveal how trails are lived and felt through the body, showing that movements through trail landscapes are inseparable from shifting senses of place and environmental change.

papers.title: Negative Atmospheres, appropriation of nature, and moments of enjoyment: Ethnographic considerations of hiking in the Alps

sessions.title: Climate justice, creativity and humour: learning with and from children and young people (2)
sessions.description:
Recent years have seen growing acknowledgement of the essential role of humour in communicating about climate change, including in education (Gerlofs and Zheng, 2025; Russell et al., 2023). This may seem at odds with such a serious topic. However, humour is a recognised tool of social justice (Borum Chattoo and Feldman, 2020) and may offer new and engaging approaches for climate change education (CCE) in the face of the climate fatigue and despair reported among many young people (Hickman et al., 2021; Sanson and Bellemo, 2021).

 As scholars and practitioners conducting research with children and young people on climate-related topics, humour has become an intriguing aspect of our work. We are interested to hear from others who are similarly interested in the ways that fun, creativity, irony, irreverence and other comedic devices have and could become tools of climate justice, complementing or disrupting more traditional pedagogic methods. 

 We welcome traditional, creative or interactive presentations exploring children and young people’s own use of humour, their responses to others’ use of humour in CCE (or in wider climate communication), and especially research that involves children and young people in a participatory process, emphasizing co-production. The use of comedic devices in presentation delivery is warmly welcomed but not required! 

 Questions to be explored include, but are not limited to:

 · Can there be fun in CCE? What role can humour play in CCE?

 · Why is humour important when working in CCE?

 · How can humour open serious questions of climate justice? 

 · How does humour enter CCE, through children and educators alike?

 · How can or could humour work across language, culture and generational divides to support climate solidarity?

 · How are humour and creativity mutually supporting?

 · What are the ethics of using humour in research with children on climate change?

papers.title: No Room for Gloom: Humour, Creativity and Climate Justice in the Classroom
No Laughing Matter: The role of humour in CCE
Beyond Environmental Justice:  Satire, Agency, and Emancipatory Worlding in AstroNuts Mission One
Climate change catastrophe! Children as seriously funny climate communicators

sessions.title: Geographies of Authoritarianism (2)
sessions.description:
State authoritarian practices are in the ascendency around the world and have been for several years (Diamond et al 2016). Geographers have an important role to play in understanding and mapping their development, analysing their causes and consequences, and identifying tactics that slow and reverse their progress (Koch 2022). The stakes are high: individual and collective freedoms are being eroded in economic, social, cultural, institutional, political, digital, intellectual, spiritual, reproductive and corporeal spaces (see Regilme 2021).

 This session features papers that use geographical approaches and insights to speak to the philosophy, epistemology, and empirical nature of authoritarian practices. Philosophically, we need to understand how human freedom has become devalued - there is a need to comprehend the modes of thought that make authoritarianism a seductive state strategy. Epistemologically, we need to confront the reality that authoritarianism is hard, and sometimes dangerous, to study owing to the vested interest of states in concealing it – perspectives that establish principles for the safe and effective study of authoritarianism in the current era of increasingly constrained academic freedom are needed. Empirically, we need approaches that connect and traverse insights from the geographical sub-disciplines in recognition of the cross-cutting nature of authoritarian practices. We also need to reflect on the balance between defense and critique of ostensibly liberal practices and institutions that claim to seek to uphold freedom and human rights.

 Whilst evidence and argument points towards the generalized pattern of increasing authoritarianism at a global scale (Diamond et al 2016) – a sense in which a similar set of authoritarian discourse, practice, and rule is taking place across the globe – it remains true that these authoritarianisms look and feel different in different places (Dahlmann 2022). There is not just one kind of authoritarianism which operates in the same way in different places. What then, can a distinctly geographical approach to authoritarianism contribute?

papers.title: Authoritarian shocks, right-wing interpretation: the war in Ukraine as catalyst for conspiracy beliefs
Nonauthoritarian Authority: Dignity, Justice, and Resistance in a Peripheral Urban Community Experiencing Authoritarian Violence
Feminist and queer autonomous urban spaces under authoritarian neoliberalism
affiliations: Universidad Nacional de Tres de Febrero Buenos Aires

sessions.title: Inequalities from Space
sessions.description:
This inclusive, hands-on workshop introduces participants to data products derived from satellite imagery and demonstrates how they can be used to better understand social and environmental inequalities. At the Imago: Data Service for Imagery, we transform complex earth observation data into accessible, policy-ready datasets that support research, health, and decision-making at the neighbourhood scale.

 Participants will explore Imago datasets and learn how to replicate analytical workflows across multiple products. The workshop will begin by demonstrating a complete workflow using an example dataset. Attendees will then apply the same steps to alternative dataset combinations, building confidence in transferring methods.

 Working with real data, participants will load spatial boundaries, merge imagery-derived indicators with deprivation measures, produce summary statistics, and create maps and visualisations to identify spatial patterns. The session also introduces simple statistical techniques—including correlation, regression, and hotspot identification—to support evidence-based interpretations while encouraging critical reflection on confounding factors and causal inference.

 Designed for all experience levels, the training welcomes those new to satellite data alongside participants familiar with geospatial tools or programming in R or Python. By the end of the workshop, attendees will have a reusable analytical workflow, practical mapping skills, and a clearer understanding of how imagery-derived data can help address real-world social policy questions.

sessions.title: Accounting for nature, or holding nature to account? Critical geographies of environmental accounting (2)
sessions.description:
This session stages a critical exploration of the links between geography, and cognate disciplines, and questions of environmental accounting. Over the last two decades, the environment has not only been increasingly taken into account - with its social and economic benefits measured, quantified, valued and priced - but also, more recently, held to account. While the former is often presented as a route to environmental improvement, it warrants scrutiny: environmental accounting is invariably partial, shaped by power relations, and uneven in its effects. The latter development is more troubling. As nature is ledgered and administered like any other asset, it becomes fungible, replaceable even, through accounting logics. This session will explore the Janus-faced nature of environmental accounting, where, on the one hand, accounting practices enable regimes of governance and place nature on the public balance sheet, on the other, they serve to demand a justification for nature’s existence via economic reasoning. 

 Carbon accounting brought something as nebulous as entities’ relations with the atmosphere onto the books, to assume (selective) carbon accountability, benchmark and rank progress, enable carbon markets and offsetting practices, and forecast decarbonisation pathways of benefit or minimal detriment to ‘business as usual’. Similarly, nature accounts, forming the basis of natural capital, biodiversity net gain, and the restoration economy, have further extended ‘stock-taking’ financial logics into the environmental domain. Governments, business, NGOs, and universities have largely acquiesced to the need for cost-benefit analyses in environmental decision-making, to the common sense of holding nature itself to account. The influence of economists, accountants, financial consultants and investors has significantly grown as a result, challenging that of ecologists and conservation bodies, at the fuzzy intersection of science, politics, and economics. 

 Beyond professionals, everyone can provide their own account of ‘the environment’. But amidst its popular conflation with economic accounting, it is often reserved as a practice for experts and technocratic management, delimiting who gets to speak for the environment. In response, geographers, STS scholars, and others have sought to foreground the potential of overlooked perspectives, including radical, decolonial, artistic, and activist backgrounds. This session invites contributions to contemplate and discuss the threats, limits and affordances of balance sheet nature.

papers.title: Democratised Carbon Accounting? A Reflection on the In Our Nature Project
affiliations: Tyndall Centre for Climate Change Research, University of Manchester

sessions.title: Negotiating Dignity: Rethinking Justice in Geographical Research (3)
sessions.description:
Papers in this session place dignity at the centre of geographical research on justice. Over the past two decades, justice has become a core concern across multiple subdisciplines, including critical urban geography, environmental and climate justice, feminist and care geographies, Black and anti-racist geographies, political geography, and mobilities and transport geography. Much of this work has productively moved beyond distributive frameworks to address recognition, participation, embodiment, affect, and everyday power (Hopkins, 2021; Przybylinski, 2022). Yet the normative and lived stakes of these debates are often articulated indirectly. Dignity helps name what is frequently at issue but insufficiently theorised: the ability to inhabit space without humiliation, erasure, or disposability, and to exercise autonomy and social standing in the face of structural inequality. At the same time, dignity is a concept with a complex and contested history. Its universalist claims—long embedded in law, bioethics, and humanitarian governance—have often failed to confront colonial, racialised, gendered, and caste-based hierarchies, and at times have actively reproduced them (Misztal, 2013). Recent geographical scholarship has responded by advancing relational, contextual, and politically grounded understandings of dignity, attentive to how it is produced, denied, and negotiated through everyday encounters with institutions, infrastructures, and publics (Grossmann and Trubina, 2022; Kariũki and Martens, 2026; Plyushteva, 2023; McIlwaine et al., 2025). Taken together, dignity-oriented research extends geographical debates on justice. It draws attention to the affective and ethical dimensions of inequality that are often muted in distributive or procedural accounts. In doing so, it highlights how injustice is reproduced through routine practices of degradation and erasure. References 

 Grossmann, K., & Trubina, E. (2022). Dignity in urban geography: Starting a conversation. Dialogues in Human Geography , 12 (3), 406-426.

 Hopkins, P. (2021). Social geography III: Committing to social justice. Progress in Human Geography , 45 (2), 382-393.

 Kariũki, W., & Martens, K. (2026). Dignified transport: The case of paratransit. Travel Behaviour and Society , 42 , 101140.

 McIlwaine, C., Peppl, R., & Cal Angrisani, C. (2025). Reclaiming dignity through creative encounters among Brazilian women survivors of violence in London. cultural geographies , 14744740251319039.

 Misztal, B. A. (2013). The idea of dignity: Its modern significance. European Journal of Social Theory , 16 (1), 101-121.

 Plyushteva, A. (2023). Negotiating dignity in public geography: The ethics of public engagement in pandemic times. Area , 55 (1), 53-61.

 Przybylinski, S. (2022). Where is justice in geography? A review of justice theorizing in the discipline. Geography Compass , 16 (3), e12615.

sessions.title: Human Presence Beyond Earth: Critical Geographies of Outer Space (2)
sessions.description:
After 54 years, humans are returning to extraterrestrial environments, namely around and on the Moon. Unlike the Apollo missions, which focused on exploration and return, today's lunar programs envision permanent settlement—transforming the Moon from a destination for brief visits into a new human territory. In February 2026, for instance, NASA plans to launch Artemis II, which will send four humans beyond Earth’s orbit for the first time since 1972 as they slingshot around the moon. As humans prepare to return to Earth’s only natural satellite, how can geographers critically engage with this development while challenging uneven power relations? Building on geographers' long-standing inquiry into outer space and recent publications debating human space activities (Armstrong & Klinger, 2025; Bennett and Cudney, 2025; Klinger, 2021; Squire et al., 2021), this session continues this line of investigation by exploring critical geographies of outer space.

 Contemporary space exploration differs fundamentally from the Apollo era, unfolding within a broader transformation of global geopolitics (Daniels & Krige, 2022) and commercialization (Denis et al., 2020). The bipolar order of the Cold War has given way to a multipolar landscape structured by technological rivalry, particularly between the United States and China, shaping these countries' space imaginations and missions (Puente-Lozano & Herring-Bazo, 2024; Wijeyeratne, 2026). Space exploration is no longer dominated by nation-states alone, but increasingly shaped by commercial–state partnerships, private capital, and hybrid governance arrangements emerging from shifting geopolitical configurations (Utrata, 2024). Proposals for "Moon villages," permanent infrastructures, and energy systems—including nuclear power and data centers—signal a move from exploration to settlement, raising urgent questions about sovereignty, territorialization, and control beyond Earth (Au, 2024; Vermeylen & Njeri, 2023).

 Earth’s orbital space is becoming increasingly crowded and integrated into social, political, and economic dynamics both on Earth and beyond (Bowen, 2020; Dunnett et al., 2019). Critical scholars have questioned who is accessing outer space and how these space missions are enabled through uneven, racialized, and even colonial exploitation on Earth (Armstrong & Klinger, 2025; Klinger et al., 2025; Redfield, 2000). Research has also explored how human perception and sensory experience shape engagements with outer space, as scholars develop and reflect on research methods for studying space and space-related practices (Praet & Pitrou, 2025; Timko et al., 2022; Vertesi, 2015). Outer space is not simply another research project: it is interwoven with terrestrial dynamics through the spacecraft, infrastructure, imagination, and legal systems that connect humanity to space (Messeri & Olson, 2025). As outer space assumes greater importance in geopolitical power and geo-economic competition, questions arise about how space infrastructure and its entanglement with Earth's social, cultural, political, and colonial histories reproduce existing uneven power relations beyond our planet.

 As outer space becomes increasingly entangled with Earth-based political economies, geographers are well positioned to interrogate this spatial reordering. This session aims to discuss the outer space geography in a critical way.

papers.title: Mineralogical Imaginaries of the Lunar Subsurface: Private and Political Discourses of Lunar Futures
Ontologies in Preparation for Contact
Intercultural Protocols for Ethical Human Presence Beyond Earth
Earth Seeds For Space: Considering Astrobotany as a Decolonial Practice in Space Exploration
